To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the Queens University Belfast Screens, Safety and Social Media study which found that over 75 per cent of 16 year olds surveyed spent at least three hours on average per day on social media, given that higher use is associated with poorer self-reported mental health.
To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of his Department's data on emotionally based school non-attendance.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of the September 2025 Queens University Belfast Screens, Safety and Social Media study which found that over half of 16 year olds surveyed reported experiencing illegal or harmful online behaviours, such as offensive content, non-consensual sharing and online stalking.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the September 2025 Queens University Belfast Screens, Safety and Social Media study which found that over 75 per cent of 16 year olds surveyed spent at least three hours on average per day on social media, given that higher use is associated with poorer self-reported mental health.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the process by which members were appointed to the Artificial Intelligence Advisory Panel.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plan to address teacher burnout, given the finding that 91 per cent of teachers surveyed reported moderate to high levels of burnout.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to promote literacy and foster a love for reading in young people, given the National Literacy Trust 2025 survey finding that fewer than three in ten children and young people aged 8 to 18 in the North said they enjoyed reading in their free time.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any services are being compromised following the £100 million overspend on the encompass system.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment made of (i) the benefits of a road safety commissioner model for road safety; and (ii) the merits of cross-border alignment of a road safety commissioner model.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 42889/22-27, in light of her confirmation that voluntary developer contributions will not close the funding gap between the amount that NI Water receives from the Executive and the amount that is needed to address wastewater constraints, to detail (i) the projected funding gap for Price Control periods 2021-27 and 2028-33; and (ii) the estimated proportion of the funding gap that will be addressed by the current three-pronged approach, during each Price Control period.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applications that have (i) been submitted to the Waiting List Reimbursement scheme; and (ii) been successful, since 2 June 2025.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any investment required by NI Water to address wastewater capacity issues, broken down by local council area.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has made of the merits of requiring signage on all ride-share vehicles, to regulate and standardise proper vehicle markings and enhance passenger and driver safety.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether any assessment has been made of NI Water's 28 January 2026 press release, stating that short-term injections of funding do not provide a sustainable solution for critical infrastructure and that long-term, fully funded investment through future Price Control periods is essential to meet housing demand, protect the environment and support economic development; and (ii) to detail how her Department will provide NI Water with long-term fully funded investment.
answer_text: I have previously stated that the need to modernise our ageing wastewater system is a top priority for my Department. We are paying for years of neglect as a result of British Government austerity and underfunding, which has created significant challenges to upgrade wastewater infrastructure, leaving the system ageing, overstretched, and unable to support housing growth or environmental compliance at scale. This is not a shortterm problem and cannot be solved within a single budget cycle. Sustained, longterm investment is required to modernise infrastructure and futureproof the systemI am working with Executive colleagues to advocate for an increase in wastewater investment beyond singleyear settlements. This includes making the case for a multi-year budget settlement that supports planned delivery over the price control period. rather than relying on adhoc allocations.  However, there is much more we can achieve beyond simply seeking additional funding.  Innovative thinking and working in partnership can unlock wastewater capacity faster than traditional capital upgrades alone. NI Water’s work in places such as Newry is an example of this approach in action, delivering quicker, lowercost solutions within current funding constraints.  Furthermore, I will continue to progress the implementation of developer contributions and new flooding and sustainable drainage legislation to support NI Water’s ability to cope with climate change, housing demand and ageing infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any assessment has been made of the merits of implementing a levy on social media companies to go towards online safety training for schools and parents.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to raise awareness of the Safer Schools app, which provides students, parents, and school staff with knowledge to navigate the online world safely.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the merits of establishing a working group to develop strategies on securing online safety guardrails to protect children, including holding social media companies responsible for paying for internet safety training and mental health programmes for children, parents, and teachers.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any resources made available to schools for the provision of age appropriate online safety training for (i) students; (ii) teachers; and (iii) parents, regarding (a) online harm; (b) data security; (c) AI deepfakes; and (d) social media safety.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of the merits of providing training to PSNI officers, regarding digital crimes, including Artificial Intelligence deepfakes and scams.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she has undertaken to support and provide certainty to businesses and employees at the Newry Quay's Shopping Centre, after the owning company entered administration.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any support her Department offered Ulster University, prior to the news that they would be letting over 400 employees go; and (ii) any measures she is proposing to reassure employees.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planning policies she intends to bring forward to address the depopulation of rural communities.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine's April 2026 report The State of Emergency Medicine in Northern Ireland finding that 1,032 excess deaths were associated with waits of 12 hours or more in Emergency Departments.

To ask the Minister of Health, following the Alzheimer’s Society’s April 2026 report The Training Gap: A Hidden Injustice in Dementia Care and How to Fix It,  whether his Department will implement its recommendation that the minimum standard for nursing care, residential care homes and domiciliary care is a requirement for staff to undertake best practice dementia training of at least eight hours, mapped to at least Tier 2, or Tier 3 for staff working in settings with a high proportion of people with dementia.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 42807/22-27, to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) Age NI; and (ii) the Commissioner for Older People NI, on Continuing Healthcare eligibility.
answer_text: (i) Age NI: There has been no recent engagement with Age NI specifically on the issue of CHC eligibility.(ii) Commissioner for Older People Northern Ireland (COPNI): To date, my Department’s engagement with the Commissioner for Older People Northern Ireland on the issue of CHC has been in the context of legal proceedings initiated by the Commissioner, including the judicial review of the 2010 and 2021 CHC policies.  The Commissioner is due to meet with the Department’s Permanent Secretary in the coming weeks to discuss the outcome of the CHC judicial review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale as to why (i) cotoneaster horizontalis; and (ii) cotoneaster simonsii, are not listed as non-native invasive species under the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail whether any (i) preliminary work; (ii) analysis; or (iii) consultation, has been undertaken within her Department to address the legal and policy questions associated with the possible development of an offence relating to domestic abuse-related suicide.
answer_text: I fully recognise the strong link between domestic abuse and related suicides and want to acknowledge the devastation felt by all families who have lost loved ones in these tragic circumstances.As my responses to AQW 41118/22-27 and AQW 40257/22-27 set out, there is currently no specific offence in any part of the UK for causing or encouraging suicide through domestic abuse or coercive control. However, sections 13 and 13A of the Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 1966 make it an offence to encourage or assist the suicide or attempted suicide of another person, or to intend to do so. Section 13A also makes clear that this includes situations where a person has encouraged or assisted another by threatening them or otherwise pressuring them to commit or attempt suicide.In addition, the introduction of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 marked a significant milestone in strengthening the courts’ ability to protect individuals from violent and abusive behaviour. The Act has transformed how the criminal justice system understands and responds to domestic abuse. Protection is no longer limited to physical violence; the legislation also criminalises a course of abusive behaviour, including controlling or coercive conduct, that may be psychological, emotional, or financial.Under section 15 of the 2021 Act, any offence (other than the domestic abuse offence itself) may be aggravated if it involves domestic abuse. Where both the offence and the aggravation are proven, the court must consider the aggravation when determining the sentence. In principle, this could apply to manslaughter if it were established that a person died by suicide as a result of domestic abuse.As such, I am satisfied that the current legislation gives the courts sufficient powers to hold perpetrators of domestic abuse to account. However, no other substantive preliminary work, analysis or consultation has been undertaken to date in relation to the development of a specific offence of domestic abuse-related suicide. Given the complexity of the legal and evidential issues involved, including the requirement to establish a clear causal link to the criminal standard, such work would be substantial and resource intensive.The Department’s current priority is the delivery of existing commitments to tackle domestic and sexual abuse. In that context, it has not been possible to initiate work in this area, which would require careful, cross-cutting consideration and would form part of any future programme of work.Nonetheless, tackling domestic abuse in all its forms remains a key priority for my Department.  My Department continues to engage with other departments and stakeholders, on how the justice system can better respond to domestic abuse and its consequences, drawing on emerging evidence and developments in neighbouring jurisdictions. I continue to acknowledge the seriousness of these cases and their impact on families and communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale for limiting membership of the Independent Monitoring Board to a maximum of nine years.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the rationale behind the proposed posthumous claim date of September 2011 in the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to extending this date to widen eligibility.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any reason why the three reports from the Interdepartmental Working Group on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Historical Clerical Child Abuse have not been published; and (ii) any potential impact this has on transparency and accountability.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any assessment has been carried out of the impact that the time taken to bring forward the recommendations from the Interdepartmental Working Group on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Historical Clerical Child Abuse is having on victims and survivors.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any reason for the length of time taken to consider the research by the Interdepartmental Working Group on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Historical Clerical Child Abuse.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether victims of Mother and Baby Homes who were both an adopted adult and a birth mother will be considered for two separate payments under the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she has given to installing traffic calming measures in Greenpark Manor, Armagh.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.I have asked my officials to undertake an assessment at Greenpark Manor to determine if any safety interventions are considered appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any supports available to community nurses who have considered or chosen to resign as a result of the costs associated with vehicle repairs due to road conditions and rising fuel prices.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when the urgent pothole repairs will be completed on the Aghmakane Road, County Armagh.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the rationale behind closing the JobStart Scheme; and (ii) whether his department plans to reopen the JobStart Scheme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when Carrickcroppan Road, Camlough, will be resurfaced.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department is providing to the haulage industry, given the increase in fuel costs.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any targets their Department has (i) set; and (ii) met, in this mandate.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the stretch of Drumalane Road, Newry, from the Church of the Assumption to Lock Keepers View, was last inspected; and (ii) any outstanding repairs.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) an update on the work of his Department to introduce a system where bereaved people only have to notify public services once about the death of a loved one; and (ii) any engagement his Department has had with the Department for Communities to progress this work.
answer_text: I am supportive of lessening the burden on bereaved families at very difficult times in their lives, and my department will do as much as possible to support this. The existing bereavement service is with the Communities Minister. Following a constructive meeting with Minister Lyons in September 2025 I agreed that my officials would investigate the legislative work that would be necessary to allow my department to share relevant death registration data with a Tell Us Once type service. My officials have examined several potential legal gateways to verify if there are viable routes that could enable the sharing of death registration information as described. They have identified several issues that would need to be addressed to progress this matter and are committed to working through these.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the British Government, in relation to temporary fuel duty relief or rebates for hauliers, given the increase in fuel costs.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has considered adopting the Tell Us Once approach used in England, Scotland, and Wales to allow grieving families to report a death to multiple government agencies in one go.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 32351 22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale behind proposals to end the provision of elective caesarean surgeries at the maternity ward in Daisy Hill Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of caesarean surgeries performed, in each of the last five years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: The information you have requested is listed below:HSC TrustDelivered In 20212022202320242025Belfast Trust Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital1,4221,4501,6151,7361,809Northern TrustAntrim Area Hospital1,0901,0761,2131,3591,421Causeway Hospital274278125**South Eastern TrustUlster Hospital1,5521,5871,6221,6941,733Southern Trust Craigavon Area Hospital9661,0201,0161,0371,095Daisy Hill Hospital921939924856818Western Trust    South West Acute Hospital428417408413471Altnagelvin Area Hospital9039519081,044963*Please note Causeway Maternity Unit closed in July 2023. Maternity services moved to Antrim Area Hospital site*Northern Ireland’s Caesarean Section rate has consistently been around onethird of all births in recent years. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of a report in the Irish News on 30 March 2026 in relation to former British agents accused of sex crimes and coercive control against women during the Troubles, to detail any work their Department is undertaking to protect and support the victims and ensure they qualify for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of week commencing 30 March 2026, 12,974 applications have been submitted to the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB), with more than £133 million paid out to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened in August 2021.The Victims’ Payments Scheme operates under Regulations put in place by the Secretary of State.  The VPB makes decisions on any entitlement to payment under the Scheme and has published guidance on its website which provides details as to how a relevant incident is defined.The assessment and determination of applications to the Scheme are a matter for the VPB.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rational for the length of time it takes her Department to (i) identify road defects; (ii) mark them for repair; (iii) fill potholes; and (iv) resurface crumbling roads.
answer_text: Defects are recorded and assessed in line with Departmental standards, including DEM 160, which applies a risk-based approach to categorising defects and setting response times. Where appropriate, defects are marked to support safe programming of repairs; however, the time taken to mark and repair will depend on the assessed risk and severity, location, traffic management and coordination requirements, weather conditions, and the capacity and availability of maintenance contractors and in-house resources.Potholes are repaired in line with the DEM 160 category assigned, with temporary repairs undertaken primarily to address immediate safety risks and used where necessary until conditions allow a durable repair. Resurfacing is delivered through planned resurfacing and structural maintenance schemes rather than as a response to individual defects, and schemes are prioritised based on need, condition and safety considerations, network importance and the resources available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 38743/22-27, to detail (i) the increase in construction output in real terms, between 2010 and 2025; (ii) the proportion of that increase relating to new dwelling starts; (iii) the proportion relating to to repair and maintenance work; and (iv) whether 2025 recorded 2,000 fewer new dwelling starts than 2018.
answer_text: Data on construction output is collected by the NISRA in the Quarterly Construction Enquiry (QCE). Key statistics are available on the NISRA website. Based on the latest available data from the QCE: The increase in annual construction output, in real terms, between the year 2010 and the year 2025 was £496.5 million. Between the year 2010 and the year 2025, annual construction output from Repair and Maintenance: Housing increased by £104.3 million in real terms. This includes all construction not defined as new construction, for example, housing conversions, extensions and improvements.New dwelling statistics are published by Land and Property Services (LPS).Official Statistics on new dwelling starts for 2018 and 2025 are available at table 3 in this link- New dwelling statistics | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's strategy to (i) recruit; and (ii) retain, Advanced Nurse Practitioners.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 42890/22-27, to detail any actions they have taken to address the current situation, where parents have no alternative other than to have their deceased children sent to Britain for paediatric pathology services.
answer_text: As I outlined during the Assembly debate on 13 January 2026, I remain committed to doing everything possible to improve hospital consented paediatric pathology services, including exploring the potential development of an allisland service and the use of lessinvasive postmortem approaches.My conversations with my counterpart in the Republic of Ireland, while positive, have confirmed they do not currently have the capacity to undertake additional post-mortems from Northern Ireland. Their immediate priority is to build the necessary capacity that could support an all-island approach in the future through the training and retention of staff with appropriate specialist expertise. It is important to acknowledge that this is in the context of a global shortage of paediatric pathologists and progress will depend on factors beyond our direct control. My Department will continue to engage constructively with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland and in England to explore every possible option for improving these services for families at this most difficult time. However, it is not possible to be definitive on timescales at this stage as progress will depend on external workforce and system-wide factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been carried out of the implications of the decision to cancel special school summer schemes in relation to to meeting disability equality obligations, as children attending special schools are being treated less favourably than their non-disabled peers, who continue to have access to a wide range of summer opportunities.
answer_text: The decision to cancel summer schemes made by the Education Authority was based on the health and safety of children and young people.  The department is not aware of any such assessment being undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale behind withdrawing the requirement to advertise public notices in newspapers; and (ii) any assessment made of the impact this decision will have on local newspapers.
answer_text: When processing road-closure orders for motorsport events, my Department must act in accordance with the powers set out in The Road Races (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 which requires promoters to provide public notice of road closures, but it does not specify that this must be done through newspaper advertisements.Until this year, the Department required promoters to place newspaper adverts for two weeks before an event.  Following representations from motorsport bodies and promoters, the Department sought legal advice which confirmed that newspaper advertising is optional, provided promoters can demonstrate to the Department that their overall approach to public notice is sufficient (for example through local engagement, advance signage, and use of social media).Promoters may still choose to advertise in local newspapers, and my understanding is that many intend to do so.  My Department currently processes approximately 18 road race closures each year and as the change applies only to road-race closures, which take place right across the North, it is not expected to have a significant impact on individual local newspapers.Officials will review the position after the 2026 motorsport season and consider any implications, including any effect on advertising.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the additional funding required to implement the provisions contained in the Areas with Natural Constraints (Payments) Bill (NIA Bill 24/22-27).
answer_text: The Private Member’s Areas with Natural Constraints (Payments) Bill calls for funding based at the minimum of the previous ANC Scheme in 2018. Based on 2018 rates and the 2025 Retail Price Index, this represents an additional budgetary pressure of approximately £11.4m for the first year of a new ANC Scheme. A clause within the PMB requires that payment rates are increased annually by the rate of inflation, which would add to that pressure each year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any advice for motorists who pay for parking in private car parks where there are faulty machines that do not provide tickets, who subsequently receive parking fines in the post followed by escalated debt collection notices.
answer_text: Parking in private car parks is a civil matter and not within the remit of my Department.  However you may wish to note a factsheet produced by the Consumer Council on the matter - Factsheet - January 2019 - Private Parking Charges Factsheet.pdf.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls, to detail any assessment made of the comments in the Irish News on 26 March 2026 from a mother whose daughter was murdered in 2022.
answer_text: We extend our sincere sympathies to all families who have lost loved ones in such tragic circumstances. Ending violence against women and girls is one of the most pressing challenges facing our society today and this is why ending violence against women and girls is an Executive priority. The focus of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework is on prevention and tackling the root causes of violence against women and girls. Prevention can help create a future that is not only safer for women and girls, but is more respectful, just and equal for everyone in our society. The scale of the problem here demands an ambitious whole of government and whole of society approach and we are working with partners across all sectors in the delivery of the Strategic Framework. Violence against women and girls is unacceptable, and we will continue to do everything we can to stop it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment made of (i) the landmark ruling in California, holding Facebook and Google accountable for social media addiction; and (ii) the implications that case will have in the North.
answer_text: Telecommunications, including the regulation of social media, is a reserved matter for which the UK Government has responsibility.  Ofcom, as the UK’s independent regulator for online safety, is responsible for ensuring full implementation of the duties set out in the Online Safety Act 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for cancelling all summer scheme programmes in special schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), which has operational responsibility for Special School Summer Schemes, made an assessment that on-site nurses would be required in order for summer schemes to operate safely, given the increasingly complex healthcare needs of children in special schools.  Following confirmation from Department of Health officials on 24 March that there was insufficient nursing resource to provide on-site nursing for the duration of the schemes, the EA concluded that it would not be possible to run summer schemes safely in special schools in 2026, and advised special schools accordingly.Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority subsequently liaised with the Department of Health to address concerns regarding on-site nursing provision.   After constructive engagement, the Minister of Health confirmed on 27 March that nursing support will be provided at every summer scheme location. This ensures that essential clinical oversight will be in place, allowing the summer schemes to be run safely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support available to first-time buyers in Newry and South Armagh to ensure they are not disadvantaged against non-first time buyers.
answer_text: My Department provides significant investment to assist working people and families on lower incomes, particularly first-time buyers, into affordable home ownership through funding for shared ownership.Last year I announced a £153 million Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding programme for CoOwnership, which will support 4,000 households across Northern Ireland to buy their own home over the next four years.  In 25/26 there have been 30 Co-Own completions in the Newry and Armagh constituency. Over 90% of CoOwnership customers are firsttime buyers, and the scheme continues to play a vital role in enabling people who could not otherwise buy a home to access homeownership.Significantly, Co-Ownership requires smaller, and in some cases no, deposit. This is crucial as saving for a deposit can often be the most significant barrier to first-time buyers accessing the property market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any schemes available to support households investing in renewable energy, such as solar, heat pumps, or electric car charging ports, for domestic purposes.
answer_text: My Officials are currently developing the Warm Healthy Homes Fund: an energy efficiency scheme to improve the warmth, health, and energy affordability for low-income households. The Fund will be aimed at owner occupiers and those in the private rented sector who are experiencing or at risk of fuel poverty. The Fund will adopt a whole house approach, offering fully funded energy efficiency measures. For owner occupiers, this will include low carbon heating solutions and other renewable technologies such as Solar PV and battery storage.The Fund will be further shaped by public consultation in the next few weeks and is anticipated to launch in April 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department is taking to increase the number of pre-school places at Loughgall Playgroup.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to AQW 43137/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 10 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have made any assessment of the policy of temporarily reducing tax applying to (i) petrol; (ii) auto diesel; and (iii) Marked Gas Oil, similar to that applied in the south, to help those affected by increasing oil prices.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has made any assessment of the policy of temporarily reducing tax applying to (i) petrol; (ii) auto diesel; and (iii) Marked Gas Oil, similar to that applied in the south, to help those affected by increasing oil prices.
answer_text: Fuel duty remains an Excepted Matter, with the power to amend rates resting solely with the British Government, my department has no powers in this regard. While the Chancellor announced that the British Government is preparing an energy bill to support those most affected by rising oil prices, the nature, scope and timing of this remains unclear. I have written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on this issue, reiterating the call from the Economy Minister that consideration be given to reducing fuel duty rates and asking that the British Government puts in place targeted measures to relieve the pressure on households and businesses. It is vital that the measures brought forward by the British Government urgently addresses the needs of workers, families and businesses across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether a temporary reduction in tax, applying to (i) petrol; (ii) auto diesel; and (iii) marked gas oil, was discussed in her meeting on 14 March 2026 with the UK Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State in the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero.
answer_text: Many of our households and businesses are already under pressure from the cost-of-living crisis, and this new energy shock is compounding those pressures. I am particularly concerned about the impact on low income and vulnerable households.		I am meeting regularly with Ministers from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, including Energy Consumers Minister, Martin McCluskey about the urgent need for support. 		I pressed for consideration of all potential support to households and businesses and for that support to be made available as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions he has taken to ensure the Barnett consequential funding from the UK Government's announcement of funding for playparks is secured for playparks in Newry & Armagh, and across the North.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work her Department has undertaken to progress the north having its own Internet Protocol address.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the reason why there is no specific provision for social housing and business owners in the regulation making powers for the Flood Protection Grant Scheme; (ii) how support for social housing and business owners is provided, if not via a grant scheme; and (iii) any plans her Department has to ensure that businesses and social housing occupants are compensated for any damage caused by flooding.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has made of the NI Affairs Committee report on the Economic growth in Northern Ireland: new and emerging sectors, which concludes that the Committee has received no evidence to date, that the Executive has a thought through or deliverable plan to address and reform the deficits in planning policy and processes.
answer_text: This Committee did not engage with my Department in relation to planning matters. If it had I would have welcomed the opportunity to outline the significant work already underway to address challenges in the planning system.  Through the planning improvement agenda, my Department is working collaboratively with all 11 councils to progress a comprehensive programme of improvements aimed at enhancing the efficiency, consistency, and transparency of the regional planning system in the North. It is worth noting there was a significant improvement in the average processing time for major applications in 2024/25, which saw the lowest processing times since the transfer of planning powers in 2015. This is in addition to improving statutory consultee response times with the latest figures for Q2 2025/26 showing that 79% were responded to on time.Good progress has been made to date, with recent achievements including the introduction of a trainee planner scheme to strengthen the workforce, new legislation for statutory validation checklists and inflationary uplifts to planning fees.  Work continues across all aspects of the system including legislation, policy, practice and procedure, as well as resourcing and workforce capacity, to deliver improvements which make a tangible difference to all citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any actions his Department is taking to address knee replacement waiting times.
answer_text: The revised Elective Care Framework (ECF), which was published on 24 May 2025, sets out a five-year road map to reduce waiting times, including how earmarked funding would be invested across a range of initiatives to support waiting list reduction and strengthen core capacity. The initiatives outlined within the ECF include £50m of funding allocated to reduce the waiting list backlog by increasing in-house and independent sector capacity to target some of the longest waiting patients, with a focus on a range of specialties, including primary hip/knee procedures. Additionally, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs, the new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme has been established. The Scheme commenced on 2 June 2025 and offers patients, who ordinarily reside in Northern Ireland, and have been on a Health and Social Care (HSC) hospital treatment waiting list for one year or more, the option to obtain and pay for treatment privately outside Northern Ireland and have the cost reimbursed. This initiative has also benefitted many patients waiting on joint replacements.Despite financial pressures, significant progress has been made in reducing the number of patients projected to be waiting over four years for inpatient or day case treatment by March 2026, with a 62% reduction from March 2025 to February 2026. The number of patients within that total waiting for a joint replacement has reduced from 1,408 to 135 over this same time period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when more (i) information; and (ii) guidance, will be made available to those wishing to apply to the Residents' Parking Zone scheme, due to open in April 2026.
answer_text: I was pleased to announce the launch of a new application process for residents’ parking schemes, which will go-live in April 2026. The new demand-led process will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward.Further information on Residents’ Parking zones, including guidance on how to apply, can be found on NI Direct at Residents' parking zones | nidirect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41757/22-27, to detail (i) the number of meetings held under the North South Ministerial Council Transport sectoral workstream on road safety since its establishment; (ii) any joint actions or policy initiatives agreed at those meetings; (iii) whether any joint targets for casualty reduction have been set on a cross-border basis; and (iv) whether she has discussed with her counterpart in the Department of Transport the Road Safety Commissioner model or equivalent statutory accountability mechanisms.
answer_text: Following my answer to AQW 41757/22-27, I can advise that:(i) Since the establishment of the NSMC Transport sectoral workstream on road safety in February 2025, there has been one sectoral meeting on 23rd October 2025, with another upcoming meeting in May 2026.(ii) At the meeting on 23rd October 2025, I welcomed and agreed with Ministers O’Brien TD and Minister Nesbitt MLA several road safety initiatives, details of which can be found in the Joint Communiqué of the meeting at: https://www.northsouthministerialcouncil.org/publications/transport-joint-communique-23-october-2025”. (iii) Joint targets have not been set on a cross-border bases. Each jurisdiction has a live Road Safety Strategy with individual targets.  These Strategies are however based on the same ‘Safe Systems’ Approach and have a target in common and that is to reduce the number of road deaths by 50% by 2030.  (iv) a Road Safety Commissioner model or equivalent statutory accountability mechanism is not an action in either jurisdiction’s current Road Safety Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools that will move from being within their approved enrolment number to exceeding it, as a result of Circular 2026/05; and (ii) any steps he will take to ensure that schools which expanded their specialist provision at the Education Authority's request are not penalised, due to insufficient accommodation, by the resulting change to their enrolment status.
answer_text: The number of pupils enrolled at schools in September 2026 is currently unknown, however, based on data from the 2025/2026 school year, it is estimated that circa 81 primary schools and 55 post primary schools will move from being within their approved enrolment number to exceeding it, as a result of Circular 2026/05From September 2026, children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) in a mainstream school will only be treated as supernumerary for the year of admission. As the policy will not increase actual enrolments at any school, there will not be any additional pressure on accommodation as a result of the change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) reason why no formal planning application has been submitted for the acquisition of land adjacent to St Paul's High School, Bessbrook, despite pre-application discussions under reference PP-11880176 having been under way since at least 2023; (ii) steps including a timeline he plans to put in place to progress this matter to a formal application.
answer_text: The pre-application discussions identified that the full planning application will require the completion of various surveys and assessments, such as ecological reports, drainage assessments, biodiversity surveys. These are currently being progressed by Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD), who are delivering the project on behalf of the Department and the school’s trustees. The Department is working with CPD to progress the necessary preparatory work to enable the planning application to be submitted. Officials will continue to keep the school updated on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the reason why his letter of 24 February 2026 to all school principals, requiring mainstream schools considered suitable by the Education Authority to engage constructively with the establishment of specialist provision, has not been published on his Department's website; and (ii) whether he will now arrange for its publication.
answer_text: The Department does not routinely publish ministerial correspondence, including letters issued to school principals, on its website. The letter of 24 February 2026 therefore was not placed online in line with this standard practice. However, I am happy to provide the Member with a copy of the letter, which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any communications she has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the extent to which inadequate water and wastewater infrastructure and treatment capacity is having on (i) capital investment; (ii)  job creation; and (ii) home building, in the constructing sector.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have made to ensure grieving families do not have to send their deceased children to Britain for post-mortem examinations.
answer_text: We acknowledge it is totally unsatisfactory that post-mortem examinations on children currently need to be conducted in England and recognise the understandable distress this inevitably causes bereaved parents.  The Health Minister is acutely aware of this situation, and as he reported to the Assembly in response to a Motion on the issue on 13 January 2026 and in answer to your Assembly Question AQW 41119/22-27, he continues to press for a resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been made of Build Home NI's statement, in response to her Ministerial Written Statement on 19 March 2026, that today’s announcement is further confirmation that neither developer contributions nor the NI Executive’s three-pronged approach will deliver the investment needed to fix Northern Ireland’s failing sewage network.
answer_text: Developer contributions form a key part of my three-pronged approach alongside securing adequate funding from the Executive for wastewater infrastructure and progressing the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill.  Since October 24, £30m of additional funding has been secured to enable the unlocking of capacity for 5,300 properties.  The voluntary developer contributions scheme will enable developers, if they so choose, to support improvements needed to unlock capacity in areas where development is currently constrained.   Whilst this will not close the funding gap between the amount that NI Water receives from the Executive and the amount that is needed to address wastewater constraints, it will however contribute to the issue. I welcome the contribution made by Build Homes NI and I will continue to work with them and in partnership with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment in NI Water to address wastewater constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any communications she has had with the Minister for Economy in relation to the extent to which inadequate water and wastewater infrastructure and treatment capacity is having on (i) capital investment; (ii) job creation; and (iii) business growth.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to reinstating Areas of Natural Constraint payments, to assist in supporting farmers operating in regions with natural disadvantages.
answer_text: My Department has considered the previous ANC scheme and the value for money it offered. A Ministerial Direction was required to provide this scheme as it did not meet the requirements of Managing Public Money. In light of this evidence, my Department has focused on other ways to support farm businesses in ANCs within its Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP). This includes the Farm Sustainability Payment (FSP), the Beef Sustainability Package, and in particular the Suckler Cow Scheme, and the Farming with Nature Package. These schemes support farm businesses operating in ANCs and deliver both productivity and environmental improvements, delivering better outcomes for society in Northern Ireland from public expenditure. The Areas with Natural Constraints (Payment) Bill passed its second stage reading on Monday 23 March.  Should this Private Members Bill receive royal assent it will create a legal requirement on my Department to establish a new ANC Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of the merits of reviewing arrangements for Skills for Life and Work participants, with a view to widening eligibility for extra funded support to all disabled learners, based on individual need; (ii) whether her Department will be conducting a full Equality Impact Assessment of the current and proposed arrangements, in line with statutory duties; and (iii) how her Department is ensuring that no learner is excluded from accessing support funding for the programme, on the basis of the nature of their disabi
answer_text: Thank you for your correspondence and for your continued interest in the Skills for Life and Work (SfLW) programme. SfLW provides an important opportunity for young people leaving school with low or no qualifications to gain recognised qualifications, build confidence, and progress to further education, training or employment. The programme continues to support a significant number of participants with disabilities each year.In response to your specific questions:(i) Assessment of widening eligibility for extra funded supportAs part of the SfLW programme, training providers have always received an additional weekly premium for every participant with a registered disability, with a number of longstanding flexibilities available to ensure that provision can be tailored to individual need. These include:access to an additional year of training for disabled participants;flexible attendance hours, particularly at programme entry; andsupport for thirdparty travel costs where public transport is unavailable or unsuitable.Under the previous programme, SfLW21, participants with higher levels of need could also access additional support through separately contracted Disability Support Services (DSS).Following extensive policy development and a full public consultation, a new fully integrated disability support model has been introduced in SfLW25 (launched September 2025). Under this model:providers receive an enhanced disability premium for all participants with a registered disability;support is delivered directly by the training provider, ensuring a more seamless, responsive system; andseparate sensory support and support for very complex or exceptional needs remains available and may be approved on a casebycase basis.My Department continues to monitor delivery closely and engage with providers to ensure that support arrangements remain effective and equitable.(ii) Equality Impact AssessmentMy Department has met, and will continue to meet, all statutory duties in relation to Equality Screening and Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) when developing and implementing programme policy.In line with Section 75 requirements, equality considerations formed an integral part of the development of SfLW25, reinforced through the public consultation, sector engagement, and the integration of support arrangements. Officials will continue to keep equality considerations under review as the programme embeds, and will bring forward further assessment activity where required.The Department also maintains ongoing engagement with training providers to ensure that the needs of disabled participants are appropriately met throughout programme delivery.(iii) Ensuring no learner is excluded from accessing supportSfLW delivery is governed by robust Operational Requirements, monitored through Contract Managers and Compliance Officers, which ensure equal access to all programme flexibilities. These include the disability premium, the additional training year, flexible hours of atten

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on road resurfacing schemes, including pothole repairs, in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: I am aware of the frustrations and inconvenience that poor road conditions have caused, however I would like to assure the Member that my Department continues to inspect and repair the highest priority defects across the road network in all council areas as quickly as resources permit.In addition to the funding received and allocated in the December Monitoring round, I was able to secure further additional funding recently, of which £5 million was directed to the Southern Division. However, given the extent of the damage to the road network caused by the prolonged period of wet weather and the unprecedented number of defects recorded as a result,  you will appreciate that it will not be possible for all defects to be actioned immediately and recovery will take time.Within the constituency area and with the additional funding, I was pleased to see resurfacing schemes completed on the A25 Rathfriland Road, C217 Tullywhinney Road, A27 New Street, A2 Sugar Island and the U6294 Erskine Street.Officials have also undertaken 6.2km of road recovery in the Tassagh area, including the C196 Farnaloy Road; Dawson Street and Abbey Street, and Loughgall Road.Details of current resurfacing schemes can be found in my Department’s Annual Reports to the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough and Newry Mourne and Down District Councils. The 2026/27 resurfacing programme will be finalised once budgets are confirmed and presented to Council in due course. Officials are doing all they can to maximise capacity to undertake inspections and repairs in line with the Department’s Limited Service policy dealing with the highest priority defects on the more heavily trafficked roads on the network. Where possible larger scale machine laid repairs are being undertaken rather than localised immediate defect removal. On 2nd December 2025, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy for public consultation and the feedback from this is currently being analysed by my officials. In support of the new approach, I have also commissioned a full digital road survey of all roads in the North.  The digital road survey and the analysis of the data it produces, is an investment in the future of our road network.  It will enable a better understanding of the condition of the network and will therefore inform strategic decision making going forward targeting road maintenance activities to where they are needed most.  This will also continue to be supported by the reporting of potholes and surface defects via the online portal and maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make safe defects in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public in so far as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of applications to the Victims' Payment Scheme; (ii) the number of successful applications; (iii) the average timeline for processing applications; and (iv) the number of applications successful on appeal, in each year since the introduction of the scheme.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Scheme is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is an Arm’s Length Body of The Executive Office (TEO).  The VPB have provided the information below for each year since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021:The number of applications to the Scheme:2021 - 1,3972022 - 2,1712023 - 2,6592024 - 4,2942025 - 1,8872026 (To date) – 486 Total as of 24 March 2026 -12,894 (386 applications have been withdrawn).The number of successful applications:2021 - 12022 - 2212023 - 5662024 - 9132025 - 1,0112026 (To date) - 252Total as of 24 March 2026 – 2,964 (This total does not include Split Pathway Determinations). The Scheme is multifaceted and involves a range of detailed processes. Each application is assessed individually and evaluated on its specific circumstances. As a result, we are unable to provide a standard or average timeframe for progression of applications through the various stages of the Scheme.The number of applications successful on appeal:2022 - 5 Successful Appeals 2023 - 24 Successful Appeals2024 - 61 Successful Appeals2025 - 78 Successful Appeals2026 (To date) - 23 Successful AppealsTotal - 191 Successful Appeals

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any recent engagement they have had on the progress to redevelop Casement Park.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the number of people who qualify for Continuing Healthcare, particularly in relation to older people.
answer_text: Continuing Healthcare (CHC) was previously available in Northern Ireland under the 2010 policy; however, following the Court of Appeal judgment in Re McMinnis (2024), key elements of the former CHC policy were found to be outside my Department’s statutory powers. My Department has issued updated guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts confirming that any CHC funding provided under the 2010 policy must cease and that any applications for CHC funding under the 2010 policy that were previously held in abeyance are to be dismissed. Although the 2021 CHC policy remains in place, it adopts an entirely different eligibility criterion concerning CHC which seeks to place the emphasis on healthcare provided in hospitals. My Department has not undertaken any assessment in relation to the number of people who qualify for CHC.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any assessment and representations made regarding guidance issued by the Victims' Payment Board which states that some paramilitary-style attacks do not fall within the definition of a Troubles-related incident, and so do not qualify for the Victims' Payment Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41750/22-27, to detail whether his Department has given any consideration to providing support directly to parents.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) already provides direct benefit to parents by reducing childcare costs at the point of payment, with a subsidy of up to 15% applied directly to invoices and clearly shown by providers.During the development of the NICSS, the Department considered different delivery models, including making payments directly to parents.  However, to do so may cause additional tax implications for parents and significantly increase administrative complexity and delivery risk without providing additional financial benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support her Department provides for community-based organisations, such as the Newcastle Regional Community Resilience Group, who are spearheading efforts to protect homes, schools, and businesses from flood events.
answer_text: The RCRG, which is co-chaired by my Department and local government, was formed in 2013 to support communities at risk of flooding and to help them prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies. At present the RCRG is working with 50 communities, including Newcastle, to help improve their preparedness and community resilience during periods of severe weather. Key to the success of the RCRG is the strong working relationship with the many local community group leads, many of whom often carry out their role on a voluntary basis. They are supported through information sharing such as weather warning updates by SMS and e-mail. Further support is provided by the provision of additional resources e.g. fully stocked sandbags stores, which can be accessed by the local community. This work represents an extra layer of support for those impacted by severe weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any public health measures his Department is taking to prevent meningitis, given a suspected meningitis case here.
answer_text: The PHA is attending UKHSA Incident Management Team meetings to monitor the outbreak in Kent and consider the public health implications for Northern Ireland.  The risk to the NI public is currently low and we are continuing to closely monitor the situation. The Northern Ireland case is no longer suspected to be meningococcal meningitis. The PHA has issued further information to parents, guardians and staff.  The public have been urged to remain vigilant for the signs and symptoms of meningitis and to act fast if they suspect they may be symptomatic.  Further information is available at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Flood Strategy Steering Group, which was formed in response to flooding incidents in Newcastle and Downpatrick, detailing (i) when the group has met; (ii) any actions or recommendations the group has agreed; and (iii) any progress on those actions or recommendations.
answer_text: The Flood Strategy Steering Group (FSSG) is a long-standing multi-agency group that brings together government departments, local councils and the emergency services to provide strategic oversight of flood risk management. It was not established in response to the flooding incidents in Newcastle and Downpatrick.In September 2024, the then Minister for Infrastructure, John O’Dowd MLA approved the establishment of a dedicated Flooding Review Recommendations Group (FRRG) to coordinate implementation of the 22 recommendations arising from the ‘Review of the South East Flooding Autumn 2023’ report that was published the previous July.The group initially met on a quarterly basis but since February 2026 has moved to monthly meetings. Work is continuing and the FRRG will formally report to me at the end of this calendar year on progress made against the 22 recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, to detail the rationale as to why the standardisation of pre-schools for students with special educational needs provision is taking place later than mainstream provision.
answer_text: The Pre-School Standardisation Programme relates to the standardisation of session lengths for provision within the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP).  Special education provision is not part of the PSEP and therefore sits outside the scope of the standardisation programme. In recognition of the need for an equitable approach for all children, the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy includes an action in 2027 to “conduct a feasibility study of the potential to provide special school pre-school provision on a full-time (22.5 hours per week) basis”.  This study will scope a move to full-time provision in special schools and the impact on a range of factors including the pressures for pre-school places in special schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of properties in Newcastle that have been impacted by flood incidents in the last ten years, broken down by (i) households; (ii) businesses; and (iii) public buildings.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance, and I can confirm that 112 households in Newcastle have received £1,000 due to flooding incidents in the last 10 years. The scheme does not record the numbers of households who may have been affected by flooding but were ineligible for the payment. The SEFA is not available for non-domestic properties.  In terms of businesses, the Department for Economy (DfE) has advised that it does not hold any records of the number of businesses affected by flooding in the last 10 years. DfE further advised that it put grant schemes in place following the 2023 floods to support a range of non-domestic property owners, and these schemes were delivered by, in this instance, Newry, Mourne and Down District Council. Those schemes have now closed.The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) is the Lead Government Department for the emergency response to extreme weather events. It has advised that there is a multi-agency approach in place involving a number of Departments, Councils and other agencies such as the Fire and Rescue Service. DfI has advised that the causes of flooding are complex and varied and the reporting of flooding does not automatically corollate to the number of properties which have actually flooded, nor the type of properties involved. No single repository holds all of the information that you require and the information you request is not held by DfI. However, DfI advised that the actions it and the flood risk management infrastructure provide prevent or reduce the impacts of flooding to many homes and businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has made any assessment, following constituent reports, of how the condition of the roads is negatively impacting farming business operations.
answer_text: I fully recognise the very real impact that the current condition of parts of the road network can have on individuals and businesses including farming, and acknowledge the inconvenience caused by road closures where resurfacing and remedial work is necessary, and the impact it can have, particularly regarding increased travel times and costs.There has been historic underfunding in many parts of the road network. In addition, recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have taken a severe toll on road surfaces across the region.I have recently allocated an additional £10.3 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network. This will be used to deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need across the north. This work will commence without delay. This funding is in addition to the £30million allocated in December for resurfacing schemes.I have also asked my officials to explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible. And to put this on a more sustainable footing, a recruitment campaign for industrial road workers has just been launched.I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worst affected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has given any consideration to the Finnish model for ending homelessness.
answer_text: The Housing First model adopted in Finland has been operating on a small scale in Northern Ireland since 2013.The Housing Executive published its Housing First Feasibility Study: Expansion of Services in Northern Ireland in December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how he is working with his Executive colleagues to promote locally-grown food within retail outlets.
answer_text: I am passionate about the quality of our locally grown food and am keen to do everything I can to promote its consumption. I recognise the potential of locally grown food to play a key role in delivering the NI Food Strategy Framework goals of better economic, health and environmental outcomes. The Framework’s 2-year Food Action Plan sets out 39 actions, many of which have a strong focus on collaboration across departments to deliver better outcomes for Northern Ireland. One of the actions is to work across government and with other key stakeholders to align messaging and develop plans to promote Northern Ireland’s food and drink offering across all settings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has made any assessment of pesticides used in food production on public heath.
answer_text: The Health and Safety Executive’s (HSE) annual Food Monitoring Programmes check food and drink placed on the market in Great Britain and Northern Ireland for traces of pesticide residues, on behalf of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra), DAERA, the Scottish Government, and the Welsh Government.One of the purposes of the programme is to determine whether residues found in food and drink are above the maximum residue levels set by law. HSE carries out screening and detailed risk assessments for any residues found, including evaluating whether intake could exceed safe levels.All results are published at https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/pesticide-residues-in-food-results-of-monitoring-programme#uk-competent-authorities-for-pesticide-residues-in-food-annual-reports
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s outreach to the National Institute for Health and Care Research regarding participation in newborn screening trials for Spinal Muscular Atrophy.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any financial support he is offering to Home to School transport providers to mitigate the sharp rise in fuel prices.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, including the management of contracts it holds with private transport operators. It has advised me that it has no plans at present to uplift the contracted rates agreed with private operators, however it will continue to monitor developments in fuel costs to assess any potential implications for current home to school transport arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the litter will be cleared following the cutting back of overgrowth on the on-slips and off-slips at the major junctions and roundabouts into Newry City off the A1.
answer_text: Under the terms of the Litter (NI) Order 1994, My Department’s responsibilities in relation to the removal of litter are limited to ‘designated roads’, which incorporates the motorway network and other roads such as the A12 Westlink.  Responsibility for litter removal on all other roads, including the A1, regardless of classification or speed limit, fall under the remit of the appropriate Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to implementing lighting in the vicinity of the pedestrian crossings on Old Warrenpoint Road, Newry.
answer_text: Officials have advised that the existing street lighting along the full length of the Old Warrenpoint Road is compliant with the current policy and is considered to give good illumination in the areas surrounding the uncontrolled pedestrian crossings. The pedestrian islands are already located in close proximity to an existing street light and benefit from appropriate ambient lighting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why potholes on Tate Road, Belleeks, required further repair within weeks of the initial repair; and (ii) when the next repair will take place.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory.  The delivery of a permanent repair on a single visit is the preferred option, however, this is not always possible, particularly during periods of severe weather or when there are high volumes of defects to be addressed. In such circumstances, such as that at Tate Road, it may be necessary to carry out a temporary repair to remove the defect and make the road safe until arrangements can be made for a permanent repair. I have been advised that a permanent repair at this location has since been completed.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the safety measures and practices being put in place to protect frontline healthcare staff from physical harm and harassment.
To ask the Minister of Education when his Department plans to undertake a formal evaluation of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the merits of a 12 month crisis intervention response to assist in reducing cancer diagnosis treatment and waiting times, given the risk of long waits resulting in patient's cancer progressing and treatment options being fewer.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of improvements made in relation to waiting lists in other areas of healthcare that could apply to reducing cancer waiting lists.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why bowel cancer screening in the North starts at age 60, compared to other parts of the UK that start screening at age 50.
answer_text: The current UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) recommendation for bowel cancer screening is that it should be offered every 2 years to men and women between the ages of 50 and 74 using the FIT test at a sensitivity threshold of 20µg Hb/g.  In Northern Ireland bowel screening is currently offered to people aged between 60 and 74 years old, every 2 years, using a faecal-immunochemical test (FIT) with a sensitivity threshold of 120µg Hb/g. Work to expand the bowel screening programme down to 50 years of age, in line with a UK NSC recommendation and the rest of the UK, is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any mechanisms through which Health and Social Care Trusts are held to account for meeting statutory targets relating to cancer waiting lists and treatment times, subsequent to a patient's diagnosis or red-flag referral.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the correlation between lung cancer rates and deprivation.
answer_text: My Department monitors deprivation inequalities in health outcomes and publishes a Health Inequalities report annually.The latest Northern Ireland Health & Social Care Inequalities Monitoring System report (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/health-inequalities-statistics) showed that the age standardised incidence rate for lung cancer in the 20% most deprived areas (137 incidences per 100,000 population) was around two and a half times the rate in the 20% least deprived areas (55 incidences per 100,000 population) in 2015-21. The report also showed that death rates for lung cancer in the most deprived areas were also around two and a half times the rate in the least deprived areas and smoking attributable death rates in the most deprived areas were double the rate in the most deprived areas.Delivering reform in cancer care is a key priority for the Department. The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland and its Funding Plan 2022-2032 were launched on 22 March 2022. The Strategy outlines sixty high-level recommendations over four key themes that will enable significant strategic change. The Strategy’s vision is to ensure that everyone in Northern Ireland, wherever they live, has equitable and timely access to the most effective, evidence-based referral, diagnosis, treatment, support and person-centred cancer care.The Cancer Charities Grant Scheme was announced on 27 November 2025. This is a £2m Grant Scheme in year 1 (2025/26), with £1m provided by DoH and £1m match funding provided by Macmillan Cancer Support. The Scheme is focused on tackling health inequalities experienced by those impacted by cancer, by ensuring that cancer support services are more easily accessible across our local communities.In addition, a targeted lung screening programme for those at higher risk of disease, is at the early stages of development. Lung cancer screening will be the first targeted screening programme introduced based on screening people at higher risk of the disease.In respect of health inequalities more widely, this is one of my areas of focus and under Making Life Better the Department leads on strategies to help improve population health and wellbeing, including strategies and policies on related risk factors, such as tobacco control and substance use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any reimbursement his Department offers patients who turn to private treatment due to NHS waiting times.
answer_text: Waiting times for elective care continue to be longer than they should and although significant work is ongoing to address this, some individuals may choose to seek private care. However, once a patient procures treatment privately, they are stepping outside of the Health and Social Care system. The Department cannot reimburse the cost of private treatment obtained independently of the HSC system.		Reimbursement is only an option if the patient has sought prior approval through the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme (WLRS). Subject to prior approval, the Scheme gives patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, and are on a Health and Social Care (HSC) treatment waiting list, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector in the Republic of Ireland or wider European Union and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to HSC in Northern Ireland.In line with my strategic direction to target long waiters, patients will only be able to access the WLRS once they have waited on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for one year.Full details and guidance on how to access the Scheme and the process and eligibility are available from https://online.hscni.net/our-work/travelfortreatment/wl-reimbursement/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment she has made of the merits of raising the parental income threshold for the Educational Maintenance Allowance.
answer_text: The Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) is a jointly owned policy between the Department for the Economy (DfE) and the Department of Education (DE).  Any consideration of changes to the scheme, including the parental income thresholds, is therefore a matter for both Departments collectively.		Officials from both Departments continue to work closely together on the wider consideration of EMA policy and any future assessment of potential changes to thresholds will be taken forward within that shared programme of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on private home to school transport operator contract retention, after operators were asked to agree to modify their contracts and reduce invoiced rates.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised me that it has had constructive engagement with private hire operators regarding voluntary reductions to contracted rates, and it remains committed to maintaining positive working relationships across the sector. To date, the EA has not withdrawn any contracts from operators as part of this process.
