Of the 195 operators engaged, three operators have chosen to return a total of 12 contracts for a range of operational reasons. These contracts have since been retendered in line with EA procurement requirements. The EA will continue to engage with operators throughout this academic year and all contracts will be monitored and reviewed ahead of the 2026/27 academic year, with a focus on value for money and sustainability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the basis on which the Education Authority concluded that there is no viable accommodation solution at St Paul's High School, Bessbrook; (ii) whether that assessment took account of adjacent lands which are the subject of a live Department of Education planning application; and (iii) any steps he will take to ensure the school's specialist provision is properly accommodated.
answer_text: The assessment of the St Paul’s High School site was based solely on the school’s existing site boundary. The current site is now at full capacity following minor works projects over the past five years which delivered eight general classrooms and two specialist classrooms.No, the assessment did not take account of this land.  The plot of land being considered for additional car parking has not yet been acquired. The acquisition cannot proceed without planning approval, particularly as the land lies outside development limits and carries significant associated risks. Although pre-application discussions have taken place, no formal planning application has yet been submitted.  Should the additional land be secured, the EA will assess the feasibility of a minor works project to provide the required accommodation in parallel with the car parking proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has carried out an assessment of developing the Belfast Dublin economic corridor, as is being pitched at the global Marché International des Professionnels de l'Immobilier event in Cannes from 9 March 2026 to 13 March 2026.
answer_text: I am aware of the work by partner organisations in highlighting the economic potential of the Dublin–Belfast Economic Corridor. I recognise the importance of effective connectivity between Belfast and Dublin and my Department continues to engage with a range of partners through established programmes and structures, including regional planning, City Deals and wider cross border engagement, where these align with my Department’s statutory responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have had with the British and Irish Governments to coordinate (i) homeward flights; (ii) guidance; and (iii) resources, available for citizens affected by the conflict in the Middle East
answer_text: We are fully engaged with both the UK and Irish governments on this matter. Since 28 February, our officials have been engaging with the Foreign Office in London and the Department of Foreign Affairs in Dublin, ensuring we are kept up to date with their plans to support British and Irish citizens impacted.We have met directly with the Prime Minister and the Irish Minister for Foreign Affairs where we discussed the impact of the conflict in the Middle East on our diaspora who are living and travelling in the region and reinforced that we stand ready to assist in helping people from here who are currently impacted in the region in any way we can. We have raised with both governments the issue of people from here being able to register with either British or Irish Embassies regardless of whether they are travelling using an Irish or a British Passport, and we are pleased that both have accommodated this in their processes.This is an evolving situation, and we are using all communication channels available to us to encourage people affected to register their presence through the relevant governmental portals and to keep up to date with the official travel guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Ministerial office has received an options paper from Officials, regarding her Department's phased review of taxi policy and legislation.
answer_text: Officials will provide me with their recommendations arising from that review, shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the Lisnalee Play Area will be upgraded.
answer_text: I can only answer questions that are the responsibility of my department, which this is clearly not.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to address flooding on Kingsmill Road, Whitecross, towards Whitecross village and before the Kingsmill monument.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to manage average daily rainfall on the carriageway, not short-duration, high-intensity rainfall events that are becoming more frequent. During such events, systems can become temporarily overwhelmed and in many instances the road drainage system connects to third party systems, and its performance dictates overall capacity.Kingsmill Road, Whitecross is included in the Department’s programme of drainage investigations to better understand the issues at this location and to identify any appropriate measures to help alleviate flooding. In the meantime, routine gully cleansing will continue to be undertaken to ensure the drainage system is operating as effectively as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has conducted any assessment of increasing parking provision for patients at Daisy Hill Hospital, given that some patients have had to park illegally and received tickets in order to attend appointments.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to determine to enable them to take local issues into account.The Southern HSC Trust has considered the feasibility of constructing a multistorey car park at Daisy Hill Hospital. However, significant capital investment would be required to realise this project, in the context of the scale of the capital funding requirements across the HSC Estate and the limited amount of capital budget available.In the last 2 years, the Trust has increased car parking provision adjacent to Daisy Hill Hospital with the acquisition of the Monaghan Row site which provides an additional 75 parking spaces and the creation of a 60 space car park on Clanrye Avenue. Parking enforcement on hospital sites, including Daisy Hill Hospital, is focused on areas where staff, visitors, and patients have parked inappropriately, such as on yellow lines, clearly marked drop off areas, or in disabled bays designated for Blue Badge holders. The purpose of this enforcement is to maintain safe and unobstructed access routes for blue-light vehicles, ambulances, and essential contractors, supporting the Trust’s ability to deliver critical services without disruption.Those who believe they have received a Parking Charge Notice (PCN) unfairly can submit an appeal, as outlined on the PCN and enforcement signage across the Daisy Hill Hospital site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the white lines in Greenpark Manor, Armagh, will be repainted.
answer_text: The markings in Greenpark Manor are currently assessed as meeting the required standard and renewal is not considered necessary at present. Officials will continue to monitor the condition of the road markings as part of routine inspections.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how long Translink has been aware that the CCTV system at Newry Train Station has not been operational; and (ii) whether her Department plans to install new CCTV at the station.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The CCTV system at Newry Train Station has been, and remains, operational. As with all CCTV systems, faults can occur from time to time which may result in cameras being offline for limited periods while repairs are carried out.  Recently, a number of cameras have been replaced and associated headend equipment upgraded. All internal cameras at the station are currently operational.    A business case is being prepared to deliver a new CCTV system for the station car park. Progress on this will depend on the availability and prioritisation of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new build social homes delivered in 2025.
answer_text: There have been 1,275 social homes started and 1,177 social homes completed in the 2025 calendar year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's plan, in partnership with the Public Prosecution Service and the PSNI, to implement an enhanced Needs Assessment Service within the Victim Witness Care Unit, as a priority to improve the use of special measures and outcomes for victims and witnesses.
answer_text: I recognise the importance and benefits of having a Victim Needs Assessment Service (VNAS) that is managed by staff who are skilled in a trauma informed approach.The new Victims and Witnesses of Crime Strategy which I launched on 11 March reflects my Department’s commitment to working towards the establishment of a VNAS and some preparatory work has already commenced to this effect. In January 2026, multi-agency scoping work was commenced and further development work in conjunction with key stakeholders like the PSNI, PPS, Victim Support NI, etc will continue throughout 2026 and 2027; however, the operational establishment of a VNAS will be dependent on additional funding being made available. My Department has bid for funding under the Transformation Fund which, if met, would cover the cost of establishing a VNAS, with the intention of the service being operational from the second half of financial year 2027/28.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with Translink in relation to (i) providing additional late night train services; or (ii) delaying the last Enterprise train service, on Saturday 14 March 2026, for the Armagh Dublin GAA match.
answer_text: Translink has advised me that it anticipates exceptionally high demand for all crossborder rail and bus services on Saturday 14 March 2026 due to the number of major events scheduled in Dublin, including the Six Nations Rugby fixture (Ireland v Scotland) and the  League football matches (Dublin v Armagh and Meath v Tyrone).Translink has proactively added extra special trains to the crossborder rail schedule for 14 March 2026. However, due to the high level of interest, these additional services have now reached full capacity. To further supplement demand, Translink has arranged additional bus capacity alongside the regular fifteen crossborder rail services and scheduled bus services.In addition, special bus services have been arranged from Armagh Bus Station specifically for GAA supporters travelling to Croke Park.Passengers wishing to secure travel arrangements for this date are encouraged to book the available special bus services in advance through Translink’s Journey Planner or via www.translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of teaching self-defense in schools.
answer_text: My Department has not made any assessment regarding the teaching of self-defence in schools.The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 sets out the statutory minimum content as high-level Areas of Learning. Within these Areas of Learning it is for individual schools to determine how the Curriculum is delivered, including the specific topics taught and the resources used to support delivery. Individual schools are best placed to decide which subjects will best suit the needs of their own pupils while ensuring all minimum content requirements are fulfilled.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any assessment has made made of, as a part of a wider strategy to end gender-based violence, of offering women's only self-defense classes to help women, especially those suffering from trauma, build self-confidence and learn safety skills in a safe, women-led space.
answer_text: As part of the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework, our focus remains on prevention. This includes tackling the root causes of genderbased violence, challenging harmful behaviours and attitudes, and strengthening earlyintervention approaches that support women and girls’ longterm safety and wellbeing.A research review informed the development of the Framework, and we continue to monitor research needs across the broader policy context.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any assessment has been made of the merits of offering women's only self-defense classes to help women, especially those suffering from trauma, build self-confidence and learn safety skills in a safe, women-led space.
answer_text: As part of the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework, our focus remains on prevention. This includes tackling the root causes of genderbased violence, challenging harmful behaviours and attitudes, and strengthening earlyintervention approaches that support women and girls’ longterm safety and wellbeing.A research review informed the development of the Framework, and we continue to monitor research needs across the broader policy context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 40922/22-27, to detail any progress on the cross-jurisdictional paper on CAR-T mentioned in the minutes of the North South Ministerial Council meeting on 29 October 2025.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been carried out on the merits of returning Ardmore Police Station to its original use as a hotel.
answer_text: The management of the PSNI estate is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. It is therefore suggested that you should direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a timeline of repairs at Glack Road, Newry.
answer_text: Glack Road, Newry was last inspected in October 2025, however, I have asked my officials to undertake a full inspection of this road as resources permit, and actionable defects will be instructed for repair in line with my Department’s Limited Service Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the draft Disability Strategy 2025-2035 will align disability legislation with international human rights standards, including but not limited to the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities.
answer_text: Changes to disability legislation in Northern Ireland will be considered in the context of the implementation of the Disability Strategy which is currently out for public consultation and which includes a commitment to review and update the existing legislation to include how the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) can best be incorporated.The UNCRPD, which is at the core of the Strategy development, is universally recognised as the eminent disability legislation globally. Notwithstanding this, my officials will continue to investigate best practice in other relevant jurisdictions in order to ensure the legislative framework here meets the needs of our Deaf and disabled communities.Any new legislation brought forward as a result of this work will be subject to approval by the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department (i) has carried out any assessment of postgraduate loan caps that do not cover course fees; and (ii) will consider changes to index loan caps to course fees.
answer_text: Following a review and public consultation which examined whether the existing £6,500 Postgraduate Tuition Fee Loan remains fit for purpose, equitable, and aligned with course fee levels, I announced a 54% increase in the loan to a maximum of £10,000 per annum from academic year 2026/27 on 6th March.  This will enable more students to undertake postgraduate study.My department will continue to monitor local postgraduate fee levels and consider any potential need for future adjustments if considered appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will be implementing any of the recommendations in the ADHD Needs Assessment Report.
answer_text: The final ADHD Needs Assessment Report was published on 12 February 2026, noting that any decisions on future commissioning of ADHD services would be in the context of overall affordability.Officials have commenced an options appraisal process to identify what options could potentially be implemented in the short to medium term to help address the need for ADHD services.  The outcome of this work will inform the development of an implementation plan, which is already underway.  It is recognised that several of the recommendations can only be progressed in the context of a future commissioned service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any financial support available to people whose cars are damaged in parking areas owned by Translink.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:There are two scenarios regarding damage to thirdparty vehicles in Translinkowned parking areas:Damage caused by a Translink vehicleIf a thirdparty vehicle is damaged by a Translink vehicle and Translink is found liable, Translink will meet the full cost of the repair.Damage caused by another third partyIf a thirdparty vehicle is damaged by another thirdparty vehicle, the matter is treated as a dispute between those parties. As Translink is not at fault, it will not provide any financial contribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will take action to remove illegal Sinn Féin advertisements from roadsides and junctions, in towns and villages, in Newry and Armagh.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the changes in criteria resulting in farmers who qualified for the Farmer's Sustainability Transition Payment no longer qualifying for the Farm Sustainability Payment.
answer_text: To ensure the Farm Sustainability Payment is directed towards farm businesses actively engaged in agricultural activity, Regulation 3 of the Farm Sustainability (Transitional Provisions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 legislates that businesses that did not meet specific agricultural production levels during an historic reference period, set by DAERA as 2020 and 2021, will not be eligible.In accordance with DAERA’s obligations under section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, the potential equality impact of this policy has been assessed across all S75 groupings. The screening exercise concluded that the historic years exercise could have a minor impact on the ‘Age’ Section 75 category (largely driven by the average age of a farmer in NI being 59 years old). To mitigate this potential minor impact DAERA issued communications to advise farm businesses of the historic years policy and the options available to them. The equality screening completed for the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment has been published on the DAERA website and can be found at the following link. https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/equality-disability-duties-screening-farm-sustainability-transition-paymentMy Department also wrote to those identified farm businesses impacted by the implementation of the historic years exercise together with the options open to them. A Rural Needs Assessment has also been completed and no issues were identified. The completed Rural Needs impact assessment has been published on the DAERA website and can be found at the following link.https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/farm-sustainability-transition-payment-fstp-scheme-rural-needs-impact-assessment
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reasons why the A1 Junctions upgrade is being put on hold, awaiting the outcome of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme court ruling; and (ii) any plans she has to ensure this road improvement scheme will commence, including a timeline for completion.
answer_text: The A1 Junctions Phase 2 project is no different to other major infrastructure projects being progressed by my Department, all of which are being considered in light of the A5 Judgement.It is also important that the Department allows appropriate time for the completion of legal proceedings, including those currently under way in relation to the A5, so that the Court of Appeal’s ruling can be fully understood. I consider it prudent to reflect any lessons learned across other major road schemes, in order to avoid further legal challenge and exposure to financial risk.Every death on our roads is a profound loss to the community and unfortunately the A1 is a road that has been impacted exponentially by tragic circumstances.  I remain committed to the construction of this vitally important infrastructure safety upgrade and will take all actions available to me to secure its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when a 20mph speed limit will be applied to the Killeen School Road, outside of St. Michael's Primary School, Newry.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits which is open for consultation. Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support his Department will be providing to ensure that the Europe Triathlon Cup held in the City of Derry in 2026 is a successful regional event.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to raise awareness of the key symptoms of ovarian cancer.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA), in partnership with the Department of Health, Health and Social Care Trusts, and a range of stakeholders, delivers initiatives to raise awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms and to signpost available support services. During Ovarian Cancer Awareness Month in March each year this is particularly important and includes targeted communications and social media messaging to improve awareness and enhance the signs and symptoms of ovarian cancer.The PHA Be Cancer Aware campaign also promotes cancer awareness of signs and symptoms and encourages the public to seek advice from their GP. Further information is available  on the also directs people to the Be Cancer Aware website, which provides information on ovarian cancer: https://www.becancerawareni.info/signs-and-symptoms/ovarian-cancer.The Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group are currently reviewing the primary care guidance for suspected gynaecological cancers, including the red flag referral criteria for ovarian cancer that will support the earlier diagnosis and treatment of this cancerMy Department continues to work closely with community and voluntary organisations, including Target Ovarian Cancer, to raise awareness and support earlier diagnosis and treatment of this and other cancers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will include a cap on private childcare fees for providers who accept government subsidies, to ensure that any funding for working families using the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme is not negated by price increases in childcare fees.
answer_text: Childcare in Northern Ireland is delivered predominantly by independent private businesses; my Department has no authority to cap or regulate the fees they charge, even where providers participate in governmentsubsidised schemes. As part of the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, my Department undertook significant scoping and analysis of how fee capping models operate in practice, including the funding, regulatory and delivery mechanisms typically required to support them. This scoping informed the position reflected in the draft Strategy, recognising that there is significant fiscal, regulatory and operational implications associated with such an approach. For these reasons, the capping of fees is not included within the draft Strategy but will be kept under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to provide statutory guidance on supporting children with medical conditions and allergies in schools.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 41034/22-27 published in the Weekly Answer Booklet for week ending 6th March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current progress of and timeline for the Women’s Health Action Plan; (ii) whether education and training on menstrual health and endometriosis for healthcare practitioners will be included as a key priority within the plan; and (iii) how the plan will ensure improved awareness and understanding across different healthcare settings, including non-specialist services.
answer_text: I plan to publish the Women's Health Action Plan following completion of the public listening exercise. My Department has helped to fund this important project by Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast who have now completed a women’s health survey and focus groups with harder to reach women.  More recently there was a further round of engagement in partnership with stakeholders and representative organisations to share the findings and to identify the priorities and recommendations which will inform the final report to my Department. When published, the Plan will describe the wide range of policy and service developments supporting women’s health, outline what has been achieved to date, and set out what is achievable within current resources in the years ahead. It will also consider how to improve awareness and understanding among healthcare professionals on a range of women’s health issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is adopting to ensure patients can access GP services promptly and seamlessly.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.  At this point in time it is not mandatory for GP practices to make online appointments available to their patients.  While all practices have the technology available to enable this, it remains their choice in how they manage and deliver their services within their individual practice.  I have been clear that improving access to GP services for patients is a key priority for me. My Department will be commencing negotiations with the NIGPC on the GMS contract for 2026/27. Developing robust measures to improve access to GP services for implementation in 2026/27 will be a core requirement in seeking agreement on the Contract. In addition, to support GPs in improving service delivery, my Department has issued a best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, which includes recommendations on how practices could maximise their telephony and technology to help meet patient demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given their role in the delivery of social change, to detail any plans they have to address the impacts of increased energy prices arising from the conflict in Iran, which may negatively affect the cost of living.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the impact of the rise in heating oil prices on households and businesses here, particularly on lower income and vulnerable households.		I am meeting regularly with the Ministers from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, Michael Shanks and Martin McCluskey to press for urgent additional support, as the £17 million announced on Monday 16 March is clearly inadequate to help those who are struggling with rising oil prices. I am committed to engaging with Executive colleagues to ensure those most in need get access to this support as quickly as possible.		I met with the Competition and Markets Authority on 19 March and welcome its formal study of the oil retail market. My officials will provide data to support this work, and I will carefully consider any recommendations arising from its findings.		Trading Standards plays a key role in protecting consumers from illegal trading practices, including unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It also provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and advice to consumers on raising complaints.		The Consumer Council provides invaluable information for consumers by monitoring heating oil prices and publishing weekly average prices. Consumers are encouraged to use its website to compare suppliers and find information on oil buying clubs and Fuel Stamp schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department plans to make available to people and businesses most affected by energy price increases, as a consequence of the conflict in Iran.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of how increased energy prices, as a consequence of the conflict in Iran, will impact her Department's projects; and (ii) any plans she has to address those impacts.
answer_text: My Department is closely monitoring potential changes in energy prices to help inform the impact upon funding requirements. I can advise that hedging arrangements are in place until 31 March 26 which will mitigate the impact of short-term price fluctuations in relation to street lighting energy and officials will continue to assess the situation should the current volatility in the energy market continue in the longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support that will be made available to those most impacted by energy price increases, as a consequence of the conflict in Iran.
answer_text: I refer you to the answer to AQW 41656/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of the impact on farmers of increased energy prices as a consequence of the conflict in Iran; and (ii) any supports his Department plans to make available to mitigate that impact.
answer_text: I am concerned about the sudden increase in energy prices, particularly oil and gas as a consequence of the conflict in Iran and resulting increase in demand.  I fully appreciate the concern within the farming community with the prospect of increased costs and volatility.  It is not possible at this stage to determine the longer term impacts but I will continue to monitor the market situation.Whilst there are currently no specific support schemes available to farmers impacted by increased energy prices, I am continuing to monitor the situation.The Farm Sustainability Payment, as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP), is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.I intend to bring forward a Crisis Framework as part of the SAP. This framework will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of an identified significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the quantity of honey that is produced, broken down by county; and (ii) any support his Department offers to beekeepers who produce honey.
answer_text: Beekeepers are not required to register their beekeeping activity with the Department however they are encouraged to do so on a voluntary basis. Therefore, details of honey produced in Northern Ireland by quantity or county are not available or published by the Department. The Department does however make an estimate of honey produced, based on information collected from a sample of registered beekeepers, which is incorporated within the Total Income From Farming (TIFF) statistics, produced on an annual basis. In 2025, the estimate used in producing the statistics was 87.3 tonnes. My Department provides advice and guidance to local beekeepers, primarily in relation to good husbandry practises and disease awareness. Additionally, my Department facilitates testing and diagnostic services by the Agrifood and Biosciences Institute (AFBI), available to local beekeepers to aid in disease prevention and control. Beekeeping courses are provided by the Ulster Beekeepers’ Association (UBKA), in conjunction with my Department’s College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and the Open College Network Northern Ireland (OCN NI).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of her Department's capacity to spend any new money received in the monitoring round for the end of the financial year, for pothole repair and roads maintenance.
answer_text: My Department has undertaken a review of capacity to deliver additional road maintenance by the end of the financial year which has informed recent funding allocations.I am committed to prioritising road maintenance and I have recently allocated over £40        million of additional funding towards improving the roads here which will be spent by the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of why passenger numbers were down in airports in the north in 2025, while island-wide air travel set new records.
answer_text: Whilst airports across the island benefited from strong overall growth in air travel, our airports in the North continued to perform robustly. Passenger numbers remained strong in 2025, finishing the year just 0.6% below the record post-pandemic recovery seen in 2024, and around 13% higher than 2023. This demonstrates notable resilience despite operating in a highly competitive aviation environment. 	Furthermore, my Department is actively working to support our airports. I will shortly launch a public consultation on an Aviation Policy for the North.  This will set out positions to strengthen connectivity and improve the sector’s competitiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the mass average (i) general; (ii) recyclable; and (iii) organic, household waste in each of the last ten years, broken down by local council.
answer_text: Information regarding Local Authority Collected (LAC) municipal waste and household waste recycling rates are publicly available online via the DAERA website (www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-local-authority-collected-municipal-waste-management-statistics).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any plans his Department has to create a sporting centre of excellence at the Ulster University sports campus in Jordanstown, encompassing world-class indoor and outdoor facilities; and (ii) his assessment of the merits of a northern high performance sporting facility, similar to Abbottstown's Sport Ireland Campus.
answer_text: The Chief Executive of Sport NI has advised me as follows:-Sport NI has had a range of conversations with various interested parties regarding establishing a high-performance sports centre in Northern Ireland over recent years, but none of these conversations have progressed beyond the stage of exploratory discussions.Instead, Sport NI has focused on the provisions of support to high performance athletes through the Sports Institute, which has been based at the Ulster University Jordanstown campus for the last 20 years. Sport NI is currently undertaking a review of existing sports infrastructure across the sporting sector. This includes consideration of High-Performance facility need.I will always welcome further support for performance athletes in Northern Ireland, whether through sport-specific facilities, the Sport NI Sports Institute, or other centres of excellence which support athletes from multiple sports.Any proposal for a dedicated highperformance facility would need a clear, sectorled strategic approach, supported by a joint submission from governing bodies and stakeholders that demonstrates need, assesses viable options, and shows how it would enhance the existing sporting system. While the Ulster University Jordanstown campus continues to play an important role in highperformance sport, it is not a Departmental facility, so any credible proposal must consider a range of potential sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Justice to address enforcement relating to e-scooter legislation, particularly on pavements and footways.
answer_text: My officials have held discussions with the PSNI on e-scooter use and enforcement at the Road Safety Strategic Forum. The group, which includes officials from the Department of Justice, meets regularly and was established to address road safety issues and support the NI Road Safety Strategy to 2030. In addition, the issue of e-scooter use and enforcement has been raised by my officials at the PSNI-led silver command meeting.Road safety continues to be of paramount importance to me, and I am committed to working proactively, along with key partners in the Road Safety Strategic Forum, to address a range of issues which negatively impact on road safety, including illegal e-scooter usage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any road safety improvement measures on Rathfriland Road, Newry.
answer_text: Last year my Department provided 2 pedestrian islands on Rathfriland Road to improve pedestrian safety. Resurfacing work is also ongoing to a section of the Rathfriland Road which will include repairs to a landslip and it is hoped that this work will be completed by the end of March 2026.   I have also asked my officials to undertake an assessment of the existing signs and road markings to ensure they are appropriate for the area.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits which is open for consultation. Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any support he is providing for St. Patrick's Day celebrations in March 2026.
answer_text: My Department and its arms-length bodies support the celebrations through through a range of established arts and culture initiatives.In particular, all local councils, in a match funding arrangement with my Department, deliver the Community Festivals Fund.The Fund is intended to support local, community-led festivals and offers a potential source of practical support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the number of criminal justice cases that are unable to proceed at present due to the dispute with members of the Criminal Bar Association over Crown Court legal aid fees.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has made any assessment of the causes of the continuous rainfall we have been experiencing in 2026.
answer_text: The Met Office, the UK’s national meteorological service, is responsible for providing weather and climate-related services to government, businesses, emergency responders and the public.  Their data indicates that Northern Ireland had the wettest January for 149 years, with 196 mm of rain, representing 170% of the average rainfall.  While weather is influenced by natural variability and atmospheric patterns, the Met Office has stated that climate change is driving a trend towards warmer wetter winters in the UK.  Future projections indicate winters will continue to become wetter, with more days of heavy rainfall, particularly for western areas of the UK.  There will be a higher likelihood of intense winter storms and of successive storms.  It is essential that we prepare for this and improve our resilience to the impacts of climate change.  My Department has been leading on the development of the Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme and I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to the publication of this Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made on the devolution of corporation tax to the north and alignment with that in the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) any representations she has made to progress this.
answer_text: The upfront costs of devolving Corporation Tax, via the British Government’s block grant adjustment mechanism, combined with the financial pressures on the Executive, means that this is currently unaffordable. 		However the Finance Minister is leading work on the development of a full fiscal framework, including on specific taxes and how they could be utilised if devolved, as per the independent Fiscal Commission’s recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any compensation available to individuals who have lost work due to damage to their work vehicles caused by potholes.
answer_text: The Department does not offer compensation for pure economic loss arising from the exercise of its statutory duty to maintain the public road network. However, each roadsrelated public liability claim is considered on its own merits and specific circumstances. Where the Department is deemed liable for the damage sustained by a vehicle, all reasonably ensuing legally compensable elements of the claim will be assessed based on the supporting vouching documentation provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any communication she has had with the Department of Health and the Department of Education, to ensure rural community transport is more embedded as a component part, within health and education services.
answer_text: Responsibility for the provision of transport to health and education services lies with the relevant department in each case.  My department currently engages with the Department of Health via a cross departmental working group regarding community transport but does not currently engage with the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the degree to which her Department consulted with industry regarding the impact of her three-pronged approach for addressing ongoing water and wastewater infrastructure barriers.
answer_text: As part of my three-pronged approach, my Department carried out a public consultation on options for a possible pathway to introduce developer contributions and on the impact of introducing a developer contributions scheme. I am currently considering the responses to this consultation, many of which were received from organisations working in the housing and construction sectors. I regularly engage with industry to seek their input on how my Department and NI Water can support development through investments in our water and wastewater infrastructure. One recent example is the National House-Building Council’s “Beyond ’26 Live” conference event in Cookstown, which provided an opportunity for me and NI Water CEO Sara Venning to discuss water infrastructure issues with a wide range of stakeholders involved in the development of new homes in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 37605/22-27, to detail the priority tasks related to the Armagh to Portadown rail line.
answer_text: The priority tasks related to the Armagh to Portadown rail line include additional assessment of potential station locations in Armagh City and associated rail alignments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any reason why the Investment Pipeline project search contains no projects in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: This problem was caused by a technical issue with the Investment Pipeline search functionality, which has now been resolved.  As of 26 February, the application lists 21 projects in the Newry and Armagh constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support his Department intends to provide to school leaders, to facilitate making specialist provision a standard feature of mainstream schools.
answer_text: A comprehensive package of support is available to help schools develop specialist provision. This includes centrally financing teaching and non-teaching costs from the EA budget, an initial furniture and equipment budget of circa £20,000 associated with any set up works required and a lump sum of £3,000 per class to purchase resources. In addition, EA Support Services provide support with individual self-evaluation and action plans, professional learning development, facilitation of cluster groups and signposting to other services.   Further information on support offered is available on EA’s website at Establishing Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools | SEND Plan.In addition to this schools which develop specialist provision for 2026 will be able to avail of the following:Whole school learning and development offer - 20% of teaching staff in mainstream and up to five classroom assistants can avail of specific SEN training with up to five days release to be completed between April and June 2026.SENCo release - one month for stabilising Specialist Provision embedding processes/workload in addition to a further one day per month per class.Administrative support - £10k to be provided to each school to cover additional administrative tasks associated with the creation and maintenance of specialist provisions.Principal Champions - To release five Principals who have implemented Specialist Provisions to provide support, advice and guidance to new schools (one Post Primary, four Primary)Schools which already have specialist provision will also be able to avail of the first three parts of the additional support package from 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department has allocated to advance the taxi driving industry, given its importance to tourism, travel and the economy.
answer_text: I recognise that reliable and safe transport is vital to our tourism offer and the wider economy. I am pleased that the Director of Transport Planning and Policy serves as the representative for the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) on the Tourism Partnership Board that oversees the delivery of my Department’s Tourism Vision and Action Plan.Before Christmas, I worked alongside the Minister for Infrastructure to advance a proposal for collaborative funding for a late-night transport pilot scheme in Belfast. I understand taxi firms have welcomed the scheme and initial feedback on uptake is positive.DfI is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry in relation to taxi driver, operator and vehicle licencing. It has two main functions that currently impact on the availability of taxi drivers: setting maximum fares and awarding taxi drivers’ licences. To that end the maximum fare was increased in December 2023 and, since the introduction of the test for taxi drivers, the Driver and Vehicle Agency has made several changes to the theory test including lowering the overall pass mark and providing revision guides.In working with the taxi industry to address the range of issues it currently faces, Minister Kimmins announced a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing. As part of this review, Minister Kimmins met with the taxi industry to better understand their concerns. DfI officials are currently analysing the responses received from the recent Class C taxi review survey and they anticipate that a consultation on any proposals should be published shortly.Invest NI provides a broad range of support to small and medium sized businesses across the North primarily in the form of advice, guidance, innovation grants and access to finance. My Department and partner organisations also continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the article in the Armaghi on 24 February 2026, entitled, Pothole plight: Young Armagh carer may be forced to quit job I love because of cost of daily damage to car.
answer_text: I am aware of the article referenced and recognise the very real impact that the current condition of parts of the road network can have on individuals, including the personal and financial pressures highlighted.There has been historic underfunding in many parts of the road network. In addition, recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have taken a severe toll on road surfaces across the region.To put the current situation in context, around 49,000 defects have been recorded in the last three months alone, which is close to half the total for the whole of the previous year and in response, I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects that are causing the most concern. On 24 February 2026 I also reallocated an additional £2.5m from my Department’s budget for essential roads maintenance.Alongside securing additional funding, I have also asked my officials to explore every avenue to maximise available workforce capacity so that we are doing as much as we can, as quickly as possible.I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads, and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worstaffected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what legal duty organisations have to provide feedback to a job applicant if they reach interview stage.
answer_text: There is no legal duty to provide feedback to a job applicant. It should, however, be considered good practice by employers to offer feedback to unsuccessful applicants (either in writing or verbally), if requested. The Labour Relations Agency provides information on this matter within its ‘Advice on Recruitment, Selection and Induction’ document.  Page 25 of this guidance states, “While an employer is not obliged to provide feedback for candidates who are unsuccessful it is beneficial for candidates to have feedback as it can enable them to improve their interview performance and make a more informed decision as to why they feel they were unsuccessful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the potholes at (i) Newtown Road; (ii) Ballycrummy Road; (iii) Damolly Road; (iv) Carrowmannan Road; (v) Ballyrath Road; (vi) Lisheffield Road; (vii) Carrickananny Road, Armagh; (viii) Bachelors Walk, Keady; and (ix) Tyrone's Ditches, Poyntzpass, will be repaired.
answer_text: I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. I have recently allocated an additional £10 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network. This will be used to deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need across the north. This work will commence without delay.While defects are prioritised for repair in line with the timescales set out in DEM 160, the scale of damage caused by recent severe weather and the resulting volume of potholes means that repairs remain subject to contractor scheduling and the availability of resources, which can change at short notice. As a result, it is not always possible to confirm when individual repairs will take place. However, alongside securing additional funding, I have asked officials to explore every opportunity to maximise workforce capacity so that as much work as possible can be progressed as quickly as resources allow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 39488/22/27, whether her Department holds any record of maintenance being performed by her Department in previous years, in Ulster Villas, Newry.
answer_text: As previously advised, Ulster Villas, Newry is a private development and does not form part of the public road network. The Department therefore has no responsibility for the maintenance of this unadopted road.While the Department does not hold maintenance responsibility for this location, records confirm that an inspection was carried out in July 2015, and that a defect was repaired that month. A blocked gully identified during the same inspection was subsequently cleared in November 2018. This work was undertaken in error and does not reflect any ongoing or intended maintenance responsibility by the Department.Responsibility for maintenance at this location lies with the developer.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Parliament Buildings will be illuminated green in celebration of St. Patrick's Day.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has illuminated Parliament Buildings in green to mark St Patrick’s Day annually since 2015.The Assembly Commission’s Special Lighting Policy was originally agreed in 2014 and reviewed 2020. In addition to provisions where applications for external lighting can be made for the Assembly Commission to consider, the policy also allows the Assembly Commission to light Parliament Buildings on a number of days annually for occasions that are significant in the wider community.Those occasions were agreed in 2015 as follows: International Women’s Day – PurpleSaint Patrick’s Day – GreenTwelfth of July – OrangeArmistice Day - Red

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have made to ensure postal addresses reflect the counties people live in.
answer_text: Postal services are a reserved matter, overseen by the Department for Business and Trade, with Ofcom regulating the local postal industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department will take to ensure that (i) deaf; and (ii) disabled, people are consulted and involved in co-producing the Executive Disability Strategy.
answer_text: The Disability Strategy, which is currently out for public consultation, has been developed as the result of an extensive and ongoing co-design process involving Deaf and disabled people and their representative organisations.This included the establishment of an Expert Advisory Panel, a Co-Design Group and a series of engagement sessions delivered both online and in person. The draft Disability Strategy includes plans for the formation of a Northern Ireland Disability Forum, made up of Deaf and disabled people, to monitor progress on the implementation of the commitments made within the Strategy and to assist in the development of an Action Plan to deliver them.As part of the ongoing public consultation process, my officials have developed a range of accessible engagement opportunities to ensure that as many people as possible can contribute to the consultation.  All feedback received will be carefully considered and fed into further Strategy development, the final content of which remains subject to approval by the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the importance of imported materials like cement, steel, and aluminium for infrastructure, to detail any representations she has made to ensure that importers are not double charged for carbon cost, under both UK and EU regulations.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for carbon pricing sits with the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. Two separate carbon pricing regimes operate here: the UK Emissions Trading Scheme and, for power generation only, the EU Emissions Trading System under the Windsor Framework. As these are entirely discrete systems, there is no circumstance in which a business here could face carbon costs under both systems, removing any risk of double charging. The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which began on 1 January 2026, does not apply here as it is not within the scope of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans his Department has to ensure that businesses are not double-charged for carbon cost under both UK and EU regulations.
answer_text: My Department currently implements two discrete carbon pricing regimes. These are: the UK Emissions Trading Scheme in respect of energy intensive industry and aviation; and the EU Emissions Trading System in respect of the power generation sector (as is required under Article 9 and Annex 4 of the Windsor Framework, to protect the operation of the Single Electricity Market on the island of Ireland). As these are discrete and separate schemes, there is no scope for both EU and UK ETS carbon costs to apply to any individual business and no scope for double charging.As you may be aware, the EU have implemented their Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) with effect from 1 January. This places an obligation on companies that import certain goods and precursors (cement, iron and steel, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity and hydrogen) into the EU to report the level of embedded emissions, as well as any carbon (ETS) price paid at origin. Importers must purchase CBAM certificates at a price corresponding to the current EU ETS price, with deductions for any explicit carbon costs paid in the country of origin. This regulation does not apply to Northern Ireland importers, as it has not been added to the scope of the Windsor Framework.The UK Government has announced its intention to implement a UK CBAM with effect from 1 January 2027, and this will apply to UK importers of goods within scope (that is the same sectors as EU, excepting electricity), including in NI. It will therefore be the UK CBAM, and not that of the EU, to which NI importers will be subject.Both the EU, and proposed UK CBAMs will take into account any carbon price already paid in the country of origin prior to determining the CBAM charge payable.The establishment of a link between the UK and EU Emissions Trading Schemes, negotiations for which are currently underway, would create the conditions for mutual CBAM exemptions between both parties. I am very supportive of this work and my department is liaising closely with counterparts in the UK, Scottish and Welsh Governments with respect to this, to ensure the best possible outcome for NI businesses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department has taken to reduce plastic packaging and stickers on fruit and other produce in shops.
answer_text: My Department is a founding signatory to the UK Plastics Pact which has seen the elimination of problematic and unnecessary plastic packaging items from UK supermarket shelves. The UK Plastics Pact lead by WRAP brings together businesses to act on priority areas with four targets taken forward for action by 2025 including eliminating problem single-use plastics, use of recyclable or compostable packaging, effective recycling and recycled content in packaging. My Department will continue to support WRAP with the new Packaging Pact, the successor to the UK Plastics Pact, which will launch in April 2026. My Department, together with other devolved governments have introduced the Extended Producer Responsibility scheme for packaging which went live operationally in April 2025. This will deliver across all four nations, the following objectives: Reduce unnecessary packaging and increase the reuse of packaging Incentivise the use of more sustainable packaging materials Improve the quality and quantity of packaging material recovered across the UK Reduce the amount of packaging sent for disposal at landfill and energy from wasteMy officials will continue to engage with Defra and other devolved administrations to consider policy options to address packaging reductions on fruit and other produce.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of introducing legislation to hold abusers criminally accountable when their victims die by suicide, as a result of a clear and explicit causal link between sustained abuse and their suicide.
answer_text: The devastating impact of abuse, including circumstances where sustained abuse may contribute to a victim’s suicide, must never be underestimated. Where there is a clear and explicit causal link between an abuser’s conduct and a victim’s death, it is essential that the justice system has the tools to hold perpetrators fully accountable.While there is currently no dedicated offence anywhere in the UK that specifically criminalises causing or encouraging suicide through abuse or coercive control, Northern Ireland law already contains provisions that can be applied in these circumstances. Sections 13 and 13A of the Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 1966 make it an offence to encourage or assist another person to take, or attempt to take, their own life. Section 13A makes clear that this includes situations in which an individual pressures, threatens, or coerces someone into suicide.Further, the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 significantly strengthened protections for victims of domestic abuse. It recognises serious harm caused not only by physical violence but also by psychological, emotional, coercive, and controlling behaviours. The domestic abuse aggravator in section 15 requires courts to treat any offence as aggravated where it is committed in the context of domestic abuse. In principle, this could apply to offences such as manslaughter where a perpetrator’s sustained abuse can be shown to have had a clear causal role in a victim’s suicide.Additional protection is also provided through section 184 of the UK-wide Online Safety Act 2023 (2023 Act), which makes it an offence for someone to  encourage or assist serious selfharm, including through inperson or electronic communication. Following legislative consent from the Assembly, I am extending a broader version of this offence through the Westminster Crime and Policing Bill. This new offence will repeal and replace section 184 and will criminalise the encouragement or assistance of serious selfharm both by communication and by any other means. Taken together, these provisions already provide the courts with significant powers to address cases in which abusive behaviour contributes to a victim’s suicide.Nonetheless, tackling abuse in all its forms remains a priority for my Department. My officials continue to work closely with other departments, operational partners, and victimsupport organisations to ensure that the justice system responds effectively to all forms of abuse. My Department will continue to keep the law in this area under review, informed by emerging evidence, the experiences of victims, and developments in neighbouring jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has carried out to determine how train and bus tickets, purchased on the Translink app, can be downloaded or made available offline, so passengers can access tickets without Wi-Fi.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink assumes this query refers to tickets held within Translink’s “mLink” mobile ticketing app. All “mLink” mobile app tickets can (already/currently) be displayed and viewed offline. An internet connection is required to sign in to the mLink app, and purchase and activate tickets. These app functions require an online connection to verify customer details, perform payments and guard against potential fraud (respectively).Translink’s policy is that all tickets must be activated before the customer boards the train or bus so Translink do not foresee a situation where the customer needs (a WiFi/Internet connection) to activate an mLink ticket on-board. The mLink app will display a message to the customer that they are in 'Offline mode' during any time period in which they have no internet connection.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to work towards all-island collaboration on paediatric pathology services.
answer_text: As I outlined in the recent Assembly debate, I am committed to doing everything possible to improve paediatric pathology services, including exploring the potential development of an allisland service and the use of lessinvasive postmortem approaches. Scoping work in both these areas has commenced. My conversations with my counterpart in the Republic of Ireland, while positive, have confirmed they do not currently have the capacity to undertake additional post-mortems from Northern Ireland. Their immediate priority is to build the necessary capacity that could support an all-island approach in the future through the training and retention of staff with appropriate specialist expertise. It is important to acknowledge that this is in the context of a global shortage of paediatric pathologists and is likely to take several years, and progress will depend on factors beyond our direct control. My Department will continue to engage constructively with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland and in England to explore every possible option for improving these services for families at this most difficult time. However, it is not possible to be definitive on timescales at this stage as progress will depend on external workforce and system-wide factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 37431/22-27, for an update on the pilot for the new CCTV system at Newry Bus Station.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink is pleased to report that it has recently secured funding for a new CCTV system at Newry Bus Station. Costs and the programme of works are currently being finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) their assessment of the statistic that Catholics made up 27 per cent of applicants to the latest PSNI recruitment drive; and (ii) any timebound, goal-oriented actions will they take to address this.
answer_text: This question has been transferred to me as Minister of Justice; however recruitment decisions are operational matters for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.   I have been consistently clear in my unwavering support to see a fully representative police service, and I have called upon fellow elected members, leaders and opinion-formers across our wider society to echo this support.  I would emphasise once again that it is incumbent upon all of us, to encourage all members of our community to consider joining the PSNI and to help to create the societal conditions where that is most likely to occur.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Newry Times article on 19 February 2026, which states that the average Newry home is £35,000 more expensive than a year ago.
answer_text: I have not made an assessment of the Newry Times article.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of how the percentage of Catholics in the PSNI, impacts on the carriage of justice.
answer_text: Whilst having a broadly representative police service is an essential part of ensuring community confidence in and cooperation with policing, there is no evidence to suggest that the carriage of justice is dependent on the identity of officers.I have been consistent in my support for a fully representative police service and in my encouragement of anyone, regardless of background, to consider a career in policing. I would emphasise once again that it is incumbent upon us, as elected representatives, leaders and opinion-formers across society as a whole, to encourage all members of our community to consider joining the PSNI and to help to create the societal conditions where that is most likely to occur.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's schedule for (i) road repairs; and (ii) reinstatement works, in Newry and Armagh, broken down by location.
answer_text: Details of the work programme for the 2026/27 financial year will be published in my Department’s Annual Report to Council and my officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy in so far as resources permit.  My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements of utility works and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.Follow-up inspections for defects are conducted at the expense of the utility company and are repeated as necessary until the faulty reinstatement has been satisfactorily addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to address dermatology appointment waiting times.
answer_text: Dermatology waiting times are too long and unfortunately the demand for dermatology in all Trusts continues to exceed capacity.  Regrettably patients are experiencing excessive waits for appointments and treatment which is not the service that Trusts would wish to provide.Primary and Secondary Care services are working collaboratively to improve waiting times, enhance access, and address workforce challenges ensuring patients are seen in the right place by the right professional.In Primary Care Elective Services:Investment has been made to deliver face-to-face and remote consultations annually for patients presenting with routine dermatology conditions such as adult and childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes, and suspected actinic keratosis.A pilot launched in March 2025 enables GPs with enhanced skills to surgically treat patients diagnosed with low-risk basal cell carcinomas in primary care.The Regional Dermatology Photo Triage Pathway, supports image capture and e-referral for suspected skin cancer and urgent basal cell carcinoma cases. As of October 2025, 90% of GP practices are contracted to provide this pathway, enabling effective decision-making at the point of triage in secondary care.In Secondary Care Services:•	Development of specialist nursing roles to assess and treat patients;•	Use of biologic medications to manage chronic dermatological                         conditions;•	Introduction of virtual and telephone consultations to provide advice           and guidance and reduce waiting times;•	Implementation of Patient-Initiated Follow-Up (PIFU) services and            enhanced triage clinics to reform outpatient care.I hope this information is helpful in addressing your query.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact that water and wastewater infrastructure is having on housing (i) waiting lists; (ii) construction; and (iii) prices.
answer_text: I am aware that when housing demand exceeds supply, it can have an adverse effect on waiting lists. I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints, to help mitigate this problem.I also fully appreciate the impact that wastewater capacity challenges are having on housing development and that is why I will continue to develop the three-pronged approach to help unlock wastewater connections. Firstly, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water. Secondly, my Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill will make provision for future regulations on sustainable drainage systems (SuDS). This will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network. Thirdly, my Department has consulted on options to introduce developer contributions to help fund wastewater infrastructure improvements. My officials are analysing the responses to that consultation to help inform next steps.	Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office, we have provided ringfenced allocations to NI Water to unlock wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North. This year, my Department has provided NI Water with over £500m of public money to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services.In addition, my officials are also working collaboratively with colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the Department for Communities (DfC) to identify and enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity in areas of housing need, thereby contributing to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy. (iii)	As with the issue of housing waiting lists, I am aware that when housing demand exceeds supply, it can also affect house prices. As outlined above, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues, NI Water and other key stakeholders to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints in order to avoid negative impacts on housing affordability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the project plan and programme for the delivery of the new haematology ward at Belfast City Hospital for 2030-31, including the timeline for (i) approval of the business case; (ii) planning design and procurement stage; (iii) statutory approvals obtained; (iv) tender documentation finalised; (v) contract awarded; (vi) handover of the new facility; (vii) necessary accretions obtained to deliver CAR-T cell therapy; and other services; and (ii) the capacity building and skills training for staff to be able to deliver CAR-T cell therapy and other services.
answer_text: (i)	Approval of the Outline Business Case (OBC) – 10th December 2025(ii)	Planning design and procurement stage - from Jan 2026 to Quarter 1 2028*(iii)	Statutory approvals obtained – during design stage(s).(iv)	Tender documentation finalised – Quarter 2 2027*(v)	 Contract awarded – Quarter 1 2028*(vi)	Handover of the new facility Quarter 2 2030*(vii) and (vii) Patients from Northern Ireland have access to CAR-T treatments as per circular “HSC(SQSD) 12-22 – NICE Technology Appraisals – Process for Endorsement, Implementation, Monitoring and Assurance in NI” which supports the timely introduction of effective new medicines.If CAR-T treatments are approved by NICE then patients in NI will be granted access to them on an equitable basis with other nations through the arrangements in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.The lead in time to ensure the Belfast Trust meet the legislative requirements with HTA and MHRA to deliver CAR-T in the new Regional Haematology Ward, and to ensure staff are trained is approximately 12 -15 months, however the service is working with the DoH to scope what could potentially be delivered in a phased approach, subject to funding being available, in advance of handover of the new haematology ward to expediate the accreditation process.  *Based on Outline Business Case (OBC) programme. Please note, as with all construction projects, there may be variance within and between RIBA Stages, influenced by the final procurement methodology adopted. Quarter years are calendar years e.g. Quarter 1 2028 is January - March 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of why more than half of the reported potholes are located in the Newry, Mourne, and Down, and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon local council areas.
answer_text: My Department places a high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network.I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. As part of this, I have recently allocated an additional £7.85 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network.I recognise that many of our rural roads have been some of the worst-impacted, which is why I have allocated £3.3 million of this Winter Recovery Fund to Southern Division. This money will be used to prioritise areas of significant concern.While I recognise that much of Newry, Mourne, and Down, and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon local council areas has seen exceptional rainfall and that the large network of rural roads mean that pothole numbers are high, the latest figures from my officials indicate that these two areas accounted for approximately 36% of all reported potholes for the current financial year.Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term.
