However, in addition to securing the additional funding, I have also asked my officials to explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.Whilst the overall budgetary position remains challenging, and difficult decisions need to be made around prioritisation of investment, I remain committed to increasing overall investment in our Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress being made on the cross-jurisdictional paper on CAR-T cell therapy referenced in the minutes of the North-South Cancer Policy and Strategy Group meeting on 29 October 2025, including (i) whether a Terms of Reference has been agreed; and (ii) the timescale for completion of the paper, including key milestones for implementing recommendations.
answer_text: Officials continue to work closely across both jurisdictions on the provision of cross-border cancer services. I can confirm that the previous position in terms of availability of cross-border CAR T-cell therapy services remains unchanged at this time and the ROI CAR T-Cell therapy service continues to operate at full capacity.Cross-border collaboration on the provision of cancer service delivery remains a strategic priority for my Department and should further exploratory work be required including the development of strategic papers, officials from both jurisdictions will continue to work collaboratively to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's definition of a billboard.
answer_text: There is no statutory definition of a ‘billboard’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to (i) reopen the gangway; and (ii) provide a non-slip access ramp to the lake, for swimmers at Camlough Lake.
answer_text: Access to Camlough Lake is not the responsibility of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to provide a direct bus service between Newry City and Armagh City on Sundays.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink. I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Given the funding currently available Translink is not in a position to introduce a direct bus service on Sundays between Newry City and Armagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the article in the Irish News on 16 February 2026, regarding train cancellations from Newry to Dublin and Belfast, due to signalling issues, to detail (i) what these issues were; and (ii) any steps she will take to address these issues.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink has confirmed that disruption on 16 February 2026 between Portadown and Newry was caused by a signalling fault following overnight planned engineering works, which led to lastminute cancellations to early morning services. Engineers quickly rectified the fault and full operations resumed before 08:30. NI Railways has apologised for the inconvenience this has caused to passengers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the white lines at Loughgall Road, Armagh, will be repainted.
answer_text: Resurfacing work was completed at this location on Sunday 15 February 2026.  Safety related markings have already been restored and the remaining parking bay markings are planned for completion this week, subject to favourable weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any collaboration she has had with her Executive colleagues, regarding the formulation of a cohesive, future-proof, mobile strategy.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of an efficient and effective planning system to support sustainable development in the North, and which facilitates the development of telecommunications infrastructure in an efficient and effective manner, whilst keeping the environmental impact to a minimum.My Department was represented on the Department for the Economy (DfE) cross-sectoral working group that led engagement with the industry and oversaw the publication of the Mobile Action Plan (MAP) in June 2022. The MAP action plan set out a number of actions, including the creation of a DFE-led Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT) and my officials sit on the  Oversight Group and Planning Subgroup with Mobile UK.I, along with my DfE counterpart, Minister Caoimhe Archibald MLA, met representatives of the mobile phone industry in July 2025. I found the meeting both constructive and informative, providing valuable first-hand insight into issues relating to mobile phone coverage and the concerns of the mobile industryMy Department continues to have a positive working relationship with mobile operators and remains committed to working constructively with industry, and other key stakeholders, to consider relevant matters as appropriate through the auspices of the BBT and its Planning SubgroupAny recommendations arising from the outworkings of the BBT and its Planning Subgroup will be brought to me and other key stakeholders in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any representations she has made to ensure network providers all have RTÉ and TG4 as part of the standard broadcast packages.
answer_text: I am very supportive of maximising access to RTÉ, TG4 and other Irish broadcasters.Broadcasting policy, including the regulation of television services and channel availability, is a reserved matter for the British Government.My Department has raised this issue with the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS), and DCMS has written to the Irish Government on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any representations he has made to ensure network providers all have RTÉ and TG4 as part of the standard broadcast packages.
answer_text: I am very supportive of maximising access to RTÉ, TG4 and other Irish broadcasters.Broadcasting policy, including the regulation of television services and channel availability, is a reserved matter for the British Government.My Department has raised this issue with the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS), and DCMS has written to the Irish Government on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the ADHD Needs Assessment Report's recommendation to establish a group to carry out an option appraisal and develop a strategy to reduce and bring ADHD waiting times down to a standard commensurate with other health conditions.
answer_text: The ADHD needs assessment recognises that there are significant numbers of children, young people and adults waiting for an ADHD needs assessment, resulting in waiting times of several years due to the ongoing lack of a commissioned service. This situation is not acceptable, and the report highlights that the aim should be to deliver equity in service provision through the introduction of similar waiting time standards across all Trusts.  Recommendation 19 of the report proposes that a group is established to carry out an options appraisal and develop a strategy to reduce and eventually bring down waiting times. A shortlist of six potential options to address existing waiting lists has been identified for future consideration, in advance of any commissioned ADHD service being established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to increase the availability of sheltered housing options that incorporate support services, enabling older people to maintain their independence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has recently completed a public consultation on the Supporting People (SP) Strategic Intent 2026–2031, which closed on 26 January 2026. The consultation sought views to inform the future direction of the SP Programme over the next five years and to shape associated operational delivery plans. In parallel, the Housing Executive has commenced a programme of work to modernise the SP Programme. A key element of the SP Modernisation Project 2025–2027 is the development of a Strategic Needs Assessment (SNA), which will provide a robust evidence base of current and projected housing-related support needs. The findings of the SNA will inform future SP Programme priorities, including the consideration of gaps in provision and the potential reprofiling of services to better meet identified needs, including the housing support needs of Older People.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to address the lack of detailed timelines in the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, launched on 17 December 2025, sets out a proposed sequencing of actions taking into account the challenging budget context and sector capacity, both of which will influence the scale and pace of implementation.Therefore, proposals have been classified into three categories as follows: Category 1: actions already underwayCategory 2: actions that will be prioritised next by April 2030.Category 3: actions that may take longer to implement, with the aim of delivering them by April 2034, subject to available resources and evaluation, where appropriate, of category 1 and 2 actions. Many of the actions in category 2 and 3 above can be scaled up or down or sequenced differently depending on available resources.It is my intention that once the public consultation has concluded I will bring forward a final strategy for Executive approval alongside an implementation plan for the agreed Category 2 actions. The implementation plan will detail the actions which will be taken forward under each objective, Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), delivery partners, timescale and estimated costs. However, all actions to be taken forward by April 2030 will be subject to the required budget being made available by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the per capita investment in sport in the north of Ireland; and (ii) how this compares to (a) Scotland; (b) Wales; (c) England; and (d) the Republic of Ireland, for each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 28708/22-27.My Department does not hold any data regarding per capita investment in sport in Northern Ireland, or in any other part of the United Kingdom or in the Republic of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of individuals (i) questioned; (ii) charged; and (iii) convicted, for aiding and abetting abuse, in relation to historical institutional abuse cases.
answer_text: Information in relation to parts (i) and (ii) of the question is not held by the Department.  The recording of information on persons questioned or charged with criminal offences is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in parts (i) and (ii) of the question to the PSNI.In relation to part (iii) of the question, while statistical datasets held by the Department, relating to convictions at courts, contain information on offence descriptions, these tend to be generic in nature.  Detail in relation to the circumstances or location of an offence for which a conviction was the outcome is not held as part of statistical datasets, so it is not possible to identify cases specifically related to historical institutional abuse, as listed in the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of establishing a community mental health hub in Armagh.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 sets the future strategic direction of mental health services in Northern Ireland.A core objective of the Strategy is to provide people across Northern Ireland with equitable access to high quality, consistent and locally based mental health services. Implementation of the actions within the Strategy will have greater focus on primary mental health, therapy hubs and integration with the community and voluntary sector. Any decision to commission mental health services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and population need and within the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the work of the new Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: My senior officials have regular scheduled meetings with the independent autism reviewer, and she has provided helpful feedback on the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan. I am encouraged she has expressed her support for the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan at Education Committee and highlighted the short window of opportunity for effective implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the multiplier effect that has cross-departmental benefits in relation to (i) physical health and wellbeing; (ii) mental health and wellbeing; (iii) community cohesion; and (iv) economic impact, in relation to participation in sport.
answer_text: Participation in sport delivers benefits across multiple government priorities. Increased physical activity contributes to improved physical and mental health, helping reduce preventable illness, lower stress, and enhance overall wellbeing. These areas directly support crossdepartmental aims in health, education, and social care. Sport also strengthens community cohesion by creating inclusive, shared spaces and opportunities for engagement, volunteering, and social connection. In addition, participation supports our economy through employment and facility use, and sporting events have the ability to showcase Northern Ireland on the global stage whilst driving and delivering economic benefits to the tourism and hospitality sector in particular, demonstrating the wideranging societal value that sport can deliver.Active Living – the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland provides the strategic framework through which these crossdepartmental benefits are realised. The Strategy aims to create a healthier and more resilient society by increasing participation and promoting an inclusive, vibrant sporting sector. Its emphasis on inclusion, engagement, shared spaces and partnership working ensures more people are more active, more of the time, directly contributing to improved health outcomes and strengthened community cohesion. Active Living’s delivery model supports long-term socio-economic resilience and reinforces the extensive benefits that sport brings across government. The Strategy is delivered under the leadership of a Strategic Implementation Group and through collaboration between Government Departments, Councils, communities and the wider sports sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures being taken by his Department to ensure farmers can access sufficient slurry storage capacity, given the current sustained period of inclement weather.
answer_text: I understand the challenges farmers are facing due to the current sustained period of inclement weather. I have discussed these issues with the President of the Ulster Farmers Union (UFU) and senior NIEA officials have engaged with UFU on the matter.  The Department remains committed to helping agriculture adapt to a changing climate. 		The closed period for slurry spreading lasts 16 weeks, with farms required to maintain at least 22 weeks’ slurry storage (26 weeks for pig and poultry farms) under the 2019 Regulations.		This is to ensure that farms have sufficient capacity to retain slurry following the closed period until the soil and weather conditions are suitable for spreading in the spring. A detailed scientific review by AFBI in 2020 found that the current closed period is the most effective way to minimise risks of nutrient losses from slurry application during the winter months.		The ongoing, exceptionally wet weather experienced this year has resulted in significant challenges for many farms, particularly those with the standard 22 weeks of slurry storage capacity. These farms are now encountering increased pressure due to the extended periods of rainfall and adverse conditions. The primary issue arises from the evolving weather patterns, including more frequent and intense rainfall events, which highlight the need for greater resilience in agricultural practices moving forward.Technical advice and support to farmers is provided by the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), through a range of different forums including press articles, online and face to face meetings and through the CAFRE website. Farmers requiring advice on managing the impacts of difficult weather conditions should contact their local CAFRE Adviser.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Taoiseach naming the army agent known as Stakeknife in the Dáil Éireann.
answer_text: We recognise that matters relating to the report of Operation Kenova will have a significant impact on victims and survivors and their families. A key commitment within our Victims and Survivors Strategy is to provide support for victims and survivors, and their families, as they move forward from experiences of the past. The Strategy also recognises the importance of truth and justice and the need to work towards greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the latest structural analysis assessment conducted by her Department on the bridge at A29 Irish Street, Armagh.
answer_text: The most recent structural assessment of Friary Road Bridge indicated that the bridge could sustain normal traffic loading.  However, to reduce the risk of damage to the structure, restrictions on abnormal load movements have been introduced.  The latest Principal Inspection took place on 14 August 2025 which identified a number of defects.Officials plan to commission detailed investigations in the coming months, including an options report for repairs to be undertaken.My Department remains committed to ensuring the continued safe operation of this structure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the potholes on Benagh Road, Mayobridge, will be repaired.
answer_text: Benagh Road was inspected in January 2026, with a follow up inspection completed on 9th February.  Actionable defects were instructed for repair, with the majority already completed. Officials will continue to undertake inspections in line with the Department’s Limited Service Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the merits of working from home.
answer_text: My Department operates in line with the NICS Hybrid Working Policy. Where a role is assessed as suitable for hybrid working based on business needs, staff are required to attend their designated workplace for at least 40% of their contractual working hours each week. In April 2025, I announced proposals to reform employment law, including measures to support better work-life balance. Consistent with this work and my commitment to promoting Good Jobs, I support the NICS Hybrid Working Policy which aims to enhance business delivery, improve efficiency and support work-life balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to legislate for the safety of pedestrians and road users, given the rising prevalence of e-scooters.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI. The Department is exploring measures to raise awareness around the dangers associated with e-scooters. The Department is also monitoring the progress of a pilot scheme for rental e-scooters in Britain, as well as the impact of the legislative changes in the south which made the use of e-scooters legal and regulated their use on public roads from May 2024. This evidence base can help inform the department of what steps to take in relation to the registration and regulation of e-scooters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of providing a basic income for the arts, to provide financial stability to artists and creative workers, similar to what is done in the south.
answer_text: The work of artists and the contribution they make to the quality of life of people in Northern Ireland is understood and is valued.The Basic Income for the Arts in Ireland is a programme that my officials and the Arts Council are aware of, however there is no provision in the budget and no plans at this stage to replicate it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how the Adult Protection Bill protects older adults against an expansive definition of harm, including (i) psychological; (ii) financial; (iii) self-directed; and (iv) physical, harm.
answer_text: The Adult Protection Bill uses a deliberately broad definition of harm, consisting of (i) a basic definition covering “all harmful conduct”, and (ii) a non-exhaustive list of examples including physical, psychological and financial harm. Officials have received advice from the Office of Legislative Counsel (OLC), confirming that these examples do not limit the wider definition, and that any emerging harm type will fall within scope if it involves harmful conduct of any description. This ensures older adults are protected from the full range of abuse instances, including behaviour that causes fear, alarm or distress. The focus of the Bill is on protection interventions rather than wider safeguarding. Self-directed harm and neglect are not included in the Bill as the harm must be caused by another person. Self-directed harm and neglect continue to be addressed through existing Health and Social Care pathways which are part of early intervention services to prevent an adult becoming increasing susceptible to harm from others.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the level of staffing in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) for his assessment of this level of staffing in ensuring sufficient capacity to care for patients, compared to other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Information on the whole-time equivalent (WTE) of staff employed in the Health and Social Care Trusts is published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  The latest information available relates to the position as at 30 September 2025 and can be found at the link below.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-health-and-social-care-hsc-workforce-statistics-september-2025In relation to the Southern Health and Social Care Trust specifically, 10,516.0 WTE staff were employed as at 30 September 2025.With the increasing drive to work across Trust boundaries, both staff and patients frequently move between Trusts. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust consultants work across multiple sites including South West Acute Hospital, Lagan Valley Hospital, Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex, and Belfast City Hospital while consultants from other Trusts in-reach to both Craigavon Area Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital. It is therefore not possible to assess the staffing levels in the Southern Health Social Care Trust compared to other Health and Social Care Trusts.There is a capacity and demand gap in all Trusts which will vary by specialty/subspecialty and the changing levels of demand. This capacity shortfall will take time to address and will require additional funding to tackle the historic underfunding of both acute and community services in Health and Social Care Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how Artificial Intelligence is being utilised for diagnosis and treatment pathways for patients.
answer_text: Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCNI) are using Artificial Intelligence (AI) in a range of projects to support health and care professionals (HCP) for diagnosis and treatment pathways. Some examples are shown below:AI in Brain Image Stroke Scanning for CT Brain Perfusion is being used to support clinicians in interpreting specific elements of the CT brain scans required for identifying patients for disability saving interventions (thrombolysis and IAT- intra-arterial therapy). This is envisaged to reduce the time to decision-making for both thrombolysis and IAT. A clinical decision tool with an ASPECTS score (Alberta Stroke program Early CT Score) is produced to assist clinicians in timely identification of acute stroke. The results used in conjunction with radiology reporting also acts as a decision aid for the stroke clinicians to make a time critical decision for lysis at time of scan. The tool has been subject to Trust governance groups prior to implementation and found it to be a useful adjunct to imaging evaluation in hyperacute stroke but does not remove the need for interpretation by a trained professional.AI Bone Image Fracture Scanning uses an AI-enhanced algorithm to support clinicians in reading X-rays within Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust Emergency Departments (EDs). The project helps to identify acute fractures, effusions and dislocations in patients from the age of two and can help reduce waiting times for patients in Minor Injuries by reducing reading time and errors.AI-enabled diagnostic tool creating 3D models from coronary CT scans.AI-enabled diagnostic technology that creates a 3D model from a standard cardiac CT scan to measure blood flow (FFRCT) in coronary arteries. It helps physicians determine the functional significance of artery blockages, often avoiding the need for more invasive tests.AI in healthcare remains an emerging and rapidly evolving field, with ongoing innovation promising to further enhance patient diagnosis and treatment pathways in the years ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she is taking to reduce the backlog and processing times of compensation claims for damage to vehicles.
answer_text: My Department has continued to progress public liability claims as efficiently as possible.The normal average timescale for dealing with a vehicle damage claim is around four months.Unfortunately, this year’s extreme weather — from belowaverage temperatures and snow toprolonged unsettled conditions and Storm Chandra — has severely impacted the road network,compounding the effects of longstanding underfunding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of how funding uncertainties and inconsistencies are acting as an impediment to high performance, strategic planning, and podium finishes in sporting organisations.
answer_text: I believe it is essential that athletes across all sports are given the best possible opportunity to succeed, supported by adequate and consistent resources, particularly at international and highperformance levels. However, as Sport NI holds responsibility for funding highperformance programmes, I sought further information from its Chief Executive regarding funding concerns. As the response contains substantial detail, I have arranged for it to be made available in the Assembly Library.

answer_text: Total investment is shown in the table below.  The information in relation to Sport NI funding has been provided to me by the Chief Executive of Sport NI.Only partial information is available at this time for 2025/26.FinancialYearSport NIExchequerSport NILotteryTotal DfCTotal2016/17£6,495,495£8,023,852£2,652,293£17,171,6402017/18£4,648,120£7,796,958£1,349,491£13,794,5692018/19£4,247,428£7,313,762£806,942£12,368,1322019/20£4,432,344£7,088,217£2,560,228£14,080,7892020/21£22,722,723£5,819,129£1,406,135£29,947,9872021/22£12,442,365£5,661,907£1,593,487£19,697,7592022/23£3,450,817£7,045,060£1,094,029£11,589,9062023/24£3,092,466£8,211,373£272,538£11,576,3772024/25£2,552,327£8,602,715£1,209,276£12,364,3182025/26£2,700,302£10,395,699£407,133£13,503,134Total£66,784,387£75,958,672£13,351,552£156,094,611	In addition, my Department has provided £11.4 million in match funding for sports-related projects with a total value of £82.2 million under the PEACE Programme. This funding cannot be attributed to specific financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35968/22-27, for an updated timeframe for the recommendations regarding school bus safety to be introduced.
answer_text: I have established a Safer Journeys to School Programme within my Department to examine options to improve safety for school journeys, including legislative options. This work is evidence-led to ensure interventions are effective and proportionate.The Department remains committed to improving road safety and will continue to engage with stakeholders to deliver the most effective measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the deterioration of Aghmakane Road, Newry, to detail when a site inspection, with associated repairs and resurfacing, can be expected.
answer_text: Aghmakane Road was inspected in early November 2025 with actionable defects repaired later that month. Officials continue to undertake inspections and identify actionable defects for repair in line with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Crossnenagh Road, Keady, will be repaired.
answer_text: I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. An inspection of the Crossnenagh Road is planned in the coming weeks and actionable defects will be instructed for repair in line with my Department’s current Limited Service policy.The winter period has taken a severe toll on the road network. To put the current situation in context, around 50,000 defects have been recorded in the last three months alone, which is close to half the total for the whole of the previous year.I have recently allocated an additional £7.85 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network. This will be used to deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need across the north. This work will commence without delay.Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term. However, in addition to securing the additional funding, I have also asked my officials to explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale behind his decision to remove £6.6million in funding from the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass project; and (ii) any actions he will take to ensure this project is properly funded.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 39959/22-27The Minister of Finance has advised that the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass project is part of the Mid South West Growth Deal.  City and Growth Deals are an Executive commitment and, as such, have first call on capital funding. His proposals for a draft Budget 2026-2029/30 include providing Executive earmarked funding of £12.9 million in 2026-27 for this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department receives on an annual basis from mobile phone masts being located on DFI land, itemised by location.
answer_text: Mobile phone companies have a statutory right to install apparatus within the public road boundary, and therefore the Department does not receive any revenue from these.The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) receive £5k annually, subject to periodic reviews, from mobile phone masts being located on DVA owned land, at Coa Road, Enniskillen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount allocated for Casement Park, that will be unspent this year; and (ii) where that funding will be reallocated.
answer_text: Negotiations are ongoing between the Department and UCGAA in relation to the development costs for 2025/26 and they are still being finalised.The Department’s funding for the Casement Park project is ringfenced and will not be used for any other purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the volume of untreated sewage, released into watercourses, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question. NI Water has advised that sewage (wastewater) discharges are generally considered under two categories: (a) discharges that occur on a continuous basis from a Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) of wastewater that has been treated to a standard that is considered safe to return to the environment; and (b) untreated discharges that occur on an intermittent basis from storm overflows when dilute wastewater is discharged direct to the environment during periods of heavy rainfall.In the case of untreated discharges from storm overflows, NI Water operates approximately 2,430 overflows across Northern Ireland. To monitor and record spills, Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) are being installed across NI Water’s wastewater network as part of PC 21.  EDMs measure spill occurrences and the duration of each spill, but do not measure volumes. Where NI Water does not have measured data, it relies on models to simulate observed flows and predict spills across the wastewater networks.  This modelling data is published by NI Water and can be accessed on the NI Water website at Storm Overflows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for the Economy, to ensure a policy is in place to enable due diligence on house purchases, taking cognisance of anti-social behaviour and police records relating to the neighbourhood.
answer_text: I have had no engagement with the Minister for the Economy on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a Mother and Baby Unit.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has approved an Outline Business Case for the construction of a six-bed regional Mother and Baby Unit on the Belfast City Hospital site. The project can proceed to the next stage, appointment of a design team, on receipt of a Commissioners’ Letter of Support which is required to evidence revenue funding availability. My Department continues to liaise with the Belfast Trust with a view to progressing a solution as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of replacing the illegally erected Sinn Fein billboard on Department of Infrastructure land, at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, with an art installation that celebrates the history and heritage of Newry.
answer_text: My Department does not own the billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, therefore I cannot comment on any proposal for replacing it with an art installation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when repairs will be carried out on the Carran Road, Crossmaglen.
answer_text: In addition to securing the additional funding, I have also asked my officials to explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worst affected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.An inspection of Carran Road, Crossmaglen was completed on the 20 January 2026 identifying one actionable defect which has been instructed for repair.  Officials will continue to undertake inspections and actionable defects will be instructed for repair in line with my Department’s current Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale for her Department using the assets of convicted sex offenders to pay for legal fees, that may ultimately reduce financial compensation available to victims.
answer_text: The Criminal Legal Aid (Recovery of Defence Costs Orders) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2012 provide for Recovery of Defence Costs Orders to be made against legally-aided defendants who have been convicted in the Crown Court. The purpose of such an order is to recover such part of the cost of legal aid incurred under the criminal aid certificate as is reasonable in all the circumstances of the case, including the financial resources of the defendant. It is in the public interest that legal aid costs should be recovered in cases where convicted defendants have adequate means to pay their defence costs. It is for the Crown Court to decide whether an order should be made and the sum which should be recovered on the basis of the information and evidence available. All sums recovered through a Recovery of Defence Costs Orders are treated as a receipt which the Legal Services Agency will use to fund further legal aid cases.The order must be sought before the Crown Court hearing the case. There is a narrow window in which to make an application for the recovery of defence costs and in that time the issue of whether victims will secure damages or the amount of those damages may not have been determined. In individual cases, the convicted defendant may not be the only mark for damages, and the granting of an order may not in fact be prejudicial to victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he will take to encourage Irish Traditional Music on St Patrick's Day, including any allocated funding.
answer_text: My Department and several of its arm’s-length bodies support events and initiatives associated with St. Patrick’s Day.  Music, which I encourage in its many genres, is an integral part of the celebrations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the (i) Lissummon Road; and (ii) Tyrone's Ditches roads, Newry, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: Details of all planned resurfacing schemes are included in my Department’s annual report to Council.In the meantime my Department will continue to undertake routine inspections and actionable defects will be prioritised for repair in accordance with the Limited Service Policy in so far as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the article in the Irish Times article on 21 January 2026, which stated that economic inequality is worsening in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Irish Times article cited recent analysis by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) as part of its January 2026 Economic Outlook. This analysis suggests that GVA per capita in Belfast increased relative to the regional average between 1998 and 2023. UUEPC also noted that disparities between council areas in terms of employment rates have not changed significantly over time. The report also highlights stronger evidence of convergence on particular indicators, such as regional wages. This is supported by recent data from the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings. Between 2024 and 2025, the regional median earnings gap between the highest and lowest earning council area fell by two percentage points. I am committed to delivering a more regionally balanced economy. The Sub-Regional Economic Plan, published in October 2024, provides the strategic framework to support this. As part of this Plan, 11 Local Economic Partnerships have been established to identify barriers to growth and develop Action Plans tailored to the circumstances and key issues of local areas. A growing number of Action Plans have been approved or are nearing approval. To support delivery, my department has allocated a £45 million Regional Balance Fund over three years. This will allow councils to invest directly in projects that reflect local priorities. The approval process for this Fund was completed in May 2025 with further investment profiled through to March 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Hillhead Road, Newry, will be (i) reconstructed; and (ii) reopened.
answer_text: My Department issued legal proceedings against the three previous quarry owners in early 2009 and has recently been advised that these proceedings have concluded. Officials are considering options to address the current situation noting the concerns raised by residents about the creation of a cul-de-sac and turning area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Irish Times headline on 28 January 2026, which stated that economic inequality is worsening in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time it takes to validate (i) minor; and (ii) major, planning applications, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The information that you have requested is contained in the table below.Average (mean) days to validation for planning applications received between 1 October 2024 and 30 September 2025.Planning authorityLocal applicationsMajor applicationsAntrim & Newtownabbey5.010.0Ards & North Down6.01.0Armagh Banbridge & Craigavon8.010.0Belfast8.011.0Causeway Coast & Glens5.07.0Derry & Strabane4.04.0Fermanagh & Omagh4.07.0Lisburn & Castlereagh4.06.0Mid & East Antrim5.07.0Mid Ulster1.02.0Newry Mourne & Down17.010.0

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why her Department declined a Freedom of Information request by Newry.ie for a copy of the findings report from the DfI Newry Southern Relief Road public consultation.
answer_text: I can advise that my department received an FOI request from Newry.ie on 7 January 2026 seeking a copy of the Findings Report of the DfI Newry Southern Relief Road Consultation with a response issuing on 30 January 2026.In line with legislation, officials determined that the information requested fell under the Environmental Regulations 2004 (EIR) and was processed accordingly.Officials confirmed that information related to the request was held by the Department.  However, the requested information consisted of internal communications and related to material in the course of completion.  Such information is excepted from the duty to disclose under EIR  12(4)(d) and 12(4)(e). A public interest test was undertaken to determine whether this information was suitable for release to the public, as required by the EIR. The public interest in disclosure was fully considered, including the Regulation 12(2) presumption in favour of disclosure.  However, given the recency of the creation of the information, and the ongoing review and internal discussions taking place, it was felt the public interest was weighted in favour of withholding the information at this time, in order to protect the safe space required to ensure fully formed, robust decision making.  As you are aware, the public consultation exercise for the Newry Southern Relief Road concluded on 4th March 2025. When finalised, a copy will be published on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the findings in the Assembly Research and Information Service briefing paper, Planning Decision Times - comparison across the UK and Ireland, tabled to the Committee for Infrastructure on 4 February 2026, which outlines the poor application processing times of planning applications in the north, in comparison to everywhere else in the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware of the RaISE paper recently discussed at the Committee for Infrastructure meeting on 4 February 2026 and would agree with the assessment provided, that comparisons in terms of how planning processing times are measured and performance reported between each jurisdiction’s planning regimes is difficult. It should however, also be recognised that there are  significant differences between jurisdictions in relation to how each planning system works both in political and administrative contexts.My Department regularly liaises with counterparts in Britain and in the South to share best practice, discuss and understand the difficulties faced across planning systems, and assess how improvements can be made. Departmental officials have also been working closely with local planning authorities through the Planning Improvement Programme to identify potential barriers and blockages within the planning process in order to improve performance. Much good work has been done to date however, I recognise that this is a continuous process, and to this end my Department will continue to work with counterparts in other jurisdictions, with local government and all key stakeholders to enhance the planning system and improve planning performance  in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the planning system is a significant impediment to timely and efficient development across various sectors, from housing to critical infrastructure.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the challenges facing various sectors and acknowledge the important role the planning system plays in supporting the delivery of sustainable development across the North. My Department continues to work in partnership with local government, who are responsible for the determination of over 99% of planning applications, and key stakeholders through the planning improvement agenda with the aim of enhancing the performance and long-term effectiveness of the planning system in the North. The measures and initiatives in the planning improvement agenda aim to improve all aspects of the system, including the local development plan and development management processes, to help ensure development is facilitated as efficiently and effectively as possible.  Key areas of focus include changes to secondary legislation; measures to improve to speed and efficiency of local development plan production; strengthening the planning workforce; improving the statutory consultee process; working with NILGA to support elected members in their important decision-making role; and the development and delivery of the Independent Inspectors project to provide assistance and support to the work of the Planning Appeals Commission. While timeliness remains important in development management, I also recognise the importance of delivering high-quality planning outcomes that positively impact communities, the environment, and the region’s long-term vision.Whilst good progress has been made to date, it is important to acknowledge that many of the measures and initiatives will take time to bed in and for sustainable improvement in system performance to be realised. It is notable that average processing times for major planning applications in 2024/25 have significantly improved, reaching their lowest point since planning powers were transferred in 2015. This is in addition to improving statutory consultee response times, as 77% were responded to on time, that is within 21 calendar days or within an agreed extended timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of having spinal muscular atrophy included in newborn screening, to ensure children have the same level of testing as children in Scotland, the Republic of Ireland, and children partaking in the NHS pilot in England.
answer_text: Screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations of the UK National Screening Committee, an independent committee which advises the four UK Health Departments on all screening matters. The UK National Screening Committee does not currently recommend screening for SMA in newborns. In the summer of 2025, the UK National Screening Committee published documents reviewing the evidence on newborn screening for SMA. This evidence concluded that an In-Service Evaluation was required to answer remaining questions about whether screening would work well, how much it would cost, and whether it would be effective in everyday practice.An In-service Evaluation (ISE) is being carried out by NHS England, a research study funded by the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR). A separate evaluation is being carried out in Scotland. Planning for the ISE is currently underway and a representative from Northern Ireland sits on the Partnership Board which is overseeing the planning of the ISE. Additionally, my Department has informed the NIHR that Northern Ireland would wish to be a participant in any ongoing research, if there is an opportunity for us to do so.Once these evaluations are completed, the UK National Screening Committee will review the evidence gathered and make a recommendation for all 4 UK countries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the potholes on Dominic Street, Newry, will be repaired.
answer_text: I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern.The winter period has taken a severe toll on the road network. To put the current situation in context, 49,000 defects have been recorded in the last three months alone, which is close to half the total for the whole of the previous year.Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term. However, in addition to securing the additional funding being announced today, I have also asked my officials to also explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.Maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make-safe defects by working to address the highest priority defects as fast as possible, in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worst affected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the merits of providing businesses with incentives to employ young people, and adults, with special educational needs.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of helping people who experience barriers to employment, including those with special educational needs, to access and sustain employment. The supports that individuals and employers need are wide and varied. My department therefore provides a range of support to businesses through programmes such as Workable (NI) and JobStart, including subsidised wage incentives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to address the 229 potholes reported in South Armagh, outlined by the South Armagh Business Improvement Group.
answer_text: I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. In addition to the 40 resurfacing schemes progressing until the end of the financial year from my December monitoring allocation. I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern.The winter period has taken a severe toll on the road network. To put the current situation in context, 49,000 defects have been recorded in the last three months alone, which is close to half the total for the whole of the previous year.Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term. However, in addition to securing the additional funding being announced today, I have also asked my officials to also explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worst affected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated cost for upgrading Newry's wastewater infrastructure, to unlock housing and economic development, over the next 10 years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water is in the process of developing its PC28 business plan, aimed at securing the necessary funding to maintain and enhance Northern Ireland’s water and wastewater services up to 2033. This plan encompasses vital investments to support housing and economic growth while protecting and improving the environment, both of which are closely interconnected.The projected financial requirement for addressing Newry’s future environmental and economic needs includes £114 million for a comprehensive upgrade of the wastewater treatment works and an additional £50 million to tackle storm overflow issues.As I have previously announced, a £26 million phase one investment is planned and funded within the current PC21 price control period to reduce pollution and expand development capacity within the existing system for a minimum of five years based on the current level of development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Armagh Road and Keady Road, Newtownhamilton, will be repaired.
answer_text: My Department has completed three significant resurfacing schemes in Newtownhamilton this financial year. 0.5km of resurfacing was undertaken through the centre of Netownhamilton village on Dundalk Street.  Schemes along the A29 Armagh Road and A29 Keady Road delivered resurfacing totalling 5,500m2 Planned drainage works on the A29 Darkley Road are currently ongoing.  These works must be completed before patching work on the A29 Darkley Road and also the B31 Newtownhamilton to Armagh Road can commence in the coming weeks.  Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term. However, in addition to securing the additional funding, I have also asked my officials to explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.Officials will continue to undertake inspections and actionable defects will be instructed for repair in line with my Department’s current Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of establishing an all island Saint Brigid’s Day bank holiday.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans to (i) extend; or (ii) provide, a dedicated Special Educational Needs Provision in Darkley, County Armagh.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Darkley Primary School engaged with the SEN Capacity Team in May 2025 to explore the feasibility of establishing specialist provision on site. During this process, the school identified an existing single modular classroom that they believed could be repurposed. Following a scoping visit by EA officers, it was concluded that the modular unit was unsuitable due to the absence of toilet facilities and the lack of essential support spaces.  In addition, the wider site was assessed and deemed unsuitable because of its limited size and restricted access.The SEN Capacity Team continues to monitor the demand for specialist provision in the area.  To support further consideration of potential solutions, the EA is currently engaging with the Council regarding the possible acquisition of land adjacent to Darkley Primary School.  Securing this land could create opportunities for accommodation options that were previously not viable.The EA remains in ongoing communication with the principal regarding specialist provision and the needs of the surrounding area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any road safety measures she plans to install on the Newtownhamilton Road, Ballymacnab, between the Ballymacnab Chapel and O’Toole's Pub.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent fatality in this area and after visiting the location, I have asked my officials to undertake a number of assessments in the area related to street lighting, footway links, pedestrian safety measures at bus stops and the existing warning signs and road markings.My officials will work with the PSNI in relation to the investigation currently ongoing and should further engineering measures be required, these will be added to a future works programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans or proposals to increase Special Educational Needs provision in Armagh City, including the future expansion of Lisanally Special School.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Armagh City and surrounding area experienced an unprecedented rise in demand for special school places over the past number of years leading to accommodation pressures and a reduction in available spaces in Lisanally Special School.  This lack of capacity within Lisanally Special School required specialist provision classes being established within a local mainstream school, for pupils with a special school profile.   The EA is actively exploring sites near Lisanally Special School, which may present a unique and strategic opportunity for expansion. Our strategic vision is to extend the existing school estate, enabling the development of additional classrooms and specialist facilities without the need for relocation or disruption to current operations.This proposed expansion would not only alleviate the current shortage of teaching spaces but also ensure the school is equipped to accommodate projected enrolment growth. Furthermore, it would facilitate the reinstatement of a range of facilities, enabling pupils to access the full spectrum of services necessary to meet their individual needs.The EA is aware of a continuing demand for special educational needs provision in Armagh City and surrounding area.  The EA is engaging with schools in Armagh City to expand or establish specialist provision with a view to providing accommodation where feasible.  To date there are 2 capital schemes in planning to create additional specialist provision at primary schools within the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is adopting to increase safety for staff and patients in healthcare settings.
answer_text: The Department implemented the new Management of Violence and Aggression in the Workplace (MOVA) HSC Framework in December 2023.  This framework outlines the HSC’s commitment, developed in partnership with staff representatives, to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace.  It also ensures that the associated structures, policies and support systems are in place to enable staff to work safely. Since its launch, all HSC organisations and key stakeholders have been working to embed the framework’s principles and support local implementation. We are clear that creating a culture of zero tolerance towards violence and aggression in the workplace is not a one-off action, but a sustained commitment. Every member of staff has the right to feel safe and respected at work, and we will continue to take all necessary steps to protect and support our workforce.My Department is currently working with Trusts to see how to best gather data in a meaningful way to identify any trends in relation to violence and aggression.I am meeting with the Minister for Justice on 10 March 2026 to discuss the issue of prosecutions in relation to violence against HSC staff. HSC staff currently report violent incidents directly to the PSNI, in line with the Regional HSC Management of Violence and Aggression Framework. The Public Prosecution Service and the Courts are responsible for taking forward prosecutions and their outcomes.  I am exploring options to obtain data on prosecutions arising from violent incidents involving HSC workers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who is responsible for flooding and drainage issues in Ulster Villas, Newry.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department is responsible for the maintenance and cleaning of gullies and associated road drainage systems on the adopted road network.Ulster Villas, Newry is a private development and does not form part of the adopted road network.  As such the Department does not undertake maintenance at this location and responsibility lies with the developer and/or property owners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the works her Department is undertaking to address the flooding on the railway lines, preventing the Enterprise services travelling between Belfast and Newry.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink proactively manages the impacts of weather-related events to all services across the network, including the Enterprise through weather management policies and safety measures taken in advance of severe weather events, including high rainfall and storms. Measures taken include forecast and real-time weather monitoring, advance drainage clearance, additional inspections, speed restrictions, and lastly closures depending on the severity of on-site conditions. This approach allows alternative transport arrangements to be put in place, including bus substitutions for cross border services. During Storm Chandra, flooding which impacted the Enterprise train service occurred predominantly at two known locations, Grants Bridge, near Jonesborough and Brackagh Bog, south of Portadown. Translink is currently investigating drainage improvements at Grants Bridge through a capital project to reduce the potential for future flooding at this site. This project is currently at feasibility stage with outcomes expected in spring 2026. Translink is currently undertaking a feasibility study at Brackagh Bog. This study will examine the risks of future flooding and will provide design options and construction costs to improve the resilience of this section of track to future flooding events. Any option which rectifies the flooding problem at this location is likely to be of high cost, requiring extensive track raising on poor ground conditions.  The risk of flooding network wide is also being assessed through an ongoing Climate Change Adaptation Strategy, which is due for completion in May 2026. This strategy will advise on future risks and the need for enhanced strategic investment to adapt and improve railway infrastructure, making sites such as Brackagh Bog more resilient for flooding events such as those encountered in 2023 during Storm Ciaran and 2026 during Storm Chandra.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of (i) the nature of accommodation for planning staff in the Newry, Mourne and Down office; and (ii) how this is impacting the working environment and wellbeing of staff.
answer_text: The issues you raise are a matter for Newry Mourne and Down District Council so you may wish to raise these matters directly with the Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to ensure that all manhole covers, that were displaced during Storm Chandra, are replaced and are safe and secure, presenting no danger for both traffic and pedestrians.
answer_text: Storm Chandra brought high winds and significant levels of rainfall in a relatively short period of time and given the extent of the disruption across the road network, it will take time for staff to reinstate all aspects of our infrastructure.Work to clear debris and water from the network is being prioritised, however my officials will address all issues, including the replacement of manhole covers as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the groups that will be protected under new hate crime legislation.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any decision-making criteria and process by which social services and local authorities deem an individual to be lacking mental capacity and decide to place them in a care home; and (ii) any steps taken to ensure the rights and wishes of these individuals are considered, protected, and respected.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA) provides a statutory framework for a person who lacks capacity (P) to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they lack capacity. The Act has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to Deprivation of Liberty (DoL) safeguards, research and money and valuables commenced to date. Under the MCA, a rigorous process is followed to ensure that no individual is deprived of their liberty arbitrarily, in instances which would include a care home. This process includes a capacity assessment, undertaken by a suitably qualified professional and determines whether P is unable to make the specific decision about their care or treatment arrangements due to an impairment of, or disturbance in, the functioning of the mind or brain. The assessment is decisionspecific and timespecific, requiring consideration of P’s ability to understand, retain, and use or weigh relevant information, as well as to communicate their decision by any means. A finding of incapacity must be clearly evidenced and cannot be presumed.A Best Interests determination must also be completed. This process requires practitioners to identify the option that is least restrictive of P’s rights and freedoms and most consistent with their welfare and autonomy. Crucially, the Act requires meaningful consideration of P’s past and present wishes, feelings, beliefs, and values. Where possible, P should be involved in the decision-making process, even when they lack capacity to make the final decision themselves.Prevention of Serious Harm is a further vital safeguard that must be in place. This requires the person carrying out the detention amounting to a DoL to believe that failure to detain P would create a risk of serious harm to P, or risk of serious physical harm to others. In addition, the likelihood of harm and the seriousness of the harm must be proportionate to the detention. P’s nominated person must be consulted as part of the decision-making process, ensuring that family or other trusted individuals have the opportunity to contribute relevant knowledge about P's views and preferences.The proposed DoL must be scrutinised and authorised by a Trust Panel, which evaluates the evidence, confirms compliance with statutory requirements, and ensures that the deprivation is necessary, proportionate, and lawful. Collectively, these steps create a rigorous system of protections to uphold individual rights and prevent arbitrary deprivation of liberty.Emergency provisions are also in place should there be a need for P to be deprived of their liberty before these safeguards are in place, or to establish if they are already in place. In these instances, there must be a reasonable belief that P lacks capacity, a reasonable belief that the act is in their best interests and that the risk of harm by not depriving them of their liberty until these safeguards are i

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of how the continued closure of bank branches on high streets is impacting older people.
answer_text: Whilst banking and financial services are reserved issues, it is well documented that bank branch closures have considerable adverse impact on older people, especially amongst the digitally excluded, financially vulnerable, or those living in rural communities.Consumer Council research in 2023 into the impact of branch closures found that amongst older consumers, 54% said that closures had a major impact on them or their community, whilst the Financial Conduct Authority reported in 2024 that consumers over 65 are more likely to use face-to-face, in-branch services. Many older people find it more difficult to withdraw cash following a branch closure.Whilst post offices can offer an alternative for “over the counter” banking services, most are not designed to meet banking needs and lack adequate privacy as they are often located in supermarkets or convenience stores.While mitigations such as Banking Hubs and Post Offices are helpful, the loss of physical banking points present significant challenges to navigate for older citizens who depend on inperson support for everyday financial management.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department financially supports the hospitality industry, that serves as a cornerstone for town centres, villages, rural communities, and tourism.
answer_text: I recently allocated an additional £300k to support marketing of the Hidden Heartlands campaign which has been extended to cover Fermanagh. This investment gives local tourism and hospitality businesses a powerful promotional platform on the global stage. My Department also committed funding for a late-night public transport pilot scheme in Belfast. Initial feedback on uptake is positive and my hope is that this pilot will lead to advancements in late-night transport provision across our region, thereby supporting our night-time economy and hospitality businesses.My Department and partner organisations continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.I remain committed to engaging with key stakeholders as we seek to develop a thriving and sustainable hospitality sector. I recently met with Hospitality Ulster and NI Food to Go Association to discuss their proposals for a VAT reduction pilot scheme. You will be aware the Minister of Finance has paused the Reval2026 process, allaying some of the immediate financial concerns of local businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the RTÉ article on 29 January 2026, which states 36,000 new homes were completed in the south in 2025; and (ii) to detail the number of new homes completed in the north in 2025.
answer_text: I have made no assessment of the RTÉ article.Details of the number of homes completed across Northern Ireland are publicly accessible on the DoF website:https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/new-dwelling-statistics-detailed-reports

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the three Community Treatment Centres in Lisburn, Banbridge, and Newry, as proposed in 2013.
answer_text: The Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre (PCCC) opened to the public in February 2023. The facility is a ‘one stop location’ for the community’s health and social care needs.  It is situated on the Lagan Valley Hospital site and provides state of the art accommodation for seven GP Practices and a wide range of primary and community care services, many of which were relocated from the existing Lisburn Health Centre and other Trust premises.  The £40m centre was developed using a 3PD (Third Party Development) model.The Community Care and Treatment centre in Banbridge has been fully operational since April 2016. The Trust awaits the outworkings of the Executive agreement on the budget and subsequent Minister of Health prioritisation of projects for funding to enable progress with the development of business case for the Newry Treatment and Care Centre. The Department has undertaken significant engagement with the Southern Health & Social Care Trust (SHSCT) on the development of the Outline Business Case for the Newry Community Treatment and Care Centre. Regrettably, there remains concern about the absence of definitive plans for the space that was originally earmarked for GP Practices. The estimated capital cost of the project is £100 million. The ability to commit to funding to capital projects, will depend on the Department’s capital allocation from the ongoing Budget process covering the years 2026/27 – 2029/30.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the constituency with the highest number of potholes.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has created significant challenges in our ability to maintain the road network in a safe condition. My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas, rather than by Constituency. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects reported and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.My department regularly publishes information in relation to reports of potholes which is made available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.The table below provides detail on the number of potholes reported in each of the last four financial years broken down by section office.  My Department only holds records of reported potholes and defects, therefore our figures don’t reflect the full level of potholes across the North.
