question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her assessment of the impact of reported staffing pressures being experienced by her Department’s line painting contractors in the Southern Division; and (ii) any steps she is taking to ensure scheduled works will proceed without hindrance.
answer_text: Officials are not aware of any issues with the delivery of road marking work undertaken by line painting contractors in Southern Division.Road marking work is generally undertaken during the spring and summer months when the weather is more favourable, however any safety critical road marking replacements have been issued for action and completed by the contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any resources available to aid cross-border remote workers in navigating different tax system laws.
answer_text: I recognise the challenges faced by cross-border workers, and my department has previously raised this issue with both HMRC and the Irish Government, pressing on what more can be done to facilitate flexible cross-border working in the all-island economy.My officials raised this matter again with HMRC as the organisation responsible for tax administration in the North.  HMRC indicated that advice for cross-border workers can be found in HMRC online manuals - in particular, for those working on the island of Ireland -  EIM43000 - Residence or employment in the Irish Republic: contents - HMRC internal manual - GOV.UK. More general guidance for cross border workers is also available at: Globally mobile employees - GOV.UK. Further guidance and links to additional resources to support cross-border workers can be found online at the Border People website - https://borderpeople.info/a-z/tax-frontier-workers.html.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the figures in an Irish News article on 28 January 2026 which states that less than one in five suspected heart attack and stroke patients in Northern Ireland get an ambulance in target time, compared to almost half across the border.
answer_text: While I acknowledge the concerns raised by the figures in the Irish News article of 28 January 2026, direct comparison between the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and the National Ambulance Service is not straightforward. Both services operate within fundamentally different healthcare systems, with distinct emergency department (ED) structures, patient pathways and access arrangements. These systemic differences have a significant bearing on measured response times and limit the validity of a likeforlike comparison.In Northern Ireland, suspected heart attack calls are categorised as Category 2, for which the anticipated response time is 18 minutes. NIAS has been open and transparent about its continued challenges in meeting this target. The single greatest factor affecting performance remains delayed hospital handovers at EDs. Although the expected handover time is 15 minutes, EDs are regularly unable to accept ambulance patients within that timeframe due to pressures across the acute hospital system. As a result, ambulances can be tied up outside hospitals for prolonged periods.These delays reduce NIAS’ operational capacity by an estimated 26% over the past year alone. This represents a substantial loss of frontline availability and directly impacts the ability to respond promptly to subsequent emergency calls. Tackling these handover delays is therefore critical to improving response times.The root of this issue lies in wider patientflow challenges within the health and social care system. Difficulties in discharging medically fit patients into community and social care settings reduce bed availability, which in turn slows movement out of EDs, leading to congestion throughout the system. All Trusts committed in late 2025 to accelerating improvements in handovers, and while early gains were achieved, maintaining those improvements has proved difficult. Evidence from elsewhere in the UK clearly demonstrates that improved hospital flow leads to improved ambulance response performance across all categories.NIAS continues to prioritise emergency calls to ensure that the most clinically urgent cases such as suspected heart attack, acute stroke, major trauma and timecritical interhospital transfers receive the fastest possible response. Senior paramedics within the Emergency Operations Centre also review Category 2 calls in real time to identify those requiring immediate intervention.My Department fully recognises the serious and sustained pressures placed on EDs, hospitals and community care services. Through the Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, we have taken a series of targeted actions to improve patient flow and reduce the pressures that contribute to ambulance delays. These actions include:Strengthening sevenday discharge services: Trusts have increased early senior clinical review, discharge co-ordination teams and allied health professional capacity to ensure timely decisionmaking and reduce avoidable delays;Expanding capacity outsid

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding resources available to aid in developing a community-led mental health safe space in Armagh, that will provide peer support, group activities, wellbeing sessions and signposting to other services
answer_text: The Department does not have any open funding programmes available to assist in the development of a community-led mental health safe space in Armagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the current amount of investment for postgraduate training provided by his Department, broken down by (a) medical; (b) nursing; and (c) allied health professions; and (ii) for his assessment of the sufficiency of this level of funding.
answer_text: The answer to parts (a), (b) and (c), in respect of the current financial year, is provided in the table belowPostgraduate ProgrammeEducation and Training Budget Medical£17,301,261Nursing and Midwifery£10,365,831 Allied Health Professions£965,345 I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the strategic workforce reviews and other commissioning groups in relation to each of these areas and will continue to make the case for essential long-term funding of pre and post-registration education and training programmes, to help deliver the optimum workforce. Whilst budgetary constraints have not allowed my Department to meet all identified training needs in full, I am committed to making the case for the necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to increase the numbers of undergraduates enrolled in radiography courses.
answer_text: I fully recognise the important contribution of diagnostic and therapeutic radiographers to the health and social care system.My Department’s Workforce Review Report Diagnostic Radiography 2019 – 2029 envisaged the annual provision of 90 undergraduate commissioned training places by 2023/24. The comparable figure from the Workforce Review Report Therapeutic Radiography 2019 – 2029 was 31 annual undergraduate commissioned training places by 2025/26.Department for the Economy funding has allowed us to increase annual undergraduate commissioned training places for diagnostic radiography to 94 from 2024/25 to 2027/28. I have also been able to increase therapeutic radiography undergraduate commissioned training places by 9, from 16 in 2024/25 to 25 in 2025/26.I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the strategic workforce reviews that have been accepted by my Department and published on its website and will continue to make the case for essential long-term funding of education and training programmes, to help deliver the optimum workforce.My Department is also exploring alternative training pathways into a career in diagnostic radiography, as a means of further strengthening the registered workforce. A pilot diagnostic radiography apprenticeship programme began on 26 January 2026, with six apprentices employed across the HSC. On its conclusion in June 2027, an assessment of the viability of apprenticeship programmes as an alternative training pathway into a career in diagnostic radiography will be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department's has to invest in stereotactic ablative radiotherapy technology, to help achieve better patient outcomes with fewer side effects for cancer radiotherapy treatment.
answer_text: Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR) is available in the Northern Ireland Cancer Centre (NICC) located within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.  Due to its specialist nature, it is not available in all hospitals across Northern Ireland however patients can be referred to the NICC for evaluation and treatment.  Initially used primarily for lung cancer, SABR is now applied to a wider range of cancers. This expansion has been supported by ongoing research and clinical trials demonstrating its efficacy in treating various tumour types.Introducing and implementing new radiotherapy techniques and technology in line with national guidance including staffing and associated training is included as an action within the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy published in 2022.  The implementation of SABR at the North West Cancer Centre is included among several service developments aimed at introducing new radiotherapy techniques and technology.  The scale and pace of funding identified will determine the order in which these can be realised.  Due to the lack of identified funding source a specific timeline for the implementation of SABR at the NWCC cannot be provided at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to engage with health professionals and trade unions regarding fair pay as early in the fiscal year as possible, in order to ensure that a skilled healthcare workforce can be retained.
answer_text: I remain committed to early and constructive engagement with health professionals and trade unions on fair pay, recognising that retaining a skilled and dedicated workforce is essential.My Department’s engagement with stakeholders in the development of the third action plan for the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy identified the need to develop a multi-disciplinary staff retention framework for the Health and Social Care workforce.  The development of this framework is scheduled to commence in March 2026. Through an examination of the factors influencing staff retention at each stage of the career pathway across all Health and Social Care roles, the framework will offer employers a comprehensive resource to assist in their provision of targeted initiatives aimed at strengthening retention within the existing workforce.The NHS Pay Review Body has recommended a 3.3% consolidated uplift for all Agenda for Change pay points from 1 April 2026, and we expect the Doctors and Dentists Review Body recommendations in early April. England and Wales have already confirmed they will implement the NHSPRB recommendation, and, subject to budgetary clarity, it is my intention to do likewise for AfC staff in 2026/27.I am fully committed to ensuring that pay will be the first priority for my Department and I and my officials continue to engage regularly with Trade Unions on delivering a pay award as early as possible in the new financial year, most recently on 16 February. I also remain fully committed to progress towards HSC becoming a Real Living Wage Employer and my officials are working  closely with Trade unions to agree a pathway towards this before the end of March 2026.Whilst it is my firm ambition that staff should receive their pay uplift as early as possible in the incoming financial year, I am fully aware that I can only deliver on those commitments when clear about my budgetary position. However, even in the absence of any agreement, I have asked my officials to progress the necessary preparatory work at pace.While my ambition is to deliver pay uplifts as early as possible, this is dependent on a confirmed budget. In the meantime, officials have been asked to progress all preparatory work at pace. The draft budget under consultation would present significant financial challenges for HSC, underscoring the need for a multi-year budget to provide stability and support long-term planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made an assessment of investment in (i) undergraduate training places and education in radiography; (ii) postgraduate education in radiography; and (iii) capital equipment for both diagnostic and therapeutic radiography, should the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30 be put in place.
answer_text: (i) and (ii) Once any future budget has been confirmed I will be considering proposals from my officials on investment in this and the range of other healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department. This will include liaison with professional colleagues, and in relation to Radiography, consideration of any outstanding recommendations from my Department’s Workforce Review Report Diagnostic Radiography 2019 – 2029.(iii) The Department has assessed capital investment needs for diagnostic radiography equipment through the Capital Subgroup of the Regional Medical Imaging Board, which monitors the age, use and configuration of imaging equipment across Health and Social Care (HSC) and maintains a regional inventory of current and projected needs. This work, including estimated project costs prioritised by demand and available budget, has informed the capital imaging bids which were included within my Department’s response to the 2026-29/30 Budget Exercise.  A recent strategic review of regional radiotherapy services found that existing equipment will meet demand until 2040 with only routine replacement, and that no additional linear accelerators (LINACs) are required unless demand grows significantly or new treatment methods change service needs.  Bids for the replacement of LINACs used in therapeutic radiography at the Northwest Cancer Centre and Belfast City Hospital were also included in the Department’s requirements.The requirements outlined above were included as part of the £3.0billion in capital funding requirements identified by my Department in its Budget submission to the Department of Finance in September 2025.Should the draft Budget capital allocations published by the Finance Minister be confirmed at final Budget, it would be expected that there would be sufficient capital budget available to the Department of Health (DoH) to fund the replacement of diagnostic imaging equipment and linear accelerators to maintain the delivery of services.  This would depend on no reduction being subsequently applied to the DoH capital budget each year as well as the scale of emerging competing pressures.  The proposals set out by the Finance Minister would not allow new diagnostic projects to be taken forward over the next four years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of patients currently diagnosed with all cancer types waiting on open pathways, with red flag referral without a decision to treat at (i) 0 to 62; (ii) 63 to 104; (iii) 105 to 146; and (iv) over 146 days since red flag referral.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of timely information on cancer pathways. However, information on cancer waiting times is derived from administrative datasets that are live operational systems. These data are subject to ongoing validation and change as patient pathways progress, clinical decisions are made, and data quality checks are completed.Individual patient pathways may be paused, closed, or reclassified as diagnostic investigations proceed, meaning figures can change daily and retrospectively.  As a result, it is not possible to provide a definitive or static figure for the total number of patients currently on open pathways following a red flag referral without a decision to treat, at a single point in time, with sufficient accuracy. The Department routinely publishes validated cancer waiting times statistics, which provide the most reliable and quality-assured information available and should be used for monitoring performance at population level.            As outlined previously, it is not possible to accurately provide the current total number of patients currently diagnosed with cancer who have received a decision to treat and are waiting for treatment as these data are derived from live administrative systems that are subject to ongoing change and validation.However, my Department publishes validated cancer waiting times statistics, including performance against the 31-day decision-to-treat standard, which provide the most reliable and quality-assured information available for monitoring cancer treatment timeliness.The target for time to start treatment following a decision to treat is that, ‘at least 98% of patients urgently referred with a suspected cancer should begin their first definitive treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat. The latest accredited Official statistics published in 15th January 2026 report that:In the quarter ending September 2025, 2,841 patients started their first definitive treatment, 9.0% (234) more than in the previous quarter (2,607).   88.0% (2,499) of those patients started treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat, compared with 87.9% (2,291) in the previous quarter.Reference: https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/health/ni-cancer-waiting-times-jul-sep-25.html

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the road defects on Main Avenue, Derrybeg, Newry, will be (i) mapped; and (ii) repaired or resurfaced.
answer_text: I can confirm that resurfacing and associated drainage work at Main Avenue Derrybeg, Newry, began on 25th March 2026 and should be complete by mid-May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of reports of GP practices only accepting emergency, same-day appointments until March 2026.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients. This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system are determined locally by the GP practice. It determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.My officials are unaware of any reports of GP practices only accepting emergency, same-day appointments until March 2026 and there has been no direction regarding the same.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of building a multi-story car park for Daisy Hill Hospital staff, in order to prevent overspill parking blocking access and parking for neighbouring houses.
answer_text: The aim of the Department’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to determine to enable them to take local issues into account.The Trust has considered the feasibility of adding multistorey car parking. However, significant capital investment would be required to realise this project.In the last 2 years, the Southern HSC Trust has increased car parking provision adjacent to Daisy Hill Hospital with the acquisition of the Monaghan Row site which provides an additional 75 parking spaces and the creation of a 60 space car park on Clanrye Avenue. The Trust continues to develop options to help encourage a reduction in the demand for onsite car parking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of to what degree dementia (i) prevention; (ii) training; (iii) diagnosis; and (iv) treatment, is being supported.
answer_text: The Regional Dementia Care Pathway 2018 sets out a service model to deliver the vision for high quality dementia care in Northern Ireland.  Prevention, training, diagnosis and treatment are all embedded within the pathway which focus on the entire individual journey.  (i) The Regional Dementia Care Pathway Pillar on “Improving Public Awareness and Healthy Active Ageing” is the foundation for dementia prevention. It highlights promoting healthy lifestyles (e.g., physical activity, social engagement) that can reduce risk.At a population level, health improvement programmes delivered by the PHA, promote healthy ageing by supporting regular physical activity, balanced nutrition, smoking cessation and alcohol reduction, all of which improve cardiovascular and cerebrovascular health, both of which are key determinants of dementia risk. NI Direct is the Governments regional information and services directory. The website provides Health and Wellbeing advice on health conditions including dementia, covering such topics as Help & Support, Living Well with Dementia, Signs of Dementia and Supporting Someone with Dementia. Dementia NI regularly delivers awareness-raising campaigns and educational events, which help inform the public about dementia and potentially risk factors. Alzheimer’s Society’s Local Dementia Strategy Recommendations include a “Preventing Well” section advocating clear, evidence-based actions for dementia risk reduction.(ii) My Department, established a Regional Dementia Project Board in May 2024 to bring a sharpened focus on improving dementia care across the region.  There currently is a workstream dedicated to Education, Training and Research in line with the dementia care pathway. It has developed training on dementia awareness and training on intimacy and relationships. These are available to all health and social care staff.  Currently work is being completed to update the Regional Dementia Learning and Development Framework 2016.In 2025, Ulster University launched Time for Dementia, a programme pairing nursing student with families living with an individual who has dementia, to deepen the understanding of the lived experience and strengthening future workforce capability.(iii)  Each Health and Social Care Trust has a memory service/pathway that provides assessment, diagnosis, treatment, education, and multidisciplinary support. Dementia Navigators operate in each Trust to coordinate care and guide families through services. Post-diagnostic support includes befriending, advocacy, education programmes, and GP-led management of co-morbidities. The Apps4Dementia library provides safe, quality-assured digital tools for symptom management and daily living.Dementia NI and Alzheimer’s Society offer a wide range of peer support, empowerment groups, and campaigning initiatives to address stigma and support wellbeing. It is recognised that whilst some improvements to the current dementia care pathway can be made through some service rede

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of all healthcare staff who are working with senior citizens, having dementia training and a Virtual Dementia Tour Mobile perspective.
answer_text: Dementia training equips healthcare staff with the understanding, empathy, and the practical skills needed to provide safe, effective and person-centred care to individuals living with dementia. Dementia training including immersive tools like the virtual dementia tour bus helps staff understand the physical and sensory changes that older adults with dementia face. This training exposes staff to altered vision, sound, touch, and cognitive overload, helping them appreciate how everyday tasks can become difficult or overwhelming.Northern Ireland has actively used the virtual dementia tour bus to train staff, families, and policymakers. It has visited Stormont multiple times since 2022, with the South Eastern Trust hosting it four times in four years, training over 2,000 people including MLAs, Trust staff and Alzheimer's Society colleagues. Northern Ireland Social care council, (NISCC) in partnership with the Public Health Agency, brought the virtual dementia tour bus to all five Health & Social Care Trust areas. These sessions provided staff with hands-on immersive dementia training.My Department, remains committed to improving training on dementia. The Regional Dementia Project Board has a dedicated workstream that focuses on education, training and research. They have updated the Dementia Awareness training for all health and social care staff which will be available from February 2026.In addition, my Department funded and produced Training on Sexuality, Intimacy and Relationships - A Right’s Based Approach for People Living with Dementia in collaboration with Queens university. This is now available to all health and social care staff within the HSCLearn training platform.The Dementia Learning and Development Framework, 2016 is currently being updated and outlines the core themes in terms of the knowledge and skills that health and social care staff require to interact and respond sensitively to the needs of people living with a dementia

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have had a diagnosis of dementia, in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts do not routinely report on the number of people diagnosed with dementia. NISRA data reports that in 2024 there were 13,900 people diagnosed with dementia. The Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system, Encompass. As the Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, the data generated within the context of service delivery is subject to a process of extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data that Trusts are able to submit for reporting purposes.My Departmental officials have convened a regional Dementia Encompass Group that meet fortnightly. The key objective of the group will include a focus upon how to standardise and streamline data reporting across the region and how to make best use of data reported in respect of dementia to shape service improvements. The work of this group will also focus on what dementia data is required across the region to ensure that the data can be collated and retrieved from Encompass and employed to assist in future commissioning activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any information his Department provides to the public to help them understand the lifestyle choices that could reduce the risk of dementia.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) in Northern Ireland is the regional body responsible for improving the health and wellbeing of the population and tackling health inequalities. It works across government, health services and local communities to support better public health outcomes.Health Improvement is one of the core functions of the PHA. This work focuses on helping people and communities to live healthier lives by addressing the causes of poor health and supporting positive changes in behaviour and environment.  At a population level, health improvement programmes promote healthy ageing by supporting regular physical activity, balanced nutrition, smoking cessation and alcohol reduction, all of which improve cardiovascular and cerebrovascular health, both of which are key determinants of dementia risk. The PHA’s ‘Living Well’ press campaign includes physical activity messaging which mentions dementia. Regular physical activity provides physical and mental health and wellbeing benefits, including having a healthier heart and helping to lower the risk of developing conditions like dementia, Type 2 diabetes, stroke and some cancers. In addition, the PHA commission a wide range of community initiatives that strengthen social connection and help tackle social isolation and loneliness and promote age friendly environments which help to improve cerebrovascular health and wellbeing.NI Direct is the Governments regional information and services directory. The website provides Health and Wellbeing advice on health conditions including dementia, covering such topics as Help & Support, Living Well with Dementia, Signs of Dementia and Supporting Someone with Dementia.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the test and diagnosis process for dementia, in comparison to elsewhere in the United Kingdom and to the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The most widely used and nationally recognised clinical guidance for testing and diagnosis of dementia within the UK and Northern Ireland comes from, NICE Clinical Guideline NG97 (England & Wales).The Republic of Ireland have a Model of Care for Dementia which is their main clinical pathway and care delivery framework.Northern Ireland aligns with the broader UK approach for testing and diagnosis but with its own regional dementia care pathway.  This pathway focuses on the entire individual journey “Finding out if it’s dementia,” living well, coping with changes, and end-of-life care.There are similarities between United Kingdom, (including Northern Ireland) and the Republic of Ireland. They all follow structured diagnostic pathways, starting in primary care and progressing to specialist memory services from first presentation, assessment, diagnosis and post-diagnostic support. This process will involve cognitive tests, bloodwork, and imaging being completed as part of the diagnostic pathway.My Department is involved in a Four Nations dementia forum with other UK and ROI colleagues which promotes collaborative working and the sharing of evidence-based practice across our respective jurisdictions.The Health and Social Care Trusts are part of an All-Ireland Dementia Forum with the purpose of strengthening networks among dementia stakeholders across Ireland, providing a platform for sharing knowledge, expertise, and best practices in dementia care and support, and identifying possible opportunities for joint initiatives and cross-border collaboration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of (i) the extent inadequate water and wastewater infrastructure is stymying housebuilding; and (ii) how this is impacting house prices, in each constituency.
answer_text: (i)	I fully appreciate the impact that wastewater challenges are having on housing and economic growth across the North and that is why I will continue to develop the three pronged approach to help unlock wastewater connections. Firstly, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, my Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill that was recently introduced in the Assembly, will make provision for future regulations on sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, my Department has consulted on options to introduce developer contributions to help fund wastewater infrastructure improvements. Consultation responses are currently being considered, with a view to publishing a Consultation Responses Report in the coming weeks.	Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office, we have provided £30m of ringfenced budget allocations to NI Water to unlock wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North. In addition, my officials are also working collaboratively with colleagues in DAERA and DfC to identify and enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity in areas of housing need, thereby contributing to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy. (ii)	I am aware that when housing demand exceeds supply, it can affect house prices and I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints, to help mitigate any impact that this may have on housing growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the jointly commissioned independent analysis, together with findings of the NI Fiscal Council, that a £2 billion funding gap in capital infrastructure for wastewater, was preventing 6,150 homes being built over the next three years; and (ii) to detail any steps she taken to address these concerns.
answer_text: Since October 2024, my predecessor and I have allocated over £30m capital funding to NI Water to enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity for over 5,300 properties across the North. This is already above the 4,300 that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control period in 2028, and it is only 2026.  The independent analysis and the NI Fiscal Council report propose the introduction of water charges through an increase in the average annual domestic rate.  For the avoidance of doubt, I will not add to the financial burden being faced by people here by imposing any form of water charges. The issues in relation to water and sewerage services are as a legacy of the years of historic underfunding of our public infrastructure from the British government.   I am continuing with my three-pronged approach which includes working with Executive Colleagues to secure appropriate investment for NI Water. I am delivering on this approach, in that I have provided NI Water with over £500m of public money to deliver water and sewerage services this financial year.  I also note that the second quarter of 2025 saw the highest number of new house starts since 2018 and that construction output has reached a 15 year high in the North in the same period. This is positive news for people, jobs and the economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the extent to which inadequate water and wastewater infrastructure is stymying housebuilding; and (ii) any impact this is having on house prices, in each constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on developments which are impacted by wastewater infrastructure limitations. However, it is clear from discussions I have had with the sector, that wastewater capacity constraint is having a significant impact on housebuilding. You may wish to note that the NI Water website provides updates advising of catchment areas where it may provide a negative planning response when considering applications. This information can be accessed by the public. There are potentially several factors that can impact on house prices. The Northern Ireland Housing Executive, as the regional housing authority, publishes a Housing Market Review & Perspectives report providing insight into the housing market here. The most recent report (2024 – 2025) can be accessed at:	https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/northern-ireland- housing-market-data-hub/housing-market-review

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to the GAA's announcement regarding the commencement of enabling works at Casement Park, to detail (i) a timeline for completion; and (ii) how funding gaps are being closed.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park remains an Executive flagship capital project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when the ADHD needs assessment report will be published.
answer_text: The needs assessment was received by the Department on 30 November 2025. I and my officials are now considering next steps and potential options for such a service, subject to funding becoming available. It is anticipated that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when the Investment Strategy will be published.
answer_text: Once ISNI has been finalised, the timeline for approval and publication will be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to ensure that NI Water’s next price control, PC28, is fully funded.
answer_text: My department is currently working with water sector stakeholders on business planning for the next Price Control. The Utility Regulator is responsible for determining outputs and setting a regulatory allowance for the PC28 price control period and I have written to both the Utility Regulator and NI Water to ask them to be cognisant of the budget outlook.  While the PC 28 process should acknowledge NI Water’s statutory obligations, it must equally reflect the affordability and deliverability of the required investment and be developed appropriately to ensure it is aligned with the budget outlook.   I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues to secure adequate budget for NI Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the (i) nature of the construction works; and (ii) programme for completion, at Newtownhamilton Police Station.
answer_text: The management of the PSNI estate is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. It is therefore suggested that you should direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) data his Department holds on the prevalence of the cuckoo; (ii) actions his Department has taken to protect the cuckoo population; and (iii) actions his Department has taken to grow the cuckoo population.
answer_text: DAERA does not hold regular data on cuckoos, with the best data sources being the British Trust for Ornithology (BTO) bird atlases. Distributional data can be accessed at https://app.bto.org/mapstore/StoreServlet?id=276 , covering distributional change between 1968 and 2011. The atlas data show a decline in numbers and a retreat towards upland and western areas. There is some evidence for a population increase in parts of Great Britain since then, but insufficient data to establish a trend for Northern Ireland.Due to low breeding frequency and occurrence in a range of habitats, no conservation actions have been targeted at this species. It occurs on various protected sites: Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSIs), Special Areas for Conservation (SACs) and Special Protection Areas (SPAs). Habitat conservation measures for those sites will indirectly benefit this species.As (ii). Pressures occurring beyond Northern Ireland are difficult to address with recent analysis of data from Great Britain showing that migration routes to and from wintering quarters in Africa are significant; cuckoos following a south-westerly route have experienced increased mortality, reducing the numbers of those returning to breed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland's findings in its Key Priorities of 2026 release, that long-term capital funding certainty by March 2026 is required, in order to (i) plan and deliver major infrastructure; (ii) reduce inflation, delays and inefficiencies; (iii) enable private sector investment in skills, apprenticeships; and (iv) to work towards decarbonisation.
answer_text: The adoption of annual budgeting practices and an increased propensity for legal challenges have hindered my Department’s ability to deliver and sustain a pipeline of major infrastructure projects.Inflationary pressures also continue to drive significant cost increases across the construction sector in both GB and the South, with major schemes experiencing rising tender prices and significant annual uplifts. These pressures are widening funding gaps and can impact on project viability, timelines and delivery models, as seen across multiple strategic infrastructure schemes.To address these issues, the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland (ISNI) 2050 includes an Enabling Action Plan designed to address longstanding issues in infrastructure delivery.  The plan identifies key risks to infrastructure delivery and groups 12 enabling actions under three themes: People, Process and Policy.The merits of long term capital funding, underpinned by a multi-year budget are extensive for my Department and contractors, providing certainty, allowing for more effective forward planning in the delivery of vital infrastructure and the development of internal and external delivery capacity including investment in skills and apprenticeships.I welcome the launch of the Draft Budget 2026-29/30 consultation, which will help inform the Executive’s consideration and decisions on a Final Budget 2026-29/30.All Departments are committed under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to reducing emissions by 2050.  My Department has a number of specific tasks under the Act including the publication of sectoral plans for both transport and infrastructure.My officals have already started to engage with stakeholders like the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland to help develop the sectoral plans and work towards decarbonisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 37606/22-27, for his assessment of the impact a lack of housing supply has had on the disproportionate increase in house prices in the North, relative to house prices across the UK.
answer_text: I have been very clear that increasing housing supply is key to reducing pressures across all tenures.  This is why I have published a Housing Supply Strategy and will shortly be bringing the first Action Plan to the Executive. The plan will detail a range of actions to be delivered over the next 3 years, focused on addressing the whole system issues that are impacting supply and affordability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to Sinn Féin’s illegally erected billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, to detail (i) any penalties available to her Department that can be taken against Sinn Féin for the illegal construction within 50 meters of a railway line, without seeking permission in advance from Translink; and (ii) any implications for rail safety, given the billboard is erected within 50 meters of a railway line.
answer_text: The billboard adjacent to the Egyptian Arch is mounted on five posts, some of which appear to be situated on my Department’s land. The other posts are on privately owned land. The Roads Order 1993 gives the Department discretionary power to either seek to have the advertisement removed, or to remove it, if considered necessary.  The billboard is considered to pose a low risk to road safety because; it is set back from the road on an embankment, does not obstruct sightlines, and is within a 30mph speed limit.  NI Rail conducted a site inspection and identified no safety issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support available to home owners whose homes have suffered damage as a result of a fire emanating from a neighbouring property.
answer_text: My Department does not provide this type of support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for increasing the price of fares for the Strangford ferry crossing.
answer_text: I continue to provide over £1m per year towards the operation and maintenance of Strangford Lough Ferry Service. However, given the financial pressures faced by public services, fares will be increased by 4.5% which is in line with the relevant CPI inflation figure and is essential. A range of fare discounts available on NI Direct.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding the works at Maydown Road, Benburb, whether her Department has an updated timeline for completion, given the current trajectory of construction and the disruption and inconvenience being caused.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to AQW 37433/22-27 and can confirm that work is on track to reopen by the previously notified date of Mid March subject to favourable weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when Craigmore Way, Newry, will be fully reopened to traffic.
answer_text: I can advise that Craigmore Way has been closed from 12th January 2026 to facilitate NIE works and is due to reopen on Friday 23rd January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 36939/22-27, to detail any supports his Department provides to supplement the flat rate of £7 per week, for child maintenance payments, when the paying parent is in receipt of a social benefit.
answer_text: In accordance with the Department for Communities Decision Making Guide for Child Maintenance, the flat rate of £7 will normally apply if:the Paying Parent has gross weekly income between £7-£100; orIf the Paying Parent or their partner receives a specified social security benefit, pension or allowance.The Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service provides help and support for individuals and families across Northern Ireland to access benefits, services and other support they may have entitlement to but are not claiming.  To 'Make the Call' you can phone 0800 232 1271, or text ADVICE to 0798440 5248. You can also visit nidirect.gov.uk/makethecall or email makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will he answer AQW 35764/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the average livestock fees for sheep and cattle, broken down by mart; and (ii) for his assessment of recent rises in livestock sale fees and its impact on farm sustainability.
answer_text: My Department does not hold details on the current livestock fees applied by individual marts.  As livestock fees are a relatively small proportion of overall farm costs, I would not expect the recent increase to have any major impact on farm sustainability but do appreciate that the rise in these costs will be unwelcome.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on progressing each of the recommendations in the PSNI's South Armagh Policing Review.
answer_text: Pursuant to both AQW 18995/22-27 and AQW 24397/22-27, matters in relation to the implementation of recommendations of the South Armagh Policing Review remain an operational matter for the Chief Constable.You may wish to seek an update from the Chief Constable or the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been made of the financial benefit to the economy of the proposal presented by Newry Next for the construction of an additional 6,000 homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor.
answer_text: I understand there was a recent article in the Irish Times regarding a report that there was potential for 6,000 new homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor, with demand coming from those commuting to Dublin. However, the report itself has not been shared with my Department.The issues highlighted in the article including infrastructure and housing do not fall within the remit of my Department and are the responsibility of the Departments for Infrastructure and Communities respectively.I would be delighted to see any plan that would bring economic growth to the north and along the Dublin Belfast Economic Corridor. I believe there is great potential in the Dublin to Belfast Economic Corridor. My Department will continue to work with relevant councils and officials on issues within our remit on the Dublin to Belfast Economic Corridor to maximise this potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any action he will take increase apple production, in light of his Department's Statistical Review of Northern Ireland Agriculture 2024 stating that it decreased by 15.8 per cent in 2024.
answer_text: My Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) provides options for all farm businesses in Northern Ireland, including apple growers, to secure and improve their viability and environmental sustainability. The SAP encompasses a wide range of schemes that will be available to apple growers.The Horticulture Pilot Scheme comprises three sub schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups pilot scheme, Growers’ Training and Support pilot scheme and Innovation Driver and Support pilot scheme. The Scheme aims to increase cooperation, investment and growers’ capabilities.The Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will provide financial support for equipment and technology to improve environmental performance and business efficiency.In addition, schemes such as the Farm Sustainability Payment, the Farming with Nature Package, Farming for the Generations Programme and the Supply Chain Scheme are applicable to apple growers.In March 2025, I confirmed that the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS) will continue in Northern Ireland until a suitable successor, co-designed with the horticulture sector is in place. Two prospective POs submitted operational programmes to my Department for approval in October 2025 and these have, very recently, been approved in principle. I welcome the increased interest in the FVAS from the local horticulture sector, including apple growers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there will be ringfenced funding for childcare, in the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30
answer_text: The proposed draft multi-year budget for 2026 –29/30 has yet to be agreed by the Executive. It would be my expectation that any funding allocated to my department in respect of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will be ring-fenced solely for that purpose, as has been the case in previous years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 35971/22-27 relating to Historical Clerical Child Abuse, for a timeline for deliberations to conclude.
answer_text: We are currently considering the findings of the three research reports and the advice from the Interdepartmental Working Group and are taking time to reflect on these fully. We have not yet taken any decisions.We are committed to progressing work on this complex and sensitive issue, and our officials will continue to keep victims and survivors informed throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether she has received the report from her officials, relating to the review of Section 10B Bus Permits; and (ii) the timeframe for when her Department will share the report with Community Transport Managers.
answer_text: I understand that the draft report from the Section 10B Bus Permits Review is undergoing final quality assurance checks.  I expect to receive a briefing in the next few weeks, so that I can explore any issues identified with officials and discuss potential next steps.   The report will then be shared with Community Transport Managers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any mitigations his Department allows for Education Maintenance Allowance payments to be paid to A-Level students who are unable to physically attend school, due to ongoing ill health.
answer_text: The Department of Education and the Department for the Economy have joint responsibility for the Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) scheme. The scheme is administered on behalf of both departments by the Student Loans Company (SLC).EMA is intended to help young people from low-income backgrounds with the day-to-day costs of staying on at school or further education college for post compulsory education. As EMA is an attendance-based allowance, pupils are expected to attend all their timetabled lessons, however there are some circumstances which the school may recognise as an authorised absence during which EMA payments may continue. This can include isolated periods of illness, however long-term illness lasting three weeks or more will not normally be considered a valid reason for authorising an absence for EMA purposes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the figures outlined in the Irish News on 14 January 2026 which show that patients in the North are three times more likely to be on hospital waiting lists than those in the Republic.
answer_text: While the Northern Ireland waiting lists are not acceptable, comparison with the Republic of Ireland is not appropriate given the differences in funding and patient access arrangements. The Republic of Ireland has a much larger private sector and private insurance market compared to Northern Ireland, and many Republic of Ireland residents pay to access local general practitioner (GP) services. In addition, Republic of Ireland patients will also be charged for attending an emergency department if they don't have a GP referral or medical card.However, it should be noted that even within the current constrained financial environment, we have made significant progress since March 2025 with the number of patients waiting more than four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 35% and the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 47%.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her assessment of the article in the Irish Times on 10 January 2026, which outlined a plan with the potential to unlock 6,000 new homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor; and (ii) whether she plans to conduct any assessment of the potential of progressing this plan.
answer_text: The Newry Next report referenced in the Irish Times is focused on leveraging Newry’s unique position on the Belfast–Dublin economic corridor to support economic growth and local housing delivery. I support this all-island approach which is consistent with the Regional Development Strategy and Newry Mourne and Down District’s Council draft Local Development Plan vision for Newry. The report contains detailed funding proposals to address the wastewater constraint in the Newry–Banbridge corridor. While I welcome this ambition, some of its detail including the certainty and timing of funding sources, suggested governance arrangements, and economic and fiscal modelling requires further scrutiny and discussion. Nevertheless, I am keen to work collaboratively with stakeholders north and south to identify and explore all options for future infrastructure upgrades to support regional growth for the benefit of local communities.I am already working hard with partners including executive colleagues, NI Water, and others to find ways forward. NI Water has already developed a £26m innovative plan for Newry to unlock additional capacity within existing budgets, using targeted interventions, advanced monitoring, and digital analytics. This includes real-time control at treatment works to improve throughput and reduce environmental impacts, repairing broken sewers to remove excess water, and extending outfalls to reduce storm spill impacts.  The article does reference potential ancillary benefits, such as easing housing pressure in Dublin, however this is identified as an associated benefit and acknowledges the cross border connections that already exist and are likely to grow in the future as the Belfast-Dublin corridor further develops economically. I remained focused on developing all island solutions that work for local communities in terms of delivering local opportunities, jobs and affordable housing.On this basis I will continue to engage with my Executive colleagues, counterparts in the South through the established North-South Ministerial Council, and other partners to examine and reflect on the potential benefits and implications of this plan and other proposals. Any decisions will be taken in the round, ensuring they deliver for local communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) her assessment of the article in the Irish Times on 10 January 2026, which outlined a plan with the potential to unlock 6,000 new homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor; and (ii) whether she plans to conduct any assessment of the potential of progressing this plan.
answer_text: The report highlights how investment in Newry’s wastewater and network infrastructure could unlock sustainable housing, commercial and cross border economic development. The issues around wastewater infrastructure highlighted in the report do not fall within the remit of my Department and are the responsibility of the Department for Infrastructure.My Department will continue to work with relevant councils and officials on issues within our remit on the Dublin to Belfast Economic Corridor to maximise its potential.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) their assessment of the article in the Irish Times on 10 January 2026, which outlined a plan with the potential to unlock 6,000 new homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor; and (ii) whether they plan to conduct any assessment of the potential of progressing this plan.
answer_text: We have previously highlighted the importance of the Newry Banbridge corridor and its potential to drive economic growth and prosperity.Providing more social, affordable and sustainable housing, and enhancing wastewater infrastructure, remain targets in the Programme for Government.  House building and wastewater infrastructure are matters for the Department for Communities and the Department of Infrastructure respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his assessment of the article in the Irish Times on 10 January 2026, which outlined a plan with the potential to unlock 6,000 new homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor; and (ii) whether he plans to conduct any assessment of the potential of progressing this plan.
answer_text: I have noted the proposals set out in the Irish Times article regarding the potential to maximise the economic and housing opportunities in the Newry-Banbridge corridor, as part of a cross-border commuter model linked to Dublin. However, any assessment in relation to the delivery of new homes in the area as proposed, would be for the Department for Communities, given its responsibilities for housing policy and supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Newry Next Building a Regional Powerhouse by 2035 report, which identifies that more than 1300 homes and almost 30 commercial projects in the Newry area are stalled, due to the lack of wastewater capacity.
answer_text: The focus of the ‘Newry Next’ report is primarily to unlock regional economic growth through significant investment in wastewater treatment and network infrastructure, to address constraints to housing development in the Newry-Banbridge corridor. The primary objective of the measures in the report is to support delivery of planned housing, strengthen environmental compliance and stimulate economic growth and job creation in the Newry, Banbridge and surrounding cross border region. As Infrastructure Minister, I strongly support the objectives of balanced regional growth, cross border cooperation and collaboration, which form the basis of this report.I fully understand the impact of wastewater challenges across the North including in Newry and I firmly believe that working in partnership with NI Water and others, is the best way to ensure wastewater connections are available to support the development of housing and the wider economy. I am keen to work collaboratively with a range of partners, to identify and consider options for future infrastructure upgrades that may be needed to support regional growth.  Indeed, the report highlights a number of areas of alignment and cross-border collaboration already in place.NI Water has already developed a £26m innovative plan within existing budgets, using targeted interventions, advanced monitoring, and digital analytics, which will provide wastewater capacity for 400 properties in Newry. This includes real-time control at treatment works to improve throughput and reduce environmental impacts, repairing broken sewers to remove excess water, and extending outfalls to reduce storm spill impacts.  NI Water will continue to work with developers to co-ordinate further connections through its impact assessment process. I will continue to address wastewater issues through the three-pronged approach of securing adequate funding for water and wastewater infrastructure from the Executive, bringing forward the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill and the consideration of developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any sanctions faced by her Department for not acting within the boundaries of its licence, as arbitrated by the Office of Environmental Protection.
answer_text: I welcome the Office for Environmental Protection’s (OEP) investigation into the regulation of sewage discharges into Belfast Lough. This investigation is ongoing and my Department will fully co-operate with the OEP.  Once the investigation has completed, I will consider the recommendations and take forward any necessary actions. However, it is important to note that it is the Department of Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs’ Northern Ireland Environment Agency that regulates the sewage discharges into Belfast Lough and  can and has  prosecuted NI Water for water pollution offences.I will of course continue to address wastewater issues through the three-pronged approach of securing adequate funding for water and wastewater infrastructure from the Executive, bringing forward the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill and the consideration of developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans her Department's has for addressing the creation of sexually explicit deepfake images, through Artificial Intelligence, as a form of violence against women, girls, and children.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned about the exponential rise in the proliferation of non-consensual sexually explicit deepfake images, which research shows are overwhelmingly targeted at women and girls, and are yet another form of sexual abuse and exploitation. The recent issues involving the AI Chatbot Grok has again illustrated how embedded this disgraceful behaviour has become. I will address this growing threat and the harm it causes by introducing new offences to deal with the creation and sharing of these images where they relate to adults. In particular, I intend to table draft provision, shaped by responses to a recent public consultation undertaken by my Department on a range of legislative proposals, by way of an amendment to the Justice Bill at Consideration Stage.  There are already protections in place for children. Under Article 3 of the Protection Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 it is an offence to take, make, distribute or show an indecent photograph, or pseudo-photograph of a child under 18.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps his Department is taking to make census data available as part of the Irish Government's release of the 1926 census.
answer_text: NISRA is working with the Central Statistics Office (CSO) in the south of Ireland to plan for a coordinated statistical release to support the promotion of the 1926 records. This will include the release of some statistics based on reports published from the 1926 Census, along with supporting commentary on the changes in society since 1926.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the PSNI's figures, highlighting a continued rise in domestic abuse-related calls over Christmas and the New Year, to detail any efforts her Department has made to address increased rates of domestic violence around holiday periods.
answer_text: I am very concerned by the PSNI figures showing a continued increase in domestic abuse-related calls over the Christmas and New Year period. Sadly, we know that holiday periods, when families spend more time together and pressures can increase, are often associated with a rise in both incidents and reporting.Addressing this is a priority for my Department.Operationally, the PSNI routinely plans for and responds to predictable spikes in demand during holiday periods, including through increased resourcing, proactive policing activity and close working with partner agencies to ensure that victims are protected and perpetrators are dealt with robustly.From a Departmental perspective, we support this response in several important ways.My Department, working in partnership with other Executive Departments, continues to fund a range of specialist domestic abuse support services, including helplines, refuges, advocacy services and outreach support, to ensure that victims can access help when they need it, including over holiday periods. We also work with partners to ensure that clear public messaging is in place to encourage reporting and to signpost victims to support.More strategically, we are implementing the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024–2031, which includes a strong focus on prevention, early intervention and improving the justice system response. This is supported by a new performance framework to track progress and ensure agencies are held to account for delivery.In addition, we continue to progress key legislative and operational reforms, including the roll-out of protective measures for victims and work to improve risk assessment, information sharing and multi-agency responses.While I am under no illusion that there is no single quick fix to this deeply complex issue, I am determined that my Department will continue to work with the PSNI, other departments and the voluntary and community sector to both respond to spikes in demand, such as those seen over holiday periods, and to tackle the underlying causes of domestic and sexual abuse in the longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the article in the Irish Times on 10 January 2026, which outlined a plan with the potential to unlock 6,000 new homes in the Newry Banbridge corridor.
answer_text: The focus of the ‘Newry Next’ report which is the subject of the Irish Times piece is primarily on unlocking regional economic growth through significant investment in wastewater treatment and network infrastructure to address constraints to housing development in the Newry–Banbridge corridor. The primary objective of the measures in the report is to support delivery of planned housing, strengthen environmental compliance and stimulate economic growth and job creation in the Newry, Banbridge and surrounding cross border region. The article does reference potential ancillary benefits, such as commuter housing easing housing pressure in Dublin, however this is identified as an associated benefit and simply acknowledges the exiting cross border connections that already exist and are likely to grow in the future as the Belfast-Dublin corridor further develops. As Infrastructure Minister I strongly support the objectives of balanced regional growth and cross border cooperation and collaboration which form the basis of this report. These principles already inform the Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035, the spatial strategy of the Executive, which identifies Newry as an important Hub and ‘South Eastern City Gateway’ on the Belfast-Dublin economic corridor. This corridor is recognised as having potential to become a significant axis of development within the wider European context. Some of the detailed report proposals, including the certainty and timing of funding sources, suggested governance arrangements, and economic and fiscal modelling, would require further consideration. Nevertheless, I am keen to work collaboratively with a range of partners to identify and consider options for future infrastructure upgrades that may be needed to support regional growth. NI Water has already developed a £26m innovative plan within existing budgets, using targeted interventions, advanced monitoring, and digital analytics. This includes real-time control at treatment works to improve throughput and reduce environmental impacts, repairing broken sewers to remove excess water, and extending outfalls to reduce storm spill impacts.  NI Water will continue to work with developers to co-ordinate further connections through its impact assessment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding support available for ice hockey players from the North competing for Team Ireland in an international ice hockey tournament in Nova Scotia, Canada, in March 2026.
answer_text: Regrettably, neither my Department nor Sport NI, have any programmes that could provide this type of funding support.		I understand some local councils may offer relevant funding programmes, and I would encourage constituents or clubs to contact their local council sports development team for further details. 		I am aware the Mary Peters Trust offers guidance around funding for young athletes. Further details and contact options are available online at; https://marypeterstrust.org/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale for downgrading the criteria for the white lining of roads; and (ii) any implications this will have for road lining longevity and road safety.
answer_text: At present, there are no plans to amend or downgrade the criteria for whitelining on roads in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail where young people with special educational needs can gain employment opportunities, in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: I would encourage any young person with Special Educational needs, to contact their local Jobs & Benefits Office (JBo), where staff can signpost them to employment or opportunities that offer pathways to employment. The full list of JBo locations is available on the Department for Communities website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the spend on (i) new street lighting; (ii) enhancements to street lighting; and (iii) kerb replacement schemes, over each of the last five years, broken down by scheme and constituency.
answer_text: Capital expenditure on Street Lighting, which includes (i) new street lighting and (ii) enhancements is detailed in the table below:New Street Lighting and Enhancements2020-212021-222022-232023-242024-25£m23.828.221.025.06.5My Department does not have distinct kerb replacement schemes, rather kerbs are replaced where necessary as part of a carriageway resurfacing or a footway replacement scheme. My Department’s Annual Report to Councils provides details of schemes undertaken in each council area, but the costs for each scheme are not identified separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether parental alienation is considered as child abuse, under the law.
answer_text: The concept of parental alienation is not recognised in law in Northern Ireland and any change in that position would be a matter for the Department of Health. Alienating behaviours may, however, constitute abusive behaviour under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (NI) 2021 which also provides for offences involving or witnessed by a child to be treated as aggravated and increased sentencing may apply.		Allegations of damaging parental behaviours in individual court cases is and must be a matter for the judge concerned, as informed by all available evidence and the paramount concern must remain the welfare of the children but I am continuing to take all the steps I can to improve the experience of families, particularly those who have experienced abuse through my Enabling Access to Justice Programme.  		That includes enhancing eligibility for legal aid to ensure that a greater range of citizens are protected and to ensuring the system cannot be weaponised to perpetrate further abuse. The Programme also includes the introduction of tailored supports at the point of entry so the needs of vulnerable parties are addressed at the earliest possible juncture so they can effectively participate in proceedings. I am also considering the findings of the recently published research commissioned by The Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime, which captures the experiences of children whose families have been involved in the family courts with a particular focus on cases involving domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent cyclist fatality on the Westlink, Belfast, for her assessment of mandating lorry manufacturers to equip lorries with effective blind spot detection and warning systems.
answer_text: First, I want to express my condolences to the family and friends of the victim of this collision.All on road vehicles here are subject to a type approval process which requires any vehicle sold or registered here to comply with the relevant EU legislation. The EU General Safety Regulation known as (GSR2) or Regulation (EU) 2019/2144 has been phased in since the 6th July 2022 and applies in the North under the  Protocol. Regulation (EU) 2019/2144 dictates that all new HGVs manufactured under the EU/UKNI type approval schemes after the 7th July 2024 be fitted with Blind Spot Information Systems (BSIS). BSIS is a safety features in vehicles designed to alert drivers to the presence of other vehicles in their blind spots, enhancing overall road safety for all. This EU-led intervention will become more prevalent in HGVs used here as companies update their vehicles.Road safety remains of paramount importance to me and my Department and I am supportive of any interventions, including BSIS, which will support a reduction in deaths and serious injuries on our roads. As there is already legislation in place I have no current plans to amend or introduce further regulations on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a feasibility study for a greenway, connecting the Newry to Portadown and Newry to Carlingford greenways, will be undertaken by her Department.
answer_text: The Member may be aware of my recent announcement with regard to the Active Travel Signature Projects, which included the Newry Inner City Canal Greenway. Design is due to commence shortly and when preliminary proposals are suitably developed, they will be brought forward for initial public engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's approach to the issue of landlords threatening, or serving, eviction notices to tenants on the basis of pet ownership.
answer_text: There is no specific private tenancies legislation in respect of pet ownership in Northern Ireland.  Any eviction must be carried out legally including that the tenant must be provided with the correct notice period and must not be subject to harassment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his assessment of a mandatory seven-day custodial sentence for anyone who breaches a non-molestation order; and (ii) any other strategies his Department has considered to ensure victims have a window of time to relocate, or seek protections, if non-molestation orders are breached.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998, which sets out the statutory criteria relating to non-molestation orders. In relation to part (i) above, Article 25 of the 1998 Order provides that the contravention of a non-molestation order is a criminal offence. The facility to impose a custodial sentence is, therefore, already available to the courts. Sentencing procedures relating to breaches of non-molestation orders are a matter for the independent judiciary. That sentence was originally set at a maximum of 3 months, but further to a review by my Department in the years following the Order, was increased to 6 months.As advised by the Justice Minister in response to AQW 24847/22-27, judges will take into consideration all of the circumstances relating to the individual case before them in making sentencing decisions, and in doing so, take account of both aggravating and mitigating factors, with the former including factors indicating higher culpability, or where there is a more than usual degree of harm. To mandate a 7-day prison sentence for all breaches of non-molestation orders would require primary legislation and would involve a careful analysis of the various issues at play in such cases to ensure compliance with the European Convention of Human Rights. At this time, no such legislative proposal has been developed by my Department. In relation to part (ii), various actions are being taken forward as part of cross-departmental strategies to protect all victims of domestic violence. For example, the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031 was published on 24 September 2024, and the Department of Justice is preparing to pilot Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders which would provide more flexible powers for police and courts. In addition, the Commissioner for Victims of Crime has commissioned research which includes an examination of the outcomes of non-molestation orders, including breaches. The findings from this work will provide evidence to inform future policy development and operational practice across relevant Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, as a consequence of the inadequate water and wastewater drainage and treatment capacity, to detail the number of housing units that cannot be progressed (i) through planning; (ii) to construction commencement; and (iii) to completion and occupation, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has advised that it can only make recommendations to local planning authorities, following a consultation request and that ultimately, the local planning authority decides if a planning application is to be granted approval. However, NI Water is able to provide details of (i) the number of housing units that will not be able to get a connection to the public foul network due to closed catchments; and (ii) the number of housing units that NI Water recommended refusal, broken down by constituency.  In respect of the latter, NI Water cannot confirm if the relevant planning authority accepted that recommendation. 	There are 1,138 housing units that will not get a connection to the public foul network due to closed catchments. However, some of these may be able to progress via other means, for example, a privately owned and maintained package wastewater treatment works or pumping station, agreed with NIEA. These units are broken down by constituency in the table below:ConstituencyNumber of housing unitsCauseway Coast & Glens1Mid and East Antrim100Antrim & Newtownabbey38Belfast88Lisburn & Castlereagh32Ards & North Down86Newry, Mourne & Down122Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon50Mid Ulster296Fermanagh & Omagh324Derry & Strabane1Total 1138(ii) 	There are 14,248 housing units for which NI Water recommended refusal.
