However, some of these may have the potential to connect pending engagement with NI Water, and the agreement and delivery of a suitable solution.  This is a developer led process and the developer needs to determine if any agreed solution is commercially viable. These units are broken down by constituency in the table below:ConstituencyNumber of housing unitsCauseway Coast & Glens901Mid and East Antrim1481Antrim & Newtownabbey1299Belfast1845Lisburn & Castlereagh565Ards & North Down728Newry, Mourne & Down1197Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon1293Mid Ulster2041Fermanagh & Omagh1124Derry & Strabane1774Total 14248For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make the best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department plans to take to promote the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme to the horticulture sector to increase the number of growers availing of the support opportunity.
answer_text: In March 2025, I confirmed that the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme (FVAS) will continue in Northern Ireland until a suitable successor, co-designed with the horticulture sector is in place. Under the scheme, my Department will provide match-funding for recognised NI Producer Organisations (POs) undertaking an approved 3-year operational programme, starting from 1 January 2026.I am pleased to confirm that two prospective POs submitted operational programmes to my Department for approval in October 2025 and these have, very recently, been approved in principle. I particularly welcome the increased interest in the FVAS from local growers covering a wider range of horticulture sectors than before, including mushrooms, field vegetables, strawberries and apples.My officials are finalising a review of the FVAS which will inform development of its successor, as well as any plans to promote the existing scheme in the interim.In addition to the FVAS, the Sustainable Agriculture Programme encompasses schemes that offer support for the sustainable growth of the local horticulture sector. The Horticulture Pilot Scheme, launched in 2025, comprising the Sustainable Sector Growth Groups pilot scheme, the Growers' Training and Support pilot scheme and the Innovation Driver and Support pilot scheme aims to increase cooperation, investment and growers' capabilities. The Sustainable Farming Investment scheme will provide financial support for equipment and technology to improve environmental performance and business efficiency. More information on this scheme will be available in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will prioritise gritting of the B2 road into and out of Derrytrasna, Armagh, given the recent bus accident.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements are finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and our salt barns are filled to capacity.My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Where possible, once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is secured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. The full policy can be viewed on the Department’s website below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The B2 Derrytrasna Road between Lurgan and Derrytrasna is gritted as is the B2 Charlestown Road between Derrytrasna and Carn. The C160 Bannfoot Road is not gritted because it does not meet the criteria set out in the Department’s winter service policy for inclusion in the winter gritting schedule. Bannfoot Road is not a through route and does not carry sufficient traffic for further consideration. 	It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of constructing a pedestrian bridge between Markethill and Gosford, Armagh, across the A28 carriageway, given the number of pedestrians and the speed of the traffic on the carriageway.
answer_text: Firstly, I would like to express my sincere condolences to the family tragically impacted by the serious collision on the Gosford Road on 3 December 2025.  Every death on our roads is a profound loss to the community.I understand that the PSNI are leading an investigation into this incident and when the findings are shared, my officials will carefully consider if any engineering measures can be introduced to enhance road safety.I have asked officials to undertake a thorough review of the current signage and road markings across this section of the A28 to include the Parrs Lane junction and ensure all measures remain suitable and effective.Officials will also review the previous pedestrian crossing assessment at this location to determine if any changes are required to the original findings.I recently launched a public consultation on speed limits on 14 January that will close on 22 April 2026 and would encourage as many as possible to contribute to this important and significant piece of work.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine's December 2025 survey Overcrowding and Corridor Care in Northern Ireland Emergency Departments, which found that across the nine Emergency Departments, 49 per cent of patients were receiving care in a non-designated space such as a corridor, the floor, or on a chair on the morning of 8 December 2025.
answer_text: My Department recognises the challenges faced by emergency departments (EDs) and is working closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays.To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has introduced targeted initiatives, including the expansion of urgent care centres, Phone First services and minor injury units. These measures provide patients with timely alternatives to ED attendance.My Department has worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and the Trusts to ensure that additional emergency medicine consultants have been appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland. To date, 18 additional consultants have taken up posts, with a further five commencing work in the coming months once they have completed their training. There are plans to recruit a further four posts into early 2026 once training is completed. This additional capacity is intended to increase senior decision making into evenings and weekends where consultant cover has previously been low.Ultimately, these measures are designed to improve patient flow throughout hospitals, reduce congestion in EDs and enhance waiting times. They form part of a broader, system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.I will be meeting representatives of the RCEM on 22 January 2026 to discuss their recent survey on overcrowding and corridor care in EDs.  I regard this as an important piece of work and I am keen to engage directly with RCEM on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the recent case in County Armagh, to detail any actions his Department will take to implement safeguards to protect young people in sport from being targeted by Artificial Intelligence deepfakes.
answer_text: The safety and wellbeing of children and young people in our communities is a responsibility shared by everyone in society. Addressing emerging risks such as AI-generated deepfakes is a wide-ranging challenge that will require input and action across multiple areas of government and public services.Responsibility for Safeguarding in sport lies with the five UK Sports Councils, which includes Sport NI for Northern Ireland, an Arm’s Length Body of my Department. Sport NI currently has a contract with NSPCC (Child Protection in Sport Unit) to provide a safeguarding advisory and guidance service for the sector.  Through the contract, Sport NI supports the sector to ensure legislation and best practice with respect to Safeguarding is followed and that the sector is supported appropriately.Sport NI will discuss this matter with NSPCC as part of contract/service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 37321/22-27, to detail when the outline business case for the Portadown to Armagh railway link will be finalised.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to exploring opportunities to enhance our rail network and improve sustainable transport options. Completion of the feasibility studies is a critical first milestone in defining the projects and their viability.The initial Strategic Outline Case for the Portadown to Armagh line has been developed, and the ringfenced funding allocated this year will allow for the further development of priority tasks related to this project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether they agreed to the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: The First and deputy First Ministers acknowledge the draft budget and its purpose. The Draft Budget 2026-29/30 has not yet been agreed by the Executive. The Finance Minister published the draft for consultation on 6th January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether she agreed the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30; and (ii) for her assessment of the merits of a multi-year budget for her Department.
answer_text: I welcome the launch of the Draft Budget 2026-29/30 consultation, which will help inform the Executive’s consideration and decisions on a Final Budget 2026-29/30. The merits of a multi-year budget for my Department are extensive, providing certainty, allowing for more effective forward planning in the delivery of vital infrastructure and the development of internal and external delivery capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the increase in house prices in Northern Ireland by 9.7 per cent in 2025, compared with an average rise of 0.6 per cent across the UK; and (ii) any factors identified by his Department as contributing to this disparity.
answer_text: Official statistics for the House Price Index (HPI) in NI are published by DoF LPS. Latest available statistics show at Quarter 3 (Jul-Sep) 2025, the annual price change for a property in Northern Ireland was 7.1%. (Source: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/ni-house-price-index-statistical-reports).Comparable HPI statistics for the UK as a whole are published by the ONS and show at the same point in time (September 2025), the annual price change for a property in the UK was 2.0%. (Source: https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/inflationandpriceindices/datasets/ukhousepriceindexmonthlypricestatistics).It should be noted from this data that average house prices in Northern Ireland remain well below the UK average, at £193,000 compared to £270,000.The Northern Ireland Housing Executive, as the regional housing authority, publishes a Housing Market Review & Perspectives report providing insight into the housing market here. The most recent report (2024 – 2025) can be accessed at:	https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/northern-ireland-housing-market-data-hub/housing-market-review

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of not having a rehabilitation unit within the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Drug and alcohol services in Northern Ireland follow a 4-tier model, ranging from prevention and harm reduction to Tier 4 specialist inpatient detoxification and residential rehabilitation. Tier 4(a) detoxification is provided regionally across three hospital sites: Holywell (9 beds), Downshire (14 beds), and Tyrone & Fermanagh (8 beds). While SHSCT does not have its own inpatient detox unit, patients can access these regional services based on clinical need.Residential rehabilitation (Tier 4b) is available through three community-based providers: Carlisle House (Belfast), Northlands Centre (Derry/Londonderry), and Cuan Mhuire (Newry). Access typically follows detoxification and supports recovery before returning to the community. A recent independent review of Tier 4 services highlighted the need for improved access and consistency of service provision, and SPPG is developing new contracts and a regional service specification to address this.SHSCT also delivers community-based addiction services, including community addiction teams, opiate substitution treatment, harm reduction initiatives, and integrated mental health support. The Department does recognise the challenges for patients within various parts of the Region, including those within SHSCT, requiring intensive rehabilitation and is progressing a number of other developments including;CAD+ Service: €8m PEACEPLUS-funded initiative for community alcohol detox and wraparound support in SHSCT and WHSCT, launching mid-2026.Implementation of Tier 4 review recommendations to improve equity and access.Substance Use Strategic Commissioning Plan 2024–2028.Regional Mental Health Crisis Service development.Mental Health Strategy Action 28 review to build capacity for co-occurring issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact on domestic abuse victims, given the ongoing dispute in relation to legal aid payments for barristers.
answer_text: I have been clear from the outset that the impact of the damaging withdrawal of services by the Criminal Bar Association on victims and witnesses, including victims of domestic abuse, cannot be overstated. The action is unwarranted and the impact unjustifiable. Significant progress has already been made with the backdated 16 percent uplift and a process, which the CBA agreed to, is already in place to address remaining issues of concern and so I have called on the CBA to end their action and rejoin that process alongside their solicitor colleagues. I have had some very constructive engagement with the CBA in the last week to that end. I have also called on the CBA to agree derogations to the withdrawal to allow cases which have been assessed as involving those most at risk of serious harm to proceed pending any partial or full return to service. That is the compassionate and right thing to do. PPS; the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime; and a range of voluntary sector support organisations including Victim Support; Assist NI; NEXUS; and Women’s Aid are continuing to provide information, advice and support to the victims and witnesses affected by the withdrawal in the interim. I am exploring with those organisations what steps my Department might be able to take to support their efforts and whether there is additional support that could usefully be provided to victims and witnesses. Those discussions are continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of vaccines for chickenpox being made available via the NHS.
answer_text: All vaccination programmes in Northern Ireland are based on the advice and recommendations of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and immunisations (JCVI) an independent expert committee, who consider all the evidence in relation to a particular vaccine, before reaching their conclusions.The addition of vaccination against chickenpox (varicella) into the routine childhood immunisation schedule offers several significant public health, economic, and social benefits.Chickenpox is often perceived as a mild childhood illness, but it can cause serious complications, particularly in babies, young children, and vulnerable groups. These include bacterial skin infections, pneumonia, brain and lung inflammation, stroke, seizures, and, in rare cases, death. Beyond its health impact, chickenpox places a substantial burden on families and the wider economy. Children are typically required to stay home from nursery or school for around five days, while parents often need to take time off work to care for them.  Research estimates that childhood chickenpox costs the UK around £24 million each year in lost productivity, alongside an additional £15 million in NHS treatment costs. Incorporating the chickenpox vaccine into the Northern Ireland childhood vaccination schedule, as part of the measles, mumps, rubella and varicella vaccine (MMRV), is expected to reduce the disease burden, improve equity, and deliver long-term health system savings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of how children in poverty will be impacted by the price increase of school meals.
answer_text: Children and young people in receipt of free school meals (FSM) will be unaffected by the Education Authority (EA) decision to raise its school meal prices. All low-income families are encouraged to apply for FSM through the EA and it provides guidance and support to facilitate all such families making an application. For families not entitled to FSM, I recognise that a rise in school meal prices will not be welcomed. However, despite its costs of production having increased substantially in recent years, the EA has not raised its prices since 2017/18. Unfortunately, the current budgetary position has meant that the EA is no longer able to sustain prices at 2017/18 levels. A family not entitled to FSM retains the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to ensure their child is able to access an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch. Consequently, if a family decides not to pay for a school meal, the availability of this guidance on preparing a healthy packed lunch means that all parents should be able to ensure that children are able to access an affordable, healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of her Department's winter preparedness plan.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. My Department’s salt barns were filled to capacity over the summer in advance of winter. To ensure sufficient salt stocks are maintained throughout the Winter Season, a ‘reorder level’ is applied to each depot. During the period from 1st October to 1st January each year, the minimum ‘re-order level’ is set as 90% of full capacity. From 1st January each year, salt stocks are maintained in linear fashion against a minimum holding calculated on the basis of 6 days’ supply for two actions per day.My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, and from the end of October, up to 300 people are on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the north to cope with the wintry conditions. Contracts are also in place to enable contractors and farmers to help to clear roads during periods of prolonged snow. While my Department targets the limited resources available for winter service on the busier main through routes, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding streams available to the Parish of Lower Killeavy, to enable them to complete repairs of the Church of the Sacred Heart, Lislea.
answer_text: There is no Departmental funding available for churches at present, however the Parish may wish to contact the National Churches Trust (NCT) who offer large and medium sized grants. My department supports them to employ a caseworker. https://www.nationalchurchestrust.org/get-support-northern-ireland.Though closed to applications, the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund may provide an additional route.  Churches must be able to demonstrate that their building is accessible and used by the wider community and not used exclusively for worship. I am planning for this to become a baselined rolling program and therefore open in the next financial year for another round of applications

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a current status report on CCTV functionality across Translink train stations and car parks.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows.Translink currently has CCTV functionality across multiple sites within the estate including stations and car parks. Although functional, much of the hardware and software is approaching end of life therefore a new project and feasibility study have commenced with a view to potentially upgrading and/or replacing much of the aging equipment over the next 3 to 4 years, subject to the availability of funding. A pilot for the new CCTV system is currently being progressed at Newry Bus Station.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road safety campaigns led by her Department that will be active over the festive season.
answer_text: Over the festive period, my Department is focusing on anti-drink driving and anti-drug driving road safety campaigns.The anti-drink driving campaign “Afraid” aired on television and radio during November. Currently, the “Wise” campaign is being broadcast and will run throughout December.In addition, the new anti-drug driving campaign “There’s No Place for Drugs Behind the Wheel” is on air until the end of December. This campaign challenges the misconception held by some drivers that it is acceptable to take drugs and drive.All television campaigns can be viewed on the Share the Road to Zero website: https://sharetheroadtozero.com/TVBoth anti-drink driving and anti-drug driving messages are also being promoted through poster sites, forecourts, bus rear, on-street advertising and ATM digital screens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 31861 / 22-27, (i) for an update on the status of the court cairn at the summit of Slieve Gullion; and (ii) for his assessment of the merits of reconstructing the entire roof corbelling on the court cairn.
answer_text: The interior of the South Cairn remains closed for safety reasons and a project has been initiated to replace the 1960s roof covering.The project is assessing options for the repair or replacement of the roof covering predicated on my Department’s Conservation Principles and international best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Maydown Road be reopened, following the reconstruction of the Maydown Bridge.
answer_text: In the interest of public safety, my Department took the decision to close a short section of the Maydown Road in Benburb following the partial collapse of a retaining wall and section of carriageway during Storm Amy.Due to the collapse exposing several utility ducts and cables, repairs to the wall and carriageway could not begin until the service pipes and cables were safely decommissioned and diverted. I am pleased to confirm that the diversion works are now complete, and reconstruction of the wall and associated carriageway repairs have commenced on site. Subject to favorable weather conditions, the road is expected to reopen to traffic by mid-March.I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and apologise to the local community, including businesses in the area, for the inconvenience caused. Local diversions are in place, and I urge the travelling public to drive with due care and attention and allow extra time for their journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the finding in the RaISe - Experiences of Managing and Delivering Major Capital Projects in Great Britain and Ireland, briefing paper that states failure to act could add £5 billion to future costs in delivering major infrastructure projects.
answer_text: Neither I nor my officials have been sighted on this briefing paper and therefore I am not able to comment on it. I look forward to consideration of the report once it is published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35461 22-27, given her assessment that Newry's Controlled Parking Zone status is one of the contributing factors to the high number of parking tickets allocated in Newry, to detail whether she has given any consideration to temporarily trialling the removal of the controlled parking zone in Newry.
answer_text: My response to AQW 35461 22-27 considered that the issue of higher per capita PCNs in Newry is reflective of local conditions and is influenced by several interrelated factors which include the level of parking restrictions in the city; traffic attendant patrol timings; and cross border traffic and economic activity.I am trialling a pilot initiative for Newry and Lisburn, introducing a one hour grace period for people parking on-street in parking spaces within CPZs over the festive period. This pilot initiative will help guide my future intentions for on-street parking in controlled parking zones, ensuring they meet the needs of local communities and businesses whilst managing parking demand and maintaining accessibility for all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 36062/22-27, to detail the average response time for statutory consultees as part of the planning application process, broken down by council area.
answer_text: My Department publishes quarterly and annual statutory consultation performance reports which are available on its website. I would therefore, refer the member to Tables 4 (d-f) in the link below which provides an annual breakdown for 2024/25 on consultations issued (by application type) from each planning authority to the statutory consultee and reports both the consultation percentage returned on-time to the planning authority and response status counts.NI Statutory Planning Consultations - Annual Performance Report 2024/25 | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32527/22-27, (i) for an update on when the tender documents for the A1 Junctions Phase 2 Scheme will be issued; and (ii) to detail the expected timeline for project completion.
answer_text: The A1 Junctions Phase 2 Project will deliver enhanced safety measures along the A1 route, costing in the region of £120 - £130m and will include the closing up of all gaps in the central median and the provision of a number of new grade separated junctions.The scheme was confirmed by my predecessor John O’Dowd as a single package of works on 2 July 2024 and a restricted list was notified to tenderers on 9 May 2025. Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I am taking time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, this includes the A1 Junctions Phase 2 Project.While we await the ruling from the Court Appeal, my officials continue to work on the next steps in relation to the project and I remain committed to the delivery of this vitally important safety scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.
answer_text: The Enniskillen Southern Bypass is a strategically important scheme which will provide significant benefits to the town and its surrounding road network. Last year the procurement process commenced, and a shortlist of contractors was invited to bid for the contract to construct the scheme. Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I am taking time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, this includes A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31167/22-27, to detail when (i) she expects to publish her Department's response to the feasibility study into the reopening of the Portadown to Armagh rail link; and (ii) the feasibility study will be made public.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to exploring opportunities to enhance our rail network and improve sustainable transport options. .  A summary report of the main findings of the Feasibility Reports was published on 17th December 2025 (www.translink.co.uk/shapingthefuture) which outlines a vision for the future development of the North’s rail network. It aligns with the ambitions of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review and presents the key findings from four major project feasibility studies, including the re-opening of the Portadown to Armagh. The findings provide a case for investment in a modern and sustainable rail network, subject to the availability of capital and resource funding, as part of a wider network vision to double the number of rail passengers through improved network accessibility, enhanced regional connectivity and inter-urban journey times.  Delivering these four projects, alongside a shift towards rail travel, could unlock significant benefits for the North, driving economic growth, promoting social inclusion and supporting environmental sustainability. This progress could also advance our climate ambitions and commitment to achieving net zero emissions. Completion of the feasibility studies is a critical first milestone in defining the projects and their viability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of cognitive decline among students and pupils, as a consequence of the prevalence of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in education provides both opportunities and challenges for pupils’ development.  Research suggests it offers significant benefits, including personalised learning, improved academic outcomes, and enhanced student engagement. While AI can generate text, summarise information and support communication, it cannot replace the human need to think deeply, reflect and express ideas authentically and an over-reliance on AI may impact on the critical thinking skills of young people.  AI also holds immense potential to enhance learning efficiency and academic performance, its successful implementation requires addressing concerns related to accuracy, cognitive disengagement, and ethical implications.  A balanced approach without an over reliance on AI is, therefore, essential to ensure young people have broad and effective learning experiences in their educational environments.My officials are working with a range of education partners to develop guidance for schools on Artificial Intelligence.  I anticipate that guidance will be made available to schools in the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the merits of the North contributing to the construction costs of Metro North, due to its importance to travellers from the North accessing Dublin Airport via train.
answer_text: We are aware of the Irish Government’s plans to proceed with the MetroLink project which will include the provision of a direct rail service to Dublin Airport.  Our understanding is that this project is to be funded in full from the Irish Government’s Infrastructure, Climate and Nature Fund with no contribution requested from any other sources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the merits of banning social media for all children under the age of 16.
answer_text: The Department of Education has not undertaken an assessment of banning social media for all children under the age of 16. It is important to note that the regulation of social media platforms is not a devolved matter and sits primarily with the UK Government. In September 2024, I launched new Guidance for Schools on Pupils’ Personal Use of Mobile Phones and other Similar Devices During the School Day, which outlined the emerging evidence between excessive use and declining mental health among adolescents. The guidance also includes some practical advice to support school leaders in restricting the personal use of mobile phones throughout the school day.I am currently piloting a phone-free solution across nine schools that prevents pupils from using their devices during the school day, with the aim of helping them to fully engage with learning, social interaction, and all aspects of school life. This pilot initiative will be independently evaluated by King’s College London and the findings will help to shape future policy decisions in this area that fall within my remit.My focus remains on promoting online safety through the curriculum and guidance for schools. My officials will continue to work closely with the Departments of Health, Justice and other key stakeholders as part of the Online Safety Strategy cross departmental working group to ensure that children and young people across Northern Ireland feel empowered and equipped for navigating the online world safely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether any of the £5.2 million for essential school maintenance, and £3.2 million for schools’ minor works and energy-saving solutions agreed in the December Monitoring round will be used to address remedial works at St. Patrick's Grammar School, Armagh.
answer_text: The additional capital allocations in the December Monitoring round will address in-year pressures and allow progress to be made on urgent capital works across the schools’ estate.The inescapable remedial works at St Patrick’s Grammar School are currently being planned by my Department and the Education Authority and are being progressed as a priority. Due to the complexity of the works and the likely need for decant accommodation, there is unlikely to be significant spend this financial year. Any required spend this financial year will be met from within the existing non-controlled minor works budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the £34.1 million, allocated to her Department for water services, public transport, rail and road projects in the 2025-26 December Monitoring Round, will be spent.
answer_text: At the outset of the financial year, I overplanned against my Department’s opening 2025-26 capital budget, maximising funding at the start of the year. The £34.1m allocated to my Department in the 2025-26 December Monitoring round is in respect of its overplanning commitment and this allocation will enable current capital projects right across my Department and its Arm’s Length Bodies to continue as planned.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of (i) the Operation Kenova Final Report relating to the Glenanne Series; and (ii) the report's impact on victims' families.
answer_text: Operation Denton, conducted under the umbrella of Operation Kenova was a complex review of a series of attacks carried out in the 1970s. We recognise that the publication of this report will have a significant impact on victims and survivors and their families. A key commitment within our Victims and Survivors Strategy is to provide support for victims and survivors, and their families, as they move forward from experiences of the past. The Strategy also recognises the importance of truth and justice and the need to work towards greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of (i) the Operation Kenova Final Report relating to Stakeknife; and (ii) the report's impact on victims' families.
answer_text: Operation Kenova was a complex and wide-ranging investigation launched in 2016 to investigate a series of allegations dating back to the 1970s. We recognise that the publication of this report will have a significant impact on victims and survivors and their families. A key commitment within our Victims and Survivors Strategy is to provide support for victims and survivors, and their families, as they move forward from experiences of the past. The Strategy also recognises the importance of truth and justice and the need to work towards greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact on children as a result of letters sent to Private Home to School Transport Operators on 5 December 2025 requesting them to reduce their tendered contracts cost by 10 per cent.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, which includes the procurement and management of contracts held with private taxi operators. Due to the scale of the anticipated shortfall in the EA’s overall budget this year, it has had to make challenging decisions aimed at reducing costs while maintaining essential services. The EA’s request of taxi operators to voluntarily reduce their invoiced rates by 10% followed an in-depth analysis of current contracts, benchmarking cost per mile against the maximum fare rates for metered taxi transport, which are set out in legislation and were updated in December 2023 to reflect rising costs in the taxi industry. This analysis identified significant variations in fare rates between operators and whilst DfI maximum fare rates for metered journeys form a benchmark only as they do not apply to EA contracts, many operators are charging significantly more than the referenced rates. The EA informs me that no contracts have been terminated, rather it has asked all operators to voluntarily agree to modify their contracts and reduce invoiced rates by 10%. Not all taxi operators will be charging high rates, however where an operator does not agree to a reduction and it is evident that a run is being operated at a rate well in excess of the comparative benchmark, the EA will consider alternatives and may terminate and re-tender the contract.   In writing to all taxi operators, the EA is seeking their cooperation in reducing costs to protect the school transport service and ensure that those pupils who need transport to school can continue to avail of it. The EA assures me it is taking a balanced and considered approach in reviewing the responses received from taxi operators and it will work at pace to minimise any impact on children availing of taxi transport to school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the Operation Kenova Final Report relating to Stakeknife; and (ii) the report's impact on victims' families.
answer_text: I note the publication of the Operation Kenova Final Report and the information it has uncovered. I hope it goes some way to providing the truth and justice victims and families have sought for too long.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the Operation Kenova Final Report relating to the Glenanne Series; and (ii) the report's impact on victims' families.
answer_text: I note the publication of the Operation Kenova Final Report and the information it has uncovered. I hope it goes some way to providing the truth and justice victims and families have sought for too long.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to ensure energy supply resilience, during severe weather events.
answer_text: My Department has in place a Road Fuel Mutual Aid Protocol which sets out the terms for the provision of emergency assistance between my Department’s Key Service Providers when a major emergency has been declared by Central Government or DfE, or when the need for mutual aid is agreed by the core-group’s Chief Executives. This Core Group covers DfI TRAM, DfI Rivers Directorate, NI Water Ltd and Translink, with emergency assistance in the form of shared road fuel reserves to ensure that supply chain resilience has been developed in order to respond to any disruption in supplier services that ultimately may impact on customer service delivery.In addition, my Department’s Rivers Directorate has measures in place to maintain energy supply resilience during severe weather events. These include onsite backup generators at key operational depots and mobile generators that can be deployed to support essential functions, such as, operating sluice gates at control structures. Fuel reserves are maintained to ensure these generators can operate for extended periods during power outages. Rivers Directorate also uses renewable options, such as, solar panels with battery storage, to power essential river monitoring systems.NI Water maintains a robust emergency infrastructure which includes over 300 critical sites that are equipped with permanent fixed generators, and a fleet of 73 mobile units stands ready to respond to short-duration, isolated outages across the North.NI Water will continue to invest in mitigation - fixed generation at critical sites, enhanced telemetry (including satellite access) and strategic planning - but the reality remains that with increasing climate change and a higher than normal incidence of severe storms, in a province-wide electrical and mobile network blackout, full coverage cannot be fully assured.Translink provide diesel generator back up at key locations across the network including Belfast Grand Central Station, Lanyon Place Station and other critical railway infrastructure to support continuity of service during severe weather events.Furthermore, Translink maintains a strategic relationship with both the System Operator for Northern Ireland (SONI), who is responsible for the high voltage transmission system, and Northern Ireland Electricity (NIE), who is responsible for the lower voltage physical network, ensuring that any disruption in energy supply is dealt with as a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how alternative fuels, such as liquefied petroleum gas, are being incorporated into contingency planning for storm-related disruptions.
answer_text: My Department does not incorporate alternative fuels such as liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) into contingency planning arrangements. Furthermore, outside of solar/battery provision, NI Water is currently not incorporating any alternative fuels, such as LPG, into contingency planning for storm-related disruptions.In terms of the Translink rail and bus fleets, the use of alternative fuel types such as LPG has not been considered as a mitigation against storm related disruptions. In both cases the transition from compression ignition to a spark ignition is technically complex, time consuming and costly. On that basis it is not a viable mitigation for short term disruption caused by a storm. Translink have a long term supply agreement with its diesel provider, and together there is stock holding of diesel fuel available to deliver service during extreme weather. In addition, Translink operates a fleet of 247 Zero Emission Hydrogen and Battery Electric buses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what proportion of the energy supply to the schools' estate is provided through (i) Liquified Petroleum Gas (LPG); and (ii) BioLPG.
answer_text: The Education Authority had advised that 2.88% of the energy supply to the school estate is provided through Liquified Petroleum Gas (LPG) and BioLPG makes up 10% of the 2.88% LPG total

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of the energy supply to the hospital estate that is provided through (i) LPG; and (ii) BioLPG.
answer_text: Circa 0.4% of the energy consumed by the hospital estate that is not self- generated from renewable sources is provided by LPG. The proportion of this energy that is BioLPG is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the responses to the consultation on support for low carbon heat in the residential sector, will be published.
answer_text: My Department consulted on future support for low carbon heating in residential properties, which closed in January this year. Officials are currently finalising the analysis of responses provided, which will be used to inform policy decisions on the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, including heat pumps. My Department plans to publish a response to the consultation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any current work with (i) Transport Infrastructure Ireland; and (ii) the National Transport Authority, to improve the integration of cross-border (a) bus services; (b) ticketing; and (c) timetabling, to support commuter and economic links.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The National Transport Authority (NTA) and Translink have arranged to meet in January 2026 in relation to the provision of cross border bus services.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on implementing traffic calming measures on Chancellor's Road, near St Moninna's, Cloughoge.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.Due to the significant housing development currently being constructed in this area, my officials have added this location to their schedule and will undertake a review to assess if the existing safety measures are appropriate. If further engineering safety measures are required, they will be taken into consideration, along with all other prioritised schemes, to be included in a future works programme.I also intend to launch a public consultation in the new year on a number of speed related matters. The consultation will seek views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions.  When the consultation is launched, I would encourage as many people as possible to contribute to this significant and important piece of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support available to parents who should be receiving child maintenance but are not, due to the paying parent being in receipt of benefits.
answer_text: AnswerWhen a Receiving Parent asks the Child Maintenance Service to collect maintenance on their behalf and the Paying Parent is in receipt of a social security benefit, a deduction from the relevant benefit can be made at a flat rate of £7 per week. Should the Paying Parent have shared care of a qualifying child for more than 52 nights in a year, the flat rate is reduced to nil. Once CMS receives the payment it is issued directly to the Receiving Parent’s bank account. The Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service (MtC) provides free, confidential needs assessments to identify eligibility for social security benefits and additional supports. Visit NI Direct www.nidirect.gov.uk/forms/contact-make-call-team

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the potholes on Doran's Hill, outside Thomas Davis GAC, will be (i) mapped; and (ii) repaired.
answer_text: My officials are aware of the condition of Doran’s Hill, outside Thomas Davis GAC and I am pleased to advise work has been instructed to our contractor and is expected to be completed before Christmas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will answer AQW 34624//22-27.
answer_text: I direct the member to the response to AQW 34624/22-27 issued on 8 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: My response of 24 November 2025 to question AQW 35855/22-27 refers.The position remains unchanged.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water provides Human Resources and accounting services to private, independent companies.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and can advise that NI Water does not provide any Human Resources or accounting services to private, independent companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the December 2025 Public Accounts Committee report on Access to General Practice.
answer_text: I welcome the Public Accounts Committee report on Access to General Practice in Northern Ireland and will consider the Committee’s findings and recommendations fully.Indeed, a number of these will be addressed in work that is already in train and set out in my Department’s Reset Plan published in July 2025.There is full agreement on the need for a new vision for primary care and new models of delivery that will meet the need for improved and more equitable access. The approach set out in the Reset Plan to develop a neighbourhood model of care with general practice and wider primary care at its heart will be essential, not only to preserve the best of current models, but also to introduce aspects of working at scale and further support towards integrated multidisciplinary provision that will support patients to receive more consistent out-of-hospital services.We will work in partnership with General Practice, their representative bodies and patients and service users to address the report’s recommendations to improve access to general practice but also to ensure that GPs and primary care staff are supported and enabled to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department will take following the Poverty in Northern Ireland 2025 report, by the Joseph Rowntree Foundation.
answer_text: The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy aims to address the root causes of poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and inequality and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty. The Strategy is focused on addressing the real-life impacts of poverty based on an ongoing analysis of a range of evidence, including the work of organisations like the Joseph Rowntree Foundation, and my officials will consider the recent Report in detail.While the Department for Communities is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy. Following consideration of the analysis of public consultation responses, my Executive colleagues and I will consider further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has undertaken an assessment of the environmental cost of importing apples, over producing apples locally.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment of the environmental cost of importing apples over producing apples locally. International Trade and the negotiation of Free Trade Agreements are not devolved policy areas and imports of agri-food goods, including apples, into Northern Ireland are under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact the new digital road network survey will have on efficiency, in relation to road resurfacing, patching and structural maintenance.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation.The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. This means instead of filling one or two potholes and skipping over others, there will be more patch resurfacing and when this is done, the repairs will last longer because they will be better quality.  This means filling more potholes, where it is needed most.Through this new strategy, my Department will ensure that the road maintenance function is organised to improve day to day effectiveness and enhance our ability to respond to incidents and weather events.Along with this strategy my Department is investing £1.3m to deliver a complete digital survey of the entire road network in the North. This will provide a much greater level of understanding of road condition and allow my officials to take data-driven decisions and deliver more focused maintenance interventions saving money in the longer term.The value of deeper, more accurate information to support intelligent road maintenance is widely recognised. A growing number of Local Authorities are already benefiting from this type of roadscaping technology, however, the North is the first to collect such a vast national data set to produce a national road condition baseline. It’s a landmark initiative that, in just six months, will give my Department an unprecedented understanding of its road infrastructure to better target maintenance, reduce costs, and react quickly to future opportunities and innovations.The survey will be completed by the end of the 25/26 financial year and data will then be processed over a period of a couple of months and used to develop the future structural maintenance programme and inform future in-year monitoring rounds for 2026/27.However, it is important to recognize that these measures on their own will not address the consequences of years of under investment and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made to improve the quality of our road network and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department will address Emergency Department waiting times at Craigavon Area Hospital, in light of reports that it reached twice as long as any other Emergency Department on the morning of 3 December 2025.
answer_text: I fully recognise the significant pressures currently being experienced within emergency departments (EDs), including Craigavon Area Hospital. These pressures are often most visible both outside and within EDs and reflect wider challenges across the entire Health and Social Care system. Seasonal illness affecting both the public and health and social care staff exacerbates these pressures during the winter months.My Winter Plan is designed to mitigate, rather than eradicate, the impact of these challenges. It focuses on improving patient flow, increasing capacity in key areas, and strengthening community-based alternatives to hospital care. This includes measures such as enhanced discharge processes, rapid access clinics, and additional staffing support to manage surges in demand.While COVID-19 presentations remain low, public health monitoring shows influenza cases rising sharply and Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) is now increasing, placing additional strain on primary care, care homes, and hospitals. Current levels are approaching last December’s peak, with hospital admissions increasing, especially among those aged over 75. Vaccination remains our strongest defence: as of 1 December, 457,000 Northern Ireland residents have been vaccinated, including 79% of those aged over 80. All care homes have now had their vaccine programmes offered on site by community pharmacy with a rate of 77% uptake by residents and Health Trusts continue to drive their staff vaccination programmes.  The pressures in EDs are driven not only by attendance numbers but also by the complexity of patient conditions and delays in hospital discharge due to limited social care capacity. This impacts patient flow and waiting times. My long-term vision is to ensure patients access the most appropriate care pathway first time, reducing unnecessary ED attendances through initiatives such as expanding Phone First services, increasing rapid access ambulatory clinics, and strengthening community provision via Hospital at Home.Managing these pressures is complex and requires both immediate actions and longer-term solutions. Staff in EDs continue to work tirelessly under extremely demanding circumstances and deserve recognition for their dedication and professionalism.As part of the Winter Plan and longer-term strategy, my Department is working closely with the Southern Trust to accelerate measures that improve patient flow and alleviate pressures at Craigavon Area Hospital. These include:Urgent Care Centre screening at the front door and enhanced post-triage navigation in ED;Development and strengthening of Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) and ambulatory models, including Frailty, Respiratory, Cardiology, Surgical, and Acute Medical pathways;Enhanced access to diagnostics (bloods and imaging);Full implementation of the site coordinator role and improved ward processes using the SAFER model to support timely care and earlier discharge;Optimisation of discharge lounge use and progress

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the new digital road network survey will reduce the (i) number; and (ii) frequency, of potholes.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation.The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when any repairs stemming from defects identified by the new digital road network survey, will begin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of the new digital road network survey, including any recurring costs.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation.The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any actions she (i) has taken; and (ii) plans to take, to improve the speed of justice, including timeframes for those actions.
answer_text: Tackling delay is one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system in Northern Ireland and is a key priority for myself and relevant justice agencies. The Criminal Justice Board (CJB) has prioritised a programme of work, based on five workstreams, covering Early Engagement, Out of Court Disposals, Committal Reform, Remit of the Magistrates’ Courts, and Digital.Together, these workstreams seek to reform the way in which cases are handled, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, and reducing the demands on courts and the wider system.  This is a five year programme of work.  Some of the key milestones include: The first phase of Committal Reform was implemented in October 2022, seeing the abolition of the option of calling victims and witnesses to provide oral evidence during committal proceedings in the magistrates’ court, helping reduce stress placed on victims and witnesses who previously may have been called to provide oral evidence more than once.The launch of the second phase of Committal Reform - direct committal is due to be introduced in November 2026.The introduction of an expansion to the range of out of court disposals available to the PSNI, which we hope to progress in the new year. A 12-week consultation exercise ran from the 7th July this year and my department is currently analysing responses and developing proposals on the way forward.  The exploration of opportunities for early engagement between police and prosecution and prosecution and defence, to speed up case progression and maximise efficiencies. This workstream is also exploring options in relation to ‘Better Case Management’, with a pilot scheduled to launch in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given their Together: Building a United Community Strategy, for their assessment of the paramilitary threat issued to elected representatives in Newry, Mourne, and Down.
answer_text: We unequivocally condemn any threats against elected officials. There is no place in our society for any type of intolerance, hatred, intimidation or violence.  We will continue to work with Executive colleagues to eliminate the influence of paramilitarism from our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the merits of the Housing Executive implementing a formal fast-track process for individuals with progressive conditions, such as motor neurone disease.
answer_text: I recognise that there are some exceptional cases for which we may consider prioritising while being mindful of the need for fairness and equity. The Housing Executive are taking forward a pilot to trial an accelerated process for delivering adaptation provision for exceptional cases, across all housing tenures.This pilot is part of the review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants, led by my Department. The findings from this review will inform policy proposals which I will launch for public consultation in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of victims of violence against women and girls who withdrew from the justice process because of undue delays, in each of the last ten years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on the circumstances relating to number of victims of violence against women and girls who withdrew from the justice process, so, from information held, it is not possible to answer the question asked.  We do however recognise the impact that delay has on victims and that this may contribute to victim attrition.  The reasons for victim attrition are not routinely recorded at all stages of the process.  Nonetheless, the Department of Justice has worked with PSNI and the PPS to conduct some research into the reasons for victim attrition in Serious Sexual Offences. The findings of that research will be published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of providing travel reimbursement to full time students for hospital visits and appointments.
answer_text: The Department of Health currently provides assistance with health costs, including travel costs, for individuals who qualify through income-related benefits. Eligibility for these benefits is determined by the Department for Communities. Depending on their personal circumstances, part-time students and some full-time students may qualify for income-related benefits and may therefore receive help with hospital travel costs. Students and prospective students can seek advice on their eligibility from their local Jobs and Benefits Office or student adviser. There is also support on the nidirect website at the following link: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/benefits-higher-education-students.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the merits of abolishing homework.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland currently there is no specific legislation governing homework policy in schools. It is a matter for each school to devise its own written homework policy which, once developed and agreed, should be shared with parents. Any policy should state the average time a pupil is required to spend on homework and the action that should be taken should this time be exceeded. The Education Authority can provide advice and guidance to schools, as required, when setting and reviewing their homework policies.  The Education (School Information and Prospectuses) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 require all schools to include a summary of their homework policy in the school prospectus. (see paragraph 11 in Schedule 3). The Department has long-standing guidance on homework for primary school children which emphasises that where homework is set, it is important that it does not place pupils under unnecessary anxiety and stress and needs to be balanced against time for family life and the need for children to develop other interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the three operators of the Shared Island Sports Club Electric Vehicle Charging Scheme were appointed.
answer_text: The Shared Island Sports Club Electric Vehicle Charging Scheme has been solely run by Pobal on behalf of the Zero Emission Vehicle Ireland (ZEVI) Office of the Department of Transport in the South. My Department was not involved in the procurement framework, tender process, or appointments for the Scheme. To understand their tendering and appointment process, ZEVI can be contacted at ZEVIreland@transport.gov.ie.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of introducing a series of legal and regulatory reforms, to accelerate the development of infrastructure and housing.
answer_text: I am aware of proposals being brought forward by the Dublin Government and I will engage with my counterparts on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any avenues available to a parent when seeking extended visitation through the court system when the case continues to be delayed, due to the parent with custody being unable to be located to be served.
answer_text: Most applications relating to arrangements for children have to be commenced in a family proceedings court (magistrates’ courts tier) and will require the issue of a summons. Court rules require personal service of a summons by a summons server and if this is unsuccessful and the court gives permission, service may then be effected by post, firstly registered post and then ordinary post.A couple of years ago, the Magistrates’ Courts Rules Committee was invited to consider whether amendment of the rules applicable to service of a summons in children proceedings would be desirable. However, the Committee concluded there was no evidence base demonstrating the need for any amendment at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an ambulance, broken down by health and social care trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Ambulance call categorisation for emergency calls is standardised across the UK. Ambulance call category definitions and response time standards are set out in Table 1 below. Table 1Call TypeCategoryMean StandardImmediately life threateningCat 18 Mins999 Emergency – potentially serious incidentsCat 218 MinsUrgent problemCat 3*120 MinsNon- emergency ED visitsCat 4 *180 Mins	*This is the 90th centile standard for category 3 and category 4 calls as there is not a mean standard.	Table 2, shows average waiting times for an ambulance, broken down by health and social care trust, in each of the last five years. Ambulance handover delays at Emergency Departments significantly impact NIAS’s ability to respond to emergency calls in the community, making improvement in handover times a key priority for my Department.NIAS receives over 200,000 calls annually and dispatches crews to approximately 170,000 incidents. At a regional workshop in August 2025, the Permanent Secretary, supported by the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, endorsed the implementation of regional ambulance handover guidance. All Trusts agreed to implement initiatives aimed at reducing delays, with a target of achieving the two-hour standard by December 2025.To support improved ambulance offload and response times, the Department has repurposed £10 million for targeted initiatives across Trusts, including:Expansion of Hospital at Home services to increase community-based capacity;Additional short-stay inpatient beds to facilitate timely discharge;Frailty assessments and multidisciplinary reviews at the front door of acute hospitals;Expansion of Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) and ambulatory pathways across acute medicine, respira

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Autumn Budget 2025.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to protect consumers from planned obsolescence, in the technology sector.
answer_text: The Department’s Trading Standards Service works to ensure that existing consumer rights under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 are upheld. The Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires that goods be of satisfactory quality, fit for purpose, as described and last a reasonable length of time. Consumers who believe products have failed prematurely can seek redress under these provisions, including a refund, repair or replacement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has carried out a feasibility assessment in relation to (i) improved streetlighting on Kidds Road, Newry; and (ii) sightline improvement and safety measures at the junction of Kidds Road and Craigmore Road, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. An initial assessment of this location considers the speed limit to be in line with departmental policy and given the current level of development and nature of the road, we cannot provide any further road safety measures at this location.However, as part of my commitment to road safety, I intend to launch a public consultation in the new year on a number of speed related matters. The consultation will seek views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions.  My Department’s policy for street lighting in rural locations considers factors such as the density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant night time use as well as areas where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night time collisions.  However, the desire for lighting has to be balanced against environmental considerations, our desire to reduce the carbon footprint and electricity costs associated with our infrastructure, and limited budgets. Decisions on provision or removal of lighting are made taking account of these considerations. My officials have assessed Kidds Road and confirmed that the section does not fulfil the above criteria for street lighting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of implementing a pre-diagnostic support plan, for families of children who are seeking a neurodivergence assessment, that includes (i) parenting programmes tailored to neurodiversity; (ii) behavioural and emotional regulation support; and (iii) school-based early adjustments.
answer_text: My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework proposes an inclusive and needs-based approach neurodiversity which will not require diagnosis or diagnostic labels as a precursor to assistance and intervention. In adopting this approach, The Framework seeks to reduce stigma, to promote earlier intervention for children and young people and to enhance outcomes for young people across the spectrum of their health, education and development. The development of this Framework has been informed by a review carried out of existing pathways across both Children’s Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) services. The Framework will also seek to clarify the roles and responsibilities of everyone tasked with working to support children and young people presenting with neuro-developmental and emotional health and wellbeing needs. This is not only within Health but across departments, including the Department of Education. The Department is currently considering responses to the consultation on the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. Work is currently ongoing with colleagues from the PHA and Child Health to develop the definitions and requirements that will underpin this pathway that will support commissioning going forward. My Department has commissioned a regional needs assessment on ADHD demand across all Trusts including the Northern Trust. This initiative represents an important point of departure in establishing the basis upon which a future commissioning model in support of ADHD services across Trusts might be based. In approaching this exercise, the primary objectives are to gauge the overall demand for an ADHD service based upon a model of provision across the lifespan, and to provide detailed recommendations to inform discussion. It is envisaged that these recommendations will offer a view upon the proposed structure of a future clinical model, the necessary steps that might be required to ensure regional consistency and equity of access, an exploration of operational feasibility and proposals associated with implementation and staffing costs.  My Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. Any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services. I hope you find this information helpful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of implementing a universal post-diagnostic package, for families of children who receive a neurodivergence diagnosis, that includes (i) key worker allocation; (ii) a personalised support plan; and (iii) access to (a) occupational therapy, (b) speech and language; (c) psychological support; and (d) school training and guidance.
answer_text: My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework endorses an inclusive, needs-based approach to neurodiversity designed to help reduce stigma and the perceived need to obtain a diagnosis in order to access support through Health and Education services. The Framework will include options for support at both pre and post assessment intervals whether a diagnosis is confirmed or not. Responses to the consultation have been very positive and officials are currently considering the feedback on offer.Autism and ADHD services currently offer post diagnostic support including personalised needs-based care plans. At the end of the assessment process for Autism and/or ADHD, parents and children/young people, depending upon their age and level of understanding will have a clinical consultation which will include discussion around options for treatment for ADHD and post-diagnostic support and intervention for ASD. The assessing Clinician may also refer to other services based on the individual needs which have been identified during the assessment. Such services might include consideration to input from Occupational Therapy (OT), Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) and Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS). Children and young people may also be referred to OT, SLT and Psychological services by their GP or another clinician involved in their care, however these services will require significant investment as there is more demand than commissioned capacity to provide this in a timely way.While Trusts have developed a range of post-diagnostic supports within their existing resources, implementing a universal, standardised package for neurodivergent conditions, including ASD, ADHD, Tourettes syndrome, developmental co-ordination disorder, sensory processing disorders as well as specific learning difficulties such as dyslexia, dysgraphia and dyscalculia is not feasible without regional commissioning, recurring investment, and multi-agency alignment. Consideration of the potential benefit of appointing a key worker or named contact will take place within the wide-ranging activity underpinning the implementation of the EHWB Framework.  The potential role and function of a key worker will need to be discussed with service users to understand their expectations of the role and how it would best meet their needs. Autism and ADHD services in Health and Social Care Trusts do not provide school training and do not provide support into schools when there are school based issues. The intervention teams work closely with the Education Authority Local Impact Teams (LIT) to provide a joined-up approach to support children/young people when there are school based issues.The Regional Integrated Support for Education NI (RISE NI) service offers support to Teachers and Classroom Assistants, as well as directly to children and their parents / carers, focused within Foundation Stage an

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether dental practices will receive National Insurance mitigations in full, in this financial year.
