answer_text: The current agreed approach is to pay the funding to offset the cost of National Insurance Contributions in two instalments, the first in December 2025 and the second in April/May 2026, aligned with the Practice Allowance payment schedule. This approach was agreed with the British Dental Association as the most practical and quickest way to award payments. There is however scope to review the payment methodology for future years to ensure it remains practical and responsive to the needs of General Dental Practitioners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any preventative measures her Department will put in place to ensure residents, surrounding Newry Train Station, do not have their driveways blocked due to overflow parking from the station.
answer_text: My Department has the powers to enforce legislated parking restrictions, which are typically marked with a single or double yellow line. Traffic Attendants (TAs) can only enforce where these restrictions exist. However, if a vehicle is parked in such a way as to cause obstruction or danger, including parking over driveways, this should be reported to the PSNI, who can take enforcement action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding support available for dancers competing in the Irish Dancing World Championships, in Chicago, in 2026.
answer_text: The Arts Council has advised the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme: Travel Awards scheme is currently open for applications.  These awards enable individual artists and established music groups (up to 2 members) to travel from Northern Ireland to develop their skills and expertise. Further information can be found on the Art Council’s website: https://artscouncil-ni.org/funding-for-individuals/siap-travel-awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the merits of funding the organisation of street cèilidhs in towns and villages on St Patricks Day, as a celebration of our Irish patron Saint, culture and, dance.
answer_text: I have made no assessment of the merits of funding the organisation of street cèilidhs. Community groups wishing to mark St. Patrick’s Day may be eligible to apply for support through their local council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether his Department has conducted a risk assessment of the impact of AI super-intelligence on cybersecurity.
answer_text: My department maintains a strong partnership with the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) to ensure we are well-positioned to understand the risks and opportunities AI presents for cyber security. Assessments from the NCSC such as the ‘Impact of AI on cyber threat from now to 2027’ report (published on 7 May 2025), and related documents, help us identify and implement the necessary interventions to mitigate these risks and enhance our cyber resilience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 33916/22-27, for her assessment of the Minister of Finance's comments to the Assembly on 25 November 2025, that her legislation on developer contributions is at Second Stage.
answer_text: On 21 March I launched a consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure. The consultation ran for 14 weeks and sought views on options for a possible pathway to introduce developer contributions – either on a voluntary basis or in the form of a compulsory levy – which may help to fund wastewater upgrades to unlock the capacity of our sewerage network. 293 responses to the consultation were received. I am currently considering my decision on the way forward on developer contributions with a view to announcing this in the coming weeks. No legislation on this topic is being progressed at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have passed away waiting for an ambulance to arrive, in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has for increasing access to out of hours healthcare services in Newry and South Armagh.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the Office for Environmental Protection’s investigation into his Department, the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and the Utility Regulator, how his Department has engaged with DfI to address sewage pollution.
answer_text: I have corresponded and met regularly with Minister Kimmins and her officials in respect of sewage pollution. My officials are also engaged with DfI officials on a range of forums focused on improving water quality with respect to sewage pollution. These include the Lough Neagh Stakeholder Forums; the Living with Water engagement; the Price Control 21/Price Control 27/Price Control 28 Environmental Quality Working Group; and the Output Review Group.I and my officials will continue working positively with DfI colleagues on this issue going forward and to provide relevant evidence to the OEP investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to install a speed monitor at Mullinure Lane, Co. Armagh, to address road safety concerns.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Concerns regarding driver behaviour including speeding at this location should be directed to the PSNI, who are responsible for enforcement of the speed limit, using their non-emergency 101 number.The provision of speed indicator devices (SIDs) are the responsibility of the Policing and Community Safety Partnership (PCSP) and you should liaise directly with them via the local Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of implementing a young cancer patient travel fund to assist children and young people with cancer attend their medical treatments and appointments, given that one in ten are missing or delaying their treatment because of travel costs and given the income and benefits eligibility criteria of the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme.
answer_text: I recognise that patients undergoing treatment for long term conditions such as cancer may incur additional travel costs that can add additional financial pressure at an already difficult time. The Help with Health Costs (HwHC) scheme, provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with help for travel costs associated with medical treatment on referral from a doctor or dentist. The scheme is open to individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes. Additionally, my Department’s current policy for car parking provision and management includes guidance on free and concessionary parking for patients on particular care paths such as cancer. Given the significant deficit in My Department’s budget, it is not possible to consider any further financial assistance for young cancer patients and their families at this time. I would encourage any cancer patient or family affected by cancer who are experiencing difficulties with travel costs associated with their treatment to discuss these with their health care provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent announcement of an investigation by the Office for Environmental Protection on sewage spills into Belfast Lough, whether she will consider issuing fines to NI Water for its role in sewage pollution.
answer_text: I welcome the Office for Environmental Protection’s investigation into the regulation of sewage discharges into Belfast Lough and can advise that my Department will cooperate fully with this investigation. However, it is important to note that the Department of Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs’ Northern Ireland Environment Agency regulates the sewage discharges into Belfast Lough and  can and has  prosecuted NI Water for water pollution offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the current investigation against Shein in France, to detail any measures her Department is taking to ensure that online retail outlets are not vending and distributing merchandise, that violate child protection laws.
answer_text: My Department does not have enforcement powers in relation to child protection laws governing online content or merchandise. These matters fall under the remit of other law enforcement agencies, such as the Police Service and Ofcom, the communications regulator, which is responsible for implementing the Online Safety Act 2023. This legislation places duties on online platforms to prevent illegal content, including material harmful to children, through measures such as age assurance, risk assessments, and prompt removal of prohibited content.The Advertising Standards Agency (ASA), Britain’s independent advertising regulator, is also responsible for ensuring that adverts comply with the Committee of Advertising Practice (CAP) Code. My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) is responsible for consumer protection, including ensuring compliance with regulations such as the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 and The Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013. However, the consumer protection framework enforced by the TSS does not extend to regulating child protection laws. While the TSS does not directly regulate child protection issues in online retail, it will work with other agencies, such as Ofcom, the ASA and the Police to promote fair trading practices and to share intelligence, when necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any legal aid services available to support victims of domestic violence in pursuing justice.
answer_text: Victims of domestic violence can access legal aid to obtain legal advice and/or representation in cases such as applying for Non-Molestation Orders – an order aimed at preventing someone from being violent, threatening violence, harassing or intimidating them; or Occupation Orders – excluding someone from their home; and also in family proceedings involving child contact or residence where the safety of the victim or their children may be at risk.The Department introduced a waiver removing the financial eligibility limits for Non-Molestation Orders and Article 8 Childrens Contact Orders. The uptake of these waivers has been lower than expected for various reasons, with respondents to a recent Review of Civil Legal Services suggesting that the applications for the waiver were too complex, requiring a lot of evidence – while their representatives said that remuneration for work done in connection with these types of cases was too low.I have met with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime to look at how we can work together to improve our family court system for victims of domestic abuse, and in particular ensure that their safety and well being as well as any children are being given the utmost priority. The Department is committed to ensuring victims of domestic violence receive the support they need to access justice and protection in a simpler way, without financial barriers.As part of an upcoming engagement exercise, views are being sought on new ways of providing legal help for victims of domestic abuse. This will include consideration of reform options to simplify the application process and ensure victims can access legal aid quickly and effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail which matters within her Department, are not operational matters.
answer_text: My Department’s purpose is to maintain and enhance critical infrastructure to improve quality of life for everyone, promote sustainable living, and stimulate economic growth. My Department undertakes this through the setting of strategic direction, the formation and delivery of policy, development of legislation, management of capital investment in infrastructure projects, sponsorship of its Arm’s-Length Bodies and the delivery of strategic planning inter alia.  In addition to these strategic matters, my Department is directly responsible for a number of operational matters such as road maintenance, MOT testing, driver licensing and managing the impact of major weather events.My Department’s Arm’s-Length Bodies (ALBs), including NI Water and Translink, are responsible for a range of operational matters such as the management of water services and public transport.  Under the governance framework set out in Managing Public Money (NI), each Departmental Arm’s Length Body (ALB) has its own Accounting Officer. This officer is responsible and accountable for the ALB’s day-to-day operations and governance, including financial management, service delivery, risk management, and human resources. Each ALB therefore has its own operational independence to deliver its services in alignment with the objectives and policies set by my Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have made any representations regarding implementing safety guardrails when building AI systems.
answer_text: We have consistently emphasised the importance of ethical and responsible use of Artificial Intelligence across all Executive Departments. This commitment is being delivered through the work of the Office of AI and Digital, which is working collaboratively with government departments, industry, and academia to formulate detailed guidance and risk frameworks for AI projects. This includes measures to strengthen oversight, accountability, and ethical standards throughout the lifecycle of AI systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of installing hoists and adult-sized changing benches in all acute and community hospitals, to address the personal care needs of disabled people.
answer_text: The provision of facilities such as hoists and adult-sized changing benches in acute and community hospitals is a matter for individual Health and Social Care Trusts, who are best placed to assess local needs and make decisions on installation based on service requirements and available resources. Accordingly, I have sought input from each Trust on the facilities and the responses provided by the Trusts are set out below:Belfast Health and Social Care TrustThe use of hoists and adult-sized changing benches in acute and community hospitals, to address the personal care needs of disabled people offers significant benefits for the disabled person and their carer as they assist in maintaining patient safety and enhancing dignity. The use of a hoist in the delivery of patient care, is based on an individual needs assessment in collaboration with the wider multidisciplinary team. The Trust will continue to design new buildings and refurbishment projects to comply with the latest statutory standards. This may include the provision of facilities and equipment such as hoists and adult changing tables.Northern Health and Social Care TrustThe Northern Trust fully recognises the necessity for all patients, visitors, and staff to access safe, dignified, and appropriate toileting and changing facilities. We are committed to ensuring our services are inclusive and accessible for everyone, in line with our obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act 1995.We support the merits of installing hoists and adult-sized changing benches and the Trust will continue to design new buildings and refurbishment projects to comply with the latest statutory standards. This may include the provision of facilities and equipment such as hoists and adult changing tables.Southern Health and Social Care TrustThe Southern Trust strives to cater for the needs of its service users where possible however there are considerable constraints in relation to the age and historical facilities provided within its estate, and the significant shortage of funding with competing priorities to address any deficiencies in this area. The Trust will continue to design new buildings and refurbishment projects to comply with the latest statutory standards. This may include the provision of facilities and equipment such as hoists and adult changing tables.South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustIn the South Eastern Trust all Physical Disabilities Day Services facilities have hoists and adult changing tables to meet service users’ needs. In addition, within the Lisburn Primary Care and Community Centre on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site there is a “Changing Places” toilet facility. Whilst we recognise the merits of these facilities across all sites the feasibility may be challenging. The South Eastern HSC Trust will continue to design new buildings and refurbishment projects to comply with the latest statutory standards. This may include the provision of facilities and equipment such as hoists and

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the merits of exempting student bursary funding from Universal Credit's income deductions.
answer_text: Certain elements of a bursary award are currently disregarded in a Universal Credit award calculation.My Department has not completed an assessment of the merits of exempting all student bursary funding from Universal Credit’s income deductions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timeframe by which each statutory consultee must respond as part of the planning application process.
answer_text: Under article 15 of The Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015,statutory consultees to the planning system have a duty to respond to consultation requests from planning authorities in relation to planning applications within 21 calendar days.  Extensions can be also agreed between a consultee and the relevant planning authority to accommodate complex cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when Meadowbrook Park, Newry, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including Meadowbrook Park, Newry, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.  Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Newry & Mourne District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of the road network that has been gritted as part of the Winter Gritting Schedule in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements have been finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are now complete and salt stocks have been replenished.Our winter service operation remains in place from October to April and every night up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt around 7,000 km of the main road network main roads, helping drivers to cope with the wintry conditions. This year I have allocated funding of £ 7.3m to ensure that my Department can deliver a full winter service programme to help keep our roads safe during snow and icy conditions. As part of the winter service, the Department salts the main through routes which carry more than 1,500 vehicles per day. In exceptional circumstances, roads with difficult topography carrying between 1,000 and 1,500 vehicles daily will also be salted. This means that 28% of the road network will be salted, directly benefiting around 80% of road users.Gritting actions can be dependent upon localised weather conditions with gritting actions varying from a full action across the whole of the North, to more localised actions or actions on high ground routes only.  As a result of these localised decisions, details of the percentage of the road network that has been gritted over the last ten years is not held within my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will be replenishing grit piles and grit bins on roadsides.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.I can confirm that our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. During the winter months grit piles and salt bins are inspected during inclement weather and replenished from our reserves as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale behind allowing Just Park to change the process of paying for parking on the app from a stop clock system to pre-paying for a set amount of time; and (ii) for her assessment of any negative consequences for motorists.
answer_text: The introduction of the new parking enforcement contract on 1 November 2025 included a change in the operation of cashless parking. This change saw the replacement of the legacy “Start/Stop” operating model to a “Buy Time” model. The Department considers the new system to provide several benefits to the user and local businesses including a more user friendly and transparent pricing system, which reduces accidental overcharges as a result of customers forgetting to stop their parking event and provides the total cost of the parking event at the point of purchase.The change also provides improved space turnover, as it encourages the pre planning of parking events, reducing overstays and creating a higher rate of turnover to benefit local business.The system also remains flexible with users still able to extend the parking duration remotely, up to the maximum permitted stay, and creates consistency with pay and display machines.The Department continues to monitor customer feedback and transaction data to ensure the system remains fair, accessible, and effective.  Cashless transactions have remained steady at 52% of all charged parking events, demonstrating that user confidence in the system has not been impacted and ensuring continued uptake of digital payment options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34898/22-27, to detail  (i) whether any Local Development Plan is currently out of date; (ii) any timetable that has been agreed by her Department for the completion of each new Local Development Plan; and (iii) when each new Local Development Plans will be published.
answer_text: All Councils are currently preparing new Local Development Plans (LDPs) to provide the spatial framework for development for their areas over a 15-year period. While this work is being taken forward, planning decisions in these Council areas will be made on the basis of extant Development Plan documents and retained planning policy together with the Strategic Planning Policy Statement. Any relevant supplementary and best practice guidance will also continue to apply in this transitional period. Legislation requires LDPs to be prepared in accordance with timetables agreed by my Department. My officials have agreed timetables with all councils. Legislation requires these to be kept under review and updated as necessary to ensure that the preparation of the LDP is proceeding in accordance with the timetable. Councils are also required to consider the need to update their evidence base and notional plan end dates to take account of any slippage in their timetables.Nine out of the eleven councils have published development plan documents, six of which, have adopted Plan Strategies and are working towards their Local Policies Plan publication. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils are due to publish draft Plan Strategy documents next year. Irrespective of plan end dates, LDPs will continue to be material to decision making until such times that they are replaced.The timing of Local Development Plan publication is primarily a matter for each Council to decide in line with its priorities and the resources available to it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 33105/22-27, to outline the schemes in the Sustainable Agriculture Policy that can help the green growth of the apple growing industry in County Armagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of school bus safety legislation.
answer_text: A team within my Department is examining options to improve safety for school journeys, including potential legislation. This work is evidence-led to ensure interventions are effective and proportionate.Officials are progressing at pace with the aim of bringing forward recommendations by early to mid-2026.The Department remains committed to improving road safety and will continue to engage with stakeholders to deliver the most effective measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the proposed changes to Personal Independence Payment (PIP); and (ii) the implications for vulnerable people in receipt of PIP.
answer_text: During the second reading of the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill in Parliament, the UK Government indicated that any future changes to the eligibility conditions for Personal Independence Payment will be subject to the outcome of a review in relation to the assessment criteria.The UK Government has now launched the Timms Review of Personal Independence Payment. The review is expected to conclude in Autumn 2026 and there are no plans to make any changes before it is completed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any arrangements made in relation to the Inter-Departmental Working Group on Historical Institutional Abuse, given that the independent chair role has not been renewed.
answer_text: The completion of the Historical Clerical Child Abuse research and provision of advice marked the conclusion of this piece of work by the IDWG. The independent chair’s contract ended on 19 November when her contract expired.  We express our thanks to Lisa for all her work. Officials will continue to keep members of the IDWG Historical Clerical Child Abuse Reference Group and others updated as this work progresses.  Our deliberations on the research reports are continuing.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 30044/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 30044/22-27 was answered on 17 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether there are any plans to increase the current bursary available for student nurses; and (ii) any financial resources available to support student nurses.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to review the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students, including student nurses. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation, which I hope will launch early next year. My Department covers the tuition fee for all commissioned student nurses and provides a non-means tested bursary of £5165 during each year of their studies. In addition, my Department also provides means-tested elements i.e., for student nurses with dependents or childcare costs.In some circumstances, such as financial hardship, student nurses can apply for extra financial help during their studies to their university or college, through their Support Funds. My Department does not hold responsibility for the allocation of these funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the level of sickness absence for school staff working with children with additional needs.
answer_text: The Department of Education is not the employer of teachers or school support staff. We have therefore requested information from the Education Authority (EA) on this issue.  The EA has advised that over the 12-month period 01 October 2024 to 30 September 2025:The EA absence rate for SEN Classroom Assistants (6.11% of available working days) was marginally lower than the absence rate for all EA school-based support staff (6.15% of available working days).Teaching absence within SEN settings (13.25 average days per annum) was higher than the overall absence rate for teachers across the Northern Ireland school sector (8.62 average days per annum).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the level of funding each local council received for the Go Succeed business support programme.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the administration of Go Succeed or decide the allocation of funding between councils for delivery of it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the sustainability of liquid gas as an alternative to oil for reducing carbon emissions.
answer_text: Earlier this year, following a period of stakeholder engagement, my Department carried out a call for evidence to assess the role of liquid biofuels, including BioLPG, could play in moving households and businesses away from fossil fuels for heating. This work is progressing through the final stages of analysis and review, with a report presenting the findings of the call for evidence due to be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of recent comments in the media that the new regional breast cancer service is not functioning, and they are not meeting the targets for women who are red-flagged.
answer_text: Prior to the implementation of the regional waiting list for breast assessment there was wide variation in access to breast services across the region, with some Trusts consistently underperforming due to limited diagnostic capacity, workforce shortages, and infrastructure constraints. This inequity resulted in a postcode lottery for patients, undermining the principle of universal, high-quality care. The establishment of a single regional waiting list for breast assessment was necessary and has addressed variation in waiting times across the region.I recognise that delays in access to breast assessment will heighten patient anxiety, but I am reassured by the gradual improvement in waiting times in recent weeks, with women now waiting around seven weeks to be seen. While this is still too long, I anticipate that this will continue to reduce. This proves that where additional capacity is available, the regional waiting list can achieve its target.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for a first-time (i) psychology; and (ii) rheumatology, appointment in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rational for postgraduate medical students being ineligible for his Department's bursary funding offered to undergraduate medical students; and (ii) whether there are any plans to implement a funding scheme to support postgraduate medical students.
answer_text: The Education (Student Support) (No.2) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 preclude students who already hold a primary degree awarded by an institution in Northern Ireland or Great Britain, from obtaining fee loans or maintenance grants for a second primary degree. This approach is based on the principle that the limited financial resources available should be targeted at primary degree level, thereby allowing a greater number of students to experience third level study. My officials are conducting an ongoing review of the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the amount of water consumed by data centers, in each of the past five years; and (ii) the cost per unit, of water consumed.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that the term data centre is not used to define or bill customers; therefore, it is unable to answer the question in the format requested.All non-domestic customers are billed in line with NI Water’s Scheme of Charges which is approved by the Utility Regulator.  The latest scheme of charges can be found on NI Water’s website at Non Domestic Charges .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any efforts his Department is making to end care in hospital corridors.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ending the practice of caring for patients in hospital corridors by improving patient flow and increasing capacity across both hospital and community settings.To support Trusts in managing unscheduled care pressures, £10 million has been re-purposed to provide additional capacity ahead of winter and beyond. Measures being implemented include:Expansion of Hospital at Home services, including new provision in some Trusts, enabling patients to receive acute-level care in their own homes and avoid unnecessary hospital admission;Additional short-stay inpatient beds and step-down/intermediate care beds to improve discharge pathways and free up acute capacity;Enhanced ambulatory and Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) for acute medicine, frailty, respiratory, and cardiology services, reducing the need for overnight admission;Dedicated frailty pathways, including rapid assessment and intervention at the front door, to ensure older and vulnerable patients receive timely, appropriate care and avoid prolonged stays;Multidisciplinary team assessment at the front door to direct patients to the most suitable care setting;Early review teams to recycle community capacity and support timely discharge; and Investment in community-based services, including intermediate care and rehabilitation, to ensure patients can transition safely from hospital to home and maintain independence.These initiatives aim to avoid unnecessary admissions, provide alternative pathways for patients, and ensure timely access to inpatient beds for those who need them. Collectively, they will strengthen hospital resilience during periods of peak demand and reduce the need for patients to be cared for in corridors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34899/22-27, to detail (i) the reasons for the Enterprise trains, currently operating with reduced carriages, and (ii) whether there are any plans to increase the number of carriages available on Enterprise journeys.
answer_text: As per my answer to 34899/22-27, this issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested additional information and have been advised as follows:(i) The cross-border corridor is operated by a mixture of NI Railways and Irish Rail fleet, comprising DeDietrich Loco hauled Enterprise train sets, Irish Rail ICR units, and NI Railways CAF units. The Enterprise set is composed of seven carriages, a Generator Van, and a Locomotive. Notably, the train configuration has remained unaltered over the past two months, with no reductions in capacity.  However, the CAF train availability over the past two months has been more challenging, occasionally operated with reduced capacity. Six CAF units are fitted with a Republic of Ireland rail safety system known as CAWS (Continuous Automatic Warning System). This is an old system which Irish Rail is in the process of replacing.  Given the age of the equipment, Irish Rail is not in a position to offer spare CAWS units, which ultimately limits the number of CAF train units that can ever operate on its network. Low Rail Adhesion has in recent weeks had an adverse impact on this small pool of CAF vehicles.  An increase in wheel defects has limited the availability, resulting in some trains having to be ‘shortformed’.  Translink continues to make every effort to ensure that Cross Border services operate with a minimum of 6 carriages. (ii) There are currently no short-term plans to increase the planned number of carriages on the Cross-Border trains. Services are however supplemented by additional special trains during events e.g. GAA and Rugby matches, to provide additional capacity. Translink and Irish Rail are currently involved in a project to procure 8 new 8 carriage Enterprise trains with the support of DfI, DoT and SEUPB. The project is at the procurement stage. These trains would be planned to enter service by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the role her Department takes in training local council planning officers, particularly in regards to renewable energy technologies.
answer_text: While it remains important that local councils should invest in training for their own planning officers and other stakeholders involved in the planning process, my department also has an important role in supporting and delivering training where this is necessary to uphold the integrity of the planning system. This includes supporting compliance with strategic policy objectives and international obligations, particularly in areas of strategic importance such as renewable energy development.Training provided by the department may be proactive, anticipating emerging challenges, or reactive, responding to specific issues as they arise. It can range from general capacity building initiatives to highly targeted interventions addressing complex or technical matters.A recent example of this approach was the development and delivery of bespoke Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) training in partnership with an external expert. This programme offered two levels of accredited training, Core and Advanced, accredited by the Institute of Environmental Management and Assessment (IEMA). Over 250 planning staff from all eleven local planning authorities, as well as departmental staff, successfully completed this training.Looking ahead, my department will continue to consider training support, in conjunction with local planning authorities in key policy areas, including renewable energy technologies, to support sound environmental decision-making.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the proportion of planning application fees allocated to (i) planning authorities; and (ii) statutory consultees.
answer_text: Planning authorities charge fees for planning applications in accordance with The Planning Fees Regulations (NI) 2015 (as amended); with each planning authority retaining their own fee income. Fee income received into my department relating to regionally significant applications forms part of general revenue and is not earmarked for specific purposes.  Funding for statutory consultees is a matter for the relevant organisation and sponsoring government department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for the delays in publishing the updated Strategic Planning Policy on Renewable and Low Carbon Energy.
answer_text: As indicated in my previous answer (AQW-35111-22-27), having recently secured Executive Committee agreement to the revised regional planning policy on renewable and low carbon energy, I intend to publish the Strategic Planning Policy Statement, Edition 2 as soon as possible, once all necessary next steps have been completed, which includes the preparations for publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the basis on which planning application fees are allocated to (i) planning authorities; and (ii) statutory consultees.
answer_text: Planning authorities charge fees for planning applications in accordance with The Planning Fees Regulations (NI) 2015 (as amended); with each planning authority retaining their own fee income. Funding for statutory consultees is a matter for the relevant sponsoring Government Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) planning staff currently employed by her Department; (ii) planning staff currently assigned to regionally significant planning applications; and (iii) regionally significant planning applications, currently being processed by her Department.
answer_text: There are 63 staff employed across the Regional Planning directorates comprising of 37 professional and technical planning staff and 26 administrative staff.  In addition, there are   2 Sandwich Students on 51 week contracts.   The Regional Planning Casework team has 7 professional and technical staff who contribute to the processing of regionally significant planning applications. However, given other departmental priorities and responsibilities, they do not work exclusively on regionally significant planning applications.Currently 9 regionally significant planning applications are being processed by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a risk assessment has been conducted to establish any consequences of delayed remedial works for pupils and staff at St. Patrick's Grammar School, Armagh.
answer_text: A risk assessment has not been undertaken for the remedial works at St Patrick’s Grammar School. The remedial works to address the damp and heating issues at the school are significant schemes and will require decant accommodation to facilitate the works. My officials are continuing to work closely with the Education Authority (EA) to progress the remedial works as quickly as possible.Interim remedial works were undertaken in summer 2025 to reduce the impact of the damp/mould at St Patrick’s Grammar School, pending delivery of the larger scheme. The EA’s Maintenance Service has also carried out emergency works to the heating system to sustain it in advance of the main heating upgrade scheme.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 30486/22-27, to detail (i) all Programme for Government commitments that relate specifically to the rights of children; and (ii) any progress against those commitments, in this mandate.
answer_text: The Programme for Government aims to ensure that the policies within it will have a positive impact either generally upon the whole population including children, or upon all children, or upon certain groups of children, and promote and protect children’s rights. This includes supporting children and young people with special educational needs and working to end violence against women and girls.The Programme for Government is intended to impact positively on a wide range of policy areas, and upon equality issues and matters. The Programme is intended to have fairness and inclusion of all citizens at its core and therefore, this includes children and young people.A Sub-Committee of the NICS Board has been established to drive forward the delivery of The Executive’s Programme for Government, it will report on key findings and strategic recommendations, provides cross-departmental guidance and support for PfG delivery, ensuring delivery and reporting are set within a wellbeing context informed by data, research, and best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any safeguards that exist within his Department's funding programmes to prevent support for projects that may be inconsistent with local development-plan policies or zoning designations.
answer_text: Most funding programmes within my Department either: a) require all associated planning approvals and other required permits, consents and licences to be submitted before they are approved for funding; or b) include conditions with offers issued, placing responsibility on the applicant to meet all statutory requirements, which include those related to planning approval.For some schemes, safeguards are built into the business case process, which includes specific research and steps to avoid funding projects that may be inconsistent with local development-plan policies or zoning designations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30851/22-27, to detail why more detailed enquires are being taken, in relation to the air ventilation at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: My officials conducted some preliminary investigations on concerns raised relating to air quality and the ventilation system at Grand Central Station. Information received from Translink indicated that air quality and the ventilation system met technical and regulatory requirements. This information was shared with the Health & Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSE NI). The HSE NI have taken an operational decision to conduct some further investigations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) who regulates private parking companies; (ii) to whom private parking companies pay levies to; and (iii) whether any proportion of fines, issued by private parking companies, are paid to her Department.
answer_text: My Department has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them. My Department does not receive any proportion of fines issued by private parking companies

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to a recent site meeting at Canal Bank, Poyntzpass, between Translink officials and residents, to detail when a survey will be carried out to explore the possibility of increasing the clearance of the railway bridge.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink is currently planning to undertake a topographical survey of the private road and low bridge (UB 01.216) at Canal Bank Road, Poyntzpass by mid-December 2025 as agreed with residents  during a site visit on 4th November 2025. The purpose of this survey is to confirm the existing headroom clearances through the bridge, check the current signed height restrictions and to gather other relevant topographical information on the private access layout, drainage and bridge foundations For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of why Newry had the highest per capita parking tickets issued, between January 2024 and June 2025.
answer_text: Analysis of Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) data indicates that the issue of higher per capita PCNs in Newry is reflective of local conditions and is influenced by several interrelated factors which include the level of parking restrictions in the city;; and cross border traffic and economic activity.Newry has a considerable number of parking restrictions across a wider area and a significant proportion of PCNs, around 27.5%, relate to yellow line infringement. Newry also permits longer maximum stays in its Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs) (up to three hours). This reduces the frequency of Traffic Attendant patrols and lowers the perceived risk of enforcement. Newry’s position as a regional retail hub on the Belfast-Dublin corridor, combined with its proximity to the border, attracts high visitor numbers. Approximately 10.8% of PCNs were issued to vehicles registered in the south highlighting the prominence of cross-border traffic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the new Enterprise train fleet.
answer_text: The provision of a replacement Enterprise Fleet and associated infrastructure is being delivered jointly by my Department and the Department of Transport. It is also being supported by the PeacePlus Fund. The two Departments are considering the Full Business Case for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she will adopt to ensure safe (i) parking at Newry Train Station; (ii) access for the 341 bus service from Newry Bus Station to Newry Train Station; and (iii) access for pedestrians to Newry Train Station, without adversely impacting those living in the surrounding areas.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows.(ii) access for the 341 bus service from Newry Bus Station to Newry Train Station Translink is aware of parking issues in the surrounding areas of Newry Train Station. The Department is currently working with Translink to consider options to Find a solution.  (iii) access for pedestrians to Newry Train Station, without adversely impacting those living in the surrounding areas There already is access, via pedestrian footway and zebra crossings, to the Newry Train Station from the Newry Town centre. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the status of the Citizen's Assembly.
answer_text: The new arrangement for the re-establishment of the Civic Forum will be brought to the Assembly for agreement in the near future

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the benefits of introducing a departmental supported taxi driver academy.
answer_text: The recruitment of new and retention of existing drivers, including the establishment of or support for a taxi driver academy, is the responsibility of the taxi industry.My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry in relation to taxi driver, operator and vehicle licencing. It has two main functions that currently impact on the availability of taxi drivers: setting maximum fares and awarding taxi drivers’ licences. To that end the maximum fare was increased in December 2023 and, since the introduction of the test for taxi drivers, the Driver and Vehicle Agency has made several changes to the theory test including lowering the overall pass mark and providing revision guides.To help address the various challenges currently facing the taxi industry, I also announced earlier this year a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing. As part of this process, I have engaged directly with representatives from the taxi sector to gain a deeper understanding of their concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will ringfence funding to upgrade and repair the water mains at the embankment of Glengoland, along the Stewartstown Road, Belfast
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised that it is aware of the ongoing issues relating to the mains water supply pipework serving the residents of Glengoland and that it has identified the source of the problem. While NI Water has identified this work as a priority for resolution, the company has advised that the work may only advance in 2026-2027, subject to budget.  NI Water has assured the residents previously that it will continue to closely monitor the area and ensure that any issues which may reoccur will be responded to immediately.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce a one-hour, free parking pilot in Newry this festive period.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to look at options to provide greater flexibility in controlled parking zones to support high street shopping and hospitality particularly during the festive season, and I plan to make an announcement once I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any efforts his Department has made to preserve habitats of the rare ring ouzel; and (ii) the number of breeding pairs that have been recorded, in each of the last 30 years.
answer_text: The ring ouzel is no longer considered to be a regular breeding species in Northern Ireland, although historically it bred in all counties apart from Armagh. Reasons for its decline, which has occurred across Britain and Ireland, are poorly understood; they may include leisure disturbance and overgrazing in upland breeding areas, climate change, and habitat destruction in wintering areas around the Mediterranean.Due to low breeding frequency, no habitat conservation actions have been targeted at this species. Work by the National Trust to restore damaged habitats in the Mournes, in part funded through the DAERA-administered Environment Fund, may help as this was previously a regular breeding area.A pair in Co Antrim in 2018 is the only confirmed breeding record for the period 2013 to 2025; birds were still present but breeding unconfirmed in 2019 and 2020. Prior to 2013, the best data sources are the British Trust for Ornithology (BTO) bird atlases. Ring Ouzel distributional can be accessed at https://app.bto.org/mapstore/StoreServlet?id=370, covering distributional change between 1968 and 2011.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the press release from the Union of Shop, Distributive and Allied Workers, dated 10 November 2025, describing the survey results from retail workers speaking out about facing violence, threats and abuse.
answer_text: Although the small downturn in the number of incidents reported in the USDAW survey results is to be welcomed, the high levels of abuse, threats and assaults faced by those working in retail remains shocking.    I have been appalled by the levels of aggression being reported against retail workers, and am fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, PSNI, as well as other Departments and organisations, to ensure that we reduce these numbers further.   My Sentencing Bill will legislate for a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service.    This will include attacks on retail workers.  	This new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty on conviction than the sentences currently available for similar less serious assault offences.    	Additionally, if a more serious assault offence is charged, I will make the fact that the victim is such a worker a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect this specifically in the sentences imposed.   These higher penalties will act as a significant deterrent and send the message that there is no place for these types of offences in our society.  	I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill in January 2026, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support customers migrating to Universal Credit, ensuring arrangements for payment of (i) rent; (ii) rates; and (iii) disability benefits, are also simultaneously transitioned.
answer_text: All claimants moving to Universal Credit as part of managed migration receive a migration notice letter which signposts to a range of help available including the Universal Credit helpline, support via any of the Departments’ Jobs and Benefit Offices, links to detailed information on nidirect and the availability also of independent advice. Regarding your points relating to rent, rates, and disability benefits: Claimants who require support with housing costs are asked to declare details of their housing costs when they make their claim, and where needed, provide supporting evidence. It is the default position of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland to pay eligible housing costs directly to the claimant’s landlord.Support for rates is administered through the Department of Finance Land & Property Services Rate Rebate Scheme. Claimants are signposted to apply to the Rate Rebate Scheme, should they require support. While disability benefits are not impacted by move to Universal Credit, claimants who have an existing assessment for limited capability for work and/or work-related activities will have their health status transferred from their previous benefit and will be reflected in their Universal Credit entitlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has completed an assessment regarding the financial cost to the economy, as a result of parking fines deterring visitors to the city of Newry.
answer_text: The Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ) in Newry was introduced in 2008 to address congestion and improve accessibility. It was developed in consultation with local representative and business groups, and I remain committed to ongoing engagement. Its purpose is to discourage all-day parking, maintain turnover of spaces, and support local businesses, while contributing to traffic management and road safety.  From the records available, officials have not identified any financial assessment specifically on the impact of parking fines deterring visitors to Newry.  Older documentation is no longer held under the Department’s record retention and disposal policy. To further support high street shopping and hospitality, I have announced a pilot initiative providing a one-hour grace period after expiry of paid parking in Lisburn and Newry CPZs over the festive season.  This trial runs from 29 November 2025 until 10 January 2026 and aims to encourage footfall while maintaining effective traffic flow.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the (i) programme for delivery; and (ii) current status, of all Executive Flagship projects, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: The flagship projects and current status are listed below: ProjectStatus Belfast Transport HubCompleteDesertcreat Training FacilityCompleteBelfast Rapid TransportComplete – Phase 2 announced in February 2025  A6 Road Phase 1Partially completed - Phase 1 completed (August 2019)Regional & Sub Regional StadiaPartially completedMother and Children's HospitalNew Children’s Hospital – Construction underway New Maternity Hospital - Complete but not yet operationalA5 Road Subject of appeal of the High Court judgement made in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to install nature friendly lighting along the Lagan Towpath.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to provide nature friendly lighting along the rural portions of the Lagan towpath at present.  Lighting is only installed where it is essential for operational health and safety purposes at appropriate river infrastructure sites or where identified as a measure to manage public safety risks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address the decrease in the number of patients registered with a NHS dentist in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In June this year I announced £7m investment for 2025/26 aimed at supporting dental practices while longer-term reform is developed. The investment will fund continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions and root canal treatments, and a further £1.6m allocation to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care.  In addition, a further £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services to help offset the impact of increased National Insurance contributions on General Dental Services. These measures are intended to stabilise Health Service dentistry and maintain patient registration levels while longer-term reforms are developed, and reflect my Department’s ongoing commitment to sustaining dental provision, despite significant financial pressures. Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the merits of removing all acronyms when defining, or communicating about, any primary or secondary school pupils.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of using clear and accessible language and my Department strives to ensure this is reflected in communications.  This includes adhering to guidance that acronyms and abbreviations should be expanded in full when they are first used, with the acronym in brackets, and then used in short form thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the extent infrastructure investment has been stymied by the lack of progress in advancing infrastructure planning policy.
answer_text: The planning system plays an important role in infrastructure investment across the North. It is worth noting there has been a significant improvement in the average processing time for major planning applications in 2024/25, which saw the lowest processing times since the transfer of planning powers in 2015. This trend is continuing with major application processing times meeting the 30-week statutory target in the first quarter of 2025/26 for the first time.  This is in addition to improving statutory consultee response times, as 77% were responded to on time, that is within 21 calendar days or within an agreed extended timeframe.My Department, working in partnership with local government and key stakeholders, continues to advance the planning improvement agenda with the aim of enhancing the performance and long term sustainability of the planning system in the North.  Work is actively progressing across three strategic areas: strengthening shared understanding of operational challenges, investing in workforce capacity, and addressing broader legislative and system improvements, and good progress has been made to date. Many of the system interventions and support measures to improve the system will take time to bed into culture, practice and procedure, and for the benefits and performance improvements to be realised. However, early indicators of positive change are encouraging.  My Department’s planning policy framework is set out in the Regional Development Strategy 2035 (RDS) and Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS).  The RDS 2035 continues to retain sound strategic and regional planning guidance.  The SPPS is in general conformity with the RDS 2035 and it is a material consideration in plan-making and decision-taking. The interpretation and implementation of planning policy is continuously being refined through operational experience, in the courts and at planning appeals. Planning policy is one of a number of material considerations to be taken into account by councils in plan-making and by planning authorities in the determination of planning applications. Where appropriate, my Department will review and bring forward new or revised regional strategic planning policy. The Department has been undertaking a focused review of the SPPS on the subject of renewable and low carbon energy. The aim of the review is to ensure that policy remains fit for purpose and up to date to inform plan-making and decision-taking.  Having recently secured Executive Committee agreement to the revised policy, I intend to publish the Strategic Planning Policy Statement, Edition 2 as soon as possible, once all necessary next steps have been completed.In relation to local planning policy, my officials are encouraging Councils to use their Local Development Plan powers to incorporate policy approaches that actively manage the release of land to ensure that development plans are responsive to changing circumstances including housing need and infrastructure availa

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's review of the Regional Strategic Planning Policy on Renewable and Low Carbon Energy.
answer_text: Having recently secured Executive Committee agreement, to the revised regional planning policy on renewable and low carbon energy, I intend to publish the Strategic Planning Policy Statement, Edition 2 as soon as possible, once all necessary next steps have been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the extent that four planning apprenticeship positions will help alleviate staffing and resource challenges, currently being experienced in local council planning offices.
answer_text: As part of the Planning Improvement Programme my Department has been working with local councils and Belfast Metropolitan College to introduce a trainee planner apprenticeship with the aim of alleviating resourcing pressures across the system. Resourcing challenges, skills shortages pose challenges for public sector planning across Britain and the South and this is one of many key measures that are ongoing here to build capacity and capability across the planning system. The apprenticeship programme was open to all councils, and it was for them to decide how many trainee planners they needed to recruit. The trainee planner programme provides an excellent and unique opportunity to build capacity within the system and will be a good benchmark for how we can resource the planning system for both the Department and Local Councils. It is hoped that going forward, other councils will become involved as the programme becomes embedded. In terms of my Department, I am pleased to advise that we are recruiting 6 trainee planners through an internal NICS wide trawl process. This is a unique opportunity for existing civil servants to change career paths and become professional planners whilst ensuring appropriate succession planning within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the defects on the Drumintee Road, between Meigh and Forkhill, will be repaired.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including Drumintee Road, Meigh, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.  The Drumintee Road, between Meigh and Forkhill was last inspected on 22 October 2025 and any actionable defects scheduled for repair.  Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has any plans to increase allotted access to council swimming pool facilities for swimming and water sport clubs.
answer_text: I have no authority, nor should I have, over the opening hours or facility hire policies of council-owned leisure facilities. These matters are determined by each individual council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32748/22-27, for her assessment of the merits of developing the road drainage systems' capacity to cope with the sudden, short duration, high intensity rain events, given that they have become more common in recent times.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to balance capacity and affordability. Upgrading all systems to cope with every extreme rainfall event would be prohibitively expensive and often impractical, especially in urban areas where space is limited. Capacity is usually constrained by the outfall, such as sewers or streams, which themselves may struggle during intense rain. Design standards accept that some surface flooding can occur during severe events. Additionally, issues like temporary blockages of gullies and potential excessive runoff can also have an impact on surface water drainage.My Department will continue to carry out maintenance and consider targeted schemes where justified, and remains committed to working collaboratively with stakeholders to deliver practical, cost-effective measures that improve resilience to extreme weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional resources being made available to GP patients to access medical care in practices, in the wake of the ongoing GP collective action.
answer_text: I regret that it was not possible to secure agreement with the NI General Practitioners Committee on this year’s contract and their subsequent decision to take collective action.The 2025/26 GMS contract included an additional £9.5m investment in General Practice. In the context of the inadequate budget allocated to my Department by the Executive, and the extremely challenging financial position the Department faces, this represented the best possible offer that could be made.In addition to the 25/26 GMS contract, my Department has announced investment of a further £61m into the Primary Care Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) programme over the next 5 years, which will expand the range of services available in Primary Care and help address the high levels of demand being dealt with by our GP practices.To date my Department has implemented the MDT model across 125 GP practices, increasing access for 845,398 patients (42% of the NI population) to one or more core MDT services.To support practices over the winter period, my Department has commissioned the Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service “Managing Winter Pressures” which commenced on 1st November 2025 and will run until 31 March 2026. This is designed to enable practices to put plans into place to manage the increase in demand and provide additional capacity to help with winter pressures. Practices are encouraged to consider contracting to provide this service.In addition, the Federation-led General Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team was established to support GP Practices at risk and to develop and implement an effective Practice Recovery Plan. The service, provided through the Eastern Federation Support Unit by the GP Federation Improvement & Crisis Response Team (GPICRT), is open to all GP Practices within a federation area that sign up to the initiative. The Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme, supporting recruitment of GPs in hard to recruit areas, been reviewed in 2025/2026 and aims to further encourage GPs to take up post in rural areas, that are finding it difficult to recruit and/or retain GPs. My priority has been, and continues to be, to ensure that patients can continue to access safe, high quality, sustainable GP services when they need to. This continues to apply during collective action. GPs must continue to deliver upon their contractual commitments as they take this action and ensure that impact on patients is minimised. Any action that is taken must respect contractual obligations, protect patient safety, and maintain access services. My Department has written to GPs to remind them of their responsibilities on these issues and will closely monitor adherence to ensure that practices continue to deliver to their contractual requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the collective action being taken by GPs against his Department; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department is taking to resolve the collective action and address the concerns of the GPs and patients impacted by this action.
answer_text: It is a matter of regret that it was not possible to secure agreement with the NI General Practitioners Committee on this year’s General Medical Services (GMS) contract and their subsequent decision to take collective action.  My priority has been, and continues to be, to ensure that patients can continue to access safe, high quality, sustainable GP services when they need to.  This continues to apply during collective action.  Collective action measures largely affect non-contractual or voluntary activity. GPs must continue to deliver upon their contractual commitments as they take this action and ensure that impact on patients is minimised. Any action that is taken must respect contractual obligations, protect patient safety, and maintain service access.My Department has written to GPs to remind them of their responsibilities on these issues and will closely monitor adherence to ensure that practices continue to deliver to their contractual requirements through a number of methods such as:Review of information submitted through the Northern Ireland Contractual Adherence Framework (NICAF) 2025/26GP Practice VisitsMonitoring of complaints received through ‘honest broker’ service.Local offices will also continue to work with GP Practices to ensure all required information is provided.The 2025/26 GMS contract included additional £9.5m investment into General Practice. The package represented the best offer the Department could make in the face of the pressures on the wider Health budget. The budget continues to be under significant pressure; on that basis it remains the case that there is no scope unfortunately for further additional investment in the GMS contract in 2025/26.Since the recommendations were received from the pay review bodies, I have been clear that I want to make payment, in full, in line with those recommendations, and issued a Ministerial Direction to that effect. Last week, the Executive agreed that Direction, which enables the Department to implement the pay award in full in 2025-26. The value of this additional investment for General Practice is £11.7m.In addition to the 2025/26 GMS contract, my Department has announced investment of a further £61m into the Primary Care MDT programme over the next 4 years, which will expand the range of services available in Primary Care and help address the high levels of demand being dealt with by our GP practices, as well as £2.9m into the GP Elective Service which will increase the levels of service being provided in important primary care services.  Looking to the future of General Practice, I am committed to working with GPs on the future of primary care services and that we continue to engage constructively with General Practice.  I have written  to the Chair of Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee seeking their agreement to an approach to the GMS contract for 2026/27 and wider issues in General Practice, with the aim of securing the long-term future of General Practice over

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the merits of PSNI officers having the option to issue Community Resolution Notices in certain cases of domestic violence, given the current rates of violence against women.
answer_text: Domestic abuse is a profoundly harmful and traumatic crime, and I want to be clear from the outset that my overriding priority is the safety and wellbeing of victims. Any response to domestic abuse must be robust, victim-centred and informed by a clear understanding of risk.I am aware that the Police Service of Northern Ireland has, for a number of years, allowed Community Resolution Notices (CRNs) to be used in a very limited set of domestic-motivated cases where the threshold criteria are met. These powers are tightly controlled and subject to enhanced oversight, and I recognise the safeguards that the PSNI has now strengthened further through the expansion of their CRN Guidance in August 2024.CRNs are never available for high-risk offending. They may only be considered where the officer has sufficient evidence to prove the offence; the suspect has made a clear and reliable admission; their previous offending history is eligible; and the offence type itself is authorised for CRN disposal. Crucially, the victim’s views and any impact on them must be fully taken into account, and all CRNs require supervisory approval. For intimate or previously intimate partners, the threshold is even more stringent and authorisation can only be given by an Inspector or above, with additional conditions including:There must be no previous domestic history between any parties in the last 3 years;No NMO or Orders are in place on the suspect against the victim or any other person;The injuries to the victim are minor or low level;The parties do not live together; and5. 	The victim’s views are taken into consideration when agreeing a tailored and meaningful resolution from the suspect.While many domestic abuse cases will rightly proceed through prosecution and the courts, I recognise that CRNs can have merit in a very narrow range of circumstances where these strict safeguards are satisfied. In such cases, they can offer a proportionate and timely response that takes careful account of the victim’s wishes and needs; they can support a restorative approach; and they may deliver a prompt, tailored resolution that can be more satisfactory for some victims than a lengthy criminal justice process.CRNs can also allow officers to deal quickly with lower-level incidents and may provide an opportunity to intervene early, challenge behaviour, and signpost both parties to appropriate support. Importantly, this approach is designed to apply only where risk is low, where the victim has been fully consulted, and where there is no indication of a pattern of abuse.The rates of violence against women and girls in Northern Ireland remain deeply concerning, and I remain committed to ensuring that we respond effectively, consistently and in a victim-focused manner. Within that context, I acknowledge that Community Resolution Notices, when applied in a controlled way and in line with the PSNI’s guidance, can provide a proportionate response in very specific domestic-motivated cases, witho

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34147/22-27, (i) for her assessment of whether each local council will be able to have its Local Development Plan (LDP) examined and adopted in a timely manner, given (a) the initial estimate that all councils would have LDPs in place by 2019; (b) the status of current progress; and (c) the retirements on the Planning Appeals Commission; and (ii) to detail the progress of each local council’s LDP, against their committed timelines.
