answer_text: There is no legislative timeframe for councils in the North to bring forward their Local Development Plans (LDPs). It was not anticipated that all councils would progress and complete LDPs simultaneously for a variety of reasons. Robust evidence is required to assist the formulation of sound policies, and this evidence gathering process takes time. In addition, at the transfer of planning functions, some councils also benefited from recently adopted Departmental Development Plans reducing the urgency to produce new plans.Nine out of the eleven councils here have published development plan documents, six of which, have adopted Plan Strategies and are working towards their Local Policies Plan publication. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils are due to publish draft Plan Strategy documents next year. It is a statutory requirement that a LDP must be prepared in accordance with timetables which are agreed with my Department. Legislation requires councils to keep their timetables under review to ensure that the preparation of the LDP is proceeding in accordance with the timetable. Council LDP progress can be monitored by viewing timetables on each individual Council website.My Department secured funding earlier this year to exercise existing powers under the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to appoint persons to carry out its ‘Hear and Report’ work as an alternative to the Planning Appeals Commission.  This is part of a wider body of work that I am committed to, in collaboration with councils, to identify measures to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the overall planning process.The retirement of staff in the Planning Appeals Commission is a matter for the Department of Justice and the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to increase the number of train carriages, available for peak hours journeys, on the Belfast to Dublin Enterprise service.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The Enterprise timetable is operated by mixed fleet with 3 types of rolling stock, including the existing Enterprise train sets supplemented by both Irish Rail and Translink trains - these are scheduled to operate with either 6 or 7 passenger carriages. Translink and Irish Rail are currently involved in a project to procure 8 new Enterprise trains with the support of DfI, DoT and SEUPB. The project is at the procurement stage. These trains are scheduled to enter service by 2030. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for reducing waiting times for those waiting for first-time neurology appointments.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address the shortage of midwives.
answer_text: In response to evidence identifying workforce needs, my Department continues to commission the maximum number of pre-registration student Midwifery places from Queen’s University Belfast that Health and Social Care Trusts can accommodate for clinical placement. In 2025/26 my Department commissioned 120 Midwifery training places from QUB, an increase of over 30% from 2019 levels. This reflects my ongoing commitment to this and other pre-registration healthcare training programmes.The expanded midwifery programme is now delivering results, with Trusts actively integrating newly qualified midwives into their workforce and for the first time, Trust are reporting waiting lists for midwifery posts due to the increased student midwifery intake over recent years. The five HSC Trusts work closely with QUB to ensure student midwives receive high-quality clinical training experiences, making Northern Ireland an attractive place to work. This collaborative approach supports recruitment of midwives from all training backgrounds, while promoting retention and recruitment of those trained locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to what extent the construction of new housing developments has stalled due to insufficient water and drainage infrastructure.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any any unused funds that can be allocated for community transport.
answer_text: I recognise the vital service that is delivered by community transport in connecting people and ensuring that some of the most vulnerable in society can more actively participate in society. That is why I agreed to meet the full additional requirements identified by community transport operators as part of my 2025-26 budget decisions, increasing the level of funding available.I am currently considering the overall financial position of my Department as part of the December monitoring round and as part of that will be considering community transport funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland claim that the road network is now at a critical point, with dangerously low levels of maintenance funding and severe staff shortages within the Department of Infrastructure, as stated in its press release of 4 November 2025.
answer_text: My Department continues to face significant resource challenges – in terms of available budget and the recruitment and retention of staff as a result of over a decade of British Government underfunding and austerity. With regards recruitment and retention, we face particular challenges in relation to specialist roles, which have impacted our ability to deliver essential services and infrastructure projects. I want to assure you that my officials are taking proactive and decisive action to address these issues in liaison with colleagues in the Department of Finance, who provide HR services for all departments.To strengthen our workforce planning arrangements internally, we have established a People Sub-Committee which reports to the Departmental Board enhancing governance and oversight. This ensures that workforce capacity risks are identified and managed. To build long-term resilience, we are investing in innovative recruitment models, and my Department is working with our HR delivery partners in the Department of Finance to implement a strategic, prioritised Workforce Plan. At present, 22 recruitment campaigns are underway and in the last 12 months our efforts have led to approximately 200 new appointments, with a further 300 anticipated soon.Our planning also considers broader workforce trends, including an ageing population, changes to the way people work and the implications of a multi-generational workforce. These factors are central to our long-term workforce modelling and policy development, especially given that a significant proportion of our workforce are approaching retirement age, presenting a real risk to our institutional knowledge and delivery capacity.All these efforts are underpinned by the DfI People Strategy (2024–2026), which aligns with the broader NICS People Strategy. Our focus is on building capacity, supporting capability, and fostering a culture of inclusive leadership and staff wellbeing. This strategic framework ensures that we are not only addressing immediate staffing needs but also laying the foundation for a more resilient and agile organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is unlocking development in Newry for the next ten years, by providing water and wastewater drainage and treatment capacity.
answer_text: I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to unlocking wastewater constraints. The recent announcement will unlock capacity for the currently known  400 properties in Newry seeking a wastewater connection  in the coming years, and shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions, which lies at the heart of my three-pronged approach.The innovative and collaborative approach to unlocking development in Newry will deliver a faster, lower-cost solution through targeted maintenance, removal of excess water, and partnerships with businesses to reduce industrial flow impacts. Having been effectively closed to significant new development due to long-standing wastewater infrastructure constraints, my department’s action means that vital economic growth in Newry will now be unlocked, thanks to this new and dynamic alternative to traditional upgrade methods that will unlock not only wastewater connections for housing but also a £100m plus industrial manufacturing plant and a new Civic Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how he is ensuring the public is aware of open consultations surrounding the future of Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment qualifications; and (ii) his assessment of the TransformED: The future of CCEA GCSEs, AS Levels and A Levels consultation.
answer_text: My Department’s consultation on proposals for the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS Levels and A Levels has been promoted extensively since its launch on the 18 September 2025. Examples of this include:The DE press release. Social media channels have been used such as Facebook and X.Post-primary principals and teachers received notification about the consultation and were encouraged to bring it to the attention of their students either directly or through their School Councils.Officials have also carried out focus groups with a number of post-primary schools and promoted the consultation with the pupils attending these events.Officials have also met with representatives of the Secondary Students Union (Northern Ireland), SSU NI, and, in collaboration with the EA, have designed a children and young persons’ version of the consultation questionnaire which has been shared with youth organisations to allow young people further opportunities to share their views.The consultation closed on 13 November 2025. After all views have been carefully considered, alongside other evidence, I intend to confirm my policy framework intentions in early 2026.  This will then inform the revision of CCEA’s qualifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's plan to address the Transport and Roads Asset Management Group staff shortages, across (i) engineering; (ii) inspection; and (iii) procurement teams.
answer_text: My Department continues to face significant resource challenges not only because of the financial constraints we operate within, but also because of our ability to recruit in a timely capacity and to compete in what is an extremely challenging labour market particularly for our specialist roles.  All of these constraints have impacted on the delivery essential services and infrastructure projects. I am however assured that my officials are taking proactive action to address recruitment issues and are working with colleagues in the Department of Finance, who provide HR services for all Departments. To strengthen our workforce planning arrangements internally, my Department has established a People Sub-Committee, which reports to the Departmental Board, enhancing governance and oversight. This ensures that workforce capacity risks are identified and proactively managed. To increase the number if staff and build resilience, we are working with our HR delivery partners in the Department of Finance to implement a strategic, prioritised Workforce Plan.  For example, TRAM is participating in the second ‘Talent Dog’ recruitment pilot, which aims to recruit Industrial Road Workers, as an alternative to the usual recruitment approach. At present, the Department is also progressing several recruitment campaigns and is in the process of appointing staff at the Professional and Technical Officer (PTO) grade from recently completed competitions. My officials are also focused on the development of career pathways and are prioritising the delivery of Skills Academy, Apprenticeship, Higher Level Apprenticeship and Graduate programmes, all of which seek to address staff shortages within the procurement, engineering and inspection teams.In addition my officials are also continuing to contribute to the delivery of the Public Procurement Capacity and Capability Strategy 2025 – 2030, published in September 2025.In the last 6 months our recruitment efforts have led to approximately 120 appointments, with a further 70 appointments ongoing, however, I accept that progress has not happened at the pace we would like, and my officials continue to engage with colleagues in the Department of Finance to identify potential improvements in the speed and efficiency of recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland, claim that resurfacing and defect repair programmes have been scaled back or delayed, as stated in its press release of 4 November 2025.
answer_text: There has been historic underfunding for the maintenance of the road network for a number of years as a result of British Government austerity, and I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available to me as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy.The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ highlighted the need for increased spending on Capital Structural Maintenance, however the impact on the integrity and safety of the road network is as a result of the longer term underfunding, rather than the impact of a single year.I have met recently with the MPANI to discuss the concerns of the industry and I remain committed to seeking additional funding to address the need to invest in the road network.There are, however, many competing financial demands across my Department and it will be challenging to address years of underfunding; however, I am committed to improving our roads here and I continue to pursue additional funding for investment in the road network.I will continue to make the case for investment in infrastructure here, which includes the quality of our road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to introduce travel reimbursement for full-time students.
answer_text: My Department currently provides c£6 million each year for Home to College travel passes for full-time Further Education (FE) students aged 16-19 in line with travel provision afforded to pupils attending schools.	Eligible full-time and part-time FE students can also avail of travel cost reimbursement through FE Grants for students aged 19 plus and the FE Hardship Fund for students aged 18 or over.	A review of all FE student support measures, including travel provision, is underway to ensure support is targeted effectively and based on student need.	For those students in Higher Education, maintenance support, including both maintenance grants and loans, is designed to help students with living costs such as travel.	In addition, Disabled Students’ Allowance support funding helps students who have extra costs, including travel-related costs, because of a disability, mental-health condition or specific learning difficulty.  	An income-assessesed Travel Grant is also available for students to meet reasonable travel costs associated with specific medical or dental courses at a hospital or other place in  Britain or South of Ireland other than a student’s normal place of attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she intends to introduce free public transport for disabled people, in line with the rest of United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I had intended to reconsider the extension of free travel as part of this year’s budget allocation process but due to a major upgrade of Translink’s ticketing systems, it was not possible to progress this option during the current financial year.However, the Department continues to work closely with Translink to explore how this policy could be implemented, and I am keen to progress this as soon as possible.I fully recognise the significant societal and economic benefits that free public transport for people with disabilities could bring.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to repair the street lights on Windsor Avenue, Newry, which have not been working for a number of weeks.
answer_text: Faults in relation to the streetlights on Windsor Avenue were caused by storm damage.  As the lights are mounted on poles owned by Northern Ireland Electricity (NIE), officials notified them of the issue and representatives attended site on 5 November 2025 to initiate repairs. Final repair works are scheduled to be completed by 6 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 30483/22-27, whether any assessment has been made in relation to the implementation and penetration of Project Gigabit.
answer_text: The contract for Project Gigabit was awarded to Fibrus on 16 December 2025.  Over 9,000 premises here are set to benefit, 95% of which are in open countryside. The rollout of the project is expected to take up to 2 years. An online checker is available at https://www.online.economy-ni.gov.uk/ProjectGigabit enabling homeowners and businesses to check if their premises will benefit from the project. More	detailed rollout plans, including timescales are expected to be available by February 2026. Due to current funding available, some premises will not be covered. Those premises are very remote, and a solution currently does not represent value for money and is not affordable. My officials are exploring viable and affordable alternatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the Northern Ireland Audit Office's report on Northern Ireland Energy Strategy, to detail the steps her Department will take to ensure (i) that key targets set out in the Energy Strategy are being met in expected timeframes; and (ii) how the effectiveness of proposed actions are being routinely monitored and measured.
answer_text: I acknowledge the Audit Office’s report on the approach and implementation of the NI Energy Strategy and will endeavour to swiftly implement its recommendations.My Department will produce an Action Plan to address the recommendations set out in the Audit Office report, ensuring these are embedded within our governance and delivery processes.The forthcoming Mid-Term Review of the Energy Strategy will set out key milestones through to 2030, ensuring transparency and accountability as we progress the delivery of the Energy Strategy.I remain committed to the delivery of a net zero energy system powered by local renewables, smart infrastructure, and shaped by the people who live and work here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of guaranteeing lifelong National Health Service dental care for people affected by cleft.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of requiring water companies to provide real time sewage leak discharge alerts.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water and DAERA are in the process of scoping a pilot project for Near Real Time Event Duration Monitor alerts. The outcomes of the pilot will help to inform the merits of these alerts and associated costs and benefits.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18214/22-27, whether her Department has any plans to make train prices equitable for people traveling from the north.
answer_text: Translink have worked with Iarnród Éireann to provide passengers with access to online fares and seat bookings providing Flexible, Semi Flexible and Low fares up to 80 minutes before travel on cross border services. This approach also provides a "manage my booking" option for passengers on the Flexible options. As part of this work, Translink and Iarnród Éireann brought in new Low, Semi Flexible and Flexible web based Cross Border fares that attempt to more closely align to each other’s e.g. NIR Belfast to Dublin Flexible at £16.99 compared to Irish Rail Dublin to Belfast Flexible at €17.50.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how the reduction in housing association grants will lead to better value and more social housing.
answer_text: I continue to allocate the vast majority of my Department’s capital budget on the delivery of new social homes, but I must ensure this delivers optimum value for money. The impact of my decisions to increase benchmark costs but reduce Housing Association Grant is to ensure that value for money is achieved and to make our budget allocation stretch further. This is about Housing Associations doing more - i.e. driving innovation, finding efficiencies and increasing private financing. It will be for each individual housing association to decide themselves how much private finance they can borrow and how much risk they take on. These changes, including the slightly lower average grant rate per unit, should enable us to build more homes with the funding available. Both the increases in the benchmark costs and the decreases in Housing Association grant are based on the existing methodology. This is why I have also announced reviews into how we subsidise new build social housing as well as the standards to which it is built.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of planning decisions that have been reversed at the Planning Committee; and (ii) the type of developments these were, broken down by local council area, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department holds data on decisions made against officer recommendation for the years 2018-19 to 2021-22. This is outlined in the table below. My officials are working to update the data it holds. I would refer the member to local councils who may hold more information in this regard. Dev. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their assessment of the removal of Daylight Saving Time.
answer_text: We have not undertaken an assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 9283/22-27, to outline whether the PSNI has a role in enforcing the removal of illegal billboards erected by political parties.
answer_text: Under Section 32 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000 and common law, the PSNI have fundamental duties to take steps to bring offenders to justice and prevent the commission of criminal offences.  The investigation of crime is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  Powers are vested in the Department for Infrastructure under planning legislation to remove items and recover the cost of doing so for any unauthorised materials on lampposts or other street furniture.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last assessment of the structural integrity of the Maydown Bridge, Benburb, was carried out; and (ii) any remedial works that were completed, in light of that assessment.
answer_text: The current closure on the Maydown Road, Benburb is due to a collapsed retaining wall, located 70m from the nearby Maydown bridge over the River Blackwater.  In line with my Department’s policy the bridge was last inspected on 31 July 2024, and no significant remedial works were identified as necessary at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to address the lack of parking at Newry Train Station.
answer_text: Translink currently provides over 300 free parking spaces at Newry Train Station to support rail and bus commuters. However, demand for parking has increased significantly.To address this issue, Translink has initiated a project aimed at restricting car park access to public transport users only. Subject to the availability of funding a parking control system could be installed on site during 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on extending Fáilte Ireland's' Ancient East tourism branding, into counties Armagh, Down, and Antrim.
answer_text: I support the extension of the Fáilte Ireland brands into the north. Ultimately this is a decision for Fáilte Ireland which is undertaking a review of the Ireland’s Ancient East brand. Updates will be provided in due course to Tourism NI and Tourism Ireland once the review is completed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on progress to redevelop Casement Park.

To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the number of business cases that adversely impacted the progress of projects within her Department, over the last five years; and (ii) to detail the projects impacted, including the extent to which they have been adversely impacted.
answer_text: I am not aware of any business cases that have adversely impacted the progress of a project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of recommendation 2 of the Independent Review of Environmental Governance in Northern Ireland, stating that his Department's website should offer a clear list of environmental legislation in force in Northern Ireland, including whether it has been commenced or not.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with his Executive colleagues, in regards to contingency measures to support the community and voluntary sector, in the wake of diverted UK Shared Prosperity funding.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on engagement with the UK Government with regards to the UK Shared Prosperity Fund and future funding including the planned Local Growth Fund. I have previously highlighted my Departmental priorities for future funding to DoF. I have also written to the Finance Minister to raise my Department’s concerns with regards to a reduction in future funding and the potential impact this will cause to employability services delivered by the voluntary and community sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of how the replacement of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund with the Local Growth Fund will impact neurodivergent and disabled people
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the Member to answers provided to previous questions, AQW 31249/22-27, AQO 2452/22-27, AQO 2458/22-27 and AQW 32531/22-27. I also outlined my assessment of the Local Growth Fund through the Assembly Motion on 21 October 2025.  I believe the unilateral decision by the British Government to end the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) at the end of March 2026 and skew the new Local Growth Fund towards Capital will have serious implications for those organisations delivering Economic Inactivity interventions, including those who deliver services for neurodivergent and disabled people.I have repeatedly raised concerns with Ministers across Westminster about the negative impact a Capital-heavy funding approach will have here. Most recently, I have been pressing the Chief Secretary to work with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government to reconsider the funding profile, both in person and in writing. In a recent meeting with the NIO Secretary of State and the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, I also agreed to a short, intensive series of  engagements at official level to progress the Local Growth Fund as quickly as possible, with the aim of providing clarity for SPF delivery bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she will take to ensure (i) pavements are kept clear in Windmill Avenue, Armagh; and (ii) that sufficient parking is available for residents.
answer_text: Inconsiderate pavement parking has a significant adverse impact in many areas across the North. Unfortunately, many drivers do not realise the consequences of their actions, particularly for people with disabilities, older people, children and people pushing prams, when their vehicle blocks the footway.I recently announced my intention to bring forward legislation which will ban parking wholly on a pavement; parking across dropped kerbs for pedestrian access; and double parking. This legislation will complement steps my Department has already taken to address the ongoing issue of inconsiderate pavement parking.Where we do not have the powers to act, concerns regarding parking obstruction should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.My Department published The Residents’ Parking Review Report on 29 November 2024 and following publication of the report there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the North. Given the level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed over the coming months.While I appreciate that the current layout of Windmill Avenue may not provide the availability of convenient parking for all residents; it is an issue that is faced by many estates throughout the North. Additional parking within developments is assessed in accordance with the Department’s Policy and Guidance RSPPG E053 Car Parking Facilities in Existing Social Housing EstatesIn accordance with this policy, the Department does not provide parking for amenity reasons and without any significant safety or traffic progression concerns impacting on the wider road network around Armagh. As Windmill Avenue is a residential cul-de-sac, it would not meet the criteria outlined above for a scheme to provide additional parking to be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of establishing an independent infrastructure commission.
answer_text: The potential merits of establishing an expert-led, independent Infrastructure Commission within my department would very much depend on the detail of any such proposal.	I am reassured to note that my department already possesses a great deal of expertise in its delivery of major infrastructure schemes and is working collaboratively with other departments and the Strategic Investment Board in preparation for the agreement of an Investment Strategy.This Strategy will determine the capital projects my department will take forward over the next ten years and will include a range of 12 enabling actions designed to improve our ability to plan for, procure and deliver these schemes.These actions include Capability Planning, improvements to our Business Case Process; enhanced Monitoring and Assurance; an Infrastructure Needs Assessment and Carbon Assessments and Standards.My department has also established a Major Projects Committee to strengthen project assurance and oversight of the major capital projects being delivery across the department and its Arm’s Length Bodies.  In support of this Committee, and to promote best practice and guidance in programme and project delivery, my department has a dedicated Portfolio, Programme and Project Office (P3O).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the status of Local Development Plans, including the current position in relation to the scheduled delivery and statutory timelines; and (ii) for his assessment of whether the status of the Plans are acting as an impediment to residential and economic development.
answer_text: All councils in the North are advancing their Local Development Plan programme. The pace of this programme is set by councils.To date nine out of the eleven councils have published development plan documents, six of which, have adopted Plan Strategies and are working towards their Local Policies Plan publication. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils are due to publish the first of their development plan documents within the next year.There is no legislative timeframe for councils to bring forward Local Development Plans (LDPs) and no target has been set by my Department for the completion of plans by councils. Councils set their own timetables for the preparation of their plans, taking into consideration their own resourcing, evidence gathering processes and governance structures, and these are forwarded to my Department for agreement.The varying rate at which councils are bringing forward LDPs does not impede residential or economic development. On the contrary, while LDPs are being prepared, planning applications continue to be determined having regard to councils Plan Strategies where relevant, and to any other material considerations including the existing departmental development plans and the provisions of the Regional Development Strategy (RDS), Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS), and supplementary planning guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the Independent Review of Environmental Governance in Northern Ireland report's position that NI Water's protection from public prosecution for pollution incidents should be ended; and (ii) to detail the actions she will take to ensure her Department's pollution incidents are prevented and penalised as appropriate.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Report on the Independent Review of Environmental Governance in the North and I will take time to consider the report fully.   I recognise the challenges outlined in the report and note that most of the recommendations relate to DAERA.  However, my Department will work with DAERA to address any recommendations, where appropriate. I would add that under the current environmental governance regime, NI Water can and has been prosecuted by the NIEA for water pollution offences. In addition, NIEA is reviewing its Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent with NI Water and I await the DAERA Minister’s decision regarding the outcome of this review. I have met with Minister Muir in relation to this and have offered mine and my departments support to him.Addressing pollution incidents is an operational matter for NI Water and is subject to environmental regulation by NIEA. My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges. I have provided NI Water with £500m of public money this year to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North. NI Water is proposing to invest almost  £431m to help address sewage discharges during Price Control 21. This includes, £214m on wastewater upgrades,  £170m on addressing unsatisfactory intermittent discharges and £47m to reduce the number of properties at risk of out-of-sewer flooding.NI Water is also carrying out a phased installation of event duration monitors (EDMs) at combined sewer overflows and the information provided by EDMs will give NI Water an accurate assessment of how discharge points are performing and will help to inform future investment plans, ensuring that money is targeted where most needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the October 2025 Review of Environmental Governance report, (i) for his assessment of the report's position that NI Water's protection from public prosecution for pollution incidents should be ended; (ii) for his assessment of their call for an independent environmental regulator; and (iii) to detail any actions he will he take to ensure pollution incidents by the Department for Infrastructure (a) are prevented; and (b) penalised as appropriate.
answer_text: I welcome the findings of the Review of Environmental Governance report. I am considering its recommendations, which include that my department should exit the current Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent (SORPI) arrangement with Northern Ireland Water (NIW). I fully support the recommendation that a new independent environmental regulator for Northern Ireland should be established as a non-departmental public body.  I intend to take a paper to the Executive and to address the Assembly on the next steps in the short time ahead.  NIW’s wastewater discharges are regulated by discharge consents under the Water Order 1999. My department assesses NIW’s compliance annually and enforces action if pollution or non-compliances are identified, in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 33426/22-27, to detail the financial cost to the economy.
answer_text: In terms of my assessment of the financial cost to the economy, I have previously noted that the second quarter of 2025 has seen the highest number of new house starts since 2018 and that construction output has reached a 15 year high in the North in the same period. Indeed, NISRA has reported that the total volume of construction output increased by 3.8% in Q2 2025, and output grew by 7.3% over the year ending in Q2 2025. In the same period, New Work increased by 6.3% and Repair and Maintenance by 2.6% while the Housing subsector rose by a significant 15.6%. This growth does not suggest a financial cost to the economy and clearly indicates the result of financial investment into the economy. I have supported this investment through the provision of over £0.5bn to the delivery of water and wastewater services this year. Since Autumn 2024 my department has been able to unlock wastewater capacity to enable future wastewater connections for some 5,300 new homes, with 2,300 new wastewater connections already enabled through the allocation of an additional £19.5m to NI Water in October 2024, and a further 3000 property connections enabled as a result of my allocation of £11m in June 2025. However, financial investment is just one approach. NI Water’s application of innovative solutions to wastewater treatment in Newry will also connect another 400 properties to the network and unlocking vital economic growth for the region. I am encouraged that the company is seeking to develop this innovation for other areas in the North where getting connections is an issue.We continue to face challenges in the delivery of water and wastewater infrastructure, and I am progressing with my three-pronged plan to tackle this issue.   This involves working with Executive colleagues to get the necessary funding for this essential service, and address years of the British government’s austerity measures, which have been ultimately responsible for the financial cost to our public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the level of unmet need relating to young people who are struggling to cope with eating disorders.
answer_text: Eating disorders are serious and often complicated mental health illnesses which can be life threatening. In addition, young people with eating disorders are at a higher risk of medical complications and co-occurring mental illnesses. The CAMHS integrated stepped care pathway is an evidenced based framework which matches the intensity of treatment to a young person’s needs starting with the least intensive intervention rising toward a more intensive level if deemed necessary. It is not possible to determine whether there are multiple referrals for individuals as the information submitted by HSC Trusts is in an aggregated format and cannot be disaggregated. 4. As part of the Encompass roll-out, all Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system. As Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, their data is undergoing extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data they are able to submit for reporting purposes.  6. This has been recognised/categorised as a feeding or eating disorder in the DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders). Having traditionally been associated with higher prevalence rates amongst teenage girls and

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their assessment of all frontline staff having mental health and suicide awareness training.
answer_text: All TEO staff have access to a range of training and other resources on mental health and wellbeing.  Staff in roles which involve engaging with people who have experienced trauma have received additional, targeted training on resilience and trauma-informed approaches.  In addition, TEO's programmes such as Communities in Transition and Urban Villages support important interventions to address mental wellbeing in the wider community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with (i) HMRC; and (ii) the Department for Work and Communities, in relation to occasions where residents in the North have had their child benefit stopped for travelling to Dublin Airport to fly to England.
answer_text: I have not had any engagement with either HMRC or the Department for Work and Pensions on this issue. 		Child Benefit is a reserved matter and is the responsibility of HMRC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support available to homeowners to upgrade their septic tanks in line with environmental compliance requirements.
answer_text: My department has no current financial support schemes in place that would cover this expenditure.  However, I understand that the Housing Executive offers grants for home renovation and repairs, based on individual circumstances, and that further details are available at:www.nihe.gov.uk/Housing-Help/Grants/Types-of-grants-available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether all island CAR-T therapy access was discussed at the most recent meeting of the North South Ministerial Council.
answer_text: The North South Ministerial Council met in Dublin on 17 October 2025.  Ministers provided updates on a wide range of areas of collaboration including on Health issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any resources her Department will allocate for the clean up efforts, following the recent heavy rainfall.
answer_text: During the recent severe weather event, staff from my Department were on site to mitigate the impacts of the severe run off and to carry out the clean-up operation. There will be further works required to repair the damage caused by the severe weather event and work is ongoing to identify the scale of the works and the associated costs.The costs associated with both the clean-up operation and the remedial works will form part of a bid for additional funding at the next available opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the rational for trees being cut down in Ballymoyer Forest; and [ii] whether there are any plans to replant trees in Ballymoyer Forest.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn caused significant damage to parts of Ballymoyer forest, resulting in public safety concerns due to the fallen and unstable trees.  Forest Service undertook initial tree clearance work in the immediate aftermath of the storm to facilitate reopening of the public road. Subsequent harvesting of the remaining storm damaged trees was completed in August 2025.  Plans are in place for the area harvested to be replanted in the 2026 planting season.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the programme for the reconstruction of the Maydown Bridge, Benburb.
answer_text: As a result of Storm Amy, a retaining wall on the Maydown Road, Benburb, suffered damage causing a partial collapse of the wall, verge and carriageway. In the interest of public safety, my Department took the decision to close a short section of Maydown Road in Benburb and is working at pace to assess the damage and carry out repairs.   Urgent discussions are ongoing with a number of utility companies to divert or deactivate their exposed pipes/cables to make the area safe for repair works to progress. = I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and apologise to the local community, including businesses in the area, and all road users for the inconvenience caused. Local diversions are in place and I urge the travelling public to drive with due care and attention and allow extra time for their journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that house building with associated design, enabling, and construction works is a major economic driver in terms of inward investment, to detail how they are working to address inadequate drainage and treatment capacity, that is stalling development.
answer_text: Infrastructure is central to everything we do as a society, including growing the economy and supporting housing connections, and I am determined to continue to deliver positive changes for everyone in the North. To help unlock developments and encourage economic growth for all parts of the North, I will continue to develop the three-pronged approach that is already showing results.  Firstly, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water and this year my Department will provide NI Water with over £500m of public money to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services.  When the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, NI Water is now receiving 90% of what it identified it could deliver this year, representing a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure.  Secondly, my Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill, which is progressing through its Assembly Stages, will make provision for future regulations on sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, my Department has consulted on proposals for developer contributions to help fund wastewater infrastructure improvements.  That consultation has now closed and a consultation response paper will issue in the coming weeks.I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints to support future housing and wider economic development. Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office we have enabled the unlocking of wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North. This is already above the 4,300 that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control period in 2028, and it is only 2025.  I also note that the second quarter of 2025 has seen the highest number of new house starts since 2018 and that construction output has reached a 15 year high in the North in the same period. This is positive news for people, jobs and the economy.My Department has also secured £15m over the next four years from the Executive’s Transformation Project to take forward an Urban Drainage Transformation Project.  The aim of this project is to retrofit a range of nature-based drainage solutions into the urban environment to manage rainwater on the surface and slow its flow into our rivers and sewers.  This innovative pilot project will help transform the way in which rainwater is managed and improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the existing infrastructure.Additionally, I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to unlocking our wastewater constraints. The recent announcement connecting 400 properties in Newry shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions - this lies at

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average waiting time for a red flag breast cancer referral, in each of the last five years including 2025 up to October. broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any work his Department is doing to bring waiting times in line with its target of 14 days.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for every Emergency Department, in each of the last five years, including 2025 up to October.
answer_text: Information on the current average waiting time for every emergency department (ED) is not available. However, information on the median time spent in each ED from arrival to discharge or admission is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics’ report, the most recent of which covers the period July to September 2025, and is available on my Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/emergency-care-waiting-timesRecognising the challenges faced by EDs, my Department has worked closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays. To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has implemented a range of targeted initiatives. These include the expansion of urgent care centres, Phone First services and minor injury units, offering patients timely alternatives to ED attendance.Ahead of the coming winter, my Department has worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and the Trusts to ensure that an additional 20 emergency medicine consultants have been appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland. A further ten consultants are expected to complete their training by December 2025, and it is anticipated that they will also be recruited locally to provide further resilience and senior decision-making capacity to our EDs this winter.Ultimately, all of these measures are aimed at improving flow throughout the hospital, reducing congestion in our EDs and improving waiting times.These efforts are part of a broader system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care, both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timeframe for the implementation of the approved controlled pedestrian crossing outside St Joseph's Convent School, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need. Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the proposal for St Joseph’s Convent School did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become available.I am however pleased to confirm that we have recently enhanced safety measures at this location by installing new pedestrian guard rails on both Catherine Street and Edward Street.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the role her Department can have in preventing homelessness, given the findings of Simon Community’s new research, Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, which found that institutional contact,  such as being in prison or a youth justice institution, significantly heightens a person’s risk of becoming homeless.
answer_text: My Department recognises that securing suitable accommodation on release is a critical factor in successful resettlement. Supporting the housing needs of those in custody is therefore a key part of our rehabilitative provision. While the Department for Communities (DfC) and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) have responsibility for housing, addressing those leaving the criminal justice system requires cross-executive collaboration and a multi-agency approach. To that end, my Department has introduced initiatives to meet our aim that everyone in custody should have accommodation available on release. We published The Protocol for the Management of the Accommodation and Related Support Needs of People in Custody in Northern Ireland in December 2023 which sets out a collaborative approach involving NIHE, Probation Board NI and the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) and have taken forward a range of initiatives to support this.We contribute to the DfC led Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan (IDHAP), supporting cross-departmental efforts to improve outcomes for those at risk of homelessness. One of our key actions is delivering early intervention support for those exiting custody. NIPS assesses the housing needs of prisoners upon committal, including their housing arrangements, which enables staff to put suitable interventions in place, such as support from the Housing Rights advocacy service (also within the prison). A pilot programme placing NIHE Housing Advisors in Maghaberry Prison provides early intervention on housing needs. This allows decisions on accommodation to take cognisance of other resettlement factors including health and employability. The evaluation of the pilot has shown positive outcomes and we plan to extend this provision to Magilligan Prison and Hydebank Wood Secure College and Womens Prison in 2025-26. NIPS and PBNI work in partnership to utilise Approved Premises to enable appropriate support and supervision arrangements for high-risk offenders who are subject to Public Protection Arrangements in NI (PPANI) requirements. We also collaborate with Belfast City Council led Complex Lives multi-disciplinary team (including Extern, NIHE and Belfast Trust) to support female prisoners who are known to the programme who come into contact with the prison system and are most at risk of homelessness.At a cross-Executive level we are also progressing the development of a new Strategic Framework and Strategy to reduce offending and reoffending in Northern Ireland. This will seek to address many of the socioeconomic factors and adversities that put people at risk of coming into contact with the criminal justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the role his Department can have in (i) identifying those at risk of; and (ii) preventing, homelessness, given the findings of Simon Community’s new research, Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, which found that homelessness often starts in childhood.
answer_text: In response to the Simon Community’s research “Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle”, my Department recognises the vital role education plays in identifying and supporting children and young people at risk of homelessness.  As not all children and young people disclose their housing status, schools are key to recognising early signs and ensuring timely intervention.Officials from my Department attended the launch event on 7 October 2025 to support the report’s findings and explore opportunities for early intervention through education.Working in collaboration with the Education Authority (EA) and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA), my Department is committed to supporting children and young people affected by all forms of homelessness.  This includes developing and promoting educational resources, providing teacher training, running youth engagement initiatives, and offering direct support through the Education Welfare Service (EWS). My Department is also actively engaged in the Upstream Homelessness Pilot, launched in October 2025 and delivered in partnership with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) and MACS Supporting Children & Young People (MACS).  Through the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan (IDHAP), led by the Department for Communities (DFC), my Department contributes to wider efforts to address the non-accommodation factors that can lead to homelessness.  My Department remain committed to breaking the cycle of homelessness through education, early intervention, and cross-sector collaboration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the number of housing units that are unable to proceed through planning to construction due to inadequate wastewater drainage and treatment infrastructure.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that the information sought is not readily available in the time permitted.  However, I commend NI Water’s approach in helping to find solutions to permit new connections to facilitate housing and economic growth and can provide the member with a high-level overview of NI Water’s responses to planning applications over the past four years. Since July 2021, NI Water as a statutory consultee in the planning process, has responded to 17,017 planning applications.Of these, 10,174 applications received a positive response, recommending approval with standard planning conditions, with no issues. For 4,500 of these applications, NI Water recommended that these could be approved with site specific conditions which could be resolved with further engagement with NI Water.  For 2,165 of these applications, NI Water recommended refusal of the application pending discussions with the Company, as the developer had not engaged with NI Water, and a wastewater impact assessment may identify a means of achieving a connection.NI Water has also recommended to local councils that they refuse 178 applications, as the wastewater capacity constraint could only be resolved by the company undertaking some form of capital upgrade. Since July 2021, NI Water has also received 1,858 wastewater impact assessments and the Company has found a solution that will permit connections to its wastewater infrastructure for all but 135.  For those, the developer was advised that there was no solution available.NI Water has also advised developers for 79 sites, that the optimal solution for the development was to provide a dedicated onsite wastewater treatment works.Despite the current wastewater capacity challenges, I note that the second quarter of 2025 has seen the highest number of new house starts since 2018 and that construction output has reached a 15 year high in the North in the same period. This is positive news for people, jobs and the economy.  To help further unlock developments and encourage economic growth for all parts of the North, I will continue to develop my three-pronged approach that is already showing results.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action her Department has taken to ensure all television providers air RTÉ as standard.
answer_text: I recognise the cultural and linguistic significance of Irish channels for many communities across the North, and I support maximising access to RTÉ, and other Irish broadcasters. However, broadcasting policy, including the regulation of television services and channel availability, is a reserved matter for the British Government.My Department has raised this issue with the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS), and DCMS has written to the Irish Government on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on all scheduled remedial works at St Patrick's Grammar School, Armagh.
answer_text: Officials from my Department and the Education Authority (EA) are working closely together to review the significant issues at St Patrick’s Grammar School relating to mould remediation and upgrade of the heating works. Both are significant schemes and will inevitably involve significant disruption to the school, so officials are reviewing all options to ensure the preferred options provide value for money but also minimise disruption as much as possible. I understand that EA Maintenance has carried out emergency works to the heating system to sustain the system in advance of the main heating upgrade scheme.Officials will continue to engage with the school and keep them updated on these urgent works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) discretionary support loans; and (ii) discretionary support grants, that have been paid by his Department, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department publishes an annual report which details Discretionary Support performance and expenditure during each financial year, including the total number of Discretionary Support loans and grants.  The reports are currently available up to 2023/24 with the 2024/25 report currently in progress to be published later this year. Due to system limitations a breakdown by constituency is not available. The annual reports can be accessed via the following website www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he has any plans to make the transition from rates relief to rates rebate more streamlined to ensure that people do not have to pay rates in the interim.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to change current arrangements for people who claim Universal Credit and transition from rates relief to rates rebate.The requirement to pay rates during the transition period is a matter for Land & Property Services, who administer the Rate Rebate scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timeframe for signalised junction upgrades at (i) Dublin Road and Dominic Street, Newry; and (ii) Francis Street and Dominic Street, Newry.
answer_text: The upgrades to the signalised junction at Dublin Road and Dominic Street are progressing and are scheduled to be completed by the end of 2025.The upgrades to the Francis Street and Dominic Street Junction in Newry have also commenced and are also scheduled to be completed by the end of 2025.The precise timing of these works is subject to the availability of contractor resources and favourable weather conditions. As such, it is not possible to provide a definitive timescale for the completion of these schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support available to single parents, in full-time education, who also homeschool their children.
answer_text: Parents may choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school, however if they choose to home educate their child they take on full responsibility for ensuring their child receives an efficient full-time education suitable to the child’s needs. Parents who choose to educate their children outside of the publicly funded education system do not receive financial assistance towards the education they provide.The Education Authority (EA) has published guidelines to support parents who choose to educate their children outside of the formal school system which provide information on home education provision and outline the roles and responsibilities of parents in ensuring their child receives a suitable education. Information is included on special educational needs and on organisations that support home educating families. Families can contact the EA Elective Home Education Team for advice or support.  https://www.eani.org.uk/services/elective-home-education The EA provides support for School Aged Mothers to continue their education and financial support is available for full time pupils aged 16-19 from low-income families through the Education Maintenance Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any financial supports there are for single parents in full-time education.
answer_text: Financial support for single parents in full-time education may be available through Universal Credit, subject to meeting the necessary eligibility criteria. Additional financial support may also be available through the Discretionary Support Scheme which can help those in need of short-term assistance in crisis or emergency situations. My Department’s Make the Call service is available to ensure people are receiving all of the benefits, services and supports they are entitled to.Further information on Universal Credit and the Discretionary Support Scheme can be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/universal-credit and https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/discretionary-supportFurther information on how to contact the Make the Call service can be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-serviceFurther information on additional financial support for single parents in full-time education can also be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/extra-help-students-children

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32184/22-27, to detail (i) the timeframe for these works; and (ii) the planned works.
answer_text: Congestion is an issue that affects many of our urban centres, including Camlough Road, particularly during the morning and evening peak periods. The planned works in relation to the sequencing of the traffic signals at this location is currently at the design stage.  This will determine the most suitable solution to increase the efficiency of the traffic lights at this roundabout without comprising safety, particularly with traffic backing onto the high speed A1.  As the works are at the design stage, I cannot provide a definitive date for their implementation, although I would expect the scheme to be completed within this financial year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment that victims of the Troubles who experienced intra-community violence are ineligible for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) makes decisions on any entitlement to payment under the Scheme.  To be eligible for payment under the Scheme, applications must meet the entitlement criteria as outlined in paragraph 5 of the Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020.The VPB has also published guidance on its website which provides details as to how a relevant incident is defined.  Where an application has been determined to be ineligible by the Board, it will be because it did not meet one or more of the criteria as set out in the Regulations.  Applications with multiple relevant incidents may result in ‘split pathway’ decisions i.e. contain eligible and ineligible relevant incidents within one application. A determination made by the Board may be appealed within 12 months of the date of the determination. This can include an appeal against a decision regarding eligibility, degree of disablement or regarding adjustments / deductions made to an award.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have had any correspondence with the Secretary of State in relation to clarifying the criteria that must be met in order to call a border poll.
answer_text: There is no agreed position on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Community Transport Review, whether funding will move to a multi-year budget.
answer_text: I have previously acknowledged my commitment to support vital community transport services.  Any commitment to providing multi-year funding to support community transport services will be dependent on the Executive setting a multiyear budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any financial loss to her Department, as a result of having no licensing agreement in place for the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department had not entered into a licensing agreement with Sinn Féin in respect of the previous billboard at Egyptian Arch, Newry, therefore no valuation has been sought from Land and Property Services.Billboards typically require a planning application, and any breaches of planning laws would generally be addressed under planning legislation, which in most cases falls under the jurisdiction of the relevant District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Community Transport Review, whether funding will be allocated on a statutory basis or as a discretionary payment.
answer_text: There is no statutory requirement for my Department to provide community transport.However, under the Part 5 of the Transport Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 my Department has powers to award discretionary funding for the purposes specified in the Act. It is under these powers that community transport schemes are funded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to the Community Transport Review, whether funding will be allocated on a needs assessment.
answer_text: I increased the level of funding awarded by my Department for 2025/26 to support community transport services by an overall 26.2%.  All of the respective community transport providers submitted grant bids sufficient to meet their requirements for the funding period and were allocated their full requested funding amounts for 2025/26.  Subject to availability of funding, my Department will continue to allocate funding based on grant applications from community transport providers.  Where the funding available is not sufficient to meet the requested bids from providers, then my Department will continue to review applications from providers and award the maximum possible to each provider based on the information available to the Department in their grant applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) regulations; and (ii) protocol, relating to carrying out construction works in the vicinity of the railway line, between Belfast and Dublin.
answer_text: Planning applications for construction works near railway infrastructure are dealt with in accordance with the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, supported by subordinate legislation made under the Act, which sets out detailed procedures and requirements. In the Republic of Ireland, the Planning and Development Act 2000, as amended, provides the legislative framework. Applications are assessed by the relevant local council or, where appropriate, the Department, on a case-by-case basis. This involves consideration of material planning factors, including applicable planning policies, and the local development plan. The views of statutory consultees, public representations, and the site-specific context are also taken into account to ensure a balanced and informed decision.The Protocol, as it relates to railways under the Windsor Framework, applies to the interoperability of the rail system, specifically concerning the placing on the market, authorising into service and movement of railway products. It does not apply to construction in the vicinity of railways. Such works are governed by planning regulations and the Construction (Design and Management) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2016, enforced by the Health and Safety Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the (i) reports that 23 per cent of non-domestic units, nearly 5,000 in total, are unoccupied; and (ii) economic impact this is having on town and city centres.
answer_text: While the issue of urban regeneration is complex and cross-cutting, the Department for Communities leads in this policy area through its Urban Regeneration Community Development policy framework and also produces the Town Centre database in question; you may therefore wish to consult the Department for Communities for any further information.  Although the current rate for vacant non-domestic units has increased slightly over the past three years, it is worth noting that businesses and consumers have endured an extremely turbulent and challenging environment during that period, including the recovery from Covid, inflationary pressures and general economic uncertainty. Even so, the database also indicates that the latest vacancy rate remains lower than any other recorded between April 2016 – April 2022, and below the long-run average for the series.My Department, alongside other Executive Departments and partner organisations, provide a broad range of support for businesses. Invest NI for example has a portfolio of grants, loans and investment funds, many of which are specifically targeted at early stage and small businesses - more information on these, and other guidance and support, can be found at  nibusinessinfo.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been made on the cost to the economy in relation to housing and development projects that are unable to proceed due to inadequate drainage and wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: Infrastructure is central to everything we do as a society, including growing the economy and supporting housing connections, and I am determined to continue to address regional imbalance and deliver positive changes for all people. In terms of my assessment of the cost to the economy, I have noted that the second quarter of 2025 has seen the highest number of new house starts since 2018 and that construction output has reached a 15 year high in the North in the same period. This is positive news for people, jobs and the economy.   Since coming into office, I have built on my predecessor’s work in addressing the capacity issues affecting our wastewater infrastructure. To date my department has provided ringfenced allocations to enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity for some 5,300 properties to connect to sewerage infrastructure across the North. This is notable as it is already above the 4,500 properties that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control period in 2028, and it is only 2025. This year my Department will provide NI Water with over £500m of public money to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North.  When the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, NI Water is now receiving 90% of what it identified it could deliver this year, representing a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure.  Furthermore, I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to addressing our wastewater constraints. To that end, NI Water is progressing innovative improvements to wastewater infrastructure in Newry, which will release capacity to unlock development in Newry for the next 10 years and enable connection for over 400 new properties, including a modern manufacturing facility and the new civic centre. This work shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions.I plan to continue that work, to seek other opportunities for similar innovation across the North, and my officials are continuing to work collaboratively with colleagues in DAERA and DfC to contribute to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, given the findings of the Simon Community’s recent report, Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, to detail his assessment of the potential of multi-year budgets, to better prevent and tackle homelessness.
answer_text: I attended the launch of the Simon Community’s report on 7 October 2025. Hearing from those with the lived experience of homelessness and the challenges they have faced has provided valuable insight into the problems we are facing and the need to address these.Access to a good home is a basic right everyone should expect to have met. Our Programme for Government sets a clear priority in relation to tackling the issues around social housing and homelessness. In addition, a key component of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy is the prevention of homelessness.    A multi-year Budget will enable the Executive to plan on a longer-term basis to create the conditions and circumstances for transformation and to support the delivery of our Programme for Government priorities. While the budget position will continue to be challenging, I will work together with my Executive colleagues to develop a budget which supports families, workers, businesses and communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average length of time from first appointment with a GP to diagnosis for a red flag breast cancer patient, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when road signage for cyclists was last updated on Canal Bank, Poyntzpass.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.While there are many locations that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.My Department has not received any recent requests to assess the existing signage along Canal Bank Road. The road is used by vehicles, cyclists and pedestrians and has a good safety record with no collisions reported in the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any practical assistance available to Members' constituency offices to allow them to best assist constituents who (i) are visually impaired; (ii) are deaf or have hearing loss; and (iii) have a speech impairment.
