answer_text: Under the provisions of the Assembly Members (Office and Staffing Costs and Allowances) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2025 (‘the Determination’) Members can avail of funding to assist them with exercising their functions as a Member, including establishing and running constituency offices.While not explicitly set out in the Determination, Members may use the Constituency Office Operating Costs (COOC) to cover expenditure incurred in offering additional assistance to each of the groups set out above.  Recognising that the level of support required will vary from Member to Member, the specific assistance which is covered by this allowance is not defined.  It may include, but not be limited to, the costs incurred to make reasonable adjustments (not covered by the terms of the lease) to the office to improved accessibility for constituents, or to cover the costs of engaging interpreters and sign language specialists.Under paragraph 14 of the 2025 Determination, Members may also recover Disability costs.  This includes those costs incurred in making reasonable adjustments to the constituency office, which are not covered under the terms of the lease for that office, to facilitate access for disabled members of the public.  These costs are not defined and are assessed on a case-by-case basis.  However, Disability costs may cover costs to improve the overall accessibility of the office, such as altering the layout, providing specialist equipment, improve signage or lighting etc, to assist a Member in providing services to all constituents.  Further guidance on what type of costs may be recovered is provided in the Administration Guide which is published to accompany the Determination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department supports independent bookstores.
answer_text: My Department along with Invest NI and Tourism NI provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. I am also committed to engaging with the local retail sector to understand the issues they are facing. My Department does not offer sector specific support for independent bookstores.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action her Department is taking to ensure that NI Water bonds are put in place urgently for Craigmore Way, Newry, to facilitate the construction programme progressing.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:   NI Water has been liaising with the developer for approximately six months regarding the folios and determination of land ownership. NI Water believes that these issues will be resolved in the near future. Draft agreements have been issued and once the outstanding issues have been resolved, NI Water will issue the Article 161 agreements for signing.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: On 21 March I launched a consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure. The consultation ran for 14 weeks and sought views on options for a possible pathway to introduce developer contributions – either on a voluntary basis or in the form of a compulsory levy – which may help to fund wastewater upgrades to unlock the capacity of our sewerage network. 293 responses to the consultation were received.  These responses are currently being considered with a view to publishing a Consultation Responses Report in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of delivery on the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: The financial constraints facing my Department are well documented and, since the publication of the Mental Health Strategy in 2021, no additional funding has been allocated to support its delivery. This presents a significant challenge and places considerable pressure on our ability to deliver to best effect and achieve the outcomes we know are needed.The absence of dedicated additional funding has necessitated a partial and phased implementation and, undoubtably, progress has been constrained in areas due to the challenging financial position.  Despite the extremely challenging financial context, good progress has been made in a number of MHS actions, including: The development and implementation of a three-year action plan focused on early intervention and prevention. The establishment of the infrastructure to support a regionally consistent Mental Health Service. Targeted investment in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS). The rollout of a region-wide perinatal mental health service; and Enhanced support for crisis and specialist services, such as those for personality disorders and eating disorders. I am personally disappointed that, halfway into the lifetime of the Strategy, it has been necessary for my Department to undertake a review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions from 2026-2029. The purpose of doing so is to provide a focus and to prioritise in light of the funding constraints we face, whilst recognising that the Strategy in its entirety is still needed.This Review, which fulfils a commitment made to the Public Accounts Committee, sets out what has been achieved with the limited funding available to date, as well as the key priorities for the next three years.  This review does not represent a dilution of our commitment to delivering for mental health, but rather a sharpening of our focus to maximise impact within the resources available.Mental Health Workforce and the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service have been identified as immediate priorities for 2026/27 with both seen as crucial enablers for broader system improvement. Any additional resources we secure for mental health will be directed into priority areas identified through the review. In the absence of additional funding, we will strengthen our efforts to maximise the impact of every existing resource, ensuring they are utilised to their fullest potential for the benefit of our population.Mental health remains a personal priority for me. I have been clear that delivering the Strategy requires sustained and additional investment and I will continue to make the case for mental health until the ambition so clearly set out in 2021 is fully realised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) when the last Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme payments are due to be made; (ii) the estimated  annual cost of continuing these payments; and (iii) the annual cost since the Scheme's inception.
answer_text: The Department has recently launched a public consultation setting out the proposals for Scheme closure. The consultation can be found at https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/consultations/closure-northern-ireland-non-domestic-renewable-heat-incentive-schemeIn respect of your specific queries:It is proposed that the current quarterly periodic support payments based on metered heat will be replaced by annual closure payments, and that installations will transfer on a staged basis linked to their meter reading quarter end date. My Department aims to implement the new closure payment model from 2026, in advance of OFGEM’s final withdrawal in April 2026, subject to the passage of the legislation through the Assembly. Eligible installations will attract Closure payments in line with their original 20-year accreditation period and therefore Closure payments are forecast to 2036 for some installations.The estimated cost of the closure arrangements, which are intended to run for ten years from 2026 to 2036, are £196m AME and £17m DEL. The annual cost since the Scheme's inception is published and is found at https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/publications/non-domestic-renewable-heat-incentive-rhi-statutory-information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any action he is taking in response to a finding in the 2025 SEN Reform NI Survey, in partnership with Caleb's Cause, that almost 70 per cent of parents' responses rated communication with the Education Authority as poor or very poor.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the new Special Education Needs and Disability (SEND) Central Contact Model (implemented in September) now serves as the single point of entry for all SEND-related queries, including SEND Support and Advice, a staffed helpline which is available Monday to Friday from 9am to 4.30pm.  This central contact model ensures queries are addressed more efficiently and by the most relevant personnel, reducing delays and improving the overall experience for parents and carers.Since its introduction 93% of calls to SEND Support and Advice have been answered, with callers having an average ‘wait time’ of 46 seconds before their call is answered.The Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) offers a named SEN Link Officer to parents of children undergoing statutory assessment or who have a statement of SEN.  In addition, SARS has offered 17 drop-in sessions (across mornings and afternoons) and 1,260 online sessions to parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail Translink's income uplift, since the introduction of an hourly Enterprise service.
answer_text: Translink’s income uplift over the last 11 months, since the introduction of an hourly Enterprise service, has been £3.5m.  This reflects a c45% increase which is in line with passenger journey growth over the same period.  Enterprise services have seen an increase of around 37% in passenger journeys in the period November 2024 to June 2025 when compared to the same period 12 months prior.  The percentage increase is not an Official Statistic but is based on Management Information received from Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the NI Audit Office report on Active Travel in Northern Ireland, highlighting her Department's underperformance against the delivery of active travel objectives; and (ii) to detail how she intends to address the issues raised by the NI Audit Office.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the NIAO report into active travel in the north.I can confirm that the report will be considered very carefully and a response to the recommendations will be provided in line with the established process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what financial support his Department provides for rural transport, broken down by organisation, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has provided no financial support for rural transport in each of the last five years. Rural transport is the responsibility of the Department for Infrastructure (DfI).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of comments made by the Chief Executive of Translink, that the budgetary situation could see Translink left with cash reserves of less than £10 million, equating to one week’s costs.
answer_text: The current financial year has been extremely challenging from a budgetary perspective for both Translink and my Department, which has been operating in a constrained financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The Department did not receive all the funding it requested for Budget 2025-26. Whilst the resource allocation to Translink did not meet its requirements in full, it did receive nearly 24% of the Department’s resource budget.  Translink also received more than 33% of the general capital budgets allocated to the Department.The NITHC Board approved an Outline Budget and Corporate Plan for 2025-26 which projected cash requirements and outturn to March 2026.  My officials are continuing to liaise closely with Translink to assist with its cash management as this year progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the double yellow lines on Sugar House Quay, Newry, outside the Funeral Directors, will be repainted following resurfacing works by NI Water in 2024.
answer_text: Works have been undertaken by a number of utility companies at this location since November 2023.  Utility companies are required to replace any road markings which they remove, however in this instance it appears that the company responsible has not complied with this requirement.  Given that it is not possible to ascertain the responsible party, I have asked my officials to arrange for the markings to be reinstated as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the potential for the apple growing industry in County Armagh to become a leader in green growth.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of the apple growing industry in County Armagh in terms of the positive impact that it delivers for the economy, the environment and biodiversity in the region. The draft Green Growth Strategy sets out our long-term vision and framework for a future where Northern Ireland transitions from a high to a net zero emissions society; where we can enjoy the longer term economic, social, health and environmental benefits that this brings. My Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Policy contains schemes that are designed to help the agri-food industry, including the apple growing industry, to grow sustainably.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how the law applies to cases when an individual has married shortly before dying and the widow or widower has subsequently claimed that the will of the deceased is null and void.
answer_text: ANSWERIn this jurisdiction, marriage automatically revokes any existing will, unless the will was made “in contemplation of that specific marriage” and explicitly states so. This is outlined in the Wills and Administration Proceedings (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 (Article 12).If the deceased married shortly before death and did not make a new will, the old will is invalid unless it meets the requirements of Article 12. The laws of intestacy would then apply in such circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the apple growing industry in County Armagh, in relation to (i) economic growth; and (ii) tourism.
answer_text: Whilst apple growing, as primary agricultural production, falls under the remit of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, post-farmgate, I fully recognise the contribution to our local economy by associated agri-businesses involved in fruit processing and cider production.My Department, through Invest NI, has provided support of £1.81m to such businesses in County Armagh in the five years up to 2024/25.Visitors are increasingly motivated by authentic and immersive food and drink experiences that showcase our local traditions and stunning landscapes. Armagh’s orchards and cideries are well placed to deliver on this interest.Long Meadow Cider and Armagh Cider Company both participate in Tourism NI’s Embrace a Giant Spirit Experience Portfolio, a continuous learning and development programme for established tourism businesses to enhance profitability, visitor appeal, innovation and commercialisation.I understand that both companies actively participate in a range of Tourism NI programmes and opportunities including farm trips, sales opportunities and are featured in Tourism NI’s Food & Drink guide. The Armagh Food and Cider Festival is a highlight event on the food and drink calendar of the North. Between 2020 - 2023, Tourism NI provided £70k of funding to events in the Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon area, including the Armagh Food & Cider Festival and Armagh Georgian Festival.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how her Department will encourage more employers to take on apprentices; and (ii) how often the Apprenticeship Action Plan 2025 will be reviewed.
answer_text: The Apprenticeship Action Plan sets out three core ambitions—Improve, Grow, and Include—to transform the apprenticeship landscape. Key measures to encourage business participation include:An Apprenticeship Navigator pilot to support SMEs and micro-businesses in navigating apprenticeship systems.A pilot Shared Apprenticeship model designed for project-based industries like construction, allowing multiple employers to share responsibility for training. Public Sector Apprenticeship development to target growth in apprenticeship uptake across government departments and agencies. An Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund to help engage underrepresented groups, including women and people with disabilities. The existing network of Sectoral Partnerships helps ensure that apprenticeship provision here meets the current and future needs of local businesses. The Department actively encouraging employers to take on apprentices through a range of promotional activities such as advertising campaigns across TV, radio, and social media, and direct engagement with potential employers and learners at events such as job fairs and career insight days.The Apprenticeship Action Plan spans the length of this mandate. Progress will be monitored on a quarterly basis and I intend to publish an update on progress each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the processing time for further education travel passes.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised me applications for transport assistance can take up to 20 working days to process. In the 2024/25 academic year, the average time taken to process a transport application for a student at further education college was 8.57 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made of the benefits of providing similar funding commitments for sport as was announced as part of the Budget 2026 by the Republic of Ireland Government on 7 October.
answer_text: I recognise the wide range of societal benefits that sport brings to communities across Northern Ireland. As Minister responsible for Sport, I am committed to advocate for a budget that will enable my Department to continue to support investment in our sports sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department's efforts to restore the phone signal in Mullaghbawn.
answer_text: Mobile infrastructure is delivered on a fully commercial basis here and maintenance of equipment is the responsibility of the private sector operators.Enquiries about disruption and restoration of service should be made to the appropriate supplier directly. Contact details for all mobile network operators have been issued separately to your office by my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last safety assessment was carried out on the B31 Newtownhamilton Road, from Pairc na nGael to the entrance to Ash View; and (ii) the outcome of this assessment.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.My Department receives information from PSNI on collisions involving personal injury.  This information is monitored on an annual basis and used to inform the development and prioritisation of improvement schemes at locations where engineering solutions would most effectively deliver road safety benefits. Officials have checked the injury related collisions and would consider that this stretch of Newtownhamilton Road has a good safety record and would not be considering any further road safety measures at this time. Officials have also confirmed that this section of road already has a number of appropriate warning signs in each direction along with associated SLOW markingsConcerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of road safety, including the levels of speeding, on Bulls Hill, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  My Department has not received any recent requests for a safety assessment to be carried out on Bulls Hill, Newry however a previous assessment undertaken by officials resulted in the installation of pedestrian warning signs and ‘SLOW’ road markings to further enhance safety. A resurfacing scheme between the junction of John Martin Street and Martin’s Lane has also been completed, further improving the condition and safety of the road.Officials have also reviewed the latest available PSNI collision data for the area and would consider Bulls Hill to have a good safety record, with no recorded injury related collisions in the past three years.Concerns regarding driver behaviour including speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the pipe bomb attack on the Sinn Fein office in Newry.
answer_text: Attacks on elected representatives and their staff and offices, which are there to serve the public, are abhorrent and have no place in a democracy. Those who serve the public should do so free from fear, intimidation, or violence. I condemn without reservation the latest attack on SF’s Newry office. It was not only a real threat to the representative but also posed serious danger to the public, recklessly placing lives at risk. By way of update, PSNI received a report of a suspicious device outside a Sinn Fein constituency office in Monaghan Street, Newry shortly before midnight on 5th October. Army ammunition technical officers (ATO) attended and declared the device as ‘viable’. ATO made the device safe, and it has been taken away for further examination. As this is an ongoing investigation it would be inappropriate for me to comment further; however, the PSNI has my full support as they investigate this serious incident.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on progress to redevelop Casement Park.
answer_text: As outlined in the PfG, we will make progress on the redevelopment of Casement Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average duration of admission for patients into hospital beds; and (ii) the proportion of patients in hospital beds with dementia.
answer_text: The questions that have been asked are very broad. For the purposes of the response, the table below provides information on the average hospital length of stay (LOS) for adult acute hospital beds in each Trust.The table also includes information on the percentage of patients in hospital beds during the reporting period with a recorded diagnosis of dementia.TrustAverage Acute Hospital LOSRecorded diagnosis of dementiaBelfast HSC Trust5.2 days3.2%Northern HSC Trust8.2 days4.2%South Eastern HSC Trust8.8 daysNot available(1)Southern HSC Trust7 days3.5%Western HSC Trust8.46 days4.4%(1) Information not available due to backlogs in clinical coding

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of comments from the President of the Olympic Federation of Ireland, at the All-Party group on Sports and Physical Recreation on 30th September 2025, that sport is a crucially important mechanism for teaching children about teamwork, resilience, and the importance of the ability to fail and keep going.
answer_text: I was not present to hear Lochlann Walsh’s comments, however, I agree that sport is a crucially important mechanism for teaching children about teamwork, resilience, and the importance of the ability to fail and keep going.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline why after every significant rainfall event, water flows out of the manholes on footpaths on the Dublin Road, Newry; and (ii) for her assessment of any danger that this presents for road users and pedestrians.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter; however, I have sought the required information.Road drainage systems are only designed to cope with normal average annual daily rainfall. They cannot, nor are they intended to, cope with the sudden, short duration, high intensity rain events that have become more common in recent times.  With the onset of autumn and winter there is an increased risk of adverse weather and unfortunately debris can sometimes make its way into the drainage network causing pipes and manholes to become blocked.   NI Water were onsite on 6th November and found grease in the outlet which they have removed.Safety for road users and pedestrians is one of my highest priorities and in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes. My Department will continue to monitor and clean the road drainage system, as well as working with NI Water, to reduce the impact of future weather events.   NI Water’s modelling of this area predicts flooding less than 50m3 from the storm system on the Dublin Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a breakdown of the town and city centre rate poundage, broken down by Local Council Area.
answer_text: ANSWERPoundages used for the calculation of annual rates are applied at District Council level. The rate poundages set by each District Council and those set by the Executive is located at the link below.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/articles/rate-poundages

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31167/22-27, to specifically detail (i) the (a) financial; and (b) staffing, resource that has been allocated to progressing the Armagh to Portadown rail link only; and (ii) the remaining recommendations within the All Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: As previously stated, I have ring-fenced £1m in Translink’s 2025-26 capital budget to progress projects identified in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) including the Portadown to Armagh rail link. Translink has submitted the feasibility study into the Portadown to Armagh rail link to my Department and I expect to publish the outcomes later in the Autumn. The Department of Transport has secured support from the European Investment Bank (EIB) Advisory Services to appoint a consultant team to help progress implementation of the AISRR. recommendations. My officials attend a steering group with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail, and the National Transport Authority, to review and oversee the work of the consultant team. My Department has nine staff, at various grades, involved with the AISRR alongside other duties.  This work includes attending Steering Group meetings, reviewing consultant outputs, managing correspondence regarding the Rail Review, and attending other related meetings, including with rail stakeholders.  As the work is taken forward alongside other duties, it is not possible to provide a specific breakdown for a specific project or for the AISRR as a whole.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they (i) have made; or (ii) will be making, to secure the safe return of Irish and British citizens aboard the Global Sumud Flotilla.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the reason the pay uplift for health service workers is now six months behind schedule; (ii) when this pay uplift will be delivered; and (iii) whether it will be backdated to 1 April 2025.
answer_text: I have been clear for several months now, and repeated this position on the floor of the Chamber on 6 October in response to AQO 2476/22-27, that the sole reason for any delay is the lack of available finance.I will continue to engage with Executive colleagues to try to find a resolution that will allow us to move forward in delivering a pay award that fully recognises the valuable services provided by our HSC workforce as soon as possible. I cannot provide a firm timescale for that.It would be my intention that the pay award will be backdated to 01 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the Irish News article on 1 October 2025 titled, Planning decision wait times have tripled in parts of Northern Ireland since Covid, to outline any actions her Department is taking to resolve the delay in planning application processing times.
answer_text: I would respectfully refer you to the response provided under AQW 32520/22-27, as it addresses a substantially similar query.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30851/22-27, to detail the relevant information that has been shared between her Department and the Health and Safety Executive NI, specifically in relation to ventilation and air systems at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: My officials have undertaken some preliminary enquiries regarding air quality and the ventilation system in place at Belfast Grand Central Station. In responding to these enquiries, Translink have been able to share information on their ventilation system and air quality monitoring procedure. The ventilation system complies with the technical requirements set out in regulations. Translink also shared a report on the results of their air quality monitoring demonstrating that Nitrogen Dioxide levels are within the legally permissible limits. This information has been shared with HSE NI who are responsible for occupational health and safety issues, and they are undertaking more detailed enquiries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any guarantees contained in the Dilapidation Bill (NIA Bill 21/22-27) that will avoid local councils being left with costly and lengthy legal cases when recovering money spent on repairing or demolishing derelict properties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16553/22-27, for an update on the enforcement of the removal of the illegally erected Sinn Féin Billboard at the Egyptian Arch.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 31058/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the temperature threshold for initiating the gritting of main roads.
answer_text: Winter gritting decisions, based on area specific forecasts, reflect a wide range of information, for example forecast road surface temperature, forecast road surface condition (such as dry, moist, wet) and other factors including the presence of residual salt from previous treatments.As a result of these variables, there is no single temperature threshold for initiating the gritting of main roads, rather gritting actions are determined through careful consideration of the information outlined above, on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any major road projects that are paused, awaiting the outcome of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme High Court judgement.
answer_text: My Department’s major project portfolio is complex with a range of projects at various stages of development and delivery. Every project will have its own specific considerations but in common with all Departments, I am mindful at present of the impact of the judgement in the A5WTC project which was extremely disappointing and where I have lodged an appeal.  		It would not be appropriate for me to comment in detail on the judgement while the appeal is live.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the tender documents for the A1 Junctions Phase 2 Scheme will be issued.
answer_text: My Department’s major project portfolio is complex with a range of projects at various stages of development and delivery. Every project will have its own specific considerations but in common with all Departments, I am very mindful at present of the impact of the judgement in the A5WTC project which was extremely disappointing and where I have lodged an appeal.  The A1 Junctions Phase 2 Project will deliver enhanced safety measures along the A1 route, costing in the region of £120 - £130m and will include the closing up of all gaps in the central median and the provision of a number of new grade separated junctions.The scheme was confirmed by my predecessor as a single package of works on 2 July 2024 and is currently at the procurement stage with Pre Qualification Questionnaire completed in May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of utility companies coordinating street works, to avoid doubling up of trenching and reinstatement works.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (“the Order”) forms the legislative framework for street works activities. The Department has issued a number of codes of practice under the Order which were prepared jointly by the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) and the Department.The Order places a duty on my Department to use best endeavours in the co-ordination of street works with a parallel duty on the undertakers of these works to co-operate with the department.The Code of Practice for the Co-ordination of Street Works and Works for Road Purposes and Related Matters defines the co-ordination function, with the purpose of minimising inconvenience and to protect the street structure and the apparatus within it.The Northern Ireland Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) is a vital tool in the co-ordination and communication of all such street works.There are already a number of groups set up for street works activities including NIRAUC and four Divisional Road and Utility Committees, DRAUC. Officials in my department are also in daily contact with utility providers to co-ordinate works and balance the potentially conflicting interests of road users and utility customers. There are undoubtedly merits in multiple organisations using the same excavation however this is normally only achievable on large scale road restructuring and public realm schemes when large sections of the street are excavated. This is mainly due to the differing depth requirements for apparatus types, minimum proximity distances required between apparatus and avoidance of apparatus being laid directly on top of another organisations apparatus to allow for future maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the (i) rights; and (ii) entitlements, a victim of domestic violence has, in respect of being notified of the (a) release; (b) licence conditions; and (c) bail address of an offender, in circumstances where the victim is unable to obtain this information from statutory bodies, on the grounds of data protection.
answer_text: The Victim Charter sets out the rights and entitlements for all victims of crime including domestic violence. These include rights and entitlements around being notified about release and licence conditions.If an offender is sentenced to prison, confined to hospital or is under Probation Board supervision a victim is entitled to be informed by the Victim and Witness Care Unit (VWCU) about access to a relevant post-conviction victim information scheme. The three schemes are the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) Prisoner Release Victim Information Scheme (PRVIS), the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) Victim Information Scheme (VIS), and the Mentally Disordered Offenders Victim Information Scheme (MDO). The schemes are administered by the PBNI Victims Information Unit.  Victims are entitled to ask the Victim Information Unit to provide certain information.Victims must register with the relevant scheme in order to obtain certain information, based on the criteria for each of the schemes. Victims are entitled to register under the NIPS PRVIS when the offender receives a sentence of 6 months or more. The aim of the scheme is to ensure that all eligible victims who register with PRVIS are advised and involved in accordance with their wishes in relation to the temporary and final release of any adult prisoner who has been convicted of a crime against them.Victims are entitled to register with PBNI’s VIS where the individual is subject to supervision by PBNI on licence following a period in custody. This would include individuals who are subject to Determinate Custodial Sentences, Extended Custodial Sentences and Life Licences.The MDO Victim Information Scheme is available where the offender is subject to a hospital order. Victims are entitled to information such as how the case is to be managed, the type and length of supervision and any extra conditions.The information schemes do not have any role in respect of sharing bail addresses for offenders.  However, as per the Victim Charter, as the case progresses, whether the person has been charged by the police or summonsed to court, victims are entitled to be informed by the VWCU of the outcome of any bail hearing (such as any relevant bail conditions and any relevant changes to these bail conditions).While data protection legislation regulates the disclosure of personal information, these schemes are designed to ensure that victims can access appropriate information in a way that balances privacy with protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the footway on Irish Street, Armagh, and the adverse camber and drainage channel impediments for wheelchair user residents; to detail (ii) when they were last assessed; and (iii) when will they be rectified, to allow safe movement.
answer_text: The last assessment of the footway on Irish Street, Armagh was undertaken on 9 August 2024.  My Department is not aware of any issues at this location, however officials would be happy to investigate any concerns if the Member provides some detail on the specific issue directly to the relevant section office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how his Department is preparing for winter pressures; and (ii) how people with dementia are being accommodated within that plan.
answer_text: Earlier this year, under the leadership of the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, I brought together senior leaders from across the Health and Social Care system to learn from last winter, as we plan for winter 2025/26. I was clear that I wanted a whole system approach looking at what could be achieved both in advance of this winter and in the longer-term.While not specifically focused on dementia, this process identified the need for a particular focus on interventions to improve the care of those older people living with frailty and other co-morbidities, who have been identified as some of the most vulnerable to the risks associated with the winter period.Seven action plans have been established which focus on reducing hospital demand by supporting older people to stay well and receive appropriate care in the community. This includes improving the use of advance clinical care planning and enhancing pathways for patients, ensuring the right care is delivered in the right place, first time.My Department recognises that winter preparedness cannot simply focus on primary and secondary health care settings, but that a significant and critical contributor to the delivery of effective services must be focused on ensuring that appropriate care is provided in social care settings and in people’s own homes.  The Department is currently leading a series of cross-sector roundtable discussions to better understand and address unmet need in the community care sector with a particular focus on the provision of more timely access to care whether this is at home or in a care home. I will publish the Winter Preparedness Plan in October and, as in previous years, we will again call upon the public to do everything possible to help protect our system, including utilising community services, where available, and getting vaccinated, if eligible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for a breakdown of the total revenue received by her Department, for every one pound spent by consumers on parking, using third-party apps and booking platforms; and (ii) to detail the amount of revenue generated by private companies in respect of parking apps and booking platforms, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: A cashless app, JustPark, is available for use in the Department’s three Park & Ride car parks in Belfast and the on-street Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs) in Belfast, Lisburn, and Newry.The table below shows the total parking revenue collected by JustPark, transferred to my Department, for each year from 2022 to 2025 (January to December) or part thereof.YearsTotal number of JustPark transactionsTotal revenue (£k)20221,201,5372,06420231,449,6952,86720241,482,3963,0482025*942,6881,922     *Jan 2025 – Aug 2025Customers who use the cashless app will also pay an additional 10p transaction charge per parking event. This charge is retained by JustPark. JustPark customers can also opt-in to receive supplementary services, such as SMS text message reminders and push notifications, for an agreed charge. My Department do not hold information on these optional services. My Department has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have formally written to the Prime Minister to object to his proposals for digital ID cards.
answer_text: We have outlined our concerns regarding the proposed Digital ID directly to Darren Jones, the Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how the junction at Edward Street and Catherine Street, Newry, can be made safer for (i) drivers; (ii) pedestrians; and (iii) adjacent properties.
answer_text: My officials have identified this location as a collision remedial cluster site and are awaiting a report which identifies options to improve safety. Subject to the selection of an appropriate solution, a scheme may then be considered in a future works programme.I can also confirm that my officials have previously provided a pedestrian island on Catherine Street outside the St Joseph’s Convent Primary School Street close to the junction. A new pedestrian guardrail was also erected recently outside the school, as an additional safety measure.Officials have also undertaken assessments for controlled crossings at this location.  The outcome of these assessments was successful, and the location has been added to a prioritised list.  The location will be considered for inclusion in a future works programme, alongside other Local and Transport Safety Measures (LTSM) schemes on the prioritised list, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the bids her Department has made to the Shared Island Unit for the reopening of the Newry Canal, creating a blue green link between Carlingford Lough and Lough Neagh.
answer_text: My Department’s remit on the Newry Canal, where it is designated between Scarva and WINIndustrial Estate in Newry, extends only to the drainage function of this waterway and thereforeit has no responsibility for recreational or navigational purposes. Therefore, my Department has not engaged with the Shared Island Unit on reopening of the Newry Canal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department's target timeline for repairing street lighting in general.
answer_text: I am pleased to inform the member that, for the current financial year, I have been able to allocate funding to enable my Department to provide a full street lighting maintenance service.I can confirm that street lighting outage repairs are generally being carried out within the established target of 5 working days from the receipt of an initial report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will be addressing the funding concerns, as outlined in recent weeks by the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. I am committed to improving our infrastructure, as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy. I have already taken action to minimise the impact of the funding concerns outlined by the Mineral Products Association, specifically around capital structural maintenance. At the outset of the financial year, I overplanned against my Department's opening 2025-26 capital budget, which allowed funding to be maximised at the start of the year. Had I not taken such action, the impact would likely have been greater. In June Monitoring, my Department bid for additional funding for Structural Maintenance. While this bid was not successful, I continue to make the case for additional funding to improve our roads. I have also set out my Department’s investment needs as part of future budget exercises and will continue to engage with my Executive colleagues on the investment required in our infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the footway on English Street, Armagh and the adverse camber and drainage channel impediments for wheelchair user residents; to detail (ii) when they were last assessed; and (iii) when will they be rectified, to allow safe movement.
answer_text: The last assessment of the footway on English Street, Armagh was undertaken on 9 August 2024.  My Department is not aware of any issues at this location, however officials would be happy to investigate any concerns if the Member provides some detail on the specific issue directly to the relevant section office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has conducted a review of the traffic lights on the Camlough Road roundabout at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, with a view to reducing tailbacks for (i) traffic travelling Newry bound from Camlough; and (ii) traffic travelling out of Newry via the Camlough Road.
answer_text: Congestion is an issue that affects many of our urban centres, including Camlough Road, particularly during the morning and evening peak periods. My officials are aware of issues with sequencing of the traffic signals at this location and work is planned to try to make the operation of this roundabout as efficient as possible without compromising on safety, particularly with traffic backing up onto the high speed A1.Unfortunately, due to the constraint in width through the Egyptian Arch, it is not possible to increase the number of lanes into and exiting the roundabout from the Camlough Road which could significantly reduce the queuing and the delays.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether she will be commencing a trial for one hour free on-street parking in Newry; and (ii) for her assessments of the merits of the removal of the controlled parking zone in Newry.
answer_text: Controlled parking zones seek to manage demand for parking in an appropriate way, deterring all day parking and improving customer access to businesses by encouraging an ongoing turnover of parking spaces. The controlled parking zone in Newry continues to help manage traffic and ensure that urban centres can be shared spaces for everyone and I currently have no plans to consider its removal. There are no plans to commence a trial for one hour free on-street parking in Newry, however, upon coming into office, I  asked my officials to explore a number of measures to potentially provide greater flexibility to my Department’s approach to controlled parking, that paper is with me for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how her Department plans to address the shortage of taxis in Newry, which is detrimental to the night-time economy.
answer_text: I would refer you to my response to AQW 26798/22-27, which you tabled on 8 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the importance of street lighting in town centres.
answer_text: I consider street lighting to be a vital component of urban infrastructure, contributing significantly to the safety, economic resilience, and aesthetic quality of town centres.The provision of good quality street lighting in town centres, contributes towards public safety by deterring crime, improving visibility for pedestrians and drivers and enhancing CCTV images. Economically and aesthetically, it supports extended commercial activity, encouraging footfall during evening hours, and generally improving the night-time environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any plans her Department has to help hospitality businesses, given the disparity in VAT rates between the north and the south.
answer_text: I’m aware of the challenges facing our hospitality industry with rising costs, particularly in relation to the higher VAT rates faced by businesses here relative to the South. I engage regularly with representatives of the hospitality industry, meeting recently with the NI Food to Go Association and Hospitality Ulster. VAT is an excepted matter and decisions to change the rate of VAT rests with the British Government, which has unfortunately not shown any willingness to address this matter so far. When Finance Minister, I commissioned a research report on the cost of doing business. It was published in May this year and sets out further evidence concerning the disparity of VAT, especially for our hospitality businesses operating near the border.This is an issue that the Finance Minister has raised with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) both in writing and when they met earlier this year. I understand the Finance Minister has urged the CST to consider the case for a reduction in VAT to enable our hospitality businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy.  I will continue to work with the Finance Minister as we continue to press the British Government to consider all possible means of putting a VAT regime in place that enables our businesses to compete within the all-Ireland economy. My Department and partner organisations provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all enforcement notices that have been served to political parties for the illegal erection of billboards, in the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 31057 / 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail who has been appointed to explore a pedestrian and cycle link between the Newry Canal towpath and Carlingford to Newry Greenway; and (ii) for her assessment of the merits in creating a linear park through Newry to facilitate this link.
answer_text: My department is currently in the process of appointing consultants to progress the design and planning phases of six signature active travel schemes across the north, including the Newry Inner City Greenway which aims to connect the Newry Canal Towpath to the Carlingford Lough Greenway. Stakeholder engagement will be a key part of the design development process, and my department would be happy to engage with potential delivery partners on any complementary measures that would further encourage and enable people in the area to choose to walk, wheel or cycle for shorter everyday journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of reinstating the basin between Sugar House Quay and the Clanrye River, Newry.
answer_text: My Department undertakes watercourse maintenance on designated sections of the Newry Canal to help ensure the free flow of water, alleviate flooding and assist land drainage. Any assessment regarding the restoration of the canal for navigation or tourism purposes would rest with the two Councils that own it, namely Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council and Newry, Mourne and Down District Council. My Department’s Rivers Directorate is progressing the development of a Flood Alleviation Scheme for Newry City. As part of the scheme’s development utilisation of the canal was considered for storage and conveyance of flood waters. However, these proposals were found not to be viable and therefore do not form part of the current preferred option. The preferred option does include construction of a flow control structure on the canal to limit flood waters entering the city. An additional phase of the scheme will consider if upstream storage can be used to further reduce flood risk in the surrounding area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the view from the Chief of the Mineral Products Association Northern Ireland, that the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme could have been the first carbon-neutral dual carriageway if proper procedures had been followed.
answer_text: Scheme design of the A5 Western Transport Corridor falls under the responsibility of the Department for Infrastructure. Throughout my time as AERA Minister I have been very clear on the importance I place on climate action and the need to meet our requirements under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how her Department categorises the Newry Canal.
answer_text: The Newry Canal from the Point of Whitecoat, Portadown to the Win Business Park, Newry is a designated watercourse. This means my Department has the power to carry out any work it considers necessary to maintain the free flow of the water on this watercourse to facilitate drainage outlets and reduce flood risk.My Department does not have responsibility for maintenance of the section of the Newry Canal that flows south through the city from Win Business Park towards Victoria Lock, as it is not designated. A designated watercourse means it has been designated by the Drainage Council for the North of Ireland to allow it to be maintained at public expense.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any recent engagement they have had on the progress to redevelop Casement Park.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park remains an Executive flagship capital project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget she will be allocating for (i) roads maintenance; and (ii) resurfacing works, in the 2025 October monitoring round.
answer_text: A formal October Monitoring Round has not been commissioned.  However, should this be commissioned, in light of the many competing financial demands across my Department, I intend to bid for investment in infrastructure, including the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of revenue raising alternatives, as presented by the Regional Director of the Mineral Products Association NI at the Committee for Infrastructure meeting on 24 September 2025.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of financial sustainability and need to consider revenue-raising measures. In doing so however, I must also carefully balance the impact of additional costs borne by citizens in the context of cost-of-living pressures, while also ensuring cost recovery, fairness, and value for money. I have been clear that I will not add to the financial burden being faced by people here by imposing any form of water charges. In relation to road tolling and other road user charging, for example congestion charging, my officials are currently exploring this as a potential revenue source. This would generate investment and act as a strategic policy tool to inform wider infrastructure financing strategies and future policy decisions. As this is at an early stage, it has not been fully assessed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to expand the public electric vehicle charge point network in Newry and Armagh, given the increasing popularity of electric and plug-in hybrid vehicles.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North.  Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you on the position of their current installations and on their plans to install further charge points within their wider networks.There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last two years. The publicly accessible commercial charge point network now has almost 700 chargers in place, a growth in EV charging provision of 75% since April 2023. The Rapid/Ultra Rapid public charging network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 400% here in the same period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of of the view, given by the Regional Director of the Mineral Products Association NI at the Committee for Infrastructure meeting on 24 September 2025, that the most serious deterioration of the road network that her administration has ever seen, is now likely to happen given current budgetary challenges.
answer_text: There has been historic underfunding for the maintenance of the road network for a number of years, and I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available to me as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy.The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ highlighted the need for increased spending on Capital Structural Maintenance, however the impact on the integrity and safety of the road network is as a result of the longer term underfunding, rather than the impact of a single year.I have met recently with the MPANI to discuss the concerns of the industry and I remain committed to seeking additional funding to address the need to invest in the road network.There are, however, many competing financial demands across my Department and it will be challenging to address years of underfunding; however, I am committed to improving our roads here and I continue to pursue additional funding for investment in the road network.I will continue to make the case for investment in infrastructure here, which includes the quality of our road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the view, given by the Regional Director of the Mineral Products Association NI at the Committee for Infrastructure meeting on 24 September 2025, that the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme could have been the first carbon neutral dual carriageway, if proper procedures had been followed.
answer_text: While I respectfully acknowledge the views expressed by the Regional Director of the Mineral Products Association NI, I would offer a differing perspective.  The contracts for the design, development and potential construction of A5WTC were awarded in 2009 and set up as a 2 Phase contract. The requirements of these contracts were in line with the DoF Procurement Policy and Guidance current at the time.  Since then, the A5WTC scheme has adhered to appropriate procedures regarding carbon management and greenhouse gas (GHG) assessments. These procedures have consistently reflected the application of evolving relevant standards, best practice guidance, and legislation. However, this did not conclude in a carbon neutral dual carriageway.These procedures included the assessment and reporting of climate related impacts within the environmental documentation for public consultation, and the identification of proposed mitigation measures to reduce these impacts. However, based upon the size and nature of this scheme, it is expected that both the construction and operational phases will generate emissions, as reported within the GHG assessments prepared by the Department.  To mitigate the impacts further to achieve full 'carbon neutrality', through balancing the emissions from the construction, operation and use of the road by removing a corresponding amount of GHGs from the atmosphere - would require extensive mitigation such as vesting very significant areas of additional land for planting. Such measures would be disproportionate when compared to the land required to construct the A5WTC and would neither be practical nor reasonable to implement.Section 7.3 of the Departmental Statement, Carbon Management Process has been developed in line with PAS 2080: 2023 (the industry standard for managing whole life carbon in buildings and infrastructure) which sets out the requirement of the contractors, designer and asset owner with the aim of achieving carbon reduction. This process aims to minimise carbon associated with the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) quantities; and (ii) locations of sewage discharge into waterways in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water interprets the term sewage discharges to mean wastewater discharges. Wastewater discharges are generally considered under 2 categories: (a) discharges that occur on a continuous basis from a Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) known as final effluent discharges. This is wastewater which has been treated to a standard that is considered safe to return to the environment on continuous basis; and (b) discharges that occur on an intermittent basis from storm overflows when dilute wastewater is discharged direct to the environment during periods of heavy rainfall. (i) (a) 	NI Water collects data on daily volume of treated discharges at some larger WwTWs however, not all WwTWs have instrumentation to measure volumes discharged to the environment. As part of Wastewater Regulation Reform NI Water is currently working with NIEA to augment and improve the collection of flow data, much of which will be implemented in the next Price Control period (PC28).NI Water has fully verified and assured flow measurement systems at approximately 80 larger WwTWs with data for the 2024 period. The total volume of treated and discharged effluent from these sites was approximately 111 million m3 (01 Jan 24 – 31 Dec 24). In 2024, NI Water estimated that it treated and discharged approximately 132 - 162 million m3 of effluent.Unfortunately, NI Water is unable to provide detailed data for metered WwTWs final effluent flows for the last 3 years (2022, 2023, 2024) in the time permitted to answer this question. (b)	NI Water operates approximately 2,430 number of storm overflows across Northern Ireland. To monitor and record spills, Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) are being installed across NI Water’s wastewater network over a multi-year investment programme. These measure the times when a spill is occurring, but do not measure volumes, consequently NI Water does not have volumetric quantities for storm overflows.Given the large number of storm overflows across the north, spreadsheets containing spill data have been deposited in the Assembly library.  	(ii)    Given the large number of sewage discharge points across the north, spreadsheets setting out these locations have been deposited in the Assembly library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com .  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to provide active travel routes in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: Delivery of future active travel routes within our towns and cities, including Armagh and Newry, will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. Public consultation on the draft plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are now considering the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration shortly.  I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan by the end of the year.In addition to this, my Department is in the process of appointing consultants to progress the design of a number of major active travel projects.  One of these projects is the Newry Inner City Greenway, which aims to connect the Newry Canal Towpath to the Carlingford Lough Greenway.  Design is also currently ongoing on proposals to complete the active travel corridor from Damolly roundabout to Camlough Road Roundabout on Carbane Way and Craigmore Way, Newry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the benefits of restoring the corbeling on the roof of the prehistoric court cairn on the summit of Slieve Gullion, County Armagh.
answer_text: The original stone corbeling over the main chamber collapsed in antiquity and is no longer extant. The Department is currently assessing options for the repair or replacement of the modern roof covering.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of school funding in Newry and Armagh in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Details of the funding for schools in the Newry and Armagh Assembly Constituency for each of the last five years has been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department has provided to (i) apple growers; and (ii) associated agri-businesses, in County Armagh, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department for Economy, through Invest NI, does not provide support to apple growers. This activity is primary agricultural production which is the preserve of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. Invest NI operates within parameters under State aid European Commission rules, with its powers set out as per the 1982 Industrial Development (NI) Order in terms of assisting industry or industrial undertaking. In relation to agri-food and drink, my Department, through Invest NI, therefore works with businesses operating post-farmgate. In looking at support to associated agri-businesses in County Armagh in each of the last five years, taking this to mean businesses engaged in fruit processing and cider production in County Armagh.  My Department, through Invest NI provided total support of £1.81m to such businesses in County Armagh. Broken down, this equates to £420k in 2020/21, £210k in 2021/22, £30k in 2022/23, £930k in 2023/24, and £230k in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail who is the commissioning body for health services within his Department.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH), is the commissioning body for health and social care services across the HSC. The SPPG consists of five directorates; Primary Care, Hospital Care and Integrated Care System (ICS), Community Care, Finance and Corporate Governance and Performance, Safety and Service Improvement. Each directorate plays a significant role in overseeing service delivery and translating health strategy into action.The SPPG works closely with other key stakeholders such as the Public Health Agency, Primary Care, the six Health and Social Care Trusts and a range of organisations including the Business Services Organisation (BSO), The Regulation And Quality Improvement Agency (RQIA), Patient Client Council (PCC) etc along with various Voluntary and Community groups. Details of the work SPPG leads on can be found at https://online.hscni.net/

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the British Government's decision to officially recognise a Palestinian State.
answer_text: There is no agreed position on this matter.
