question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when traffic calming measures will be delivered in Orpen Park, Finaghy.
answer_text: I can advise the member that my officials are considering options for changing the current traffic calming measures within Orpen Park, Finaghy. As such, I am unable to provide a timescale at present as to when adjustments if any will be carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the cancellation of rural service users existing care packages with two weeks’ notice, in relation to the duty of care required by Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department has been in contact with the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in relation to your query.  The Trust have had a number of ‘hand backs’ from independent domiciliary care providers in their geographical area.  Often, this is because the provider cannot sustain a run/rota within a specific area.  The reasons for this include not having enough staff or not having enough service users in that area to make the run/rota an economically viable option for the provider.  The Trust have given assurance that when a package of care is cancelled or ‘handed back’ by a provider, they will work closely with all of those impacted to seek an alternative service provider as soon as possible, to meet the Service Users’ needs.  If an alternative Provider cannot be secured the key worker will discuss a range of options with the Service User and their family. This will include reviewing the potential of family support and/or access to a direct payment. If these options are not suitable or declined and the service user cannot manage safely at home, a temporary placement will be offered and sourced, until a suitable homecare package is secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's phased review of taxi policy and legislation.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline whether her budget allocation for roads maintenance, is allocated by division or by need.
answer_text: In distributing the resources available for roads maintenance, allocations relating to the areas covered by my Department’s four Roads Divisions are made on the basis of need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity (i.e. resurfacing, surface dressing etc.).  These take into consideration criteria such as road length, structural condition, traffic and population (for footway resurfacing).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether a limited service is still in place for roads maintenance; and (ii) for her assessment of whether a return to full service could be funded by the compensation given for road defect claims.
answer_text: While there has been an upward trend in total claims expenditure across all claim types in recent years, the figure currently paid out in compensation for road defect claims falls far short of the estimate needed to return to a full service, which does not address the deterioration of the network that has occurred as a result of years of sustained underfunding as a result of austerity by the British Government.£8m was spent on claims in 2024/25, whereas it is estimated that in the region of an additional £20m above the average annual investment provided to deliver the reduced Limited Service that has been in place since 2015 would be required to return to a full service.There is no doubt that historical under-investment in road maintenance is a key contributing factor to increasing roads related vehicle damage claim numbers. However, the number of roads related claims can also increase due to external factors beyond our control including severe weather conditions; increased traffic using the road network; and greater public awareness of the deterioration of our roads and a citizen’s right to submit claims. Additionally, I have allocated increased funding for essential maintenance in 2024-25 to deliver, for example, an enhanced programme of road marking renewal and sign maintenance.I have set out my Department’s needs as part of future budget exercises and will continue to engage with my Executive colleagues on the investment required in our infrastructure to maximise benefit to our communities and generate economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the IT outage in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on 17 September 2025, (i) to detail the contingency plan that was in place for such an event taking place; (ii) whether this was implemented; (iii) how will contingency plans be evolved in light of the experience of the Health and Social Care Trust and patients following this event; and (iv) for his assessment of whether efficiencies or savings led to the failure of IT infrastructure.
answer_text: My written statement of Friday 19 September addresses a number of the concerns raised, however it may be helpful to emphasise the following:-Rescheduling postponed appointmentsSince the systems have been restored, teams of staff have been working at pace to ensure approximately 1600 patients impacted by the IT Outage will have their cases prioritised and rescheduled at the earliest opportunity.The Trust has a full list of all patients/ service users whose appointment or procedure was cancelled due to the IT Major Incident. The Trust is currently rebooking these patients based on clinical priority.  A significant number of patients have already been rebooked.CommunicationSignificant IT issues caused widespread disruption to Southern Trust services last week with most planned surgery and out-patient hospital appointments being cancelled on 17 and 18 September. Despite the IT challenges, the Trust issued regular statements over both days to keep the public/media/elected representatives and staff updated on the    developing situation. The Trust also responded to significant media interest from local and regional outlets. The Trust’s Medical Director Dr Stephen Austin, facilitated numerous interviews including live pieces with BBC Good Morning Ulster and BBC Nolan Show as well as television interviews. A telephone helpline was set up (18 – 20 Sep) for patients whose appointments or surgery has been postponed as a result of the IT issues. There were 78 calls received during this period. In addition to frequent social media updates across all Trust channels, a dedicated section was created on the Trust’s website. https://southerntrust.hscni.net/trust-statement/All updates were shared with media and local elected representatives. Individual briefings were provided to those who requested them.A dedicated internal staff webpage was also created to house all the latest internal and external communications to keep staff up to date throughout the incident. Internal digital channels / alerts were setup to direct staff to this online resource for further information. Next StepsThe Trust has established a Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Working Group, chaired by the Director of Planning, Performance and Informatics. The SHSCT provider of network support (Eircom) will attend these meetings and will provide the technical assessment of the root cause of the IT Outage.  Independent members from the BSO, DHCNI and two of the other HSC Trusts have been confirmed as members of this group and will provide independent challenge to the approach to the RCA and the evidence provided. The first meeting of this group took place on Friday 19 September and regular meetings continued throughout w/c 22 September and 29 September. This work will include assessment of any mitigating actions that can be taken to avoid a future similar event.In tandem, the SHSCT is setting up an Incident Review group, with an independent chair. DHCNI have been approached and agreed to identify some option

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the IT Outage in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on 17 September 2025; (ii) for his assessment of the impact of the outage on patients' health and wellbeing; and (iii) how his Department is moving to reassure patients and staff that it will not happen again.
answer_text: In accordance with my Written Ministerial Statement of 19 September, I reiterate that Southern Health and Social Care Trust experienced an operational IT outage on the morning of 17 September. The issue was isolated to the Southern Trust but impacted across their sites and there is no suggestion that this was a cyber related matter. The outage led to a loss of the computer network via 2 data centres which prevented access to key systems across the Trust. The resultant impact necessitated the postponement of elective activity on 17 and 18 September and the diversion of ambulances to other Trust emergency departments throughout 17 September.  Community Services were impacted to a lesser extent.Both impacted data centres were restored by 5pm on 17 September, providing access in the first instance to priority systems relating to labs, EDs, ICU and night medicines, which allowed both Daisy Hill and Craigavon Area Hospitals to recommence receipt of ambulance arrivals from 9pm and 10.30pm respectively.  The Trust are working at pace to rebook postponed appointments and procedures as soon as possible and my Department will support this prompt rebooking via waiting list funding if required. Initial unverified data indicates approximately 1,600 patients were impacted by postponements.  It should be noted that there were no postponements beyond the 17 and 18 September and Adult Community Clinics were not impacted during the major incident timeframe.  The major incident was formally stood down the morning of 18 September 2025, however recovery efforts will continue for some time. As with any major incident, the HSC has a process to understand the cause and impact, and to identify any learning or rectifications to prevent a recurrence.  To this end, I can confirm that a root cause analysis of the reasons for the outage is underway.  My Department will oversee the recovery and learning processes associated with this major incident.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23457/22-26, to detail progress on the business case for the provision of CAR-T therapy in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Cross specialty engagements are ongoing consistent with patient’s needs.The delivery of CAR-T locally is dependent on the capital programme for the new haematology ward proceeding. An updated Outline Business Case for the new haematology ward was submitted to the Department of Health on the 21 August 2025. Departmental officials have reviewed the business case and have requested clarification on some areas from the Trust.  Delivery of CAR-T services in the new ward will require additional revenue service development costs to increase the capacity required and this will be taken forward by the DoH and the Trust in advance of the completion of the ward in line with policy direction as per established commissioning processes and available funding at the time.A clinical lead has been appointed.The delivery of CAR-T locally is entirely dependent on the capital programme for the new haematology ward proceeding. Relevant stakeholder engagement to deliver CAR-T Therapy will be integral to project plan, alongside additional revenue costs which will require funded via SPPG.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any funding allocated or committed to support regeneration and public realm improvement projects in Newry and Armagh city centres in each of the last five years; and (ii) how future funding will be prioritised under any revised Urban Regeneration and Community Development Framework.
answer_text: The total funding allocated or committed to support regeneration and public realm improvements Newry and Armagh in the previous five years is set out in the table below:Apr 20 – Mar 21Apr 21 – Mar 22Apr 22 – Mar 23Apr 23 – Mar 24Apr 24 – Mar 25Newry£436,817£225,453£5,219,540*£16,225,000*£195,000Armagh£20,000£360,000*This includes funding allocated from Belfast Region City Deal and the Complementary Fund.The table does not include funding directed toward district-wide schemes, as this funding is recorded against the council district rather than a town/city.My Department works with stakeholders and, in particular, local Councils to develop regeneration schemes across NI. Decisions on funding for individual projects are taken within the context of my Department’s strategic priorities and policies as well as budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the capital funding allocated for the refurbishment or replacement of school buildings in the Newry and Armagh constituency in each of the last five years; and (ii) how this compares to other constituencies.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children in the Newry and Armagh constituency currently awaiting statutory assessments for special educational needs; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to reduce the backlog in Education Authority processing times.
answer_text: (i)  The Education Authority (EA) has advised there are currently 114 children undergoing statutory assessment – this does not mean that they will all receive a statement.(ii) The EA have undertaken a review of their processes, backfilled vacant posts and are currently developing their digital system to go live in the second term of this academic year.  Delays often arise because of the ongoing issues with availability of school places which is a priority for the EA and the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average (a) Category 1; and (b) Category 2 ambulance response times in rural parts of the Newry and Armagh constituency in the last 12 months; and (ii) any steps being taken to improve response times in these areas.
answer_text: Ambulance call categorisation for emergency calls is standardised across the UK.  The table below shows Category 1 and 2 response times in Newry and Armagh Areas during the last 12 months. YearMonthCategory of CallCategory 1Category 22024August0:18:090:52:20September0:14:061:12:55October0:17:551:04:08November0:16:531:06:32December0:16:121:21:352025January0:13:171:15:51February0:17:410:49:32March0:17:401:16:08April0:14:191:01:23May0:15:301:14:59June0:17:211:16:37July0:15:181:22:02August0:15:341:31:18Grand Total0:16:061:11:08I recognise that ambulance response times remain a significant challenge for the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS). These difficulties are driven by a range of factors, including delays in hospital handovers and the impact of on-going industrial action.The importance of timely and appropriate ambulance responses was recognised in the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine. I am particularly concerned about the impact of ambulance handover delays at emergency departments (EDs), which directly affect NIAS’s ability to respond to emergency calls in the community. Improving handover times is therefore a key priority for my Department.Earlier this year, Departmental officials, alongside clinicians from Trusts and NIAS, visited a hospital in London to learn from their successful approach to releasing ambulances more quickly. Following this visit, I asked Trusts and NIAS to adopt a similar model.At a regional workshop in August 2025, the Permanent Secretary, supported by the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, endorsed the implementation of regional ambulance handover guidance. This guidance sets out a clear ambition to eliminate handovers exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025, with the longer-term goal of achieving the 15-minute handover standard.This is a system-wide challenge, and I am committed to leading the co-ordinated action required to deliver meaningful and sustained improvements.In addition, my Department is working with NIAS to ensure sufficient ambulance and staffing capacity to meet Category 1 and Category 2 response time standards. This includes the development of a comprehensive 10-year workforce and financial plan to support long-term service resilience and patient care.My Department is working closely with NIAS to address these issues through a number of targeted initiatives. NIAS is actively increasing its front-line workforce and has launched an ambitious recruitment programme. In parallel, the service continues to develop alternative care pathways, enabling more patients to be treated in the community and reducing reliance on EDs.In 2025, NIAS introduced the first tier of the Advanced Paramedics Urgent Care model. This important development enhances the paramedic workforce, allowing a higher level of care to be delivered directly to patients and supporting treatment in community settings.Specifically, in relation to rural parts of the Newry and Armagh constituency, I

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made in respect of access to community sports and leisure facilities in rural areas of Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI’s ‘Infrastructure Review – Supply Audit’ has reviewed Northern Ireland’s existing supply of sports facilities, including an assessment of the distribution of sports facilities across rural and urban areas.  This included rural areas of Newry and Armagh via information shared from the respective Local Councils.Some of the high-level findings from the review across all areas include:Urban areas tend to benefit from higher facility density than rural areas.Not all urban settlements will have the same access to sports facilities, highlighting regional disparity amongst urban areas.With a higher concentration of the population living in urban areas the pressure on sports facilities is high.Rural areas have significantly fewer facilities and much less variety in the type of facility that is easily accessible.Rural sports facility provision is dominated by playing pitches and small sports halls.Across settlement bands there is a range of facilities available, but not all settlements within a band will have equal access to the same facilities.There is a need to maximise facilities located within the school estate, particularly in rural communities, where no public facility exists.Development of sports facilities in rural and urban areas should be supported to maximise their use across as many sports as possible.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of (a) housing development applications; and (b) planning approvals in the Newry and Armagh constituency which are currently stalled due to a lack of wastewater treatment capacity; and (ii) what short-term and medium-term interventions are being planned by NI Water or her Department to unlock development in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	(a)   There are currently thirty housing development applications in the Newry and         Armagh constituency for which wastewater connections were not available; and (b)	There are currently thirteen approved planning applications in the Newry and Armagh constituency for which a wastewater connection was not available.However, NI Water is now assessing those housing development and approved planning applications, which are in the Newry catchment, to determine if the £26m investment in the Newry catchment will now permit a connection.(ii)	NI Water has recently set-out proposals to unlock wastewater capacity within the Newry catchment through an innovative and collaborative approach, which has the potential to permit connection of up to 400 homes within the Newry catchment through a £26m investment. This planned work will not remove all the wastewater capacity constraints but it will permit NI Water to reopen the Newry wastewater catchment and to achieve connections, that it had previously advised were not possible. However, developer funded solutions may also still be required and NI Water is now undertaking a review and assessment of all wastewater impact assessment applications previously received for the Newry catchment and has advised that, in most cases, it expects the reassessment will permit a wastewater connection albeit with some level of developer intervention. NI Water is also examining if similar methods can be used to unlock capacity in other areas, including Armagh, where getting a wastewater connection is an issue.In addition, I will continue to develop the three-pronged approach that is already in place.  Firstly, I will work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, my  Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill that was recently introduced in the Assembly, will make provision for future regulations on sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, my Department has consulted on options to introduce developer contributions to help fund wastewater infrastructure improvements. That consultation has now closed and responses are currently being considered, with a view to publishing a Consultation Responses Report in the coming weeks.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on (i) the Murlough Road, Ballycastle; and (ii) the road leading to the Bothy National Trust, over the last 10 years.
answer_text: Minor resurfacing work was carried out on the Murlough Road, Ballycastle in 2022, by my Department at a cost of £13,500.Additionally, routine maintenance activities have also been carried out on the Murlough Road, including on the section of adopted road network leading to the National Trust Bothy, over the last 10 years.  These works include bitmac patching, gully emptying and the replenishment of salt piles. However, the costs for activities have not been broken down as they were carried out by my Department’s internal contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what (i) dedicated (a) financial; and (b) staffing, resources have been allocated within her Department, to progress the restoration of the Armagh to Portadown rail link; and (ii) whether she will publish a timeline for advancing the project to detailed design and delivery phases.
answer_text: I have ring-fenced £1m in Translink’s 202-26 capital budget to progress projects identified in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review including the Portadown to Armagh rail link. Translink has submitted the feasibility study into the Portadown to Armagh rail link to my Department and I expect to publish the outcomes later in the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to the Written Ministerial Statement made by his predecessor on 17 February 2021, for an update on the Connect2 Hub planned for Newry which was scheduled to be opened in 2023.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 31180/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed greenway project, linking the Newry Canal towpath to the Carlingford Lough Greenway, including (i) the expected completion date of the design consultancy phase; (ii) whether capital funding has been secured for full delivery; and (iii) her Department's plans to engage with the local community and Newry Mourne and Down District Council on these proposals.
answer_text: The Newry Inner City Greenway is one of the six active travel signature schemes being taken forward by my department.  Officials are currently in the process of commissioning consultants to commence design work on the scheme.  The design and planning phases are expected to take approximately 3 years to complete.In this financial year, funding has been allocated to allow the design phase to commence. Future funding allocations will be subject to the outcome of future Budget settlements, public expenditure processes and the successful completion of each phase of the design process.Engagement will be a key part of the design and planning phases and engagement with stakeholders including Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all enforcement notices, that her Department has served to political parties for the erection of unauthorised billboards, over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has not issued any enforcement notices to political parties regarding unauthorised billboards in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16553/22-27, for an update on the enforcement of the removal of the Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry.
answer_text: As stated in my predecessor’s response to AQW 16553/22-27, officials have contacted those responsible for erecting the billboard and have asked them to remove it, no further action has been taken by my Department at this time.My Department will take action where unauthorised attachments to its property are likely to have an immediate impact on road safety (e.g. obscure a sightline, obstruct the passage of vehicles or pedestrians, or compromise the structural stability of a street lighting column or traffic sign).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have made to the Department for Communities regarding the allocation of stadia funding under the Northern Ireland Football Fund for clubs in the North West.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether a bid has been made to the Shared Island Fund in collaboration with the Department of Education and Youth, for continuation of the Scoil Spreaghta scheme.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged in constructive dialogue with representatives from Gael Linn to explore viable opportunities for securing funding to support the continuation of the Scoil Spreagtha scheme. These discussions remain ongoing. All potential funding avenues continue to be explored in relation to this proposal, however, I understand that the Shared Island Fund is not a bid process.  Therefore, my Department has not submitted a direct application to the Shared Island Fund, nor has it done so in collaboration with the Department of Education and Youth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of how daily physical activity can be embedded into the education system.
answer_text: My department recommends that schools should provide children and young people with at least two hours of good quality curricular Physical Education (PE) each week which is designed to develop physically literate individuals who have the knowledge, skills and confidence to enjoy a lifetime of healthful physical activity. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) supports this and provides guidance and resources to support inclusive PE. These resources place an emphasis on physical literacy, promoting not only physical competence but also confidence and motivation to participate in physical activity throughout life.  The Education Authority, in collaboration with my department and CCEA, has recently produced resources for teachers, leaders & PE coordinators in primary schools which focus on enhancing the delivery of PE delivery in primary schools.Furthermore, the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, published in June 2025, includes recommendations for a new curriculum framework. I have established a Curriculum Taskforce, to be supported by subject working groups, to develop content and capabilities across all areas of learning. This presents an opportunity to strengthen the role of physical activity in daily school life and ensure it is embedded more consistently across the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) a list of the football clubs that applied to the Northern Ireland Football Fund and were unsuccessful; and (ii) the criteria used to make these decisions.
answer_text: A total of 37 applications from 38 clubs were received for the Northern Ireland Football Fund Performance Programme. A prioritised cohort of 20 applicants will now be taken forward to the next stage of the programme. My intention is that those applicants not in the prioritised cohort will be brought forward to a further stage of the Programme if more funding is made available. The 17 applicants not in the prioritised cohort are: Annagh United FCArds FCBallyclare Comrades FCCoagh United FCColeraine FCCrusaders FCDerry FC Dollingstown FCDundela FCInstitute FC Knockbreda FCLimavady FC Linfield Women’s FCNewington FC Portadown FC Portstewart FC Warrenpoint FCThe criteria to be met for each application and how applications would be scored are set out in the guidance notes and the scoring matrix  on the Department for Communities website The Northern Ireland Football Fund Performance Programme documents | Department for Communities.  Applications were scored by Departmental officials and then moderated internally and externally to ensure accuracy and consistency. A merit order of applicants was then produced for each tier. I had no role in the scoring process or in determining the merit order.  My role in this process was to decide how many of the eligible applications would be taken forward in merit order from each tier.  I did this based on a range of options presented by my officials and I opted to take forward a range of small, medium and large projects which I believed best ensured that budget could be spent in a way that would bring maximum benefits whilst avoiding any further erosion of its value.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 29775/22-27, to detail (i) the data that will be retrieved through these cameras; and (ii) whether the cameras will have facial recognition capabilities.
answer_text: The cameras are used by Traffic Control Operators to monitor real time traffic conditions to enable them to identify and manage traffic related incidents on the A1.The cameras do not have facial recognition capabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline when her Department last carried out a road safety assessment on Drumgullion Avenue, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.My Department has not received any recent requests for an assessment to be carried out on Drumgullion Avenue. Officials have however, reviewed the collision history from the latest available PSNI data for Drumgullion Avenue and have determined that this road has a good safety record.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what body is the regulator for the discharge of untreated sewage into watercourses.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency is the environmental regulator in the North and is responsible for the regulation of discharges to the water environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the reinstatement of the railway line between Portadown and Armagh, following the report on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: Translink has submitted the Portadown to Armagh feasibility study to my Department. This is currently under consideration and I expect to publish the outcomes later in the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department's policies on the erection of illegal roadside signage.
answer_text: The illegal erection of advertising signs within the boundaries of the public road is an offence under Article 87 of The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993. This Article gives the Department discretionary power to either seek to have the advertisement removed, or to remove it ourselves if considered necessaryMy Department will take action where unauthorised attachments to its property are likely to have an immediate impact on road safety (e.g. obscure a sightline, obstruct the passage of vehicles or pedestrians, or compromise the structural stability of a street lighting column or traffic sign).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any priorities contained within the Programme for Government relating to children; and (ii) an update on progress on each of these priorities.
answer_text: Many policies within the Programme will lead to improved outcomes for all, including children and young people, and will serve to promote and protect children’s rights.These impacts are fully set out in the Children’s Rights Impact Assessment for the Programme for Government which can be found on the NI Executive website https://www.northernireland.gov.uk/publications/programme-government-2024-2027-our-plan-doing-what-matters-most-documents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding support being provided to the Franciscan Missionary Sisters.
answer_text: My Department provides no funding support to the Franciscan Missionary Sisters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with a Statement of Special Educational Needs who remain without an appropriate school placement, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: As of the 5 September 2025, no children with a statement of SEN remained unplaced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any health and safety concerns that have been raised in relation to Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: Translink takes a proactive approach to identifying and managing health and safety concerns on a continuous basis which entails a range of methods to collate any emerging or present risks. Items raised to date include. The ongoing interface with construction activities has led to temporary access and diversions on roads and footpaths which are being monitored daily by its contractor. This involves regular health and safety inspections undertaken and the arrangements monitored by Translink. Unauthorised vehicle movements on or around the footprint of Belfast Grand Central Station have required engagement with my Department, including Roads and Traffic Enforcement to mitigate the risk in the area. As per industry norms, slips, trips and falls contribute to items raised within the station and each one is recorded, investigated and actioned as required. Public behaviour is a continued focus ensuring that Translink staff are supported where there are assaults, threats of assault or anti-social behaviour within the Station. Due to increased train service provision and doubling of rail platforms, air quality is being monitored continuously and is within the required limits. Translink take all health and safety risks seriously and whilst it does not currently have any significant concerns it continues to focus on areas of known risk. This is collated within Translink’s 3rd Party Accredited and Authorised Safety Management System, its health and safety risk register and its task risk assessments.  My Department’s Rail Safety Branch acts as National Safety Authority for the NI rail network which includes responsibilities for regulating the safe operation of the railway. This responsibility overlaps with HSE NI and their responsibilities for workplace health and safety, and as such there is a memorandum of understanding with HSE NI to ensure that relevant information relating to health and safety is shared between the organisations.It should be noted that Rail Safety officials monitor NIR compliance with rail safety and technical standards. Through the monitoring activity which includes reviewing quarterly statistics on a range of indicators there are no discernible patterns of incidents at Grand Central Station.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the Cervical Screening Review in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, how screener underperformance will be (i) monitored; and (ii) promptly rectified.
answer_text: Since the Cervical Cytology Review was undertaken, HPV testing has been introduced into the NI Cervical Screening Programme as the ‘first test/primary test,’ whereby all cervical smear tests are now tested for the presence of high-risk infection. The HPV test is an automated test and is a more accurate test than cytology alone. As a result of the introduction of HPV testing, the number of cytology assessments has drastically reduced and there is now one centralised provider of laboratory services for the NI Cervical Screening Programme based in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.Individual screeners may have episodes where their performance statistics fall below the expected threshold. This is not unexpected, and the laboratory has quality control measures in place to detect and address this. The performance of all screeners is actively monitored and managed by the Belfast Trust through regular checks and reviews; and follows the Trust’s procedure for performance management. Belfast Trust has further advised my department that it participates in External Quality Assurance (EQA) schemes, such as QCMD HPV EQA and the UKNEQA for molecular detection of HPV; as well as interlab comparisons with the Scottish HPV reference Lab interlab comparison scheme (SHPVRL).The Public Health Agency reviews all cervical laboratory data on an annual basis (in line with national practice). This includes screener performance data using a standard report extracted from the Cyres reporting system. The PHA is now tracking trend data for individual screeners which will be considered as part of this annual review process. All deviations from standards/key indicator thresholds are discussed with the laboratory team in detail and assurance sought that Trust procedures (as outlined above) are being followed. If the PHA have any ongoing concerns about performance or management of underperformance, these would be escalated via normal governance pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has undertaken any review of staff morale in DfI Roads.
answer_text: Staff in my department contributed to the most recent NICS People Survey for 2025, the results of which have just been released.  With almost 13,000 people taking part, the response rate was 47% which was an increase of 3% from the previous survey.  The key Employee Engagement Index also improved, reaching 56%.A full breakdown for each department will be provided in due course, and my officials will take time to review the particular data for my department in more detail, including any analysis of staff morale indicators, to determine if any targeted improvement actions can be implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the rationale as to why pupils who chose to attend the nearest secondary school to them in a particular category are not eligible for home to school transport, as they did not chose to attend a geographically closer secondary school in a different category.
answer_text: It is for the Education Authority (EA) to assess eligibility for transport assistance in line with my Department’s Home to School Transport Policy. Under the policy, eligibility is determined based on two criteria – ‘distance’ and ‘suitable school’. Post-primary pupils may qualify if they live over three miles from their school and have formally applied for and been unsuccessful at gaining a place in all schools of the same category within three miles of home. Suitable school is defined in the transport policy as a grant-aided school in any of the following categories: catholic maintained, controlled or other voluntary, denominational grammar, non-denominational grammar, integrated and Irish medium. Parents are not obliged to send their child to the nearest school in category; however, transport assistance cannot be provided if they choose a school beyond the qualifying distance, bypassing a suitable school in the same category within that distance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting list time for mental health services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Current waiting times for children and adult mental health services are unacceptable across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including higher prevalence rates in NI linked to deprivation and legacy issues, covid, increased levels of acuity and funding that falls short of objectively assessed need. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.All Trusts have migrated to the new digital patient record system, Encompass. As a result of this transition, Health and Social Care Trusts are at various stages in the process of validating and reconciling their data. Due to this process, some Trusts have been unable to confirm and validate with confidence their current waiting list data, making regional comparisons difficult.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT)Belfast Health and Social Care Trust provides Community CAMHS for both Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust. Details regarding average waiting list times are outlined below.Child & Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS)ServiceAverage Wait TimeStep 25 weeksStep 312 weeksDrug and Alcohol Misuse5 weeksEating Disorders2 weeksFamily Trauma (Regional)23 weeksKnowing our Identity (Regional)80 weeksYouth Justice CAMHS4 weeksBelfast Health and Social Care Trust are continuing to validate and reconcile their data following the migration to the new digital patient record, Encompass.  The average wait time for a routine new patient adult mental health assessment cannot be confirmed due to the current low confidence in data validation and therefore may not be a true reflectionNorthern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT)The Northern Health and Social Care Trust are in the process of continuing to validate and reconcile data following the migration to Encompass. The validation process relating to the average wait time for a routine mental health assessment remains at low confidence and therefore cannot be confirmed due to the ongoing data validation.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT)As outlined, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust provides CAMHS services for the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust. The average waiting list times for CAMHS services are therefore detailed above.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust are continuing to validate and reconcile their data following migration to the new digital patient record, Encompass. The average waiting list time for adult mental health, routine new patient mental health assessment, cannot be confirmed due to the current low confidence in data validation and therefore may not be a true reflection.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT)Adult Mental Health ServicesThe average waiting time for new patients to Adult Community Mental Health Services, including Dementia within the SHSCT as of April 2025 was 78 daysChild & Adolescent Mental Health Service - CAMHSService0-3 Weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeksTotalCAMHS Eating Disorde

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last inspection of Cargie Road, Cullyhanna, was undertaken; and (ii) what subsequent repairs were completed.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.I am advised that an inspection of Cargie Road was carried out on 5th September 2025 when one actionable defect which met the current limited service intervention criteria was located and subsequently repaired.  Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average time spent on the housing waiting list, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 30594/22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of the number of transfer requests submitted to the Housing Executive by tenants citing anti-social behaviour by neighbours as a reason, in each constituency for the last five years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive and she has responded as follows.“The specified application/transfer reason is not a reportable category in the Housing Executive’s Housing Management System (HMS).”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the preference criteria for Tranche 3 of the 20mph speed limit zones outside schools 2025-26 programme.
answer_text: The criteria used to select the additional 40 schools in Tranche 3 of the part-time 20 mph speed limit initiative is publicly available to view on my Department’s website at: DEM 194/25 - Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools - Tranche 3.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Sands Lost in the System campaign briefing in July 2025; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department will be taking as a result.
answer_text: (i) The leading stillbirth and neonatal death charity (SANDs UK), published its Lost in the System campaign briefing in July 2025. The report highlights significant gaps in mental health and bereavement support for parents following pregnancy or baby loss across the UK, including Northern Ireland.  The report calls for national standards and expanded perinatal mental health services to ensure equitable access.(ii) The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS UK, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis.  Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.You may also be aware that the Department of Finance is introducing ‘The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill’ in the Assembly which includes provision for the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for Northern Ireland.  This initiative, currently under public consultation, would enable a pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, to be recognised.  Departmental officials will continue to work closely with the Department of Finance, around the consultation process and development of the scheme. The Department of Health Northern Ireland and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-makin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department will ensure that lessons are learned from this year's admissions difficulties at St Paul's High School, Bessbrook, in order to inform the process in 2026-27 and beyond.
answer_text: St Paul’s High School (HS) was oversubscribed with applications for September 2025 admission, as were many schools across Northern Ireland.  My department allocated the school additional places and sufficient capacity remained available in the area to ensure every child could secure a school place in their preferred school sector.In respect of the admission of children with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN), the Education Authority (EA) has advised that St Paul’s HS has both specialist provision learning and social communication classes.  For the last number of years there has been an annual challenge to meet the high demand for places at the school, which often exceeds capacity.  The EA’s SEN Capacity Team liaised with the school last year with a view to expanding the existing provision to help address the placement need within the area. The EA’s Operations and Estates Directorate is progressing an assessment of the site with a view to providing new accommodation for the proposed expansion.  In addition to this, departmental officials and EA officers met with the school on 12 September.  Pupils with a statement of SEN are admitted outside of Open Enrolment arrangements and are placed in line with criteria outlined in legislation i.e.: If a parent chooses a school for their child, the EA must name that school in the child’s statement unless:(a) The school is unsuitable to the child’s age, ability or aptitude or to his special educational needs, or(b) The attendance of the child at the school would be incompatible with the provision of efficient education for the children with whom he would be educated or the efficient use of resources.The EA continues to work with the school to ensure appropriate resources are allocated to meet the needs of pupils with Statement of SEN across both mainstream and specialist provisions.  The EA acknowledges the need for additional specialist provision in the surrounding area and continues to liaise with school principals to increase capacity for the 2026/27 academic year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail what measures they are taking to enshrine and protect the rights of the child.
answer_text: Alongside our commitment to deliver and embed Section 75 duties, where policies are likely to have a particular impact on children and young people, the Department considers whether a Child Rights Impact Assessment (CRIA) is appropriate.  The CRIA aims to embed children’s rights considerations at the outset of developing legislation, strategies and policies. Many policies within the Programme for Government will lead to improved outcomes for all, including children and young people, and will serve to promote and protect children’s rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide her assessment of (i) the risk posed to children’s safety at Cloughoge Primary School, Newry, as a consequence of the 40mph speed limit at the school; and (ii) the merits of reviewing the speed limit to enhance pupil and pedestrian safety.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users particularly vulnerable road user groups, including pedestrians.My officials have previously assessed safety measures including the speed limit on Chancellors Road and the safety measures in relation to Cloughoge (St Moninna’s) Primary School, providing school warning signs and road markings as a result.  However, due to planned future housing development at this location, my officials will undertake a further review on this road which will include the speed limit and a traffic calming assessment.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail all Programme for Government commitments made in respect of the rights of children in this mandate.
answer_text: These impacts are fully set out in the Children’s Rights Impact Assessment for the Programme for Government which can be found on the NI Executive website:https://www.northernireland.gov.uk/publications/programme-government-2024-2027-our-plan-doing-what-matters-most-documents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the implementation of Project Gigabit.
answer_text: Project Gigabit is still in the procurement phase as my officials, along with Building Digital UK (BDUK) are assessing new commercial rollout plans that became available over the summer. That assessment is due to complete shortly, allowing the procurement to complete and a contract awarded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on plans to improve road safety and traffic calming measures at Cloughoge Primary School, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users particularly vulnerable road user groups, including pedestrians.My officials have previously assessed safety measures including the speed limit on Chancellors Road and the safety measures in relation to Cloughoge (St Moninna’s) Primary School, providing school warning signs and road markings as a result.  However, due to planned future housing development at this location, my officials will undertake a further review on this road which will include the speed limit and a traffic calming assessment.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) how his Department is financially supporting apple growers in County Armagh; and (ii) the financial support on offer for the upcoming orchard harvest and associated agri-businesses and events.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of the apple harvest to growers in County Armagh in terms of the positive impact that it delivers for the economy, the environment and biodiversity in the region.Land used for the commercial production of apples is eligible for financial support under my department’s Farm Sustainability Transition Payment (FSTP). It will continue to be eligible as the scheme transitions to the Farm Sustainability Payment within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) in 2026. On 1 September 2025, my department commenced 2025 FSTP payments to farm businesses. A total of £299,388 will be paid against land in County Armagh registered for use as commercial orchards. This includes payments to commercial apple growers. Since 2015, funding totalling £123,758 has been awarded for projects delivered through my department’s Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme (NIRFP) for promotional activities associated with the annual Food and Cider Weekend. This funding has supported a wide range of activities, all of which focus on celebrating the region’s PGI-status (Protected Geographical Indication) Armagh Bramley Apple and associated products.As part of this year’s Food and Cider Festival, I recently attended the ‘Apple Spirits Tasting Masterclass’ in Armagh at which I was pleased to learn more about the journey of the apple from the orchard to a range finished products. This year’s festival was awarded £14,100 from the NIRFP.My department is also working with the apple industry, through the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), in the delivery of industry training, knowledge and technology transfer to meet their needs. This support includes disseminating modelling reports to growers on the influence of environmental conditions on pest and disease risk. CAFRE’s Loughry Campus also helps the top-fruit industry by providing technical support, advice and training related to the processing and packaging of apples and apple products.Commercial apple growers who hold a Category 1 or 2 Farm Business ID can also avail of support through the Horticulture Pilot Scheme. The Scheme, which will be delivered as part of the SAP, comprises three pilot sub schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups pilot scheme, Growers’ Training and Support pilot scheme and Innovation Driver and Support pilot scheme. Public awareness sessions to publicise the scheme are ongoing and have included sessions with apple growers. Applications for the Sustainable Sector Growth groups and Growers’ Training and Support Scheme will open on the 31 October 2025.In addition, under SAP, my department is developing the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, which will provide financial support for equipment and technology to improve environmental performance and business efficiency. It is proposed that the scheme will be open to all primary production sectors, including apple growers. More information on this new scheme will be announced in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Newry Southern Relief Road Consultation launched in January 2025.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the uplift in train passenger numbers since the introduction of the hourly Enterprise service between Belfast and Dublin.
answer_text: Enterprise services have seen an increase of around 37% in passenger journeys in the period November 2024 to June 2025 when compared to the same period 12 months prior.  The percentage increase is not an Official Statistic but is based on Management Information received from Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of recent media reports of a toxic and unnerving culture in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, to outline (i) how this is being resolved; and (ii) for his assessment of whether similar cultures exist within other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that the overall culture of our healthcare system is welcoming to all working there. In relation to Belfast Trust, in order to gain an assessment of the extent of the reported cultural issues, I have convened an intervention team. Peter McBride, with expertise in workplace culture and behaviours has agreed to be part of this. I have asked him to form a view on how widespread the cultural and behavioural issues evident in the cardiac surgical unit, may be across the Belfast Trust. While this concern was raised in relation to one single service, it is prudent to consider if these cultural concerns extend beyond this service and the HSC as a whole. In respect of established practice, Trusts’ already complete regular Trustwide ‘Pulse’ cultural surveys, which are a considered part of ongoing governance oversight. However, given matters raised by the Review Team, consideration has been given to a wider cultural survey in surgical services across Northern Ireland, with a particular focus on theatres. Other assessment tools are being considered.Established Trade Union arrangements across all Trusts are considered the most appropriate vehicle for escalation of any matter. However, it is important to underline that matters can, and should, be raised to Ministerial level if not managed.There are whistleblowing arrangements and Employee Relations policies and procedures in place across all Trusts. My Department’s governance oversight arrangements will ask for reassurance that there are arrangements for Non-Executive oversight. In addition, I expect Trusts to ensure that staff are regularly made aware of local arrangements.I have been seeking assurances that any employee working within our healthcare system, who may have concerns about bullying or unacceptable behaviour, will be listened to and supported. Therefore, I will continue to work with key interests to ensure improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any investment in active travel infrastructure in the constituency of Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: Funding for dedicated Active Travel measures is allocated by division rather than by constituency. The Newry and Armagh constituency falls within Southern Division. Provisional outturn for 2024/25 shows that my department spent £1.9m on dedicated active travel in Southern Division, which included the reconstruction of the Black Paths from South Lake Leisure Centre to Drumgor Centre and commencement of the Moylinn Pedestrian and Cycle Bridge. The opening budget allocations for 2025-26 towards dedicated active travel in Southern Division is £1m, which will fund the completion of the Moylinn Pedestrian and Cycle Bridge and the ongoing reconstruction of the Black paths from Drumgor Centre to the Curlywurly Bridge. My department also carries out many other activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists which are captured under wider departmental active travel spend. Details of upcoming activities of this nature will be outlined in the annual Newry, Mourne and Down District Council (NMDDC) and Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council reports.In addition, my department issued a letter of offer to NMDDC for the Newcastle Active Travel Hub which included funding for 2024-25 of up to £0.3m and £0.3m in 2025-26. In recent years my department also issued a funding letter of offer to NMDDC for almost £1.4m to support delivery of the cross-border Carlingford Lough Greenway, which was opened recently.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the innovative wastewater approach that has unlocked economic growth in Newry.
answer_text: I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to unlocking our wastewater constraints.  Connecting 400 properties in Newry shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions – this lies at the heart of my three-pronged approach.The details are an operational matter for NI Water; however, my officials have sought the relevant information. In terms of the innovative wastewater approach in Newry, NI Water has advised my Department of an alternative solution to the full upgrade of Newry Wastewater Treatment Works by:Targeted maintenance on existing infrastructure;Removal of excess water from the sewerage system; and The development of partnerships with businesses to reduce industrial flow impacts in the form of excess trade effluent.The impact of this will lead to the reopening of Newry for development for potentially the next 10 years, accommodating all known developments and supporting economic growth while maintaining environmental compliance.I would refer you to NI Water for further information in relation to the operational delivery of this proposal. Any requests and/or media enquiries should be directed to: press.office@niwater.com. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how parents of children with additional needs are informed about support and funding options are available to them as carers, and to enable them to access opportunities for respite.
answer_text: It is important that HSC Trusts identify and assess the needs of all vulnerable children to ensure that needs are appropriately met at an early stage. Support services and any potential funding that may be available to parents / carers of children with disabilities are based on an assessment of need / eligibility made by the respective Health & Social Care Trust.Across all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland – the Understanding The Needs of Children In Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) assessment framework is the standardised assessment model that is used within children’s services to measure assessed need for children and families – this includes assessed need in terms of support required, and also for services such as Short Breaks. The UNOCINI is an inter-agency assessment model that was developed in Northern Ireland to:improve the quality of assessment within stakeholder agencies.assist in communicating the needs of children across agencies.avoid the escalation of children’s needs through early identification of need and effective intervention. HSC Trusts will work in partnership with parents / carers in terms of ensuring the information is provided regarding how needs may be best met. There may be the option of Direct Payments being made to parents and carers (aged 16 and over) with parental responsibility of a child(ren) with disability to allow services to be arranged rather than receiving the service directly from the HSC Trust. An assessment in respect of eligibility for Direct Payments is made by the respective Health & Social Care Trust. Following an assessment, Direct Payments can be used for a variety of services which offer the child stimulation, new experiences and independence. These may include: short breaks, support to attend youth facilities / engage with activities, personal care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the multi-sensory centres that are accessible for children with additional needs; and (ii) the benefits of children and families having access to these centres.
answer_text: (i) Across Northern Ireland there are several privately operated multi-sensory centres that are accessible to children and families. The Education Authority does not hold information on the number of sensory rooms across the school estate.(ii) Sensory rooms, also referred to as calm spaces, regulation hubs, or multi-sensory environments, are increasingly recognised as essential components of inclusive education. These spaces are designed to support children and young people with a range of additional needs, including autism, ADHD, sensory processing difficulties, and social, emotional and behavioural challenges.   These safe and calming environments can reduce sensory overload and support emotional regulation and well-being.  Sensory rooms benefit all learners by honouring individual sensory preferences and offering choice-led, inclusive support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how the talents of children with special educational needs are being identified and fostered through the education system.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring that all children, including those with special educational needs (SEN), are supported to access and benefit from high-quality education on an equal basis.  A key part of this commitment is recognising and nurturing the individual talents and strengths of children with SEN.All schools have a duty to identify and meet the needs of pupils with SEN, as set out in the ‘Northern Ireland Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs’.  This includes identifying not only areas of need, but also areas of ability and potential that can be enhanced and nurtured.  Inclusion is a core principle of this approach and schools are expected to ensure the participation, progress and personal development of all pupils, including those with SEN.The development of an inclusive education system is a key underpinning aim of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.  As part of this, my department plans to take forward a range of actions to strengthen inclusive practice, including the publication and implementation of an Inclusive System Development Programme and the research, design and trial of inclusion methodologies to promote equitable inclusive learning experiences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is engaging with families of children with sensory processing difficulties so that their calming needs are being catered for through the education system in an appropriate and engaging manner.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that from September 2025, 28 new SEND Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) have been launched across Northern Ireland. Each team comprises specialists in Autism, Language and Communication, Literacy, and Social, Behavioural and Emotional Wellbeing, working collaboratively to provide responsive, localised support to schools, families, and communities.The EA Graduated Response Framework offers clear, staged guidance to assist educational settings on how to identify, assess, and support children and young people with special educational needs and/or disabilities (SEND). It promotes a strengths-based, child-centred approach, ensuring that interventions are proportionate, timely, and tailored to individual profiles.Where sensory processing differences are identified, autism specialists within the LITs engage directly with the child or young person and their family throughout the duration of support. This ensures that individual needs, preferences and regulation strategies are meaningfully incorporated into the support plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the forward planning that has been undertaken by his Department in respect of the future provision of special educational needs services and supports in schools, based on all available data sets.
answer_text: The EA has advised that forward planning for services and support includes a demand forecasting model built on historic referral data from pre-existing services, extrapolating trends forward and use of information from a range of sources including:SENCo referral portal Local IMPACT Team networkreal time data on school enrolmentsite data and building capacityforecasted trends and information provided from Health.statutory assessment process dataEducational Psychologist early indicators of need wider external data from the DE school Census and NISRA

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the commissioning of 12 Serious Adverse Incident reports for patients in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, why staffing levels were insufficient in the cervical screening laboratory.
answer_text: As a result of a decline in the use of cytology (due to the introduction of primary HPV testing across the rest of the UK and Ireland), the number of trained screening staff and staff being trained across the UK significantly reduced. This created significant difficulty recruiting screeners across Northern Ireland over the period in question and led to a reliance on agency workers and the use of overtime in order to meet the demand for cytology screening.With the introduction of primary HPV screening in Northern Ireland, in December 2023, the number of cervical cytology slides needing cytology screening markedly decreased.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the commissioning of 12 Serious Adverse Incident reports for patients in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, to detail the actions his Department is taking to ensure that cytology staff have clear guidance on (i) lines of responsibility for staff underperformance; and (ii) clinical governance systems.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency has commissioned the Belfast Trust to provide a regional cervical screening laboratory service for Northern Ireland, as detailed in a laboratory service specification, with the Trust.This specification states that the Trust will ensure that the performance of all staff involved in the reporting of cervical cytology tests is monitored according to relevant NI Cervical Screening Programme, national and professional body guidance; and take appropriate action where performance is outside national standards and guidelines. At the time of award of the regional laboratory contract, the Trust was required to have a plan for implementing a robust governance structure, including broad functions of clinical lead and senior Biomedical Scientists workforce. The Trust is also responsible for developing Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and work instructions for operationalising their internal arrangements, including how staff performance is monitored and managed. The PHA has agreed the parameters used for performance data with Belfast Trust, in line with national guidance and this is monitored on an ongoing basis by the Trust, with protocols and their application is also reviewed as part of UKAS visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the cytology laboratory in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust operated a unique service model of joint histology and cytology services with some staff working across both, when this is not a service model recognised across other laboratories.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust operated a joint Diagnostic Pathology service since 1988 which included Histology and Cytology. The Southern Trust advises that many Trusts have combined histopathology and cytopathology departments, often referred to as "Cellular Pathology" services and this integrated model reflects the established recommendation to combine both disciplines for a more comprehensive diagnostic approach. The Trust has also advised that joint histology/cytology is a well-established service endorsed by the Institute of Biomedical Science (IBMS) who offer joint qualifications in all cellular pathology specialities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the commissioning of 12 Serious Adverse Incident reports for patients in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, for his assessment of why significant underperformance of two screeners over seven years was not identified sooner.
answer_text: Underperformance was identified and managed by the Southern Trust using the Trust’s laboratory protocols in place at the time. The procedure for the management of screener underperformance from 2006 to 2023 looked at screening sensitivity in the form of rolling 12-month performance data to provide a single overall performance measure for the respective period. This rolling figure then was reviewed with appropriate corrective actions undertaken if the performance for that period was suboptimal.Actions put in place by the Southern Trust to try and address performance included increased Quality Control, targeted retraining, attendance at a gynaecology cytology update course, rescreen of relevant slides and dual screening of slides during the retraining period. Peer support involved support from other senior screening staff.The Trust has since stated that it is apparent that a review of data from multiple consecutive years would have been beneficial, in order to detect trends. This was not part of its’ underperformance protocols from 2006 until 2021.Following the publication of the Royal College of Pathologists’ report into underperformance of cytology screeners in the Southern Trust, the Trust fully accepted the findings that there were failings in the governance processes and management of underperformance of these screeners; and that the actions taken by the Trust at that time were not sufficiently robust. I am on record as saying the report’s findings were clearly unacceptable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the commissioning of 12 Serious Adverse Incident reports for patients in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, (i) when underperformance of cervical screening laboratory staff was first identified; and (ii) why no actions were immediately taken to rectify this.
answer_text: The performance of any screener can dip from time to time for a range of reasons. There are systems in place to pick this up and screeners are given annual training to keep their skills up.During the period 2008 to 2021, some screeners performance in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust did fall below the recognised standard. The underperformance during this time was identified by the Trust and managed using the Trust’s laboratory protocols. Actions put into place by the Southern Trust to try and address performance included increased Quality Control, targeted retraining, attendance at a gynaecology cytology update course, rescreen of relevant slides and dual screening of slides during the retraining period. Peer support involved support from other senior screening staff.In late 2021, there was recognition of persistent underperformance of some cervical screening laboratory staff. At this time, the respective screening staff were removed from screening duties.In July 2022, senior laboratory staff within the Southern Health and Social Care Trust notified the Trust’s management team that they had concerns about performance in some steps of their laboratory’s screening system.
