To investigate these concerns, the Trust commissioned the Royal College of Pathologists (RCPath Consulting) to undertake an independent assessment of its cervical screening services from 1 January 2008 and October 2021. The Southern Trust ceased its cervical screening service in October 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of new homes expected to be constructed in the 2025-26 financial year; (ii) the number of new homes required to meet demand; (iii) the obstacles to constructing sufficient homes to meet demand; and (iv) his assessment of how best to remove those obstacles to meet current demand.
answer_text: Land & Property Services publish reports on the numbers of new homes started and completed each quarter. These reports are available for public access at: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/topics/new-dwelling-statistics.In relation to social homes only, the initial 2025/26 capital allocation will allow in the region of 1,000 new build social homes to start in 2025-26. Additional budget secured in the June Monitoring Round is expected to generate additional new social homes, however, this is significantly short of the target of 2,000 and a bid will be submitted in October monitoring round to address the shortfall. There are around 5,500 social homes currently under construction, and of these, it is expected that around 1,400 will be completed in 2025/26.The NIHE in its role as the strategic Regional Housing Authority for Northern Ireland, is responsible for the identification and analysis of housing need. It has published the Strategic Housing Market Analysis (SHMA) that set out projections of future housing need and demand at Northern Ireland and Local Government District (LGD) levels. These reports are available for public access at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/housing-market-analysis. My Executive Housing Supply Strategy outlines the key barriers that impact on building homes. These include wastewater infrastructure, availability of land, the planning system and most importantly, budget. The Strategy acknowledges that a whole system approach is needed to address these barriers, and it provides a long-term framework for the policies and actions needed to meet our housing needs and demand. This includes an ambition to deliver 100, 000 homes and more, if needed, over the next 15 years. My Department is developing the first Executive action plan to implement the Strategy. This is being taken forward with Executive Departments and the NIHE. This cross-Executive approach will identify innovative and deliverable actions which will begin to address the range of whole system issues that impact on supply.I am working to bring a final draft of the Action Plan to the Executive in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28849/22-27, for his assessment of (i) the announcement by the Irish Government that CAR T-cell therapy will be made available in Galway; and (ii) what bearing this will have on issues of capacity in respect of Northern patients being able to access this treatment in the Republic.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of providing care as close to home as possible for patients and their families.  I remain committed to the ongoing joint working arrangements with RoI colleagues to consider opportunities for cross border cooperation in cancer services and will continue to work towards improving access to care more locally, to the benefit of both jurisdictions.ROI officials have advised that the opening of the CAR-T service in Galway University Hospital is contingent on improvements in ward staffing with an intention to build patient numbers over time.  Challenges with inpatient bed capacity were noted as impacting on the number of patients able to be treated.In this context, RoI officials have confirmed that overall, the CAR-T service is currently operating at capacity and would require significant capital investment to increase further.  Their view remains that the transfer of Northern Ireland patients to GB for treatment remains the only viable option for CAR-T for the foreseeable future.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) when they received the finalised report and recommendations on Historical Institutional Abuse; and (ii) when they will consider this.
answer_text: Following the completion of research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse (HCCA) in late spring, the Inter-Departmental Working Group (IDWG) developed a set of evidence-based recommendations. These address the potential for an effective investigation into historical clerical child abuse and the best approaches to support victims and survivors.We received the IDWG’s advice and recommendations in early summer. We are now taking time to carefully consider their proposals and determine the most appropriate next steps

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients who have availed of CAR T-cell therapy in each of the last five years, broken down by the hospital in which they received their treatment; (ii) the patient (a) success; and (b) mortality rates following their treatment; (iii) the rejection rates from the approval panel; and (iv) the rejection rates by potential patients due to not wanting to travel.
answer_text: (i) Below are the number of patients from NI who have been approved for CAR-T therapy in the last five years.  To note, after approval, some of these patients may not have been treated for medical reasons.  They were treated in Addenbrooke’s Hospital Cambridge, Great Northern Children’s Hospital, King's College Hospital, Manchester Royal Infirmary, Newcastle Upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust and Freeman HospitalRelease of information in respect of a very small volume of patients creates the potential for patients to be identified and would breach HSC confidentiality responsibilities.  The extensive media coverage of CAR-T in recent months has the potential to increase the risk of breach as the wider public may have an increased interest in the therapy. To protect patient confidentiality the number of patients approved for CAR-T have therefore been grouped as follows:2020/2021/2022282023/202441The larger volumes in the latter years category are consistent with the publication of additional NICE recommendations.(ii)(a)(b) Disaggregation of the information in respect of individual patients would create the potential for patients to be identified and breach HSC confidentiality responsibilities as outlined above.More generally mortality rates post CAR-T vary.  The most common overall cause of death post CAR-T is relapse or progression of the original cancer, which further varies depending on the type of cancer treated.  Hospital mortality rates vary by study, but some data shows the non-relapse mortality rate is around 5 – 6%.  The most common cause of death in this group is attributed to infection.(iii) 	the rejection rates by the panel are <5.  Further detail cannot be provided due to possible identification of patients.(iv)	the rejection rates by patients not wanting to travel are <5.  Further detail cannot be provided due to possible identification of patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Education Authority regarding the review of eligibility policies for home to school transport.
answer_text: Eligibility for transport assistance is assessed by the Education Authority (EA) according to the criteria laid out in my Department’s Home to School Transport Policy. The walking distances in the policy are laid out in legislation and are not subject to routine review. The EA has responsibility for the delivery of the transport policy and my officials have regular discussions with their EA colleagues about various aspects of policy implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any correspondence she has had with the UK Home Secretary on the release of all documentation to victim's families in relation to Operation Kenova.
answer_text: I have not had any correspondence with the UK Home Secretary on this specific issue, though I have engaged with her around the need for openness and transparency in providing families with the truth in legacy cases generally. While I support the release of as much information to families as possible, the Department of Justice has no governance or oversight role in relation to Operation Kenova.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what collaborative measures her Department is taking to (i) address the imbalance in police recruitment; and (ii) ensure that the police service is representative of the community it serves.
answer_text: I am confident that there is an appropriate framework in place to encourage applications from all under-represented groups, and I have been proactive in calling for a fully representative police service and encouraging recruits from under-represented groups, including during my time as Minister. I share the widely held ambition to see a police service for the future which is reflective of all parts of our society; however, clearly, the security threat faced by officers is a deterrent to many and the sacrifices which are often required by those who come from a Catholic or Nationalist background remain a significant barrier to recruitment and retention. I would emphasise once again that it is incumbent upon us, as elected representatives, to encourage all members of our community to consider joining the PSNI and to help to create the societal conditions where that is most likely to occur.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work his Department has undertaken in relation the financial implications of constitutional change on the island of Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 24647/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 27896/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 27896/22-27 was answered on 13/06/2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the rationale for his Department's decision to provide £1 million of additional funding for GPs in 2025-26, compared with £969 million for GPs in England, £290 million for GPs in Scotland and £52.1 million for GPs in Wales, for the same time period; and (ii) for his assessment of the impacts that this funding disparity will have in respect of (a) access to GP services; (b) recruitment and retention of GPs; and (c) long-term health outcomes.
answer_text: The General Medical Services (GMS) contract for 2025/26 will see an additional investment of £9.5m into core GP services. This encompasses £5m to address the issue of GP indemnity; £3.5m to mitigate the cost of the increase in employer’s National Insurance costs, and a further £1m core investment. As I am on record as saying, it is a matter of regret that it was not possible to reach agreement in relation to the 2025/26 GMS Contract.  Whilst I would wish to be in the position where I could put substantially more investment into primary care, given the extreme budgetary challenges facing my Department, the £9.5m funding was the best and final offer that could be made for 2025/26.Total investment in GP services this year will be more than £414m. This includes pay uplifts from last year, and the recently announced investment in MDTs, which will go to directly to support GPs in meeting the needs of their patients by expanding capacity in practices. I have also recently announced investment of £2.9m in GP Elective Care, which will benefit GPs directly while meeting the needs of patients.I have also stated my intention to see the pay recommendations from the Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body report for 2025/26 implemented in full.  I have issued a Ministerial Direction instructing immediate implementation of the pay awards for 2025/26. This Direction is currently with the Executive for consideration.It is important to note that Health is a devolved matter and in terms of General Medical Services, there are differences in the services delivered across the UK nations as well as in GMS contractual arrangements. This makes comparison of GP investment between other parts of the UK difficult.In terms of access to GP services, as part of the 2025/26 GMS contract, my Department has introduced a range of measures which, while relatively small in scale, will make a tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services.  These measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered nor to make GPs work to unsafe levels. Rather, they aim to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike. In many cases, these are things that practices here are already doing and the aim has been to seek to spread that good practice across the region.Beyond the GMS Contract offer for 2025/26, I recently announced investment of £61 million in Primary Care MDTs over the next four years.  In 2025/26, the value of this investment is estimated at £6.8m. This is in addition to over £25m already invested in MDTs for primary care.My Department continues to support GP recruitment and retention.  The increase in GP training places to 121 per year will be maintained, despite pressures in the training budget.  Furthermore, the GP Training Group, which includes the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency and GP repres

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail who owns Camlough Lake.
answer_text: Ownership details of land may be obtained in person by appointment (telephone 0300 200 7803) at the public counter of Land Registry, Land & Property Services (LPS), Lanyon Plaza, 7 Lanyon Place, Belfast, BT1 3LP, in writing or by emailing customerinformation.landregistration@finance-ni.gov.uk and requesting an Official Search. A map clearly identifying the area is usually required. If attending in person a member of LPS staff will carry out the search on the LandWeb computer system, and if registered, establish the relevant Land Registry Folio number. There is a fee of £5.00 for the Search and a fee of £5.00 to view a Folio or £7.00 for a copy of the Folio. Cheques should be made payable to DOF General Account or customers can pay by phone using a credit or debit card via our worldpay system.  If the land is not registered in the Land Registry you may be able to obtain ownership information from the Registry of Deeds Register. Information on how to search the Registry of Deeds may also be obtained by contacting the LPS Belfast office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28347/22-27, why St. Paul’s High School, Bessbrook, has not been listed as a school that is oversubscribed for the 2025/26 academic year, given the ongoing and unresolved issues with admissions and places at the school.
answer_text: St Paul's High School was oversubscribed with applications for Year 8 for September 2025 admission through the competitive admissions process.  There is no admissions number set for the admission of children with a statement of special educational needs therefore no school can be oversubscribed in this respect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the investigation by the Northern Ireland Audit Office into waste crime in Northern Ireland which reported that profits from illegal dumping far outweigh the sanctions and that current measures on regulating waste cannot be considered to be achieving best value for money.
answer_text: The Member will be aware of the guiding principle that it is inappropriate to comment in detail on reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office in advance of consideration of the report by the Assembly’s Public Accounts Committee.What I can, though, confirm is that I welcome the publication of this report and can provide assurance that its recommendations will be considered very carefully by officials in my Department.  Reflecting the requirements now in place, my Department will publish its response to the recommendations in the report within the eight week timeframe that applies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 23048/22-27, for a breakdown of financial or material support provided by (i) Invest NI; and (ii) any other publicly funded body, to firms involved in the manufacture or distribution of weapons or munitions, including component parts and subsystems, to (a) Israel; (b) the Israeli Defence Forces; and (c) Israel’s private defence industry, broken down by funding recipient, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Invest NI has advised that it is not aware of any businesses it supports that are engaged in the manufacture or distribution of weapons or munitions—including component parts and subsystems—supplied directly to (a) Israel, (b) the Israeli Defence Forces, or (c) Israel’s private defence industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any closure, including temporary closures, of wards at Daisy Hill Hospital that were being used as overflow accommodation for Emergency Department patients.
answer_text: Daisy Hill Hospital is commissioned for 104 medical beds and constantly has additional patients in the medical wards as well as vacant surgical bed spaces. There is also Emergency Department (ED) overcrowding with corridor care.In December 2024, the Trust provide temporary funding to open 13 additional winter beds to reduce ED overcrowding and corridor care in the ED and adjoining area, with a plan to close these at the end of March 2025.  There is no funding available for these temporary beds.There have been plans in place to reduce these temporary winter beds, however this has been delayed due to ongoing unscheduled care pressures. There had been some bed closures over the last 3 weeks with the beds on occasions dropping to 10. There are currently 13 temporary winter beds open. There is currently no funding for these additional beds and thus there are continued plans to reduce the beds and close this ward. Consideration will be given in the winter, funding permitting, to reopen the beds as part of winter planning contingency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the Northern Ireland Anti-Poverty Network's open letter to the Executive calling for the withdrawal of the draft anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: The Anti-Poverty Strategy is the culmination of hard work and collaboration from a broad range of stakeholders across government and representative groups.I have listened carefully to the recommendations made, many of which have been included and actioned in the Anti-Poverty Strategy.The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy is currently undergoing public consultation until 19 September, providing opportunities for a wide range of organisations to engage with and provide comment on the Strategy.I would encourage all organisations and individuals with an interest or view on poverty to engage with the consultation on the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any communications between their Department and the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs relating to the Casement Park project meeting obligations under the The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: We will continue to work with all stakeholders to make progress on the redevelopment of Casement Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the implication of welfare reform decisions that are transferred matters being made at Westminster, including the impact on the most vulnerable; and (ii) to what extent this inhibits the ability of the Executive to protect the most vulnerable from the most recent tranche of welfare cuts.
answer_text: I have been clear that I want to ensure Northern Ireland has a welfare system that is fair for claimants and taxpayers and protects those most in needThe inclusion of provisions for Northern Ireland in the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill was driven by the UK Government’s commitment to maintaining parity.  Our ability to develop and implement locally tailored mitigation measures in response is limited due to the financial implications for the block grant. I, and my Executive colleagues, have voiced our concern about the latest round of welfare reforms to the Minister for Social Security and Disability. The concessions secured at the second reading of the Bill, which was a clear sign that listening to disabled people and protecting their dignity must remain at the core of policy development, are to be welcomed. However, I will continue to engage with the UK Government to ensure Northern Ireland’s voice is heard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the purpose of new poles with square panels erected adjacent to major carriageways, such as those on the A1 Northbound found shortly after the border.
answer_text: The new poles on the A1 have been installed to facilitate the mounting of CCTV cameras to allow my department to monitor and manage incidents on the strategic A1 Belfast to Dublin corridor.The square plate is a temporary ballast to protect the winch mechanism in the pole until the camera can be installed. The CCTV cameras will be installed in August 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last road safety assessment was undertaken on Chapel Road, Camlough; and (ii) whether she will be approving 20mph speed limit zones for St. Malachy's Primary School, Camlough.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users, particularly near schools.I am advised that my officials last undertook a safety assessment of Chapel Road, Camlough in March 2023. Officials have also reviewed the collision history from the latest available PSNI data and have determined that this road has a good safety record.I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the next tranche is available on my department’s website 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for InfrastructureThe programme of schools to be treated this financial year was developed, based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  Our approach to selecting schools is outlined in our interim policy document DEM 194/25 ‘Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools – Tranche 3 2025/26’ which is publicly available at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/part-time-20mph-speed-limits-at-schools-tranche3-2025-26-dem-19425.pdf Regrettably, St Malachy’s Primary School, Camlough did not score as highly as other locations and was not included in this tranche.  However, the school will be considered in any future extensions of the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any school (i) extension, (ii) construction; and (iii) improvement works that are currently being delayed due to inadequate water and wastewater drainage infrastructure.
answer_text: Currently, no new school build projects have been explicitly halted due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure. However, the capacity of existing water infrastructure, particularly wastewater infrastructure is a significant and escalating reason for delays in building new schools across Northern Ireland.    In addition, wastewater infrastructure issues have led to increased capital project costs for new school builds, including the installation of localised wastewater treatment systems, due to an inability to connect to the public wastewater network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the financial status of NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water Ltd was established through the Water and Sewerage Services (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 as a government owned company (GoCo) limited by shares. In terms of NI Water’s financial status in government, NI Water Ltd and its subsidiaries are classified as non-departmental public bodies (NDPB) for Public Expenditure and national accounts, administration and policy purposes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the locations of; and (ii) the current status of, the disability sports hubs.
answer_text: The Disability Sports Hubs were established as part of the Active Living No Limits Action Plan, which was funded by my Department through Sport NI.Since this is an operational matter for Sport NI I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:		“(i) the locations the disability sports hubs	DSNI ran a pilot in three council areas which led to a partnership with the Progressive Building Society and to the development of the Progressive Disability Sports Hubs for three years to recreate the pilot model and locate a Disability Sports Hub in every council area of Northern Ireland.		(ii) the current status of the disability sports hubs	The Disability Sports Hubs provided local councils with access to inclusive sports equipment. Sport NI is not aware of the hubs having been discontinued or the removal of the inclusive sports equipment”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the manufacturing companies scheduled to locate major facilities in the north and have relocated elsewhere because of inadequate infrastructure, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Businesses will evaluate a range of factors when selecting locations in which to invest and/or expand and decisions to not proceed may be influenced by a number of variables. Data pertaining to specific infrastructure issues is not collected but the Executive is working to create the best possible environment for businesses to establish grow and thrive, to attract continued internationally mobile investment and drive economic prosperity. My work across the Department and the Executive is to create the best possible environment for businesses to establish grow and thrive, to attract continued internationally mobile investment and drive economic prosperity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline his assessment of the impact of the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, for all children with disabilities accessing sport in education.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, which is not yet fully commenced, places a renewed emphasis on the continuum of provision across all stages of the Code of Practice, reinforcing the need for schools to be confident and capable in recognising and responding to the diverse abilities and interests of pupils in their setting. This approach aims to ensure that all children with disabilities receive appropriate support tailored to their individual needs, promoting inclusive and equitable access to the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any funding opportunities that exist for Sticky Fingers Imaginarium, to ensure that they do not have to close some of their programmes.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland, as the funding and development body for the arts, may be contacted directly via info@artscouncil-ni.org to establish if any funding opportunities exist.  It may also be useful to contact the local council to establish if any funding opportunities exist which Sticky Fingers Imaginarium could avail of.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail discussions at the North South Ministerial Council Plenary Meeting on 20 June 2025 relating to All-Island Health.
answer_text: Health is one of the defined Areas of Cooperation under the NSMC structures and Health Ministers regularly discuss how our Administrations can work together for mutual benefit.  Joint Communiqués from NSMC Health meetings can be found at www.northsouthministerialcouncil.org.While the Health Minister was unable to attend the NSMC Plenary meeting on 20 June, we did discuss opportunities for cooperation in the Health sector.  In particular, we recognised the potential of working together to support people who have rare diseases, especially children, as being an area which could be explored further.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action her Department is taking to tackle scam text messages claiming to be from the Government.
answer_text: My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) has a dedicated scams team that are responsible for raising public awareness of scams through regular media appearances as well as issuing press releases and social media alerts about the latest frauds. They deliver educational talks to local community groups while working with banks, charities, and law enforcement to identify victims of fraud. Officers have visited hundreds of scam victims in the last year, helping many of them take cases to the Financial Ombudsman Service, resulting in over £1million being returned to those individuals defrauded by criminals. Trading Standards staff continue to educate victims on how to spot scams and protect themselves from further financial losses. Trading Standards officials regularly conduct visits to consumers homes and encourage them to report scam text messages to the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC). Most phone providers are part of a scheme that allows customers to report suspicious text messages to the NCSC for free. If customers forward a text to 7726, their mobile phone provider can investigate the origin of the text and arrange to block or ban the sender if it is found to be malicious. Customers can also report a text message by contacting their phone provider directly. TSS participates in the Scamwise NI Partnership with 45 partner organisations, including the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Commissioner for Older People, the Consumer Council, and financial institutions. The objective of the Partnership is to engage and inform the community, particularly the vulnerable, about the risks and range of scams and frauds that exist.Additionally, my Department’s partner organisation, the Consumer Council, supports consumers and vulnerable groups through outreach programmes ensuring they are better equipped to identify and avoid scams. It works with partners to promote awareness of scams and agree referral mechanisms to ensure vulnerable consumers are supported. Ofcom, the communications regulator, oversees how mobile networks manage and block spam and scam messages in Britain. They have responsibility for regulating the telecommunications industry and have a key role in protecting consumers from harm caused by scam text messages, working with mobile providers to monitor the misuse of SMS services. Ofcom publishes rules and expectations for networks to protect users and can sanction or work with providers to block scammers abusing telecom systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the impact that controlled burning has on densely populated urban areas; and (ii) what plans he has to regulate this.
answer_text: My Department has no evidence that controlled burning has had any significant impact on densely populated urban areas in Northern Ireland.I intend to undertake a public consultation in relation to the controlled prescribed burning dates in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what advice her Department offer people faced with a large legal fees, in cases where the charges against them have been dismissed.
answer_text: The Access to Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 provides for individuals to have access to civil legal services that effectively meet their needs, while the Legal Aid, Advice and Assistance Order (Northern Ireland) 1981 provides for free legal aid in criminal proceedings if the defendant has insufficient means and it is desirable in the interests of justice. 	The courts have discretion to determine whether defence costs relating to individuals who have been acquitted, or have had their charges dropped, should be recouped, taking into account the interests of justice and the conduct of the case. The Costs in Criminal Cases Act (Northern Ireland) 1968 includes provision for the court to order the Director of Public Prosecutions to pay to the accused the whole or any part of the costs of defence in cases of acquittal, dismissal or discharge. Any decision on whether to award defence costs in individual cases lies solely with the Lady Chief Justice and the Judiciary. 	Legal aid is provided for individuals who cannot afford to pay for their representation, subject to financial eligibility and means testing. Individuals who are involved in criminal proceedings will have their financial eligibility and merits of the case considered by the court if they do not have enough income to pay for their defence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what publicity campaigns his Department has launched to educate and warn young people about the dangers of recreational drug use.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead on the implementation of the cross-Departmental strategy to prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use in Northern Ireland. 		The Public Health Agency commission the Regional Connections Service, to implement awareness campaigns, coordinate and disseminate harm reduction messaging and ensure there are clear pathways to support services within communities. This service has been commissioned across the five Health and Social Care Trust Areas and is delivered by 3 community/voluntary services.	At a locality level, the Connections Service often implement more targeted interventions/projects with partners based on the specific needs of their communities. These can include direct outreach and educational activities focussed on recreational drug use, which are designed and delivered in collaboration with local stakeholders, schools, youth groups and healthcare providers. 	In addition, targeted prevention services develop and deliver age-appropriate drug & alcohol life skills/harm reduction programmes for young people identified as being at risk of substance. Eight Youth Engagement services for young people aged 11 – 25 years are also available across Northern Ireland which provide up to date objective information about personal health and wellbeing issues (including drugs and alcohol). 	Details of awareness-raising activity and local services can be found at: http://services.drugsandalcoholni.info/. Drug prevalence In terms of the nature and prevalence of drug use, your question has been transferred to my Department. Whilst the information is not collated in the format you have requested; a summary of available information is set out below.AdultsInformation on drug prevalence is typically collected from surveys; the last NI drug prevalence survey (DPS) ran in 2014/15 however a pilot module on drug use was included in the 2017/18 Health Survey Northern Ireland (HSNI) and a module is currently running in the 2024/25 Health Survey, with findings available later this year. The findings from the pilot (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/findings-pilot-drugs-module-201718) indicated similar levels of last year prevalence of illegal drugs compared with 2014/15. The table below outlines some of the findings.% of respondents2017/18HSNI2014/15DPSAny illegal drug*4.85.9Cannabis4.44.6Ecstasy0.50.8Cocaine0.91.8Crack0.00.3Magic mushrooms0.20.4Amphetamines/speed0.20.5Poppers0.11.1LSD0.10.5Mephedrone0.00.6Other New Psychoactive Substances0.00.3Heroin0.00.2Solvents0.10.0*Any illegal drug includes the drugs listed in the table.Whilst comparable results were found for overall use of illegal drugs, use of drugs such as antidepressants and sedatives were not of a similar nature. Much lower rates were reported in the health survey than would have been expected, potentially due to the blunt nature of the questions failing to pick up use of legally available drugs, with respondents instead focusing on reporting illicit drug

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what savings; and (ii) what percentage of their total budget he has asked each Health and Social Care Trust to make in the (a) 2025-26; and (b) 2026-27 financial year.
answer_text: The initial first phase savings set out in the following table are targets set to reflect the requirement for the five HSC Trusts to return to their 2024/25 reported deficit levels.  It should be noted that, whilst no formal savings target has been set for the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), NIAS has been asked to contain emerging pressures within the organisation.  This will include, for example, measures to address funding pressures relating to the increase in Employer National Insurance Contributions, introduced in April 2025. The savings targets set out in Table 1 are in addition to any savings measures required by HSC Trusts to address the £181.6m 2024/25 Trust savings targets, not yet recurrently achieved by HSC Trusts.   Table 1:  2025/26 Phase 1 savings targets for HSC TrustsInitial 2025/26 savings target (Phase 1)* (£m)Phase 1 savings as a percentage of funding**BHSCT38.21.9%NHSCT16.41.5%SEHSCT21.02.1%SHSCT14.91.5%WHSCT14.01.4%Total104.51.7%* Rounded to nearest £0.1m** Rounded percentage of funding compared to recurrent revenue funding from DOH and PHA.In addition, HSC Trusts have been set a further savings target totalling £100m, individually equating to their proportional level of funding.  Savings measures to address this target are being worked through on a regional basis, via the Systems Financial management Group (SFMG), chaired by the Permanent Secretary.  Identified workstreams are being taken forward and led at the most senior level and the exact split of the initial share of savings targets, set out in Table 2 below as initially based on proportionate funding levels, will be confirmed once further work is completed by the SFMG.Table 2:  2025/26 Phase 2 initial savings targets for HSC Trusts2025/26 initial savings target (Phase 2) (£m)*Phase 2 savings as a percentage of funding**BHSCT32.71.7%NHSCT18.11.7%SEHSCT16.51.7%SHSCT16.41.7%WHSCT16.31.7%Total100.01.7%* Rounded to nearest £0.1m** Rounded percentage of funding compared to recurrent revenue funding from DOH and PHA.In respect of 2026/27 savings targets, we are in the early stages of planning and no formal targets have yet been set in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what publicity campaigns his Department has launched to educate and warn young people about the dangers of recreational drug use.
answer_text: My department has not launched any publicity campaigns covering this subject.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when Casement Park will be built.
answer_text: Your question has been passed to me to respond to. As the Member should be aware by now, it is not possible to be definitive about project delivery at this time, as so many cost and design factors remain uncertain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch on the Camlough Road, Newry, (i) what other land, other than land owned by her Department, is the billboard erected on; (ii) whether the contractor that was engaged to remove racist graffiti from nearby Department for Infrastructure bridges was used to remove graffiti from this billboard; and (iii) for her latest assessment as to whether this billboard has been illegally erected.
answer_text: The billboard at the Egyptian Arch on the Camlough Road, Newry is mounted on five posts, some of which appear to be situated on the Department’s land. The other posts are on privately owned land.The Department recently received a report regarding racist graffiti on a railway bridge in the area. As responsibility for the maintenance of the bridge rests with Translink, it was added to their list for action.  Translink has advised that the PSNI also notified them of several other structures with similar graffiti issues, and that works undertaken by their contractor did not include any graffiti removal at or on Egyptian Arch.Priority is given to the removal of advertising where there is a clear road safety risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what urgent actions are being taken by his Department to (i) address children's services social workers' workforce planning in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust to address staff workload, well-being and safety concerns; and (ii) ensure children and their families get the timely support and care they need.
answer_text: My Department has been engaged in a range of actions which are designed to increase the stability of the HSC social work workforce. A Social Work Workforce Review was published in 2022. The Review contained a series of recommendations, the implementation of which are overseen by the Chief Social Worker. The Review identified the following key priorities;  1. Fill vacant social work posts.  Since ceasing the use of recruitment agencies to employ social workers in HSC in June 2023, there has been a huge effort to attract as many newly qualified social workers as possible into Trust vacancies.  The number of social work vacancies recorded by Trusts peaked at 642 in 2020 and has been reducing  year-on-year since then. The following table gives a breakdown of social work posts filled in 2023, 2024 and the number of current applications to Trusts, made by final year students at QUB and UU in 2025;    BHSCT    NHSCT    SHSCT   SEHSCT    WHSCT TOTAL2023 HSC SW posts filled61473240292092024 HSC SW posts filled58494337512382025 HSC SW applications 6461413851255             TOTAL1831571161151317022.Stabilize & support the workforce  The decision was taken to reduce the opportunity for newly qualified social workers to move around services and/or Trusts during their Assessed Year of Employment (AYE). This has had many consequential benefits by enabling more stability in front-line teams and providing a better consolidation of learning for early-career social workers. The vast majority of social workers in years two and three of practice have chosen to remain in their allocated posts, well-beyond the completion of their AYE, which has also improved workforce stability.All Trusts have also been engaged in a range of diverse activities designed to support social workers in practice. The Department has produced guidance for employers and a compendium of wellbeing activities which Trusts have identified as helpful. Social workers have been encouraged to avail of free mindfulness instruction classes, which have also been hugely popular. 3.Invest in more social work student placesThe Department has been steadily increasing investment in additional social work student places since 2019, beginning with 15 places on the newly-approved Degree in Social Work at the Open University. The table below provides an account of how investment in training places has increased from a baseline of 260 in 2018;QUBUUOUTotal20181481120260201914811215275202014811215275202114811225285202214811240300202314811245305202416112751339*NB - 339 is the largest number of commissioned social worker training places since the establishment of the Degree in Social Work in 2004.The Southern HSC Trust reports that, in addition to these regional actions, significant efforts have been made within the Trust’s Children and Young People’s Directorate to alleviate workforce pressures, with specific focus on the attraction, recruitment and retention of social work staff. This includes;the establishment o

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the efforts her Department is making to ensure that the key sentencing principle of punishment is applied, in relation to individuals convicted of technology-assisted child sexual abuse.
answer_text: Sentencing decisions are determined by the independent judiciary based on the individual circumstances for each case, which can vary widely from case-to-case, as well as any relevant sentencing guidance that is available, and the sentence maxima for the offence in question as specified in legislation.The Department’s last Sentencing Policy Review, the outcome of which was published in April 2021, considered that it would be desirable to have a clear understanding of the principles and purposes of sentencing as this could improve awareness, understanding, clarity and public confidence in sentencing; and, while not treading on judicial independence, might assist the courts with achieving consistency in sentencing decisions.  Therefore, my Sentencing Bill will make provision for the principles and purposes of sentencing for adult offenders.  Punishment will be included as one of the purposes of sentencing, along with protection of the public, deterrence, rehabilitation and reparation.  During this mandate, I also intend to undertake a further review of sentencing issues that have emerged since the last review was completed.  One of the issues which I intend to include for consideration is sentencing for serious sexual offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the Education Authority's press release of 25 June 2025 and potentially compelling schools to enrol pupils with special educational needs (SEN), to detail what resourcing he will provide to schools to enable them to facilitate children with SEN.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that;All schools with specialist provision classes will receive the following package:The EA meets all teaching and classroom assistant staffing costs related to members of staff employed by the school to work in a specialist provision class.In line with the Common Funding Formula schools with specialist provision classes receive additional funding within their LMS budget.  The pupil elements of the budget allocations for Specialist Provisions use a reduced Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) weighting to reflect the fact that the Education Authority meets the full staffing costs. This equates to schools receiving approximately 75% of the AWPU value for each child placed within the specialist provision class. In addition, a fixed amount of £3,000 per class per annum is allocated to the school through their LMS budget.Where a capital works project is required, schools will receive an Initial Furniture and Equipment budget of approximately £20,000 per class.Schools will also receive a one off £3,000 per class initial set up allowance to provide resources.  This is allocated directly to the school's LMS budget in the Autumn term of the classes first operational year.Universal support:Each school/setting is allocated an Advisory Teacher as a point of contact with the team for support, signposting and advice.Each school/setting will continue to have access to a named School Improvement Professional from the School Development Service to support the school leader and strategic planning.Programmes have been intentionally designed to build the capacity of settings to meet the needs of CYP and parents/carers, with increasing confidence and skill. This is done through training, modelling, and capacity building.Targeted Support: Targeted Support programmes are aimed at staff, parents/carers and/or CYP in specific targeted groups or at specific times in a child’s developmental journey. This could include, for example, transition programmes, parent/carer and setting cluster groups, school-based training, teacher advice and guidance or group intervention.Specialist Support:Short term specialist support for CYP up to 6 weeks, staff and parents builds on the foundations laid in schools through both teacher professional learning and targeted support programmes. Intensive support will focus on the needs of an individual CYP and will have clearly agreed outcomes. A programme of intensive support will focus on the identified and assessed needs of an individual pupil.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to construct a mother and baby unit.
answer_text: An earlier independent review identified that the model of the proposed 6/7 bedded Mother and Baby Unit should be that as recommended by the Royal College of Psychiatrists and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit should be the City Hospital site, within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. I have already made it clear to officials within the Department that I wish to see the issue progressed at pace.  However, ensuring that the service meets future demand, the latest position is that PHA colleagues have been asked for their input to strengthen the case for a 7 bedded unit, if appropriate. Belfast Trust are at a very advanced stage in the development of a capital and revenue business case. They have advised that once they receive instruction on the number of beds required, it will take 6 weeks to finalise the business case.Progression of the unit will remain subject to the necessary funding.While the development of a Mother and Baby Unit remains a longer-term strategic objective, my Department has committed to strengthening existing provision in the interim through:Investment in community-based specialist perinatal mental health services.Upskilling of perinatal clinical staff.Implementation of the Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathways and associated guidanceImplementation of crisis intervention pathways and out-of-hours support for women experiencing acute distress.Ongoing engagement with service users and advocacy groups to ensure care remains person-centred and responsive to the needs of mothers and families. Development of a Service User Leaflet to explain the Perinatal Mental Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following confirmation of works in the 2023-24 financial year, to provide a timeline for the commencement of resurfacing works for High Street, Newry.
answer_text: My Department intended to resurface High Street, Newry in the autumn of 2023. However, the scheme was postponed due to the construction of a large development which began at that time. As the development’s construction would have considerable impact on road traffic and alignment, this would render any resurfacing works nugatory. Construction work on this development is still on-going.When works associated with this development are complete, consideration will be given to resurfacing High Street, subject to the availability of funding and consideration of competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will facilitate additional train and bus services for Armagh GAA supporters travelling to Croke Park on 29 June 2025, given that existing services are now at capacity.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The weekend of 27-30 June 2025 is one of the most demanding of the events season, featuring four Belsonic concerts, two concerts at the Aviva Stadium, one concert in Malahide Castle, Dublin Pride, the conclusion of the school exam season, and the GAA Senior Football Championship Quarter Finals.  Understandably, this presents considerable logistical challenges in terms of planning and resource allocation for Translink. However, given the increased demand Ulsterbus have added additional ‘Special’ capacity from Lurgan, Armagh, and Newry depots, which are currently on-line for sale on Translink’s web site: www.translink.co.uk/special-events/gaa-sfc-quarter-finalFor any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional resources will be allocated to the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Adult Autism Services Team to enable them to meet levels of demand.
answer_text: At present, there are limited additional resources available to allocate to Southern Health and Social Care Trust Adult Autism Services Team to enable them to meet levels of demand. This is due to broader budgetary constraints across the health and social care system at this time.We recognise that demand for adult autism diagnostic assessment is outpacing the capacity of existing Adult Autism diagnostic clinics across the region, resulting in lengthy and growing waiting times. This is unacceptable and we are committed working with Health and Social Care Trusts and the wider system to find sustainable solutions and develop, coordinate and drive forward delivery of regional work to improve timeliness to diagnosis, reduce variation and better meet population needs now and in the future. My Department has recently appointed a project lead for Adult Autism to take forward this work and on 2nd July, we convened a first regional workshop focusing on care pathways for autistic adults without a co-occurring learning disability. This workshop brought Trusts together with autistic adults to reflect on where we are currently, why it is important we get this pathway right and consider collectively what we can do within existing resources to improve timeliness to diagnosis and meet support needs. An initial action plan was co-developed during the workshop and a task and finish group will be convened to take this work forward over upcoming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering an all-island mother and baby service for mothers experiencing post-natal depression.
answer_text: Improving mental health services, including perinatal care, remains a key priority of mine.  Regionally all five Trusts now have operational community perinatal mental health teams and the business case for the Regional Mother and Baby Unit is well-progressed with further work currently being carried out to strengthen the proposal and to future proof the Unit.Additionally, my officials continue to liaise with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland regarding their plans for a mother and baby inpatient service. This engagement forms part of our broader work on enhancing perinatal mental health services. While there are currently no formal proposals for all-island services for mothers experiencing post-natal depression, we remain committed to exploring collaborative opportunities that support improved outcomes for mothers experiencing postnatal depression.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the clinical rationale behind creating an additional urgent criteria for cancer red flag and reviews; and (ii) what action will be taken to inform the public of the differences between red flag and urgent criteria.
answer_text: There has been no change to the urgent referral criteria for suspected cancer.  All patients continue to be referred via the Red Flag Suspected Cancer Pathway, as outlined in the Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) Primary Care Referral Guidance.  GPs in Northern Ireland continue to use this guidance, including the two-week urgent referral for suspected breast cancer. The guidance is available here NICaN-Suspect-Cancer-GP-Referral-Guidance-10th-June-2024-qFIT-threshold-1.pdf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what recommendations emerged from the 2019 public consultation on breast assessment proposals; and (ii) why these recommendations were not published.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the rationale for reducing Health and Social Care Trust-based breast cancer services; and (ii) what action his Department took to advise (a) Health and Social Care Trust-based practitioners; (b) GPs; and (c) the public, of those changes.
answer_text: There has been no reduction in Health and Social Care Trust based breast cancer services, instead, My Department’s ongoing review of breast cancer services seeks to increase capacity for breast assessment services.My Department has implemented a new regional booking system which is expected to help equalise waiting times across all Trusts. Furthermore, £5m has been allocated regionally under the Elective Care Framework for breast assessment and surgical services. This funding aims to address immediate capacity shortfalls, reduce backlogs and increase core capacity of breast services to meet demand more effectively.Any changes to services will be communicated to relevant parties in line with agreed processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment on waiting times for accessing the regional breast cancer service increasing; and (ii) what action his Department plans to take to address this.
answer_text: i) Regionally Breast Services in Northern Ireland have been under significant pressure for some time resulting in increased waiting times across the region.  A new regional waiting list for breast cancer assessment went live on 8 May 2025 with the aim of equalising wait times.  While some Trusts have experienced improvement in waiting times, overall regional trends reflect the growing pressures on the service. ii) My Department remains actively engaged with Trusts to address challenges and is committed to reducing waiting times and building sustainable capacity within breast services, ensuring timely, high-quality care for all patients.Work is ongoing to assess and expand capacity across all Trusts.  £5millon addition funding has been earmarked within the Elective Care Framework to address the demand/capacity gap.My Department will continue to monitor performance, evaluate the impact of these changes and support capacity-building efforts across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what consideration has been given to supporting patients receiving cancer care appointments at distant locations; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact on patients from Newry and Armagh undergoing cancer treatment who are being offered appointments at 8am in Altnagelvin Cancer Centre, without access to independent transport.
answer_text: Patients from across Northern Ireland may need to travel to access highly specialist cancer assessment and treatment from one of two cancer centres in Northern Ireland, North West Cancer Centre (Londonderry) and Northern Ireland Cancer Centre (Belfast).  Cancer Services are also provided by each Health and Social Care Trust to ensure when possible ongoing cancer treatment for example Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT) is provided as close to the patients home as possible.   My department acknowledges that early appointments at distant locations can be challenging.  Patients can discuss with the HSC Trust the appointment times offered in terms of distances, travel time and support.  To mitigate the impact of travel each Health & Social Care Trust operates a hospital travel costs scheme.  In addition, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) provides non-emergency patient transport services across the province for patients to get to routine appointments at hospital and treatment centres throughout Northern Ireland, eligible patients can seek support via NIAS.  The Hospital Travel Costs Scheme: www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/hospital-travel-costs-schemePatient Transport Service via NIAS: https://nias.hscni.net/services/patient-transport-service/ This service can be accessed through the patient’s GP if eligible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide (i) a progress update on all school build projects announced in the last ten years; and (ii) an anticipated (a) start date; and (b) completion date for each project.
answer_text: The current position of new school build projects announced since 2015 are detailed in the tables below. It should therefore be noted that anticipated start dates for projects, other than those already on site or due to commence this financial year, are indicative only. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the extent of the maintenance backlog in schools in monetary terms; (ii) how much has has been spent on school maintenance in each of the last ten years; and (iii) his plans to improve maintenance in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is currently in year three of a five-year condition survey cycle across the schools’ estate. Based on the surveys completed to date, the maintenance backlog is circa. £250m. Following completion of the full cycle of condition surveys in 2028, it is projected that the maintenance backlog in schools will be circa. £800m.		Earmarked resource funding for Schools’ Maintenance in controlled and maintained schools is allocated to the EA from the Department. In line with record retention policies, details of the EA Schools’ Maintenance final expenditure figures for the past seven years are shown in the table below. 	These figures reflect spend against the EA’s School Maintenance earmarked resource allocation. They exclude spend on ‘tenant maintenance’ under the Common Funding Formula.	Please note that the information provided for 2024-25 is provisional outturn expenditure, and as such may be subject to change.	Resource Budget DescriptionFinal Outturn Expenditure by Financial Year£m2018-192019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24*2024-25* (Provisional Outturn)Schools’ Maintenance(*includes Special Schools’ Maintenance)20.45618.53722.68925.23527.92730.93230.586	The EA is rolling out new Term Service Contracts for the provision of response, planned maintenance and minor building, mechanical and electrical maintenance works to the EA and Libraries property estate. 		The implementation of these new contracts will lead to the delivery of significant changes including the completion of task orders in compliance with specified Managed Performance Measures (Key Performance Indicators) to include, for example, quality of workmanship; adherence to health and safety requirements; and adherence to task order response and completion times. 		The schools’ estate is in need of increased and sustained funding to address the many years of underinvestment, and I will continue to make the case for this additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many mobile classrooms on the school estate have been deemed unsuitable for educational activities by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Maintenance Service does not specifically assess the suitability of mobile classrooms for educational activities. Condition surveyors identify mobiles that are of poor condition only. Where appropriate, a full structural engineer’s report will be undertaken to consider if a mobile should be replaced. If a mobile unit has been recommended for replacement, this will be progressed via the Minor Capital Works Programme. The current five-year condition survey programme of the schools’ estate is 39% complete with 309 schools surveyed to date. The EA has advised that of the 309 schools surveyed, 166 mobile classrooms within 107 mobiles located at 45 various schools have been identified as being of poor condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many (i) mobile classrooms; and (ii) modular classrooms, are currently on the school estate.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Maintenance Service is currently in year 3 of a 5-year surveying cycle of the school’s estate.Therefore, based on the EA estate surveyed to date there are:309 Modular Classrooms616 Mobile Classrooms

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any changes in referral criteria for breast cancer assessments in the last five years, including the rationale for those changes.
answer_text: NICE NG12 (2021) was adopted in Northern Ireland in August 2022. This brought referral criteria for breast cancer assessment in line with national agreed standards. No further changes have been made to referral criteria in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether (i) she is aware that the economic appraisal of Newry Canal, is currently being reviewed by Newry Mourne and Down District Council; (ii) the review's findings will be factored into her decision, in respect of a fixed bridge; and (iii) no decision will be made on a fixed bridge until the review is completed.
answer_text: My officials are aware of the Newry Mourne and Down District Council motion seeking to develop Newry Ship Canal, agreed at the full council meeting held on 7 April 2025.As you will be aware, the public consultation exercise for the Newry Southern Relief Road concluded on 4th March 2025. My department received a number of detailed representations as part of the consultation process, and my officials are working through the information to ensure that all comments are considered as part of a report for my consideration. I will then take the necessary time to consider the report in detail before making any announcement on this important scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of roads identified as being in need of resurfacing in the Newry and Armagh constituency; (ii) the projected cost of completing these resurfacing works; and (iii) the total budget allocated to DfI Southern Division in respect of road resurfacing.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as carriageway resurfacing.Whilst there are many roads which would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to limited budgets, resurfacing and maintenance work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those areas deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes in the Newry and Armagh constituency planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Reports to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a breakdown of any financial supports currently available through her Department and its arm’s length bodies to facilitate the redevelopment and regeneration of waterways as tourism assets.
answer_text: Whilst Tourism NI does not currently offer any financial supports to third parties to support the development and regeneration of waterways as tourism assets, it does recognise the importance of this provision. The Embracing the Outdoors Tourism framework seeks to cohere the work of many partners to maximise the tourism potential of our outdoor assets. Tourism NI works with the all-island Blueway Partnership to support visitor focused development of our waterways. Membership includes Tourism NI, Fáilte Ireland, Sport NI, Sport Ireland and Waterways Ireland. The Partnership focuses on several key aspects:Infrastructure Development such as launching points, signage, and safety features.Promotion and Marketing: marketing campaigns, informational materials, and online resources.Sustainable Tourism: ensuring that the development and promotion of Blueways are conducted in an environmentally sustainable mannerCommunity Engagement: to ensure they benefit from increased tourism and recreational opportunities.Economic Development: Leveraging Blueways as a means to stimulate economic growth.This initiative is designed to work with Blueway Developers to provide outdoor recreation opportunities on and along waterways such as rivers, lakes, and canals. Blueways typically include facilities for activities like kayaking, canoeing, paddleboarding, and walking or cycling along adjacent paths. Tourism NI has provided the Partnership with £13,445 annual support for the delivery of the Blueway Action Plan 2023-2025, with a review process currently underway to assess future commitment. Tourism NI’s commitment represents 15% of the total Partnership contribution, taking into account organisation size. The estimated yearly contribution by the all-island Partnership, totals €105,040.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the current provision of statutory animal welfare services; (ii) to detail current funding for animal welfare services, broken down by constituency; and (iii) to outline any action he will be taking to enhance animal welfare services.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 delineates responsibilities for enforcement.  My Department maintains the Act, as it relates to farmed animals, while the welfare of companion animals is a matter for local councils.  Animal fighting, other crime involving animals, or the law as it applies wildlife is the domain of the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).My Department has no statutory obligation to provide financial support to animal welfare enforcement services delivered by other public bodies.  Each enforcement body has complete autonomy to determine the activities required to uphold animal welfare laws and direct resources as the need arises.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any (i) financial; and (ii) practical, assistance that is available through his Department and its arms' length bodies for local writers seeking support in getting their work published.
answer_text: My Department does not have any direct funding streams available.  However, the Arts Council, as the Arm’s-Length Body of my Department responsible for the funding and development of the arts, may be contacted for advice via info@artscouncil-ni.org for anyone seeking support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail a breakdown of all Executive capital projects that overspent against budget, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: Details of overspends against agreed budgets for Executive capital projects would be held by the relevant Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to the recent reports from Transport Salaried Staffs' Association, (i) for her assessment of the implications of the industrial action by Translink workers will have upon commuters; and (ii) what action her Department is taking to avoid strike action and service disruption.
answer_text: The responsibility for negotiating and resolving the pay award related dispute lies with Translink and the Transport Salaried Staffs' Association union. It is not clear what impact, any proposed industrial action by staff represented by this union, will have on the operation of public transport services. This will continue to be assessed by Translink. I would encourage both parties to continue their discussions to reach agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total expenditure by (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies, in each of the last five years for facilitating the (a) transport; (b) accommodation; and (c) treatment of patients travelling to Britain for medical care that is available on the island of Ireland.
answer_text: The Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) protocol provides arrangements for individuals who have clinically exceptional health or social care needs that cannot be provided within Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland. The ECR process applies to those patients who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland and entitled to access NHS healthcare, to receive the care or treatment they require, outside Northern Ireland Health and Social Care services where this criterion is met, as seamlessly as possible and free at the point of delivery.My Department has a total budget of £40m for Extra Contractual Referrals. This accounts for both Acute and Non-Acute programmes of care, non-contracted activity (emergency care) and associated patient travel costs together. The total costs for treatment and associated travel and accommodation costs for each of the 5 years are as follows:  Summary of ECR Expenditure2020/21COVID2021/222022/232023/242024/25£m£m£m£m£mAcute Elective6.968 13.996 19.62120.99619.674Acute Non-Elective1.160 1.425 1.8303.1703.3518.128 15.421 21.45124.16623.025Non-Acute11.323 12.258 11.3337.7594.380Travel4.145 6.115 5.4326.0017.449Total ECRs23.597 33.793 38.21637.92634.854ECR protocol applies directly to patient healthcare provisions working with Northern Ireland HSC Trusts and does not include arm’s length bodies. These costs apply to provision of medical care in Britain for those patients that cannot be treated in Northern Ireland. We do not hold a comprehensive list of services available in the Republic of Ireland and are therefore are unable to determine what proportion of this care could be provided to NI patients there.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy is not available; (ii) what consideration he has given to making this treatment available; and (iii) for his assessment of the merits of enabling patients to avail of this treatment in Dublin, rather than having to travel to Britain.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) a breakdown of publicly owned property that is currently unused or derelict; and (ii) any plans he has for greater public utilisation of these properties.
