question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when carers for adults requiring short-term respite care will have the option to make an advanced booking.
answer_text: This response is in relation to bed-based short breaks (respite) for adult programmes of care.Contact has been made by Department of Health Officials with HSC Trusts across adult programmes of care.  HSC Trusts have advised that where possible they do book short breaks in advance.  Keyworkers work with service users and carers to plan services as per assessed need considering their needs and wishes.  HSC Trusts utilise short break bed-based provision within both statutory and independent sector care homes.  However, bed-based short breaks, are heavily reliant upon care home capacity and as such, advance booking may not always be an available option. When organising short breaks consideration has also to be given as to the needs of the individual alongside the needs of other residents in the care home.  For those people with complex needs, thought has to be given to compatibility with other residents which can affect capacity.  The need to expedite hospital discharges also affects care home availability.  Emergency short breaks are also available for adults.  They can be provided in critical or unforeseen situations for example when primary caregivers are unexpectedly unavailable through illness. Pressures of this type can limit opportunities to secure advanced bookings at short break facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the implementation of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: As highlighted in AQW 17982 22-27, the Department continues to work with our colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail and NTA, with the assistance of European Investment Bank consultants, to consider the recommendations in the Review. This work is expected to continue over the next number of months. Translink are also undertaking several feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which are due to report in the coming months.  The outcomes from these studies and the work with our southern colleagues will help inform my prioritisation of projects to progress as funding opportunities arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on all engagement between his Department and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in respect of enhancing the provision of cross-border health services at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern HSC Trust have confirmed they are currently in active discussion with colleagues in HSE to explore opportunities for cross border working across services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many adult short-term respite beds were available in nursing homes within the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in (i) 2022-23; (ii) 2023-24; (iii) 2024-25; and (iv) 2025-26.
answer_text: The Southern Trust does not gather information in a manner that easily facilitates the Trust responding to this request in the way it has been asked.While the Trust will have supported some individuals to avail of respite placements, this will have been in small numbers, frequently at short notice and based on opportune vacancies presenting across care homes, rather than part of planned admissions to identified long term respite beds. Care Homes prefer to fill their homes with long term residents, rather than dealing with respite residents, who by nature would only be staying in the care home for a short period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department’s (i) spending priorities; and (ii) planned projects, in respect of capital expenditure, in the 2025-2026 financial year.
answer_text: I am keen to get infrastructure right and in taking final decisions on my opening capital allocations, I will seek to ensure that investment in infrastructure supports our society, economy and well-being.The Equality Consultation for my Capital budget closed on 5 June 2025 and I am considering the responses to this before making final budget decisions.This year, my Department received £917m capital. Of this, £367.2m is ring fenced for Flagship, City and Growth Deal projects, EU schemes and RRI borrowing. Flagships schemes include the A5, the A6 and the Belfast Transport Hub. City and Growth Deal schemes include Belfast Rapid Transit, the Enniskillen Bypass, Lagan Bridge, Derry Waterfront, Strabane footbridge and the Newry Southern Relief Road.I will also invest in other schemes/programmes of work including the Derry to Coleraine track renewal, structural maintenance, Active Travel Schemes, a range of other road related schemes, a range of Translink schemes, flood alleviation schemes and releasing constraints on housing supply by funding waste water capacity projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a detailed breakdown of all capital funding for sporting infrastructure provided by his Department in the last 15 years, broken down by project and recipient sporting organisation.
answer_text: As the Department for Communities was only established in 2016, I am only able to provide information from that point onward.Due to the volume of information required to answer this question, I have arranged for the information to be provided in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) his assessment of whether all sports and sporting bodies are funded equitably; and (ii) any data his Department holds in relation to per capita distribution of sports funding.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that all sports and sporting organisations receive equitable funding, with particular attention given to clearly identified needs.I asked the Chief Executive of Sport NI to comment on their approach to ensuring equity in the distribution of exchequer funding, and he advised me as follows:-“Sport NI develops funding programmes with programme criteria that are open to all. Sport NI welcomes applications to our programmes no matter what the background of the applicant. Applications are assessed upon their ability to meet the criteria and deliver the objectives set out within the respective programme’s guidance and which are aligned to the NI Executive Strategy for Sport. All programmes undergo equality and rural needs impact assessments to ensure they are equitable, in accordance with Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, and the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.”My Department does not hold data regarding per capita distribution of sports funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) the total funding commitment secured for Casement Park redevelopment to date, broken down by (a) funding source and (b) the date of funding commitment; and (ii) all options that he is currently considering in respect of securing the additional funding required.
answer_text: The current budget allocation for the Casement Park project, approved by the Executive in 2011, is £62.5 million.  The UCGAA contribution remains at £15 million, while the Irish Government announced in February 2024 its intention to contribute €50 million (circa £43 million).  On 11 June 2025, the UK Government announced its financial commitment of £50million.I will continue to work with UCGAA and other stakeholders to determine the funding available for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) his assessment of the role that the GAA plays in respect of (a) inclusion (b) community cohesion (c) cultural promotion (d) health and wellness; and (ii) a breakdown of all analysis carried out by his Department in respect of the social, community, and economic value of the GAA outside of sport.
answer_text: My Department has not conducted any analysis of the social, community and economic value of the GAA outside sport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total cost to (i) the PSNI; and (ii) the judiciary for (a) policing civil unrest; and (b) prosecuting those suspected of civil unrest, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: I have been advised by PSNI and Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service that expenditure is not recorded in a way that makes it possible to determine the total cost to (i) PSNI; and (ii) the judiciary for (a) policing civil unrest; and (b) prosecuting those suspected of civil unrest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any consideration she has given to taking forward Recommendation 15 of the Hate Crime Legislation in Northern Ireland Independent Review report as part of the Justice Bill (NIA Bill 07/22-27).

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their assessment of whether the Executive's original £62.5 million funding commitment toward the redevelopment of Casement Park, made in 2011, remains an appropriate and viable contribution in light of inflationary pressures and the significant rise in construction costs since that time.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of whether the Executive's original £62.5 million funding commitment toward the redevelopment of Casement Park, made in 2011, remains an appropriate and viable contribution in light of inflationary pressures and the significant rise in construction costs since that time.
answer_text: There remains a significant gap between the cost for current plans for the redevelopment and the money that has been secured to date. As Minister for Sport I have repeatedly said that additional money coming to Northern Ireland for sport must be allocated on a fair and equitable basis, a principle first agreed as part of the 2011 Executive agreement and one I remain firmly committed to in the interests of fairness to all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of whether the Executive's original £62.5 million funding commitment toward the redevelopment of Casement Park, made in 2011, remains an appropriate and viable contribution in light of inflationary pressures and the significant rise in construction costs since that time.
answer_text: The positive allocation of £50 million to Casement Park as part of the Spending Review on 11 June marked a significant step forward for the Executive Flagship Project. The British Government has joined the Executive, the Irish Government and the GAA in the commitment of new and additional funding.	This additional funding commitment brings new momentum to this vital project which will deliver a modern sporting venue for generations to come, as well as being an economic driver for the surrounding communities and region.	I, and my Department, stand ready to play a full and positive role in the next steps to get Casement built. I have written to the Communities Minister seeking a meeting to discuss the delivery of Casement Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the socio-economic benefits of the Casement Park project for (i) the West Belfast constituency; (ii) the Greater Belfast Area; and (iii) the North of Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 8221/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a detailed breakdown of all assessments and analysis his Department has undertaken in respect of the potential (i) economic; (ii) social; and (iii) community, return on investment for Casement Park.
answer_text: These assessments and analysis have been undertaken by the UCGAA as part of their Business Case up to November 2019. DfC also completed these as part of the Casement EURO Outline Business Case in September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the progression of a Temporary Variation in Year 8 pupil intake for St. Paul’s High School, Bessbrook for the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: St Paul’s High School was allocated a Temporary Variation during the post-primary admissions process to permit the admission of an additional 15 pupils.  Given the availability of places in the area there are no plans to provide any further Year 8 places at the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, for his assessment of the impact that the NI Assembly agreeing the Opposing Plans for the Nutrients Action Programme 2026-2029 motion on 10 June 2025 will have on farmers.
answer_text: Having listened to the views expressed during the motion on 10 June 2025, my assessment is that it should encourage farmers to participate in the consultation process. I want to hear the views of everyone on the draft proposals in the Nutrients Action Programme 2026 – 2029 consultation, which will remain open until 24 July. I reiterate that this is a consultation on a set of draft proposals, and no final decisions have been taken. 		Until any new regulations are agreed, the existing NAP regulations will remain in place for all farmers whether they receive subsidies or not.  The purpose of NAP is to protect water quality and promote the efficient use of nutrients based on the Code of Good Agricultural Practice.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of the impact of comments by elected representatives in relation to incidents of violence and civil disorder.
answer_text: No formal assessment has been made of the impact of comments by elected representatives in relation to incidents of violence or disorder.  All elected representatives, however, have an important responsibility when making any public comment to moderate their language and tone and to ensure that they seek to calm any tensions and not escalate disorder within communities.  The amplification of misinformation which is divisive and inflammatory in nature is particularly dangerous and elected representatives should seek to ground their comments in facts, challenge false narratives and do so in a temperate manner and one which is conducive to good relations and the upholding a culture of lawfulness.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail what steps they will be taking to publicly endorse and support police and emergency services in the midst of violence and civil disorder in Ballymena.
answer_text: The Executive issued a joint public statement on 11 June 2025 strongly condemning the racially motivated violence and appealing for calm across our society.  All Ministers offered their support and gratitude to members of the emergency services for their efforts in ensuring that no one has been seriously injured or worse. The full text of the statement can be found at: https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/news/statement-executive-public-disorderThroughout the disorder, the First Minister and deputy First Minister have received daily updates from the PSNI.  They have also been in regular contact with the PSNI and with officials within the department.  They have engaged with the Racial Equality Sub-Group.   TEO are also now leading on the social cohesion stage of the emergency response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the urgent action required subsequent to Environmental Health assessments, to detail when the new pre-school accommodation for Tiny Tots, Clady on the grounds of the neighbouring St. Michael’s Primary School,  will be in operation.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the strategic importance to the Pre-School Education Programme, Tiny Tots, Clady provides. However, it is not my Department’s responsibility to provide grant aid or fund capital works for non-statutory pre-school providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any bids she has made to the Minister of Finance in respect of increased funding for water and sewerage infrastructure; and (ii) what options she is considering to adequately fund NI Water, following a report from the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council that the current funding model is not fit for purpose.
answer_text: I have submitted a bid for £60m capital DEL and £20m resource DEL for water and wastewater infrastructure as part of the June Monitoring process.  These bids have been classed as inescapable. I welcome the report by the NI Fiscal Council.  The recent experience of water companies in England and Wales has demonstrated that privatised and mutualised water companies do not necessarily provide the better levels of services that people expect.  I have been clear that I will not implement any measures that will lead to household water charges on already hard-pressed families.I am progressing my three-pronged approach to address wastewater issues by continuing to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment, consulting on developer contributions; and introducing the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) location of road resurfacing schemes, completed in the Newry and Armagh constituency, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme. Regrettably, for some time, the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all our roads to a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of completed resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year for the Newry and Armagh constituencies are provided in the table below by local section.DfI Roads Section Office AreaNumber of Resurfacing SchemesArmagh City, Banbridge & Craigavon15Newry Mourne & Down (E&W)22Total37The detail on the location of the resurfacing schemes completed in 2024/25 is included in my Department’s report to Council. The planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2025/26 financial year will be included within the Department’s Annual Report which will be presented to Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits of introducing free school meals on a universal basis.
answer_text: Free school meals (FSM) continue to provide vital help to many low-income families. For some children, access to a nutritious school lunch may be their main meal of the day. In addition to providing financial assistance to families in need, FSM play an important role in supporting a child’s health, wellbeing and learning outcomes. Universal FSM provision would widen access to these benefits and potentially help to reduce any perception of stigma associated with FSM. However, there would be very significant additional annual resource requirements to provide universal FSM as well as additional capital funding needed to expand the kitchen and dining hall capacity in schools.My Department continues to face significant funding pressures and the budget allocation for 2025/26 was less than required leaving a considerable shortfall to be managed across the education sector. The issue of affordability is therefore critical in considering any expansion of FSM provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when Ashton Heights, Newry, was last inspected for road surface defects; and (ii) whether her Department has any plans to resurface Ashton Heights.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. I can advise that the most recent inspection of Ashton Heights, Newry was undertaken on 12th March 2024.  Details of the 2025/26 resurfacing programme will be in my Department’s annual report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide (i) a detailed breakdown of the engagement his Department had with the charity and voluntary sector in developing the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy; and (ii) his assessment of the evaluation provided by Barnardo’s NI that the current draft of the Anti-Poverty Strategy is not fit for purpose.
answer_text: Substantial engagement between DfC officials and the Anti-Poverty Co-Design group, which brought together over 20 organisations from the Community and Voluntary Sector as well as Equality and Human Rights Organisations, took place from November 2020 until October 2022. The Co-Design Group’s report was shared with all Departments and has been considered in developing strategic commitments within the Strategy.	I myself have met with representatives from the Co-Design group as recently as 3 June this year.	The Executive has agreed a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy that is affordable, deliverable and which focuses on tackling the root causes of poverty, while continuing to deliver on interventions that will make a long-term difference to poverty in Northern Ireland. I would encourage Barnardo’s, alongside all other stakeholders, to respond to the current public consultation with suggestions and recommendations  on where they feel the draft Strategy could be strengthened.I have asked my officials to allow for a 14-week consultation period, and we will carefully listen to the views of all those who respond. The contents of the final Anti-poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive Approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Education in respect of cross-departmental cooperation to improve processes for obtaining a statement of special educational needs for a child.
answer_text: I am fully committed to working with Minister Givan and welcome the ongoing engagement via the Executive and the priority in the Programme for Government in relation to Special Educational Needs (SEN), recognising the inseparable relationship between Health and Education to support children with SEN to ensure they reach their full potential.My officials continue to work closely with Education to deliver the SEN Reform Plan 2025, including streamlining of Statutory Assessment and Review process.Whilst the Department of Education, alongside the Education Authority (EA), has lead responsibility for the statutory assessment and statementing (SARS) process for SEN, Health plays a key role. The Department, Public Health Agency, and HSC Trusts continue to work actively to identify the needs of children and young people at the earliest possible stage and to respond in a timely manner in providing necessary input as part of the SARS process. This is facilitated through close interdepartmental cooperation and a shared focus on improving outcomes for children and families.The PHA are working closely with HSC Trusts and EA to streamline the statutory assessment and review processes, including improving the secure transfer of relevant information across sectors and enhancing the coordination between health and education professionals. These steps are aimed at reducing delays and ensuring a more integrated and responsive service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail all correspondence she has had with (i) the Irish Government; (ii) Fáilte Ireland; (c) Tourism NI; and (d) Tourism Ireland, in respect of extending Ireland’s Ancient East to include counties Armagh and Down.
answer_text: I met with Peter Burke TD on 12 February 2025, and we agreed that the extension of Fáilte Ireland’s branding into the North is a key collaborative area of mutual interest for our tourism industry. The All Island Strategic Tourism Group, established following agreement by the North South Ministerial Council, optimises broader collaboration on opportunities that will support the sustainable growth of the tourism sector across the island. Its membership comprises officials from my Department, the Department of Enterprise, Tourism and Employment and the Chief Executive Officers of Tourism Ireland, Tourism Northern Ireland and Fáilte Ireland. This group is taking forward consideration of the extension of Fáilte Ireland’s regional branding, exploring how regions here that fit the character of these brands might be incorporated, and how this would strengthen the offering of the regional brands alongside other key priority areas of co-ordination for the tourism sector. This includes the Fáilte Ireland brand, Ireland’s Ancient East. Fáilte Ireland is continuing its own review of the effectiveness of the Ireland’s Ancient East brand. I look forward to hearing the outcome of that review and how we might be able to support its next stage.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to outline their assessment of the merits of a public inquiry into the pollution crime at the Mobouy dump; and (ii) whether they will be calling for a public inquiry.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what representations they have made to ensure aid is able to enter Gaza unimpeded.
answer_text: We are clear that all efforts must be made to address humanitarian issues and ensure aid can get to where it is needed. The position has been conveyed to the Prime Minister, the Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, and the Irish Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the scale of pollution into our waterways caused by the Department for Infrastructure and its arm's-length bodies; and (ii) to detail what correspondence his Department has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to the pollution being caused by her Department.
answer_text: Excess nutrients from a range of sources including sewage / wastewater treatment are presenting a significant pressure on water quality in Northern Ireland. This is primarily due to the excessive enrichment of waters with nutrients such as phosphorus and nitrogen. The Agri-food and Biosciences Institute’s (AFBI) have used source apportionment modelling under the RephoKUs project to estimate total Phosphorus loads from different polluting sources within the Lough Neagh catchment. It estimated that 62% of phosphorus lost to NI waterbodies was from the agricultural sector, 24% from the wastewater treatment sector and 12% from septic tanks. A copy of this report can be viewed at; RePhoKUs Report (Oct 2020). Further modelling of phosphorus for the Neagh-Bann catchment is in progress as part of the new Harmonica project. Also you may be aware that the Office of Environmental Protection (OEP) has initiated a new 2-year Nutrient Programme (2024) that will focus on an evaluation and assessment of wastewater nutrients on the environment in addition to other significant nutrient sources from agriculture. My Department is working with the OEP on this Nutrient Programme. 	I have engaged with the Minister for Infrastructure, most recently on the 2 June 2025, in regard to the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent (SORPI) between the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and NI Water. The NIEA wrote to the Department for Infrastructure and NI Water to provide both with an opportunity to comment on the NIEA’s proposal to withdraw from the SORPI (2007). This consultation will close on 7 July 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the workload implications and equality considerations for teachers required to deliver different lessons to multiple streams within the same school.
answer_text: There has been no formal assessment of the workload implications for teachers required to deliver different lessons to multiple streams within the same school and there have been no equality considerations highlighted.You will be aware that I have commissioned an independent review into all aspects of workload issues of both teachers and school leaders and a panel has already been appointed and is progressing with this review. In addition to this there has been ongoing engagement with the five recognised teaching trade unions to discuss and agree a programme of work to address workload concerns. Through negotiations with trade unions which resulted in the 2024/25 teachers’ pay agreement a number of actions were agreed to be taken forward. Throughout the negotiations and discussions the workload implications for teachers required to deliver different lessons to multiple streams within the same school has not been raised as an issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the steps his Department is taking to address the current shortage of school admissions places in St. Paul's High School, Bessbrook; and (ii) why the spike in the birth rate in the area was not anticipated and factored into local area planning to ensure balanced sufficient capacity in local schools.
answer_text: Many schools across Northern Ireland are oversubscribed with applications every year and as no child can be guaranteed admission to a given school, it is not unusual for children to be placed at lower preference schools.Given the remaining availability of places in Newry, there is no current shortage of Year 8 places in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the admission allocation of children with a statement of Special Educational Needs into schools in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) school; (ii) mainstream or special educational needs; and (iii) sector.
answer_text: The answer has been placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why a special educational needs inclusion agenda is not accommodated and supported equally in all schools.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring that all children, including those with special educational needs (SEN), are supported to access and benefit from high-quality education on an equal basis.  All schools have a duty to identify and meet the needs of pupils with SEN, as set out in the ‘Northern Ireland Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs’.  Inclusion is a core principle of this and schools are expected to ensure the participation and progress of all pupils, including those with SEN.The development of an inclusive education system is a key underpinning aim of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.  As part of this, my department plans to take forward a range of actions to strengthen inclusive practice, including the publication and implementation of an Inclusive System Development Programme and the research, design and trial of inclusion methodologies to promote equitable inclusive learning experiences. Through delivery of the SEN Reform Agenda, my department aims to strengthen inclusive practice across all educational settings and ensure that every child with SEN benefits from greater inclusion and is supported to thrive by getting the right support from the right people at the right time and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what compensation is available to patients who have been injured by pelvic mesh insertion.
answer_text: The Patient Safety Commissioner for England, Dr Henrietta Hughes, made recommendations on redress for those who were harmed by Pelvic Mesh implants in the “Hughes Report”, published in February 2024. Whilst Dr Hughes’ remit was specifically for England, it is acknowledged that patients from across the UK have been affected by these issues.  The three Devolved Administrations are awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health & Social Care (DHSC), however the UK Government has not yet formally responded to the Hughes Report and a UK Government decision on redress has not yet been taken.I wrote to Baroness Merron, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Patient Safety, Women’s Health & Mental Health at DHSC, requesting an update on the consideration of the Hughes Report recommendations and have sought assurances that consultation with the Devolved Administrations will take place in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government decisions. Baroness Merron has informed me that the UK Government is carefully considering the report before coming to a decision. She recognises that any response by the UK Government is likely to have implications for the Devolved Administrations and is keen to work closely with officials across the UK in considering the report’s recommendations. She has provided assurance that my officials and I will be engaged in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government Response.The approach to redress for those who have been harmed by Pelvic Mesh in Northern Ireland will be informed by the UK Government position which is not yet determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to funding requirements for the redevelopment of Casement Park.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline any capital underspend at the Daisy Hill Hospital site in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The capital underspend at the Daisy Hill Hospital of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last ten years is shown below (in £’000s):Year2014-152015-162016-172017-182018-192019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24Capital Underspend £’000621-16-111----During this period, the Department of Health provided funding to SHSCT for specific schemes at DHH, as well as allocations for general capital, backlog maintenance, Invest to Save and Covid funding for SHSCT to use across their estate portfolio. The £111k underspend in 2019/20 related to the late delivery of equipment purchased from general capital for DHH which was subsequently received in April 2020 and funded from the Trust’s 2020/21 general capital allocation.Some of the underspends were used to offset DHH capital overspends in 2016/17 and 2022/23. The total DHH underspend during this period represented 0.2% of the total Departmental capital underspend.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what provision his Department currently offers for free soil testing at farms; and (ii) for his assessment of the benefits of providing free soil testing for farmers to (a) identify the optimum level of nutrients required for maximum grass production; (b) identify deficiencies; and(c) reduce the use of excessive fertiliser.
answer_text: The Soil Nutrient Health Scheme offers every Category 1 farm business in Northern Ireland the opportunity to register for free soil testing, with the scheme providing soil analysis results and CAFRE training on how to interpret the results.  The final opportunity for farm businesses across Northern Ireland to register for the scheme will be this summer. The benefits to farm businesses who register for the scheme include access to the online results viewer which provides information on nutrient deficiency or excess which can assist farmers to pin-point nutrient requirements for every field to maximise grass yield.  Efficiencies may also be identified through, for example, reduced costs where artificial fertiliser is not required, thereby helping farms to improve their profitability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the total number of traffic deaths on rural roads in the last five years; and (ii) what action she is taking to reduce speeding and vehicle related deaths on rural roads.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively with partners to reduce death and serious injuries on our roads, to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.  My Department’s approach to road safety engineering, including the provision of  speed control measures, is outlined in a number of documents that are publicly available to view at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/road-safety-engineering. The PSNI is responsible for the enforcement of limits set by my Department.    My Department can and does introduce lower local limits where we have identified a need to do so.I am acutely aware of the issue of speeding on rural roads, and I have asked that this year’s programme of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools concentrates on rural schools located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on a number of speed related matters including the national speed limit on rural roads, HGV speed limits and 20mph speed limits in general, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise on this important issue.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour, particularly to speeding. Drivers need to view speed limits as a limit, not a target, and drive at speed appropriate to the road and surrounding conditions.  My Department’s public information campaigns repeat the message that speeding is not acceptable.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on capital plans for (i) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) Craigavon Area Hospital.
answer_text: Following my response to your previous request in November 2024 to detail the capital investment plans for Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH) and Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH) over the next five years (AQW 17078/22-27 refers), the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) has been allocated £2 million of capital funding in 2025/26 for the Low Voltage electrical project at DHH to progress enhancement to electrical infrastructure.The Trust continues to develop the long-term plans for the site redevelopment programme at CAH.  Due to the scale of the programme the project will be broken down into phases, and the Trust is currently refining the preferred phased redevelopment approach at CAH.The substantial projected cost of the programme means that affordability will be a key issue, in the context of the challenging financial environment and the scale of demand for other capital investment projects in the Health and Social Care sector.The Trust will be invited to submit business cases for these proposals once budget affordability is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether there should be a public inquiry into clerical abuse.
answer_text: In 2016 the Executive agreed to establish an independently-chaired Inter-Departmental Working Group (IDWG) to take forward work on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Historical Clerical Child Abuse which fell outside the terms of reference of the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry. I understand that on Historical Clerical Child Abuse, the IDWG will report to ministers later this year.  It would be inappropriate for me to comment in advance of the publication of the group's findings and recommendations.This work is being led by the Executive Office and I would advise you to direct any future questions to the First Minister and deputy First Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total number of (i) reports; (ii) charges; (iii) prosecutions; and (iv) convictions for domestic violence, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In relation to the information requested in parts (i) and (ii) of the question, the Police Service of Northern Ireland record information in relation to domestic abuse related offences reported to them and in relation to resultant sanctioned outcomes.  Issues around recording and analysis of data recorded by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the number of reports and charges for domestic violence related offences, as raised in the question, to the PSNI. The Department of Justice collates information in relation to prosecutions, convictions at courts for domestic abuse related offences.  Information on the number of cases where there was a prosecution of conviction for a domestic abuse motivated offence has been provided in the following table.  Prosecutions and convictions at court for domestic abuse related offences, 2020 to 2024YearProsecutionsConvictions20201,44887020212,4861,42120222,9651,73820233,8172,24220243,3371,995Note:1. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures for cases dealt with in 2020 - 2021 include cases where at least one offence was motivated by domestic violence, as assessed by PPS.3. For 2022 onwards, figures include cases where at least one offence was motivated by domestic violence, as assessed by PPS or those relating to the domestic abuse (DA) offence or any offence aggravated by reason of involving DA as laid out under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.4. Figures for cases dealt with in 2024 should be considered as provisional.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the last time her Department undertook river maintenance on the Clanrye River to (a) improve water flow; and (b) address sediment build up; and (ii) the total cost of works.
answer_text: The Department last undertook maintenance on the Clanrye River in June 2021 to ensure the free flow of water. This included the removal of fallen trees, clearance of blockages, and other routine works to maintain the channel’s capacity.While no specific works have been carried out solely to address sediment build-up since that time, the most recent inspection in October 2024 identified minor deposits of silt and gravel, which naturally accumulate in the channel and can restrict the flow of water. A programme of works to address these issues is scheduled for delivery in the 2025/26 financial year, subject to the availability of resources.Maintenance expenditure is not recorded against individual watercourses. Costs are accounted for annually under broader rural or urban watercourse maintenance categories. Therefore, a specific cost for works on the Clanrye River is not readily available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Newry flood alleviation works; and (ii) to detail any works that have been completed to date.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing the Newry flood alleviation scheme. Phase 1 of the scheme was completed in 2023 at a cost of £2.9m. The works included upgrading sections of the existing culvert network on the Armagh and Downshire Roads, an upgraded culvert inlet screen and the construction of new flood walls along the Knox-Pebbles Drain at Greenbank Industrial Estate.Development of Phase 2 continues following the identification of a preferred option in 2024. The preferred option will involve construction of flood defences along the Newry River through Newry City and construction of a flow control mechanism on the Newry Canal to the north of the city. Work on assessing this preferred option in terms of economic and environmental viability has commenced and this stage will include development of an Outline Business Case as well as an environmental screening exercise. The project team are continuing to actively engage with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council regarding the potential for accelerated delivery of a section of the proposed Phase 2 works in the vicinity of Newry Town Hall through the Council's Theatre Conference Centre Project.It is intended that a study on Phase 3 will commence soon which will consider upstream water storage and a further study will commence in autumn 2025 which will consider the tidal flood risk in Newry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will create a register of individuals convicted of domestic abuse with a view to preventing others from experiencing domestic abuse by these individuals.
answer_text: A Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) is already in place. This scheme is intended to empower individuals who are at risk of domestic abuse to make informed decisions about their relationship and their safety. The Scheme was introduced in Northern Ireland in March 2018 with the principal aim of keeping people safe.  It was developed by my Department and is operated by PSNI in conjunction with its statutory and voluntary partners.  DAVDS includes a ‘right to ask’ where individuals, or those concerned about them, can make enquiries to the police, by way of an online application, if they are worried that a partner or ex-partner may have a history of domestic abuse, and they are at risk of harm. This enables the person who is at risk to receive information and make an informed decision (including whether to stay or return to the relationship) to help keep safe.  The police will take the necessary steps to explore the potential risk to an individual and the necessity for disclosure, depending on the particular circumstances of the case.There is also a police ‘power to tell’ pathway to information provision which creates a formal mechanism for the Police Service of Northern Ireland to tell anyone who is potentially at risk of abuse from their partner, about that partner’s past.  If Police checks show that the individual has a record of abusive behaviour, or there is other information to indicate the person may be at risk from their new partner, the Police will consider sharing this information with the person at risk.Whilst not a formal register, the two operate to provide essential information to protect potential victims from domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any underspend by her Department on road resurfacing and maintenance works, in each of the last three years, broken down by division.
answer_text: My Department has fully spent all of the budget allocations made to road resurfacing and maintenance works for each of the past three years.  Therefore, there are no budget underspends in maintaining the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the financial commitment made by (i) her Department; (ii) the Irish Government; and (iii) external funding bodies ,for the Ulster Canal Project.
answer_text: I have not taken decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget as these are subject to equality considerations following closure of the Draft 2025-26 Budget Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) consultation on 5 June 2025.  Budgets beyond 2025-26 have not been agreed, however it is expected that multi-year budgets will be in place from 2026-27 which will allow my Department to consider its longer-term funding position for this scheme.The Irish Government, through the Shared Island Fund, with match funding from the Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage, has committed total funding of €80 million for phase 3 of this scheme.To date there has been no funding commitments from external funding bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any underspend in respect of services for adults with learning disabilities in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts noted the following underspend in respect of services for adults with learning disabilities in each of the last five years, as detailed below:Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):The BHSCT reported no underspend in its Learning Disability budget for the last 5 years. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):In the last five years the SEHSCT reported an underspend in services for adults with learning disabilities as follows: 2020/21- 1.4m, 2021/22- 1.2m and 2023/24 - 1.2m. The SEHSCT also reported overspend in 2022/23 of 0.1m and an overspend in 2024/25 of 4.3m. Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):In the last five years the NHSCT reported an underspend in services for adults with learning disabilities as follows: 2020/21 - 4.337m; 2021/22- 2.578m; 2023/24 - 2.456m and 2024/25 - 617k. The NHSCT reported an overspend of 71k in 2022/23.Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT):In the last five years the Trust reported an underspend in services for adults with learning disabilities in the 2023/24 year of £0.4m and in year 2024/25 of £0.4m.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):In the last five years the SHSCT reported an underspend in services for adults with learning disabilities as follows: 2020/21 - 4.26m and 2021/22- 0.4m. The SHSCT reported overspends in 2022/23 of 1.85m; 2023/24 of 2.60m and a further overspend in 2024/25 of 2.51m.Health and Social Care Trusts attribute underspends to reduced service activity linked to the Covid pandemic, with associated limitations regarding the provision of placements and services. Trusts further advise post Covid, service changes and workforce challenges in terms of staff vacancies and ongoing recruitment pressures, have contributed to more recent underspends. Within Adult Learning Disability Services Trusts also cite increasing growth and demand, alongside increasing complexity of need as factors contributing to overspends within a limited funding envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any underspend by her Department, in respect of rural and community transport services, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There has been no underspend in the last five years in respect of rural and community transport services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the publication of the Newry Southern Relief Road public consultation; and (ii) whether the consultation findings will be published before 5 July 2025.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the public consultation exercise for the Newry Southern Relief Road concluded on 4th March 2025. My department received a number of detailed representations as part of the consultation process, and my officials are working through the information to ensure that all comments are considered as part of a report for my consideration. To ensure that each representation is appropriately reviewed for this important scheme, I would not wish to commit to a timescale for publication.  Instead, I would like to take the necessary time to consider the report in detail before making any announcement on the next steps for the Newry Southern Relief Road scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many incidents of vandalism have been recorded at archaeological and heritage sites, in each of the last five years, broken down by site; and (ii) what action his Department is taking to protect these sites from vandalism and criminal damage.
answer_text: (i) There have been over 300 recorded incidents of vandalism/criminal damage at heritage sites in the last five years. A list of incidents is enclosed as Annex A. Please note that this lists the incidents of which the department is aware and considers involve vandalism/criminal damage. It is possible that there were other such incidents which have not been recorded. Incidents subject to ongoing investigation or court proceedings have not been included. (ii) My department manages monuments in state care, carrying out regular inspections and engaging with the public, local councils and police to help detect, reduce and prevent damage. We also have a team which assists police with criminal investigation of damage to scheduled monuments. Local councils take the lead in tackling damage to Listed Buildings. 		Due to the amount of information it contains, I have arranged for the Annex to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how first time benefits claimants over the age of 65 can access the Motability scheme.
answer_text: First time claimants who are aged over 65 and under State Pension Age can access the Motability Scheme if they receive the enhanced rate mobility component of Personal Independence Payment (PIP) and have at least 12 months of their award remaining. Those over State Pension Age cannot access the Motability scheme as they are not eligible to claim PIP. Claimants who wish to access the scheme apply directly to Motability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact delays in obtaining autism diagnoses have on parents trying to access supports.
answer_text: A delayed diagnosis can deny a child or young person and their family the opportunity to understand the factors underpinning the affected child’s behaviours and the challenges sometimes encountered by the child in navigating the range of interpersonal, experiential and educational contexts central to being a child. It is acknowledged that for families this delay can create undue stress, with significant impact upon family functioning. Frequently children’s responses to stimuli arising in diverse interpersonal contexts and settings can be misinterpreted as poor or challenging behaviour. It is also recognised that this delay impacts those children and young people awaiting assessment across a number of domains including; social relationships, recreation and social mobility and education.It is envisaged that the referral and diagnostic pathways and approaches to intervention underpinning ASD in childhood will be addressed through the implementation of the HSC Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which is currently published for public consultation. In conjunction with the PHA and Child Health colleagues my Department is developing a regionally agreed needs- based neurodevelopmental pathway. This will allow individual needs to be assessed within a regionally consistent framework and will promote opportunities for earlier intervention to be provided without the need for a diagnosis. Thank you for raising this important matter, I trust that this response has been helpful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the status of maternity services at (i) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) Craigavon Area Hospital.
answer_text: Southern Health and Social Care Trust continues to experience workforce challenges within maternity and gynaecology services across both Craigavon Area and Daisy Hill Hospitals. As a consequence of ongoing workforce challenges particularly at Daisy Hill, significant efforts have been made to manage and stabilise maternity services across both sites. This requires constant attention and focus, despite which the situation remains fragile.  A clinical continuity plan has been developed for implementation should that be required.   Also, as an interim measure and to safeguard mothers and babies, as the Trust work through the review process, the maternity team has updated their risk assessment protocols. Women with certain risk factors or presenting issues may be advised to have their birth and, in some cases, full antenatal care at Craigavon Hospital, where there is a greater infrastructure of supporting services e.g. diabetes, surgery, critical care, and neonatology. A project structure has been established to review maternity and gynaecology services and develop a longer term safe, sustainable, best practice model for the local population.The project team, supported by three independent experts is reviewing midwifery and medical workforce and gathering information and data to inform the development of a proposed future service model. A lay reference group has also been established to work alongside the project, to share service user views and feedback. I greatly appreciate the support and understanding of women, families and the public, and flexibility and dedication of maternity and gynaecology staff as the Trust work to improve services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) why there is no legislation for equal protections for children; and (ii) the implications this has for ensuring every childhood is free from harm.
answer_text: The current law in Northern Ireland on the physical punishment of children is based on the concept of “reasonable chastisement”.  Article 2 of the Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 2006 has the effect of restricting the grounds on which the defence of reasonable chastisement can be used.In essence, this means that if a parent or adult smacks a child and is prosecuted, they can defend themselves in terms of “reasonable chastisement” but only provided that the harm is minor.  Anything which causes more than transitory or minor discomfort is unlawful and can result in prosecution.  Consequently, a mild smack is, therefore, still legal in Northern Ireland.The Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health published a report in 2024 on the impact of physical punishment on children and young people. The report found that the use of physical punishment has a negative impact on children’s physical and mental health, as well as their social, emotional and behavioural outcomes. This is why I am fully supportive of changing the law here in Northern Ireland, however, the issues are broader than criminal justice alone, and any change to the law requires Executive agreement.Despite my efforts to do so, and support from expert stakeholders including the NI Children’s Commissioner, I have not been able to secure the Executive agreement needed to deliver a change in the law in this area but I will continue to work with my ministerial colleagues to seek their support to repeal the defence of reasonable chastisement here in Northern Ireland and move towards a position of equal protection for children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) whether his Department intends to introduce bursaries for post-graduate students from Northern Ireland studying social work but who have not been offered a university place here, to allow them to study in England; and (ii) the rationale for this decision.
answer_text: Student social workers who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland can access a non-means tested allowance of £4,000 for each year of their study from the NI Student Incentive Scheme. In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. Payments in relation to the Student Incentive Scheme are in addition to any student finance support, which can be applied for separately.The rationale for restricting the Student Incentive Scheme to social work students who are domicile and studying full-time in NI is that such students are most likely to remain and deliver services here after they qualify. Given the extremely challenging budgetary situation faced by my Department, there are no plans to extend the scope of the social work Student Incentive Scheme at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assurance she can provide to individuals that their freedom to assemble, demonstrate and protest will not be infringed.
answer_text: The right to freedom of speech and freedom of assembly are fundamental human rights and essential in a democratic society. They are protected in law and allow individuals to engage in peaceful protest. These rights are limited, in some circumstances, by the need to uphold the rights of others, protect public health and safety, minimise disruption to normal life and by the need to prevent and detect crime.The policing of demonstrations and protests is an operational matter for the PSNI, accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment on individual policing decisions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have made to the US Administration in respect of protections for the undocumented Irish.
answer_text: This is an Excepted Matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) why Quinn Coaches Limited found out on 27 May 2025 that its transport contract with the Southern Health and Social Care Trust would not be renewed on 1 June 2025; (ii) the timeframe for the tendering process for a new contract; and (iii) his assessment of the implications on the employees and service users of Quinn Coaches Limited.
answer_text: Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) cannot discuss commercially sensitive contractual details involving any individual third-party provider. SHSCT can advise that due to the end of a contract with a provider in the South Armagh area, alternative transport arrangements are being made for some adults attending learning disability, physical disability and older people’s day care and do not anticipate any interruption to services. Key workers are engaging with all service users to advise of new arrangements and minimise disruption. SHSCT thank service users, carers and staff for their support and understanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of post-graduate students from the north, who are studying social work in England and are unable to receive a maintenance loan or bursary; and (ii) what actions her Department will take to rectify this.
answer_text: A postgraduate tuition fee loan of up to £6,500 is available to eligible students from here. Maintenance loans are not currently available to students from the north who are engaged in postgraduate study. My Department’s review of higher education funding will include consideration of the merit and practicability of adjustments to the current package of student support, including support for postgraduate students. Provision of bursaries in disciplines such as social work would be a matter for the Department of Health. The higher education funding review will include engagement with that Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) the funding opportunities available through his Department to support the work of Head Injury Support; and (ii) his assessment of the means by which Head Injury support can secure the necessary funding to continue the work they do.
answer_text: My department has no funding opportunities available to support the work of the Head Injury Support.Other funding schemes may be available, and I would encourage Head Injury Support to access NICVA’s Grant Tracker website (https://www.grant-tracker.org) to find available funding schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his assessment of the implications for children and their families of not being able to access an autism diagnosis on a timeline that facilitates early intervention and support.
answer_text: A delayed diagnosis can deny a child or young person and their family the opportunity to understand the factors underpinning the affected child’s behaviours and the challenges sometimes encountered by the child in navigating the range of interpersonal, experiential and educational contexts central to being a child. It is acknowledged that for families this delay can create undue stress, with significant impact upon family functioning. Frequently children’s responses to stimuli arising in diverse interpersonal contexts and settings can be misinterpreted as poor or challenging behaviour. It is also recognised that this delay impacts those children and young people awaiting assessment across a number of domains including; social relationships, recreation and social mobility and education.It is envisaged that the referral and diagnostic pathways and approaches to intervention underpinning ASD in childhood will be addressed through the implementation of the HSC Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which is currently published for public consultation. In conjunction with the PHA and Child Health colleagues my Department is developing a regionally agreed needs- based neurodevelopmental pathway. This will allow individual needs to be assessed within a regionally consistent framework and will promote opportunities for earlier intervention to be provided without the need for a diagnosis. Thank you for raising this important matter, I trust that this response has been helpful.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount incurred through transitioning MLA offices to new IT and printing systems.
answer_text: Member devices and printers had aged beyond the typical life span (five years), with some reaching seven years old.  Maintaining the devices was becoming increasingly problematic and presenting additional challenges regarding security and patching of systems. Warranties for these devices were no longer in place and fixing devices was becoming piecemeal and increasingly challenging.  To support and improve the service to Members, the Assembly Commission approved projects to refresh the IT equipment. As a result, each Member received an allocation of five devices, five docking stations, five monitors and associated accessories (keyboard/mouse) and a digital ink pen. This equates to approximately £5,950 per Member. The anticipated lifespan of these devices is between five and seven years.The Assembly Commission also approved an uplift of £300 to each Member’s Constituency Office Operating Cost budgets to enable Members to procure their own print capability for constituency offices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a Temporary Variation in pupil numbers will be offered to St. Paul’s High School, Bessbrook for the upcoming academic year.
answer_text: My department has already allocated an additional 15 places via Temporary Variation to St Paul’s High School, Bessbrook for September 2025 admission.  There are no plans to allocate any further places.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) his assessment of the success of the Market Place Theatre, Armagh; and (ii) any plans his Department has to support the 25th anniversary celebrations of the Market Place Theatre.
answer_text: The Arts Council supported the development of the Market Place Theatre and Arts Centre with a capital investment of over £3.6m.  It is a mark of success that the Market Place continues to be a vibrant, creative and active focal point in the historic city.  The Arts Council, an arm’s-length body of my Department, worked with the Market Place to mark the 25th celebrations by bringing a special exhibition featuring artworks from the Arts Council’s Collection through its Art Lending Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the main pressures on his 2025-26 budget position.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Executive agreed the 2025-26 Budget on 3 April 2025. My Department’s Resource budget position reflects a funding gap of £98.6m (-12%) against total forecast requirements; the Capital budget position reflects a shortfall of £161.3m (-38%).My Department faces difficulties in managing its 2025-26 Resource Budget, with £17m of inescapable pressures, relating to pay, Employers National Insurance Contribution increases and inflationary pressures. The Capital DEL budget allocation for my Department falls significantly short of the Departmental 2025-26 requirements, with inescapable pressures of £82.0m. This funding is required to deliver on the key Programme for Government target for Social Housing Development New Build starts, Disabled Adaptations, the Non-ACM Remediation Scheme (Cladding Safety), a Housing Development Grant and the Affordable Warmth Scheme.   In light of the shortfalls facing my Department, I have submitted bids in the June Monitoring round to fund these critical requirements.

answer_text: My Department’s 2025-26 Budget represents an improved position in comparison to previous years, however due to historic underfunding, a number of pressures remain. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with her southern counterpart, since taking up post, in relation to harmonising public transport (i) ticketing processes; and (ii) fare structures, on an all-island basis.
answer_text: Ticketing is an operational matter for Translink, I have not engaged with the Minister of Transport on this issue. However, you will be aware that Translink and Iarnród Éireann are subsidised in different ways and in April 2022 the Irish Government reduced fares on all types of public transport by 20%. For Translink to emulate these reductions would have a major impact on the sustainability of delivering public transport services.  It is also important to recognise that in the South, to qualify for a Senior Smart Pass, a person must be aged 66 and over and therefore in some respects our passengers are better off than their peers in the South.  Translink also continues to work with Iarnród Éireann to provide passengers with the same access to on-line fares and seat bookings providing Flexible, Semi Flexible and Low fares up to 80 minutes before travel on cross border services.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have made to the UK Government in relation to (i) ensuring the delivery of humanitarian aid to Gaza; (ii) an immediate cessation of hostilities; and (iii) the recognition of the State of Palestine.
answer_text: There is no agreed position on these matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total number of complaints received in relation to cross-border train ticketing at Belfast Grand Central Station, since its opening.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Since Belfast Grand Central Station opened on Sunday 13th October 2024, there have been 655,000 passengers using the Enterprise Train services that are provided by NIR. Translink has received 134 complaints relating to Enterprise Fares and Tickets. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department has entered into a licencing agreement with Sinn Féin in respect of its political advertising billboard, illegally erected at the Egyptian Arch, Newry; and (ii) what is the status of planning enforcement on this.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has not entered in a licensing agreement with Sinn Féin in respect of the billboard at Egyptian Arch, Newry.Billboards typically require a planning application, and any breaches of planning laws would generally be addressed under planning legislation, which in most cases falls under the jurisdiction of the relevant District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of unoccupied (i) social homes and (ii) affordable homes in the Newry & Armagh constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:(i) “As of 23rd May 2025, there were 17 actionable* and 40 non-actionable** void Housing Executive properties in the Newry and Armagh Parliamentary Constituency.*Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated/ awaiting change of tenancy repairs. These may also include properties deemed difficult to let. **Non-Actionable voids are properties that are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons i.e. decanting purposes (where they are being used to house a tenant on a temporary basis pending works to their home), planned maintenance schemes, and properties which have been identified for sale/transfer or have approval for demolition.”(i) and (ii) The Department does not hold the requested information for Registered Housing Associations or affordable homes.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) the funding opportunities available through his Department to support the work of the Brain Injury Foundation; and (ii) his assessment of the means by which the Brain Injury Foundation can secure the funding necessary to continue the work they do.
answer_text: My department does not have any funding streams to support the work of the Brain Injury Foundation.You may find it beneficial to access NICVA’s Grant Tracker website (https://www.grant-tracker.org) to check funding which may be available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the implications of the UK-EU trade deal of 19 May 2025 for businesses.
answer_text: The suite of documents published on 19 May represents a step forward towards resetting and rebuilding relations between Britain and the EU, across several areas including energy, the environment and some economic/trade aspects. I am conscious that further detailed negotiation is required to flesh out the details of what are essentially political Heads of Agreement, but I welcome the progress on practical areas such as youth mobility and the SPS veterinary agreement. 		These are steps that will reduce trade barriers resulting from EU Exit, and benefit communities, workers, and businesses across these islands. 			It is positive that the new SPS arrangements, when implemented, will mean that there will be significantly fewer checks and controls on animal, plant and related products moving east-to-west, from Britain to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will be supplementing her Department’s recently announced advertising campaign, highlighting the dangers children face getting on and off buses, with legislation requiring vehicles to stop for school buses that are picking up or dropping off children.
answer_text: I am committed to considering any initiatives that can improve road safety, which is a priority for me as Minister.  On 2 June 2025 I announced my intention to bring forward legislation which will prohibit overtaking school buses which have stopped to drop off or pick up their passengers.  The legislation will not work in isolation but will be part of a package of road safety measures, which I am introducing or have already put in place, in order to safeguard schoolchildren on their journey to and from school in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how much additional funding his Department will be making available for the provision of domiciliary care packages in the 2025-26 financial year.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the rates of (i) economic inactivity; and (ii) unemployment, amongst adults with learning disabilities, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Labour Force Survey provides official statistics on economic inactivity and unemployment.  NISRA has confirmed that due to the sample size for this group being too low to provide a robust estimate, it is not possible to provide a breakdown of economic inactivity and unemployment for adults with learning disabilities in each of the last 5 years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the total expenditure by their Department on: (i) overseas travel; (ii) 5 star hotel accommodation; and (iii) business or first class flights, since the restoration of the Assembly.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what sporting facilities are being proposed by his Department for consideration to access Shared Island Funding, in the next five years.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQO 2001/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what advice his Department has published in respect of the impact of the UK Government’s welfare changes.
answer_text: Since publication of the UK Government’s Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper my officials have been working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to assess the proposed changes and their implications for Northern Ireland. This complex and extensive exercise is ongoing. Pending completion of this analysis my Department has not published any guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the total expenditure by Executive Ministers on: (i) overseas travel; (ii) 5 star hotel accommodation; and (iii) business or first class flights, since the restoration of the Assembly.
answer_text: There has been no expenditure on items i, ii or iii by Finance Ministers since the restoration of the Assembly on 3 February 2024.My Department does not hold this information for other Ministers.

answer_text: A breakdown of the information requested is set out below:Financial YearProgrammeGrant Amount2023-2024Arts Council NI Small Grants Programme£3,120.002022-2023Arts Council NI Annual Funding Programme£9,684.002022-2023Arts Council NI Arts and Older People£7,840.002022-2023Arts Council NI Annual Funding Programme£115,113.002021-2022Arts Council NI Health & Safety Capital Programme£13,902.002021-2022Arts Council NI Arts and Older People£6,174.002021-2022Arts Council NI Annual Funding Programme£115,113.002020-2021Arts Council NI Stability & Renewal Programme for Organisations£52,169.002020-2021Arts Council NI Health & Safety Capital Programme£5,228.002020-2021Arts Council NI Annual Funding Programme£115,113.00My Department also provides funding to the 11 Northern Ireland local councils for the Community Festivals Fund.
