question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the rationale behind the distances used to calculate eligibility for home to school transport.
answer_text: The qualifying distances of two miles for pupils registered at a primary school and three miles for pupils at post-primary school are laid out in the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to the publication of the Residents’ Parking Review report on 29 November 2024, to provide an update on the development of an application and assessment process for the establishment of residents parking zones.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for residents’ parking schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider modifying the wide footpath at the exit from Daisy Hill Hospital on Hospital Road, Newry, to facilitate additional parking spaces for hospital visitors.
answer_text: The exits of Daisy Hill Hospital are restricted with Double Yellow Lines (No parking at any time), that apply to both the carriageway and footway. Traffic Attendants are deployed Monday to Saturday between 08:30 and 18:00 to provide on-street parking enforcement at Hospital Road, Newry. The enforcement patrols are reviewed every six months to confirm they remain proportionate and address the needs of the area.The Department has no further plans to modify the footpath at the exit from Daisy Hill Hospital, as the footpath is a visibility splay for vehicles exiting the Hospital and should be retained as such for safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has plans to make weight loss medications available via the NHS, as in England.
answer_text: The Department of Health has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC). In practice, this means that treatments recommended by NICE for use on the NHS in England are made available in Northern Ireland. NICE has published TA664 (liraglutide) and TA875 (semaglutide) which advises that these treatments are recommended as an option for weight management alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults, however it can only be prescribed in secondary care by a specialist multidisciplinary tier 3 weight management service.  NICE has also published TA1026 (tirzepatide) which is recommended as an option for managing overweight and obesity, alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults, only if they have an initial body mass index (BMI) of at least 35 kg/m2 and at least 1 weight-related comorbidity.I have recently given support for the introduction of a new Regional Obesity Management Service for Northern Ireland which will enable access to these treatments. My Department is currently considering how these treatment options could be prescribed in Northern Ireland to patients for obesity management, with rollout carefully managed in a phased manner to ensure that HSC healthcare professionals can continue to meet the full range of health needs for all their patientsThe initial phase will focus on the development of a community-based service where patients will have access to lifestyle support as well as obesity medication, if clinically appropriate, in line with NICE guidance.Implementation will also require consideration of financial and service requirements including clinical capacity, specialist support and the development of coordinated and sustainable service models.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the evidence base behind the proposed changes to the role of the Taxing Master in the draft Justice Bill; and (ii) the potential impacts of this proposal on accountability and transparency in expenditure of public money.
answer_text: The provision in the Justice Bill to amend the Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 imposes a restriction on the circumstances in which an order for taxation of legal aid costs may be made by the High Court or Court of Appeal.  The restriction will only apply when the Department has developed and implemented a remuneration order made under Articles 12(3) and 47 of the Access to Justice Order (NI) 2003.  Article 12(3) grants the Department the power to “by order make provision about the payment of remuneration by the Department to persons who provide civil legal services”.  The Civil Legal Services (Remuneration) Order 2015 sets out the remuneration framework for solicitors and counsel who provide civil legal services. Remuneration payable is determined by the Legal Services Agency, except for remuneration in the High Court and Court of Appeal which is currently determined by the taxing master.  When a remuneration framework has been developed for High Court and Court of Appeal cases, the provision in the Justice Bill will remove any potential scope for a representative to argue that the High Court or Court of Appeal retains some inherent power to order taxation of legal aid costs.The proposed amendment underpins taxation reform, and the long-term objective of the Department to reform non-criminal legal aid which has been recommended by the NI Audit Office, the Public Accounts Committee and in the Access to Justice report. Any revised remuneration framework for legally aided cases currently subject to taxation will be set out in remuneration orders made under the Access to Justice (NI) Order 2003.  Those remuneration orders are secondary legislation and are subject to the normal statutory consultation and legislative processes. By enacting reform in this way, expenditure currently taxed will be subject to normal government audit and accountability mechanisms which currently apply to all other legal aid expenditure. This will help deliver increased predictability, accountability and transparency to legal aid expenditure and ensure that public funds are not vulnerable to unfunded increases to fees and rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) live; (ii) lapsed; and (iii) tendered domiciliary care package contracts in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the North South Ministerial Council Inland Waterways meeting on 12 May 2025; and (ii) to detail any agreed actions that were taken in relation to developing (a) Newry Canal; and (b) the Carlingford Lough Region, as a tourism destination of excellence.
answer_text: The Joint Communique provides a record of decisions taken at the NSMC Inland Waterways meeting on 12 May 2025. It was published after the meeting and is available at the following address: https://www.northsouthministerialcouncil.org/publications/InlandWaterwaysJointCommuniqué12May2025There were no actions taken in relation to (a) Newry Canal, or (b) Carlingford Lough Region as they do not fall under the remit of Waterways Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the engagement she has had with (i) Fáilte Ireland; (ii) Tourism Northern Ireland; and (iii) Tourism Ireland in relation to the Shared Island Unit’s allocation of up to €23 million for the development of sustainable tourism amenities in the Carlingford Lough region.
answer_text: The Shared Island Tourism Destination projects, including the development of sustainable tourism amenities in the Carlingford Lough region, have been progressed by the local authorities, communities and the three tourism agencies. At this early stage, engagement continues with all stakeholders. I meet with Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI officials and board members on a regular basis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any recent engagement they have had on the progress to redevelop Casement Park.

To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits of reviewing the eligibility criteria for free school bus passes to include: (i) a means-tested eligibility criteria for pupils living within 3 miles of their school who are not currently entitled to free school bus passes; and (ii) students whose home is within 3 miles of their school, but their nearest bus stop falls outside of the 3 mile radius.
answer_text: The aim of the Home to School Transport Policy is to facilitate attendance at school by ensuring that all school aged children may access a suitable school regardless of where they live and it is not a means tested policy. Eligibility for transport assistance is based on the distance from a pupil’s home to their school and the qualifying distances are laid out in legislation. The distance from the bus stop to the school is not used in the determination of eligibility for transport assistance.A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. The transport policy underpins other policies within my department such as school attendance and is a key support for many pupils with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND).  Any amendments to the transport eligibility criteria would need to complement the work of the SEN Reform Agenda and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision. The cost of delivering the current school transport service for over 90,000 pupils is significant at over £120 million in 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the reasons for the significant increase in traffic on the Dublin Road, Newry; and (ii) to detail what steps her Department is taking to address this issue.
answer_text: My Department is committed to improving the road network to address safety and the needs ofall road users.A scheme to upgrade the traffic signals at the A2 Dublin Road junction with Dominic Street / Bridge Street / The Glen / Drumalane Road, Newry commenced in March 2025. Upon completion, the upgraded traffic signals will operate under specialised software to optimise traffic progression through this major junction as well as improve pedestrian safety. The project is expected to be completed by mid-June 2025. Officials were aware that during these works, there would be some disruption to traffic, however work is restricted to off peak hours and queues are being monitored and adjustments made to the traffic signals to minimise delays.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the level of funding that his Department has allocated to Foras Na Gaeilge in the last 12 months.
answer_text: ANSWERIt is the responsibility of sponsor departments, both North and South, to jointly develop and agree budgets for Foras na Gaeilge. The agreed provision for the relevant contributions North and South respectively will be funded from the budget of the Sponsor Department in the relevant jurisdiction. On this basis, responsibility for funding for Foras na Gaeilge is primarily a matter for the Minister for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will undertake a review of the traffic light sequencing at the Abbey Way, Abbey Yard, and William Street junction, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  My officials are unaware of any traffic signal sequence issues at this location. However I can confirm that they will arrange for the traffic lights at this location to be monitored to evaluate the efficiency and operation of this busy junction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans she has to extend discounted, unlimited use day tickets for Translink passengers outside of Belfast, similar to those available for Metro and Glider passengers.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink. I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink offers unlimited day travel through the iLink integrated smartcard.  iLink allows unlimited travel on scheduled bus and train services within the chosen zone (5 zones available). Daily, Weekly and Monthly options are available for each zone as well as offering both Adult and Child versions.  There are also travelcards available for customers travelling on Ulsterbus Town Services which provide unlimited travel for a day, week or month.    Translink is planning to roll out Account Based Ticketing across the Bus, Rail and Glider network as part of the Translink Future Ticketing Systems Project.  This is already in place on Metro bus services in Belfast where customers reach the Daily cap of £4 when they make a second journey in any day. The plan is to implement the same automatic fare capping on all services, providing good value fares to customers both on a daily basis and also for fixed weekly travel.    For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for a detailed breakdown of all environmental assessments that have been undertaken in respect of the Newry Southern Relief Road Project; and (ii) for her assessment of the environmental impact of the Project at (a) Flagstaff; (b) Benson’s Glen; (c) Barracric; and (d) Fathom.
answer_text: My Department has published a complete Environmental Impact Assessment Report, which includes a Habitats Regulations Assessment.  This has been made available through the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/newry-southern-relief-road-draft-statutory-orders-and-eiar The areas of Flagstaff, Benson’s Glen, Barracric and Fathom are included within this study.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the process for planning approval in relation to the Newry Southern Relief Road Project.
answer_text: The Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (NI) 2015 sets out the types of development that can be undertaken without the requirement to submit a planning application. Part 23 of this Order provides Permitted Development status for major road development schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a detailed breakdown of all (i) preparatory works that have been undertaken; and (ii) expenditure that has been incurred by her Department to date, in respect of the Newry Southern Relief Road Project.
answer_text: A full detailed breakdown of the preparatory works that have been undertaken by my Department as part of the Newry Southern Relief Road scheme is available on the scheme’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/newry-southern-relief-road.The total scheme expenditure at the end of the 2024/25 financial year was c.£8.1m.This comprises of development costs, including topographical surveys, archaeological and ground investigation works, of c.£7.3m and land costs of c.£0.8m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many children with learning disabilities are not accessing the full range of support services to which they are entitled, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. For my Department to gather this information this would incur a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures which are being implemented in: (i) Primary; and (ii) Post-Primary schools, to ensure all young people who come through our education system are equipped with the knowledge and skills to stay safe in and around water.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland swimming is a statutory area of the curriculum within the Physical Education (P.E.) area of learning. The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 states that Key Stage 2 pupils should be enabled to develop basic swimming and personal survival skills, understand the importance of personal hygiene in relation to pool use and progress from using a swimming aid to developing their confidence and competence in being able to swim without the use of any aids using recognised swimming strokes. In post-primary Key Stage 3, the Minimum Content Order states that pupils should have opportunities to build on and increase their knowledge, understanding and skills through frequent and regular participation in a balanced programme, including in swimming.However, it is a matter for schools and the relevant Board of Governors to determine the operational aspects of how they actually deliver swimming, including the frequency and timing of the lessons provided to children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to introduce legislative amendments to ban controlled burning during (i) nesting season; and (ii) during periods of Met Office wildfire warnings.
answer_text: I have asked officials to explore the approach to burn periods in Northern Ireland. Future consultation on the matter shall provide an opportunity to provide feedback including with regards to (i) and (ii) if those responding so desire.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any advice his Department offers: (i) The Consumer Council for Northern Ireland; and (ii) Community Advice Clinics, to enable them to assist consumers in engaging with Private Parking Companies who issue penalty charge notices on private land.
answer_text: My Department does not have the policy remit for this issue and therefore is not in a position to offer any advice to the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland or Community Advice Clinics to enable them to assist consumers in engaging with Private Parking Companies which have issued penalty charge notices on private land.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans on attending (i) Newry Agricultural Show; and (ii) Armagh County Agricultural Show this year.
answer_text: I am happy to consider all invitations to agricultural shows and, diary permitting, am looking forward to visiting as many shows as possible this summer.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the position of his Department in respect of the Camogie Association players call for choice around wearing comfortable attire during matches.
answer_text: I have no position on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the impact that an increase in university tuition fees will have upon the ability of students from disadvantaged background to access third level education; and (ii) whether an equality impact assessment has been undertaken by her Department in respect of the proposal to increase tuition fees.
answer_text: I recognise the financial pressures that students are under given the cost-of-living crisis. My Department recently uplifted maintenance loans by 20% to help student with these pressures. Fees will only increase at the usual level of inflation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department holds responsibility for gully maintenance in (i) Hickey’s Lane; and (ii) Drumgullion Avenue, Newry.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for the maintenance and cleaning of gullies on adopted roads in the North, which includes urban and rural areas.Our records show Hickeys Lane in Newry is unadopted therefore the Department has no maintenance responsibilities for this road, including for gully cleaning.Drumgullion Avenue in Newry is adopted by the Department, and therefore the Department is responsible for the gully maintenance of this road.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to educate children and young people about the harms and downstream health implications of regularly consuming energy drinks.
answer_text: Energy drinks can be high in caffeine but can also contain high amounts of sugar. The Eatwell Guide recommends that foods that are high in fat, salt and sugar are not essential in the diet and therefore should be consumed less often and in small amounts. The Eatwell guide, and associated resources provided by the Food Standards Agency (FSA), are used by schools in Northern Ireland to make healthier eating easier to understand.The FSA has also published standards for healthier and more sustainable vending at https://www.food.gov.uk/business-guidance/healthier-and-more-sustainable-vending. These voluntary standards recommend that energy drinks should not be available for sale in vending machines. The FSA is working with local government and wider public sector officials to implement guidance in vending machines in public sector sites that children regularly attend, including leisure centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the road surface of Meadowbrook Park, Newry, was last inspected; and (ii) whether road resurfacing works are scheduled to take place in Meadowbrook Park.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. My Department carries out a programme of regular cyclic inspections of all roads, including the Meadowbrook Park, Newry, which was last inspected on 8th July 2024.Details of the 2025/26 resurfacing programme will be in my Department’s annual report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.Officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to repair the road surface of the Old Red Lion Road, Armagh, to facilitate a scheduled triathlon event.
answer_text: My Department has not been approached with a request to repair the road surface of the Old Red Lion Road, Armagh, to facilitate a scheduled triathlon event.  If details are provided to the local maintenance office in Armagh, and resources and time permit, every effort will be made to address any actionable defects ahead of the event.Applications to hold special events on public roads should be made to the relevant local council in a timely manner to ensure other road users can be informed and that there are no works planned which may impact the event

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any engagement he has had with the National Trust since the restoration of the Assembly in respect of providing funding for enhanced (i) human resourcing; (ii) organic waste management; and (iii) facilities development, at Derrymore Woods, Bessbrook.
answer_text: My Department has had no engagement with the National Trust since the restoration of the Assembly, in respect of funding to enhance human resourcing, organic waste management or facilities development at Derrymore Woods.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any engagement he has had with the National Trust since the restoration of the Assembly in respect of a coordinated response to incidents of vandalism and antisocial behaviour at Derrymore Woods, Bessbrook.
answer_text: My Department has had no communication with the National Trust in relation to incidents of vandalism and antisocial behaviour at Derrymore Woods, Bessbrook.The investigation of potential criminal offences linked to these behaviours does not fall within the remit of my Department but rather that of the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI). Any incidents of criminal damage or anti-social behaviour should be reported to the PSNI by calling 101 or by reporting online at www.psni.police.uk.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any engagement she has had with the National Trust since the restoration of the Assembly in respect of (i) traffic management; and (ii) road safety in Derramore Woods, Bessbrook.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Assembly, my Department has not engaged with the National Trust regarding traffic management and road safety in Derramore Woods, Bessbrook.In July 2022, the Facilities Manager of Derrymore House informed the Department that the National Trust, in collaboration with the Council, had implemented a one-way system at the site. This alteration has however created difficulties for coaches attempting to exit, particularly when cars are parked on the opposite side of the road. Consequently, the National Trust requested that double yellow lines be marked at this location to alleviate the issue. Although the Department requested further information from the National Trust regarding the specific exit, this has not been received. However, my officials have indicated that it would not be appropriate to provide waiting restrictions in the form of double yellow lines opposite the private exit on Derrymore Road. If vehicles are parking on the footway in such a manner which causes an obstruction, then the PSNI should be contacted directly regarding this matter.I can also confirm that road safety in Derramore Woods would be the responsibility of the National Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total number of annual visitors to Derrymore Woods, Bessbrook in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Derrymore Woods is not owned or managed by my Department. The National Trust own the site and manage the visitor facilities at Derrymore Woods in conjunction with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of the investment made by his Department in Derrymore Woods, Bessbrook in each of the last ten years, broken down by funding stream.
answer_text: My Department, through the Rural Development Programme (RDP) Co-Operation Scheme provided grant funding of £378,733 to upgrade facilities at Derrymore Woods, Bessbrook in 2021/22.  In addition, in 2020/21, Newry, Mourne & Down District Council were granted £15,000 funding under the Water Quality Improvement Strand of the Departments’ Environment Fund. The project entitled ‘Delivery of water resource management programme for the Ring of Gullion’ covered a wide area which included Derrymore Woods.I trust you find this information helpful.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the recruitment and retention of consultant obstetricians at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust is actively engaged in addressing the recruitment challenges for Consultant Obstetricians at Daisy Hill Hospital. Southern Health and Social Care Trust will be advertising for 2 Consultant Obstetrician and Gynaecologist posts for Daisy Hill Hospital week beginning 19 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps she is taking to enhance the (i) availability of taxis; and (ii) sustainability of the taxi industry.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, operator and vehicle licencing. The recruitment of new and retention of existing drivers is the responsibility of the taxi industry.  My department has two main functions that currently impact on the availability of taxi drivers: setting maximum fares and awarding taxi drivers’ licences.My department increased the maximum fare for taxis in December 2023 and since the introduction of the test for taxi drivers, the Driver Vehicle Agency has made a number of changes to the theory test including lowering the overall pass mark for the theory test and providing revision guides.In working with the taxi industry, to address the range of issues it currently faces, I was also pleased to recently announce a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the reforestation of (i) Slieve Gullion Forest Park; and (ii) Gosford Forest Park.
answer_text: My officials have advised that land adjacent to Slieve Gullion forest, which was previously leased by Forest Service until 2022, is currently undergoing reforestation by the landowner.   I understand this includes planting trees and natural regeneration with native species on the lower slopes and the development of native flora on upper slopes in line with an environmental management plan. The reforestation of areas at Gosford Forest, where clearance of trees damaged by Storm Éowyn is currently ongoing, is scheduled to commence in the forthcoming 2026 planting season.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the law in respect of underage patrons being served non-alcoholic beers, wines and spirits at licensed premises.
answer_text: The Licensing (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 regulates the sale and consumption of alcoholic drinks in licensed premises in Northern Ireland.  There is nothing in liquor licensing legislation which prohibits a person under the age of 18 being served non-alcoholic beers, wines and spirits in licensed premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of individuals that have been (a) cautioned; (b) charged; and (c) convicted, for participation in Palestinian solidarity protests in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The recording of information on the number of individuals charged for offences is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct this part of your question to the PSNI. In relation to the information requested in part (a) and part c) of the question, if a protest is un-notified or does not comply with relevant notice conditions, an individual taking part in such a protest may be prosecuted under the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998.  However, Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information on the nature or purpose of the protest in which such an offence occurred.  Therefore, from information held in Departmental datasets, it is not possible to identify those specifically convicted of, or receiving a caution for, participation in Palestinian solidarity protests.However, information has been provided for the total number of convictions and diversionary cautions issued for any offences under the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998 in the period 2020 - 2024.Convictions and out of court diversionary disposals for offences under the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998, 2020 - 2024YearConvictionsDiversions2020                        3                      * 2021                      38                      8 2022                      17                      - 2023                      41                    43 2024                      24                      4 Note:Figures for convictions relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction at court or diversionary caution issued for at least one offence under the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998.Figures for 2024 are provisional.     ‘*’ means a figure of less than 3 has been treated under rules of disclosure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department’s (i) policies; and (ii) assessment procedures in respect of enhanced road safety and traffic control measures at schools.
answer_text: My Department primarily uses its policy and procedures guide ‘RsPPG E070 - Road Safety at Schools’ for the assessment and implementation of road safety measures at schools which is publicly available at: https://www.northernireland.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070-v3 .  As well as this we can employ a range of other measures intended to improve road safety which can also be used at schools such as traffic calming, speed limits, waiting restrictions etc, the policies for which are also publicly available via the following link:  https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/dfi-roads-policy-statements. I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the 2025/26 financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies and obviously considering the safety of introducing such measures.  I intend to make an announcement on plans for the next tranche in the coming weeks.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.  To ensure that others’ views are considered in the setting of speed limits here I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year.My Department has also recently carried out a public consultation on a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan.  The draft network plans for our larger towns and cities prioritise routes to key destinations including schools.  Officials are reviewing the responses received to the public consultation with a view to presenting a final plan for my consideration in the Autumn.As part of the work on the Plan, officials are also updating design guidance for active travel. This includes the development of a toolkit of measures that can be considered by designers around schools aimed at encouraging more people to consider walking, wheeling or cycling for the school journey.  It will include a range of measures such as the provision of greater priority for pedestrians at crossings in the vicinity of schools and measures to discourage inconsiderate parking that can cause difficulties for those walking or wheeling. Reduced speed around schools is also important, and 20mph speed limits are an additional measure that could be introduced as part of a particular active travel scheme, depending on whether the main issue is one of speeding motorists, congestion at school times, or inconsiderate parking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline any engagement her Department has had with St. Theresa’s Primary School, Tullyherron, since the restoration of the Assembly in relation to road safety concerns; (ii) when her Department last undertook a road safety assessment at the school; and (iii) for her assessment of the benefits of enhancing road safety measures at the school.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users, particularly near schools.The Department has had no engagement with St. Teresa’s Primary School, Tullyherron since the restoration of the Assembly.The location was reviewed for increased signs and a decrease in speed limit in March 2024, however, taking into account the latest collision history, these are considered appropriate at this time and in line with policy.A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools . The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.   I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies and obviously considering the safety of introducing such measures. Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools is included within my budget plans for the next financial year.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.  I have also asked that consideration be also given to the national speed limits that apply on rural roads and HGV speed limits.To ensure that other’s views are considered in the setting of speed limits here, I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when High Street, Bessbrook, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there are there any circumstances where individuals participating in lawful protests expressing support for Palestine would be subject to police or court action.
answer_text: The policing of demonstrations is an operational matter for the PSNI, accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment.Decisions on prosecution rest with the Public Prosecution Service as prosecuting authority in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the annual budget provided by her Department for (i) road resurfacing; and (ii) road repairs, in each of the last five years, broken down by DfI Division.
answer_text: Road resurfacing and road repairs are included in Capital and Resource Structural Maintenance expenditure as set out in the Table below for each of the last five financial years, by DfI Division. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the progress she has made in addressing road safety concerns on the Crieve Road, Newry.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. Details of the safety improvement schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the merits of introducing legislation to amend the Traffic Management Order (Northern Ireland) 2005 to enable local councils to implement their own parking strategies; and (ii) whether she will commit to making those legislative changes in the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: I am however aware of a legislative anomaly pertaining to the ability of Local Councils to set their own off-street parking tariffs under The Off-Street Parking Order (Northern Ireland) 2000. My officials are engaging with representatives from the Councils in order to progress a legislative solution, using powers in the Local Government Act 2014, that would disapply the provisions of the 2000 Order for Councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Lady Chief Justice’s ruling on 2 May 2025 in relation to the Sean Brown public inquiry order.
answer_text: It would not be appropriate for me, in my role as Justice Minister, to provide any assessment of the recent Court of Appeal judgment as the case is a matter for the UK Government and it also remains subject to ongoing legal proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of local councils that have re-profiled funding for City and Growth Deal projects.
answer_text: For projects falling under the responsibility of my Department, only one Council has re-profiled City and Growth Deal funding following its initial approval by the British Government and the Executive.  In April 2025, Derry City and Strabane District Council approved the reallocation of £1.3m City Deal funds from the Walled City project to the Derry on the North Atlantic, DNA museum, both of which are within the Derry City and Strabane Deal tourism and regeneration pillar.  		An updated business case outlining the additional funding for the DNA Museum is progressing through the final stages of approval within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on negotiations to secure a fair pay settlement for further education lecturers.
answer_text: I am pleased to update that I was able to facilitate a multi-year fair pay offer as a further step towards parity with teachers. The Further Education college employers have communicated this pay offer to their lecturers and to Trade Union officials as part of on-going negotiations. I hope this pay offer will be accepted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the boundary wall of the Demense on the B77 Redlion Road, owned by his Department, will be repaired.
answer_text: My Department has frequently undertaken repairs to the boundary wall on safety grounds, as a result of third-party traffic incidents causing damage. My officials will inspect the wall for any current damage which may pose risk to public safety and undertake repairs as appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department will improve access to NHS dentistry services.
answer_text: I acknowledge the challenges facing many patients, particularly those not registered with a dental practice, in accessing dental care when they need it.Access to Health Service dentistry remains a priority for me, and my Department has specifically targeted investment at improving access for priority groups.An investment package of £9.2m has supported General Dental Services in 2024/25, including:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme. There are currently 29 practices participating in the Dental Access Scheme.The Dental Access Scheme commenced on 1st August last year and has since allowed nearly 11,000 high-need patients to receive treatment. The scheme is funded until 2027.The ECE scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay.	From its reintroduction in June 2024 to March 2025, over 33,800 newly registered children have been seen under the scheme.The 30% enhancement applies to Item of Service fees associated with fillings, extractions and root canal treatment. Instances of these treatments being provided have increased while the enhancement has been in place.I am currently considering investment options for 2025/26 with decisions having to be made in face of a projected funding gap of £400m for my Department.While these additional investment interventions are necessary to stabilise General Dental Services in the short term, I am clear that, in common with other services, it will require sustained effort to ensure sustainability over the longer term. The first step is the commissioning of a cost of service review, to be completed in 2025/26, which will inform the future of General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting time for children's (i) ADHD assessments; and (ii) ASD assessments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of successful prosecutions of historical sexual abuse cases in each of the last ten years; (ii) the average sentence for a historical sexual abuse conviction in each of the last ten years; and (iii) any consideration she has given to introducing stronger sentences for historical sexual abuse convictions.
answer_text: Departmental prosecutions datasets do not hold information that allows historical sexual abuse cases to be identified, so it is not possible, from information held, to provide an answer to the issues raised in parts (i) and (ii) of the question.In relation to part (iii), it is the role of the independent judiciary to decide on the appropriate sentence in individual cases.  In coming to their decisions, they must take into consideration all the facts of the case including any mitigating or aggravating factors, any relevant sentencing guidance, and the maximum penalty available for the particular offence.  It would be a breach of Article 7 of ECHR to increase the maximum penalty available for any offence which has already been committed, and accordingly I have no plans to do so.I do, however, share concerns about sentences for serious sexual offences and have recently tasked my officials to scope a new sentencing review to consider this issue along with a range of other sentencing matters which have also caused me concern over recent months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the classification of Pregabalin as a Class C drug.
answer_text: The policy and legal framework relating to the Misuse of Drugs Act is reserved to the UK Government. Prescription drug pregabalin was classified as a Class C controlled substance under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 in April 2019 following advice from the Advisory Council of the Misuse of Drugs and a UK Government consultation.  Its classification means it is illegal to possess pregabalin without a prescription and illegal to supply or sell the drug to others.I have not been made aware of any plans to review the classification of pregabalin.  Any review of, or change to, the current classification would be led by the UK Government and would require the engagement of all four UK Administrations.   The Department of Health leads on the Northern Ireland Executive’s Substance Use Strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery.  That strategy focuses on ensuring that people in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of harm and stigma related to the use of alcohol and other drugs; have access to high quality treatment and support services; and will be empowered to maintain recovery.  My role as Minister of Justice is understandably focussed on the criminal justice response: that includes enforcement of the law, and actions aimed at reducing the level of drug-related harm and offending in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Secretary of State in relation to the UK Government’s decision to challenge the Sean Brown public inquiry ruling.
answer_text: The family of Sean Brown deserves access to the truth about what happened to their loved one. I am very aware of the hurt and disappointment felt by the Brown family as they continue to seek answers. I have made clear in my discussions with the Secretary of State that it is incumbent upon the UK Government to address the needs of those affected by the legacy of our past.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new (i) social homes; and (ii) affordable homes, that have been built or acquired in Newry and Armagh in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of social homes delivered is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. We have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised as follows.In the Newry and Armagh areaThere have been 555 Social Housing Units Completed in 2015/16 - 2024/25. The detail of these units is provided in Table 1 and 2 below.Table 1YearTotal SHDP Completions2015/16342016/17162017/1832018/19512019/20142020/21392021/22702022/23462023/241372024/25145Total555The Northern Ireland Co-Ownership Housing Association (Co-Ownership) remains the Department’s primary delivery partner for intermediate housing, a form of affordable housing. Co-Ownership does not build homes, rather it operates on a DIYSO (Do It Yourself Shared Ownership) basis, where customers select an existing or new build property of their choice to purchase anywhere in Northern Ireland through its shared ownership product, Co-Own. Table 2 below sets out how many Co-Own purchases have been made in the Newry and Armagh constituency in the last 10 years, and how many of these were new buildsTable 2YearTotal Homes PurchasedNew Builds2015/1624112016/1719112017/1829182018/1945252019/2039232020/2146162021/2240152022/232682023/2429112024/25276

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, and subsequent to the Police Ombudsman’s report on 29 April 2025, whether they will be calling for a public inquiry into the Kingsmills Massacre.
answer_text: We remain committed to doing what we can to meet the needs of victims and survivors. The Victims and Survivors Strategy emphasises the vital importance of truth and justice, and the need to foster greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma associated with the tragic events in our past. The Strategy also recognises the importance of providing ongoing support to victims, survivors, and their families as they move forward from their experiences of the past. We will continue to support victims and survivors through initiatives such as the Advocacy Support Network, delivered by community organisations funded by the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18596/22-27, and subsequent to the First Minister’s commitment that Casement Park will be built on her watch, for an update on the progress made to redevelop Casement Park.
answer_text: As Minister with overall responsibility for the Casement Park project, your question has been passed to me to respond to. I have been working closely with Ulster Council of the GAA and other stakeholders to explore options for progressing the project. I understand that a definitive UK Government decision on funding for Casement Park may not be known until the UK Government Spending Review is concluded in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of parking spaces available for (a) staff; and (b) patients and visitors at Daisy Hill Hospital and Craigavon Area Hospital; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to increase the number of parking spaces available at both hospital sites.
answer_text: There are 75 dedicated parking spaces available for staff at Daisy Hill Hospital and 74 dedicated parking spaces available for staff at Craigavon Area Hospital.In addition, there are 467 parking spaces available for patients and visitors at Daisy Hill Hospital and 2082 parking spaces available for patients and visitors at Craigavon Area Hospital.The Southern Health & Social Trust has referenced additional parking spaces at Daisy Hill Hospital as a capital priority, however this will be subject to securing the required funding in the context of the severe funding pressures facing the Health and Social Care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Aughanduff Road, Mullaghbawn, will undergo road recovery works as outlined in the DFI Roads Southern Division 2024-25 Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.
answer_text: I can confirm that the road recovery works outlined in the 2024-25 Annual Report to Council are complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of Newry’s controlled parking zone status; and (ii) whether she has any plans to review this status to allow for a period of free on-street parking.
answer_text: My Department’s controlled parking zone policy seeks to manage demand for parking in an appropriate way, deterring all day parking and improving customer access to businesses by encouraging an ongoing turnover of parking spaces. The controlled parking zones in Belfast, Lisburn and Newry continue to help manage traffic and ensure that urban centres can be shared spaces for everyone. My officials continue to work with councils to produce tailored, evidence-based Parking Strategies as part of the forthcoming suite of Transport Plans, which will recognise the role that controlled parking zones can play in managing ongoing demand. I have also asked my officials to explore a number of measures to potentially provide greater flexibility to my Department’s approach to controlled parking and I await the findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will answer AQW 26010/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 26010/22-27 was answered on the 30 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any extra allowance or mitigation measures in place to assist pupils who are sitting public examinations when a parent or sibling (i) is having treatment for a life-threatening or terminal illness; and (ii) has recently passed away.
answer_text: The Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ) oversees a framework and provides guidelines within which all its member Awarding Organisations (AO) across England, Wales and Northern Ireland operate.  JCQ has processes in place, agreed by all members, in relation to special consideration and also, where appropriate, reasonable adjustments, for pupils facing extraordinary circumstances.  Operating within the JCQ guidelines ensures consistency and fairness for all pupils.In advance of any examination series, I would encourage any parent or guardian of a family where there has been a bereavement, or where a family member is undergoing treatment for a terminal illness, to talk to their child’s school in the first instance.  In turn, recognising the sensitivity and uniqueness of each set of circumstances, the school can contact the relevant AO for advice and guidance on how to ensure the student is best supported in the most appropriate way.More information on the detail of JCQ access arrangements, reasonable adjustments and special consideration, is available via the following website link: https://www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/access-arrangements-and-special-consideration/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the timeline for reintroducing the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will commence in June 2025 and will give patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to HSC in Northern Ireland.  Patients will only be able to access the Scheme once they have waited on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for two years.The new Scheme will be initiated on a phased approach.  The first phase will allow reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (RoI). The second phase will be introduced at a later date and will see the geographical scope of the Scheme expanded to all countries in the European Union (EU).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any funding opportunities available to nursery schools seeking to upgrade their outdoor spaces.
answer_text: The constrained capital budget position has meant that the Department’s priorities for investment are keeping schools open, children safe and providing places for our most vulnerable learners. Regrettably that means there is currently no budget available to provide financial support for nursery schools to upgrade their outdoor spaces, unless a health and safety risk exists. All nursery schools were provided with a funding allocation from the Outdoor Learning Project earlier this year enabling them to purchase the furniture and equipment they most needed to enhance the outdoor learning experience of their pupils.  This was one of the first projects rolled out under my Curriculum-led Capital Programme which aims to ensure that every pupil benefits from capital funding.I will continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained investment in our education system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the current provision of immigration law training to legal professionals.
answer_text: The Law Society and Bar Council are responsible for the training of solicitors and barristers here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Maximum Aggregate Student Number Cap, including whether this limits an educational institution's ability to grow and access for students.
answer_text: Due to the significant level of public funding provided to the universities, the Department has to control spending to ensure it stays within its budget allocation. The Maximum Student Number (MaSN) limits the Department’s financial exposure and ensures that funding per place is maintained at a level appropriate to sustain quality. The MaSN, therefore, plays a crucial role in balancing the growth of educational institutions with the available public funding.However, recognizing the need for educational institutions to grow and thrive, my Department maintains a dialogue with all of the higher education institutions on the full range of issues affecting their sustainability and growth.  My Department increased the number of funded undergraduate student places at local universities by 596 for academic year 2024/25.  This included increased places to reflect the Executive's commitment to support the expansion of Ulster University's Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will engage with the new lead investigator in the Independent Commission for the Location of Victims’ Remains to assist in the search for the Disappeared.
answer_text: We welcome the appointment of Eamonn Henry as the new lead investigator in the Independent Commission for the Location of Victims’ Remains (ICLVR) and look forward to engaging with him. We wish Mr Henry well as he takes up this important role to locate the remaining Disappeared and bring long awaited closure to the families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the implications that Brexit has had upon the availability of cancer drugs and cancer treatment.
answer_text: From 1 January 2025, all medicines released onto the Northern Ireland (NI) market are authorised by the UK regulator, the Medicines Healthcare Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and packaged on a UK-wide basis. Therefore, my Department is not anticipating implications on the availability of cancer medicines that relate specifically to  EU Exit. However, following EU Exit and until Windsor Framework arrangements for medicines came into force on 1 January 2025, novel and innovative medicines, including some cancer treatments, were authorised differently in NI to those in Great Britain (GB). As medicines were authorised and packaged separately, there were some authorised medicines that were not available on the NI market. However, a mitigation was in place and if there was a clinical patient need and NI compliant stock wasn’t available, but GB compliant stock was available, then the medicine could be added to the NI MHRA Authorised Route (NIMAR) list. Addition to the NIMAR list permits the supply of GB authorised medicines into NI to meet patients’ clinical needs, ensuring patients in NI have equitable access to medicines as those in GB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the provision of modern slavery and human trafficking training for legal professionals.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Law Society and Bar Council are responsible for the training of solicitors and barristers here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the approach adopted in the education system to equip children and young people with the skills and resilience to navigate social media in a way that does not adversely impact their mental health and wellbeing.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum includes elements of digital literacy and online safety, which is helpful in relation to social media, within the cross-curricular skill of Using ICT, the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding area of learning at primary level and the Learning for Life and Work area of learning at post-primary level.My officials are working with the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) and the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app to ensure that any issues with social media are appropriately addressed. In addition, CCEA also offer on their website a range of resources covering online safety and digital literacy, including the ‘Digital for Life and Work’ non-statutory guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any extent to which her Department’s (i) funds; and (ii) resources were used in relation the construction of the illegal Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has provided no funding or resources in relation to the construction of the billboard at the Egyptian Arch railway bridge at the Camlough Road Roundabout in Newry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact that the removal of housing intimidation points will have on housing allocation waiting times.
answer_text: As I outlined in my announcement, the removal of intimidation points will enable all those who are victims of violence, harassment or trauma for any reason, including domestic abuse, to access the same categories of points in the scheme. The decision ensures that the points for violence or threat of violence in the scheme will not exclude any victim of abuse, violence or trauma. That will level the playing field and enable the Housing Executive to award primary social needs points based on the impact on victims, regardless of the cause.I have also sought input from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows: “A total of 226 Applicants were awarded Intimidation points during 2023/24 out of a waiting list of 47,312 households, accounting for less than 0.5% of the Waiting List. Rule 23 and Intimidation Points were removed from the Scheme effective from 1 April 2025. A snapshot taken on 11th April 2025 indicated that there were 114 Applicants remaining on the Waiting List with Intimidation Points. Therefore, given the low numbers of households who were awarded Intimidation Points, the removal of these points will have no significant impact on waiting times.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they have taken to ensure that the spending of Executive Departments is compliant with human rights law and that no public monies are directly, or indirectly, funding the Israeli government.
answer_text: The principles for managing public resources in Executive departments and across public sector organisations are set out in Managing Public Money NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of (i) the economic potential of the Newry Canal; and (ii) how that potential can be fully explored.
answer_text: My Department’s remit on the Newry Canal, where it is designated between Scarva and WIN Industrial Estate in Newry, extends only to the drainage function of this waterway. Consequently, my Department has not carried out any assessment on the economic potential of the Newry Canal.Newry Canal is owned by Newry, Mourne and Down Council and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Council. Any assessment of the economic potential of Newry Canal may rest with these Councils to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the rebuilding of the retaining wall on the Ballynaclosha Road, Newry.
answer_text: I can advise that a two-phased project at Ballynaclosha Road commenced in Autumn 2024.  Phase One works to rebuild the retaining structure to the rear of the filling station are now complete.  Phase Two works which include the reinstatement of the Ballynaclosha Road and completion of accommodation works for adjacent residents and landowners are well underway.  It is anticipated that this project will be completed later this year which will allow the road to be reopened.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will respond to correspondence from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council requesting the establishment of a multi-agency taskforce for the regeneration of the Newry Canal.
answer_text: Neither I, nor my predecessor Minister O’Dowd, have received any correspondence from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council requesting the establishment of a multi-agency taskforce for the regeneration of the Newry Canal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will be responding to correspondence from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council requesting the establishment of a multi-agency taskforce for the regeneration of the Newry Canal.
answer_text: I have not received correspondence from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council requesting the establishment of a multi-agency taskforce for the regeneration of the Newry Canal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will be undertaking a review of the allocation of flood recovery funding to ascertain why the majority of it was returned unspent.
answer_text: Whilst Conor Murphy allocated up to £10 million from his 2024/25 budget to deliver three schemes, the cost of delivering the schemes was lower than anticipated.  There has, however, been no flood support funding returned to the Department by local councils.  Adequate funding remains with the councils to allow them to pay grants to eligible businesses up to the value of their letters of offer providing the businesses submit the required documentation within the specified timeframes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) why more than half of the funding provided for flood recovery in Newry was returned to her Department; and (ii) whether her Department anticipated such under-utilisation of the funding.
answer_text: When coming into office, Conor Murphy allocated up to £10 million from his 2024/25 budget to deliver three flood schemes.  Analysis undertaken at the time, using survey data provided to the Department by Newry Mourne and Down District Council, indicated that the cost of delivering the three schemes could be up to £10 million.  However, the final cost of delivering the schemes, and in particular the enhanced scheme, is lower than the maximum anticipated. There has, however, been no flood support funding returned to the Department by local councils.  Adequate funding remains with the councils to allow them to pay grants to eligible businesses up to the value of their letters of offer providing the businesses submit the required documentation within the specified timeframes. When it became clear that the cost of delivering the schemes would be lower than anticipated, Minister Murphy asked officials to explore options for utilising any unused funding, including options to transfer funding to other Departments for flood related spend, but given the time frame available, no realistic options were identified. DfE officials are currently exploring options for implementing a more permanent flood support scheme for businesses.  A cross departmental group is also being established to explore options for future flood schemes to support recovery and resilience for all those impacted by flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland report on the findings of the Review of Effectiveness of Part 1 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: I welcome CJINI’s second annual report into the effectiveness of Part 1 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.  This was the first piece of legislation that I brought through as Justice Minister, which demonstrates its importance.  I was clear then and I am clear now that domestic abuse, in all its forms, is wrong and will not be tolerated in Northern Ireland.  I accept that creating a new offence will not of itself tackle domestic abuse.  We need a concerted effort across criminal justice agencies, to ensure that the legislation is properly implemented and used.This Report provides us with an independent, unbiased view of how the legislation has been implemented.  It notes that positive action has been taken across the entire justice system and I welcome this.  However, it also notes where more needs to be done and I urge all criminal justice agencies not to lose focus and to continue efforts to implement the recommendations CJINI has made.  This is the only way that we will see real progress.Reasonable progress was found to have been made against the three recommendations made for my Department:  •	In relation to Operational Recommendation 1, my Department recognises the importance of promoting the statutory guidance on the domestic abuse offence amongst staff working in operational roles and efforts are ongoing in this regard.  The outcome we are hoping to achieve through this promotion is a shared understanding of the legislation, across both statutory and non-statutory organisations, to ensure consistent terminology is adopted, and implementation is as effective as possible.  •	In relation to Operational Recommendation 2, despite my Department’s further promotion of the package, CJINI noted that a total of only 3500 NICS staff had completed the e-learning as of 31 July 2024.  Feedback received has indicated that the e-learning is too long and repetitive. My officials are therefore working with Women’s Aid to produce an easily accessible, summarised and refreshed version of the e-learning package. This revised package will again be promoted to all Northern Ireland Civil Service employees, and a range of other public sector staff with access to the LInKs platform.  My officials have also held discussions with the Department of Health to discuss how this could be promoted across Health and Social Care staff and with the Executive Office to discuss its availability and relevance in the context of the response to Violence Against Women and Girls.     •	Operational Recommendation 6 recommended that the legislation should be amended so that there is an assumption of personal connection, which can be challenged in appropriate circumstances.  My officials have taken the views of the PPS, who are supportive of this proposal.  However, there is limited scope and capacity to make such legislative provision within the remainder of this Assembly mandate, but we are progressing preparatory policy thinking by seeking the vi

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any enforcement measures available to his Department in relation to contaminated water in Carlingford Lough.
answer_text: The NIEA takes all reports of pollution very seriously and is committed to tackling water pollution across NI, including Carlingford Lough, pursuing offenders in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy. In addition to formal prosecution, under the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, other enforcement actions available to the Department include the issuing of Warning Letters, Notices, Cost Recovery actions and Breaches of Cross-Compliance for agricultural incidents. NIEA actively encourages the public to report water pollution issues to it using the NIEA Incident Hotline on 0800 807060.My Department’s Enforcement Liaison Group has engaged with the Fisheries Bill team who are reviewing enforcement across a range of policy proposals to inform the draft Fisheries Bill. This will include the introduction of fixed and variable monetary penalties for non-compliance with certain offences and the increase in some maximum penalties including for water pollution. My Department is engaged with officials from the Department of Justice on this issue and it is hoped that the policy consultation will take place in late June.  NIEA continues to investigate all reports of water pollution and apply robust and proportionate enforcement action where appropriate. For minor water pollution offences, NIEA’s key objective is to ensure any pollution identified is stopped. In these cases, NIEA will work with the polluter to prevent recurrence and seek a return to compliance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the merits of introducing legislation to prohibit overtaking stationary school buses, similar to existing legislation in the United States of America.
answer_text: I am committed to considering any initiatives that can improve road safety, which is a priority for me as Minister.  At this stage, I have asked my officials to look at all the options that may be available to improve bus safety for school children and to report back to me as a matter of urgency; following which I will decide on next steps. I also want to explore wider road safety measures, such as speed limit reductions, including 20mph zones in residential areas and outside schools. I have also asked my officials to reinforce the messaging to help keep all road users safe, especially the vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the additional funding that enabling charity lotteries to operate in the North, on the same basis as they can in Britain, would bring to the voluntary and community sector.
answer_text: My department is not responsible for overall funding models or financial support for the charity sector.  It is therefore not in the department’s remit to assess the benefits to the voluntary and community sector of enabling charity lotteries to operate in Northern Ireland on the same basis as they can in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any enforcement measures available to her Department in relation to contaminated water in Carlingford Lough.
answer_text: The NIEA, within the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, is the environmental regulator and has the relevant enforcement powers to address water pollution to protect and improve the water environment.  There are some general powers, under the Water and Sewerage Services (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, which provide NI Water with a power to close a drainage misconnection, where a foul water flow is connected in error to a surface water drain, as the contaminated water could pollute a river or waterway. In addition, my Department’s Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill will provide NI Water with an additional power to repair a drainage misconnection, should the property owner refuse to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to enable charity lotteries to operate in the North, on the same basis as in Britain, given the opportunities for additional funding and the current financial challenges which the charity sector is facing.
answer_text: Nothing in NI law prohibits GB lotteries from operating in NI provided they adhere to the requirements of the relevant NI legislation. I have no plans at present to change this position.
