question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a timeline for the implementation of Project Gigabit.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract.  Those suppliers who met the published criteria of the selection questionnaire have now been invited to submit a response to the requirements of the Invitation to Tender for the project by 24 April 2025. The procurement is seeking a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit shortly after the above date, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of fire hydrants across the water network; and (ii) the policy used to decide where they are located.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have contacted  NI Water and have been advised that it currently has 105,431 fire hydrants on its Corporate Asset Register.NI Water has advised that fire hydrants should be located at strategic points throughout the distribution system, taking into account the location of pockets of housing and other critical customers, as well as considering the requirements of the Fire Authority.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the findings of the Newry Southern Relief Road public consultation will be published.
answer_text: The public consultation on the Newry Southern Relief Road closed on 4th March 2025.  My officials are currently reviewing the responses received during the consultation process to compile a detailed report for my consideration.Following my consideration of this report, officials will arrange for its publication on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has requested the assistance of the Irish Air Corps to assist the Fire and Rescue Service in fighting the recent gorse fires.
answer_text: NIFRS evaluated the situation on Tuesday 8 April 2025 regarding the potential need for assistance from the Irish Air Corps. However, after assessing the fire and receiving practical advice at the scene, it was determined that the involvement of the Irish Air Corps was not necessary due to the specific circumstances of the fire.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she has given to mandating contributions from utilities companies for road and street resurfacing schemes, where those companies have engaged in underground works in the area.
answer_text: Utility companies are required to reinstate the road to the standards set out in the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition). Where a utility company fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the utility company is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and The Street Works (Amendment) (Northern Ireland) Order 2007 make provision for my Department either to require a contribution from street works undertakers towards the cost of resurfacing of a street, or to require a street works undertaker to resurface a street, with the Department making a contribution towards the cost. These provisions have not been brought into operation, and no regulations have been made. I have asked my officials how the Department could bring these into operation. However, they do not apply to cases of failure of utility companies to reinstate the road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has made an assessment of the annual cost savings to her Department in relation to road and street resurfacing if multi-year budgets are put in place.
answer_text: The advantage of multi year budgets to my Department lies with the ability to provide certainty around future budgets and workloads. Such certainty would permit planning and the development of internal capacity through investment in staff, training and equipment. It would also provide funding and workload projections for the Contracting sector who deliver a considerable portion of my Department's workload. Such certainty allows Contractors to invest in the staff, training and equipment needed to reliably deliver high quality works in a more robust way than is possible under single year budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any legislative mechanisms available to her Department in relation to requiring road and street surface conditions to be (i) maintained; and (ii) improved, following underground works by utilities companies.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.If an undertaker does not reinstate the street in a satisfactory manner, my Department applies the procedures set out in the Code of Practice for Inspections.Undertakers remain responsible for the permanent reinstatement for a minimum period of two years, increased to three years for a deep excavation.There is no requirement for an undertaker to improve the street surface beyond the immediate area of their excavations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of personal injuries that have arisen from defective footpaths and street surfaces in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Not all instances of personal injury due to issues with the adopted road network are reported to my Department. Officials have, however, interrogated the management information available on roads related personal injury claims and the relevant public enquiries reported via the ‘Report a Fault’ System. Table 1 below details the number of personal injury claims recorded in each of the last three full financial years, where the cause of the incident has been identified as either a pothole, or other issues relating to the adopted road network. Table 12022/20232023/20242024/2025Number of pothole claims:143162171Number of claims for other issues:538504528Table 2 below details the number of enquiries members of the public have logged on the ‘Report a Fault’ system where the words ‘injury’, ‘injuries’ or ‘injured’ were referenced against the following enquiry types:pothole; damaged uneven surface;other surface defects (e.g. broken flag / kerb);manhole cover missing;  sunken manhole cover; andmud and debris.Table 22022/20232023/20242024/2025Pothole:85137139Damaged uneven surface:1Other surface defects (e.g. broken flag / kerb):499883Manhole cover missing:10112Sunken manhole cover:482Mud and debris:243

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of instances when utility companies have conducted underground works on roads and streets within 12 months of resurfacing works being carried out by her Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The NI Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) provides an essential tool for communication of works notices between the utility companies and the Department. NISRANS is a vital component of the process and is used to coordinate works by utility companies and resurfacing works carried out by my Department. Road restriction notices are placed on NISRANS when resurfacing schemes are planned giving utility companies an opportunity to respond advising if they have any planned works. Should any works promoter start to enter a works notice on a street the system provides a warning of proposed resurfacing works or road restriction.There are however a number of exemptions when the twelve-month restriction following resurfacing works does not apply:- emergency or urgent works; - works not involving breaking up the street, repairing, resetting and replacing manhole or chamber covers and frames, resurfacing up to 20 square metres, pole, lamp, column or sign replacement, pole testing, and works of a similar nature;- works required to respond to a request for a new service or supply to a customer which was not received at a time when it was practicable for the works to be done before the period of restriction began; and - other works to which my Department has given its consent (which must not be unreasonably withheld).NISRANS does not report on the number of works which proceeded following resurfacing works. It is designed to alert utility companies to planned resurfacing works and when a road restriction is in place with the intention of avoiding road openings in a recently surfaced street whenever possible. NISRANS contains a full noticing history for works allowing manual checks to be made on individual works should any investigation be required as to whether works have been undertaken during a road restriction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how much more efficient the health service will be following the new encompass patient data system.
answer_text: The encompass programme is a major transformational change with an ultimate goal to empower patients and improve their access to care. The system will also support an increase in efficiency and capacity across HSCNI. Evidence from other UK sites who are ahead of us on their Epic implementation journey show significant improvements including additional first time outpatient appointments, increased inpatient volumes, faster turnaround times, reduced diagnostic waiting lists, improved patient flow, improved referral time to triage and time to first consult and reduction in length of stay and DNAs (did not attend). As the system stabilises and is embedded across HSCNI we would aim to see similar benefits on a regional scale.Additionally, the system has significantly boosted community care efficiency. The introduction of mobile tools like Rover has streamlined community documentation, enhancing governance of care regardless of location. Community staff now have a clearer overview of patient’s health needs and ongoing treatments, with immediate access to other professional’s notes, ensuring better continuity of care. This has been particularly beneficial in bridging social and clinical care.  Moreover, the ability to review patient charts from anywhere has expedited patient care and decision-making. Improved communication pathways between acute and community services have led to better resource utilisation. Appointment booking processes have also improved, preventing double bookings and ensuring smoother service delivery. With the introduction of the MyCare Patient Portal, services users will also see greater efficiencies in terms of correspondence, appointment management and communications with those involved in their care journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the implications of the separation of obstetrics and gynaecological specialisms.
answer_text: The separation of obstetrics and gynaecology services varies across different Health and Social Care Trusts. Some Trusts operate these services with separate consultant rotas, while others combine them. This organisational structure is not uniform across all Trusts, which suggests that the implications of separating these specialisms can vary depending on the specific operational and staffing strategies of each Trust. This could imply that the separation or integration of these services might impact the flexibility and efficiency of healthcare delivery, potentially affecting patient care depending on the specific needs and resources of each Trust and service area.Moreover, the separation might influence recruitment and staffing strategies, as Trusts may need to consider different expertise and staffing levels for each specialism if they are separated. This could also affect the continuity of care for patients who may require services from both specialisms.In summary, the separation of obstetrics and gynaecology specialisms within Trusts can have varied implications, potentially affecting the organisation and operation of services, staffing, and patient care. Each Trust's approach to managing these specialisms should ideally be tailored to meet the specific healthcare needs of their patient population while ensuring efficient and effective service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale behind the proposed redeployment of the pre-operative assessment team from Craigavon Area Hospital to St. Luke’s Hospital, Armagh.
answer_text: Despite working at full capacity, it has been long acknowledged by the pre-operative team that the service is not meeting demand. Their current accommodation within the Craigavon Area Hospital site is spread across various departments, making communication and efficiency very difficult within any given session. Co-locating the team together in one building will decrease the amount of time colleagues spend walking between rooms to communicate and share information and ultimately enhance their overall working conditions. The St Luke’s site has more accessible and free parking facilities and very pleasant outdoor surroundings and walkways should staff wish to use at breaktimes. Improved communication and efficiency of the team’s working practice will enhance the standard of service they can offer to their patients. It is anticipated that patients will be able to park at the St Luke’s site much more easily than Craigavon Area Hospital and appointments should be shorter.  There will be no change to the range of services that the team can provide from the planned new location in St Luke’s Hospital site, Armagh. The proposed relocation of the pre-operative assessment service aims to improve working conditions for staff and ensure a more patient centred service. There will be no change to service provision and no patient safety issues have been identified. It is anticipated that the new arrangements will support the team in offering a more professional and effective practice. The management team have very openly engaged with staff and Trade Union colleagues throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23360/22-27 to detail (i) the basis on which she arrived at the understanding that the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, has been removed; and (ii) whether this remains her understanding.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my answer to AQW 24558/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the encompass system integrates with patient data systems across these islands.
answer_text: The encompass system offers secure and seamless sharing of patient and service user data with authorised users across HSCNI. Key integrations include connectivity with GP systems for routing of GP letters, imaging and lab results to GP systems.Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) for ambulance booking and arrival management services. Northern Ireland Digital Identity Service (NIDIS) to correctly identify patients across all Health and Social Care systems.Northern Ireland Picture Archive and Communication System (NIPACS+) for regional Radiology and Digital Pathology services.Northern Ireland Pathology Information Management System (NIPIMS) for regional laboratories and blood production and tracking services.Regional clinical trial services.In addition to this, EpicCare Link is HSCNI's secure online tool for connecting referring clinicians, clinical staff, social care workers, and other approved third parties to HSCNI's encompass electronic health record system.EpicCare Link provides access to patient information such as hospitalisations, specialist referrals, and real-time clinical data. This supports comprehensive and coordinated care delivery for cross-border services like the All-Ireland Children's Heart Centre. Read-only access is granted to individuals approved through regional Information Governance protocols.The Neurology inquiry recommended that all healthcare providers, both public and independent sector, have full and unhindered access to the integrated Electronic Care Record to enhance patient safety. Progression of this broader access requires regional policy. Work led by DHCNI within the NI Department of Health is underway with projected timescales forecasting approximately 18 months to complete. In the interim, encompass will continue to collaborate with Trust SIROs, the encompass Information Advisory Council, the Information Commissioners Office, and other key stakeholders to ensure that individual access requests are thoroughly impact assessed and evaluated within the framework of continuity of care, patient safety, and robust information governance, technical and cyber controls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the annual increase in average home insurance costs in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Regulation of the Home Insurance sector is not a devolved matter and my Department does not have authority to regulate the sector. However, I did press the Association of British Insurers (ABI) on the need for local council level data on the cost of home insurance, which is not currently available, at my meeting with them on 13 February 2025. The ABI indicated to me that they will raise this directly with their member organisations and my officials continue to follow up on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how much has been spent by her Department on compensation for damage to vehicles as a result of road surface defects, since February 2024.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline whether they have any plans to convene a Citizen’s Assembly to consider any impacts arising from constitutional change.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the Financial Conduct Authority in relation to (i) insurance market integrity; and (ii) the conduct of insurance companies, particularly in respect of rising insurance premiums in the North.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 25583/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether it is lawful for insurance companies to institute blanket increases in home insurance premiums across entire postcode areas, on the basis of elevated natural risk without differentiation or consideration of mitigating factors.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 25583/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any recourse that home insurance customers have in respect of exponential annual cost increases.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 25583/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) legal; and (ii) regulatory framework governing the conduct of insurance providers in the north of Ireland.
answer_text: Regulation of the insurance sector is not a devolved matter and my Department does not have authority to regulate the sector, or the detail of that. The legislative authority lies with the British Treasury and the two regulatory bodies in London, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and the Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA). The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 and subsequent legislation sets the overarching framework under which financial services are regulated. However, I have pressed the Association of British Insurers (ABI) on the need for the industry to do all it can to control the cost of insurance at our meeting on 13 February 2025. The previous Finance Minister also raised concerns around the rising costs of insurance and the impacts on consumers at the Inter-Ministerial Group for Transport on 19 November 2024 and directly with the FCA, Chief Executive, on 20 November 2024. Should consumers feel they are unhappy with an insurance product, the Consumer Council and ABI both advise that they should raise this with their insurer in the first instance. If they are not content with the response, they can then escalate this to the Financial Ombudsman Service, whose role is to settle complaints between consumers and businesses providing financial services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to mitigate the economic impacts of the UK Government’s electronic travel authorisation scheme on the (i) tourism sector; and (ii) hospitality sector.
answer_text: I am ambitious to grow the value of our tourism economy to mitigate any impacts of the ETA Scheme. In January my Department published a 10 Year Tourism Vision & Action Plan, which set a Strategic Goal that the economic value of our tourism expenditure from overnight visitors will exceed £2 billion by 2035.  The Vision and Action Plan outlined 17 actions to drive sustainable, regionally balanced economic growth in the sector, including: Incorporating local regions into Failte Ireland’s regional brands; Developing peace tourism;Ensuring an adequate mix of accommodation right across the north;Developing skills in the industry;Development of Destination Stewardship Plans; andRestoring appropriate funding to Tourism Ireland to increase tourism revenue from overseas. It has launched exciting international marketing plans for 2025.In addition, my officials and colleagues from Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI continue to engage with Home Office and other partners, to inform their development and roll-out of a targeted and bespoke communications strategy, particular to Ireland travel.We have also been working alongside industry representatives and key stakeholders regarding the potential impact on our tourism sector from the introduction of the ETA Scheme. This has included close engagement and discussions with representative bodies including the NI Tourism Alliance (NITA) and the Inbound Tour Operators Association (ITOA). The implementation of the ETA scheme is already having a damaging impact on the local tourism sector. NITA is reporting the impacts on its members, especially tour operators, with tours and trips being cancelled and the North being removed from itineraries due to the requirement for visitors to have an ETA.NITA and ITOA are jointly working with other industry stakeholders to further assess the impact that the requirement for an ETA is having on businesses offering travel to the North. This will add to its evidence base to support its call for an exemption for tourists travelling from the South into the North. I wrote to Seema Malhotra MP, Home Office Minister for Migration and Citizenship last month in advance of the scheme going live for EU Nationals on 2 April. I urged Minister Malhotra to reconsider the implementation of the ETA scheme including the potential to introduce an exemption to protect our valuable tourism industry, as proposed by the NI Tourism Alliance. I also communicated that the scheme is already having a damaging impact on our local tourism sector. I will continue to press the British Government on this issue, to reiterate my own strong concerns, and to seek a response to the correspondence issued to Minister Malhotra from Minister Murphy in December and January which sought a pragmatic solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that the PSNI online child sex abuse unit is adequately resourced to meet demand.
answer_text: The allocation of policing resources is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of both organisations.I remain committed to working with the Chief Constable, the Minister of Finance and my Executive colleagues to ensure that we have a Police Service that is funded appropriately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to funding the expansion of the free hot water project currently being piloted by the Housing Executive and EnergyCloud NI.
answer_text: EnergyCloud NI is a collaboration across the energy generation, transmission, network distribution and supply sector. Its purpose is to utilise wind energy which would otherwise be unused to provide free hot water to families living in social housing.  In its role as the Home Energy Conservation Authority for Northern Ireland, the Housing Executive is working with EnergyCloud NI to enable this. This pilot of up to 20 dwellings in Omagh was put in place to assess the customer’s experience of EnergyCloud NI’s opportunity.  The EnergyCloud NI team will evaluate the outputs over an 18-month period which will help inform the potential for future scalability. Further development would be considered in line with this evaluation and other funding commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure greater utilisation of the energy generated by renewable energy sources; and (ii) for her assessment of the merits of redirecting unused energy generated from renewable sources to households living in fuel poverty.
answer_text: Making the most use of the renewable energy we can produce is a priority of mine.  It will assist us in meeting our target for renewable energy and decarbonisation, enhance the green economy, provide good jobs in our regions and ensure we deliver a just transition to the people of the North.Through the Executive’s Energy Strategy, my Department is engaging with partners in industry, government and the Utility Regulator on a range of measures which will help to utilise available renewable energy here.  These include developing research to support policy development in areas such as storage and interconnection, working in partnership to support grid development, increased electrification in activities currently dominated by fossil fuels such as transport and heating, and taking steps to improve the way in which we plan and use the electricity grid through consulting on initiatives such as smart metering.     My Department continues to engage with SONI, in the production of their Dispatch Down Draft Action Plan.  This plan aims to improve how we use the renewable electricity, which is available to the market, but which at times cannot be utilised due to factors such as grid constraints and supply and demand reasons. 	In the North we have some of the highest levels of fuel poverty in these islands, and it is a priority for me to reduce this.  Officials from my Department are working closely with the Department for Communities in the development of a fuel poverty strategy. 		Directing unused energy generated from renewable sources to households living in fuel poverty is an interesting concept, which was pioneered in the South and is being trialed here and in Scotland.    		I am supportive of trials here, being led by the Housing Executive, to further understand the issues. I am currently engaged with the promoters of the concept to understand its wider feasibility in the North. The evidence gathered from this trial will be important to inform policy development as we move to integrate more renewables into our energy mix.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current number of adult day care and respite facilities operating in the Southern Health and Social Trust area; and (ii) the number of funded adult day care and respite places in the Southern Health and Social Trust area.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with individual assessed need. HSCTs utilise both statutory and independent sector providers to provide day care and respite/short breaks for adults. HSCTs have procedures in place to optimise capacity within the resources available to them. Day CareThe Southern HSC Trust (SHSCT) have 14 statutory day care facilities and 5 independent providers for people with Learning Disabilities or Physical Disabilities.10 Learning disability day care facilities (statutory)- 355 places 4   Physical disability day care facilities (statutory)- 47 places 5   Independent day care facilities - 62 places The Trust also avails of 8 standalone day services registered to provide day care for people with Dementia offering 783 places per week for people over the age of 65. The Trust also contracts day care services for people with Dementia through 1 independent sector provider offering 28 places per day.Respite/ Short BreaksThe Southern Trust provide short breaks through 14 contracted beds with residential and nursing facilities funded to provide 420 bed nights per month. The SHSCT also have 1 statutory short break facility with 8 beds providing 225 bed nights per month.  The SHSCT contract two short break beds on a rotational basis with two private nursing homes for older persons known to memory services. Further short breaks are sourced on a needs basis at any time dependent on bed availability within the nursing homes contracted to the Southern Health & Social Care Trust. It is noted that there is a variance in how available places are reported within each service, this is directly related to how services are delivered within contractual arrangements. My Department remains committed to working with HSCTS and other agencies to offer day care and respite/short break provision to adults and their families/carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for her assessment of the impact of current rating structures on the competitiveness of town and city centre businesses; and (ii) to detail any consideration she has given to (a) rates reform; and (b) rates relief for town and city centre businesses.
answer_text: There are a range of factors impacting on the competitiveness of town and city centre businesses, primarily outside the rating system. My Department, however, took the initiative on this area, commissioning research from Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) to provide an independent evidence base on the cost of doing business locally, as well as to gain a robust assessment on the specific impact of the Autumn Budget on local businesses. My Department will be following this work with two strategic reviews of key rate reliefs following requests from business stakeholders – those reviews will look at Small Business Rate Relief and Non Domestic Vacant Rating Exclusions.The business rates system here is based on rateable values that reflect the rental market. Those valuations have been updated more than anywhere else on these islands, with Land & Property Services revaluing properties in 2015, 2020, 2023 and a fresh revaluation now underway to come into effect next year. Our non-domestic rates system also provides over a quarter of a billion pounds in rate support each year to ratepayers, including 30,000 small businesses that receive Small Business Rate Relief in a taxbase of 75,000 properties.  The system remains our only substantive revenue raising lever for public spending, and every pound provided in new rate relief has to be paid for either by other ratepayers in the system paying more or by reduction in public spending.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the roads currently scheduled for resurfacing works in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including those in the Newry and Armagh constituency, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing programme of works for the 2024/25 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-southern-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-24-25.pdf https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-to-armagh-banbridge-and-craigavon-borough-council-2024.pdfDetails of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be published in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to address the volume of sewage and untreated wastewater being discharged into waterways.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges from NI Water’s wastewater treatment works and collection systems to the water environment under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. These regulatory consents require NI Water to treat sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution.Where NI Water falls below the standards of its Discharge Consent and / or where the company has been the cause of a pollution incident, then DAERA’s Enforcement Policy is applied in these circumstances.A recent review of the third cycle river basin management plan for 2021-27 has been completed, and, subject to Executive approval, I hope to publish this plan in the near future. I am committed to working in partnership across government, the public and private sectors and wider society to improve water quality in Northern Ireland but would stress that there are no quick fixes and that collective action is essential.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to address the volume of sewage and untreated wastewater being discharged into waterways.
answer_text: Addressing the volume of sewage and untreated wastewater discharging into waterways is an operational matter for NI Water and is subject to environmental regulation by the NIEA. My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges. I have provided NI Water with £500m of public money this year to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North.   During the first 4 years of Price Control 21 (2021-25), NI Water has invested approximately £83.6m to address 12 unsatisfactory intermittent discharges (UIDs) from its wastewater assets. NI Water is also carrying out a phased installation of event duration monitors (EDMs) at combined sewer overflows and the information provided by EDMs will give NI Water an accurate assessment of how discharge points are performing and will help to inform future investment plans, ensuring that money is targeted where most needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the average time taken to secure a diagnosis of endometriosis; (ii) for his assessment of the barriers facing women in relation to endometriosis diagnosis; and (iii) to detail any steps his Department is taking to shorten the waiting period for a diagnosis of endometriosis.
answer_text: (i) Average Time for Endometriosis Diagnosis: Unfortunately, HSC Trusts are unable to distinguish at the point of referral which patients  are requiring an endometriosis diagnosis and which patients are requiring access to another gynaecology diagnosis, as this information is not recorded on hospital systems.(ii) Assessment of Barriers to Endometriosis Diagnosis: Endometriosis diagnosis can be complex and is influenced by various factors including the individual’s symptoms, medical history, and the availability of clinical tests and diagnostic procedures. Barriers to timely diagnosis may include variability in symptoms, the need for multiple consultations and tests, and waiting times for specialist appointments. These factors contribute to delays and can affect the overall timeliness of receiving a diagnosis.(iii) Steps to Shorten the Waiting Period for Diagnosis: In response to the challenges faced in diagnosing endometriosis, my department has taken several steps. Following the publication of the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) report, which reviewed gynaecology services including endometriosis, the Department of Health has established a forum with representation from Trusts, GPs, and the Public Health Agency to oversee service improvements. This includes the implementation of 20 recommendations aimed at improving gynaecology services. Additionally, Task and Finish groups have been established to develop and enhance clinical pathways for conditions like endometriosis. These measures are intended to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the diagnostic process, thereby reducing waiting timesThese initiatives demonstrate our commitment to addressing the needs of women facing endometriosis and improving their access to timely and effective diagnosis and treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the Housing Executive will have the power to borrow and finance the construction of new homes.
answer_text: I refer the member to my recent response to AQW/25209.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the work undertaken by his Department to restore forests and woodlands devastated by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn caused significant damage to our forests across my Department’s estate.  Planning and restoration of damaged areas is ongoing, following the initial phase of clearing storm damaged trees impacting on neighbours, businesses, access routes, utility infrastructure and recreation facilities. Work has already commenced on harvesting extensively wind damaged areas, which have been substituted for previously planned harvesting sites. Due to the extent of damage across the forest estate, this is expected to continue over the forthcoming year. Re-establishing the woodlands will follow the tree clearance operations. This will include complete replanting in areas worst affected and supplementary planting where sufficient trees have withstood the effects of the storm.  Opportunities to regenerate trees naturally from local seed sources, where available, will also form part of woodland re-establishment. This will provide increased diversity of species and subsequent improved resilience in the woodlands affected by the storm.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail any steps his Department is taking to address regional imbalance; and (ii) for his assessment of the implications his Department’s proposals in relation to hospital network reconfiguration will have on regional imbalance.
answer_text: Reform is one way we can ensure better and equitable access to healthcare for everyone and address any regional imbalances. I have said before that the pressures evident in our health and social care system underscore the necessity for change. But it is not change for change’s sake. The goal is to ensure people have access to the right services at the right time, resulting in better outcomes.Last December I set out a three-year plan to improve primary and hospital care, social care, productivity, and patient safety. Not only does the plan set ambitious goals to improve services such as Mental Health and Elective Care. It also brings a stronger focus on reducing health inequalities.  The HSC has experienced unprecedented financial challenges in recent years. This has meant the ability to address equity issues has been extremely limited.  The financial outlook undoubtedly remains challenging; however, reform and having a three-year road map will go some way to help mitigate the impact of an inadequate budget.On a more technical front, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) uses a formula to determine fair shares of funding provided to local areas. This takes account of issues such as population, age/gender weighting, additional need and rurality.  SPPG then carries out detailed equity comparisons of Trust baseline funding, to inform a strategy to address any gaps between fair shares and Trust baseline funding.It is also important to note that robust Equality and Rural Needs assessment processes are in place to ensure any potential impact of policies or other decisions on people and rural areas are fully considered.(ii) The Hospital Network consultation did not propose the reconfiguration of any specific services but rather outlined a set of high- level principles to maximise collaborative working and ensure that people receive the right care, in the right place, at the right time.  It is not possible for all hospitals to do all things, so different hospitals will provide different services.  I also want to see more care moving closer to people’s homes, to be delivered in the community, rather than in our acute hospitals. Moving to an increasingly networked hospital approach should mean that hospital services should be equally accessible to everyone for example through the creation of regional lists. This would also mean that some people may have to travel further to receive services. The goal is equal access and safer, more sustainable services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he is taking to identify additional funding to support Irish language organisations to mitigate the impact of planned funding cuts to Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: There are no planned funding cuts to Foras na Gaeilge, the cost pressures faced by the organisation are as a result of funding not keeping pace with rising costs.North South bodies receive core funding from the Executive and the Irish Government via their Sponsor Departments through an agreed funding ratio from each jurisdiction, which was established during the early operation of the Bodies and based on an estimation of the benefit to the respective jurisdictions of the operation of the Body.More recently it has become apparent that the inflexible application of the funding ratios has had a constraining effect on the Bodies, usually as a result of the differing financial contexts that Sponsor departments are working in.In response to this issue, my department worked with Southern counterparts to develop an Alternative Funding Proposal which my predecessor Dr Caoimhe Archibald approved as Finance Minister in October 2024. This new arrangement would mean that the Bodies would be able to avail of funding that might have previously been lost to them as a result of the inflexible funding ratio and reduces the risk that Bodies will miss out on funding merely due to financial constraints in one jurisdiction.As it is cross cutting, the Alternative Funding proposal requires Executive approval, and I have been pressing and will continue to press for this to happen. In the meantime, I met with Conradh na Gaeilge and my counterpart Jack Chambers TD in the Irish Government, to discuss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how mortuary staffing levels vary across individual hospital sites in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on (i) weekdays; (ii) weekday evenings; and (iii) weekends.
answer_text: Mortuary staffing levels across 2 sites, Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH) and Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH) in Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):On weekdays: CAH Mon – Fri, 2 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) Medical Technical Officer (MTO) are based in CAH 9am – 5pm.CAH Mon – Thu 0.6WTE Mortuary Admin assistant based in CAH 9am – 3pm.DHH Mon – Tue in week 1, 0.6WTE MTO based in DHH 9am – 5pm  DHH Mon – Wed in week 2, 0.6WTE MTO based in DHH 9am – 5pm Weekday evenings: Mon – Fri 1WTE MTO works On-call - covering both sites CAH and DHH, every evening.Weekends:Sat – Sun 1WTE MTO based in CAH 9am – 6pm. Also evening On-call cover both sites CAH and DHH.NB in ii and iii Evenings are covered on-call for the following reasons only:•	admission, ID, and release of Coroner's cases •	admission of ambulance service•	relevant telephone queries

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any financial support her Department can provide to assist with retrofitting (i) public sector premises; and (ii) voluntary sector premises, to increase energy efficiency.
answer_text: My Department, leads on the strategic delivery of the Energy Management Strategy & Action Plan to 2030 for Central Government. One of the key recommendations of this strategy was to create an ‘Energy Invest to Save’ fund. Since 2021, my Department has invested £72million through this fund, with estimated emissions savings of 12,000 tonnes of CO2 and annual revenue savings of £8million across a range of energy technologies and infrastructure investment. Successful projects to date have covered areas such as energy efficiency, deep retrofit, renewable energy, energy storage, EV charging infrastructure and automatic metering.  My Department looks forward to collaborating with the Department for Communities who have responsibility for providing financial support to the Voluntary and Community Sector, to explore options for future energy efficiency support for retrofitting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all children are automatically entitled to NHS dental care.
answer_text: Children in Northern Ireland are not automatically entitled to Health Service dental care. Under the current Health Service dental contract, dental practitioners have the right to register or not to register a patient as a Health Service patient. This is true for both adults and children. The current Health Service contract only requires dentists to provide care to their registered patients. Likewise, the General Dental Services (GDS) Regulations do not place an obligation on dental practitioners to provide any form of care to non-registered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all pensioners are automatically entitled to NHS dental care.
answer_text: Pensioners in Northern Ireland are not automatically entitled to Health Service dental care. Under the current Health Service dental contract, dental practitioners have the right to register or not to register a patient as a Health Service patient. This is true for adults or children. Their Health Service contract only requires dentists to provide care to their registered patients. Likewise, the General Dental Services (GDS) Regulations do not place an obligation on dental practitioners to provide any form of care to non-registered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when a safety assessment was last carried out on Ashtree Lane, Tandragee; and (ii) when required surface repairs will be carried out.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.I am advised that my officials undertook an inspection on Ashtree Lane, Tandragee on 31 March 2025 following this enquiry and no actionable defects were recorded.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy, as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the nature and purpose of the recently-erected recording apparatus on the Camlough Road and Dublin Road, Newry.
answer_text: I can confirm that traffic monitoring equipment has been erected temporarily at several junctions in Newry to record current traffic data as part of the planned Newry Southern Relief Road scheme. Several of these cameras will remain in place until 11 April 2025 and will then be removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Back in Business scheme.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when her Department last carried out an inspection of Ardmore Road, Newry; and (ii) when resurfacing works on Ardmore Road, Newry, are scheduled to commence.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.I am advised that an inspection of Ardmore Road was last carried out on 10th January 2025. Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his Department’s current free school meals funding rate per pupil; and (ii) when this rate was last (a) reviewed; and (b) increased.
answer_text: Post-primary pupils entitled to free school meals (FSM) are provided with a daily allowance of £2.80 per pupil by the Education Authority (EA) to be used to select from the range of food items available in the cafeteria-style canteens used in post-primary schools. Full-time nursery and primary pupils entitled to FSM have access to the available meal options in their dining facility without a need for the provision of a daily allowance.   The EA is responsible for determining the daily allowance provided to post-primary pupils. A review of its prices for school meals across all school sectors, including the allowance provided to post-primary pupils entitled to FSM, is currently being undertaken by the EA. The current price of a school meal and the aforementioned daily allowance were last increased by the EA in 2017.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any financial support his Department can provide to schools seeking to retrofit their estates to increase energy efficiency.
answer_text: The constrained capital budget position has meant that the priorities for investment are keeping schools open, children safe and providing places for our most vulnerable learners. My Department has not received any additional funding to support net zero or sustainability initiatives. Regrettably that means there is currently no budget available to provided financial support for schools to retrofit their estates. Energy efficiency measures are included in major works and School Enhancement Programme schemes, where appropriate.I will continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained investment in our education system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any progress his Department has made in relation to reintroducing the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme; and (ii) whether any financial assistance is currently available to patients seeking access to treatment through private providers.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme, a limited administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD), was introduced for a 12-month period from 30 June 2021 (which was extended to September 2022) to help reduce Northern Ireland waiting lists.A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted during the 2024/25 budget setting process as part of a wider bid of £135m of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients in 2024/2025. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 budget, as agreed by a majority of MLAs, no additional funding was provided for tackling our waiting lists and the focus has had to be on supporting those on cancer and time critical waiting lists.The Executive’s Programme for Government (PfG), includes a reference to investing up to £215m a year on the waiting list backlog, including up to £135m a year for Waiting List Initiatives, in line with the required investment levels in the revised Elective Care Framework published last year. However, the draft 2025/26 budget does not contain any additional funding for waiting lists. As such, there is no funding available to reintroduce any reimbursement scheme. The consideration of any replacement scheme will be subject to confirmation of additional resource investment for Waiting List Initiatives. Any funding provided, must be prioritised based on maximising clinical outcomes and value for money.The current policy is that patients cannot be reimbursed for expenses incurred in private treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of the proposal to relocate the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Pre-Operative Service from Craigavon Area Hospital to the St Luke’s site in Armagh will have on (i) the well-being of the pre-operative service team; (ii) the standard of services the team can provide; and (iii) the range of services the team can provide.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has advised that despite working at full capacity it has been long acknowledged by the pre-operative team that the service is not meeting demand. Their current accommodation within the Craigavon Area Hospital site is spread across various departments, making communication and efficiency very difficult within any given session. Co-locating the team together in one building will decrease the amount of time colleagues spend walking between rooms to communicate and share information and ultimately enhance their overall working conditions. The St Luke’s Hospital site has more accessible and free parking facilities and very pleasant outdoor surroundings and walkways should staff wish to use at breaktimes. Staff have been offered individual sessions with HR and Head of Service to discuss and resolve any specific concerns they may have regarding the relocation. Improved communication and efficiency of the team’s working practice will enhance the standard of service they can offer to their patients. It is anticipated that patients will be able to park at the St Luke’s Hospital site much more easily than Craigavon Area Hospital and appointments should be shorter.  There will be no change to the range of services that the team can provide from the planned new location in Armagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how the proposal to relocate the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Pre-Operative Service from Craigavon Area Hospital to the St Luke’s site in Armagh will impact the team’s ability to prioritise holistic, safe and equitable care.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has a dedicated pre-operative team, committed to providing the best possible service for their patients. Significant efforts are being made to increase elective theatre activity regionally and improve waiting times for patients, which consequently has an impact on demand for pre-operative assessment. There are approximately 104 elective main theatre sessions each week in the Southern Trust which the team must have patients assessed and ready for.  Pre-operative assessment was previously offered from both Daisy Hill Hospital and Craigavon Area Hospital sites. The pre-op team were dispersed from their then outpatient footprint as a result of pressure for clinical space during the height of the covid-19 pandemic. In recent years, the service has been disrupted on a number of occasions, having to move location, in response to wider demands on physical space. Despite working at full capacity, this is not a productive environment for staff and regrettably not meeting demand, resulting in delays for patients in getting to theatre. The Trust’s accommodation group has recognised the need to find a more suitable location for this important service. Accommodation across Trust sites is a major challenge, particularly at busy acute hospitals. With rising demand for services, it has been a priority where possible to relocate those services, that are not dependent on a hospital setting, to other suitable sites where patients will also benefit from improved access to parking.  Over recent months they have been exploring options to improve working conditions for staff, efficiency of their pre-operative process and access to the service for patients. They have engaged with staff, Trade Union and other stakeholders throughout this process and are committed to providing a service that meets the needs of patients living throughout the entire Southern Trust area.Suitable accommodation has been identified on the St Luke’s hospital site in a purpose facility with adequate, accessible parking for both patients and staff.  The service will remain the same in this new location and have the same access to anaesthetic colleagues as currently. The team will benefit professionally from being located centrally together, through improved communications, efficiency and safety and being able to offer overall more holistic care to their patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a public consultation was held in relation to the proposal to relocate the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Pre-Operative Service from Craigavon Area Hospital to the St Luke’s site in Armagh.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have confirmed that this is a relocation not a change or withdrawal to service. Equality, Good Relations and Human Rights Screening is currently underway to identify any impact on staff and patients and determine if a public consultation is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of service users that will be impacted per week by the proposal to relocate the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Pre-Operative Service from Craigavon Area Hospital to the St Luke’s site in Armagh; and (ii) whether patients’ accessibility and care requirements have been considered.
answer_text: (i) The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have confirmed that approximately 100 patients from across the entire Southern Health and Social Care Trust area (Armagh, Dungannon, Craigavon, Banbridge, Newry and Mourne) attend for pre-operative assessment each week. This proposed relocation will offer staff the benefits of being co-located alongside their colleagues in a dedicated pre-operative assessment facility. Patients will benefit from more accessible parking, away from a busy acute hospital site.  (ii) The Southern Health & Social Care Trust has confirmed that Equality, Good Relations and Human Rights Screening is currently underway to identify any impact on staff and service users and determine if a public consultation is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the proposal to relocate the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Pre-Operative Service from Craigavon Area Hospital to the St Luke’s site in Armagh is in accordance with the Nursing and Midwifery Council Code of Conduct, specifically in relation to (i) prioritising people; (ii) preserving safety; (iii) professionalism; (iv) trust; and (v) practice effectiveness.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that the proposal to relocate the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Pre-Operative Service from Craigavon Area Hospital to the St Luke’s site in Armagh is in accordance with the Nursing and Midwifery Council Code of Conduct.The proposed relocation of the pre-operative assessment service aims to improve working conditions for staff and ensure a more patient centred service. There will be no change to service provision and no patient safety issues have been identified. It is anticipated that the new arrangements will support the team in offering a more professional and effective practice. The management team have very openly engaged with staff and Trade Union colleagues throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail why the X4 Dublin Airport to Derry bus service operates a set-down only service in Armagh; and (ii) for her assessment of Derry bound passengers being unable to board the X4 service in Armagh and must instead board the number 72 bus service from Armagh to Dungannon before switching to the X4 Dungannon to Derry service.
answer_text: The provision of bus services is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The X4 Goldliner service operates as an express cross border service between Derry and Dublin Airport/Dublin City. The service picks up passengers in Dungiven, Maghera, Castledawson, Cookstown, Dungannon, Moy and Armagh before travelling express to / from Dublin, and setting down on the return journey. These express services are designed to keep stops and journey times as low as possible, making them an attractive option for cross border passengers. Passengers travelling from Armagh to Derry should use Translink’s Journey Planner for tailored options to connect to the frequent 273 service to Derry at Dungannon. Translink continuously review all services and make adjustments based on passenger demand. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the potential benefits to job seekers of reopening the JobStart Scheme; and (ii) whether his Department has any immediate plans to reopen the JobStart Scheme.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the first JobStart Scheme in April 2021, I recognise the excellent opportunities and outcomes that the 16–24-year-old and 50+ Schemes have provided for job seekers.  2,872 job seekers have successfully completed opportunities to date, of which 2,150 (75%) have sustained employment.The final outcome of the 25/26 is still to be determined and I am currently exploring options with regards to the future of employability programs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any immediate action her Department can take in relation to traffic light sequencing on the Dublin Road, Newry, to ease traffic pressures arising from her Department’s traffic signal upgrades in the area.
answer_text: My Department is committed to improving the road network to address safety and the needs of all road users. Work commenced on Tuesday 11 March 2025 to upgrade the traffic signals at the Drumalane junction of the A2 Dublin Road - Dominic Street / Bridge Street / The Glen / Drumalane Road, Newry. Upon completion the upgraded traffic signals will operate under specialised software to optimise traffic progression through this major junction. The project is expected to be completed by mid-May 2025, subject to the progress of the scheme and favourable weather conditions. Most of the work will be carried out during off-peak hours to minimise inconvenience to commuter traffic, although some disruption is inevitable to ensure the completion of this essential work. Officials were aware of the increased queues and have made an alteration to the traffic light sequences which has resulted in an improvement to the traffic flows at this junction.  In the meantime, my Department will continue to monitor the site to manage traffic progression as work continues around this junction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when her Department last carried out a safety inspection of (a) Aghincurk Road, Newtownhamilton; (b) Armagh Road, Newtownhamilton; (c) Keady Road, Newtownhamilton; and (ii) when these roads have been scheduled for resurfacing works.
answer_text: My Department conducts a programme of regular cyclic inspections across all roads in the North.  Aghincurk Road was inspected on 4 March 2025 with actionable defects meeting my Department’s Limited Service policy being reported for repair.The Armagh and Keady Roads were inspected on 10 March 2025 with resurfacing works scheduled to start on 7 April 2025.My Department will detail a full programme of resurfacing works for 2025/26 in its Annual Report to Council which will be published on my Department’s website in due course.  Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the health and safety implications arising from the X4 Dublin Airport to Derry bus service set-down on Lonsdale Road, Armagh, where passengers alight and retrieve luggage on a busy main road, as opposed to inside Armagh Bus Station; and (ii) whether she will commit to undertaking a review of this practice.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Access to the luggage compartments on Goldline vehicles is via both the nearside and offside, which can be operated in conjunction or independently of each other. For the X4 Goldline route, Translink drivers are instructed to only open the nearside luggage compartments at Dublin Airport and for the remainder of the onward journey to Derry. This is to try and mitigate the safety risk, you have rightly identified, whereby passengers retrieve their luggage from the roadside rather than the kerbside. Further, Translink drivers should pull in and set down at the lay-by close to Armagh Bus Station on the journey to Derry and not obstruct the road. Translink has advised that it will undertake to remind its drivers of the required safety standard and monitor compliance.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the revenue she has allocated for street and footpath restoration in residential estates for the 2025-26 financial year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Resurfacing works on footways falls within the broader scope of my Department’s capital Structural Maintenance activities. My Department’s 2025-26 budget is currently under consideration and once agreed, I will consider allocations in line with my priorities, the wider Executive PfG commitments and equality considerations, following closure of my Department’s Equality Impact Assessment consultation on 5 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when Bernish Road, Newry, was last inspected for surface defects; and (ii) when remedial works will be carried out.
answer_text: Bernish Road, Newry was last inspected for surface defects on 9 February 2025Five priority defects were identified and instructed for repair within 5 working days. This repair work was carried out on 12 February 2025. My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when Old Warrenpoint Road, Newry, was last inspected for surface defects; and (ii) when remedial works will be carried out.
answer_text: Old Warrenpoint Road, Newry, is inspected on a 3-month cycle, with the last inspection carried out on 26 January 2025 and all actionable defects meeting my Department’s Limited Service policy being repaired on 17 February 2025.My Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a detailed breakdown of the cost of all overseas trips taken by the First Minister and deputy First Minister in each of the last five years, broken down by destination.
answer_text: The costs of all overseas trips taken by the First Minister and deputy First Minister between 1 April 2020 to date are outlined below and include Ministers, Special Advisers and others.  This table therefore includes combined costs of a number of people.DestinationDateFlightsHotelsSubsistenceTransportOtherFrance18-10 Nov 2021£793£297£143£0£6Washington DC13 - 17 March 2024£31,183£13,196£754£6,340£2309France5 - 7 June 2024£1,092£635£229£1,702£272Portugal7 - 8 August 2024£1,166£300£16£207£10North America9 – 14 March 2025£36,266£11,136£1,236£10,774£2398Italy25-27April 2025£2942.24TBC154.90£13£0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the penalties in law for dog fouling; and (ii) the number of fines and cautions issued for dog fouling in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Clean Neighbourhoods & Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 provides for councils to issue Fixed Penalty Notices (FPN) for dog fouling offences. In December 2022, The Environmental Offences (Fixed Penalties) (Miscellaneous Provisions) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 came into operation and increased the maximum FPN that each council can set for dog fouling offences from £80 to £200. The minimum FPN that a council can set for a dog fouling offence is £50.   If a council does not specify the FPN, the amount payable is £75. Failure to pay the FPN may lead to a court summons, and if convicted a fine of up to £1,000 can be imposed. Where councils have enacted legislation, oversight and enforcement of this legislation is a matter for councils to take forward. My Department does not hold or collate any information on fines or cautions issued by councils for dog fouling, each council holds this data for their own area.  However, my officials have liaised with Department of Justice who provided the information below.Information on the number of convictions for dog fouling offences dealt with in the period 2020 to 2024, the most recent five years for which information is available, has been provided in the table below.  All the convictions resulted in a monetary penalty; no cautions or any other diversionary disposals, were issued for such offences over the five-year period. Offences relating to dog fouling may result in a fixed penalty fine being issued by a District Council and therefore not all such offences result in a prosecution or diversionary disposal.  You may, therefore, wish to approach councils directly for the number of fixed penalties issued in relation to dog fouling, as the Department of Justice does not hold this information.Convictions at courts for dog fouling offences, 2020 – 2024YearConvictions2020420211020223202332024*Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was at least one instance of the offence specified.3. Figures for 2024 are provisional.4. Figures relate to dog fouling/faeces offences prosecuted under:the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994, the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, the Dog Fouling of Land by Dogs (Down District) Order 2012, the Fouling of Land by Dogs (Belfast City Council) Order 2012, the North Down Borough Council (Fouling of Land by Dogs) (General) Order 2013 or the Fouling of Lands by Dogs Order 2015.‘*’ means a figure of less than 3 has been treated under rules of disclosure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of inspections of Newry Canal by the NIEA in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the outcome of those inspections.
answer_text: NIEA do not routinely monitor Newry Canal as there are no suitable classification tools or standard to allocate a water body classification to a canal	as determined by Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 and the Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.		NIEA respond to all reports of pollution. The number of inspections of Newry canal by NIEA in response to pollution incidents in the last ten years, together with the outcome of those inspections is outlined in Table 1. Figures for 2024 have yet to be finalised.		Table 1: NIEA inspections of Newry canal 2014-2023 in relation to reported 		pollution incidents	SeverityTotalReportsSubstantiated TotalLowMediumHigh2014533002015973402016533002017932102018833002019922002020511002021622002022633002023123210Total74302460	Of the six incidents classified as of Medium severity, there was one prosecution, two warning letters were issued and in three cases, no further action was progressed following investigation.Addressing water pollution remains a priority for my Department and all reports are investigated. The NIEA Incident Hotline enables the public to report incidents of pollution at any time of the day.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any action her Department will be taking to ease traffic congestion in Armagh city; (ii) when the East and West Link roads, Armagh, are scheduled for completion; and (iii) her assessment of the impact the completion of the East and West Link roads will have upon traffic congestion in Armagh city.
answer_text: My Department is aware that traffic can build up in Armagh City centre for short periods at peak times which is consistent with traffic patterns and conditions in many of towns and cities across the North. In 2023, in the absence of Ministers, a decision was taken by the then Permanent Secretary on  prioritisation of major road schemes. This included pausing work on both the Armagh East Link and Armagh North and West Link schemes. Therefore, development work on both schemes has been paused since 2023. My Department is currently developing a Transport Strategy and suite of Transport Plans, that will set priorities for the future development of the road and rail networks up to 2035. The Strategy will also outline how my Department is working to address the challenges we face such as congestion, climate emergency and transport safety.Plans for the development of major road schemes including the Armagh East Link and Armagh North and West Link will be considered as part of the Transport plan development. I am also keen to promote and encourage the use of sustainable transport modes such as walking, cycling and public transport as an alternative to the private car. Consultation on Department’s Draft Active Travel Delivery Plan stage ended on 28 February 2025, details can be found at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/consultations/active-travel-delivery-plan

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Enterprise Steam Train is next scheduled for a return journey between Belfast and Dublin.
answer_text: This issue is a matter for the Railway Preservation Society of Ireland.  However, in conjunction with Translink, I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink do not operate Steam traction, this is undertaken by the Rail Preservation Society of Ireland (RPSI).  Translink has advised that it has had no requests to facilitate such a movement for Steam traction in passenger services between Belfast and Dublin.  This is a question which should be asked of the RPSI, who are currently working their way through Certification to acquire a licence for running steam trains in the North.  The Certification process is currently being progressed by the RPSI through the DfI.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact the Railway Preservation Society of Ireland directly at info@steamtrainsireland.com.  Making direct contact with the RPSI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the investment by her Department in the (i) maintenance; and (ii) restoration of Newry Canal in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: As a designated watercourse under the terms of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973, my Department carries out cyclical inspection and maintenance on the Newry Canal on an annual basis, with an average annual expenditure of approximately £9,200. The approximate breakdown of the drainage maintenance expenditure for the last 10 years on the Newry Canal is as follows:2015/16 - £12,0002020/21 - £6,0002016/17 - £11,0002021/22 - £15,0002017/18 - £5,0002022/23 - £10,0002018/19 - £9,0002023/24 - £9,0002019/20 - £3,0002024/25 - £12,000My Department’s remit on the Newry Canal, where it is designated between Scarva and WIN Industrial Estate in Newry, extends only to the drainage function of this waterway and therefore we have no responsibility for recreational or navigational purposes. Newry Canal is owned by Newry, Mourne and Down Council and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Council. Initiatives to promote access to Newry Canal for boats and sailing vessels, and the allocation of resources for this work, may rest with these Councils to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when her Department will be resurfacing streets and footpaths in College Gardens and Violet Hill Avenue, Newry.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads and footpaths that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.In the meantime, my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total capital value of the Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF); (ii) the number of applications to TNIFF; (iii) the total value of funding applied for through applications to TNIFF; (iv) any consideration he has given to allocating additional capital to TNIFF; and (v) any consideration he has given to increasing funding to the Regional Stadia Fund to ensure that the construction of Casement Park can be completed promptly.
