answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund has an existing budget of £36.2million.The applications are currently being reviewed before details are confirmed. The value of the applications is still being assessed.I will continue to make the case for additional funding to be allocated to this important long-term investment in football facilities. I am moving forward with the funding that is available to me now.NI Executive funding was allocated for the redevelopment of Casement Park in 2011 and remains in place (£62.5m). A further £43million has been made available by the Irish Government and UCGAA has committed a further £15million. Funding considerations are for all partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the implications of reforms on welfare since the Welfare Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 2015 was made.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of the investment made by his Department in the regeneration of (i) waterways; (ii) blue-green infrastructure in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department’s investment in the regeneration of waterways and blue-green infrastructure in each of the last 7 years is detailed in the below table. As per Waterways Ireland’s Retention & Disposal Schedule, financial records are only retained for 7 years therefore I am unable to provide figures for the full 10 years.Year WaterwaysBlue-Green InfrastructureTotal 2018£660,133£234,423£894,5562019£441,402£11,164£452,5662020£436,011£68,030£504,0412021£1,164,034£    -  £1,164,0342022£1,348,051£    -  £1,348,0512023£1,646,350£35,512£1,681,8632024£1,548,232£    -  £1,548,232Total £7,244,213£349,130£7,593,342All the costs associated with the regeneration of waterways and blue-green infrastructure provided above refers to the relevant capital projects delivered on the Erne System and Lower Bann from 2018 – 2024. Please note that no recurring costs have been included in the above table.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any engagement she has had with the Shared Island Unit following the announcement of €23 million for Tourism Shared Destinations; and (ii) for her assessment of where this funding could most effectively be utilised.
answer_text: I very much welcomed the €23m Shared Island Funding allocated to Cuilcagh Lakelands UNESCO Global Geopark, Carlingford Lough and Sliabh Beagh. All three proposals have been developed by the local authorities, communities and tourism agencies in both jurisdictions. Engagement continues with all stakeholders at this early stage.All three projects will deliver sustained economic, social and environmental benefits across counties in both jurisdictions.Of the total funding, €5 million will be allocated for investment in overseas marketing by Tourism Ireland.  This will target visitors who are motivated to come to the island of Ireland with an interest in exploring scenery, our culture and heritage. These Shared Island projects will help inspire visitors to stay longer and spend more in regions therefore bringing greater economic benefits to this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail any engagement he has had with the Shared Island Unit following the announcement of €10 million for nature restoration and biodiversity; and (ii) for his assessment of where this funding could most effectively be utilised.
answer_text: (i) NIEA officials are engaging directly with National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS) on an initial proposal for nature recovery and biodiversity on an all-island basis, building on  the strong partnership developed through the current Shared Island Initiative Peatland Programme.(ii) The proposal is to establish and implement a Natura Communities network of locally based groups across both Ireland and Northern Ireland.  Natura Communities is an initiative that has been piloted in Ireland since 2022 by NPWS, delivering nature restoration actions using locally based labour and expertise that aligns environmental and rural development policies.  The aim is to help manage and restore our most important wetland and peatland ecosystems and build local capacity and skills. Officials will be engaging further with NPWS on how the funding can be utilised and on the development and administration of an effective restoration programme to deliver this Shared Island Funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail any engagement he has had with the Shared Island Unit following the announcement of €20 million for Arts and Cultural Heritage; and (ii) for his assessment of where this funding could most effectively be utilised.
answer_text: As part of their routine engagement with counterparts in Ireland, my officials have been briefed on the nature of the Shared Island proposals.  Any decisions in respect of the Shared Island Fund are a matter for the Irish Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the findings of Young Lives vs Cancer's The Cost of Waiting Report; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department will be taking following this.
answer_text: I welcome the recent publication of Young Lives vs Cancer's The Cost of Waiting Report and I have noted its contents.Patients undergoing hospital treatment face unexpected costs that can add additional financial pressure at an already difficult time. However, support is already available for individuals and their dependents in Northern Ireland who need access to healthcare. The Help with Health Costs scheme provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards travel costs for health and social care treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefits automatically passport on to the scheme.  In addition, the Department’s current Policy for Car Parking Provision and Management includes guidance on free and concessionary parking for patients on particular care paths like cancer.Unfortunately, it is not possible to consider any further financial assistance for cancer patients and their families due to the significant deficit in my Department’s budget. The provision of disability benefits in Northern Ireland falls under the remit of the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23899/22-27, to detail (i) the authority she holds to address cross-border rail ticketing issues at Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) any engagement she has had with Translink to have these issues addressed.
answer_text: Translink, as the Railway Undertaking, are best placed to determine the most appropriate way to manage their ticketing services to meet regulatory requirements.  Translink have previously advised that due to the main Bus and Rail cross border services operating under a capacity management system the purchase of adhoc tickets from the Ticket Vending Machines is not possible. This is to ensure that customers who have purchased a ticket and booked a seat from a bus stop other than the departure stop of the bus service, can avail of the service ie seat is available. Regarding those who have purchased their ticket from Iarnród Éireann and are travelling through from Grand Central Station, these tickets require visual inspection at the gate line due to the variety of Iarnród Éireann ticket types which cannot be validated electronically. This would be the same convention when Translink Rail tickets are presented to Iarnród Éireann staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she is taking to ensure parked vehicles are not impacting sightlines at the exit from Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: Enforcement patrols are specifically developed for each area and reviewed every six months to confirm they remain proportionate and address the needs of the area. Hospital Road, Newry at the exits of Daisy Hill Hospital currently receives scheduled enforcement patrols, Monday to Saturday between 08:30 and 18:00.  Requests for additional enforcement can be made to the following email address Parking.request@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has undertaken any assessment of the provision of an economic framework for a new Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is working to promote the all-Ireland economy and discussing with ESRI future analysis work using their all-Ireland economic model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of the provision of a fiscal framework for a new Ireland.
answer_text: ANSWERI think the best way forward towards a new Ireland is the establishment of a Citizens Assembly and I would encourage the Governments in Dublin and London to establish such a body. The Fiscal Framework for a new Ireland could be part of the discussions of the Assembly and my Department stands ready to help inform those discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department currently holds all road bond monies in a ring-fenced account, similar to a client account held by solicitors.
answer_text: My Department retains all cash road bond monies in the general Departmental bank account, underpinned by detailed records for potential draw down or return to developers upon successful completion of works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the value of bonds held by her Department; and (ii) the estimated cost to bring (a) roads; and (b) sewerage to an acceptable standard in the Meadow Farm development, Enniskillen.
answer_text: The value of bonds held by my Department in respect of Meadow Farm development, Enniskillen is £13k. A preliminary estimate of costs of remedial works required to bring the roads and sewage infrastructure to an acceptable standard is estimated to cost around £100k.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a pay uplift has been approved for Southern GP Federation Support Unit staff.
answer_text: My Department has no role in setting or agreeing the terms and conditions of employment of staff directly employed by or through a GP Federation.  		My Department provides funding support to GP Federations for a range of services.  		For 2024/25, an uplift in funding for Federation-led services has been approved to provide financial assistance towards pay uplifts for a number of staff groups employed through GP Federations: General Practice Pharmacists Advanced Nurse PractitionersGeneral Practice NursesGP Federation MDT staff (including Mental Health Practitioners) and GP Federation Primary Care Elective Care Services.Historically, my Department has not funded annual uplifts for GP Federation Support Unit staff.  Any additional or new funding required to support such an uplift would require a business case to be provided by the Federation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23360/22-27, to detail (i) any correspondence she received confirming the removal of the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry; and (ii) whether her Department’s officials have verified that this billboard has been removed.
answer_text: I can advise that as Minister for Infrastructure, I have not received any official correspondence confirming that the billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry has been removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the upgrade of the A1, including the anticipated completion date.
answer_text: My predecessor Minister O’Dowd announced the delivery of the A1 Junctions Scheme in the Assembly on 2 July 2024 as a single package of works and committed departmental funding to this critical safety improvement scheme.The scheme will include the provision of 4 new grade separated junctions, the closing up of all gaps in the central reservation and the provision of a continuous central reserve safety barrier along the whole route.The initial stage of the procurement process commenced with the advertisement of the Pre-Qualification Questionnaire on 12 December 2024. Submissions were returned on 5 February 2025 and are currently being assessed. It is planned that the tender stage will commence in Spring 2025, to facilitate award of the contract early in 2026. The construction of the scheme is due to commence in Spring 2026 and take 3 years to complete. The approved cost estimate for the project is in the range of £120m to £130m

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has undertaken an assessment of the benefits of the establishment of a Minister with sole responsibility for mental health.
answer_text: As you know mental health is one of my key priorities, and I have been very focused on addressing this issue since I took up the role of Health Minister.  Whilst I can see merits in the idea of the appointment of a Minister for Mental Health as a means for increasing focus, such a proposal would ultimately be a matter for the Executive Office and the NI Executive more widely.  However, delivering actions from the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, subject to budget availability, remains a key priority for me.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any advice her Department can provide individuals being pursued by debt collection agencies acting on behalf of private parking companies.
answer_text: I can advise the member that my Department does not have responsibility for the enforcement actions in private car parks.The Consumer Protection Team of the Consumer Council offers advice and assistance on matters relating to private car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the current provision of legal and immigration advice to those detained at Larne House Immigration Detention Centre.
answer_text: As Minister, I am committed to ensuring the most vulnerable within our society have access to legally aided help and representation. I am aware that stakeholder groups working with migrants, refugees and victims of human trafficking have made consistent representation about significant unmet need for legal help for these groups.  In response, I am currently consulting on proposals aimed at enhancing access to justice through the provision of fair remuneration for legal practitioners providing legally aided services, and my officials are working closely with immigration practitioners to ensure that the proposals, when finalised, will help address the identified need.  I have also committed, through the Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Plan, to publish proposals to improve specialist support, including legal support, for migrants, asylum seekers and refugees and those detained at Larne House Immigration Detention Centre to ensure continuing access to legally aided help and representation in future.Between 2021 to 2024, 6301 applications for legal help and representation were granted in relation to asylum and immigration matters with an approved total expenditure of £2.39m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale for not pursuing mediation as a means to resolve the ongoing dispute between her Department and the Criminal Bar.
answer_text: Decisions on public spend must be evidence based and robust. The steps I have taken to address the concerns of the professions, including a significant 16% uplift in fees backdated to December 2024, are based on the available evidence, the recommendations of the independent Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid and work undertaken in the Department to support its implementation. All respondents, including the Criminal Bar Association, had 10 months to make submissions to the Review and, in establishing in the Working Group Chaired by His Honour Judge Burgess, I have created yet another opportunity for evidence to be submitted to inform further decisions on fees. The Working Group presents a constructive and positive mechanism for developing proposals which will meet the requirements of good governance and withstand the scrutiny that rightfully attaches to our duty to protect public funds. Mediation would not further aid that process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they were aware of the legislative divergence between Scotland and the North in relation to goodwill payments to NIE customers who lost power due to Storm Éowyn prior to their public call for compensation payments.
answer_text: Energy Policy does not sit within the remit of The Executive Office, it would therefore be appropriate for the Department for the Economy to respond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16553/22-27, (i) when the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, will be eligible to receive a Certificate of Lawful Existing Use or Development (CLEUD); and (ii) whether an assessment has been made of the impact a potential CLEUD for this billboard would have on her Department in the event that junction upgrades are required.
answer_text: As advised in my previous response on this matter, hoardings are typically subject to a planning application, and any breaches of planning regulations are generally addressed under planning legislation, which in most cases falls within the jurisdiction of the relevant Council. As Certificates of Lawful Existing Use or Development (CLEUDs) are issued by the relevant Council they would be best placed to advise you on eligibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of adults registered for NHS dentistry in each of the last ten years; and (ii) any action his Department is taking to protect NHS dentistry.
answer_text: The number of adult NHS registrations over the past 10 years can be found in the table below: YearNo. of NHS Adults Registered2024967,06320231,010,0712022968,8242021925,1872020887,5372019883,5662018876,6242017864,6832016853,4442015842,991General Dental Services play a key role in improving the general health of the population and reducing pressure on other areas of the Health Service. I am aware of the challenges currently being experienced within General Dental Services and I am committed to taking meaningful action to ensure the service works better for both patients and dental professionals. The changes required to improve General Dental Services and ensure that we have a service that works for both patients and the profession will require sustained effort. Dental access enhancement initiatives which my Department has funded in 2024/25 include £1m for an Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, £4.3m for a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments, and £3.9m for a Dental Access Scheme for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients. The Enhanced Child Examination Scheme has resulted in over 29,000 children becoming registered with a general dental practitioner since it began in June 2024. Approximately 500 patients per week are currently seen under the Dental Access Scheme.Options for investment in 2025/26 are being considered, however following the announcement of the Draft Budget allocation my Department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/2026 because of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) measures her Department has taken to develop a fair and sustainable funding model for NI Water; and (ii) immediate actions her Department will be taking to facilitate the connection of new homes to the NI Water network.
answer_text: (i) I will be progressing the three-pronged approach already put in place by my predecessor, in terms of: 1. Continuing to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water; 2. Bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS) and implementing a pilot scheme for natural drainage following a successful Transformation Funding bid, which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and for which Executive agreement has already been secured, and 3. I have launched a consultation to explore developer contributions.Additionally, Officials are also exploring an amendment to the Social and Environmental Guidance that would include housing as a priority alongside treatment of drinking water and wastewater. (ii) I have provided NI Water with £500m of public money this year to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North. In the October monitoring round, NI Water were allocated £19.5m capital DEL, specifically to address constraints in wastewater infrastructure and to unlock capacity for housing connections. This investment is projected to deliver capacity for some 2,300 new properties to connect to the water and sewerage infrastructure.I plan to continue that focus on unlocking capacity and can confirm that my officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in DAERA and DfC to contribute to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy.  Additionally, a Development Constraints Project Team has been put in place within NI Water’s Developer Services function, to help alleviate capacity constraints. NI Water is encouraging early engagement with developers, through the Pre-Development Enquiry process, to try to find a resolution to any issues at an early stage.I will not implement any measures that will lead to household water charges on already hard-pressed families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the progress of the development of the Nutrients Action Programme.
answer_text: The Nutrients Action Programme or NAP has been in place since 2007 and has been reviewed and revised on three previous occasions. A fourth review is currently being carried out.This review is due to be finalised in the coming weeks after which a public consultation on the proposals for the updated Action Programme will be launched this Spring.  In parallel, there will also be consultations on the associated Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulation Assessment.The review of the NAP takes into consideration recent research and science, as well as the findings of the Implementation Report for 2020 to 2023, which was published by my Department in June 2024. Following the consultation and consideration of the responses, I envisage that, subject to Executive approval, the updated NAP Regulations will be introduced in the second half of this year.A fully implemented and effective NAP will be a key contributor to improving water quality in our rivers and lakes.  However, we must recognise that Agriculture is not the only sector impacting on water quality. Wastewater treatment is also a significant source of nutrient inputs which are a key cause of poor water quality. It is important that we work collectively, and all sectors play their part in improving water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for an update on the implementation of the South Armagh Policing Review; and (ii) to detail when outstanding recommendations from the review will be implemented.
answer_text: Pursuant to the AQW 18995/22-27 response provided, matters in relation to the implementation of recommendations of the South Armagh Policing Review remain an operational matter for the Chief Constable.You may wish to seek an update from the Chief Constable or the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will recommend the construction of an opening bridge across Newry Canal as part of the Southern Relief Road project.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work her Department is undertaking to (i) support; and (ii) encourage people with disabilities into paid employment.
answer_text: My department offers a range of practical, financial and other support to those with disabilities across the further and higher education sectors, as well as to those participating on vocational training programmes, to enable them to build the skills and qualifications needed to compete for employment opportunities at all levels.Amongst these measures are Breaking Barriers in education, and Step Up’s Disability Access Route to Employment and Education (DARE). Additionally, targeted support for Widening Participation into higher education is provided through interventions and financial support for several target groups, including those with a disability.  Flexible online learning is provided through Skill Up and enables individuals to study in a time and manner that is most appropriate to their needs.We have a strong apprenticeship offering which does provide training-related funding to support apprentices with a disability, but we recognise more can be done to increase participation from individuals with a disability. 	The Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund will provide up to £600,000 to facilitate collaborative projects to derive innovative solutions, test new inclusion approaches and deliver key methodologies to tackle our inclusion challenges. This fund will support a more diverse cohort of individuals, including those with disabilities, to undertake apprenticeships. 	My Department has supported the Business In The Community Opening Doors Inclusive Recruitment Campaign.  This campaign is aimed at employers and will support them to extend their recruitment practices to reach more people who are economically inactive or face barriers to entering the labour market. One of my priorities is good jobs, this means ensuring that good quality work is accessible to everyone.  There are clear business benefits to widening the pool of potential employees and clear economic and social benefits to ensuring that no groups are locked out of good quality work. This campaign will provide vital support for businesses on this journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to provide a detailed breakdown of the number of unadopted roads as of 1 March 2025; and (ii) to detail the reasons for the roads being unadopted.
answer_text: Unadopted roads can fall into two broad categories, those within new developments, and private lanes which form part of the wider road network.  Private lanes are usually unsuitable for adoption as they fall below the standards required.The adopted road network can be viewed using this link.  Any road not included on this map remains unadopted.  The department do not hold details of unadopted roads on the wider road network as they do not for part of my department’s responsibility under the Roads Order.A list of phases within new developments which are 80% occupied but contain unadopted roads has been placed in the Assembly Library under the reference AQW 22978. It should be noted that an individual housing development may contain multiple phases and multiple roads within each phase.Roads within new developments may remain unadopted for many reasons.  Unfortunately, my Department does not hold this information in a readily available format.  However, I can confirm that the most common reasons for not progressing new developments to adoption include.Failure to complete the NI Water infrastructure to their adoptable standardFailure to provide development access arrangements to the required standardsFailure to gain technical approval for structuresFailure to attain geotechnical approvalsConstruction not in compliance with the Private Streets Determination (PSD) layoutInfringement into service strips on shared surface developmentsDeveloper not in control of the required landsConstruction and materials not in compliance with the Private Streets Construction Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any actions set out in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 that fall under her Department's remit; (ii) the associated timelines; and (iii) how her Department will measure the success of these actions.
answer_text: The actions within the Programme for Government (PfG) are not aligned to any specific Department.  However, my Department is leading on the commitment to have progressed by the end of this mandate, a Speeding Up Justice Programme, and also to have an Executive agreed approach to reducing offending and reoffending, contributing to delivering improved outcomes for those who engage with the justice system.There are also two actions under the Safer Communities priority for delivery this financial year (2024/25) which the Department is leading as follows: Explore options for expanding the use of Out of Courts Disposals including Community Resolution Notices to speed up the time taken to dispose of those cases and help to reduce pressure on Courts and other justice agencies.Develop a robust business case that will seek to stabilise, and begin to grow, police officer numbers.The cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime is hosted by my Department; the PfG includes an action for this financial year to secure Executive agreement to an extension of the Programme until March 2027.In addition, my Department is a contributor to the Power-to-Change campaign, under the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls priority, to challenge unacceptable attitudes and reduce harmful behaviours of men and boys.As outlined in the PfG, the Executive has committed to publishing annual reports on progress against all of the commitments.  That will be in addition to any internal monitoring within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any grants or funding supports currently available for renovations, repairs or reconstruction of a derelict property.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) has responsibility for administering several grants to help keep homes in a reasonable state of repair, including:The Repair grant can help landlords where a council has issued a ‘statutory notice’ about repairs on their property. Owners or landlords of a vacant property can apply for this grant to repair or replace existing items.The Renovation Grant is for homes that have been inspected and found to be unfit to live in. The property must be the owner’s main or only home. The Replacement grant is available where a home is unfit to live in and repair is not an option.Due to budgetary constraints, the NIHE only awards the above grants in exceptional circumstances and when certain criteria are met.The Historic Environment Repair Fund is also expected to reopen later this year. It will offer support of £8k towards repairs to roofs and windows of listed buildings. Buildings on the Heritage at Risk register, including derelict listed buildings, are a priority category for support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any exercises his Department has taken to identify and address wasteful spending in Executive Departments (i) in each of the last 10 years; and (ii) on an ongoing basis.
answer_text: Each department needs to ensure it is fulfilling its functions as efficiently as possible. Given the constrained financial position in recent years, each department has had to deliver significant efficiencies to live within their agreed budget. The longer-term financial sustainability plans exercise will ask departments to set out their future plans, associated efficiencies and transformation opportunities.  Departments will be asked to complete these plans by Autumn.  It is envisaged that once in place, these longer-term plans will be updated on a rolling basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Newry Community Treatment and Care Centre.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 19574/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total expenditure by his Department on sports; and (ii) the National Lottery funding provided for sports, in each of the last ten years, broken down by capital and resource funding.
answer_text: Due to the volume and the format of this answer I have arranged for the details to be deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail where legacy consequential funds for sport have been directed in the last ten years.
answer_text: While the Northern Ireland Executive may receive Barnett consequential as a result of a UK Government policy/programme decision, this funding is unhypothecated, it is therefore a matter for the Executive to determine how funding is allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the extent to which he is collaborating with the Department of Health on encouraging participation in sport from early years and throughout life.
answer_text: Both my Department and Sport NI engage directly with the Department of Health and the Public Health Authority on a range of sport related issues, including encouraging participation in sport from early years and throughout life. ‘Active Living’ is the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland, its main aim is to get more people, more active, more of the time. One of the goals of the Strategy is to improve physical literacy and increase lifelong participation in sport and physical activity for all. It recognises the importance of early years engagement in sport and physical activity to build healthy habits and encourage lifelong participation.The Department of Health are a key member of the Cross Departmental Strategic Delivery Group for the ‘Active Living’ Strategy and are also members of the Strategic Implementation Group which is developing the Delivery Action Plan for the Strategy. The Public Health Authority are also represented on the Strategic Implementation Group. Since this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the merits of an immediate audit of existing sports infrastructure, including schools, council facilities and clubs to assess both current capacity and future demand.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI have written a Sports Facility Audit based on information drawn from our Active Places Database, a repository of over 5200 facilities across 2200 venues.The findings from this audit have been presented to Department for Communities, District Councils and the Sport NI Board.Sport NI have drawn up a sectoral engagement plan, to enhance our knowledge of the needs across the sector and how this ties with the facilities that are outlined in the Sports Facility Audit”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the percentage of sporting infrastructure that is behind school gates; and (ii) the degree to which these facilities are accessible to the public out of school hours.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information pertaining to the percentage of sporting infrastructure that is behind school gates.The Education Authority (EA) has a statutory responsibility to encourage, promote and facilitate community use of schools. The EA Community & Schools Team manages the EA’s statutory responsibility and provides direct support and advice to all schools.All schools can make their sporting facilities available for community use and guidance for doing so is contained in the Departmental Toolkit for Community Use of Schools: Community use of school premises - A guidance toolkits | Department of Education.  As outlined in the toolkit, the decision on whether to make facilities available for community use rests with the Board of Governors of each school. Several operational requirements must be taken into account, including the school’s ability to provide staff to open the school out of hours and whether the User can provide public liability insurance of £10m.The EA does not retain a register of community use across all schools, however a survey of Community Use of Schools was carried out as part of the 2018 DE Omnibus Survey. Results indicated that 80% of schools had some community use of facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of public inquiries initiated by her Department in each of the last ten years, including the average cost of each inquiry.
answer_text: My Department has not initiated any public inquiries under the Inquiries Act 2005 in the last ten years.In the process of assessing planning applications and developing our road network, my Department has initiated a number of Public Local Inquiries in the last 10 years. Although they are commonly referred to as public inquiries, local inquiries are a statutory requirement in some cases and ensure that all aspects of complex projects are fully explored.  I have included a list of these local inquiries below along with the costs of the enquiries raised under the Roads Order 1993.My Department does not collate total costs for Public Local Inquiries raised under the Planning Act 2011 as they are organised by the Planning Appeals Commission. A financial breakdown of staff time and wages are not recorded or apportioned for individual planning applications or any subsequent public local inquiry, therefore no information is held in this regard.Local Inquiries under the Roads Order 1993A29 Cookstown Bypass – 2024: £190,144.04A5WTC Public Inquiry – 2020 & 2023: £342,000A1J2 Public Inquiry – 2020: £40,809.73 A24 Ballynahinch Bypass Public Inquiry – 2016: 24,352.85A5WTC Public Inquiry – 2016: £340,000York Street Interchange – 2015 £210,000Local Inquiries under the Planning Act 2011North/South Interconnector 400kV overhead line – 2016North/South Interconnector associated works – 2016Doraville Wind Farm – 2019Extraction, transportation and working of sand and gravel from Lough Neagh - 2018Dalradian – Mining operations – 2021Dalradian – Powerline associated with mining operations – 2021Gruggandoo Wind Farm – 2022Carnalbanagh Wind Farm – 2022Ballygilbert Wind Farm – 2022

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the Irish Government, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, in relation to (i) increasing north south partnership on higher education; (ii) improving access for northern students to third level institutions in the Republic of Ireland; and (iii) improving cross-border cooperation on skills and reciprocal recognition of qualifications.
answer_text: On 15 April, I had a very positive meeting with my counterpart in the South, James Lawless TD, to discuss how we can work together to address the various barriers to all-Ireland student mobility, including those detailed in the question. I am very encouraged that this is a mutual concern for us both, and I look forward to engaging with him further. My officials will be actively engaging with their counterparts in the South to take forward and progress these discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when compensation will be made available to NIE Neworks' customers who were left without power as a result of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Following disruption and damage caused by Storm Éowyn on 24th of January, a working group involving the Department for the Economy, NIE Networks, and the Utility Regulator, was established to consider compensation payments for electricity customers most affected by power outages.The working group was established at the request of the Executive to consider options where compensation payments would be recovered through customer electricity bills. The working group has now updated the Executive of its findings that there is currently no existing route to make payments to customers. NIE Networks has applied a severe weather exemption provided for in legislation under which customers can apply for compensation if they have been off electricity supply. The working group concluded that any other options involving recovery of payments from network charges would require legal or regulatory modifications which cannot be applied retrospectively for those customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the decision by Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to adopt a policy position opposing the construction of a fixed bridge across Newry Canal as part of the Newry Southern Relief Road.
answer_text: When I took office on 3 February 2025 the proposals and detailed assessments for the Newry Southern Relief Road were within a period of statutory public consultation which commenced on 21 January 2025 and concluded 4 March 2025. The consultation was based on the installation of a fixed bridge.Officials are currently reviewing the responses and compiling a report for my consideration.  I would not wish to comment on any of the responses provided to the consultation until this work has been concluded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the current number of funded university places in the North, relative to demand; and (ii) to detail any steps her Department is taking to increase university places in the North.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring our students who want to study here can do so.  My Department will shortly be beginning a review of higher education funding which will help support the future growth and sustainability of the higher education sector here.  In advance of that review I have increased the number of funded student places at the local universities by 596 in academic year 2024/25.  I also remain firmly committed to expanding Ulster University’s Magee campus to 10,000 students in line with the NDNA commitment and Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how businesses that have experienced major damage to their sites can access funding from the £17.4 million allocated to respond to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: We are mindful of the widespread disruption to individuals, businesses, communities, and to public services as a result of Storm Éowyn.On the 6 February 2025, the Executive agreed a range of funding allocations, including £17.4 million provided to help a number of  departments deal with the additional pressures they faced as a result of the storm.The Minister of Finance subsequently provided a written statement to the Assembly regarding 2024-25 Public Expenditure, in which he set out the pressures identified by Departments as a consequence of Storm Éowyn and the funding allocated. The Minister for Finance statement can be found at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-02/Written,MinisteriaL Statement on Storm Eowyn and Other Pressures.pdf It is for individual Departments to determine how that funding is prioritised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the approach of private parking companies that use solicitors letters and referrals to debt recovery agencies as a means of enforcement, despite the law stating that the driver, not the registered keeper of a car, is liable for fines or infringements; and (ii) to detail any action her Department will take to regulate the business practises of private parking companies.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.My Department currently has no plans to regulate the business practices of private car parking companies. However, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme (AOS) managed by the British Parking Association (BPA). Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that the legislative framework in the North effectively renders private parking fines unenforceable, (i) for her assessment of the practise of pursuing individuals with debt recovery notices and solicitors letters; and (ii) to detail any advice and guidance her Department can provide to people who are subject to debt recovery proceedings for one of these fines.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.I understand that the Consumer Protection Team of the Consumer Council offers advice and assistance on matters relating to private car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will be making representations to the UK Government for Casement Park to be included in the UK joint bid to host the 2035 Women's World Cup.
answer_text: The bidding process for the 2035 FIFA Women's World Cup is at a very early stage and details regarding potential host venues have not yet been determined.My Department remains committed to maximising opportunities for Northern Ireland to benefit from major sporting events and we will continue to engage with relevant partners as discussions on the UK joint bid progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action her Department is taking to eliminate scam calls to mobile phones and landlines.
answer_text: My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) has a dedicated scams team,  responsible for raising public awareness of scams through regular media appearances and issuing press releases and social media alerts about the latest frauds. They deliver educational talks to local community groups while working with banks, charities and law enforcement to identify victims of fraud. Officers have visited hundreds of scam victims in the last year, helping many of them take cases to the Financial Ombudsman Service, resulting in over £1million being returned to those individuals defrauded by criminals. Trading Standards officials regularly conduct visits to consumers’ homes and encourage them to register their landline and mobile numbers with the Telephone Preference Service (TPS) in order to eliminate unsolicited live sales and marketing calls. Officers also offer to provide and fit call blockers in potential scam victims’ homes, which prevent unwanted phone calls by automatically blocking calls from specific numbers or types of callers, such as spam calls, telemarketers, or unknown numbers. The TSS participates in the ScamwiseNI Partnership with 45 partner organisations, including the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Commissioner for Older People, the Consumer Council and financial institutions. The objective of the Partnership is to engage and inform the community, particularly vulnerable people, about the risks and range of scams and frauds that exist. Additionally, my Department’s partner organisation, the Consumer Council, supports consumers and vulnerable groups through outreach programmes ensuring they are better equipped to identify and avoid scams. It works with partners to promote awareness of scams and agree referral mechanisms to ensure vulnerable consumers are supported. Ofcom, the communications regulator, has primary responsibility for tackling scam calls. They have responsibility for regulating the telecommunications industry and have a key role in protecting consumers from harm caused by scam calls facilitated through phone lines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the finding by the Royal Courts of Justice that the Executive is in breach of its legal obligation to adopt an anti-poverty strategy; and (ii) when an anti-poverty strategy will be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of passengers not being able to purchase cross-border tickets from the ticket machines in Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) to detail any action she will take to address this issue.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Due to Translink's main Bus and Rail cross border services operating under a capacity management system the purchase of ad hoc tickets from the Ticket Vending Machines is not possible as Translink needs to ensure that customers who have purchased a ticket and booked a seat from a bus stop other than the departure stop of the bus service, can avail of the service ie seat is available. For this reason all tickets must either be booked online in advance via the Translink website. Alternatively, they can be purchased from the station ticket office, however these walk up sales would be dependent on availability on a service-by-service basis. This approach ensures a more streamlined and efficient ticketing process for passengers.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has received a bid from the Minister for Infrastructure to facilitate the installation of an opening bridge as part of the Southern Relief Road project.
answer_text: ANSWERThis project is currently out for public consultation.  The Infrastructure Minister will be best placed to provide further details regarding the Newry Southern Relief Road project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current wait time for a first outpatient appointment with a consultant psychiatrist in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the engagement she has had, since taking office, in relation to securing the necessary funding to facilitate the installation of an opening bridge as part of the Newry Southern Relief Road scheme.
answer_text: When I took office on 3 February 2025 the proposals and detailed assessments for the Newry Southern Relief Road were within a period of statutory public consultation which commenced on 21 January 2025 and concluded 4 March 2025. The consultation was based on the installation of a fixed bridge and in order to respect that process I have not undertaken any such engagement.My officials are currently reviewing the consultation responses, and a report will be compiled for my consideration in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process by which an individual can seek a diagnosis of adult ADHD in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how an individual with a diagnosis of adult ADHD can access the medication they require through the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD. Southern Health and Social Care Trust currently do not accept adult ADHD referrals, provide a service or hold a waiting list. As stated previously there is no commissioned service for Adult ADHD. Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts. If someone has a private diagnosis of ADHD, it would be for their GP to decide, that if under the Shared Care arrangements, they are content to prescribe the necessary medication.   All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability.  Work will be progressed in line with the agreed Terms of Reference, the needs assessment will consider barriers and challenges in relation to diagnosis and treatment. The exercise will also (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers  in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, the research exercise will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment on the need for a residents' parking scheme on Boat Street, Newry.
answer_text: My Department has completed a review of residents’ parking schemes and the Residents’ Parking Review Report was published on 29 November 2024. During the development of the report, and following publication, there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north.Given this level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests going forward.  It is my intention to make an announcement over the coming months as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the code of practice for GPs; and (ii) the rights of patients in respect of access to GP services.
answer_text: GPs provide General Medical Services (GMS) under a GMS contract which is agreed between the GP practice and the Department of Health.  The Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contract) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 prescribe the terms which must be included in a General Medical Services contract.  These include ‘Essential Services’ which must be provided to patients.Under Essential Services, a practice must provide services required for the management of their registered patients and temporary residents who are, or believe themselves to be –ill, with conditions from which recovery is generally expected;terminally ill; orsuffering from chronic disease,delivered in the manner determined by the practice in discussion with the patient.‘Management’ of patients includes offering consultation and where appropriate physical examination to identify need for treatment or further investigation.  It also includes making available such treatment or further investigation as is necessary and appropriate, including referring the patient for other services and liaison with other health care professionals involved in the patient’s treatment and care.Practices are also required to provide appropriate ongoing treatment and care to all registered patients and temporary residents taking account of their specific needs. This includes the provision of advice in connection with the patient’s health (including relevant health promotion advice) and the referral of the patient for other services. A practice must provide essential services throughout its “core” contracted hours which are 8.00am to 6.30pm each day, except public and bank holidays.Eligibility to access publicly funded healthcare in Northern Ireland is based on ordinary residence. A person will be ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland when that residence is lawful, voluntary, and for a settled purpose as part of the regular order of their life for the time being, whether of short or long duration. Anyone who is not ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland is a visitor. Entitlement to access publicly funded healthcare for visitors to Northern Ireland is provided for in the Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident (PNOR) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015. The PNOR Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 provide exemptions from healthcare charges in secondary care and enables certain visitors to access General Health Services, on the same basis as someone who is ‘ordinarily resident’ which include: GP-led services, general dental services, general ophthalmic services and pharmaceutical services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to extend the Saturday late night Irish Rail service from Dublin Connolly to enable passengers to disembark at Newry, Portadown and Belfast stations.
answer_text: The service the Member has asked about is operated by Iarnród Éireann and as such I am unable to comment.  Any questions relating to services operated by Iarnród Éireann should be directed to it for comment.In relation to services operated by Translink, the timetabling and operation of services, including stops along the route is primarily an operational matter.  The Public Service Agreement allows Translink to amend services. However, given the success of the hourly enterprise service, this is something I will raise with my counterpart in the Dublin Government, Minister O’Brien.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any grants available to private homeowners for necessary structural repairs to their homes.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has responsibility for administering a range of home improvement grants including the statutory Repair Grant and three discretionary Home Improvement Grants:•Renovation Grant,•Replacement Grant, and•Home Repair Assistance Grant.Due to budget pressures the discretionary grants are currently only awarded in exceptional circumstances and are subject to budget availability within the overall pot of home improvement grant funding provided to the Housing Executive (which also covers statutory grants).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the health service does not accept private diagnoses of ADHD for the purposes of prescribing medication for ADHD.
answer_text: The rationale for this decision is based on issues of clinical and legal liability.  Medications recommended by a specialist for the treatment of ADHD often fall into a category known as ‘Amber Drugs’ on the Red-Amber list.  This classification is decided by the Interface Pharmacist Network for Health and Social Care (HSC) and relates to a recommendation on prescribing responsibility for the medication and whether or not the supply of a medicine should be organised through the hospital pharmacy network.  ‘Amber Drugs’ are specialist medications which have significant pharmacological complexity and/or rarity of use to make the prescribing of the medicine relatively uncommon in the community.For these reasons, ‘amber’ medications require a written shared care arrangement between a GP, the recommending specialist and the patient, covering respective roles and responsibilities for ongoing follow up e.g. bloods, blood pressure, weight or ECG checks.As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the prescription, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to him/her and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  The Red-Amber list has been designed for use in Health and Social Care only and some of the difficulties presented with private shared care agreements include concerns with assurance of private outpatient follow-up, accessibility to private consultants if advice is needed, and availability of documentation from the private sector, all of which could potentially lead to patient safety concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the potential impact of the changes being introduced by the National Insurance Contributions (Secondary Class 1 Contributions) Bill on the ability of children with special educational needs and disabilities requiring home-to-school transport to attend lessons.
answer_text: School transport is one of many vital services provided by private sector employers on behalf of the public sector and I am concerned about the impact the recent Budget may have on the cost to employers of providing those services. The impact on employers of the increase in National Insurance Contributions (NIC) will vary depending on the size of the business, how many it employs and how it manages the new arrangements. I understand that a number of the private bus and taxi operators, who hold contracts with the Education Authority (EA) to provide school transport, are smaller businesses whose NIC liability may not increase or are self-employed and therefore unaffected. The EA does not, however, hold information that would allow it to identify the private bus and taxi operators who will be impacted or to make a detailed assessment of the extent to which this may increase their operating costs. The EA works closely with its transport operators and has advised that to date none have raised concerns about the rise in operating costs due to the increase in NICs, however it will engage with any who are impacted and for whom this presents difficulties.  It is clear that the increase in employers NICs has the potential to increase the already significant cost of the school transport service, however the picture is still emerging. At this stage, I believe the increase in operational costs of those transport operators who are impacted can be managed by the EA through the annual tendering process and I do not anticipate that this will result in children who are transported by private bus and taxi being unable to attend school.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how a family who have experienced major structural damage to their home can access funding from the £17.4 million allocated to respond to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: We are mindful of the widespread disruption to individuals, businesses, communities, and to public services as a result of Storm Éowyn.On the 6 February 2025, the Executive agreed a range of funding allocations, including £17.4 million provided to help a number of departments deal with the additional pressures they faced as a result of the storm.It is for individual Departments to determine how that funding is prioritised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the medical specialisms and services provided in (i) Craigavon Area Hospital; and (ii) Daisy Hill Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of the digital world on children's reading proficiency.
answer_text: When used effectively, digital devices can add value to young people’s learning experiences.  Using ICT is one of the three cross-curricular skills at the heart of the curriculum.  However the pedagogy of how technology is used is vital and it is important that technology should enhance and supplement teaching rather than replacing it.A new Literacy Strategy will consider relevant evidence and research to improve children’s reading proficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she will be introducing measures to allow the use of Irish language in courtrooms and other legal spaces.
answer_text: The very welcome, and long overdue, repeal of the Administration of Justice (Language) Act (Ireland) 1737 removes the statutory prohibition of the use of Irish in our courts. Any application to use Irish in courts, however, is a matter for the courts to determine. My department already facilitates the use of the Irish language in accordance with our code of courtesy on the use of Irish in official business, which is designed to assist staff in fulfilling the Department’s obligations under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of consultants that have applied for a position at Daisy Hill Hospital but were offered a post at Craigavon Area Hospital during the recruitment process, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Southern HSC Trust, as the relevant HSC employer, has advised there has been no time when a Consultant has applied for a position at Daisy Hill Hospital but were offered a post at Craigavon Area Hospital during the recruitment process, in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21901/22-27, to detail the envisaged timeline for a local delivery of Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-cell therapy.
answer_text: Northern Ireland is unable to deliver CAR-T locally as the current haematology facility in Belfast Trust is not compliant with the extant accommodation standards for this very specialist service.  Patients from Northern Ireland however have access to CAR-T on an equitable basis with other nations as arrangements are in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.The Belfast Trust have submitted a business case to the DoH for a new Regional Haematology Ward which plans to improve facilities with additional single rooms and transplant beds.  It is the expectation that if this project is approved the Belfast Trust will be able to work towards delivering CAR-T locally to NI patients.The current timeline for the Regional Haematology Ward build estimates that handover of the new facility for quarter 1 2029/30 and the service estimates that there is a 12 month work up to gain all necessary accreditations and skills to deliver CAR-T locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last safety assessment of Granemore crossroads, Tassagh, was carried out; (ii) the outcome of this assessment; and (iii) whether her Department will consider carrying out a follow-up review.
answer_text: A safety assessment of Granemore crossroads, Tassagh was carried out recently and has a good safety record as per the latest PSNI injury collision data.  Given this information, my Department is not currently considering any further measures in the vicinity of Granemore crossroads.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 22280/22-27, to detail (i) when he will be engaging with his Southern counterpart in relation to funding challenges faced by Foras na Gaeilge; and (ii) any action his Department will be taking to ensure that Foras na Gaeilge has the necessary funding to perform at full operational capacity.
answer_text: I will be engaging my Southern counterpart at the next North/South Ministerial Council (NSMC) Language sectoral meeting, which is planned for early spring. The NSMC Joint Secretariat is working to establish a date.My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Irish government on all matters relating to North/South Language Body sponsorship, including funding.It is for Foras na Gaeilge, and their Board, to make operational decisions around programme and funding priorities.My Department provided around £3.6m in funding to Foras na Gaeilge in 2024 to allow them to meet their statutory responsibilities. My Department has no plans to reduce this funding in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22846/22-27, to detail the measures her Department will be taking to identify and remove illegally erected political advertising billboards.
answer_text: The removal of unauthorised signs or advertisements on functional land owned by my Department is dealt with on a case by case basis as resources permit.When assessing the risks associated with billboards, my Department evaluates various factors related to road safety, including potential obstructions to vehicles and pedestrians, impairment of sightlines, and the likelihood of an errant vehicle colliding with the structure. In cases where unauthorised signage presents a clear safety hazard, its removal will be prioritised. However, when no immediate danger is identified, officials will collaborate closely with key stakeholders to determine an appropriate resolution. Billboards are typically subject to a planning application, and any breaches of planning regulations are generally addressed under planning legislation, which in most cases falls within the jurisdiction of the relevant Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16553/22-27, (i) for her assessment of the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch; and (ii) when this billboard will be removed.
answer_text: When assessing the risks associated with billboards, my Department evaluates various factors related to road safety, including potential obstructions to vehicles and pedestrians, impairment of sightlines, and the likelihood of an errant vehicle colliding with the structure. In cases where unauthorised signage presents a clear safety hazard, its removal will be prioritised. However, when no immediate danger is identified, officials will collaborate closely with key stakeholders to determine an appropriate resolution. It is my understanding that this Billboard has been removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to establishing an emergency fund to assist the agricultural sector in responding to potential future extreme weather events.
answer_text: A key objective of DAERA’s approach through the new Sustainable Agriculture Programme is to encourage farmers to better manage risk within their own businesses, to become more resilient and better prepared to cope in situations such as bad weather. A Crisis Framework that will enable my Department to assess potential industry impacts such as weather or market failure is planned to be developed as part of the Programme. This Framework will aim to determine the most appropriate timing and form of intervention necessary for a specific crisis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
