question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any options she is currently considering to improve the funding model for NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water has been provided with a total budget allocation of £0.5bn in 2024-25, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my department; this reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society.  I will continue to develop the three-pronged approach that is already in place.  Firstly, I will work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, my officials are examining the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions with a view to consulting on the proposals in March 2025.I acknowledge the significant financial pressures that NI Water is operating under; however, the Company must work within this funding and prioritise accordingly. As we seek to recover our public services from 14 years of austerity by the British Government there is a need for additional funding, and I will continue to make the case for this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any representations her Department has made to the British Government in relation to establishing a standalone internet protocol address to the north to end the geo-blocking of RTÉ broadcasts and programming.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have met with officials from the Department for Culture, Media and Sport to discuss this issue; I understand that the Secretary of State is writing to the Irish Minister for Arts, Culture, Communications, Media and Sport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of dental practices that have withdrawn from their NHS commitments in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) any actions his Department has taken to address this.
answer_text: Between the 1st April 2021 and 31st December 2024, 13 practices no longer have an arrangement with my Department to carry out Health Service dental work. The table below details this information broken down by Assembly Area. These practices may have closed completely, merged with other practices, transferred their patient lists to other dentists, or changed to treating private patients only. Dental practices no longer carrying out Health Service dental work between 1st April 2021 and 31st December 2024 by financial year and Assembly AreaAssembly AreaFinancial Year2021-222022-232023-242024-251BELFAST EAST11--BELFAST NORTH-1-1BELFAST SOUTH---1BELFAST WEST--1-EAST ANTRIM---1EAST LONDONDERRY----FERMANAGH AND SOUTH TYRONE--1-FOYLE1---LAGAN VALLEY----MID ULSTER----NEWRY AND ARMAGH----NORTH ANTRIM1---NORTH DOWN1---SOUTH ANTRIM----SOUTH DOWN---1STRANGFORD----UPPER BANN----WEST TYRONE--1-Total4234Count of dental practices no longer carrying out health service dental work based on financial year i.e. the 1st of April to the following 31st March. Latest financial year count is from the 1st of April 2024 to 31st December 2024, the reference date of the latest published figures, sourced from the dataset underpinning the Accredited Official Statistics release ‘Dental Services for Northern Ireland, Quarterly Series to Q3 2024/25 - Provisional’. Practices may include a number of Orthodontic and Specialist Practices. Orthodontics is a specialist area of dentistry concerned with the growth and development of the teeth and jaws and the prevention and treatment of abnormalities of this development. Therefore, most patients are children. These practices do not typically have registered patient lists, with instead patients normally seen on referral from a GDS dental practice. (Source: Business Services Organisation)		General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are independent contractors who are, unlike General Medical Practitioners, under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they treat under Health Service arrangements. They may also deregister their existing Health Service patients with three months’ notice.		A number of dental access initiatives were announced for the 2024/2025 financial year. This investment included £1m to fund an Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, £4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments, and £3.9m to fund a Dental Access Scheme for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients.  There are 29 practices participating in the scheme, providing access to dental care for around 500 patients each week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department has made available for addressing dereliction in towns and city centres.
answer_text: Support for tackling dereliction in town centres does not fall under the remit of my Department. Financial support  is however available from Councils e.g. Belfast City Council’s ‘Vacant to Vibrant’ grant scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how much of the £8.1 million, announced in February 2024 for road network repairs, has been spent; and (ii) where this funding has been spent, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: In February 2024, former Minister O’Dowd allocated £8.1m of capital structural maintenance funding to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding was allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions and is now fully expended.My Department does not manage the road network on a constituency basis, but I can confirm that the additional £8.1m for structural maintenance activities was allocated and spent as follows:Northern Division 	£2265kSouthern Division 	£2050kEastern Division	£915kWestern Division	£2870k

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any efforts they have made to aid Belfast's bid to host the Fleadh cheoil na heireann in 2026.
answer_text: Officials from my Department alongside Tourism NI have had ongoing engagement with Belfast City Council and Ards CCÉ in support of their bid to host the Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann in 2026. This includes active engagement in relation to potential funding sources. If successful, the Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann would be global showcase of our music, dance and language, creating community and social connections and would generate significant economic impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the five year survival rate for pancreatic cancer patients who have had the Whipple procedure in (i) the UK; (ii) Northern Ireland; and (iii) the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not hold comparable data for five-year survival rates for patients undergoing the Whipple procedure in the UK, Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland. However, clinical audit data is available for one-year survival rate for pancreatic patients who have had the Whipple procedure in Northern Ireland and England. Clinical audit data for pancreatic cancer patients who have undergone the Whipple procedure reports a one-year survival rate of 76% in Northern Ireland (audit of patients 2019-2020) while England reports a one-year survival rate of 81% (audit of patients 2020-2021).In Northern Ireland data is available from the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry and in England from the National Cancer Audit Collaborating Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of the digital world on children's literacy.
answer_text: Ensuring our children and young people are digitally literate and equipped to succeed in a digital world is a key priority for my Department.  In an era dominated by technological advancements, equipping students with digital proficiency is imperative for their future success.Working in collaboration with key stakeholders including the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment and industry professionals, my Department is progressing a number of actions which aim to enhance and integrate digital skills across all areas of the curriculum, at all key stages.  These focus on developing a digital curriculum framework, sharing of good practice, teacher training and professional development, and resourcing schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the bids he made to the Department of Finance on behalf of those impacted by cuts to the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26; and (ii) for his assessment of the removal of this funding.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 23144/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all Departure from Standard, as set out in the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges, on the A1 dual carriageway.
answer_text: The Department holds records of all approved Departures from Standards (DFS) implemented along the A1 since 1999.  The lists of DFS are extensive and due to the intensive resource requirement to compile, the commercially sensitive nature of this information and potential for the DFS to be protected by intellectual property rights, the Department unfortunately cannot provide the detail as requested. All DFS information relating to the Junctions Phase 2 scheme, which is currently in procurement, will remain commercially sensitive until approved and implemented in the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when her Department plans to resurface High Street, Newry.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, and due to years of austerity by the British Government, the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.  The 2025/26 resurfacing programme will be developed when the budgets are approved and included in the 2025/26 Annual Roads Report to Council that will be presented to Council and then published on my Department’s website in due course. In the meantime, staff will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of any funding provided by Invest NI to firms involved in the manufacture, sale or supply of weaponry to Israel.
answer_text: Invest NI does not support projects that supply arms to Israel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of parental complaints to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust about the Neuromuscular Paediatric Clinic in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.  Belfast Trust has advised that it does not routinely hold this information over the time period requested.  The scale of the work to secure the information would involve a historic review with a disproportionate impact on service capacity and costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created by foreign direct investment in Newry and Armagh in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2024 being the most recent available.  Over the period 1st April 2019 to the 31st March 2024, Invest NI provided support to externally owned businesses, within the Newry and Armagh Parliamentary Constituency area that will lead to the creation of 32 new jobs.Invest NI Inward Investment New Jobs in Newry and Armagh Parliamentary Constituency Area (2019-20 to 2023-24)YearNew Jobs2019-2042020-2102021-22212022-2352023-242Total32	During the same period, Invest NI also offered support to locally owned businesses that will lead to the creation of an additional 621 new jobs. In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal.  For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will conduct an assessment of the junction at Chancellors Road and Camlough Road, Newry, with a view to implementing necessary traffic calming and parking restriction measures.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  My Department has introduced traffic calming measures on the Camlough Road which include central hatching road markings, right turn pockets, pedestrian refuge islands and warning signs along with a 40mph speed limit. These measures have enhanced driver awareness, pedestrian safety and helped to reduce driver speeds by reducing road width through road markings.  There is also a controlled crossing between Chancellors Road and Derrymore Road which provides safe passage for pedestrians crossing Camlough Road. I have been advised that waiting restrictions in the form of double yellow lines are considered to address traffic progression or road safety issues in an urban area.  However, based on collision data provided by the PSNI, they would not be considered appropriate at this location. Therefore, I can advise that there are no current plans to introduce additional safety improvements at this time.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to her Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time; (ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions are overdue; and (iii) the average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: To date, four Assembly Written Questions tabled to my Department during the 2022-2027 mandate have not been answered on time.  Just to note, however, that a further three AQWs spanned the period when the Assembly wasn’t sitting.The four unanswered AQWs were overdue by an average of 16.5 days. While three AQWs were one day overdue, one AQW was 63 days overdue due to an administrative oversight.  Across the 2022-2027 mandate, to date, the average length of time taken to answer AQWs by my Department is 7.7 days.  That figure does not include the three AQWs that spanned the period when the Assembly wasn’t sitting.This is broken down as follows for each calendar year:2022 – 6.6 days2024 – 8.4 days2025 (to 20 February 2025) – 6.2 days

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 21022/22-27 and AQW 21023/22-27, to detail (i) why the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) does not use the scale rate expenses for accommodation and subsistence outlined on the HMRC website for NICS officials travelling on official business to Dublin; (ii) whether an Equality Impact Assessment has been conducted on the application of NICS subsistence arrangements for conducting official business in Dublin; and (iii) his assessment of the impact of the current application of NICS subsistence arrangements on the functions and outworkings of Stra
answer_text: HMRC scale rates are designed for tax compliance purposes for expense reimbursement within the UK tax system and organisations are not required to pay that level of subsistence.  The Civil Service here pays receipted actuals up to a ceiling subsistence rate, based on operational and budgetary considerations. Subsistence rates for civil servants travelling on official business to Dublin were last increased on 1 February 2024.Equality impact assessments are not carried out on the application of Civil Service subsistence rates to particular regions of travel.I have not been informed that the impact of Civil Service subsistence rates has had any detrimental effect on the important engagement on North-South Institutions and Implementation Bodies. Subject to other priority work, a further review of Civil Service subsistence rates will take place during 2025/6.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the financial support that has been made available to people who experienced damage to property as a consequence of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: With regards to domestic properties, I advise private homeowners to check with their insurance provider to determine what support, including financial, that may be available to those who experienced damage to their property. Tenants of the Housing Executive should contact the Housing Executive to report repairs, either through their dedicated telephone line, or tenant portal.  Private tenants should engage with their landlord to report repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to his Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time; (ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions are overdue; and (iii) the average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: As of 12 March 2025, of the 963 Assembly Written Questions tabled to my department during the 2022-27 mandate there were 158 which were not answered on time. Please note this figure excludes questions when the Assembly wasn’t sitting.The average length of time these unanswered questions were overdue was 7 days.The average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate was 8 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the core funding allocated to Samaritans NI in each of the last five years; (ii) the core funding that will be allocated to Samaritans NI in 2025-26; and (ii) his assessment of whether this funding meets the needs of Samaritans NI.
answer_text: The Samaritans received the following Core Grant Scheme Funding in the last five years:Core Grant FundingYear£2020/21 (25% uplift during pandenmic)15,5052021/2212,4042022/2312,4042023/246,2022024/256,202The Samaritans have not been successful in their application for Core Grant in 2025/26, which I understand will be disappointing for the organisation. My officials will be in contact with all applicants to offer feedback over the coming weeks, which will assist them in applying again in future funding rounds. I expect to issue another call for applications this Autumn for 2026/27 funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22459/22-27, to detail (i) the number of enforcement notices issued by her Department in relation to political advertising billboards erected on Department for Infrastructure land; (ii) the number of active licence agreements for party political advertising hoardings on Department for Infrastructure land; (iii) the number of political advertising billboards that have been removed from Department for Infrastructure land following enforcement; and (iv) whether she has instructed her officials to remove all political advertising billboards f
answer_text: For clarity, my officials understand that the Member’s question relates to party political advertising hoardings placed, without the Department’s permission, in functional departmental land, such as road verges and roundabouts.My Department does not hold records of the number of enforcement notices issued in relation unauthorised signs or advertisements, including those of a political nature, in a readily accessible format. To try to provide this information would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.With regard to advertising hoardings erected on non-functional land belonging to the Department, the Department has a number of licences in place with advertising firms but is not aware of any advertising of a political nature being displayed on these legal advertising hoardings. The removal of unauthorised signs or advertisements on functional land is dealt with on a case by case basis as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the primary reasons for 1,900 farm businesses not qualifying for the Farm Sustainability Payment.
answer_text: Under World Trade Organisation rules, it is permissible to set production requirements in a reference period to ensure that payments are directed towards farm businesses that are actively engaged in agricultural activity.  Given that the purpose of the Farm Sustainability Payment is to enable otherwise viable farm businesses to withstand market and other shocks, the inclusion of businesses that do not undertake agricultural production or produce only grass silage cannot be justified.Similarly, those farm businesses that leased out all of their Basic Payment Scheme entitlements during the historic year period of 2020 and 2021 will not be eligible to claim the Farm Sustainability Payment. From 1 January 2026, applicants to the Farm Sustainability Payment must for the year 2020 or 2021 meet the requirements as specified in the The Farm Sustainability (Transitional Provisions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the amount of funding her Department allocated to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme; (ii) the amount of this funding allocated to applicants; (iii) the level of underspend in the scheme; and (iv) whether the remaining funding will be allocated or carried forward.
answer_text: When coming into office, Conor Murphy agreed to provide up to £10 million to fund three schemes that provided additional support to businesses impacted by the severe flooding in November 2004, i.e. the Enhanced flood Support Scheme (EFSS) and two hardship funds. There was no specific amount of funding set aside for the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme (EFSS).  Letters of Offer to the value of approximately £4.5 million have been issued to applicants in relation to the scheme. Whilst it was estimated that the flood schemes would cost up to £10 million, the final cost of delivering the schemes was less than anticipated.  Conor had, therefore, asked officials to explore options for utilising any unused funding, including options to transfer funding to other Departments, but no realistic deliverable options were identified given the time frame available. However, before leaving office, Conor asked officials to explore options for implementing a more permanent flood support scheme for businesses similar to schemes operating in Britain.  In recognition of the increased number of flood incidents over the past few years, he also felt it would be an opportune time to include a review of support for everyone impacted by flooding, including residents, sports clubs etc.  He, therefore, instructed officials to establish a cross departmental group to explore options for future flood schemes to support recovery and resilience for all those impacted by flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the engagement and correspondence she has had with her counterpart in the Republic in relation to incentivising the construction of data centres; and (ii) for her assessment of the approach being adopted in the Republic in relation to data centres.
answer_text: Invest NI does not offer financial support to this sector given its limited scope for employment creation and high capital costs. However, the North does offer a skilled workforce from over 20 companies that work on the construction, kit out and maintenance of datacentres internationally.		Invest NI has engaged with potential data centre investors seeking to identify sites for construction. The location of such sites is complex due to many factors including the energy intensity of the facility and, in certain instances, the need to be with within a specified distance from a similar facility. Any potential investor will need to engage directly with the appropriate utility provider to determine if adequate supplies are available for their demand requirements.		Invest NI recognises the important role that data centres play in modern digital infrastructure, in facilitating increased digitalization of the economy and in supporting technology services and related activities such as cloud computing and artificial intelligence. In this context, Invest NI continues to hold discussions with data centre vendors and data centre users about developing the sector in the North. None of this requires engagement between Ministers in the north and the south.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schedule of upcoming road resurfacing works on the Moy Road, Armagh.
answer_text: Details of the planned resurfacing work on the A29 Moy Road, Armagh is included in Annual Report to Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council 2024.  However, to minimise the impact on traffic congestion, consideration is being given to carrying out the resurfacing work on Moy Road during the 2025 summer months when traffic volumes are lower.In the meantime, my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the merits of using CCTV in special education units as a tool to (i) improve educational practices; and (ii) provide protections for children and staff.
answer_text: Currently CCTV in schools is utilised to protect the school premises and grounds from vandalism and theft and is an external arrangement, with the exception of one internal camera covering the reception area at a building’s main entrance. The use of CCTV must comply with General Data Protection Regulations (GDPR) and the Data Protection Act and also consider the privacy of those who may be captured by the system, in a classroom setting this includes the pupil as well as teaching and non-teaching staff.  There are no plans to install CCTV in classrooms.  The installation of an internal CCTV system is only considered in exceptional circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any barriers currently preventing him from publishing an anti-poverty strategy; and (ii) a time frame for the publication of an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: My intention remains to present a paper on the Anti-Poverty Strategy to the Executive before the end of March.	Final decisions on the content, publication and implementation of the Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions planned under the Housing Supply Strategy to address housing shortages in Newry and Armagh in the next three years.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Executive Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December, work is underway to establish cross-Departmental structures to develop the first action plan, which will be focussed on addressing the whole system issues that impact on overall supply across all of Northern Ireland.This work will supplement actions taken by Local Councils, alongside the Housing Executive Place Shaping teams, to address local issues that may impact on housing requirements. This includes through the delivery of Local Development Plans, which set out the zonings and policies required to ensure the spatial growth requirements of the local area can be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of the schedule for road resurfacing works in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, and due to years of austerity by the British Government, the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. Details of the current planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Armagh and Newry areas are listed in my Department’s respective reports to Councils. The 2025/26 reports will be provided to councils in due course.In the meantime, my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s road maintenance policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to her Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time; (ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions are overdue; and (iii) the average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: As of 14 February 2025, there were a total of 1001 Assembly Written Questions tabled to my Department during the 2022-27 mandate which were not answered on time, with the average length of time overdue being 3 days.The average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in in each year of the 2022-27 mandate are as follows:2022 – 7 days2024 – 11 days2025 – 11 days

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to his Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time; (ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions are overdue; and (iii) the average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Historically DoH receives almost 30% of the overall AQWs across all Departments. As of 14 February 2025, there were a total of 769 Assembly Written Questions tabled to my Department during the 2022-27 mandate which were not answered on time, with the average length of time overdue being 2.65 working days. The average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-27 mandate is as follows:2022 – 3.63 working days2024 – 3.08 working days2025 – 0.41 working days

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to his Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time; (ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions are overdue; and (iii) the average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: The requested information can be derived from the Assembly Questions database which is publicly available on the Northern Ireland Assembly website.However, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, the requested information is as follows;(i) As at 16 February 2025, the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to my Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time is 219.(ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions were overdue is 5.09 days.(iii) the average length of time taken to answer Assembly Written Questions in 2024 was 6.67 days and in 2025 is currently 5.56 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the budget his Department has allocated to the development and regeneration of blueways and greenways in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The Final Budget for 2025-26 has not yet been agreed by the Executive. However, based on my Draft Budget proposals there are no plans, at this stage, for any funding to be allocated within my Department to the development and regeneration of blueways and greenways in 2025-26.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget her Department has allocated to the development and regeneration of blueways and greenways in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: I have not taken any decisions on the outcome of the draft 2025-26 Budget for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18277/22-27, to detail the results of the assessment for a pedestrian crossing at the Dublin Road and Chancellors Road junction.
answer_text: My officials have added a pedestrian survey, at the Dublin Road and Chancellors Road junction in Newry, to their assessment programme and will be carried out as soon as resources permit.  If the assessment meets the threshold, it will be considered for inclusion in a future works’ programme, subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of Assembly Written Questions tabled to their Department during the 2022-2027 mandate that have not been answered on time; (ii) the average length of time these unanswered questions are overdue; and (iii) the average time taken to answer an Assembly Written Question in each year of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: In respect of the 2022-27 mandate, the Executive Office received AQWs from 5 February 2024. In relation to the information requested, the figures for the period up to 13 February 2025 are: 416 21 working days The average time taken to answer an AQW in 2024 was 21 working days and 7 working days in 2025 to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the environmental impact on Benson's Glen from the current proposed alignment of the Newry Southern Relief Road scheme.
answer_text: As part of the Environmental Assessment process undertaken by my Department, the impact the proposed alignment would have on its surrounding areas, including Benson’s Glen was considered. The assessment reviewed aspects such as air quality, landscape and visual effects, biodiversity and road drainage, and the water environment.Scheme proposals including the Environmental Impact Assessment Report are currently open to statutory public consultation.  This consultation commenced on 21 January 2025 and will remain open until 4 March 2025.  I would encourage all members with an interest in this scheme to submit their views via Newry Southern Relief Road Consultation | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that changes to agriculture property relief will have on farming families.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of funded beds in each of the last ten years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: The total available beds in each Health and Social Care hospital broken down by quarter can be found at hs-inpatient-hts-tables-23-24.xlsxThis contains data for each of the last ten years, for all Acute, Elderly Care, Maternity & Child Health, Mental Health, and Learning Disability Programmes of Care. Specific details on how many of these available beds are funded is not held by the Department.On 9th November 2023, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) launched ‘encompass’ - a new electronic patient record system. Its rollout across the other Trusts will follow on a phased basis throughout 2024/25. Bed data on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern HSCT are still being developed following the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records. Therefore, only South Eastern HSCT data until 8th November 2023 has been included in this file.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 21576/22-27, to detail how much of her Department’s expenditure on external consultancy has been directed to firms with headquarters in the north.
answer_text: The Department does not hold details on the location of the headquarters of the firms which have provided it with external consultancy services. As a result, it is not possible for the Department to identify how much external consultancy was directed to firms with headquarters in the North of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what (i) engagement; and (ii) consultation her Department has had with (a) local authorities; (b) communities; and (c) statutory bodies in the Republic of Ireland in relation to the Newry Southern Relief Road scheme.
answer_text: My Department has engaged and consulted with a wide variety of stakeholders in the north and south on the Newry Southern Relief Road scheme.As part of the Environmental Impact Assessment process, consultations were undertaken to inform the associated report.  Details of the department’s engagement can be found at Newry Southern Relief Road - overview | Department for Infrastructure In addition, various engagement events have been held between 2017 and 2025, with the most recent being an Orders Exhibition in January 2025.  This event was open to members of the public, and stakeholders, across the island of Ireland.The current public consultation will continue until 4 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process for erecting a billboard on her Department's property for political advertising.
answer_text: My Department does not permit the erection of billboards on departmental land for the purposes of political advertising, however as a revenue raising measure, it may enter into licence agreements for advertising hoardings on DfI land. The applicant must provide proof of planning permission for their advertisement under the Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (NI) 2015.  In the event of it being considered by the Department that any advertisement or advertising matter displayed on the sign is of an objectionable or unsuitable nature the licensee will receive notice in writing to that effect with a requirement to immediately remove the sign.Election posters are subject to control under the above planning regulations.   Under these regulations election posters may be displayed, without submitting a planning application, provided they do not pose a risk to road users, including pedestrians using pavements, and that they are removed within 14 days after the close of the poll.Article 87 of the Roads Order makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts, but Article 87(5) exempts advertisements relating to pending elections for Parliament, the NI Assembly or district councils.My Department’s policy in relation to this matter can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/advertisements-placed-within-road-boundaries-rsppg-s035

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact that a fixed bridge across the Newry Canal will have on (i) the future economic potential of the Canal; and (ii) ongoing efforts to regenerate the Canal.
answer_text: My Department has engaged extensively with the Department for Communities (DfC) Historic Environment Division (HED), who have responsibility for the designated Scheduled Monument of Newry Ship Canal which is considered as being of high historical significance. The decision to proceed with a fixed bridge option was taken during the period the Assembly was not sitting and was based on engineering, heritage, economic and environmental matters. Both design options would permit vessels access to the Albert Basin.  Whilst the lifting bridge would allow unhindered navigation, an opening bridge would cause restrictions on the strategic route when operational creating the potential for further traffic congestion.  The fixed bridge option provides a clearance of 12m height, which would provide access for the majority of vessels using the Newry Canal, thereby maintaining the function of the canal.However, I am cognisant of the concerns of the local community in relation to the implementation of a fixed bridge, and following the conclusion of the current period of statutory public consultation on 4th March 2025 regarding the proposals and detailed assessments for the Newry Southern Relief Road, I will then consider responses as part of the consultation process, including whether or not to move forward to public inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the lack of dual diagnosis for people experiencing both addiction and mental health issues.
answer_text: The issue of dual diagnosis or co-occurring mental health and substance use issues, is recognised as a strategic priority in both the Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’, and the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. The HSC Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan 2024-2028 also prioritises improved pathways of care for people with co-occurring mental health and substance use issuesPeople with co-occurring mental health and substance use issues currently receive a service within either mental health services or addiction services with Health and Social Care Trusts. However, it is important to ensure that treatment and support is easily accessed by those who need it, and that people with co-occurring issues get the right treatment at the right time, by the right team in order to support them during recovery.  To progress this work, a co-occurring mental health and substance use project manager has been successfully appointed by Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group. This project manager will support a Regional Co-Occurring task and finish group to help improve pathways within HSCTs for those with co-occurring issues and reduce barriers between services, by promoting a “No Wrong Door” approach. This work is in addition to a number of other relevant workstreams in line with both the HSC Substance Use Strategic and Implementation Plan and the Mental Health Action Plan to promote integrated pathways between Mental Health and Substance Use services, in line with best practice.The work of this group will link with the newly established Regional Mental Health Service, and the development of the Regional Mental Health Crisis service.While this is a strategic priority for my Department, any decision to commission new or additional services will be taken in line with future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the reasons behind the 18 per cent drop-off in the participation level in sport for disabled people over the last five years.
answer_text: The drop off in the participation level in sport for people with a disability is likely the result of a complex range of issues, including but not limited to:	The lasting negative impact of Covid-19 on sports participation habits.	The provision of fewer sports opportunities for disabled people.	The disruption of Governing Body of Sport programmes by Covid-19 resulting in fewer opportunities for disabled people.	The negative impact of the cost of living crisis on disabled people’s ability to pay for and travel to sports activities.		Increasing participation in sport and physical activity for people with a disability is identified as a key objective in ‘Active Living’ - the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland. 		Over the past 5 years my Department and Sport NI have provided, through Exchequer and National Lottery funding, £7.4m investment into Disability Sport to increase suitable participation opportunities for people with a disability and to provide support to disabled athletes. This funding has been provided to Disability Sport NI, Special Olympics Ulster, National Governing Bodies and other disability sports organisations.	 		The 2023/24 Continuous Household Survey showed that 28% of people with a disability had taken part in sport on one or more days per week, compared to 56% for people who do not have a disability. These figures demonstrate a clear gap in participation figures and show that there is still work to be done.		My Department and Sport NI will continue to work with the disability sport sector to provide support, identify and address barriers to participation and to increase opportunities for people with a disability to participate in sport and physical activity that is suitable to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to mental health services for people facing homelessness.
answer_text: Whilst I have not had any correspondence with the Health Minister regarding mental health services for people facing homelessness, I am due to attend a meeting of the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention on Thursday 3 April 2025 where mental health services for people facing homelessness may be discussed

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer (i) AQW 12069/22-27; (ii) AQW 12070/22-27; (iii) AQW 12071/22-27; (iv) AQW 12410/22-27; (v) AQW 13355/22-27; (vi) AQW 19419/22-27; (vii) AQW 21313/22-27; (viii) AQO 1176/22-27; and (ix) AQO 1365/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 12069/22-27 was answered on 25 February 2025.AQW 12070/22-27 was answered on 25 February 2025.AQW 12071/22-27 was answered on 25 February 2025.AQW 12410/22-27 was answered on 25 February 2025.AQW 13355/22-27 was answered on 23 February 2026.AQW 19419/22-27 was answered on 27 June 2025.AQW 21313/22-27 was answered on 8 October 2025.AQO 1176/22-27 was answered by deputy First Minister during Question Time on 25 November 2024.AQO 1365/22-27 was answered on 8 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any supports available to homeless people trying to gain employment.
answer_text: My Jobs and Benefits offices provide tailored Work Coach support that recognises the need to help homeless claimants move closer, into or to stay in work.  They offer a personalised, practical approach to employment support, focused on removing barriers to work and will apply flexibility as regards looking for work in recognition of the increased possibility of fluctuating circumstances and health conditions a homeless claimant may experience. They help claimants write a CV, complete job applications, search for jobs and prepare for interviews.  They promote my Department’s Work Experience programme, the non-repayable grant to help remove a barrier to work available from my Adviser Discretion fund and the skills opportunities offered by the Department for the Economy.  They offer help to manage a health condition through participation in the Condition Management programme and signpost to organisations offering other specialist help services.To increase our presence in the community the Jobs and Benefits offices have developed established links with local homelessness organisations including hostels, housing providers, foodbanks, charities, and advice organisations and offer to deliver their services onsite.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she has taken to address the staffing issue affecting extended practical driving tests at the Newry Test Centre.
answer_text: An extended practical driving test is required for those who have been mandatory disqualified following an offence or repeated offences.  The extended test allows more time for the assessment of a driver’s ability over a wider variety of road and traffic conditions and for behavioural or attitudinal problems to emerge during the test.The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) offers extended practical driving tests at Belfast, Craigavon, Derry and Newry test centres, candidates can apply to take their extended practical driving test at any of these test centres.Due to the temporary unavailability of a driving examiner in Newry, the DVA has put in place arrangements to ensure the continuation of extended practical driving tests at this location.  The DVA has also plans to train more driving examiners to conduct these tests at the four locations where they are offered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Rural, Community Development and the Gaeltacht in Ireland in relation to the funding challenges facing Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: I have not yet had the opportunity to have discussions with the Minister for Rural and Community Development and the Gaeltacht since his appointment last month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost of car insurance in the North in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the requested information. The industry body, the Association of British Insurers (ABI) do not hold motor insurance premium data for the North as their data is held at a UK level and is not broken down by region.The ABI issues a quarterly motor insurance premium tracker which you can view online here: https://www.abi.org.uk/news/news-articles/?category=37

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given recent media reports that over £45,000 per day is being spent on non-standard accommodation for those experiencing homelessness, to detail his plans to ensure that housing solutions and homelessness prevention services are given adequate funding in the 2025-26 Budget.
answer_text: The Finance Minister launched a public consultation on the draft 2025-26 Budget in December 2024 and this process is ongoing.	Work is ongoing within my Department to align budget allocations with priorities and Programme for Government objectives.  Final decisions will depend on the outcome of the budget consultation. Therefore, at this stage, I am unable to confirm 2025-26 funding allocations for housing solutions and homelessness prevention services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the New Foundations Programme which aims to prevent homelessness among young people leaving care; and (ii) to detail the organisations and partners involved in the development of this programme; and (iii) to detail the timescale for its development and implementation.
answer_text: The New Foundations action plan is currently being developed in collaboration with a range of statutory and sectoral partners. However, this work is in the initial stages and the scope of the pilot programme is yet to be agreed. Consequently, a timescale for delivery is yet to be determined.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, whether they intend to take any action for victims and victims’ families in light of testimonies given thus far in the Omagh Bomb Public Inquiry.
answer_text: We welcome the establishment of the Omagh Bomb Public Inquiry and that direct arrangements for support have been put in place by the Inquiry to assist those giving evidence, and those who wish to attend at any of the Inquiry’s public hearings.Victims impacted by the Omagh bomb can also access established support arrangements through the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS), including advocacy and health and wellbeing support. This financial year, our department has provided over £3.1m in funding to groups in the Omagh area through VSS’s Victim Support Programme.VSS also provides funding to organisations who are not located within Omagh but also provide support to families who will be impacted by the Omagh Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to take any action in relation access to justice for victims and victims’ families in light of testimonies given thus far in the Omagh Bomb Public Inquiry.
answer_text: The Omagh Bombing Inquiry is an independent statutory public inquiry, established by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland, to investigate whether the bombing in the town of Omagh in August 1998 could reasonably have been prevented by UK state authorities. The Inquiry is considering the adequacy of the measures taken by a number of state authorities, including the police, to disrupt those dissident republican terrorists who had been involved in terrorist attacks or attempted terrorist attacks in the period leading up to the Omagh Bombing. While my Department has no role in the conduct of the Inquiry, I have been deeply moved by the testimonies given thus far and I fervently hope that victims and victims’ families are able to access justice through this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the Chief Constable’s recovery plan; and (ii) to detail how her Department intends to support the Chief Constable in meeting his priorities.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely with the PSNI to develop a robust business case for the funding required to recover workforce numbers over the course of the current mandate.  I am pleased to confirm that the business case is now ready to be submitted to the Department of Finance (DoF) for its consideration.  However, affordability remains a key issue in terms of business case approval.  Subject to the DoF approval, I will be making a case to the Executive for the additional funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he is supporting the GAA in their effort to host an NFL game in Dublin in 2025.
answer_text: I have no plans to support efforts to host an NFL game in Dublin in 2025 as this is not a responsibility of my Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any initiatives their Department has supported in relation to guidance for women and girls on personal protection, including self-defence instruction, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: A number of initiatives have been identified, details of which can be found in the table below.NORTH BELFASTCRJI Women’s Focus GroupA focus group highlighted the issue of violence against women and girls.The delivery partner created a safe space, in which before or after the workshops the women can stay to chat or discuss any concerns.A martial arts trainer, who specialises in self-defence provided six tailored sessions to the group.The delivery partner provided literature around Women’s Aid and support services.CRJI Girls Self DefenceThe delivery partner delivered a specific programme with younger girls which may help to reduce their potential vulnerability.They increased their awareness of Community Safety issues, risk factors, assessing situations, looking at increasing protective factors.They ran self-defence sessions with the girls and also looked at the theme of social media and feminism and empowerment.The community safety officer worked alongside two youth facilitators to deliver. Each session last approximately two hours, one hour comprising of self-defence workshops and risk assessing and the other would focus on community safety and young woman and associated key themes around this.NB the delivery partner also ran a vape testing kit project, whilst this was not solely for girls. Young girls would have taken part in this project.  The aim of it was to identify opioids that the young people may not be aware are present in the vape oils. LURGANCRJI Girls Self DefenceThe delivery partner completed a specific programme with younger girls which they believed may help to reduce their potential vulnerability, via increasing their awareness of Community Safety issues.Topics such risk factors, assessing situations, looking at increasing protective factors were covered.There was also self-defence classes held whilst also looking at the theme of social media and feminism and empowerment.The community safety officer worked alongside two youth facilitators to deliver. Each session last approximately two hours, one hour comprising of self-defence workshops and risk assessing and the other would focus on community safety and young woman and associated key themes around this.NB Like North Belfast the delivery partner also ran a vape testing kit project that young girls would have participated in.WEST BELFASTUpper Springfield Development Trust (along with Community Partner - Falls Residents Association) Common Sense DefenceThis 8-week programme will focus on personal safety, self-awareness, self-defence and education.The programme was around types of domestic abuse among women aged 21-45.The programme was structured around themes such as coercive control, physical and sexual violence and the impact on families and children.  This programme helped aid women in understanding the signs of domestic abuse, what action they can take and types of support available.  The programme was completed with the help of the PSNI and Nexus and will carry out activities s

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure that special educational needs (SEN) transport operators are not forced to reduce the number of journeys they provide to pupils with SEN due to the rise in employers National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the delivery of school transport and this includes the management of contracts with private transport operators. Transport is provided for around 10,000 pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) who have additional transport requirements and over half of these pupils are transported through the use of the EA’s own bus fleet. Transport for the remainder of these pupils with SEN is provided through contracts the EA holds with private bus and taxi operators.The increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions represents an additional cost to education, including the home to school transport service delivered by the EA. The delivery of transport for eligible pupils, however, is a statutory duty and there is no indication that transport operators will be forced to reduce the number of journeys they provide. The Chancellor has stated that support will be provided for public sector employers in relation to the upcoming changes to employers' National Insurance Contributions. That said, the quantum has not yet been confirmed by HM Treasury. I can assure you that I will strongly advocate for these additional costs to the education sector to be met in full, as will my Executive colleagues for their departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any compensation that will be available to horticultural businesses to assist with repairs and crop damage following damage from Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Many people across Northern Ireland have been directly or indirectly impacted by Storm Éowyn and recovery from this very significant weather event will continue for some time.In terms of the availability of compensation for horticultural businesses, my department currently has no relevant financial support schemes.	I am aware the storm has caused damage to the assets of businesses in the horticulture sector and I appreciate the impact this is having on business owners.  		Therefore, although DAERA is not in a position to provide financial assistance, my officials within the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) are available to provide technical and advisory support to assist horticulture businesses with their recovery and indeed to help enhance their longer-term resilience to our increasingly turbulent climate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with (i) Foras na Gaeilge; and (ii) the North South Ministerial Council in relation to the impact of funding cuts on the promotion of the Irish language in the north.
answer_text: Foras na Gaeilge have delegated legislative responsibility for the promotion of the Irish language in this jurisdiction and it is for Foras na Gaeilge, and its Board, to make judgements regarding the impact of their financial decisions.North South Ministerial Council (NSMC) meetings are the established forum for Ministers from both jurisdictions to discuss matters relating to North/South Bodies. I chaired the most recent NSMC Language Sectoral meeting on 29 May 2024. 	My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Irish government, and with the North/South Language Body agencies, on all matters relating to North/South Language Body sponsorship, including funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of funding cuts by Foras na Gaeilge on the promotion of the Irish language in the north.
answer_text: Foras na Gaeilge have delegated legislative responsibility for the promotion of the Irish language in this jurisdiction and it is for Foras na Gaeilge, and its Board, to make an assessment of the impact of financial decisions on the language sector when agreeing its annual business plan. My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Irish government, and with the North/South Language Body agencies, on all matters relating to North/South Language Body sponsorship, including funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any financial assistance that exists for homeowners seeking to install a heat pump in their home.
answer_text: At present, financial assistance is available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP), for fully supported, and part funded schemes, for the installation of energy efficiency measures and heat pumps, with most of the funding targeted at lower income customers.	My department has recently consulted on future support of low carbon technologies in residential properties which closed 10th January 2025. The views provided will support my department in the development of policy in the area of heat decarbonisation to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, which will include heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any actions on Casement Park that will be incorporated into the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The Programme for Government 2024-2027 and associated documents are available on the Executive’s website at  https://www.northernireland.gov.uk/publications/programme-government-2024-2027-our-plan-doing-what-matters-most-documents

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the extent to which youth sports participation will be incorporated into the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The Programme for Government was agreed by the Executive on 27th February 2025 and by the Assembly on 3rd March 2025.It can be viewed here: https://www.northernireland.gov.uk/publications/programme-government-2024-2027-our-plan-doing-what-matters-most-documents.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any shared island initiatives that will be incorporated into the Programme for Government; and (ii) whether the commitment in the Irish Government’s Programme for Government to increase the Shared Island Fund by a further £1 billion up to 2035 will be reflected in their final Programme for Government 2024-27.
answer_text: The Programme for Government was published on 3 March 2025 and approved by the NI Assembly on 3 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for an update on the publication of a Programme for Government; and (ii) for their assessment of whether the absence of a Programme for Government has impacted on the Executive's delivery.
answer_text: Consultation on the draft Programme for Government began on the 9th September 2024, and ran for 8 weeks, closing on 4th November. A consultation on the corresponding impact assessments ran for twelve weeks in line with the Equality Commission’s guidance and closed on 2nd December 2024.In total 1,375 responses were received. All responses have now been analysed and a consultation report is being finalised. We hope to bring the Programme for Government to the Executive for consideration. Following Executive agreement, we will finalise the Programme for Government and bring it to the Assembly for cross-community approval. Over the last 12 months the Executive have delivered significant outcomes for workers, families, and communities, including Public Sector Pay, improvements in Childcare provision and a strategy to End Violence Against Women and Girls. We know that there is much more to do in the remainder of this mandate, and we will continue to build upon what we have been able to achieve for people in this first year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre and Dublin City University's findings that economic inactivity in the north stands at 22.5 per cent; and (ii) to detail any action her Department is taking to reduce economic inactivity rates.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the All-Island Economic Intelligence produced by Ulster University Economic Policy Centre and Dublin City University and believe it will prove to be a very useful tool for making labour market comparisons across the island.In the third quarter of 2024, the North had the highest rate of people outside of the labour market when compared with regions across the island. The North’s so called economic inactivity rate (for ages 20-64) stood at 22.5%, while the average in the Republic of Ireland was 17.1%. It is notable that in the final quarter of 2012, the North had a lower Economic Inactivity rate than any other region of Ireland and my department is seeking to rectify this trend.The research highlights the need for further upskilling and reskilling in the North. Although the Department for Communities has lead policy responsibility for employability and reducing economic inactivity, my Department will continue to make an important contribution through our skills and education remit.Education, training, and skills development opportunities are available at our Higher Education Institutions, Further Education (FE) Colleges, and through a range of other training providers. For FE learners, there is a range of financial support available. My Department also runs skills and education programmes, such as Skills for Life and Work, Skill Up, Step Up and All Age Apprenticeships. Furthermore, we work with the Department for Communities to support individuals into employment through the Labour Market Partnerships.I recognise the importance of adequate childcare support to enable parents to participate in education and employment. To this end, £740k of the Skills Fund was allocated this year to support pathways into the childcare sector. Finally, our 2024 Good Jobs consultation sought views on several policy proposals which would help those with ill health to remain within or access the labour market. Ultimately, this is a complex and persistent issue which will require a range of approaches and collaboration across government agencies and the private sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how cancer patients in the north can access lifesaving Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-cell therapy in Dublin to avoid the necessity to travel by plane to London, Manchester or Glasgow.
answer_text: Currently chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy (CAR-T) cannot be delivered in Northern Ireland.  HSCNI has arrangements with NHS centres in Great Britain to treat patients requiring CAR-T.  Work to enhance and expand specialist haematology capacity through refurbishment and expansion of ward space in the Belfast Trust to accommodate this service locally is in progress.The extant arrangement for commissioning specialist care which cannot be delivered in Northern Ireland is referral to wider NHS.  Hospital services, referrals and care pathways across the UK operate within the same clinical, safety, quality and governance standards providing an extant level of assurance on referrals and consistency of care.Consideration of using providers outwith the NHS is possible but would require a case to be made setting out why this was necessary given ready access to the pre-assured NHS network.  Achieving the same levels of assurance with providers in ROI is possible but given the differences in systems, these would need time to work through in detail to confirm that assurance.Given the timelines envisaged for local delivery of CAR-T a process to establish a second provider arrangement in ROI could prove nugatory as once capacity is established locally patients will not be required to travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the funding provided by his Department for the Rates Support Grant in each of the last ten years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of his Department’s expenditure on the maintenance of inland waterways in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the maintenance of the Lagan Navigation which includes the Lagan Towpath and associated assets from Stranmillis to Blaris, and from Moira to Aghalee, with an average annual expenditure over the last 10 years of approximately £150,000. My Department also has a statutory duty to ensure that the entrance to Sixmilewater River remains navigable, and maintains two navigational marker posts in that location, with additional discretionary maintenance of 46 navigation marker posts on Lough Neagh, with an average annual expenditure of approximately £68,000. The dredging of the fisherman’s quays around Lough Neagh is estimated to cost £60,000-£65,000 annually, depending on the volume of material removed.My Department also carries out cyclical inspection and maintenance on the Newry Canal, as a designated watercourse, on an annual basis, with an average annual expenditure of approximately £9,200.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP shortages, broken down by surgery.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not collected or held by my Department.  GP Partners in a General Medical Services Practice are Independent Contractors rather than employees within the Health and Social Care System. GMS Contractors may employ salaried GPs according to the needs of their practice.My Department recognises the ongoing challenges with the GP workforce across Northern Ireland.  Against the background of a difficult financial climate, the Department continues its focus on building the workforce. A number of schemes have been introduced and developed for this purpose including:  the ‘Attract, Recruit and Retain’ scheme, the ‘GP Retainer’ scheme, the ‘GP Mentor’ scheme, the ‘GP Fellowship’ scheme, the GP Induction and Refresher’ scheme, the International Induction Programme (IIP) and the Return to Practice (RtP) scheme.My Department has worked with key stakeholders, to increase the exposure of undergraduates to General Practice; encourage more GP trainees; and ensure that there are sufficient GP training practices to meet demand and support GPs in training.

It is a list on which all doctors are legally required to be included in order to work as a GP in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any action being taken to implement the outstanding recommendations outlined in Lord Justice Gillen’s Review of Civil and Family Justice.
answer_text: While my Department did not commission the Gillen Review of Civil and Family Justice, and the majority of recommendations fall outside my remit, and are instead matters for the judiciary, the legal professions and the Departments of Health and Finance, the Review has been instrumental in informing my priorities for civil and family justice reform under the Modernisation Programme. My recently published Enabling Access to Justice Programme Delivery Plan addresses a number of themes raised in the Review and I have no doubt Sir John’s detailed work will continue to inform policy development both within my Department and others.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any further improvements her Department is planning in relation to rail services between Belfast and Dublin in the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:October 2024 saw a seismic change in the Cross-Border rail timetable, increasing the frequency from 8 to 15 return services per day, almost doubling the capacity.  In addition, further enhancements in the seat reservation system, a review of the pricing structure and the opening of Belfast Grand Central Station have all contributed to significant growth on the corridor.  In the first 3 months of operation demand for cross-border rail services has increased by a staggering 49%, up from 260,932 (October 2023 – January 2024) to 389,333 (October 2024 to January 2025).  Further enhancements are due in Spring 2025 when a revamped on-train catering offering is launched.  Plans are also at an advanced stage to procure new fleet for the route, with an announcement due before the end of the year.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the increase in passenger numbers following the introduction of the hourly Enterprise train services between Belfast and Dublin.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows.The hourly Enterprise cross-border rail services commenced on 29 October 2024.  Since then, some 389,333 customers have availed of the Enterprise service (29 Oct 2024 to 26 Jan 2025).  This is an increase of some 49% compared to the similar period last year (260,932 passengers from 23 October 2023 to 21 Jan 2024).For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any (i) domestic abuse; and (ii) trauma-informed training provided to professionals involved in family courts.
answer_text: Training is a matter for individual organisations. However, I am aware that the Judicial Studies Board, the Bar and the Law Society provide training on domestic abuse and coercive control. Court children’s officers (social workers who provide reports for court) fall under the remit of the Department of Health, but I have been advised that Department is supporting social workers to develop the right knowledge and skills to respond appropriately to those who have experienced domestic abuse.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.
