question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department’s plans to recruit trainee nursing staff locally; and (ii) whether his plans to reconfigure the hospital network will increase the number of locally-recruited nurses in training.
answer_text: (i) I am currently considering with my officials proposals for the commissioning of pre-registration nursing and midwifery programmes by my Department from local universities for the 2025/26 year. I anticipate, however, that this will be a very challenging process given the financial pressures facing my Department.(ii) Any major future service reconfiguration decisions will be the subject of full public consultation. I cannot, at this stage, predict how they might impact upon the future commissioning of pre-registration nursing and midwifery programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what the licensing terms will be with the private health sector in relation to accessing and utilising the encompass system.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a Discovery Project to establish a robust and secure framework to allow reasonable and appropriate access for relevant outside bodies and Independent Sector providers. This Project is in an early phase and will consider legal and contractual frameworks/guidance, Information Governance, Technical and Cyber infrastructure related matters.Discussions are also underway on the use of the EpicCare Link to provide managed, read only access to clinical information documented in encompass by other non-HSC bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department’s plans to recruit trainee GPs locally; and (ii) whether his plans to reconfigure the hospital network will increase the number of locally-recruited GPs in training.
answer_text: The GP Medical Specialty training programme in Northern Ireland is administered by NIMDTA on my Department’s behalf. Recruitment to this programme is performed on a UK-wide basis. I am currently considering with my officials proposals for the commissioning of the Northern Ireland GP training programme for entry in 2025/26. I anticipate, however, that this will be a very challenging process given the financial pressures facing my Department.Any major future service reconfiguration decisions will be the subject of full public consultation. I cannot, at this stage, predict how they might impact upon the future commissioning of GP medical specialty training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any service impacts that have arisen through the implementation of the encompass system; and (ii) whether any further service impacts are anticipated through its implementation.
answer_text: South Eastern, Belfast and Northern HSC Trusts all had service impact at the point of encompass implementation. This impact was commensurate with equivalent Go-Lives across the UK with plans reviewed with each department / division in relation to activity downturn and subsequent recovery for elective activity.  Both South Eastern and Belfast Trusts are back to normal activity.  Many of Northern Trust services have returned to previous levels of activity and they are continuing to work with others to return to baseline. Many services, especially within unscheduled care (Emergency Department / medical and surgical admissions) and maternity services carry on throughout Go-Live without decrease in service.Western & Southern HSC Trusts will Go-Live on 8th May 2025 and there will be an impact on service, however, learning from each implementation and stabilisation period has been used to support planning and to minimise impact on patients and clients

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the extent to which the encompass data management system is integrated across the entirety of the health and social care system.
answer_text: The implementation of encompass will create a single digital care record for every citizen in Northern Ireland who receives health and social care. To date encompass has been implemented in the South Eastern, Belfast and Northern HSC Trusts with the final two HSC Trusts, Southern and Western, set to Go-Live in May 2025.  The system spans Acute, Community & Social Care to provide a holistic record and enables staff across all disciplines in providing patients and service users with the safest, highest quality of care that is possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the redevelopment of Casement Park, including a timeframe for completion.
answer_text: In 2011, the Executive made a commitment of £62.5m towards the redevelopment of Casement Park. That commitment remains. I continue to engage with the UCGAA and other stakeholders in relation to this project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 18671/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 18671/22-27 was answered on 3 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the impact of the proposed cessation of funding for the Employment Support Scheme on people with disabilities; (ii) any measures his Department will be undertaking to mitigate the impacts of this on those who currently avail of the scheme; and (iii) any measures his Department has taken to ensure that those who will be most affected are aware of the consultation.
answer_text: An EQIA was published on 25th November 2024 for 12 weeks seeking the views of the public on the proposal to cease funding for the Employment Support Scheme.  This public consultation closes on 17th February 2025 and no final decision will be made until the public consultation process has concluded and report completed. As no final decision has been made, the Department has not considered any further mitigation measures required above and beyond the current suite of provision and support for customers including Workable (NI), Access to Work (NI), the Condition Management Programme, the Work Experience Programme, Labour Market Partnerships and a range of more general labour market interventions.  Officials have continuously engaged with the two providers of the Scheme informing them of this proposal, prior to the launch of and throughout the period of public consultation and they make participants aware of any changes including this proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the mechanisms by which prisoners at, or before, release can access rehabilitation and services that address the root cause of their incarceration.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) continues to prioritise rehabilitation work to support and challenge prisoners to change and address their offending behaviour.Evidence shows us the socio-economic factors which impact on offending include poverty and social deprivation, poor mental health, substance misuse, homelessness, low levels of educational attainment, and a lack of employment opportunities.  We use these factors to drive rehabilitation and resettlement in custody and provide continuing support as individuals transition through the justice system.The Prisoner Development Model (PDM) is the mechanism through which the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) plan and deliver rehabilitation for those in custody.  The PDM is a multi-agency approach focused on needs analysis, risk assessment and reduction of risk through structured personal development planning.The Model involves meetings with prisoners to assess their needs, strengths and risks and the creation of a plan to address them.  This will then include referral to suitable rehabilitative programmes, delivered by either NIPS staff or Voluntary and Community Sector and Statutory Partners.  The plan will record and report on actions taken to support the individual to address and reduce both the risk of harm and likelihood of further offending and prepare that prisoner for their return to the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average timeline for cases to be taken through the criminal justice system from reporting to disposal in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Figures for the average time for criminal cases dealt with at courts for the years 2014/15 to 2023/24 have been provided in the following table. Time taken for cases from date incident reported to disposal at court, 2014/15 - 2023/24YearMedian days2014/15               143 2015/16               146 2016/17               165 2017/18               162 2018/19               167 2019/20               149 2020/21               193 2021/22               226 2022/23               206 2023/24               190 Note:1. Figures relate to the number of calendar days between the listed dates.2. Figures relate to cases disposed at court during the time period specified. Each period is a financial year, running from 1st April to the following 31st March.3. Figures relate to cases dealt with at both Crown Court and magistrates’ courts.4. The measure of average used is the median. 5. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, Harbour and Airport police in Northern Ireland that resulted in a court disposal.  Appeals are not included.6. Figures do not include cases where the case was dismissed but a caution was administered, fixed penalty registrations, penalty notices for disorder or cases resulting from a breach of a court order. Successful youth engagement cases were also excluded.7. Figures in relation to the time taken to complete a case are normally calculated between the date on which the case is reported to the date it has been completed in court.  The Department’s publishes figures on average case processing times annually.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how and when families and businesses can access the goodwill payment called for by the First Minister and deputy First Minister.
answer_text: We are acutely aware of the significant impact on the thousands of people without power in the past days.  We met with the Managing Director from Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE) on Saturday 25 January and urged them to consider how they could provide support to families and businesses, and to consider provision of a goodwill payment for those impacted due to the impacts of Storm Éowyn.  We will continue to engage with NIE Networks on this issue and will share more details with the Assembly when possible.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their knowledge of (i) how electricity supply reconnections are being prioritised; and (ii) the action being taken to prioritise the reconnection of elderly and medically vulnerable residents.
answer_text: We are mindful of the impacts that Storm Éowyn had on all members of the public and especially on vulnerable people.Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE) prioritised and restored power to all affected customers in line with their restoration timeline and informed by their vulnerable customers list. From the initial establishment of the Strategic Co-ordination Group, a Vulnerability Cell met on a regular basis to assess prioritisation of vulnerable persons and to co-ordinate resources for them. This group continued to meet through the recovery phase as part of the work of the Recovery Co-ordination Group. In addition, a Humanitarian Assistance Cell was established to support those who would not normally be considered vulnerable but were impacted by the ongoing disruption to the distribution network. It is important to note that in line with the principle of subsidiarity each Department, including the Department for Economy in their role of lead government department for energy, are responsible for operational planning.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact that differentiated weather warnings North and South in advance of Storm Éowyn had upon (i) storm preparedness; and (ii) storm response.
answer_text: Civil contingencies structures were activated upon notification of the initial Yellow Warning by the Met Office on Wednesday 22 January 2025. The Met Office subsequently upgraded to a red weather warning.  This was a meteorological decision.  In line with established protocol, the inter-agency civil contingency structures co-ordinated efforts during both response and recovery phases.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether an equivalent to the Republic’s National Emergency Coordination Group has been established in the north.
answer_text: The NI Civil Contingencies Framework – Building Resilience Together - lays out arrangements for effective emergency management for preparing for, responding to, and recovering from an emergency.  The Strategic Co-ordination Group, as described in the Framework, sets strategic direction, co-ordinates responders and prioritises resources and has the overall responsibility for the multi-agency management of an incident.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the merits of a unified storm warning and response model across the island of Ireland.
answer_text: The Met Office (MO) and Met Éireann (ME) use different weather warning systems and as such there are sometimes different warnings in place in each jurisdiction. We understand that this position can cause confusion for some citizens. We encourage both the Met Office and Met Éireann to continue to work collaboratively in respect of their projections and simplifying their messaging. In respect of response, we have well exercised civil contingencies structures which were established from as early as the Wednesday before the storm.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 18596/22-27, for an updated timeline for the completion of Casement Park, in line with the First Minister's assertion that Casement Park will be built.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Irish Government in relation to enhanced cross-border healthcare cooperation.
answer_text: I had a good working relationship with the former Irish Health Minister, Stephen Donnelly.  Unfortunately, due to the Irish General Election, latterly we had to cancel both a North-South Ministerial Council Health Sectoral Meeting and a separate visit I had planned to make to Dublin.However, I have already written to Stephen Donnelly’s successor, Jennifer Carroll MacNeill, seeking an early meeting, so that we can discuss areas of mutual interest to the benefit of health and social care users in both jurisdictions. In the meantime, our respective officials continue to engage on a regular basis across a range of matters, as both Administrations seek to enhance cross-border cooperation where it is advantageous to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any initiatives her Department has supported in relation to guidance for women and girls on personal protection, including self-defence instruction, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department alongside the NI Policing Board provides funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) across Northern Ireland. PCSPs engage with local communities, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector to develop planned, purposeful and structured interventions, including awareness raising and early intervention to address crime, the fear of crime, anti-social behaviour and confidence in policing.While PCSPs do not routinely provide self-defence instruction, several PCSPs have provided funding for personal safety packs including personal alarms, however this has not been specific to women and girls. This year Ards and North Down PCSP funded a scenario-based 6-week course on personal protection for women and girls while community safety wardens in Derry City and Strabane District PCSP have been engaged in a number of initiatives over the last number of months on the safeguarding of women and girls. They have provided talks and presentations on safety advice to a variety of women’s groups within the district area and have worked in partnership with the PSNI in providing guidance on personal safety.South Belfast District PCSP has worked with the PSNI and Queens University of Belfast (QUB) to deliver a ‘Tackling Violence Against Women & Girls Project’.  This was a workshop aimed at giving local students some tools to be informed about techniques to practically defend themselves and feel safe. A three-hour Brazilian Jiu Jitsu workshop was delivered to QUB students while funding was also provided to purchase 50 licences of the Hollie Guard app, a personal safety app that includes features designed to be used in situations where people feel unsafe. These licences were distributed to girls and women in the wider university/lower Ormeau area.My Department also provides funding to community projects through the Assets Recovery Community Scheme, however, funding has not been provided on personal protection outside of attack alarms which were not specifically provided for women and girls. Ending violence against women and girls is a priority for all Executive Departments and it is right, therefore, that The Executive Office should lead on the Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG), launched last year.  The Department of Justice has an important role to play and is contributing to the delivery of EVAWG outcomes through a number of new measures that are helping to protect women and girls. These include the introduction of a range of new offences including up-skirting, down blousing, cyber-flashing, stalking and non-fatal strangulation; the introduction of new tools for law enforcement, such as Stalking Protection Orders to enhance protections and offender management; and new interventions to tackle domestic and sexual abuse that are being delivered under the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031, which launched in September 2024 and is jointly led by my Department and the Department

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what accommodations are made for children who are young in their school year.
answer_text: The School Age (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 allows the parents or guardians of Young for Year children to defer the start of pre-school and, in turn, primary school for their child for one year. Deferral of primary school starting age is available on parental request to any child born between 1 April and 1 July and to any child due to be born after 1 April but born before this date.The vast majority of children regardless of their age within the year group thrive at primary school and we have many excellent pre-schools and primary schools in Northern Ireland. Children start primary school with a whole range of abilities and at different levels of maturity. The Foundation Stage of the primary curriculum enables children to learn through well-planned, challenging play.  Teachers adapt their teaching to match the age and stage of development of the children in their classes. For example, in Foundation Stage classes, children who are young for the school year may be provided with appropriate, additional play-based learning opportunities to develop further their gross and fine motor skills.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the support currently available to assist first-time buyers with access to home ownership; and (ii) any options he is currently considering to enhance supports for first-time buyers.
answer_text: My Department currently provides significant investment to assist working people and families on lower incomes, particularly first-time buyers, into affordable home ownership, through funding for shared ownership.The Northern Ireland Co-Ownership Housing Association (Co-Ownership) is currently our sole delivery partner for shared ownership. Currently £158 million of funding has been approved to Co-Ownership to support over 4,400 households into homeownership between 2020/21 and 2024/25.Significantly, Co-Ownership requires smaller, and in some cases no, deposit. This is crucial as saving for a deposit can often be the most significant barrier to first-time buyers accessing the property market.You can find further information on Co-Ownership and the products it offers via its website: www.co-ownership.orgHowever, it is important to note that simply introducing further interventions for first-time buyers without a focus on increasing housing supply runs the risk of inflating house prices and locking more people out of the mortgage market. Housing officials continue to explore ways to increase supply across all tenures. My Housing Supply Strategy (https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/housing-supply-strategy-2024-2039), launched in December 2024, will be critical to addressing this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the British Government in relation to the north’s inclusion in a statutory levy to fund research and treatment for gambling harm.
answer_text: During September 2024, I wrote jointly with the Minister for Communities and the Minister of Education to the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, where we raised our concerns with Northern Ireland being excluded from the funds raised through the proposed statutory levy in Great Britain.  My officials and cross departmental officials will continue to engage with relevant DCMS colleagues to further discuss and explore the issues regarding the levy and how collaborative working between the nations can be maximised to improve outcomes for those who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 15451/22-27, for an update on his engagement with his counterpart in Dublin in relation to removing barriers for those who want to study in the south.
answer_text: Last year I engaged on this issue with my Ministerial counterpart in the South at the time, Simon Harris. I also sought a meeting with his successor, Minister O’Donovan.I intend to engage with James Lawless, the newly appointed Minister for Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science, to discuss actions that can be taken, separately and collectively, to open up greater opportunities for our young people to study throughout Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the measures his Department is taking in relation to storm preparedness.
answer_text: On receipt of the weather warnings issued by the Met Office, my Department immediately began to prepare to respond to the impacts of Storm Éowyn.My Department’s roads and rivers teams checked and cleared road gullies and other drainage infrastructure and additional staff were placed on standby to respond to incidents. Monitoring of river levels and sea levels was undertaken. Officials attended multi-agency co-ordination meetings and additional staff were brought in to deal with the expected increase in reported incidents and to prioritise and supervise response work. My Department also established a multi-agency infrastructure cell in the Traffic Information and Control Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Irish Minister for Health in relation to creating a streamlined process for residents in the north to register with the Nursing and Midwifery Board of Ireland.
answer_text: I very much recognise the benefits of working jointly with the Republic of Ireland on areas of health and social care where there are mutual benefits to be gained, not least with respect to the regulation of healthcare professionals. Although I have not had any engagement with the Irish Minister of Health on the specific issue raised, we did agree late last year, to mandate our officials to explore possible opportunities for collaboration on health workforce issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of wards in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust that have a funded pharmacy service, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: Please find below, detail on the number of wards in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust that have a funded pharmacy service, broken down by hospital site: LocationWards covered (inc ED):CAH16Bluestone6DHH7Lurgan3STH2

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current case list numbers in each planning office, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Councils are independent organisations responsible for their own functions and processes. The Department does not hold the requested information and the Member may wish to approach the relevant Councils directly for the relevant figutes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to preserve the Poet’s Trail in South Armagh.
answer_text: In general, Tourism NI does not have a specific remit to preserve or maintain trails, with this responsibility falling to either the private landowner, local council or another agency as appropriate.I understand that responsibility for the Poet’s Trail in South Armagh lies with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.The Poet’s Trail is currently listed on Tourism NI’s consumer website, www.discovernorthernireland.com as part of the offer available to visitors to the Ring of Gullion. Advice and guidance on ways to develop heritage assets, such as the Poet’s Trail, for visitors are available on www.tourismni.com. More broadly, Tourism NI is focused on the development of outdoor activities, including signature walks, as a key action to deliver the 10-year plan for tourism. Initially, a small set of signature walks aimed at families are being progressed in conjunction with Councils. In the first instance, I encourage the lead representative to engage with the tourism team at Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and the Experience Development Team at Tourism NI to discuss potential opportunities to further the development of the trail.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when a review of (i) safety; and (ii) lighting was last undertaken of Department for Infrastructure-maintained pedestrian tunnels at Abbey Way, Newry, and Boat Street, Newry.
answer_text: I am advised that when last structural inspection of the pedestrian underpasses at Abbey Way and Boat Street in Newry was undertaken, both were deemed to be in a good condition with only minor maintenance works required.  I have asked my officials to continue to monitor these underpasses and take forward any necessary repairs that meet my Department’s policy.I am advised that the lighting at the Abbey Way underpass is in poor condition and requires replacement and Boat Street does not currently have any lighting, however it does meet the criteria for its provision.  I have asked my officials to take forward lighting replacement and installation works at these underpasses in the 2025/26 financial year, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with his counterpart in Dublin in relation to expanding the remit of Waterways Ireland.
answer_text: To date, I have had no specific engagement with my counterparts in Dublin in relation to expanding the current remit of Waterways Ireland beyond what is set out in the establishing legislation. I would be open to starting this engagement should there be a compelling reason to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any action he has taken to facilitate the introduction of a unified rail fare structure, following the passage of the motion on All-island Rail Fares in the Assembly on 3 December 2024.
answer_text: As I explained to the Assembly during the All-island Motion on 3 December 2024, Translink and Irish Rail are subsidised in different ways.  In April 2022 the Irish Government reduced fares on all types of public transport by 20%.  My Department has been operating in difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  Consequently, a similar discount is not available for Enterprise tickets for journeys originating in the North.  Translink continues to work with Irish Rail to provide our passengers with the same access to on-line fares and seat bookings providing Flexible, Semi Flexible and Low fares up to 80 minutes before travel on cross border services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the Maximum Aggregate Student Number formula that constrains university enrolment numbers; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to update thresholds to allow universities to expand and thrive.
answer_text: Due to the significant level of public funding provided to the universities, the Department has to control spending to ensure it stays within its budget allocation. The Maximum Student Number (MaSN) limits the Department’s financial exposure and ensures that funding per place is maintained at a level appropriate to sustain quality. My Department maintains a dialogue with all of the higher education institutions on the full range of issues affecting their sustainability and growth and will shortly be beginning a review of higher education funding, including consideration of MaSN, which will help support the future growth and sustainability of the higher education sector here.  However, in advance of that review I have increased the number of funded student places at the local universities by 596 in academic year 2024/25.  This reflects the Executive’s commitment to support expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will change Civil Service subsistence rates to receipted actuals for officials travelling and working in Dublin as part of their outworking of strand 2 of the Good Friday Agreement, to ensure they are not at a financial loss given the high costs in Dublin.
answer_text: A review of Civil Service subsistence rates took place in 2023 and new rates came into effect on 1 February 2024. Civil Service subsistence allowances are paid on a receipted actuals basis up to a ceiling of the current rates in the HR Handbook while away from home or the workplace on official duty. There are currently no plans to change the Civil Service subsistence rates to receipted actuals for officials travelling and working in Dublin.  Subject to other priority work, a further review of Civil Service subsistence rates will take place during 2025/6.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will review existing day subsistence arrangements for Civil Service officials travelling and working in Dublin as part of their outworking of strand 2 of the Good Friday Agreement, to ensure they are not at a financial loss given the high costs in Dublin.
answer_text: A review of Civil Service day subsistence rates took place in 2023, and new rates came into effect on 1 February 2024. Subject to other priority work, a further review of Civil Service subsistence rates will take place during 2025/6.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action (i) his Department has taken to increase the number of places at each university; and (ii) he plans to take to support the development of universities.
answer_text: I have increased the number of student places at the local universities by 596 in academic year 2024/25. My Department maintains a dialogue with all of the higher education institutions on the full range of issues affecting their sustainability and growth and will shortly be beginning a review of higher education funding which will help support the future growth and sustainability of the higher education sector here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of school leavers (i) that attend a university or college in Britain; and (ii) that return from a university or college in Britain to take up employment in the north within 12 months of their graduation, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on school leavers. Data on school leavers is collected and published by the Department for Education and can be found at the following link: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/school-leavers However, my Department does publish data on enrolments in Britain higher education institutions.(i) The table below sets out the number of first year undergraduate students from here enrolled at HE institutions in Britain from 2012/13 to 2021/22.	12/13	13/14	14/15	15/16	16/17	17/18	18/19	19/20	20/21	21/22	4,345	4,600	4,630	5,115	5,120	5,045	4,805	4,280	4,335	4,715	Source: HESA Higher Education Enrolment data 			My Department also publishes data from HESA’s Graduate Outcome’s Survey. Graduate Outcomes is a survey which first commenced with the 2017/18 academic year's graduate population. Data is collected approximately 15 months after HE course completion and includes those leaving with undergraduate or postgraduate qualifications.  		(ii) The table below sets the percentage of  domiciled graduates in employment who returned here to work after graduating from a university in Britain from 2017/18 to 2021/22.		% returning from Britain to work here	Total responses	2017/18	42%	1,900	2018/19	46%	2,090	2019/20	49%	2,110	2020/21	46%	2,005	2021/22	47%	1,980	Source: Graduates Otutcomes survey (HESA).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current legal protections for someone who has been the victim of catfishing; and (ii) the legal repercussions for someone who has engaged in catfishing.
answer_text: The term catfishing refers to the creation of a fake identity and where a person communicates with another, typically online, often with the intention to deceive or mislead the individual into a romantic or sexual relationship; or to extort money or commit fraud.  I am conscious of the intrusive nature of catfishing which can have a devastating and far-reaching impact on its victims.  The creation of a fake identity in of itself is not a criminal offence.  However, where such an identity has been created with particular intentions, then the behaviour may be captured by existing offences and the relevant sanctions which apply.On the wider matter of malicious communications, telecommunications and internet legislation are reserved matters.The recently enacted Online Safety Act 2023 introduced new UK-wide laws that protects both children and adults from online harm. It created new communication offences including a false communications offence and a threatening communications offence.  I had sought to influence the content of the then Online Safety Bill during its passage, in particular urging more to be done to address the lack of accountability of anonymous account holders which I consider to be a key issue when seeking to prevent and detect online harms and offences.    Where the act of catfishing involves sexual motivation, there is already a comprehensive and robust framework of law on sexual offences in place in Northern Ireland which, for the main part, is included in the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008. The offences apply equally to both online as well as offline behaviours. I recently strengthened the offences of the 2008 Order via the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to provide a further range of protections from sexual harm.  Amongst others, this included four new offences of an adult pretending to be a child for the purposes of child grooming, which are unique offences to this jurisdiction.  Online safety is also a cross-cutting departmental issue which goes further than my Department and we must take a holistic approach to addressing the issue.  The cross Executive Online Safety Strategy has been put in place to assist children and young people to participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way.  This includes a particular focus on prevention and education to help tackle online abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in relation to addressing current planning backlogs; (ii) the extent to which the movement of planners from local councils to local authorities in the Republic of Ireland has contributed to the shortfall in staffing; and (iii) his assessment of the benefits of offering enhanced packages to incentivise planners to work in Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.
answer_text: My officials met with Newry Mourne and Down Council in November 2024 to discuss, understand and identify key challenges faced by them and to consider measures to improve planning performance. There are also plans to follow up this engagement to collectively identify some solutions.  As autonomous organisations, councils are responsible for human resource issues relating to their own staff and any information on the potential impact of council planning staff moving across jurisdictions is a matter for the council itself and I would refer the member to the council in that regard. My Department will however, through the next phase of the planning improvement programme, consider issues affecting the longer-term sustainability of the planning system including initiatives to attract and retain planning staff along with measures to build and enhance capacity and capability across the system.  I am committed to ensuring that the planning system operates in an efficient and effective way, and my officials will continue to work with Newry Mourne and Down District Council and other key stakeholders, to collectively improve the performance of the planning system in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how local residents can request and secure anti-littering and dog fouling signage in their local areas.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of local councils, under the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994, to keep land or any road it is responsible for, clear of litter and refuse, including dog fouling.  Residents should approach their respective council authority to secure anti-littering and dog fouling signage in their local areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the monetary value of proposed (i) investment; and (ii) development in Newry and Armagh that has been (a) delayed; and (b) withdrawn as a consequence of wastewater network constraints, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water, however, I have requested the required information and NI Water has advised that it does not hold details on the monetary value of investments or developments in the Newry and Armagh areas, which may be delayed or withdrawn as a consequence of wastewater network constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16553/22-27, to detail any other occasion whereby the Department has contacted Sinn Féin via email in relation to illegally-erected party political advertising billboards and posters.
answer_text: My Department does not hold correspondence records in such a way as to identify the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: As detailed in AQW 17982 22-27, the Department continues to work with our colleagues in the Department of Transport, supported by the European Investment Bank (EIB), to consider the recommendations in the Review and identify those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035.Translink also have a number of feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which are due to report by spring/summer this year. I will then need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available. The outcomes from the EIB work and the ongoing feasibility studies will help inform this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the resurfacing projects currently delayed due to a legal challenge, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Currently there are no resurfacing projects delayed due to legal challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the benefits of introducing an independent legal ombudsman office to respond to solicitor and barrister service complaints.
answer_text: The Legal Complaints and Regulation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides for the handling of complaints against both solicitors and barristers to move from professionally-led consideration to a system where functionally separate and lay-led complaints committees determine cases. Such committees, which I anticipate will be in place in the autumn, will have greater powers, including the power to award compensation. Central to this is the creation under the Act of a Legal Services Oversight Commissioner (“LSOC”), with appropriate powers relating to the complaints handling process and a consultative role in relation to other aspects of regulation. Those powers include the power to investigate how complaints are handled by the relevant professional body, the power to set targets in relation to complaints handling, the power to require a professional body to submit plans relating to complaints handling, and the power to penalise the professional body in certain circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures his Department will take in 2025 to help address congestion in Armagh city.
answer_text: My Department is aware that traffic can build up in Armagh City centre for short periods at peak times which is consistent with traffic patterns and conditions in many of towns and cities across the North.  My Department is currently developing a Transport Strategy and suite of Transport Plans, that will set priorities for the future development of the road and rail networks up to 2035.  The Strategy will also outline how the Department is working to address the challenges we face such as congestion, climate emergency and transport safety.Plans for the development of major road schemes including the Armagh East and Armagh North and West proposed road improvement projects will be considered as part of the Transport plan development. Any planned local road improvements can be found in the 2024/25 Annual Roads Report to Council at:  Annual Report to Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council 2024.  I am also keen to promote and encourage the use of sustainable transport modes such as walking, cycling and public transport as an alternative to the private car.  My Department’s Draft Active Travel Delivery Plan is currently at consultation stage ending on 28 February 2025 and can be found at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/consultations/active-travel-delivery-plan

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the complaints and compensation procedures that exist for damage caused to (a) property; and (b) lands through works carried out by utilities companies.
answer_text: Under the Street Works Order 1995 (“the 1995 Order”), utility companies are permitted to carry out street works on the adopted road network under their respective statutory powers and must comply with Article 31 of the 1995 Order in reinstating the street to the requirements in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee – Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition).  Utility companies are, however, liable for any issues or damage that may arise from their works. This includes damage caused to property or lands.If a member of the public is aware that a utility company has caused damage to property or lands, they should raise a complaint directly or claim directly from the company in question. If details of the utility are not known, the public can raise a complaint with my Department regarding works carried out by a utility company using the link below:-https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/report-issue-roadworks If details of the utility are not known and a member of the public wishes to claim for damage to property or lands, they can submit a claim with my Department.  Details of how to instigate the claim process with my Department can be found at:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/claim-compensation-due-road-or-street-problem

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when an inspection of Aughanduff Road, Mullaghbawn, was last carried out; (ii) the surface defects identified in that inspection; and (iii) when resurfacing works are scheduled to take place on Aughanduff Road.
answer_text: My Department conducts a programme of regular cyclic inspections across all roads in the North, including the Aughanduff Road in Mullaghbawn. The most recent inspection of Aughanduff Road was carried out on 29 July 2024, during which one actionable defect, in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy, was identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be a public information or engagement session in Newry as part of the public consultation into hospital network reconfiguration.
answer_text: In my response to your previous question (AQW 20009/22-27), I provided details regarding all engagement events being held in respect of the region-wide public consultation. The events are being held in a central location in each Trust area. The timing and locations have been selected to try and maximise public participation within the consultation timeframe. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust event will take place on 18 February 2025 at Armagh City Hotel, 2 Friary Road, Armagh, BT60 4FR. For those unable to travel to an in-person meeting, and to ensure equitable access and opportunity for all, we have organised an online consultation event. This online event is scheduled for Tuesday 11 February and due to popular demand a second online event is being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the potential benefits of a grant scheme to assist homeowners with the cost of installing heat pumps (i) for the first time; and (ii) in the case of existing units needing replaced.
answer_text: My department have recently consulted on future support of low carbon technologies in residential properties which closed 10th January 2025. The views provided will support my department in the development of policy in the area of heat decarbonisation to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, which will include heat pumps. My department recognises the need for solutions that address not only our environmental challenges and legislative commitments under the Climate Change Act, but that also foster economic growth. The early adoption and support of low carbon heating technologies will act as a catalyst for growth within our green economy and present huge economic opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of requests for (a) new grit boxes; (b) grit piles; and (c) grit box refills were received between 1 December 2024 and 14 January 2025; (ii) the number of those requests were fulfilled; and (iii) the average time taken to process each request.
answer_text: The number of requests for new grit boxes, grit piles, and grit box refills which were received during the requested period is provided in table below Enquiry TypeTotalEnquiries Responded to( a ) Request Salt Box995742( b ) Grit pile   613426( c ) Refill Salt Box13301037Grand Total29382205Of the 2,205 enquiries received during that period, it took an average of 2.25 days to respond to the request.  For many of the enquiries the 2.25 average response time may include an acknowledgement of the action being added to the works programme, rather than the time taken for the completion of the refilling of salt boxes and grit piles. Responses in relation to the provision of a salt box may have included the decline of a box, and does not provide a figure on the total number of salt boxes provided during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of unsuccessful applications for traffic calming measures in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Traffic calming schemes are funded through my Department’s Local Transport Safety Measures (LTSM) budget which covers a range of measures aimed at providing a safer road transport system.  Regrettably, the available investment has not been sufficient to deliver all schemes that have been assessed as worthwhile.My Department prepares priority lists of traffic calming measures and annual planned programmes of work are presented to respective Councils when budgets have been confirmed.Unfortunately my Department does not actively keep records of the number of unsuccessful applications for traffic calming measures. Any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of MOT tests undertaken at each test centre in each of the last five years; and (ii) the current waiting time for an MOT appointment at each test centre.
answer_text: Since September 2020, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) publish monthly provisional headline statistics in the publication ‘Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics’ which is available at Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. The number of vehicle tests conducted, broken down by test centre, are detailed in table 1.3 of Section 1 - Vehicle Testing. The following table shows the average MOT waiting times for private cars at each test centre from 1 to 19 January 2025 for all full tests booked during that period. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Killyruddan Road, Hamiltonsbawn, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. Whilst many roads including the Killyruddan Road would undoubtedly benefit from resurfacing investment, due to a limited budget, work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of interventionIn the meantime, I have asked my officials to continue to monitor Killruddan Road and carry out any repairs that meet my Department’s Limited Service maintenance policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will raise (i) the support for the facilitation of a Southern Relief Road lifting bridge; and (ii) the regeneration of Newry Canal at the next North South Ministerial Council Transport meeting.
answer_text: The provision of the Newry Southern Relief Road is a priority scheme for my Department and I will provide an update on the project at the next North South Ministerial Council Transport meeting. I would also intend to advise the North South Ministerial Council Transport meeting of my decision of April 2024 to move forward with a fixed bridge as this demonstrates better value for money after careful consideration of all relevant information.I am unable to raise the regeneration of the Newry Canal as the canal is in the ownership of Newry Mourne and Down District Council, and its regeneration is a matter for their consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legislation he intends to introduce during the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: I am considering introducing primary legislation in a number of areas. My department has identified three potential bills, which include measures for dealing with drainage and flooding, ports and harbours, rail safety and public transport. I will also be taking forward subordinate legislation in a number of areas, including reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the economic impact of restoring the Newry Canal as a functional waterway between Newry and Portadown.
answer_text: My Department’s remit on the Newry Canal, where it is designated between Scarva and WIN Industrial Estate in Newry, extends only to the drainage function of this waterway and therefore we have no responsibility for recreational or navigational purposes at this location. Consequently, my Department has not carried out any economic assessment of Newry Canal as a functional waterway between Newry and Portadown.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the progress that has been made on (i) proposal number 10; and (ii) proposal number 19 from the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations.
answer_text: Work on the Fundamental Review of Allocations is ongoing with 20 proposals grouped in four delivery stages. The first 2 stages (and implementation of 9 proposals) are complete. Work on progressing Proposal 19, as part of Stage 3, is now underway and my officials continue to work with the Housing Executive to implement this proposal. Planning is underway for Proposal 10 to be delivered in Stage 4.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) which proposals from the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations have been implemented thus far; (ii) which proposals have not yet been implemented; and (iii) the reason some proposals have not yet been implemented.
answer_text: I can confirm that:Proposals 1, 5, 15 and 16 were implemented in January 2023 (Stage 1). Proposals 6, 12, 14, 17 and 18 were implemented in September 2024 (Stage 2). Proposals 2, 3, 4, 7-11, 13, 19 and 20 are not yet implemented.The proposals are being planned and implemented in four delivery stages, of which two are complete. The reason for this approach is that implementation can require detailed research, modelling, policy development and system changes, which are facilitated by grouping proposals into stages.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) planned level; and (ii) format of public engagement his Department will be facilitating in relation to the Southern Relief Road project.
answer_text: The Newry Southern Relief Road (NSRR) webpage https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/road-improvements/newry-southern-relief-road. provides access to reports on consultations, including the Stage 1 Feasibility Report & Preliminary Options Report, Stage 2 Scheme Assessment Report & Community Consultation and Stage 3 Community Consultation.Officials have also included regular engagement with key stakeholders, businesses and affected landowners during scheme progression which will continue until scheme conclusion.  An Environmental Impact Assessment Report (EIAR) for the proposed scheme has been finalised with publication, alongside the associated draft Orders, planned for 21 January 2025 to coincide with a public consultation event (Orders Exhibition) in Newry Leisure Centre, from 10:00am to 8:00pm.The event marks the launch of the formal consultation period, lasting for 6 weeks, and closing on 4th March 2025 during which period all interested parties will have opportunity to express their views on the current proposals. This will include the availability of scheme information in various formats (including electronic, on the web and in hard copy) and at 6 different locations across the province.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the current support mechanisms that exist to empower local communities to lead clean-up and rejuvenation initiatives in their areas.
answer_text: My Department through the Neighbourhood Renewal Investment Fund provides Revenue funding to Community and Voluntary organisations which empowers local community groups to enhance the physical appearance of their neighbourhoods, fostering a sense of community pride and improving residents’ well-being by creating a more welcoming environment.  Neighbourhood Renewal Capital Funding (subject to budget availability) is available to groups to facilitate building repair and improvement works. Many local councils also provide assistance and support to local communities with such initiatives too.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any representations they have made to the UK Government following the High Court ruling in December 2024 on a public inquiry into the murder of Sean Brown in 1997.
answer_text: We have not made any Joint representations to the UK Government following this High Court ruling.We remain committed to ensuring that the needs of victims and survivors continue to be met. Our officials are developing an action plan for implementation of the new Victims and Survivors Strategy, which has been endorsed by the Executive. A key part of the Strategy is to provide support for victims and survivors, and their families, as they move forward from experiences of the past. The Strategy recognises the importance of truth and justice and the need to work towards greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past. We will continue to provide support for victims and survivors in dealing with the legacy of the past and will take a flexible approach to deal with changing needs and circumstances.This will include providing support through the Advocacy Support Network which is delivered by community organisations funded by Victims and Survivors Service, and which is expected to be part of the forthcoming PEACE PLUS programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with a statement of special educational need are in a school placement that has been deemed not suitable for their needs.
answer_text: For September 2025, 233 children who have a statement of special educational needs (SEN) and who are outside ‘transition years’ have been recommended for a change of school placement.  The Education Authority (EA) anticipates this number will change as Annual Reviews are completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with Translink in relation to ensuring that the times of the Newry Bus Shuttle 341 are linked with the arrival train times to Newry Train Station.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“There are timetabled connections for every Enterprise and the standard NIR service.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of (a) homicides; (b) attempted murders; and (c) aggravated assaults committed against women and girls in each of the last five years; and (ii) how this compares with other jurisdictions on these islands.
answer_text: The recording of information in relation to offences that have occurred is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of offenders of violent crime that have reoffended when on parole in each of the last five years; and (ii) any action her Department will take to reduce the risk of reoffending by prisoners on parole.
answer_text: Information on who is under supervision, as directed by the Parole Commissioners, is not readily available for reoffending analysis.  The response therefore sets out reoffending rates for individuals released from custody on licence, following a principal violence against the person offence.   The Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 makes provision for the courts to impose sentences which include a statutory period on licence.  The numbers of individuals that reoffended are outlined below.	One-year proven reoffending rate for Violence against the Person offenders released from custody on license – 2017/18 to 2021/22YearTotal number in cohortTotal number reoffendedOne-year proven reoffending rate (%)2017/181152521.7%2018/191223629.5%2019/201012221.8%2020/211233226.0%2021/22992121.2%It is the role of the Probation Board for Northern Ireland to supervise people who have been released from prison on licence.  People may be released subject to licence conditions, to help protect the public, keep communities safe, prevent reoffending and support rehabilitation.  Probation’s role is to ensure that the offender is complying with those conditions, assess and manage risks posed on an ongoing basis, and provide interventions appropriate to the risk profile.If an offenders’ level of risk is assessed as being no longer manageable in the community, e.g. they reoffend, or breach licence conditions, they may be recalled to prison custody.  In the case of life licensees, a recommendation from the Parole Commissioners for Northern Ireland is sought prior to the department considering whether an individual should be recalled.  There is also provision for recall without a Parole Commissioners recommendation in time critical cases.Once recalled, an offender can only be released following a decision by the Parole Commissioners’ for Northern Ireland, or at the expiry of their sentence.Notes to the table:1. The cohort includes all individuals released from custody on license (i.e. under PBNI supervision) within the given financial year.  License conditions are imposed for the following disposals – Life License, Indeterminate Custodial Sentence, Extended Custodial Sentence, Determinate Custodial Sentence, Custody Probation Order and Juvenile Justice Centre Order.2. The cohort includes all individuals whose principal offence at disposal was categorised as Violence against the Person; reoffences may fall across any offence categories.						3. An offence is counted as a reoffence if it:(i) occurs within one year of custodial release or non-custodial disposal;(ii) has been committed in Northern Ireland;(iii) has been prosecuted by PSNI and not a third party; (iv) is not a breach offence; and (v) has been 'proven' in court.				4. Given the nature of reoffending, there is an 18-month time lag in the publication of statistics to allow for the one-year reoffending interval and an additional six-months for the offence(s) to be proven in court

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of (a) the reasons that a disproportionate number of women are accessing community advice services; and (b) whether current funding for community advice is adequate to meet demand on services; and (ii) to detail the outreach his Department has undertaken in each of the last three years to encourage access to community advice services.
answer_text: Of the clients who accessed community advice services in NI during 2023-24, 58% were female.  This figure is comparable with the gender profile of those accessing advice across the United Kingdom.  Universal Credit, Pension Credit, Carer’s Allowance and Personal Independence Payment have a higher proportion of female than male claimants.  As 79% of community advice casework relates to benefits, this may also have an impact on the percentage of females accessing advice services.My Department continues to provide £6.6m of funding annually for independent advice services.  In addition, I recently launched the £1.3m Integrated Advice Partnership Fund, aimed at improving the uptake of free, independent regulated debt advice through a collaborative partnership approach with councils. My Department has established referral pathways between its customer-facing teams and the local advice sector to ensure customers’ advice needs are met.  Those claiming Universal Credit as part of the ‘Move to Universal Credit’ process receive a migration notice which includes signposting to available help, support and independent advice.  Each of our 35 Jobs & Benefits Offices actively engages with advice organisations, allowing them to provide tailored support and signposting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of cold weather payments that have been triggered since 1 November 2024, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Cold Weather Payments are triggered when the average temperature is, or is forecast to be, zero degrees centigrade or below for seven consecutive days, from 1 November to 31 March each financial year. The Cold Weather Payments, £25 for each seven day period triggered, are designed to support those on the lowest incomes during extreme weather conditions.Information for triggering payments is supplied to the Department for Communities by the Met Office, based on daily forecast and actual temperatures at seven separate NI weather stations located at Aldergrove, Castlederg, Glennane, Katesbridge, Magilligan, Stormont Castle and Thomastown. Each weather station covers a designated range of postcodes.Since 1 November 2024, four Cold Weather Payment triggers have been reached at Katesbridge, Castlederg, Glenanne and Thomastown weather stations. These were based on the forecast and recorded temperatures for the seven-day periods 3 January to 9 January and 4 January to 10 January 2025. The postcodes covered by Katesbridge weather station are BT24 – BT26 and BT30 – BT34. The postcodes covered by the Castlederg weather station are BT78 – BT79 and BT81 – BT82. The postcodes covered by the Glenanne weather station are BT35 and BT60 – BT71 and the postcodes covered by the Thomastown weather station are BT74 – BT77 and BT92 -BT94.In total for the four triggers, approximately 66,405 Cold Weather Payments totalling £1,660,125 will be paid to eligible customers. The Department is not able to break this down by constituency.Further information on Cold Weather Payments, including details of any triggers reached during the 2024/25 Cold Weather Payment season, can be found on NI Direct at – https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cold-weather-payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to the negative impact on Universal Credit entitlement for student nurses in receipt of bursaries.
answer_text: My officials are currently exploring possible future changes to the financial support provided to healthcare students on programmes for which my Department is responsible. I anticipate that these considerations will include the potential impact on Universal Credit entitlement of the bursary and other financial support provided by my Department to student nurses. To date, I have not had any engagement with the Minister for Communities on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19670/22-27, to detail the estimated future cost saving for his Department as a result of the roll-out of the encompass system.
answer_text: The estimated future cost savings arising from the implementation of the encompass programme are – cash releasing benefits of £29.6million over 14 years, and non-cash releasing benefits value £514 million.The non-cash benefits include,increased theatre utilisation, reduction in rejected lab samples, reduced error in charts and medication administration, reduced delayed doses of medication, reduction in outpatient Did Not Attend (DNA) rates, reduction in venous thromboembolism (VTE or blood clots) episodes and pressure ulcers, improved outpatient utilisation, andimproved patient experience. There is also potential for income generation through increases in academic grants for research and benefits from health sector development.The programme also commissioned a report by Deloitte in February 2023 that highlighted that further benefits might be accrued.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18150/22-27, to detail any work his Department has undertaken to gather more recent statistics on fuel poverty with a view to shaping the Fuel Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: Fuel Poverty Statistics are obtained from the Northern Ireland House Condition Survey. As such, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the NIHE, as they carry out the data collection for the Northern Ireland House Condition Survey. She has advised me as follows: Fieldwork for the latest House Condition Survey was completed over 2023 and it is anticipated that the results from the 2023 survey will be available in 2026. To fill the data gap since the last Northern Ireland House Condition Survey in 2016, modelled estimates of fuel poverty have been produced using the 2016 Survey as a base and adjusting by the three main components of fuel poverty (fuel prices, household incomes and energy efficiency). This data is available at Northern Ireland level and in 2021, modelled estimates of Fuel Poverty indicate that 24% of households here are in fuel poverty.  The draft Fuel Poverty Strategy proposes the introduction of a basket of indicators to improve the understanding of fuel poverty, help us measure progress towards our outcomes and provide more frequent data on fuel poverty.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 15121/22-27, to detail any measures being taken within the scope of the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme to raise awareness of signs of coercive control or intimidation by paramilitary organisations, particularly against women.
answer_text: Understanding and addressing the coercive control of communities by paramilitaries is a priority for the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC). The Programme combines robust, specialist law enforcement with projects and approaches that build the resilience and capacity of individuals and communities to recognise and resist the harmful influence of paramilitaries. The Programme is active across Northern Ireland to support people and communities vulnerable to paramilitary influence and harm, with commitments being delivered collaboratively by Government Departments, statutory agencies and partners in the voluntary and community sector. 	I am, of course, only too aware of the particular impact of the abuse, coercion and exploitation of women by paramilitaries. Initiatives delivered by EPPOC include the Department for Communities led Developing Women Project, which aims to strengthen the capacity and confidence of women in those areas most impacted by ongoing paramilitary activity.  The project provides women with the skills, knowledge and support needed to help them become more resilient to paramilitary exploitation. The long-term objective is an increase in wider community resilience and cohesion, so that people feel safe and confident to contribute fully to their communities free from paramilitary interference and intimidation. EPPOC recognises the importance of raising awareness around the signs of paramilitary coercion; enhancing public awareness about these issues has a real-life impact by encouraging societal debate around the hidden harms associated with paramilitary violence and control. You may have seen the hard-hitting posters and videos that form part of the Programme’s ‘Ending the Harm’ campaign. These highlight paramilitaries for what they are - manipulators of people and communities, and abusers and exploiters of women, children and young people. The most recent phase of the campaign (initiated in September 2024) calls out the breadth of ways in which paramilitary gangs harm communities. The campaign challenges the coercive control of paramilitaries and builds on the wider public conversation on the issue. This latest phase highlights a number of themes, including violence against women and girls; child criminal exploitation; drugs; and the intimidation and extortion of the business community.EPPOC also commissions and collates research to enable a better understanding of the impact of paramilitary harm, including that perpetrated against women and girls. You can find a range of relevant research on the Programme’s ‘Ending the Harm’ website: www.endingtheharm.com. I would encourage anyone interested in this issue to take time to read it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19416/22-27, to provide the scoping review used for the development of the anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: This Scoping Review can be found online at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/scoping-review-literature-poverty-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of appointments not attended in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the total cost of these missed appointments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19416/22-27, for his assessment of the extent to which poor nutrition contributes to poor educational attainment.
answer_text: My Department’s scoping review of the literature on poverty identified a number of  factors that increase the risk of falling into poverty, however it did not look closely at the relationship between nutrition and educational attainment. My Department is currently engaging with officials from all Departments including the Department of Education to discuss the development of the Strategy and the delivery of a range of keystone actions which will help mitigate impacts of poverty including those affecting educational attainment. While issues around educational attainment would be primarily addressed to the Minister for Education, my officials  will ensure that this issue is raised and that the work of my Department on developing the broader Anti-Poverty Strategy aligns with and supports the Department of Education’s work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19934/22-27, (i) to detail the number of schools that provide breakfast clubs, broken down by (a) primary schools; and (b) post-primary schools; and (ii) for his assessment of the social value of free breakfast clubs.
answer_text: My department funds the Extended Schools (ES) programme which may be used for a wide range of services, including breakfast clubs, in the most disadvantaged areas. Some schools in the ES programme may implement a charge for breakfast clubs to ensure sustainability of the provision but are encouraged to do so at a level which ensures that those most in need are able to access this provision Funding used for breakfast clubs, free or otherwise, cannot be disaggregated from wider funding allocations made under the ES programme. Funding allocated to schools under the ES programme is not available broken down by constituency. ES funding provided by my department over the last six years is as follows:£9.153m in 2019/20£9.153m in 2020/21£9.153m in 2021/22£9,153m in 2022/23£8.1m in 2023/24£8.1m in 2024/25In 2023/24, 142 primary schools and 18 post-primary schools participating in the ES programme provided breakfast clubs for pupils. As stated in my answer to AQW 19934/22-27, schools outside of the ES programme may also offer breakfast club provision. The provision of breakfast is an important issue and is part of the Extended Schools programme which aims to raise standards through the provision of targeted support services which have a focus on learning, development and progress for those children and young people most in need or who are risk of low educational attainment. Breakfast clubs ensure that children have access to healthy eating, establish regular eating patterns each day and are ready to learn and excel in school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19934/22-27, to detail the funding allocated to free breakfast clubs in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My department funds the Extended Schools (ES) programme which may be used for a wide range of services, including breakfast clubs, in the most disadvantaged areas. Some schools in the ES programme may implement a charge for breakfast clubs to ensure sustainability of the provision but are encouraged to do so at a level which ensures that those most in need are able to access this provision Funding used for breakfast clubs, free or otherwise, cannot be disaggregated from wider funding allocations made under the ES programme. Funding allocated to schools under the ES programme is not available broken down by constituency. ES funding provided by my department over the last six years is as follows:£9.153m in 2019/20£9.153m in 2020/21£9.153m in 2021/22£9,153m in 2022/23£8.1m in 2023/24£8.1m in 2024/25In 2023/24, 142 primary schools and 18 post-primary schools participating in the ES programme provided breakfast clubs for pupils. As stated in my answer to AQW 19934/22-27, schools outside of the ES programme may also offer breakfast club provision.
