question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many animals have been disposed of due to bovine TB during this financial year.
answer_text: 17,447 cattle have been slaughtered for the control of bovine Tuberculosis since April 2024. This figure is much too high and places an awful burden on hard-working farm businesses.  The compensation bill associated with this slaughter also remains a significant ongoing pressure on my departmental budget.  As members will be aware, at my request, the Chief Veterinary Officer has undertaken a comprehensive review of bTB in Northern Ireland which considers fully all the factors which are contributing to our current high rate of disease. It consists of almost 40 proposals across three key pillars – the importance of ensuring that we all – government and industry - work in partnership to reduce disease levels; proposals to limit and prevent the spread of TB from cattle to cattle; and proposals to address the transmission of bTB from wildlife to cattle.I have already accepted his recommendation and confirmed that a new TB Partnership Steering Group will be established to consider carefully the Chief Veterinary Officer’s (CVO)’s proposals and contribute to the development of a delivery plan by March 2025. This delivery plan will clearly set out our approach and the actions to be taken to tackle bTB in the time ahead. Please be assured that addressing bTB is one of my key priorities and, through hard work by both government and industry, I believe we can put Northern Ireland on a path where the impact of this disease on family farms becomes less severe.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of why the north has one of the highest rates of femicide in Europe.
answer_text: The question does not make clear what information has been drawn on to support the claim that Northern Ireland has one of the highest rates of femicide in Europe and so I am unable to verify the accuracy of this claim.Nonetheless the recent increase in femicide and in violence against women in Northern Ireland is deeply concerning.  My thoughts are with the families and communities who have lost loved ones to such devastating violence. Each life lost to femicide is a tragedy that leaves a lasting impact on those left behind.Femicide is an urgent societal problem that reflects broader systemic issues, such as gender inequality, domestic violence, and a lack of sufficient support for those at risk or, at times, for those who pose risk. Ending all forms of violence against women and tackling femicide is a priority for the whole of the Executive, not just for my Department, and all of us across our community, institutions and Executive Departments must work collaboratively to end it. With respect to our local circumstances, there is a growing body of internationally based studies which evidence that violence against women and girls tends to be more prevalent in post-conflict societies: further, recent research conducted locally, has also highlighted the linkages between paramilitary and terrorist groups and domestic abuse, intimate partner violence and coercive control.We must continue to remember those who have lost their life by committing to making our society safer for all, ensuring that their voices are heard and that meaningful action is taken to prevent such tragedies in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of homicides in each of the last five years, broken down by cause; and (ii) how this compares with other jurisdictions on these islands.
answer_text: The recording of information in relation to homicides that have occurred is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI, and in the interim, their published statistics are available here: https://www.psni.police.uk/about-us/our-publications-and-reports/official-statistics/police-recorded-crime-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 15935/22-27, (i) to detail the measures she has since taken to address the backlog; and (ii) to detail the number of Rate Rebate applications that have been processed in comparison to her departmental targets.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 15935/22-27, my Department continues to take measures to meet increasing volumes of applications for Rate Rebate linked to the ongoing move to Universal Credit.To meet increased application volumes, since my previous letter to you on 8th October 2024, Land & Property Services (LPS) recruited a further six staff to work in this area in November 2024. The provision of Rate Rebate is demand led and is determined by individual claimants’ circumstances. It is anticipated that as Universal Credit volumes increase, the number of applications for Rate Rebate will also increase. LPS closely monitor application volumes and align resource requirements accordingly.From 1st April 2024 to 31st December 2024, 21,754 Rate Rebate applications have been processed. At 6th January, 10,545 Rate Rebate applications were in a pending assessment phase. Of these, 2,662 were received in the previous 30 days, 2,471 are pending landlord verification, 3,606 are pending UC information needed to assess rate rebate entitlement. The remaining 1,806 claims are subject to ongoing assessment.	Should you require further information on the Rate Rebate Scheme, LPS officials are willing to meet with you. If this is of interest to you, please contact Eamon Clarke (Eamon.Clarke@finance-ni.gov.uk) and arrangements can be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of in-person public engagement his Department will facilitate in relation to the proposed reconfiguration of the hospital network.
answer_text: As part of the consultation process the Department has organised a series of events across Northern Ireland, both in-person and online.  The in-person events have been arranged in collaboration with each of the five Trusts.  The full schedule is set out below: EventLocationDateSouth Eastern Health & Social TrustArds Community HospitalTuesday 17 December 2024, 7:00pm-9.00pmWestern Health & Social Care TrustSilver Birch Hotel, Omagh Wednesday 15 January 2025, 7:00pm-9.00pmBelfast Health & Social Care TrustRamada by Wyndham Hotel, Talbot Street, BelfastTuesday 21 January 2025, 7:00pm-9:00pmNorthern Health & Social Care TrustTullyglass Hotel, Ballymena Wednesday 5 February 2025, 7:00pm-9:00pmOpen to AllOnlineTuesday 11 February 2025, 7:00pm-9:00pm Southern Health & Social Care TrustArmagh City Hotel, Armagh Tuesday 18 February 2025, 7:00pm-9:00pmTo provide sufficient time for conducting these consultation events, the consultation period has been extended until 28 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently on orthopaedic surgery waiting lists, including the median wait time, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following table details the total number of patients and the median wait times for orthopaedic surgery waiting lists at 30 September 2024. This is the most recent date for which validated data is available.HSC Trust SpecialtyNumber of patients Median wait time (weeks)SouthernT & O Surgery4,195100.4WesternT & O Surgery6,39668.0BelfastAll Orthopaedics13,47681.0Source: Department of Health Hospital Waits Information Branch, Inpatient Waiting Times Dataset.Notes- Patients can be waiting for more than one inpatient or day case admission. The encompass specialty classification differs from, and is not directly comparable to, the legacy (pre-encompass) data sources currently used by other HSC Trusts.The current waiting times for primary joint replacement are unacceptable. In 2024 a bid was made by my Department for £135m to help reduce elective waiting lists and more recently a further £10m bid was submitted as part of October Monitoring.  Unfortunately, these bids were not supported, which was a missed opportunity to improve our waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to list the schools that provide a free breakfast club across the north.
answer_text: Information on breakfast clubs is not routinely collected from schools. The Department’s Extended Schools (ES) programme provides additional funding for those schools serving the most disadvantaged areas to provide for a wide range of services and activities, which can include breakfast clubs. A total of 168 schools and 1 Extended Schools Cluster provided a breakfast club as part of their Extended Schools funding in 2023-24. Those schools outside the ES programme can also offer Breakfast club provision. The Education Authority Catering Service has reported that it provides support to 171 schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues in relation to establishing a ring-fenced agriculture budget.
answer_text: In light of the change in treatment as a result of the Chancellor’s Budget on 30 October 2024 I formally asked the Minister of Finance that the £332.5 million Resource DEL that has been baselined in the block grant is treated as ‘Executive Earmarked’ funding for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development purposes.On 19 December 2024 the Executive agreed to my request to earmark this funding to my Department as part of the wider agreement on the Draft Budget 2025-26.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with his Republic of Ireland counterpart with a view to establishing an all-island agricultural body.
answer_text: I have regular contact with my Republic of Ireland counterpart and also engage through the North South Ministerial Council (NSMC). My officials are in frequent dialogue and meet regularly with Department of Agriculture Food and the Marine officials to discuss agricultural policy and share best practice. To date the establishment of an all-island agricultural body has not been discussed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether urgent transfer times from Daisy Hill Hospital to Craigavon Area Hospital for cardiac patients adhere to the relevant clinical service standards.
answer_text: Urgent transfers of cardiac patients from Daisy Hill to Craigavon Area hospitals are carried out by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) according to the national interfacility transfer criteria. It is challenging for NIAS to meet the expected response times as the calls from hospitals requesting these transfers are managed by the same cohort of emergency ambulances and NIAS staff as all other 999 calls from the public; and, as a result, the expected response times are not always met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to address cash flow issues for farmers whose herds are affected by bovine TB.
answer_text: I have heard directly from industry the financial impact a bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) breakdown can have on a farm – from lower milk cheques, carrying additional livestock or issues around loss of genetics. To help mitigate against these impacts during a breakdown, my Department aims to value reactor cattle in a timely manner following their disclosure at a bTB test.  The latest available figures produced in November 2024 show that we are currently meeting our target to ensure that 90% of reactor cattle are valued within 15 working days of disclosure.  Once the animal is slaughtered, and provided all required documentation is received, we then aim to process bTB compensation payments within 10 working days.  In the last financial year, the department paid out £36.5m in compensation for cattle removed for the control of this disease.My Department is not in a position to offer direct financial support in terms of consequential losses to farmers, however, I am committed to working with my officials, and industry, in tackling the high rates of incidence to ensure farms are less at risk of facing the devastating financial impact of a TB breakdown. My Chief Veterinary Officer has recently completed a review of issues related to bTB programme and policy, including the actions contained in the bTB Eradication Strategy. Subject to further policy work and prioritisation, including engagement with a new stakeholder partnership to be established in the New Year, I have asked that a new delivery plan, which will seek to address the current high levels of bovine TB incidence in Northern Ireland, the high cost to government and industry, will be published by March 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department has taken to recognise (i) domestic violence; and (ii) intimate partner abuse as a factor for consideration in the award of housing points.
answer_text: My officials are currently working alongside the Housing Executive on the Fundamental Review of Allocations. Currently, someone who leaves their home as a result of domestic abuse can be allocated points for homelessness and primary social needs points for violence or the threat of violence. I will further consider the awards of points in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18149/22-27, to detail the number of children living in poverty, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to provide an answer to your question, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a detailed breakdown of any funding that has been allocated to organisations with links to proscribed groups in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has not allocated any funding to organisations with links to proscribed groups in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any representations she has made to the Minister for Communities in relation to recognising (i) domestic violence; and (ii) intimate partner abuse as a factor for consideration in the award of housing points.
answer_text: My officials are in regular communications with Department for Communities (DfC) officials regarding the implementation of the Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategy.  They are also represented on the DSA Strategy Oversight Board.  Discussions have taken place on housing points, and I understand that DfC officials are currently collaborating with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) to conduct a comprehensive review of the points allocation system.  At present, individuals who leave their home as a result of domestic abuse can be allocated points for homelessness and additional points for social needs related to violence or the threat of violence. I understand the DfC Minister will further consider the allocation of these points in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of individuals under 18 years old that have been (i) arrested; and (ii) imprisoned for paramilitary-related activity in each of the last five years, broken down by age.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of persons arrested by police.  The recording of information in relation to persons arrested by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issue raised in part (i) of the question to the PSNI.In relation to part (ii) of the question, it is not possible, from information held by the Department, to provide the information you requested.  Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not hold information on whether an offence for which a conviction was handed down was paramilitary-related. To identify these cases would require a manual trawl of court files.  We estimate that to locate, retrieve and extract any information that might be relevant to your request would involve work of more than the appropriate limit.  However, with the above in mind, in the period 2019 - 2023, the most recent five years for which information is available, there were no convictions at courts for persons aged under 18 for offences under terrorism legislation, not including appeals. These figures relate to offences under the Terrorism Prevention and Investigation Measures Act 2011, Terrorism Act 2006, Terrorism Act 2002, Terrorism Act 2000, Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005, Prevention of Terrorism (Temporary Provisions) Act 1989, Prevention of Terrorism (Temporary Provisions) Act 1984, Counter-Terrorism Act 2008,  Anti-Terrorism and Security Act 2001.  It should be noted that these figures relate only to cases where there was a prosecution under the legislation specified.  It may be that persons were convicted for other paramilitary-related offences which did not fall under these pieces of legislation, during the period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18149/22-27, for his assessment of the reasons why Newry and Armagh has the highest level of children living in poverty.
answer_text: The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) annually release official statistics for “Children in low income families: local area statistics”. The proportion of children in each parliamentary constituency in NI estimated to be living in poverty are presented in the publication.In 2022/23, 23.0% of children in Newry and Armagh were in relative poverty before housing costs, and 18.7% were in absolute poverty before housing costs. This was the 3rd highest level among parliamentary constituencies in Northern Ireland using both measures.Further information regarding the Children in Low Income Families (CILIF) statistics can be found at the link below.www.gov.uk/government/collections/children-in-low-income-families-local-area-statisticsTo support the development of the Anti-Poverty strategy DfC has conducted a scoping review of the literature on poverty in NI, ROI and the UK. The scoping review identified many factors that increase the risk of falling into poverty.  Many of these risk factors have been found to stem from early years, with many childhood and other factors, such as poor educational attainment, family breakdown, disability and low-paid work, heavily affecting the risk of poverty in the future.Such factors will increase the risk of falling into poverty, however from the research it isn’t possible to say which reasons are the most likely to influence poverty in Newry and Armagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the findings of the December 2024 Southern Trust Cervical Screening Review; (ii) to detail the steps that will be taken to rebuild women’s trust in the cervical screening service; and (iii) to detail whether avenues of recourse will be available to women affected by misread cytology slides.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the of the Cervical Cytology Review Outcome Report and note that a detailed analysis of the findings is being commissioned by the PHA. It is expected that this analysis, supported by appropriate independent experts, will be completed in January 2025.  These reports will help inform my next steps.  Primary HPV testing, with cervical cytology as a second line test for those women who test positive for HPV, was fully implemented into the NI Cervical Screening programme from 11 December 2023. Primary HPV testing is an automated test which identifies the presence of HPV and was recommended by the UK National Screening Committee because it is a more accurate test, and will save more lives, than cervical cytology alone.  The Public Health Agency (PHA) is monitoring awareness, confidence and barriers to participation in cervical screening and will continue to use this information to inform future promotional activities.  In relation to avenues of recourse, any decision regarding compensation would be a matter between the individual involved and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 18150/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 15788 22-27 was answered on 12 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16553/22-27, to detail the structural integrity tests that have been conducted on the illegally erected Sinn Féin billboard at the Egyptian Arch roundabout, Newry.
answer_text: When considering the risk posed by billboards, my Department considers a range of factors related to road safety risk including, impediment to vehicles and/or pedestrians travelling along a road, obstruction of sightlines, and the likelihood of an errant vehicle striking the billboard. Where unauthorised signage poses a clear safety risk, my Department will prioritise its removal, however, where there is no such danger, officials will liaise closely with other key stakeholders to provide a solution. Hoardings are normally the subject of a planning application, and any infringements of planning laws would normally be pursued under planning legislation which in most cases would fall to the relevant District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the merits of establishing a pedestrian crossing on the Forkhill Road, Newry, a safe distance from the junction with Carn Road, to facilitate the safe crossing of shoppers, members of Killeavy St Moninna Gaelic Athletic Club and school children.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.As part of a recent planning application for the adjacent Mulkerns Eurospar on the Forkhill Road (LA07/2020/0426/F), the developer has agreed to undertake road improvement works across the frontage of the site, including the provision of right turn access facilities, and an uncontrolled crossing point which will aid pedestrian movements in the locality.Officials will also assess Forkhill Road, between Carn Road and Newtown Road, to determine if it meets the criteria for consideration of an additional crossing point in line with current policy.  If the location meets the necessary threshold, it may be considered for inclusion in a future works programme, subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to protect GP practices from the increase in National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: I recognise that the changes arising from the Autumn Budget will have a significant impact on future budget and costs.  It is not possible to provide a definitive assessment on our future funding position until a budget settlement for future years has been reached.  We will likely be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.I acknowledge that providers within Family Health Services and the Independent Sector will have increased costs.  I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these costs and therefore they will add to the many demands I will need to consider and prioritise once a budget settlement is received.Recognising this and the valuable role played by those providers, these funding pressures will nevertheless need to be considered and prioritised alongside other priorities before I am in a position to make any commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the economic impact of Belfast’s current traffic congestion; and (ii) to detail any engagement he has had with the Department for Infrastructure to address these issues.
answer_text: I recognise the impact congestion can have on the local economy. I have had informal discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure and continue to engage with him on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail number of (i) political poster points; and (ii) advertising billboards illegally erected on public land that have been identified by his Department in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Whilst members of the public can report fly posters and unlawful advertising on the online reporting system, it does not automatically record further detail on the nature of the sign, therefore the department cannot determine accurately whether the sign is political or not.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 17810/22-27, whether gambling harm is addressed through teaching on gambling, gaming and financial capability across several areas of the curriculum in (i) all primary schools; and (ii) all post-primary schools.
answer_text: In the Northern Ireland curriculum, the topic of gambling harm is explored through the wider themes of financial capability, risk management and making responsible choices.  While the term "gambling harm" is not always explicitly referenced, the curriculum includes aspects that enable students to comprehend the dangers and outcomes linked to gambling and related behaviours. Financial capability, encompassing awareness of gambling and gaming, is already established as a statutory and essential component of the curriculum.  Opportunities for the teaching and learning of financial capability occur across the curriculum and through young people’s everyday experiences inside and outside school.  At primary level, financial capability is most closely associated with Mathematics and Numeracy, however, it features across all the areas of learning.  For example, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding, using the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s (CCEA) Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils understand what gaming is, the types of games available and the associated risks.  This includes the amount of time spent on gaming, in-game transactions, and the difference between ‘selected’ and ‘random’ (gambling) purchases.At post-primary level, financial capability is further embedded across the curriculum and teachers should provide pupils with opportunities to enhance their knowledge and understanding of a range of finance issues, to develop skills that will enable them to make competent and responsible financial decisions throughout their lives.  Under the Learning for Life and Work (LLW) area of learning, using CCEA’s Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils recognise that gambling and gaming products are designed to make money, and that advertising actively promotes them.  Furthermore, LLW also provides young people with opportunities to explore issues around addiction and its implications for financial wellbeing.  Educating students about the risks associated with gambling is consistent with the objectives of the curriculum.  These aims include promoting critical thinking and informed decision-making, providing students with the necessary skills to navigate risks in their personal and financial affairs, and tackling contemporary societal issues, such as the increasing acceptance of gambling via digital platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the emergency protocols that were implemented during an emergency situation on the 07:00 Enterprise service from Belfast to Dublin on Thursday 28 November 2024.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that on the 28 November 2024 the 07:00 Enterprise service from Belfast Grand Central to Dublin Connolly stopped between Newry and Dundalk due to a mechanical fault. The train driver responded to an audible alarm in the cab and stopped the train in accordance with the procedures. The fault was due to faulty distributor that led to some smoke entering the air conditioning units of the train.   The train Manager on board assisted the Driver in carrying out internal and external checks of the train and mechanical engineers were tasked to assist. During this time an emergency egress button was pushed by a passenger onboard the train. After the fault was rectified, the train was able to continue to Dundalk at a reduced speed and passengers were transferred to another train for onward travel so that further mechanical work could be carried out on the train.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a list of dental practices in the Newry and Armagh constituency currently accepting new patient registrations.
answer_text: Access to Health Service dentistry is currently very difficult, especially for unregistered patients, and this is likely to be the case for some time. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and, unlike General Medical Practitioners, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements. They may also de-register their existing Health Service patients with three months’ notice. Those who wish to register with a dental practice as a Health Service patient are advised to contact practices in their area directly. They may have to check with many dental practices and / or be prepared to travel outside of their local area, in order to find a practice that is currently registering Health Service patients. Further advice, including the HSC Service Finder, can be found at: Registering as a Health Service dental patient - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)As part of a £9.2m financial investment for 2024/2025, a Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) began on 1 August 2024. The DAS  aims to improve access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing dental condition.  There are 30 practices participating in the scheme, seven of which are located in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area. Currently, the DAS is providing access to dental care for around 450 patients each week.Patients can access the scheme by contacting participating practices directly. Patients will be required to outline the dental problem they are experiencing and ask if the practice has any appointments available under the scheme. Contact details of participating practices are available on the SPPG website at: Dental Access Scheme - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)In addition to the DAS, undergraduate dental students in Belfast Dental Hospital provide emergency dental appointments for unregistered patients under the supervision of dental teaching staff. Patients can self-refer to this service but it is only available during university term time. Information on how to access this service can be found at:  Dental services | Belfast Health & Social Care Trust website (hscni.net)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16937/22-27, to detail the timeline for the next steps in the development of the primary care centre in Newry.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust submitted to the Department of Health on the 6th February 2025 the revised business case for Newry Community Treatment and Care Centre with updated costs, proposed programme of implementation and a letter of Commissioner Support.  The Trust awaits feedback on this submission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the median individual income; and (ii) the median household income, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The latest data on median individual income at constituency level is contained within the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings, which can be accessed at the following link: NISRA - ASHE - NI Geographies The latest data on average gross disposable household income is available from the Office for National Statistics at Council level and can be accessed at: ONS - GDHI in your local area

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the powers that the PSNI has to investigate a traffic offence committed in the north by a vehicle registered in the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) the advice her Department can provide to a person wishing to pursue a complaint against a vehicle registered in the Republic of Ireland for an offence in the north.
answer_text: This matter is not within my remit as Justice Minister as it relates to PSNI operations for which the Chief Constable is responsible and the Policing Board accountable.Therefore, as I am committed to respecting the operational independence of both organisations, I would recommend that you direct your queries to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any rehabilitation opportunities that exist for patients living with heart failure.
answer_text: Cardiac rehabilitation services offer programmes tailored to the clinical needs of patients with symptoms connected to a cardiac event.  This includes addressing cardiac risk factors, a review of medication, psychological support, and physical activity in groups or at home. Patients with ischaemic heart failure from the five Health and Social Care Trusts are offered the opportunity to avail of these services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure full access to heart failure services across all areas.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of Department’s Cardiac Network and therefore it established a Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. The Group has reviewed the current heart failure service provision across five Health and Social Care Trusts and devised a proposed heart failure model to allow for a regionally standardised approach and timely access to heart failure services. The proposed model reflects the need for multidisciplinary team input into heart failure services. To date, variance in the service delivery and workforce levels across the region has been identified. Work is in progress in relation to a more detailed analysis in order to inform commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to establish appropriately commissioned and staffed heart failure services to ensure speed of diagnosis and access to specialist input.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of Department’s Cardiac Network and therefore it established a Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. The Group has reviewed the current heart failure service provision across five Health and Social Care Trusts and devised a proposed heart failure model to allow for a regionally standardised approach and timely access to heart failure services. The proposed model reflects the need for multidisciplinary team input into heart failure services. To date, variance in the service delivery and workforce levels across the region has been identified. Work is in progress in relation to a more detailed analysis in order to inform commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the accessibility of multidisciplinary team input for people with heart failure.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of Department’s Cardiac Network and therefore it established a Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. The Group has reviewed the current heart failure service provision across five Health and Social Care Trusts and devised a proposed heart failure model to allow for a regionally standardised approach and timely access to heart failure services.
