Work is in progress in relation to a more detailed analysis in order to inform commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of an all-island model to allow for quick transport to the advanced heart failure centre in Dublin, as opposed to having to travel to England.
answer_text: The majority of Northern Ireland patients with heart failure can receive advanced treatments and therapy in Northern Ireland. A small number of patients, who require highly specialised treatment, are transferred to England to receive it, where this is appropriate to their individual clinical needs, including transplantation. A number of these patients, if clinically appropriate, receive any necessary follow-up care in Northern Ireland. The services are provided in England under a UK-wide agreement, from which Northern Ireland patients receive a net overall benefit, for instance by having an access to a wider pool of donated organs. As such, there are no plans to depart from these arrangements at this time. I am open to exploring any areas for cross-border collaboration where there is demonstrable benefit to patients and health services.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any engagement they have had with Executive colleagues in relation to addressing child poverty; and (ii) any measures that will be included in the Programme for Government specifically to reduce the number of children living in poverty.
answer_text: The Programme for Government 2024-2027 was agreed by the Executive on 27th February and by the Assembly on 3rd March 2025.The Programme for Government sets out a number of commitments aimed at reducing poverty for people of all ages, including children and young people. These include additional support for families struggling with the costs of childcare, support for people affected by fuel poverty, investment in new homes, and improved support for people wanting to build skills and work in better jobs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a breakdown of the financial allocation that has been provided by her Department to reduce the number of children living in poverty in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Tackling poverty is a cross-cutting issue and therefore an Executive-wide responsibility. Once an allocation is made to each department in the Budget, it is for individual Ministers to allocate the budget to wherever they deem appropriate, including towards tackling child poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of the primary factors contributing to child poverty that have been identified by his Department.
answer_text: Many of the factors that contribute to child poverty were outlined in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report entitled “Child Poverty in Northern Ireland” published in March 2024. This report references the causes of poverty “Poverty rarely has a single cause but is a consequence of a range of factors including rising living costs, low pay, lack of work and inadequate social security benefits.” The report also states that the causes of poverty are complex and interlinked and that “Growing up without enough money means that children…are likely to have poorer health, education and wellbeing outcomes than their more well-off peers”.My Department has an important role in contributing to the wider Executive work to alleviate child poverty. This has focussed on a range of programmes and interventions that support children who may be impacted by social disadvantage or poverty. These include:the provision of free school meals to those low-income families in receipt of certain benefits; £75m for Targeting Social Need, provided to schools through the Common Funding Formula; £8.1m for the Extended Schools Programme;the funding of two full-service programmes in North and West Belfast totalling £0.7m. the rollout of a community-informed, place-based RAISE programme in fifteen localities across Northern Ireland to support the Department’s vision that “Every child and young person is Happy, Learning and Succeeding”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18149-22/27, to provide a breakdown of the primary factors contributing to child poverty that have been identified by his Department.
answer_text: To support the development of the Anti-Poverty strategy DfC has conducted a scoping review of the literature on poverty in NI, ROI and the UK. The scoping review identified many factors that increase the risk of falling into poverty.  Many of these risk factors have been found to stem from early years, with many childhood and other factors, such as poor educational attainment, family breakdown, disability and low-paid work, heavily affecting the risk of poverty in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his announcement of £13 million multi-year funding and £2 million for this financial year to improve provision for children with disabilities, to detail (i) the amount of this funding that has already been allocated: and (ii) the organisations it has been allocated to.
answer_text: (i) On 23rd October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with both increasing short breaks capacity and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.With regard to the allocation of the funding – each HSC Trust has worked intensively with my Department to prioritise proposals to assist with improving the Children with Disability service.In terms of the distribution of funding this has been agreed that it would be split per a capitation model based on population size. At this time, the DoH SPPG has reviewed Trusts Prioritisation Proposals, in terms of their proposed Action plans to assist with alleviating the pressures experienced within the Children with Disability Service. These proposals and indicative costs and timeframes will need to be discussed further to ascertain if they will be deliverable prior to allocation of finances.My Department and HSC Trusts continue to work collaboratively at pace in terms of progressing matters. (ii) In relation to the short-term action plans - the majority of these are in relation to increasing contracts with a range of voluntary and community services to provide additional family support, or breaks from caring through youth club type provision, after school clubs and enhanced opportunities for socialisation and personal development. Until the procurement process is completed and matters finalised – it would not be appropriate to name at this time the organisations that the Trusts intend to use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the temporary diversion of Daisy Hill Hospital’s high risk maternity clinic to Craigavon Area Hospital; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust are taking to restore that service.
answer_text: (i) Like many other specialties throughout health and social care, Obstetrics and Gynaecology services have been facing growing staffing challenges, impacting on midwifery and consultant teams. The high-risk maternity clinic in Daisy Hill Hospital is a specialised antenatal clinic for a small group of women and relies on appropriately skilled medical staff.Depending on clinical need, some higher risk women have previously been directed to Craigavon for particular specialty support.In the immediate term, with ongoing Consultant Obstetric workforce challenges, to mitigate risk and ensure safe care for mothers and babies, some additional categories of women attending the high-risk clinic are being redirected to receive their care at Craigavon Area Hospital.  All decisions taken in this regard are based on patient safety considerations. (ii) Significant efforts continue, to manage workforce issues and to develop a longer term, safe and sustainable model for Obstetrics and Gynaecology services across the Southern area.  A project team is now established to advise and support work to sustain safe and effective Obstetrics and Gynaecology services in the medium and longer term across the Southern Trust region.The Trust is actively exploring recruitment to attract the permanent staff required to safely deliver services on both sites, including seeking to source medical locums outside of NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of occasions departmental budgets have been subject to external audit in each of the last five years.
answer_text: While departmental budgets are not audited on an individual or recurring basis, there have been two relevant NI Audit Office (NIAO) reports produced over the last 5 years examining the NI budget process, the initial report in 2021 and a follow up in 2024. The reports can be accessed on the NIAO website via the below links:- https://www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/files/niauditoffice/NIAO_Report_NI%20Budget%20Process%20Report_Combo_4_WEB.pdf  https://www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/files/niauditoffice/documents/2024-05/NI%20Audit%20Office%20Report%20-%20Budgeting%20and%20Accountability.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 18835/22-27, whether she received a bid from the Minister of Health to fund a replacement scheme for the cross-border healthcare directive during the budget setting process for 2024-25.
answer_text: I did not receive a bid from the Minister of Health to fund a replacement scheme for the cross-border healthcare directive during the budget setting process for 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how many of the RHI Inquiry's 42 recommendations have been implemented; and (ii) the timeline for implementation of the outstanding recommendations.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Audit Office in its report published in October 2024 assessed that 26 of the 42 recommendations of the RHI Inquiry had been fully implemented.  Eleven were assessed as likely to be implemented based upon the planned actions.  A plan will be brought to the Executive shortly setting out the actions to be taken to complete the implementation of the remaining recommendations, including timescales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the statutory responsibility his Department holds for addressing (i) lighting; and (ii) surface defects which pose a risk to public safety, irrespective of land ownership.
answer_text: Under Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, my Department has a statutory duty to maintain all public roads in a safe and serviceable condition.In unadopted developments where the developer is still operating, they, and not my Department, are responsible for the repairing of street lighting and surface defects.  However, should a public safety risk arise within an unadopted development, irrespective of whether the developer is still operating, my Department can initiate repairs using Article 22 of the Private Street Order (PSO) and complete any remedial measures to make the development safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the construction of data centres; and (ii) to detail any efforts his Department has made to attract foreign direct investment by providing suitable sites with appropriate infrastructure to accommodate data centres.
answer_text: Invest NI does not track the construction status of data centres as it does not offer financial support to this sector given its limited scope for employment creation and high capital costs.  It is aware of the planned Atlantic Hub data centre adjacent to Foyle Port and available data centre sites at Kilroot and Ballykelly.The North does offer a skilled workforce from over 20 companies that work on the construction, kit out and maintenance of datacentres internationally.Invest NI has engaged with potential data centre investors seeking to identify sites for construction.  The location of such sites is complex due to many factors including the energy intensity of the facility and, in certain instances, the need to be with within a specified distance from a similar facility.  Any potential investor will need to engage directly with the appropriate utility provider to determine if adequate supplies are available for their demand requirements.	Invest NI recognises the important role that data centres play in modern digital infrastructure, in facilitating increased digitalization of the economy and in supporting technology services and related activities such as cloud computing and artificial intelligence.   		In this context,  Invest NI continues to hold discussions with data centre vendors and data centre users about developing the sector in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount his Department has spent on bovine TB compensation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last five financial years, the following amounts have been paid to herd owners as compensation for cattle slaughtered for the control of bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland:YearTotal TB Compensation Paid2019/20£19,481,5772020/21£22,271,5012021/22£28,036,0002022/23£38,008,3702023/24£36,498,440
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of investment provided by his Department to community and rural transport initiatives (i) during the 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) for the 2025-26 financial year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department provides grant support to Rural Community Transport Partnerships to support the delivery of rural Dial-a-Lift Scheme services.  The grant is allocated to an organisation’s operational area and cannot be mapped against constituency.  A table of 2024/25 funding is provided below.  Rural Transport Fund - Dial-a-Lift SchemeGrant Awards2024/25CDM Community Transport*£409,857Down District Accessible Transport£299,280Easilink Community Transport£308,953Fermanagh Community Transport£324,319Lagan Valley Rural Transport£116,168North Coast Community Transport£387,692South Antrim Community Transport£118,728Southern Area Partnership**£287,334Total£2,252,331* CDM Community Transport deliver services in a partnership arrangement with Out and About Community Transport ** Southern Area Partnership is comprised of three transport providers; Armagh Rural Transport, Down Armagh Rural Transport and Newry and Mourne Community Transport who deliver services in a partnership arrangement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to funding and support for initiatives to address isolation, loneliness and mental ill health in the farming community.
answer_text: I am in regular contact with the Minister of Health and other Executive colleagues on a range of rural and farming issues including health and wellbeing matters.    Further to this my Department’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework, provides a mechanism to fund initiatives which address loneliness and isolation, and improve physical and mental health and well-being. The Farm Families Health Check Programme (FFHCP) in conjunction with the Public Health Agency and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust provides a comprehensive physical and mental health screening and advice service to farmers who visit livestock markets and for rural people who attend a range of community events which the screening vehicle attends annually across Northern Ireland. Where necessary referrals are then made to General Practitioners or appropriate support services. Also, the Rural Support charity, who are also funded through the TRPSI Programme, are the “go to” resource for farmers and their families in rural Northern Ireland whenever they have a personal and/or farm business-related crisis. Rural Support provides a range of programmes and services for farmers and farm family members in support of their farm business and personal wellbeing. Their objective is to reduce emotional distress and help alleviate poor mental health triggers.My Department also has a long-standing funding relationship with the Young Farmers’ Clubs of Ulster (YFCU), through which they promote good health and wellbeing for young people in rural areas of Northern Ireland. A key element is to facilitate good health and wellbeing among the young people, and this is brought about through the YFCU’s engagement with the Agri-Rural Health Forum and also the training of Young Farmers Ambassadors to raise awareness of mental and emotional heath.Finally, the College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) provides a Student Support Service, accessible to all learners, which includes a focus on health and wellbeing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of the engagement he has had with the UK Government in relation to their changes to farmers’ inheritance tax.
answer_text: Since the announced changes in the Autumn Statement regarding inheritance tax and agricultural property relief and business property relief, I have had sustained and regular engagement with UK Government on this matter. I met with the NI Secretary of State on 4th November 2024 and expressed my concerns and urged that the matter be reconsidered. I also asked him to engage with the farming community to respond to the issues being raised. A joint letter from the First Minister, Deputy first Minister, Finance Minister and I was sent to the Chancellor of the Exchequer on 18th November 2024, which highlighted the impact of the inheritance tax changes on NI farms and urged the Chancellor to reconsider this decision. I attended an Inter-Ministerial Group meeting on 18th November 2024, at which the impact of the budget changes to inheritance tax was discussed. As a follow up to this meeting, I wrote a letter to Defra Minister of State on 26th November 2024, providing a copy of the analysis paper undertaken by my Department, on the impact of inheritance tax changes on NI farms and outlining concerns that Treasury figures significantly understate the impact. Most recently I have provided evidence to the House of Commons NI Affairs Committee in advance of an oral evidence meeting on Tuesday 10th December 2024, to discuss the potential impact of the planned inheritance tax changes on the farming sector in Northern Ireland.I will continue to make representations to UK Government and do all that I can to support the farming industry in Northern Ireland on this issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) claims; and (ii) compensation made to date in 2024 for vehicle damage due to potholes and surface defects, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department maintains its records by Roads Section Office, which broadly align with Council areas, and not by Parliamentary or Assembly constituency areas. In addition, my Department maintains its records by Financial Year and as the NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type. Therefore, the table below details the total number of vehicle damage claims received and the amount of vehicle damage compensation paid from 1 April 2024 to 30 November 2024 broken down by Roads Section Office.1 April 2024 to 30 November 2024Vehicle damage claims received2,348Vehicle damage compensation£802,668.43Please note that claims received in a financial year are not always concluded in the same financial year. Therefore, the compensation figures for a financial year or a specified period will usually include claims that were received in prior financial years or a previous period.Table 1 and Table 7 of the management information on roads related public liability claims, which is published online via the link below, include details on the full 2023/24 financial year which takes in to account figures for the period January 2024 to March 2024.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20192020I would, however, advise the member and other members using the above link to be cognisant of the caveats noted in respect to the information provided on roads related public liability claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an updated contact list for staff at the Department for Infrastructure's Southern Division, including telephone and email contacts, broken down by roles and areas of responsibility.
answer_text: Contact details and the roles of officials within my Department’s Southern Division are set out in each of the three Annual Roads Reports to Council;	Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council 2024/25 | Department for InfrastructureAnnual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2024 25Annual Report to Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council 2024Individual phone numbers and email addresses of officials are generally used for internal communication.   Divisional generic email address and central telephone numbers are monitored regularly by officials and are forwarded to the most appropriate person available to deal with the query.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department’s work to review and reform the housing points system.
answer_text: Work on the Fundamental Review of Allocations is ongoing. Stages 1 and 2 are complete, with 9 of the 20 proposals now implemented. My officials continue to work with the Housing Executive on Stage 3. More information is available at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/apply-for-a-home/fundamental-review-of-allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to improve (i) the condition of the electric vehicle charge point network; and (ii) the volume of electric vehicles that current electric vehicle charge point infrastructure can adequately service.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development, and utilisation of those charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs).Individual CPOs are best placed to advise if they have plans to install further charge points within their wider networks.My officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters.The projected future need for enhanced EV charging demand has been considered in NIE’s ‘A FUTURE NETWORK FOR ALL’ – NIE Network Business Plan, published in March 2023.Investing For The Future: Our RP6 Business Plan | Northern Ireland Electricity NetworksThe Systems Operator for Northern Ireland is responsible for the operation and planning of the transmission network. They have recently consulted on a draft strategy for 2025-2031, which will consider grid capability and capacity needs for the North over that period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the decision to refuse registration to the NI Search and Rescue Register for K9 Search and Rescue; and (ii) whether she has directed her officials to review the refusal.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the decision was made following the correct and established process and based on an assessment against the agreed criteria set out in the application process.I have not directed officials to review the decision.  An appeal mechanism within the application process is available to K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland should they wish to challenge the decision to decline membership to the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners Group. The details of this mechanism have been provided to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to list all locations where there are permits for the storage of refused-derived fuel (RDF) bails; and (ii) to provide a breakdown of the current planning applications in process for combined heat and power energy generation through the combustion of RDF.
answer_text: The information you request for (i) falls under the remit of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), who have provided the following information in the Table below:Permit / Licence NumberOperator NameSite AddressLN/13/35ReGen Waste LimitedWarrenpoint Harbour,WarrenpointLN/20/18ReGen Waste LimitedWarrenpoint Harbour, WarrenpointP0418/13AReGen Waste LimitedShepherds Drive, Carnbane Industrial Estate, NewryP0478/15AMcNabb Bros23 Downpatrick Road,DownpatrickP0489/15ARiver Ridge RecyclingMoy Road, PortadownP0534/16ARiver Ridge RecyclingEnviroparc, Maydown, DerryP0509/15AMcKinstry Skip Hire Limited81 – 83 Belfast Road, CrumlinLN/13/51Belfast Harbour CommissionersPollock Dock, Belfast HarbourLN/13/44Belfast Harbour CommissionersGotto Wharf, Belfast HarbourLN/18/01Ace Bates1 Duncrue Pass, DuncrueLN/13/30McKinstry Skip Hire Limited4 Northern Road, BelfastLN/20/01Irish Waste Services Limited94 – 96 Hillsborough Road, CarryduffP0087/05ARiverridge Recycling Ltd56 Craigmore Rd, GarvaghP0610/23AReGen Waste LtdDerryboy Road, Carnbane Industrial Estate, NewryWML 35/19Natural World Products Ltd32a Glenside Road Dunmurry LisburnWML 34/24Fermanagh and Omagh District CouncilDrummee, Derrygonnelly Rd, EnniskillenIn addition to the above list DAERA has had a Regulatory Position Statement (RPS) in place regarding the short term storage of Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) and/or Solid Recovered Fuel (SRF) at a dockside. As this is a RPS DAERA does not consider or grant permits when utilised.									A copy of	this RPS can be viewed at:									https://www.daera- ni.gov.uk/publications/regulatory-position-statement-short-term-storage-refuse- derived-fuel-and-solidIn relation to the information you request for (ii), my Department is currently processing one planning application. The application reference is T/2014/0114/F and is for the proposed development of a Residual Waste Treatment Facility incorporating a Mechanical and Biological Treatment (MBT) facility, an Energy from Waste (EfW) Thermal Treatment facility, an Incinerator Bottom Ash (IBA) Treatment facility, a Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) Bale Storage building, an Administration/Visitor Centre and related infrastructure. The application remains under consideration by my planning officials.It is possible that local planning authorities are processing applications of this type of development also, therefore the Member may wish to contact each council directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of recommendations in the South Armagh Policing Review that have been implemented; (ii) the number of outstanding recommendations still awaiting implementation; and (iii) who holds responsibility for the implementation of the outstanding recommendations.
answer_text: The recommendations from the South Armagh Policing Review are an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the funding for winter service, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that the additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round has enabled me to fully fund my Department’s Winter Service operation for 2024-25 with a budget of £7.1m. Allocations will be issued to Divisions throughout the winter based on need and severity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration his Department has given to including Maphoner Road, Mullaghbawn, in the winter gritting schedule.
answer_text: The salted network is 7,000km long, covering 28% of the road network and facilitating approximately 80% of traffic. This network has been developed in line with my Department’s Winter Service Policy RSPPG E022.Details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via the following link; DFI Roads Salted Network.Unfortunately the Maphoner Road does not meet the qualifying criteria for inclusion in the schedule of salted routes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration her Department has given to making adjustments to the physical infrastructure of police stations so that they are in keeping with surrounding areas.
answer_text: The management of the PSNI estate is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, for which he is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.Respecting the operational independence of both organisations, is paramount, therefore, you may wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the advice he would provide to residents of Ballykeel Road, Mullaghbawn, whose requests for the installation of a grit box has been refused.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria-based system which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.Members of the public can report issues with ice or snow on the road network using the NI Direct website: Report an issue with ice or snow | nidirect. The website also provides useful information and links to other sources of information on dealing with snow and ice conditions including my Department’s Winter Service Information Leaflet.I can also advise that the current policy is the same as when Minister Mallon was in my post and the  Highway Code provides useful advice for driving in wintry weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) when she received a bid from the Minister of Health for the necessary funding to reinstate the cross-border healthcare directive; and (ii) the response she provided to that bid.
answer_text: I have not received any specific bids for funding to reinstate the cross-border healthcare directive. The Minister of Health submitted bids to address waiting lists in both the Budget 2024-25 exercise and also in the October monitoring round. An earmarked allocation of £34m was provided as part of Budget 2024-25 for waiting lists, however it would be a matter for the Minister of Health to determine how this funding should be prioritised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when he made a bid to the Minister of Finance for the necessary funds to reinstate the cross-border healthcare directive; and (ii) the response he received to that bid.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether his Department has carried out an inspection of Greyhilla Road, Armagh; and (ii) when resurfacing works on Greyhilla Road will be carried out.
answer_text: My Department carries out a programme of regular cyclic inspections of all roads across the North including the Greyhilla Road.While many roads including Greyhilla Road, Armagh would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) housing developments; and (ii) housing units that cannot proceed due to an inability to connect to the water and wastewater drainage network because of capacity issues.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive (Development Programme Group – DPG) is responsible for the management of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) on behalf of the Department for Communities. DPG is responsible for both the management of the 3-Year SHDP and the ‘in-year’ management / Delivery Risk Assessment for the current SHDP year. As part of the Delivery Risk Assessment for the 2024/25 year, DPG has engaged with NI Water officials regarding potential risks to programme delivery in relation to wastewater infrastructure, in addition to reviewing information available via the Planning Portal. Many of new social housing schemes planned as part of the SHDP can still achieve Planning Approval and NI Water approval to connect to wastewater infrastructure, subject to site-specific wastewater engineering solutions being agreed via the Wastewater Impact Assessment (WwIA) process. Wider engagement with NI Water on the impacts on SHDP delivery going forward remains on-going at this stage. The Housing Executive has been recently advised by Apex Housing to the Department for Communities, specifically in relation to new social housing schemes under construction and programmed to start in Derry-~Londonderry. NI Water has informed Apex that, due to substantial cuts in its PC21 funding, the planned infrastructure works in Derry~Londonderry (Coshquin pumping station), including those needed for new housing on Apex lands at Buncrana Road (H2 zoned lands), have been postponed indefinitely until funding becomes available. Apex is in contract with BW Homes & Construction Ltd to build 421 homes under Phase 1 of the H2 lands development. Given the recent announcement by NI Water, they are not in a position to provide a timeline for the delivery of the necessary NI Water infrastructure to connect to the homes. This is impacting on Apex’s current phase of development, which is on-site, and is also impacting on Apex’s future development plans in this area which includes further planned phases at the H2 Lands, Buncrana Road and at H1B Lands, Skeoge”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.ukMaking direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) commercial; and (ii) industrial development projects that have been (a) delayed; and (b) withdrawn as a consequence of an inability to connect to the water network in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has advised that it does not hold any information on the number of commercial or industrial development projects that have been delayed or withdrawn.  It has also advised that approximately 18,500 properties could be connected if developers design, fund and construct alternative solutions, such as stormwater offsetting.  No legislative amendment is required to permit this type of developer contribution.  You will be aware that I am taking a three-pronged approach to help address wastewater capacity challenges. Firstly, I continue to work with my Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, I have asked my officials to examine the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) value of development projects that have been stalled as a result of an inability to connect to the water network in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My department does not hold an official verified record of the figures the member requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the findings provided by NI Water in August 2024 that applications for the development of 19,000 homes cannot go ahead due to inadequate wastewater capacity; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact of this to (a) housing demand; (b) homelessness; and (c) private rental rates.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that 18,000 properties can be connected if developers design, fund and construct alternative solutions such as stormwater offsetting.   The 19,000 properties that require infrastructure upgrades or expansions that are deemed beyond the scope of current developer-led solutions require NI Water capital upgrades.  If NI Water’s Price Control 21 investment plan was fully funded, connections would have been limited to 4,500 of these 19,000.    It should be noted that these numbers are estimated figures that NI Water has obtained   through discussion with developers and they are subject  to a degree of flux as developer’s plans change with time and other market pressures. (ii)  (a), (b) & (c)I am fully aware of the impact wastewater capacity issues are having on delivering housing supply, including social housing. Therefore, I am taking a three-pronged approach to this matter.  Firstly, I continue to work with my Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, I have asked my officials to examine the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a detailed breakdown of the number of (i) housing developments; and (ii) housing units that cannot proceed due to an inability to connect to the water network.
answer_text: My department does not hold an official verified record of  the figures the member requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the inherent ineffectiveness of NI Water’s funding arrangements, as published in March 2024 in the report by the Northern Ireland Audit Office on Funding water infrastructure in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have noted the Comptroller & Auditor General’s report on the funding of water infrastructure.We all want to see a sustainable water and wastewater infrastructure which benefits everyone across our society. The Department has been operating in a challenging budgetary position for some time. Despite this, NI Water was fully funded for the first three years of PC21 and this year I have provided NI Water with a budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion for 2024/25. This represents just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for the Department for Infrastructure. I am committed to working with Executive colleagues to secure the necessary funding for this vital infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he has commissioned an independent expert-led review of NI Water since taking office; and (ii) to detail the number of (a) internal reviews of NI Water; and (b) independent expert-led reviews of NI Water in the last three years.
answer_text: I have not commissioned an independent expert led review of NI Water since taking office. My Department has previously carried out considerable work on the funding and governance options for NI Water and officials continue to engage with the company within my policy framework.   The working group established to oversee the previous work comprised corporate finance and public expenditure experts from the Strategic Investment Board, Department of Finance and Department for Infrastructure.  The group was also attended and advised by water sector industry experts from the Utility Regulator and NI Water.   I continue to progress a three-pronged approach in (i) continuing to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment; (ii) reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions; and (iii) in securing Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a detailed breakdown of the current funding and grants streams available to arts and voluntary sector organisations.
answer_text: Please see in the table below a list of current funding and grant streams available to arts sector organisations and voluntary sector organisations by my Department. Name of Funding/Grant StreamSport NI Creating Opportunities Crowdfunder small grants programme. Foras na Gaeilge Scéim na gCampaí Samhraidh / Summer Camp Scheme  Foras na Gaeilge Scéim na nImeachtaí Oige / Youth Event Scheme Foras na Gaeilge Ciste Infheistíochta do sholáthar réamhscolaíochta / Investment fund for early years provisionForas na Gaeilge Scéim Scoláireachtaí Gaeltachta / Gaeltacht Scholarship Scheme Foras na Gaeilge Festivals SchemeForas na Gaeilge The Professional and Amateur Drama Companies SchemeForas na Gaeilge The Major Arts Events SchemeForas na Gaeilge Irish Officers SchemeForas na Gaeilge Scheme for Providers of Specialised Irish Language Training Courses for the Public SectorForas na Gaeilge Colmcille Projects SchemeForas na Gaeilge Colmcille Bursary SchemeForas na Gaeilge Colmcille Small Grants SchemeUlster–Scots Agency Music and Dance Tuition in the community Ulster–Scots Agency Community Festivals containing Ulster Scots contents Ulster–Scots Agency Burns Events and small events promoting Ulster-ScotsUlster–Scots Agency Five-day Ulster-Scots programmes for children and young peopleUlster–Scots Agency A variety of programmes including the Community Impact programme, Community Radio Stations

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to the First Minister's comments in the Assembly on 12 November 2024 in relation to the progress of the Narrow Water Bridge, for her assessment of the proposal for a fixed bridge as part of the Southern Relief Road project approximately three miles upstream from the lifting bridge at Narrow Water.
answer_text: We welcome the progress being made on the Narrow Water Bridge project.  The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) has responsibility for the design and development of the Southern Relief Road project in Newry.  We recognise the benefits that the Relief Road project will bring, notably the easing of traffic congestion in Newry City centre and the improved access to other regional gateways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts to secure alternative provisions following the closure of Newry Early Years Children and Family Centre.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have confirmed that they are very confident that this service will be reinstated in the early new year. A provider has been agreed, a venue in Newry has been confirmed and the Trust is currently going through necessary contractual and required checks before the service can recommence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13588/22-27 and the publishing of the Department’s annual report to council, to detail the (i) inspections; and (ii) works that have been carried out on (a) Cladymore Road; (b) Friary Road; and (c) the Clady Road and Blackquarter Road junction, Armagh, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my Department’s ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired. In addition to the budget pressures my Department has been suffering with staffing vacancies which limits our ability to complete inspections and manage the repair of identified defects. Cladymore Road was resurfaced in 2022 and is in good condition. It was inspected on 13 December 2023 with no actionable defects identified. Friary Road was inspected on 25 January 2024 when 3 actionable defects were identified for repair and the Clady Road/Blackquarter Road junction was inspected on 25 January 2024 with one actionable defect identified which has now been repaired. However, due to continued resource pressures, work to repair the remaining defects has been delayed. My Department continues to work hard to identify and undertake repairs but this highlights the difficult circumstances in which my Department is operating and I will continue to seek the necessary resources to ensure proper maintenance of the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a detailed breakdown of the engagement his Department has had to date in relation to the installation of a lifting bridge as part of the Southern Relief Road project.
answer_text: I have reviewed the previous decision made on the form of the bridge over the Newry Ship Canal, in the absence of an Executive, under the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 and concluded that the decision for a 50m span fixed bridge remained unchanged.  Confirmation of my decision was communicated to Newry and Armagh MLAs on 25th April 2024.I am aware of concerns regarding the proposed fixed bridge and my department has considered several alternative bridge span and structural form options. As the Newry Ship Canal has been scheduled under Article 3 of the Historic Monuments and Archaeological Object (NI) Order 1995, my Department has engaged with the Department for Communities (DfC) Historic Environment Division (HED) to assess the impact of those options on the Newry Ship Canal Scheduled monument as part of those considerations.  Public consultations have also taken place at each relevant stage of the project.Given the important strategic benefits that the Newry Southern Relief Road will bring to the city and surrounding area, through improved connectivity; reduced congestion; lower emissions, and improvements to air quality within Newry city centre, officials are progressing the scheme to the next development stage. This will include the production of draft Orders and an Environmental Impact Assessment Report (EIAR) for the project, which will be subject to further public consultation when published in January 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the First Minister's comments following a meeting of the North South Ministerial Council on 13 September 2024, (i) for an update on any action their Department (a) has taken; and (b) will take to advance the construction of Casement Park; and (ii) for an update on the latest timeline for the completion of the Casement Park project.
answer_text: The First Minister raised this matter on a number of occasions with the UK Government and the Government of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the action (i) his Department; and (ii) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust have taken to reinforce maternity services at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: (i) My Officials coordinated a regional meeting with other Trusts to highlight the issues Daisy Hill are currently facing and to ascertain if any support may be forthcoming.   In addition, my officials are meeting with Southern Health and Social Care colleagues twice weekly at present to develop short, medium and long terms plans for both contingency arrangements and to stabilise the service.  SPPG have also requested and are now receiving a daily sitrep report which provides an update on staffing levels, escalation status, any potential diverts that may take place or have taken place and planned activity.(ii) Currently the out of hours rota gaps in Daisy Hill Hospital are being covered by the substantive consultant staff undertaking locum shifts to cover gaps and vacancies. Out of hours rota gaps in Craigavon Area Hospital are similarly covered by substantive consultant staff undertaking locum shifts.This is an added pressure on the substantive staff, particularly when they take leave. In addition to the staffing above, through the Trust’s International Medical Recruitment programme, a new Obstetrics and Gynaecology  Senior Doctor is working on the Daisy Hill Hospital site and is undergoing a period of induction and upskilling to enable them to work at Consultant level. It is expected that this will take approximately 6-9 months. A retired obstetrician has also returned to provide support to the Trust.The Trust continues to progress recruitment across all vacant posts with the objective of filling the vacant substantive positions. Recruitment for consultant Obstetrics and Gynaecology vacant posts which includes two for Daisy Hill Hospital is ongoing including exploring sourcing medical locums outside Northern Ireland. The Trust is actively looking at innovative recruitment solutions to attract the permanent workforce required to safely deliver this service on both sites.As part of the Trust’s Obstetrics and Gynaecology Stabilisation Plan, further urgent actions to stabilise and support the retention of the Trust’s existing Obstetrics and Gynaecology  consultant workforce are underway including:Sourcing of further locums from UK. Seeking further international recruitment options. Scoping to determine if any retired consultants would be interested in locum opportunities (one retired consultant providing cover one day a week since the 11th November 2024). Ongoing discussion with the medical teams on both the Craigavon and Daisy Hill Hospital sites to consider service sustainability with the intention to develop a workforce plan for the short / medium term focusing on recruitment and retention, review of the medical models of care to provide sustainable quality and safe obstetric services across the Southern Trust area.Midwifery Staffing Midwifery services are provided across Craigavon Area Hospital, Daisy Hill Hospital and Community Areas of Craigavon & Banbridge, Newry & Mourne and Armagh and Dungannon localities. The Trust midwifery teams

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the steps that residents must take to secure the installation of grit boxes in their area; (ii) the reasons that requests to his Department for the installation of grit boxes would not be fulfilled in areas without grit boxes; and (iii) his Department’s policy in relation to applications for a grit box.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether his Department has carried out an inspection of Derrymagowan Road, Armagh; and (ii) when resurfacing works on Derrymagowan Road will commence.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. While many roads including Derrymagowan Road, Armagh, would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a detailed breakdown of the roads in the Newry and Armagh constituency that have been included in the winter gritting schedule.
answer_text: The salted network is 7,000km long, covering 28% of the road network and facilitating approximately 80% of traffic.  Details of all salting routes that meet the criteria for treatment can be viewed on my Department’s website via the following link; DFI Roads Salted Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the impact of the rise in Employers National Insurance on Social Enterprises; and (ii) to detail any measures that will be implemented to mitigate this impact.
answer_text: The Budget will affect local businesses in a variety of ways, though the nature of that will depend upon the characteristics of each individual business, including its size, which sector it operates in and how it chooses to manage new arrangements.There are welcome benefits for workers as a result of the increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage. However, rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget increases employer National Insurance contributions. At a time of high costs on businesses this will put many businesses under more strain.The full impacts of the Autumn Budget on individual sectors, including the social enterprise sector, will take time to become clear.My officials and I will continue to engage with local social enterprise business stakeholders and consider the full detail of the announcements, as well as the possible implications for the North’s economy, in the context of my economic plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to ensure pay parity for Health and Social Care workers.
answer_text: Following the meetings with trades unions on 14 November, my Department has continued a rigorous examination of available funding, and sought additional savings from across the HSC.While we remain short of the overall funding required to meet the recommendations of health pay review bodies in full, my Department is now in a position to improve on the offer made previously. This has been communicated to trades unions.I remain determined to implement, in full, the recommendations of the health pay review bodies. Resolving all public sector pay issues remains an issue for the Executive as a whole; I will continue to seek a collaborative Executive response. Efforts to secure additional savings within the health sector will also continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for her assessment of the impact of the rise in Employers National Insurance on Social Enterprises; and (ii) to detail any measures that will be implemented to mitigate this impact.
answer_text: The Chancellor’s announcement of a rise in employers’ National Insurance Contributions will mean that many of our employers, including Social Enterprises, will face higher costs and increased pressure in already difficult times. I discussed this issue with a number of representatives from the voluntary and community sector earlier this week, alongside the Communities and Economy Ministers. The critical role social enterprises play in delivering services to citizens here, including support for some of the most vulnerable individuals in our society, is vitally important. Therefore, it is extremely disappointing that the British Government has not recognised the need to either provide support for, or to mitigate the impact of the changes to employers’ National Insurance Contributions for social enterprises. My officials have already raised this matter with their counterparts in Westminster, and rest assured, I will also be taking this matter up with British Government Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount his Department has (i) invested in public transport fare subsidies in each of the last three years; and (ii) allocated for public transport fare subsidies for the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Funding allocated specifically to subsidise fares, through the Concessionary Fare Scheme, on services provided by Translink and other transport operators, in the last three years is detailed below.2022/23: £38.9m 2023/24: £44.6m2024/25: £47.8m Budget allocations for the 2025-26 financial year remain under consideration.Translink are primarily funded through a combination of fare revenue and Department funding, therefore, all funding provide by my Department to Translink helps to keep fares low.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any Programme for Government commitments he has secured to make public transport more accessible and more affordable.
answer_text: Whilst my Department will not lead any of the nine priorities within the draft Programme for Government (PfG), it will play a fundamental role as an enabler to the achievement of these priorities including: Grow a globally competitive and sustainable economy.Provide more social, affordable and sustainable housing.Protecting Lough Neagh and the environment; andReform and transformation of public services. 	A well-maintained, safe and reliable public transport system will contribute to the successful achievement of the draft PfG priorities including providing accessible public transport for our rural and disabled communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the current impediments to the development and delivery of a housing supply chain which adequately meets demand; and (ii) any action he is taking to address these impediments.
answer_text: The work I have taken forward in developing a new Housing Supply Strategy identified the need for a whole system approach to housing supply issues, which recognises there is not one single overarching challenge impacting on supply.The Strategy has identified several key levers needed to address supply chain issues including infrastructure, the planning system, finance, land availability and construction skills. It also sets out a strategic long-term framework of the policies and actions required to deliver the supply needed.I am therefore taking forward the Strategy as an Executive one, given the need for a collective and committed response to action and investment across Government to address these challenges. Alongside this, I have also successfully secured the inclusion of Housing as a priority in the PfG.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement she has had with Executive colleagues in relation to efficiency savings in their departments.
answer_text: I have always been clear with my Executive colleagues that they must take action to ensure their department lives within its budget allocation this year. The consequences of not doing so are severe.Most recently, I wrote to Executive colleagues on 14 November, recognising that we left the October Monitoring round with a funding shortfall of £180 million but highlighting that we must do everything we can to find the money to cover public sector pay awards. Therefore, I asked Executive colleagues to do what they can to deliver savings that can be redirected to public sector pay and to notify my department of these as early as possible. I have been clear that I am determined to work collaboratively with colleagues to bring the best possible outcomes for our workers and for our citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with (i) Executive colleagues; and (ii) the Irish Government in relation to the reintroduction of the cross-border healthcare directive.
answer_text: I am deeply invested in effective engagement and collaboration with my Executive colleagues on areas such as waiting list initiatives amongst many others to deliver better outcomes for the people of Northern Ireland. The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme was introduced on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted to the Department of Finance during this year’s budget setting process. The Reimbursement Scheme bid of £31million was part of a wider bid for £135million of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. This was in addition to a separate bid of £53million, within the wider £135million, specifically to address some 4,400 patients waiting over 2 years for orthopaedic procedures. Regrettably, none of that bid was met in the 2024/25 Budget.The funding allocated to my Department in June Monitoring was insufficient to enable me to cover the cost of delivering existing services. My Department has continued to seek additional funding to be provided to assist with addressing waiting lists. A £10m bid was submitted as part of October Monitoring which unfortunately was unsuccessful. The introduction of a replacement scheme will be subject to the allocation of additional resources to help reduce waiting lists. As such, there is insufficient funding at present to reinstate a cross-border reimbursement scheme. It must also be noted that whilst there are clear benefits with the delivery of a replacement scheme, any funding provided, must be prioritised based on maximising clinical outcomes and value for money. Finally, my Department is committed to exploring any North-South cooperation which will improve outcomes for patients in Northern Ireland. We work directly with the Department of Health in Dublin, as well as via the North-South Ministerial Council, across a range of cross-border health issues and welcome any opportunity for further collaboration to tackle shared challenges in health and social care across both jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support that will be available for low-income families over the winter period.
answer_text: My Department provides financial support through the Discretionary Support Scheme that can offer help towards short-term living expenses or with the purchase of household items for those people in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.  Funding has also been allocated to our Social Supermarket Scheme to help those in or at risk of food poverty. My Department provides help through the Affordable Warmth scheme, as well as the Winter Fuel Payments to those who are eligible. I am pleased to have secured £17m to help mitigate the shortfall felt by many of our pensioners losing out on the Winter Fuel Payment. This will be used to issue a one-off payment of £100 to all pensioner households in Northern Ireland. It is anticipated the payment will be made via existing payment channels before the end of March 2025.I urge those pensioners impacted by the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment scheme to contact with my Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service which puts people in touch with any benefits, supports and services to which they may be entitled. There is a window up to 21st December 2024 within which a Pension Credit award can be backdated to allow eligible customers to receive this year’s Winter Fuel Payment. From April 2024 benefits were increased by 6.7%, in line with the September 2023 rate of inflation, whilst the state pension was increased by 8.5%. The UK Government has announced that from April 2025 the basic and new state pensions, and the standard minimum guarantee in pension credit, will be increased by 4.1%. All other benefits will be increased by 1.7%. I will bring forward legislation to introduce corresponding increases in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to retain (i) GPs; and (ii) other primary care professionals.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs and their practice teams.  I also recognise the importance of building a sustainable GP and wider general practice workforce.My Department has taken forward a range of actions to address workforce and workload challenges.  These include:  Taking speedy action to progress the 2023/24 Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Board recommendation for GPs and applying a 6.0% uplift for GP pay.  In addition, a 6% uplift for practice staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift for other expenses (3% recurrent) were also applied.     Agreeing the new 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract.  A key aim of these new contract arrangements was to provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices.  Increasing the annual number of GP training places from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 (an increase of 86%).  Working with NIMDTA on innovations to the local GP training programme with the objective of enhancing the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.Focussing on building the GP workforce through funding a number of initiatives, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, GP Retainer Scheme, GP Mentor Scheme and GP Fellowship Schemes.The Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme supports recruitment of GPs in hard to recruit areas. Since its establishment in January 2023, 77 GP practices have applied and received approval in principle for funding under the scheme. 41 practices have applied and received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP. The introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, Practice based Pharmacists along with additional General Practice Nurses and Primary Care MDTs is also making a difference to the capacity and sustainability of primary care services.A bid for transformation funding to support the further rollout of the MDT Programme across the five-year span of the transformation fund has been submitted.  Despite the continuing challenging budgetary position, I remain committed to supporting general practice so that it can continue to provide vital services to our communities both now, and in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Criminal Bar Association following the one-day strike by criminal solicitors and criminal barristers.
answer_text: My Department is in regular contact with the representative bodies for the legal professions, both on an ad hoc basis and through monthly stocktake meetings. I also enjoy regular engagement with representatives of the professions and both my officials and I have met separately with representatives of the Criminal Bar Association and Solicitors Criminal Bar Association since 4 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the process for complaints on the handling of a PSNI investigation.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman’s Office investigates complaints about the conduct of police officers in Northern Ireland. You can make a complaint online, by email or by phone.  Further details of the process for making a complaint can be found on the Police Ombudsman’s website at: https://www.policeombudsman.org/

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any changes in the journey time from Newry to Dublin on the Enterprise rail service.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that the new Cross-Border rail timetable has increased the number of daily Enterprise trains between Newry and Dublin Connolly from 8 to 15 services per day (Monday to Saturday).  These additional services have provided significant service enhancements on the eastern railway corridor, almost doubling the daily seat capacity.  With more trains now operating through the greater Dublin suburban area at peak times. The average journey time between Newry & Dublin Connolly was 1 hour 17 minutes in May 2024. This has marginally increased by 4 minutes, with the average journey time between Newry & Dublin Connolly now 1 hour 21 minutes. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rates relief available to high street businesses following the announcement of the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 17794/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he has taken to secure a fair pay deal for Translink workers.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  Following the Executive’s agreement to allocate the Department additional resource budget of £22.8m in the recent October Monitoring Round, a further budget allocation was provided to Translink.  This funding will assist the Group as it prepares to negotiate a 2024-25 pay award with trade unions.I and my Department will not be negotiating with the transport unions, this is the responsibility of Translink however my officials will continue to engage with the employer as it seeks to put forward a fair and equitable offer to its workers that is also affordable within a constrained budget envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the merits of establishing a pedestrian crossing at the Dublin Road and Chancellors Road junction, Newry, to allow schoolchildren from St. Moninna's Primary School to cross safely to the Church of the Sacred Heart, Cloghogue.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.I have asked my officials to undertake an assessment for a pedestrian crossing at Dublin Road / Chancellors Road junction in line with current policy.  If the location meets the necessary threshold, it may be considered for inclusion in a future works programme, subject to the availability of funding.
