question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department has taken to retain First Derivatives jobs in Newry.
answer_text: FD Technologies plc, incorporating the business units of First Derivative and KX, is one of our largest capital markets consulting companies.  The business is particularly important to the sub-regional economy around Newry, and employs over 900 locally. Earlier this year FD Technologies plc announced restructuring plans to separate the company into 3 separate firms. Its marketing technology division, MRP, merged with US firm CONTENTgine in March to form PharosIQ.  US software company EPAM Systems, Inc. (NYSE: EPAM) has announced it will acquire First Derivative and the deal is expected to complete in December 2024.Since the announcement of the restructure, I have met with the First Derivative senior management team to discuss any potential risk to the operations in the North of Ireland. I have also highlighted the importance of maintaining the business in the North and the opportunities to continue to grow the business, based on the strong track record of success and the capability of local talent.Invest NI CEO Kieran Donoghue has also met with FD to discuss the business’ re-structure plans. Invest NI has a long-term strategic relationship with the business and is engaging with the management team to help ensure a strong future for the business in Newry.Early indications are that the announcement does not initially change or impact the roles of FD Technologies staff in the North Ireland, who will continue to work as normal. I am scheduled to meet the senior team in January 2025, post-acquisition, to discuss any further developments and opportunities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what representations they have made to the British Government to impose sanctions on and encourage divestment from the state of Israel.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to explain the rationale as to why a same day return train ticket from Newry to Belfast costs 68 per cent more than a same day return train ticket from Dundalk to Belfast.
answer_text: Translink have confirmed that customers can travel from Newry to Belfast for £19 per day on NI Railways using the iLink product.  It costs €16.38 for a Day Return from Dundalk to Belfast on Irish Rail.    Both companies, Translink and Irish Rail, are funded in different ways. Translink last increased Enterprise fares in March 2023 which had not been increased for 5 years prior to this revision. Irish Rail have been able to reduce both their standard and online fares by 20% in recent years due to funding provided by the Department of Transport.   Translink have recently introduced a new online fares structure for the Enterprise service which is more closely aligned with Irish Rail’s web fares.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the engagement he has had with the British Government in relation to the establishment of a standalone IP address for the north to prevent geo-blocking of (i) RTÉ; (ii) TG4; and (iii) Virgin Media programming.
answer_text: The remit of my Department in relation to this specific matter is limited and the issues are complex, involving broadcasting and intellectual rights issues, as well as efficient use of technical resources.  Legislation available to me does not include powers to establish Internet Protocol addresses. However, I have written to the Minister for Culture, Media and Sport to ask to be kept informed of discussions between the British and Irish Governments on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when works to address surface defects on the B28 Summerisland Road, Armagh, will commence.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years as a result of Tory cuts and austerity. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired. My officials are aware of the condition of Summerisland Road and I can confirm that the highest priority defects have been instructed for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action she is taking to ensure that the pay uplift for public sector workers is honoured.
answer_text: I fully recognise the difficult financial challenges facing all departments and I am committed to working together with Ministerial colleagues to try and find a way forward.As an Executive, we have previously committed to ensuring that public sector workers are paid fairly as they are vital to the delivery of our public services.  Following the October Monitoring round, we are left with a funding shortfall of £180 million. However, we must do everything we can to find the money to cover public sector pay awards. I have asked Ministers to do what they can to deliver savings and that any such savings are identified as early as possible and notified to my department, so that any funding available can be prioritised for public sector pay awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to her statement on 11 November 2024 in relation to the October Monitoring Round, to detail how the allocation of £24 million to the Department for Communities for new build social housing will facilitate the construction of 1,400 new houses in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: This question has been passed to my Department as the delivery of social housing falls under my remit.My department has not yet set a target for new social homes based on the recently announced increased allocation. The target will be set by my department following engagement with the Housing Executive. As the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, the Housing Executive will assess how the additional budget secured in October monitoring round may best be used to meet identified housing need before any final starts target in 2024/25 can be confirmed. I am advised that work on this is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to the Minister of Finance statement on 11 November 2024 in relation to the October Monitoring Round, to detail how the allocation of £24 million to his Department for new build social housing will facilitate the construction of 1,400 new houses in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: As the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, the Housing Executive will assess how the additional budget secured in October monitoring round may best be used to meet identified housing need before any final starts target in 2024/25 can be confirmed. I am advised that work on this is underway.

answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the NIHE, as they carry out the data collection for the Northern Ireland House Condition Survey. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of children living in poverty, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) annually release official statistics for “Children in low income families: local area statistics”. answer_text: In answering this question, we have interpreted this it as relating to Children with Disabilities (CwD) services.  The business cases have been divided into those that have been approved and funding allocated, those submitted for consideration with a funding stream available and those that have been submitted for consideration with no funding stream available. The timeframe provided in respect of these business cases is from 2022/23 to date (November 2024). In relation to points (i) and (ii) the business cases are made up of a number of elements covering both new provision and expansion of existing provision. In the tables current year effect (CYE) refers to initial allocation to Trust in year of application which for timing reasons can be less then what is needed for a full year of operation. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether Health Trusts are adequately responding to the need for respite services and facilities.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of respite/short break services, aligned to assessed needs and within financial resources available.Within Children’s Services respite/short breaks are provided by HSCTs in partnership with the community and voluntary sector. Respite/short breaks may be provided through hospitals, children’s homes and specialist foster care settings. Within Adult Services planned and emergency respite/short break services are primarily provided in statutory and independent sector care homes. HSCTs cite challenges regarding respite/short break provision across Children and Adult Services, linked to increasing demand, complexity of need, response to placement breakdowns, emergency/crisis situations and facilitating access to care and treatment in hospital settings, as required.HSCTs continue to offer Carer’s Assessments and alternatives to bed based respite/short breaks including Day Care, Self-Directed Support e.g. Direct Payments, Managed Budgets, Cash grants, Domiciliary Care or Shared Lives Services, as appropriate.My Department is currently finalising the Children with Disability Framework in response to increasing challenges faced by families of Children with Disability, and the corresponding difficulties experienced by HSCTS in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. It is recognised that a review and reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a regional perspective and locally in each of the HSCTs is required, including the provision of respite/Short Break Services. On 17 October 24, I approved the allocation of £2 million in year for children with disabilities, with a recurrent impact of £13.1 million. My Department has ensured this funding has been ringfenced to directly improve short breaks capacity, while rapidly expanding a range of family, therapeutic and behavioural supports. My Department is also leading on the reform of adult social care via the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The Commissioning and Contracting workstream is taking forward the review of the regional care homes contract and specification which will include provision for respite placements within the care home setting. The Maximising capacity workstream is focusing on effective use of the available regional care home resource.Furthermore, my Department has also established a Task & Finish Group to finalise a Learning Disability Service Model. The draft model sets out a regionally consistent framework to improve outcomes across several HSC areas including support for families and carers. It is envisaged that families and carers will be offered flexible, practical and emotional support to support adults with a learning disability to live in the community and have a meaningful life. Consideration will be given to increasing the level and diversity of short breaks services and adapting existing services and environments to better support those with more

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the outcome of the emergency disability summit that involved his Department, the Health Trust and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to discuss services for disabled children; and (ii) any action he has subsequently taken in relation to this.
answer_text: My Department held a regional summit with Trusts, RQIA and PHA on 26 September 2024, to identify areas that could be rapidly progressed to address the immediate unmet needs of children with disabilities.I refer you to my written statement to the assembly on 23 October 2024, which provides detail on the outcome of the summit and actions subsequently taken.https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/assembly-business/official-report/written-ministerial-statements/department-of-health---children-with-disabilities---short-breaks/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of the Department of Health, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority and Health Trusts convening a round table meeting to interrogate how to proceed in relation to respite services for families with children with complex needs.
answer_text: My Department, including policy and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, engaged with Trusts, the RQIA and the wider sector to develop the Framework for Children with Disabilities. This is the strategic response the growing HSC challenges, which will require several years of sustained investment. Since 2023, partners have finalised and implemented the Framework through a dedicated workstream as part of the Children’s Social Care Strategic Reform Programme. In September 2024, my officials convened Trusts and the RQIA to identify proposals and actions to rapidly accelerate our work and I refer you to my written statement to the assembly on 23 October 2024, which provides detail on the outcome of the summit and actions subsequently taken.https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/assembly-business/official-report/written-ministerial-statements/department-of-health---children-with-disabilities---short-breaks/ To progress this package, partners have effectively collaborated to develop a significant funding package at pace.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there has been an analysis of the number of residential beds needed to provide adequate respite provision to meet current demand.
answer_text: Respite or “Short Break Care” is defined as the provision of regular, occasional or emergency but time limited breaks to those who are caring for others. Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of respite/short break services, aligned to assessed needs and within financial resources available. Whilst Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) carried out and published a comprehensive analysis of care home bed availability across Northern Ireland in December 2023, there has been no recent regional analysis of the number of residential beds needed to provide adequate respite provision to meet current demand. It is acknowledged that there are challenges accessing respite/short break services regionally across all directorates of care. Challenges regarding service provision faced by HSCTs are associated with increasing demand, complexity of need, the requirement to facilitate hospital discharge, and the need to respond to emergency/crisis situations such as placement breakdown, when required.My department, working with Trusts remains committed to ensuring Service Users and Carers receive appropriate service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to review traffic calming measures on Cavanacaw Road, Armagh.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  As such, my officials review Road Traffic Collision data provided by the PSNI, identifying sites where there are clusters of collisions to determine if engineering solutions could be introduced to improve road safety. My Department has recently constructed a new footway as well as a number of pedestrian islands along Cavanacaw Road.  Given the good safety record along this stretch, no further measures are currently being considered at this time.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schedule for resurfacing works on Church Road, Bessbrook.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on our ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. While many roads including Church Road, Bessbrook would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. Details of the planned works programmes for the 2024/25 financial year are provided in the Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council 2024/25 which can be found at;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-southern-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-24-25.pdf   In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress his Department has made on the implementation of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review recommendations.
answer_text: The recommendations in the Review provide an evidence-based framework to inform future investment in the railways across the island. However, more work is needed to test the feasibility and affordability of the Review’s recommendations and to secure the necessary funding to take projects forward. Already, rail services on the island are being developed towards the Report’s vision. This includes an increase to an hourly train service between Dublin and Belfast, while procurement has also started on a new, faster, more sustainable “Enterprise” train fleet for the Dublin-Belfast route.The Department of Transport in Dublin has secured support from the European Investment Bank Advisory Services to help progress implementation of the Rail Review’s recommendations. With the assistance of EIB consultants, we are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail and NTA to consider the recommendations and identify those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035. Translink have a number of feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which are due to report by spring/summer next year. These include four funded from the Union Connectivity Development Fund including: the reopening of Antrim-Lisburn (with a link to Belfast International Airport); Armagh-Portadown; Electrification of Belfast to the Border and Portadown-Derry.We are working with Translink to identify the next stages in these studies and to also identify the recommendations in the Review for which feasibility studies have not yet commenced. I will then need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available. The outcomes from the EIB work and the ongoing feasibility studies will help inform this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of where blue-green infrastructure funding has been allocated in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Blue Green Infrastructure Fund was launched in 2020 and ended in 2023. An outline of the amount recorded against each year can be found at the links below:The details for 2022/23 are as follows: AIMS PortalThe details for 2021/22 are as follows: AIMS PortalThe details for 2020/21 are as follows: AIMS PortalNo budget has been allocated to the Blue Green Fund in the current financial year.Whilst I have no specific plans for a dedicated investment programme for blue/green infrastructure going forward, I am confident an approach to having greener, cleaner and better infrastructure is being effectively embedded across the work of my Department.The new Transport Strategy along with a suite of Transport Plans, currently in preparation, will set a framework for making transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035. Active Travel and Placemaking will play a key role within these plans which aspire to better connect neighbouring communities to towns and city centres by facilitating healthy and sustainable travel choices and minimising the use of the private car.The Living With Water in Belfast Plan includes proposals for investment in blue/green infrastructure including Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) and Natural Flood Management (NFM). My Department will also be consulting on a draft Living With Water in Derry Plan. These Plans are primarily to help address flood risk, drainage and environmental issues and their geographical scope extends beyond the urban centres to include residential and outlying areas. Going forward consideration will also be given to nature-based solutions for improved water management in urban and other road drainage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a breakdown of the cost to the health and social care system of (i) gambling; (ii) alcohol; (iii) drug-related harms in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Currently, there are no specific gambling treatment services within Health and Social Care Trusts, although those presenting with dual diagnosis, such as existing substance use issues or mental ill health may receive treatment through those pathways. In line with the usual process of commissioning services, any decision to commission bespoke gambling treatment services in the future would need to be based on an assessment of the level of demand for such services, and in the context of future budget availability. In respect of drug related harms and alcohol, the specific figures requested are not available for each of the last 10 years as these issues span a range of services. However, in 2022/23 the PHA, SPPG and HSCTs (combined) spent circa £30m on substance use related services. However, treatment costs extend beyond specific commissioned services. A recent NIAO report estimated that 200 hospital beds per day are occupied by patients with substance use listed as a contributing factor, and estimates have shown that alcohol alone costs the health service in the region of £250m per annum. More generally, while the financial cost can never bring home the full impact that substance use related harm has on individuals, families and communities across Northern Ireland, the harms related to the use of alcohol and other drugs cost Northern Ireland’s economy hundreds of millions of pounds every year. The cost of alcohol misuse alone was estimated at up to £900 million in 2008/09, and if we were to add in the costs of the harms related to other drugs this would almost certainly take this figure to approximately £1.5 billion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the price differential of flying to destinations within the UK from a Belfast airport compared to Dublin Airport.
answer_text: The York Aviation Report “Air Connectivity to Support Inbound Tourism and Business Growth” commissioned by my Department, demonstrated that the lack of Air Passenger Duty and the ability to sell duty free products into Britain, enables airlines to offer cheaper air fares at Dublin Airport. This has a major impact on routes operating from the Belfast airports, including the range of direct flights offered and the frequency of services.Both domestic APD and VAT and excise are reserved matters, however I share the concerns of airport operators at the doubt economic impacts of the removal of duty-free and tax-free shopping at local airports for passengers from both Britian and the EU. My Department has also repeatedly made it clear that APD is an unfair tax that needs to be addressed by the British Government, given its particularly detrimental impact on our passengers, our physical remoteness to Britain, and the lack of an alternative means of travel such as road or rail options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current processing times for planning applications, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My department publishes quarterly and annual statistical bulletins presenting a summary of planning performance across Northern Ireland.  The most recent information relates to the first quarter of 2024-25 and can be found by following the attached link to the department’s website.  The information required is contained within Table 3.2.6 for major applications and Table 4.2.6 for local applications.Planning activity statistics | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the engagement he has had to include Armagh and Down in Ireland’s Ancient East destination marketing brand.
answer_text: Incorporating the three Fáilte Ireland experience brands into the North remains one of my key priorities to help deliver regionally balanced economic growth for the tourism sector. At present, Fáilte Ireland is undertaking a review of the Ireland’s Ancient East brand and hopes to better define the experience and how that is best communicated to the visitor.  I look forward to the outcome of this process and subsequent engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the provisions that exist for rural residents whose properties were not included in Project Stratum.
answer_text: Premises which were not included in Project Stratum, and which are unable to access a gigabit-capable broadband service, and there are no commercial plans, will be considered for Project Gigabit, a programme to enable hard-to-reach communities to access gigabit-capable broadband. My Department has commenced a procurement on 11 November 2024 to seek a contractor for Project Gigabit, and expects to award a contract by March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process for requesting a resident-only parking zone.
answer_text: Requests for Residents’ Parking schemes should be sent to the relevant Division for consideration.The Department’s Policy and Procedure Guidance RsPPG_E042 ‘Residents’ Parking’ sets out the approach when considering such schemes:Residents parking RSPPG E042 | Department for InfrastructureYou will also be interested to know that the report on the review of Residents’ Parking schemes has been shared with the Infrastructure Committee and will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will consider a statutory requirement for schools to educate pupils on the dangers of gambling as part of the core curriculum.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 17640/22-27.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 22 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average length of time from when a patient is told that they are being discharged from hospital until they are discharged; and (ii) the actions he is taking to improve the speed at which pharmacy dispenses medications in hospitals to allow patients to leave the hospital.
answer_text: This information is not available as all HSC Trusts have confirmed that they do not routinely collect the data requested. The following responses have been provided by HSC Trusts as the Department of Health does not hold the relevant information.   Trusts have commented, however, that although it may appear at times that discharges are delayed due to pharmacy there are many other reasons for patient delays in discharge other than waiting for medications.Northern TrustThe Northern Trust has optimised the speed at which pharmacy dispenses medications in hospitals to allow patients to leave the hospital by:Installing a dispensing robot in Antrim Area Hospital pharmacy department – this has reduced dispensing time in Antrim pharmacy. An application will be made for capital funding for a dispensing robot for Causeway PharmacyDispensing the majority of inpatient discharge prescriptions, Monday to Friday, at ward level using the medicines that have been placed in patients’ bedside lockers. This reduces the dispensing time for discharge prescriptions and reduces waste of medicines.Ensuring clinical pharmacy technicians are trained as Accredited Checking Pharmacy Technicians to undertake the final check of discharge prescriptions at ward levelOpening seven days per week to facilitate the dispensing of discharge prescriptions in Antrim. A business case for additional funding is required to extend the pharmacy opening hours in Causeway hospital to include SundaysMonitoring the time taken to dispense discharge prescriptions and regularly review dispensing efficiencyBelfast TrustThe clinical pharmacy teams provide ward based services which optimise the safe and timely discharge of patients from our hospitals.  Pharmacy technicians are also embedded within ward teams and work in ‘near patient pharmacy discharge teams’ to dispense discharge medication at ward level.  The pharmacy technicians operate as a team across multiple wards to identify patients prioritised for discharge.  The pharmacy teams also identify medicine related delays in the transfer of care (e.g. any need for increased support required to manage medicines at home) and implement solutions to prevent a delayed discharge. Other measures that have been taken include;Access to seven-day adult and paediatric clinical pharmacy service in high admission/discharge areas to prevent delays at weekends and bank holidays. A clinical extended hours model (for one specialty) which is available until 9.00pm to facilitate timely discharges in the evening. The pharmacy dispensary teams deliver a Belfast HSC Trust extended hours service to support discharges until 9.00pm on weeknights and at weekends until 5.00pmBelfast HSC Trust implemented Encompass on the 6th June 2024 which included an update to the hospital pharmacy dispensing system.  Western TrustThe Western Trust has implemented a number of improvements to help ensure that medicines are dispensed in in a timely manner for discharge. These include:Pharmac

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the financial support provided to each dental practice (i) during; and (ii) since the outbreak of Covid-19.
answer_text: The majority of General Dental Services (GDS) payments are made to General Dental Practitioners (GDPs), not practices, and the Department does not hold a record of individual payments made.The total values of General Dental Services Covid-19 payments are included in the annual General Dental Services Statistics and can be seen in the table below. Financial YearCovid 19 Payments2020-21£51,900,0002021-22£43,700,0002022-23£13,000,0002023-24£2,300,000These payments include the following:Financial Support Scheme (FSS), which provided an additional payment to eligible GDPs each month based on the average gross (including patient contributions) monthly Item of Service (IoS) payments received by a GDP in 2019-20. FSS ran from April 2020 to March 2022, Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which provided eligible practitioners a flat 25% enhancement to their IoS claims each month. RSS ran from April 2022 to July 2023, Personal Protective Equipment (PPE); the Department has provided funding for enhanced PPE since March 2020.Revenue Grant Schemes made funding available to practices to support investment in new technologies such as improved ventilation. The schemes ran in 2020/21 and 2021/22.Fit testingUrgent Dental Care CentresDuring the pandemic reduced capacity within GDS meant that many patients could not obtain a routine appointment with their dentist. To prevent large numbers of patient registrations lapsing as a result, dental registrations due to end during 2020/21 and 2021/22 were extended. This enabled GDPs to continue to receive capitation and continuing care payments for these patients without any treatment being provided.Since the end of the pandemic, practices have continued to receive support to maintain stability. In 2022 a permanent 25% enhancement was put in place on fees relating to provision of dentures. In March 2024, a £9.2m investment package for GDS was also announced, to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme.£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for fillings, extractions and root canals for 2024/25. £3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current number of substantive consultants at Daisy Hill Hospital; (ii) the number of substantive consultants that have been recruited to Daisy Hill Hospital since 1 January 2022; and (iii) the number of substantive consultants that have resigned from Daisy Hill Hospital since 1 January 2022.
answer_text: The Southern Trust has provided information relating to the permanent consultants only in Daisy Hill Hospital.  Postholders are expected to cover across sites and figures do not include staff based in Craigavon Area Hospital who also work at Daisy Hill Hospital.  Information provided is for their main base location as stated on HRPTS at time of reporting.  Those staff who are on career breaks have not been included. The figures provided by the Southern Trust are the information held on the HRPTS system up to September 2024 as per the reporting capabilities of HRPTS.  This does not include posts where a candidate has accepted an offer but has not yet started in the Trust due to pre-employment checks, notice periods etc.  Leavers are defined as those on HRPTS with a Reason for Leaving as “Resignation”.  Numbers do not include resignations from those consultants who are undergoing their period of notice and have not yet left the employment of the Trust.the current number of substantive consultants at Daisy Hill Hospital; There are 55 permanent staff in post as at 30th September 2024. the number of substantive consultants that have been recruited to Daisy Hill Hospital since 1 January 2022; From 1st January 2022 to 30th September 2024 there have been 9 staff recruited.  the number of substantive consultants that have resigned from Daisy Hill Hospital since 1 January 2022.10 staff have resigned between 1st January 2022 to 30th September 2024 As at 30th Sept 2024 January 2022 – September 2024Permanent Staff in PostNew StartsResignationHeadcountHeadcountHeadcount55910

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total overtime payments made to consultants; (i) the total amount paid to nursing agencies; and (iii) the total amount paid to agencies for (a) administrative staff; and (b) management, in each of the last five years broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Consultants do not get overtime payments.Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ agency staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years there have been concerns of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognition that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department therefore has prioritised significant work to alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes urgently addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure. Work in this area has shown a reduction of approximately £30m for nursing and midwifery staffing alone from 2022/23 to 2023/24. The total amount paid to nursing agencies over the last 5 years by Health and Social Care Trust is shown in Table 1 below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what engagement (i) his Department; and (ii) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust have had with the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency with respect to increasing the number of training posts and programmes at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern HSC Trust is a member of the strategic group of HSC employers and NIMDTA, led by officials in my Department, seeking to enhance the quality of medical education provided for resident doctors in speciality training programmes. This group considered issues relating to the distribution of training posts at appropriate locations across the HSC, including at Daisy Hill Hospital, at its meetings in September 2023; May 2024 and October 2024. There is currently ongoing engagement with all HSC Trusts, regarding their appetite for funding possible expansion of the Internal Medicine Training Programme run by NIMDTA across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what services provided by the Education Authority, outside of traditional school settings, exist at post-16 level for individuals with a Statement of Special Educational Needs in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that their Transitions 14+ service liaises with the following bodies and organisations which make provision for individuals beyond the age of 16 and leaving statutory education services, who are diagnosed with special educational needs (SEN) with a moderate learning difficulty (MLD).Further Education CollegesSERC South Eastern Regional CollegeSRC Southern Regional CollegeEmployment and Skills ProgrammesClanryeRutledge (Armagh)People FirstVoluntary Organisations Barnardo’sMencapCedar FoundationYouth ActionThe Gathering Rooms (Armagh) / The Appleby Trust Day OpportunitiesSocial FarmingSouthern Health and Social Care Trust- Day Care and Day Opportunity Options The 14+ Transitions Service also works closely with the Northern Ireland Careers Service, the health and social care trusts and other statutory agencies, to support pathway planning for each young person leaving education at 16.On occasion, an application is made to Key Stage 4 Education Other Than At School Service (EOTAS) for a young person to remain in EOTAS education for an additional year under the Over Age Retention processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the locations where his Department is considering applications for new schools.
answer_text: Due to the many existing and emerging pressures on an already constrained Capital Budget for Education, it is not my intention to make a fresh call for new major capital projects anytime in the near future.  I will of course continue to make the case for significantly increased and sustained investment in our schools’ estate, but until I receive additional funding, difficult decisions will continue to have to be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of the negative impact on Universal Credit entitlement for student nurses in receipt of bursaries; and (ii) for his assessment of whether this is a disincentive for those seeking to pursue a nursing pathway.
answer_text: (i)	I am aware that, according to individual circumstances, there may be an impact on Universal Credit entitlement of the bursary and other financial support provided by my Department to student nurses; (ii)	I am currently exploring with my Departmental officials possible future changes to the financial support provided to healthcare students on programmes for which my Department is responsible.  The impact on Universal Credit entitlement will be included in these considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he is aware of the negative impact on Universal Credit entitlement for student nurses in receipt of bursaries; and (ii) for his assessment of whether this is a disincentive for those seeking to pursue a nursing pathway.
answer_text: Student nurses who are undertaking a full-time undergraduate course are not usually entitled to Universal Credit. Exceptions may apply to student nurses who have additional needs.  Where a student nurse is eligible for Universal Credit, the first £110 per month of their bursary is disregarded and the remainder is deducted from their Universal credit payment.   Further detail is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/claiming-universal-credit-if-youre-student. More information is available at the Department’s Jobs and Benefits offices.To help raise student’s awareness of Universal Credit and increase understanding of the impact of different forms of student finance, Universal Credit staff engage with representatives from Queens University Belfast and the University of Ulster on an ongoing basis.  My Department has not made an assessment as to whether the rules around unearned income in Universal Credit disincentivise those seeking to pursue a nursing pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans to upgrade street lighting infrastructure to LED lights in the Newry and Armagh constituency in the next five years, broken down by location.
answer_text: My Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis – instead it is collated on a Council area basis.  The Newry and Armagh Constituency forms part of Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area and part of the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council area.Details of the planned programme for the 2024/25 financial year in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area, are included in my Department’s Annual Report for Newry, Mourne and Down District Council which was presented to the Council on the 23 September. This report can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-southern-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-24-25.pdf  The 2024/25 Report to Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council is scheduled to be presented to Council on 28 October 2024. (if the report is presented today, this line needs to be amended and the link to the report provided).Following the presentation to Council, the Report will be published on the Department’s website, issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective council area and placed in the Assembly library.As my Department operates on an annual fixed programme of work, I am unable to provide a five-year projection for planned street lighting upgrades. The long-term schedule depends on multiple variables, such as resource availability and funding levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to Daisy Hill Hospital Maternity Unit, (i) when the Southern Health and Social Care Trust transitioned from a model of staff funding on the basis of operational need to funded establishment; and (ii) for his assessment of the implications of this new model on (a) staff retention levels; and (b) patient safety.
answer_text: (i) I have been advised by my officials that the operational and funded establishment levels for staffing have always corresponded albeit the distribution of the funding has been in different locations within maternity setting in Daisy Hill Hospital.  This has been realigned to the clinical areas to ensure the funding is representative of the operational demand required since 2023/2024.  (ii) The overall staffing requirements and funded staffing levels are now streamlined for all clinical areas for their required demand.  The overall funding/operational requirements for the Daisy Hill Hospital site have no impact on staff retention levels or patient safety as the funded staffing level is above the level identified as required to maintain patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) an approval has been sought from his Department; and (ii) information has been provided to his Department, by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in relation to a temporary closure of the Maternity Unit at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern Heath and  Social Care Trust have contacted my Department in relation to ongoing workforce challenges across Obstetric and Gynaecology services, particularly at the Daisy Hill site. My officials are currently in daily contact with Southern Trust Senior Management to develop a comprehensive report which will outline an assessment of the situation; details of the Trust efforts to maintain the current service to date, options under consideration and an appraisal of each option, any external support which would assist the Trust to maintain services at Daisy Hill and the risks associated with this.I want to assure you that my focus is on maintaining accessible and safe in-patient maternity care at Daisy Hill Hospital and my officials are working with the Trust to endeavour to do this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the workforce management planning that has been undertaken by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust to address staffing deficits at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern HSC Trust has advised the following:General SurgeryThere are currently two vacancies within general surgery at consultant level, which will reduce to one vacancy in February 2025 upon successful commencement of a new appointee.  General surgery recruitment is trust-wide, not hospital specific, to ensure there is sufficient rota cover across sites. AnaestheticsThere are currently two vacancies within anaesthetics at consultant level, which will reduce to one vacancy following successful appointment of a candidate following a recent recruitment process.  The recruitment of anaesthetics is also trust-wide, not hospital specific.  The trust intends to re-advertise and explore recruitment opportunities in respect of the remaining vacancy.  There are also two vacancies at specialty doctor level, currently covered by locum recruitment.Radiology Services There are two vacancies at consultant level which are to be advertised in the next week.  Radiology works on a cross-site basis.  Maternity Services There are currently no vacant posts.Emergency MedicineThe trust has advertised two consultant posts in emergency medicine.  Interviews will take place within the next fortnight and it hopes it will be possible to fill these appointments.  The trust has appointed three specialty doctors and clinical fellows to support the department.  There are four nursing vacancies within the department which will be filled with newly qualified nurses.  All staff should be in post within the next 4 – 6 weeks. General Medicine There are five consultant vacancies.  Recruitment will commence imminently with interviews anticipated to take place in December 2024. There are four vacancies at specialty doctor level.  These posts will be advertised in early December 2024. An advertisement is currently in place for clinical sister vacancies (2.6 WTE).  Nurse vacancies across in-patient wards (1.4 WTE) will be filled by newly qualified nurses.A rolling recruitment process in place to fill healthcare assistant vacancies, currently 5.2 WTE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) plans to take to increase maternity staffing from funded establishment to operational need at the Maternity Unit at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern Heath and  Social Care Trust has identified that the operational staffing requirement for band 5, 6 & 7 midwives equate to the funded establishment when the 1.86 WTE commences in January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has undertaken a risk assessment to measure the potential impact on mothers and babies in the Newry, South Armagh and Mourne area if maternity services are removed from Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust are undertaking risk assessments to enable risk stratification to stabilise the maternity services to ensure safety of mothers and babies in the Newry, South Armagh and Mourne Area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail capital investment plans under consideration for (i) Craigavon Area Hospital; and (ii) Daisy Hill Hospital in the next five years, broken down by project.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have submitted proposals to increase the bed capacity at Craigavon Area Hospital, to commence Phase 1 of the Redevelopment of Craigavon Hospital and to provide additional dementia beds at Bluestone Mental Health Unit.  At Daisy Hill Hospital the Trust plan to install an MRI refurbishment solution in 2025/26 in advance of a twin MRI and CT diagnostic suite due to commence once the current low voltage works have completed.  The Trust will be invited to submit business cases for these proposals once budget affordability is confirmed.Low voltage works are continuing at Craigavon Area Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital. In addition, Encompass will go live in the Trust in 2025/26.Annually, the Department issues the Trust with a general capital allocation for capital works, including estate maintenance, medical equipment, ICT and transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the properties (i) owned; and (ii) managed by his Department in the Newry and Armagh constituency which are (a) vacant; and (b) listed for disposal.
answer_text: The Department neither (i) owns or (ii) manages any property in the Newry and Armagh constituency which are (a) vacant; and (b) listed for disposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the properties (i) owned; and (ii) managed by her Department in the Newry and Armagh constituency which are (a) vacant; and (b) listed for disposal.
answer_text: Within the Newry and Armagh constituency area, my Department owns three properties: • Glenree House (Newry) • Phoenix House, Jobs and Benefits Office (Newry); and• Armagh Jobs and Benefits Office.In addition, my Department leases the A-Tek Building in Armagh City. At present, none of the properties are vacant or listed for disposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the investment needed in wastewater infrastructure by NI Water in the Newry and Armagh constituency over the next ten years, broken down by location.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water however officials have sought the information as requested.NI Water has advised that it records information on investments by Council area not Parliamentary Constituency. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recruitment of a permanent managing GP at Maphoner Surgery, Mullaghbawn.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have been holding the contract temporarily for the Mullaghbawn Surgery since 01 July 2023.  This is currently extended until 31 March 2025.   SPPG had previously widely advertised the contract, however no applicants were received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of the Newry Primary Care Centre.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the business case for the Newry development for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact of the delay in processing Ill Health Retirement pensions on medically-retired police officers.
answer_text: Where an officer has been medically retired, he will already be in receipt of an ill-health retirement pension.  It is the Northern Ireland Policing Board in its role as Police Pension Authority, who determines whether an officer may retire on Ill-health grounds. I am committed to respecting the statutory role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board and for this reason it would be inappropriate for me to make such an assessment.You may wish to direct your question to the Northern Ireland Policing Board for a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the lane running parallel to Church Street and connecting Meeting Street and Church Street, Poyntzpass, will be repaired.
answer_text: This area of Poyntzpass is currently subject to a planning application for the provision of a new car park and on street bays.  Construction work to deliver this new infrastructure is likely to result in further deterioration of the road network due to heavy construction traffic and other activities associated with its development.  Officials in my department have advised that once this work has been completed, the road will be assessed for inclusion in a future resurfacing programme.In the meantime, actionable defects will be repaired in line with the limited-service policy, subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to support the growing (i) young population; and (ii) elderly population in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area to ensure that services are correctly funded in line with population need.
answer_text: The Southern Health & Social Care Trust (SHSCT) provides hospital and community health and social care services to a population of 391,796 adults and children. The population indicates inequalities in health and life expectancy across localities, with specific areas of deprivation and rurality noted. The Trust’s aim is to deliver, safe, high quality care that is co-produced and co-designed in partnership with service users and staff who deliver their services. The SHSCT is committed to its values in all interactions with others including colleagues, patients, carers, and service users.As you are aware my department works very closely with The Public Health Agency (PHA), who have a key role in health protection and health and social wellbeing improvement.  The PHA currently support and commissions a number of services both locally across the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) area and regionally to help improve and protect our growing young and older people’s population health and social wellbeing. All services and activities are commissioned in line with best practice to plan, purchase and monitor investments, taking into consideration both the needs identified through local engagement, assessment and research and evidence. Public Health Planning TeamsThe Agency has recently established Starting Well and Ageing Well Public Health Planning Teams. These multi–disciplinary strategic planning teams will lead on PHA planning and take account of local need including population growth and change with the overall aim to improve the health and wellbeing of children, young people and older people in Northern Ireland.PHA Commissioned Services – Starting WellThe Agency are committed to both innovation in, and implementation of, work that helps infants, children and young people secure the best start in life. Within the SHSCT, the Agency commissions programmes relating to the following themes/areas:Evidence-Based Parenting Programmes Early Intervention Focussed Family Support including the Southern Early Intervention Support Service supports 160+ families per yearSchools initiatives such as Roots of Empathy, a preventative mental health & wellbeing programme delivered to primary school childrenLooked After ChildrenBreastfeeding including Peer Support programmes and lead roles been developed in Maternity, Health Visiting and Neonatal settingsEarly Years Obesity PreventionPHA Commissioned Services - Ageing WellThe following areas of work are priorities for the PHA to help older adults to age well.Creating Age Friendly Communities - Southern Area investment £108K across x 2 Age Friendly Officer Posts Preventing Accidents at home – Southern Area investment £85K across (Armagh Banbridge Craigavon, Newry Mourne and Down)Falls Prevention Programme - Southern Area investment £30kArts and Health / Small Grants Programme - Current investment which covers all 11 council areas in NI (regional) £93k Artscare / 89k Arts Council Linking Generations - Inte

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to reconfiguring inpatient maternity, delivery, and gynaecological services at Daisy Hill Hospital, to detail (i) the representations his Department has received from (a) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Board; and (b) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Chief Executive; and (ii) whether he has approved those recommendations.
answer_text: My Department has recently been contacted by both the Chair of and the Chief Executive of Southern Health and Social Care Trust to raise concerns in relation to Obstetric and Gynaecology services at Daisy Hill Hospital. My officials are having ongoing discussions with the Trust to discuss options for stabilising the Obstetric and Gynaecology services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the minimum midwifery whole time equivalent (WTE) staffing complement that has been identified by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust as necessary to provide a safe maternity service at Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) the total WTE midwifery staffing complement that has been funded by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) The Southern Health and Social Care Trust identified that the minimum whole time equivalent (WTE) band 5, 6 & 7 midwifery staffing complement required to provide a safe maternity service at Daisy Hill Hospital is as follows: -2020/21	53.96 WTE2021/22        54.32 WTE2022/23	53.88 WTE2023/24	56.88 WTE2024/25	56.88 WTE (ii) The total WTE midwifery staffing complement that has been funded by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years is as follows:2020/21	54.27 WTE2021/22	54.27 WTE2022/23	54.27 WTE2023/24	58.96 WTE2024/25	58.96 WTE

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on international recruitment for nursing; (ii) to detail the specialities that recruitment is targeting, broken down by (a) general; and (b) specialist areas.
answer_text: International recruitment is a key avenue in addressing our workforce resource issues and ensuring that the health & social care service can deliver the safe and effective service for patients. The international recruits who arrive to work here across our HSC system provide an immensely valuable contribution to the delivery of health and social care services, let alone to our communities. Northern Ireland has a proud reputation of providing excellent pastoral care for newly arrived international recruits, underpinned by adherence to ethical international recruitment principles. The Department is committed to upholding the highest ethical standards in international recruitment.The Department published the new updated Code of Practice for International Recruitment in April 2023 which aims to protect the rights of international recruits across health and social care, including those employed within the independent sector. Since 2016, 1,858 internationally educated nurses have arrived to work across the 5 HSC Trusts via the regional international nurse recruitment programme. Of those arrivals 1,807 have been for adult nursing roles and 51 for mental health roles. All recruits to date have been successful in passing their Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE), the common method of assessing clinical competence in medical education, to achieve NMC registration. The focus for 24/25 has been solely for mental health nurse recruitment with a further 51 mental health nurses scheduled to arrive to work across HSC by the end of April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (i) is taking; and (ii) plans to take to increase staffing levels at the Daisy Hill Hospital maternity department.
answer_text: The Southern HSC Trust have advised the following:When staffing and acuity has permitted, midwives have been temporarily redeployed from the Craigavon Area Hospital site to maintain safe service for the whole of the Southern HSC Trust.  Non-clinical Band 7s and specialist midwives have been redeployed from those roles to assist with clinical duties.  The HSC Trust’s aim is to maintain continuity of midwifery care.  Medical recruitment is being progressed for specialty doctors and consultants with locum appointments being pursued until permanent recruitment can be secured.  Additionally, the HSC Trust has reached out regionally across HSCNI seeking any available support both in terms of midwifery and medical staffing. The HSC Trust are readvertising to create waiting lists for midwifery posts and scoping their ability to source midwives from midwifery agencies.  Shift analysis will be carried out and enhanced payment may need to become available for midwifery staff.  The HSC Trust will further scope the availability of medical and midwifery staff through nurse/locum bank and agencies.The Chair and Chief Executive of the Southern HSC Trust recently contacted my Department to raise concerns relating to Obstetrics and Gynaecology services at Daisy Hill Hospital.  My officials are engaging with the HSC Trust to support the stabilisation of these services.My Department has increased commissioned training places for midwives over the last number of years, from 60 per annum in 2014/15 to 120 per annum in 2024/25.  Graduates from these programmes are adding to the available midwifery workforce across the HSC, including within the Southern HSC Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for his assessment of the impact that the north’s exclusion from the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Claims Management Activity) Order 2018 is having upon insurance customers; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to extend the Order’s protections so that consumers can have the same protections as those in other regions in the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: I met with insurance bodies on 3rd September 2024 to express my concerns that increases in insurance costs are adding further financial pressure to households, businesses and communities here and to challenge them on what the sector is doing to contain costs. The industry bodies indicated that the increases are being driven by a number of different factors - some of which are being experienced globally due to inflationary pressures in the industry, and suggested others appear to be more specific to the market here, such as the non regulation of Claims Management Companies here.Given insurance is a reserved matter, my officials are engaging with British Treasury and Financial Conduct Authority to establish the evidence on the impact of the North’s exclusion from the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 on insurance customers here. They are also liaising with Consumerline, the Trading Standards consumer advice helpline, which refers consumer complaints about Claims Management Companies to the Financial Ombudsman Service to understand the scale of those.Once the evidence on this is more fully established, I will then be in a better position to consider whether there is merit in seeking to have this legislation extended to the North, or indeed what other action might be possible in relation to the insurance market locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) positions advertised; and (ii) appointments made for (a) newly-qualified doctors; (b) consultants; (c) surgeons; (d) midwives; and (e) anesthetists at Daisy Hill Hospital in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timeline for the delivery of the Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: It is my intention to have a draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy ready for Executive consideration and public consultation in 2025.   The development of the strategy will be informed by evaluation of the measures being implemented in 2024/25 and new data emerging, including from a NISRA parental survey I have commissioned, the results of which will be available by April 2025.   Further stakeholder engagement is also planned on key themes such as workforce planning and accessibility of provision.Given the level of investment likely to be required and sector workforce capacity constraints, it will take a number of years to deliver on all the commitments in the Strategy, once agreed.  The scale and pace of implementation will largely depend on the budget made available by the Executive.
