question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any recent works his Department has carried out on the Cregganbane Road, Crossmaglen.
answer_text: Resurfacing works were carried out on Cregganbane Road during the 2019-2020 financial year, with further works, including surface dressing on connecting roads that year.  There are no plans to undertake any additional works at this time beyond the repair of actionable defects in accordance with the Departments limited service policy.Details of the planned works programmes for the 2024/25 financial year are provided in the Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council 2024/25 which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-southern-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-24-25.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to launch a public inquiry into cervical screening performance.
answer_text: The Cervical Cytology Review in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) is due to conclude shortly after which a factual report will be produced by the Public Health Agency and the SHSCT before a detailed analysis of all findings will be undertaken. This analysis will be supported by appropriate independent experts. It is important that we await the outcome of these processes as they will help determine our next steps.  As Health Minister I remain committed to finding the most appropriate method to understand what happened and to learn lessons to ensure that our screening programmes deliver on behalf of our population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact the delay in the PSNI ill-health retirement pension process is having on officers injured on duty.
answer_text: The impact of delay in the ill-health retirement process is an operational matter for the Chief Constable. As I remain committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable it would not be appropriate for me to make such an assessment.I suggest that you may wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable for a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of why Recommendation 5 of the Medical Student Places Review 2018 has not been met; and (ii) when a follow-up review will be conducted.
answer_text: (i) A lack of available funding has meant that my Department has been unable to progress Recommendation 5 of the 2018 Medical Student Places Review.(ii) Officials in my Department hope to be able to commence an update of the Medical Student Places Review before the end of this calendar year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his Department's guidance to (i) long-term applicants on the housing waiting list; and (ii) homeless people on the housing waiting list.
answer_text: The Department does not issue guidance to applicants on the Waiting List. Any advice or guidance given to social housing applicants is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“For clarity it may be useful to point out that the Housing Selection Scheme does not contain any reference or definition in terms of a “long term housing applicant.The Housing Executive operates a Housing Solutions approach with prospective applicants.  When a customer approaches the Housing Executive for housing assistance the full range of possible housing options are discussed to identify possible solutions.  To help inform this a housing prospects discussion also takes place.  This discussion will examine areas where the customer has indicated they may wish to be rehoused in.  The prospects discussion will cover social housing supply, demand and turnover in these areas including the indicative points level at which allocations are made.  Where a move to social housing is agreed or identified as the best or the customer’s preferred option then they will be placed on the Waiting List for their nominated Areas Of Choice.  Customers are allowed to select as many areas as they wish in terms of rehousing and are advised that the selection of a greater number of Areas Of Choice will improve their chances of rehousing.A Housing Options and prospects discussion is available to all customers at any point in their housing journey and are often discussed as part of the case review process.In practice there is no difference between a housing and a homeless case in terms of the social housing waiting lists and advice given around permanent rehousing, however the homeless applicant will have a right to temporary accommodation until a solution is found.Any customer whose circumstances change should contact the Housing Executive to discuss this.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14845/22-27, and in relation to the removal of the billboard adjacent to the Egyptian Arch, (i) to whom the request to remove the billboard was issued; (ii) when the request was issued; (iii) how the request was issued; and (iv) by whom the request was made.
answer_text: On 18th August 2023, Sinn Féin were asked via email to remove the billboard adjacent to the Egyptian Arch. This e-mail was issued by an official working within the Newry, Mourne and Down West Section Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether the budget allocated by NI Water in the PC21 period is sufficient to meet the current and future demand on the water and wastewater system in Newry.

To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for a breakdown of the investment mix of public sector pension fund investments; and (ii) whether the public sector pension fund holds any investments in Israeli Government bonds.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 16112/22-27 which contains relevant information for the Civil Service pension schemes where my Department has direct responsibility.It can be noted that the main unfunded defined benefit schemes for other public service workers also operate on a pay-as-you-go scheme design. In this scheme design no investment fund is built up or held. The Local Government Pension Scheme is the only main public service defined benefit scheme which is of a funded scheme design. The sponsor Department is the Department for Communities. My Department has no control over its pension fund investments. Money invested in any additional defined contribution pension options provided by other scheme responsible Departments to their employees is also not under the control of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the impact of the funding withdrawal by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on Newry and District Gateway Club members; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to ensure that the funding is reinstated immediately.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have been in contact with the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.Department of Health Officials were advised that the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has clarified directly with the Newry and District Gateway Club that there will be no change in funding arrangements and it is the Trust’s understanding therefore that extant service provision will continue.  The Trust will continue to engage with the Newry and District Gateway Club to fully understand the valuable service they provide and to ensure all governance and contractual arrangements are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for a breakdown of the investment mix of Health and Social Care Pension Scheme fund investments; and (ii) whether the Health and Social Care Pension Scheme fund holds any investments in Israeli Government bonds.
answer_text: The HSC Pension Scheme is an unfunded, defined benefit Public Service Pension Scheme, which operates on a pay-as-you-go basis.  This means that no investment fund is built up or held. Pension benefits, which are based on earnings and how long a member has been in the Scheme, are paid from AME.  This is funded by payroll contributions from employees and employers, plus additional funding from the Exchequer, as required. The HSC Pension Scheme does not therefore hold funds or investments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients discharged from hospitals in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust with assistance from the Red Cross in each year since 2021; (ii) the rationale to cease funding for assisted discharge services in November 2024; and (iii) any alternate provisions that will be in place following the cessation of this service.
answer_text: The SHSCT has provided a supported discharge scheme to patients through the assistance of the British Red Cross (BRC) for the following number of patients: YearNumber of Patients202166020227822023764Jan – Oct 2024784This additional service was established on a temporary basis. The Trust is required to operate within their current allocated funding budget avoiding over expenditure and is not in a position to sustain this service.  On a daily basis the majority of patients return to their homes by other forms of transport other than those provided by the BRC. In the absence of this supported discharge transport scheme, the hospital staff will continue to work with patients, their families and carers to identify how all of the patients will be returned to their homes safely.  To ensure all patients are discharged home in a timely basis the hospital staff will make all efforts to ensure that they provide as much notice as possible to the patient and their families of the agreed discharge date and times.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any provisions in the Strategy for Victims and Survivors 2024-2034 to improve access to justice for victims and survivors of the Troubles.
answer_text: We remain committed to ensuring that the needs of victims and survivors continue to be met and we are delighted to have agreed the new Victims and Survivors Strategy which was developed and co-designed with a range of stakeholders including funded victims’ and survivors’ groups and individual victims and survivors.A key part of the Strategy is providing support for victims and survivors, and their families, as they move forward from experiences of the past. The Strategy recognises the importance of truth and justice and the need to work towards greater societal recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past, as well as the widespread opposition to the Legacy Act.We have made a number of commitments to supporting victims and survivors including continued advocacy support which is delivered by community organisations funded by the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS) and is part of the current PEACE PLUS programme. We will also continue to engage with the Irish and UK Governments to ensure they are aware of the needs of victims and survivors, and the potential impact on individuals and communities of failing to meet those needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 15549/22-27, to detail (i) the number of occasions, and between which sites, there has been a maternity service divert in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last ten years; and (iii) the steps he is taking to prevent future diverts.
answer_text: (i) The information requested is not readily available and could only be compiled at a disproportionate cost up until this year.  From January 2024 there have been 44 diverts between Daisy Hill Hospital and Craigavon Area Hospital.  33 from Daisy Hill Hospital to Craigavon Area Hospital and 11 from Craigavon Area Hospital to Daisy Hill Hospital.(ii) Southern Health and Social Care Trust is working to mitigate the risk of further diverts, however this remains dependent on staffing, acuity and clinical need at a point in time as is the nature of maternity services.  Measures taken to mitigate these risks include reviewing workload and planned activity, risk stratification, regional request for mutual aid.  Shift analysis will be carried out and enhanced payment may need to become available for Midwifery staff.  Further scoping availability of staff both medical and midwifery through nurse/locum bank and agencies.The Trust remain committed to ensuring the provision of safe, high quality maternity services for women across the Southern Trust, continually reviewing and taking account of national and regional policy guidelines.The Trust appreciate the ongoing support of families and the public as they work through these challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of pharmacies providing free prescription deliveries for patients; (ii) for his assessment of why an increasing number of pharmacies have decided to withdraw free prescription deliveries; (iii) whether his Department has undertaken an assessment of the impact upon vulnerable and isolated people of the withdrawal of free prescription deliveries; and (iv) for his assessment of the merits of including medicine delivery services as a commissioned service in the next commissioning plan.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan (2022-25). The Commissioning Plan was developed in partnership between Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) and Departmental officials. CPNI is the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland.Prescription delivery is not a service that is commissioned by SPPG; therefore, the Department does not hold any information in relation to the number of contractors providing this service. Provision or withdrawal of this service is a commercial matter for individual contractors, however, not all community pharmacies in Northern Ireland provide a prescription delivery service.My Department will be engaging with Community Pharmacy NI over the coming months to develop future commissioning arrangements for pharmacy services. These new commissioning arrangements will progress the ambitions set out by my Department’s strategic plan for community pharmacy which outlines a vision for delivery of more clinical services that utilise the skills of pharmacy teams and offer safe, convenient and faster access to care. Community Pharmacy NI contributed to the development of this strategic plan and my officials will continue to work with them to progress development of a new commissioning plan going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 15638/22-27, to detail the guidance his Department can provide to parents of children who previously attended Early Years and who have not yet received an official diagnosis, therefore have not been offered alternative provisions from the Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have worked to offer support to all children affected by the closure of the Early Years – the Organisation for Children’s contracted service in Newry. For those children not yet in receipt of an official diagnosis they will have been offered additional health visiting services and were appropriately sign posted to other support services in the locality such as Sure Start, Home Start and the Family Support Hubs. The children and their families will also be able to access early intervention support from the SHSCT while they await autism or ADHD assessment. The Southern Area Outcomes guidance for parents “Our Guide to Disability” is given to parents to support their journey in receiving support. Families are also given Education Authority guidance for youth service provision for young children with additional needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has had any discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding introducing a developer charge to fund wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: I have had recent discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the funding requirements for NI Water. During a recent meeting in September in relation to the in-year budget position, the issue of the capital funding position of NI Water was discussed. I have also had a further meeting with the Minister for Infrastructure to discuss specifically the question of developer contributions as a means of funding water and wastewater infrastructure. At that meeting, the Infrastructure Minister outlined that legislative change would be required before NI Water could introduce developer contributions in relation to water and wastewater infrastructure and that he is currently considering how best to proceed. I recognise that a lack of water and wastewater capacity is acting as a constraint on development in many locations, particularly in relation to social housing, and this does need to be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women that have received a cervical cancer diagnosis following a misread cytology slide in the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Once a case of cervical cancer is diagnosed, the lead HSC Trust will review all elements of a woman’s screening history, where one exists, in line with the regional ‘Framework for the audit of invasive cervical cancers and disclosures of findings (2019).’ The audit is undertaken for learning and improvement purposes. This is normal practice and happens on an ongoing basis. The audit process covers the whole screening pathway, including previous screening tests. The table below provides information on the number of ‘unsatisfactory’ audit outcomes (category 3) reported for 2019-2024.  An unsatisfactory or category 3 audit outcome is defined as “False negative cases, or significant process, or management shortcomings, that constitute a patient safety incident.”Number of category 3 findings by Trust and year 2019-November 2024BHSCTWHSCTSHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTTOTAL<515*13<5<5Source: PHA*12 of these cases (with a category 3 outcomes and notified as SAIs) occurred as a result of the WHSCT applying a different threshold than others when categorising cervical cancer audits with an ‘unsatisfactory’ outcomeAn unsatisfactory outcome does not imply that a cancer was missed or misread. It reflects that a process, or interpretation issues (during any stage of their screening history or pathway) may have impacted on how the patient was managed. As outlined in my Written Ministerial Statement, of 25 July 2024, HSC Trusts are prioritising all outstanding audits of invasive cervical cancer and disclosure. Prior to 2019 there was no standard approach to categorising outcomes of audits of invasive cervical cancer. It should be noted that for most of this period screening was provided using cervical cytology as the primary test.  Since December 2023, HPV (human papilloma virus) testing has now replaced cytology as the primary screening test.    Introducing primary HPV testing into the cervical screening pathway was recommended by the UK National Screening Committee because it is a more accurate test, and will save more lives, than using cervical cytology as the primary test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action his Department will take to address flood risk factors in Newry and South Armagh in a manner that will ease the burden of insurance premiums for households and businesses.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing a study to assess the viability of further flood alleviation measures to reduce flood risk in Newry. The project team are also currently engaging with Newry, Mourne and Down Council with a view to potentially accelerating a section of proposed flood alleviation works through the Council’s Theatre and Conference Facility project. My Department is also continuing to work closely with the Newry Business community to establish a Regional Community Resilience Group for them, and others, to improve preparedness for any future flooding. To alleviate the impacts of flooding from rivers, my Department will also continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure and an extensive programme of culvert desilting works is also scheduled to be delivered this financial year.Any homeowner who experiences difficulty getting home insurance following a flood event may be able to avail of Flood Re. Flood Re is a joint initiative between Government and insurers and aims to make the flood cover element of household insurance policies more generally available and affordable. The Flood Re scheme does not include businesses, which require commercial insurance which is often bespoke and based on the individual nature of the business. Further information on Flood Re is available on the Flood RE website (www.floodre.co.uk). However, the extent to which insurance companies take flood risk management measures into account when determining premiums is a matter for the insurance industry to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) legislation; and (ii) funding he will commit to during the remainder of this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: I am considering introducing primary legislation in a number of areas.  My department has identified three potential bills, which include measures for dealing with drainage and flooding, ports and harbours, rail safety and public transport. I will also be considering secondary legislation in a number of areas, including reservoirs.Budgets beyond 2024-25 have not been agreed. However, the Departmental Business Plan for 2024-25 sets out my vision for the future and is available on the Departmental website https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/dfi-business-plan-2024-25

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on investment in traffic calming measures in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: The Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis – instead it is collated on a Council area basis.  The Newry and Armagh Constituency forms part of Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area and part of the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council area.Work programmes are prioritised based on a series of factors including engineering need, road classification / traffic volumes, and the availability of funding. Details of the planned programme for the 2024/25 financial year in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area, are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Newry, Mourne and Down District Council which was presented to the Council on the 23 September. This report can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-southern-report-newry-mourne-down-district-council-24-25.pdf  The 2024/25 Report to Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council is scheduled to be presented to Council on 28 October 2024.Following the presentation to Council, the Report will be published on the Department’s website and issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any investments and financial holdings held by their Department and associated arm’s-length bodies in Israel, including (i) actively managed equities; (ii) actively managed fixed income including any government or corporate bonds; (iii) passively managed equities; (iv) passively managed fixed incoming including any government or corporate bonds; and (v) any other investments including real estate investment trusts, direct funding provided to commercial lenders or any other financial investment or product.
answer_text: Neither the Department nor any of its Arm’s length Bodies (ALBs) have any such investments or financial holdings in Israel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children currently without a special educational needs school placement for the 2024-25 academic year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) advised that as of 2 October 2024, there are no children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) without a confirmed school placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action she is taking to address the current backlog in Rates Rebate Applications.
answer_text: To meet an increase in uptake of the Rate Rebate Scheme, Land & Property Services recruited an additional 14 staff in July 2024 to address application backlogs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the finding that 43 per cent of people said that they would not report a crime to the police again based on their experience with police, as laid out in the key findings of the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey; and (ii) the implications of this on effective policing.
answer_text: Whilst the operational response to the findings of this survey will be a matter for the Chief Constable of Police Service of Northern, I have a wider responsibility to ensure confidence in all parts of the criminal justice system, including policing, and I am therefore concerned by this finding. I intend to follow up with the Chief Constable and the Policing Board on this matter.I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system. My Department is also reviewing future options for seeking effective feedback from a wide pool of victims to provide insight into all aspects of their experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the finding that 63 per cent of people whose crimes were investigated, said police did not investigate their case thoroughly, as laid out in the key findings of the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey; and (ii) what steps she will take to address this issue.
answer_text: Whilst the operational response to the findings of this survey will be a matter for the Chief Constable of the Police Service of Northern Ireland, I have a wider responsibility to ensure confidence in all parts of the criminal justice system, including investigations, and I am therefore concerned by this finding. I intend to follow up with the Chief Constable and the Policing Board on this matter.I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system. My Department will engage with the Commissioner Designate and criminal justice partners on the detail of the recent survey results to establish whether any specific gaps in services can be identified and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the finding that only 7 per cent of crime victims are fairly confident that Northern Ireland’s criminal justice system can deliver for them, as laid out in the key findings of Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey; and (ii) what steps she will take to improve confidence.
answer_text: I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system. The results from this survey reflect the challenges we face to improve confidence in the justice system. It is acknowledged that this is a multifaceted issue which is affected by a range of factors, and that not all victims will have a positive experience of the justice system. However, feedback can and should help inform future change, and my Department continues to develop new initiatives and strategies to help support victims. There are many examples of positive feedback, including from those who can give their evidence from a Remote Evidence Centre or who have had access to free legal advice from a Sexual Offences Legal Adviser.   Extending best practice across a diverse system is a complex process; reform takes time to embed and for the impact to be felt by all, it also requires resources. I am acutely aware of the impact of funding pressures on each part of the system, and of the subsequent impact on victims going through the system. My Department is developing a new Victim and Witness Strategy which aims to place victims at the centre of its focus. The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and services that victims of crime can expect to receive from the criminal justice system  and provides information about what to do if not satisfied with the support received. Victim Champions, who are leaders in each of the criminal justice organisations, will soon hold a Summit to promote and reinforce that a trauma informed approach to supporting victims must be embedded across all parts of the system. I plan to introduce a Sentencing Bill which will aim to improve confidence by making clear the purposes and principles of sentencing; rationalising and simplifying the arrangements for unduly lenient sentence referrals; creating a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing a public service or performing a public duty; and increasing the maximum penalties for offences of causing death by driving. The Probation Board for Northern Ireland’s Victim Information Unit also works to inform victims about their rights, about Victim Information Schemes and about the key stages of the sentence of the person who has offended in a manner which is accessible, understandable and supportive.My Department will continue to engage with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime and all criminal justice partners on the detail of the recent survey results to establish whether any specific gaps in services can be identified and addressed. I am determined to improve confidence in the criminal justice system and will continue to do all I can to secure additional resource for the system, which will be invested in ways that improve victim experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of women that have died from cervical cancer as a result of misread cytology slides in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: NISRA produces official statistics on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. Information recorded on the Medical Certificate of cause of death (MCCD) is coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).  NISRA can only provide data on the number of women who have died from cervical cancer (ICD10 code C53 ‘Malignant neoplasm of cervix uteri’) over the last ten years by Health and Social Care Trust; this information is shown in Table 1. The MCCD does not hold information about medical circumstances connected to a diagnosis including misreading of cytology slides. Data for 2023 remains provisional (p) and is therefore subject to minor change. The assigned Health and Social Care Trust of a death is based on the usual residence of the deceased, as provided by the informant. Usual residence can include a care home. Where the deceased was not usually resident in Northern Ireland, their death is mapped to the place of death.Table 1: deaths due to Cervical Cancer by Health and Social Care Trust: 2014-2023.YearTotalBelfastNorthern South Eastern SouthernWestern 2014256853320151964162201623563542017196441420181745233201927844382020165243220212249324202221537242023 (p)1251231

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 15124/22-27, (i) to detail the protocol for permanent secretaries in relation to issuing letters to heads of departments; and (ii) for her assessment of the obligation for permanent secretaries to seek Ministerial approval before issuing such letters.
answer_text: (i) There is no formal protocol for permanent secretaries in relation to issuing letters to heads of departments. (ii) There is no requirement for a permanent secretary to seek Ministerial approval before writing to another official, whether in their own department, an arms’ length body or another department.  The specific circumstances should be considered on a case-by-case basis, and in the first instance, it would be for the individual permanent secretary  to judge as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the (a) Gross Domestic Product; and (b) Gross Domestic Product per capita locally, in each of the last 10 years; and (ii) for his assessment of how this compares to (a) Republic of Ireland; (b) England; (c) Scotland; and (d) Wales.
answer_text: Local Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and GDP per capita data for the period 1998 to 2022, as well as that for England, Scotland and Wales can be accessed on the Office for National Statistics website using the following link: Regional gross domestic product: all ITL regions.Equivalent data for south of Ireland  is available on the Central Statistics Office (CSO) website using the following link: Gross Domestic Product (GDP).Increases to GDP and GDP per capita are reflective of improving living standards.  My ambition, through delivery of my Economic Vision, is to ensure we improve the standard of living of all citizens living throughout the North.  We aim to do this by creating more Good Jobs, raising our productivity, promoting regional balance and transitioning to a greener and more sustainable economy.

answer_text: The provision of financial support to the Voluntary and Community Sector is commissioned and overseen by a number of different sectors within the Department of Health, to include the Public Health Agency (PHA); Strategic Performance and Planning Group (SPPG) and Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCT). These organisations are contracted to provide a wide range of support services across all areas of the community to support children, families, mental health provision, learning disability services and older people.   Whilst 26 of these organisations are based in the Newry and Mourne/Armagh locality, the majority provide services for residents across the wider Southern Trust locality. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial support his Department has provided to community and voluntary sector organisations in Newry and Armagh in each of the last ten years, broken down by organisation.
answer_text: There is no requirement for my Department to retain financial records beyond seven years, therefore the response will reflect the past seven financial years only.Due to the volume of detail required to provide an answer, I have arranged for this to be deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail how often the storm sewer system in Newry city is inspected for blockages and pipe failures; and (ii) when a blockage has been identified, to detail the time frame that the Department is obliged to inspect damage and complete remedial works.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water.However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that: (i) NI Water’s storm drains are designed to be self-cleansing and do not have any planned inspections; and (ii) All blockages are dealt with by NI Water as soon as possible within their Customer Promises guidelines. If contacted before 2.00pm, NI Water will unblock the sewer within 8 hours. If NI Water is contacted after 2.00pm, it will unblock the sewer before mid-day the following day. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of funding for NI Water in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: I have provided NI Water with an opening 2024-25 total budget allocation of £461.4 million. This includes £137.7 million resource funding for its day-to-day operating costs and £323.7 million for capital delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when works to facilitate additional accommodation at Rathore School, Newry, will be completed.
answer_text: There are two additional accommodation schemes currently progressing at Rathore School with combined costs of circa £8m.The acquisition of a three-hectare plot of land adjacent to the school site completed in February 2024 and an Integrated Design Team is currently working on the design of a nine-classroom accommodation block which will be placed on this site.  This block will also include ancillary and support space and the scheme will provide the school with much needed additional car parking.  It had been anticipated that this new accommodation block would be handed over to the school in September 2026, however following site surveys and investigations, this date is currently under review.  I should add that the date also hinges on the receipt of the relevant statutory approvals.There is also a scheme progressing to deliver a two-class modular unit on the existing school site.  The business case for this accommodation was approved in June 2024 and planning submitted in July.  An anticipated handover date for this accommodation has yet to be confirmed; officials in the Education Authority will continue to liaise with the school and confirm a date as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the actions his Department has been taking to address potholes in footpaths.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential repairs are identified and completed as necessary.My  Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, due to budgetary pressures, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Where my Department becomes aware of defects on footpaths within the Department’s control, my officials will record the defect for repair in line with our current road maintenance standards which can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount his Department has spent on electric vehicle infrastructure in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The development of publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity, and the provision and operation of publicly accessible EV Charge Points rests solely with the Charge Point Operator (CPO). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last 18 months. Available data has shown a considerable rise both in the number of publicly available charge points, and in the number of CPOs entering the market here, since April 2023. More than 20 CPOs are now operating commercially in the north.Latest statistics show that there are 625 publicly accessible EV charge points representing a growth of 60.5% in the total number of available points since April 2023 and the number of rapid charge points, which provide significantly faster opportunity to charge, has grown by over 300% in that same period. My Department has also committed over £470,000 match funding to support applications from Councils to the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme of which just over £133,500 has yet to be drawn down by Councils.Confidence in the market is very encouraging. We have nearly 30,000 EVs on our roads and CPOs providing key services to EV drivers at multiple sites across the north including shopping centres, hotels and on the strategic road network. Through the Shared Island Fund we will soon have additional charging facilities at nearly 50 sports clubs across the north bringing new access to charging to the heart of our communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road defects identified since 1 January 2024, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The number of road defects identified since the 1 January 2024, broken down by local council area, is included in the table below:ALL DEFECTS BY LOCATION BETWEEN JAN'24 TO OCT'24DEFECT COUNTEASTERN DIVISION                                                            17,787 BELFAST NORTH                            7,991 BELFAST SOUTH                            4,926 CASTLEREAGH and LISBURN                            4,870 NORTHERN DIVISION                                                           27,384 ANTRIM and NEWTOWNABBEY                            9,464 CAUSEWAY COAST and GLENS EAST                            2,028 CAUSEWAY COAST and GLENS WEST                            6,339 MID and EAST ANTRIM                            9,553 SOUTHERN DIVISION                                                           35,065 ARDS and NORTH DOWN                            3,807 ARMAGH CITY BANBRIDGE and CRAIGAVON EAST                            7,428 ARMAGH CITY BANBRIDGE and CRAIGAVON WEST                            4,642 NEWRY MOURNE and DOWN EAST                            6,836 NEWRY MOURNE and DOWN WEST                          12,352 WESTERN DIVISION                                                            38,584 FERMANAGH and OMAGH EAST                            6,405 FERMANAGH and OMAGH WEST                            6,543 LONDONDERRY and STRABANE                          11,221 MID ULSTER NORTH                            9,845 MID ULSTER SOUTH                            4,570 Grand Total                       118,820 Further information specifically on carriageway surface defects data dating back to 2008/2009 is regularly updated and publicly available on the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects. It is important to note that there can be a time lag between when each surface defect record is repaired and when this is updated on the maintenance management system.  Repair work is also only carried out subject to a defect meeting our current intervention levels set out in the ‘Limited Service’ policy, (see Director of Engineering memorandum ‘Road maintenance standards for safety – limited service': https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/network-maintenance).You may also be interested in my Department’s official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how much of his Department's budget has been allocated to investment in the road network in the 2024-25 financial year; (ii) how much of this budget is allocated for structural maintenance; and (iii) how much will be allocated for rural roads.
answer_text: My Department has allocated £228.1m for investment in the road network in 2024-25 to improve connectivity and help address regional imbalance. This includes a capital allocation of £89m for structural maintenance which incudes an allocation of £10m towards a Roads Recovery Fund of which £8m has been targeted specifically towards rural roads.   In addition, in 2024-25 £38m has been allocated towards delivery of essential road maintenance activities which are funded from the Department’s constrained resource budget which benefits the urban and rural road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14588/22-27, to detail (i) whether his proposed wastewater infrastructure investment will include Newry; (ii) the target date for commencing works; and (iii) the scope of works.
answer_text: To help ensure that the funding available is suitably prioritised to maximise investment, NI Water works with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency and the Utility Regulator, to develop a prioritised list of wastewater infrastructure schemes across the North, including Newry, to deliver defined quality improvements or major upgrades within the available budget. These proposed schemes are evidence-based, agreed by all key water stakeholders and prioritised with a strong emphasis on environmental compliance, headroom capacity issues and the potential to ease development constraints;NI Water is currently preparing a business case for the upgrade of Newry wastewater treatment works. This significant upgrade project, due to last a number of years, will be progressed, subject to approvals and the availability of funding; and (iii) 	The scope of the work is for the upgrade of Newry wastewater treatment works, and will provide greater wastewater capacity, replace existing assets and ensure that the company remains compliant with relevant environmental legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of funding for cycling infrastructure in (i) Armagh city; and (ii) Newry in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My departmental active travel budgets are allocated for the  development of walking, wheeling, and cycling and are not specific to the mode of travel.The current active travel budget allocation for the 2024-25 financial year for the divisional area which includes Armagh City and Newry is £2.5m. Budgets are monitored closely during the year, in line with sound financial management, and are subject to change.  In addition, the Department issued a letter of offer to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council for the Carlingford Lough Greenway which included funding for 2024-25 of approximately £0.6m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the budget; and (ii) the indicative spend of his Department's Blue/Green Fund for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Blue Green Infrastructure Fund was launched in 2020 and ended in 2023.  No budget has been allocated to the Blue Green Fund in the current financial year as projects are now being funded through the active travel budget allocation following closure of the fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment of Armagh Gaol.
answer_text: My officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council to develop proposals for the Armagh Gaol redevelopment. This project is being considered as part of the Mid South West Growth Deal. The Strategic Outline Case was approved by my Department in October 2023 and work is underway to develop an Outline Business case. The UK Government has paused the Mid South West Growth Deal and advised that it will be considered as part of the planned Comprehensive Spending Review. A decision is expected at the end of October. In the meantime, my officials will continue to liaise with council officers to develop proposals for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14591/22-27, for an update on efforts to secure alternative provisions for the Early Years Centre, Newry, pending its lease ending on 30 September 2024.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust fund the supported daycare and community pathway services for children with disabilities through a contract held by Early Years - the organisation for young children. The lease which Early Years - the organisation for young children have at their Newry Early Years Children and Family Centre premises, will come to an end on 30 September 2024. Regrettably, the Early Years Organisation have had to withdraw from the contract to provide services. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have been working hard over the last number of weeks to support families through interim measures while in discussions with an alternative provider for parts of the service. Discussions with a new provider for part of the services are underway with a number of legal and TUPE issues being explored at present by SHSCT before a new service can begin. Interim support measures have been discussed with affected families. These have included temporary increases to Direct Payments or families using Direct Payments to fund places at another day care provider and further SHSCT support options to those were Direct Payments are not an option.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) access to; and (ii) affordability of childcare.
answer_text: I have introduced a range of measures in the 2024/25 financial year to support the affordability and accessibility of early learning and childcare services.  This includes a Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) to ease the financial pressure on households and enable more parents to work.  At 27 September, 12,269 children across Northern Ireland were registered to benefit from this.   Other measures include the stabilisation and expansion of a range of existing early learning and childcare programmes and commencement of the standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme which, in time, will provide all children with at least 22.5 hours funded provision in the year before they start primary school.    	The measures currently being implemented will be evaluated to inform the development of future proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the action his Department is taking to reduce the decline in sports participation following entry to post-primary school; and (ii) his assessment of the benefits of promoting participation in sports and exercise.
answer_text: Physical Education (PE) is a stand-alone area of learning within the Northern Ireland Curriculum and is a statutory entitlement for every pupil from foundation stage to key stage 4. The aim of the PE curriculum is to develop physically literate individuals who have the knowledge, skills, and confidence to enjoy a lifetime of healthy physical activity.Physical Education (PE) provides numerous benefits, contributing to the holistic development of students including promoting regular physical activity which is essential for maintaining a healthy lifestyle and overall fitness, developing pupils’ interests, talents and resilience and extending knowledge, understanding and skills.  PE also enhances social skills by encouraging teamwork and cooperation among students helping to build relationships and learn about sportsmanship and fair play.This contributes to the well-being and development of resilience, and allows for the progression of skills, knowledge and understanding in the range of activity areas that make up the PE curriculum. There is no statutory time prescribed for any subject, including PE, in post-primary schools; however, the Department recommends that schools should provide children and young people with at least two hours of good quality curricular PE each week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional resources being committed to respite provisions in Newry and Armagh for children, young people, adults and their families to avoid the need for individuals to be admitted full-time into the care system.
answer_text: The Southern Trust (SHSCT) is responsible for providing respite services in the Newry and Mourne area.  The Southern Trust has advised the Department that there are a number of different services targeted from early intervention/prevention, community services, to residential and short breaks fostering overnight options. The Trust has services located across the locality and not exclusively area specific. This list includes additionality over the summer period as this is acknowledged as a particularly difficult time for families without the consistency offered by schools as follows: ABC Council & SHSCT Armagh City Summer Scheme; ABC Council & SHSCT All Stars Summer Scheme; Cedar Summer Scheme; EA & SHSCT summer schemes in Annalong and Newry City; St. Oliver Plunkett’s & SHSCT summer scheme Crossmaglen; IncredABLE summer scheme; Ogras Coalisland & SHSCT summer scheme; Cancer Fund for Children; Child Brain Injury Trust; Barnardos Sixth Sense Group; Cedar Outlook Scheme; Cedar Youth Matters Scheme; Don’t Box Me In Group; IncredABLE Peer Outings Programme including residential overnights; IncredABLE Saturday Day Respite Service; IncredABLE Youth Empowerment Programme including residential overnights; NI Music Therapy Trust; Self-Directed Support including Direct Payments service; SHSCT Supported Playgroup Service for pre-school children; SHSCT Specialist Child-minding Scheme; SHSCT After School Scheme; SHSCT Family Support Workers; Domiciliary Support Service via private providers; Goal Line Youth Trust; YMCA Lurgan Independent Living Skills group including an overnight residential; Oaklands Residential Short Breaks Unit Armagh; Willowgrove Residential Short Breaks Unit Banbridge that facilitates placements for Armagh and Newry children in addition to C&B children and SHSCT Short Breaks family based fostering scheme. All of these services meet the defined criteria of a short break (respite provision) for families (including day care and overnight provision) and are designed to be enjoyable and developmental for the child whilst ultimately enabling their parents/carers to continue caring for them in their home environment and avoid admissions to the care system. These services provide much needed short breaks for hundreds of children and are fully utilised. In Mental Health and Disability Directorate, Short Break Services are provided across the Southern Trust and include day care, day opportunities and overnight provision of bed based short breaks as follows: Bed based short break provision within the Trust and across Independent Sector Providers, IncreadAble weekend short breaks, SHSCT Short breaks with Host Families including overnights and day support; SHSCT Adult Placement with Host Families; SHSCT Self Directed Support including Direct payment; Cedar Day Time opportunities PD; Derryamore House joint initiative with the National Trust Conservation Group; SHSCT Arts and Crafts group The Win and SLH, Gosford Park sports and r

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has a policy in relation to preventing Health and Social Care Trust staff from whistleblowing or alerting their elected representatives to planned service changes at hospitals, including in instances where medical staff believe those changes will have adverse impact on patient safety.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of births recorded at (i) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) Craigavon Area Hospital, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: NISRA produces official statistics on birth registration information as collected by the General Register Office. Data on registered births by place of birth is available in the Registrar General Annual Report 2022 in table 3.9. Data on births registered in 2023 have not yet been finalised so are unavailable at present. The Registrar General Annual Report 2023 will be published in November 2024 and will contain the numbers for births registered in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why a divert was placed on Daisy Hill maternity services on 21 September and 22 September 2024; and (ii) whether the Southern Health and Social Care Trust will be maintaining a full suite of maternity services on a 24/7 basis on site at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: Due to ongoing issues with staff sickness absence, temporary diverts have been in place from Craigavon to Daisy Hill and from Daisy Hill to Craigavon, on a number of occasions since July.   Most recently, due to difficulties in safely filling midwifery rotas, there was a divert in place from Daisy Hill to Craigavon Hospital for unplanned maternity admissions last weekend from Friday night (20th September) until Monday morning (23rd September). Three midwives from Daisy Hill Hospital worked at Craigavon on Sunday to support this divert. The Trust have a highly skilled and compassionate maternity team and acknowledge their consistent dedication to mothers and babies in sometimes difficult circumstances. The Trust remain committed to ensuring the provision of safe, high quality maternity services for women across the Southern Trust, continually reviewing and taking account of national and regional policy guidelines. They are very mindful of the continuing workforce challenges across health and social care and Southern Trust is not immune from these.The Trust appreciate the ongoing support of families and the public as they work through these challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the decision by Southern Health and Social Care Trust management to reverse the retire and return commitments for Southern Health and Social Care Trust midwives.
answer_text: My Department is committed to an effective midwifery service.  This is underpinned by the Chief Nursing Officer’s ‘Shaping Our Future’ vision, launched in May last year, focussing on sustainability, recruitment, retention and the value of both midwives and nurses.I am advised by the Southern HSC Trust, as the employer, that the contracts of some block-booked midwives remain subject to an active case lodged with the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal.  It would, therefore, be inappropriate to offer an assessment of this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women from the Republic of Ireland that have accessed maternity services at Daisy Hill Hospital in each of the last ten years, broken down by patient county of origin.
answer_text: Please see below the number of women from the Republic of Ireland that have accessed maternity services at Daisy Hill for the last 10 years by patient county.
