question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any awareness campaigns his Department has launched in relation to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: My Department has worked in partnership with a number of sector stakeholders to raise awareness of the new Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) and to maximise its uptake. Stakeholders have promoted the benefits of the Scheme through their own networks and on social media channels.My Department has also funded a radio and social media campaign to promote the full range of financial assistance available with childcare costs, including the NICSS.  This is ongoing. The Early Years – the organisation for young children (EYO), who administer the Scheme on behalf of my Department, has promoted the scheme amongst providers and carried out multiple training sessions.  They have also designed and distributed posters to be displayed in childcare premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any barriers hindering students in Northern Ireland from attending universities in Ireland; and (ii) any engagement he has had with his counterpart in Ireland with a view to removing these barriers.
answer_text: There are several factors that may disincentivise cross-border student mobility in higher education, including: funding and cost of living pressures; arrangements governing equivalence of secondary qualifications for university admissions; disparities between the respective application systems North and South; and the need for better information to help prospective students make appropriate choices and applications.I am committed to removing barriers to people from here who want to study in the South and vice versa.  I have instructed officials to consider options for addressing these barriers and am seeking a meeting with my counterpart in the South, Minister Patrick O’Donovan, so that we can identify actions that can be taken, separately and collectively, to open up greater opportunities for our young people to study throughout Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recent court ruling that the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023 gives the UK Government too much veto power over the disclosure of material.
answer_text: I noted with interest the Court of Appeal’s findings in the judgment delivered on 20 September in relation to a number of challenges against the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023, including its concerns that the Act places the final say on disclosure of material in the hands of the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland.  I regard it as important that where victims and survivors, or their families, choose to request a review by the Independent Commission on Reconciliation and Information Recovery they should not be unduly frustrated in their efforts to obtain information through the Commission. It is a matter for the Secretary of State and the Labour Government to consider how it will respond to this and other judgements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Justice of Ireland in relation to harmonising the justice systems.
answer_text: I met with Helen McEntee TD in April 2024, under the auspices of the Inter-Governmental Agreement on Criminal Justice Cooperation (IGA). The IGA provides a structured mechanism to facilitate effective co-operation and co-ordination on criminal justice matters in areas of mutual interest or concern. The IGA mechanism is supported by five Project Advisory Groups which are taking forward workstreams in the areas of public protection, forensic science, youth justice, victims’ issues, and criminal justice and social diversity issues. Copies of the IGA 2023-2025 Work Programme are available in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any additional funding which will be allocated to sporting organisations to build on the success of the 2024 Paris Olympics and Paralympics, with a focus on improving participation and success at the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics and Paralympics.
answer_text: I welcome the success of our Northern Ireland athletes on the Olympic and Paralympic stage in Paris this year and I was delighted to be able to mark these successes at my Department and Sport NI’s celebration event hosted in the SSE Arena on Thursday 3 October. This was not only an opportunity to pay tribute to our athletes, but also to the coaches and support staff who have encouraged and supported them to become who they are today.The Sport NI Sports Institute continues to support elite sports through science, medicine and programme support to Sport National Governing Bodies, and working directly with coaches and athletes.  The Chief Executive Officer of Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, has advised me as follows:-“The current Sport Institute Service Delivery plan ends in 2024 and a new Service Delivery plan will be developed with sports to identify their needs and provide the necessary support to athletes going forward in the 2028 Los Angeles Olympic and Paralympic cycle.”I want to build on the successes from this year’s Games with a legacy to ensure both investment in elite athletes – those who will represent us at the 2026 Commonwealth Games, the 2028 Olympics and Paralympics, and other top level international events – and grassroots sport and participation.I have therefore instructed my officials to work on further proposals which will see not only the very necessary investment the Member has called for in elite athletics, but also increased support for grassroots sports.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the specific provisions in the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 to raise awareness of the symptoms of (i) ovarian cancer; and (ii) blood cancers.
answer_text: Early diagnosis is one of the strategic themes within the Cancer Strategy and improved awareness raising of the potential signs and symptoms of cancer is essential to ensuring this.   Action 6 of the strategy is to ‘deliver regular, effective, targeted evidence-based ‘Be Cancer Aware’ campaigns harnessing the expertise in the community and voluntary sector’.In 2022 Cancer Research UK in partnership with Public Health Agency (PHA) and NI Cancer Network (NICaN) launched a Spotting Cancer Early Campaign. This included a 6-week multi-channel publicity campaign comprising of TV, Social Media, Print, outdoor posters and community pharmacies across NI.  It was intended to encourage those aged 50+ from deprived areas of NI to contact their GP if they have noticed any signs or symptoms. It was also supported by CRUK web resources specifically for NI at Spot cancer early | Cancer Research UK. The campaign was funded through the Community Foundation for Northern Ireland through the Department of Health.  The CRUK campaign in 2022 ran in parallel with a PHA Living Well Campaign. The Living Well campaign ran within all Community Pharmacies across Northern Ireland (500 approx.) from August to October 2022 and focused on general awareness for both pharmacists and the public around the signs and symptoms of cancer and encouraged pharmacists to engage with patients who may be presenting with possible cancer symptoms.  Booklet - Be Cancer Aware - Spotting symptoms and reducing your risk.pdf (hscni.net)(i) Ovarian Cancer – The NICaN Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group and NI Gynaecology GIRFT (Getting It Right First Time) Regional Forum are currently taking forward a task and finish group to review and update Primary Care Guidance for Suspected Gynaecological Cancers for red flag referral.  The guidance once updated and agreed regionally will be shared across all GPs and Trusts and will be available on the NICaN website.  Monthly Primary Care Education Sessions aimed at GP’s, nurses, pharmacists and Allied Health Professionals have taken place since 2021 to help improve understanding and awareness of red flag symptoms.  These sessions are delivered in partnership with BHSCT, Macmillan GP facilitators and NICaN.  In November 2022 a session was held specific to Ovarian Cancer.  Sessions are recorded and available to view on the NICaN website. Primary Care Education Sessions | Northern Ireland Cancer Network (hscni.net)In March 2024 the Public Health Agency provided a press release to raise awareness of the signs and symptoms of Ovarian Cancer during Ovarian Cancer month. Ovarian Cancer Awareness Month: be aware of the signs and symptoms | HSC Public Health Agency (hscni.net). The PHA Be Cancer Aware website also includes signs and symptoms on Ovarian Cancer. Ovarian cancer | Be Cancer Aware (becancerawareni.info).  In April 2024 officials from my department met with representatives from Target Ovarian Cancer, the UK's leading ovarian cancer charity.   Continuing to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the decision by the UK Government to pause two City and Growth Deals whilst progressing two City and Growth Deals.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my Written Ministerial Statement of 18 September.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/pausing-city-and-growth-deals-funding-commitment

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the primary purpose of the public health bill that is being consulted on; (ii) whether any concerns have been raised in relation to unintended consequences of the proposed bill; and (iii) whether any specific areas of the proposed bill have invoked public concern and opposition.
answer_text: The primary purpose of a new Public Health Bill is to protect the population of Northern Ireland against threats to public health and to ensure that Northern Ireland can respond to a wide range of severe threats to public health. The planned legislation will strike the correct balance between the state’s responsibility to protect the public’s health, our collective responsibility to protect each other, and the autonomy, rights and dignity of individuals.I am aware of some public concern, and, indeed some misunderstanding, around the proposed health protection measures in relation to persons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14512/22-27, to detail the lessons his Department can learn from the Water Action Plan 2024 – A River Basin Management Plan for Ireland.
answer_text: Under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, the Department must review and update River Basin Management Plans (RBMP), setting out how water quality will be improved across Northern Ireland, every six years.Although still in draft, Northern Ireland’s third cycle River Basin Management Plan has been prepared and consulted on. However, I will need time to review it in the context of emerging information on water quality, including reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office, the Office for Environmental Protection and the Lough Neagh Report.  I am aware of the urgency of this issue and will complete this work as quickly as possible.I am aware that the Water Action Plan 2024 - A River Basin Management Plan for Ireland was published on 5 September 2024.  Officials from both jurisdictions will continue to liaise on areas of mutual interest.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 in (i) 2022; (ii) 2023; and (iii) 2024.
answer_text: Funding allocated to the Cancer Strategy:2022/23 - £2.3m recurrent2023/24 - additional £6m recurrent to make a total of £8.3m recurrent2024/25 - additional £2.3m recurrent to make a total of £10.6m recurrent

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) a breakdown of the areas where complaints have been lodged in relation a stale odour and taste to water; and (ii) the origin of the water supply in relation to each of the complaints
answer_text: The question you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows: Water Abstraction SiteImpacted AreaLough NeaghMagherafeltUnagh MormealMoneymorePortadownTandrageeDungannonCraigavon EastStoneyfordMoiraBreda NorthBreda SouthBelfast PurdysburnNewry WestThe table details the impacted areas and origin based on logged customer contacts, from late August to 18th September 2024.Should you have any further queries with regards this you can contact NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any conversations he has had with the Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero to expand the aspirations of GB Energy to benefit the North of Ireland.
answer_text: I met with Secretary of State Ed Miliband in July and emphasised my energy priorities for the Economic Vision. I put to him my ambition to realise self-sufficiency in affordable renewable energy here and I gained his agreement that GB Energy can and must also support decarbonisation in the North of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department will implement the recommendations outlined in the Chartered Institute of Housing Tackling the Housing and Homelessness Crisis Report.
answer_text: A number of the recommendations made in the report are already aligned with the work of my Department, for example: securing housing as a priority in the draft Programme for Government; bringing forward a new housing supply strategy; revitalising the NIHE; ensuring sustained funding for the Supporting People programme; and addressing residential housing challenges in relation to energy efficiency, fuel poverty & decarbonisation. While other recommendations are more challenging or complex, I welcome the Chartered Institute’s contribution to public debate on these issues. I will continue to engage with the Chartered Institute for Housing and other stakeholders to explore solutions to the issues raised in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council will be tabled.
answer_text: I am advised by officials in my Department that the Newry, Mourne & Down District Council Report is scheduled for presentation to Council on 23 September 2024.Following this presentation, the Report will be published on my Department’s website. It will also be issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of businesses that are accredited as Living Wage employers; (ii) the application process for businesses to become Living Wage employers; and (iii) the actions his Department is taking to promote the adoption of the real Living Wage across the economy.
answer_text: (i) As of 19 September, there are 106 Living Wage accredited employers in the north of Ireland. (ii)  In order to accredit as a Living Wage employer a business must meet the following criteria,All directly employed staff need to be paid the current real Living Wage, which is £12 for Britain (£13.15 for London), at the point of accreditation.Pay the real Living Wage, or have a plan in place to pay to the real Living Wage to any subcontractor who works for two or more hours a week, over an eight-week consecutive period on behalf of the business.Pay the accreditation fee. The cost varies according to the size of an organisation, starting at £69 per year for those with fewer than 10 employees. Businesses must complete and sign a license form; meet with Living Wage NI staff to ensure they meet the criteria and pay the fee to accredit.  The Living Wage Foundation announces new rates each year and this year the announcement will be in late October. Generally, all accredited Living Wage Employers have until the following May to implement the new rates.(iii) Advice NI is the Living Wage Foundation’s accreditation partner in the north.  The Department for the Economy, together with the Living Wage Foundation, has provided the funding to allow Advice NI to set up and operationalise Living Wage NI.  The initiative was launched in June 2024 and aims to increase awareness of the real Living Wage and drive up the number of accredited Living Wage employers and the number of workers being paid the real Living Wage in the north. Living Wage NI is modelled on similar successful Living Wage Foundation accreditation partnerships in Scotland and Wales.  Living Wage Scotland and Living Wage Wales have demonstrated the success of having a local agency promoting the Living Wage and accrediting employers in their jurisdictions.  Latest statistics show that there are 15356 Living Wage accredited employers in Britain, with 3728 in Scotland and 587 in Wales.  Living Wage NI has already undertaken significant publicising of the Living Wage with local media outlets including BBC Newsline. A range of businesses have now successfully accredited in the north, including Invest NI and QUB as well as a range of smaller SME’s and charities.  Living Wage NI celebrated the milestone of 100 accredited NI employers in September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the entitlement a long-term substitute teacher has to paid leave for (i) holidays; and (ii) sickness.
answer_text: A teacher engaged and paid for each day worked at a rate of 1/195 of the annual salary of a full-time permanent teacher receives a higher daily rate to account for holiday pay. Since 2004 the following statement has been included on the pay slips of supply teachers: ‘Holiday pay is included within your daily rate of pay calculated as annual salary over 195 working days’. Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the 5 main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and their employers via TNC Circulars.Through the TNC, and with limited resources, my officials are working with the employers of teachers to improve employment rights for supply teachers.A review of the use of supply teachers has been carried out through the TNC and the recommendations from this review are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of (i) the merits of forming an environmental protection agency; and (ii) how this would positively impact water quality.
answer_text: There is an environmental protection agency, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), which is an Executive Agency under the direction of the DAERA Minister.  One of NIEA’s key priorities is to deliver a freshwater environment at good status and my Department works closely with NI Water, DAERA and NIEA, to help achieve this aim. 	In addition, the Office for Environmental Protection, with a mission to protect and improve the environment, will also help to achieve this aim by holding government and other public authorities to account.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of first year medical student places in each of the last five years, broken down by university.
answer_text: The information requested is set out in the table below:YearNumber of Year 1 Medical Students – Queen’s University BelfastNumber of Year 1 Medical Students – Ulster University2020/21294N/A2021/22312772022/23262772023/24262772024/2528877

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to his Department's proposed Live Better Programme, to detail his plans to form closer cooperation and relationships with the community and voluntary sector, including charities, to specifically target mental health difficulties.
answer_text: You will be aware that health inequalities are one of my main areas of focus as Health Minister. The recently announced Live Better initiative is being designed to bring targeted health support to communities which need it most. The initiative will build on the good work developed by the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the wider Health and Social Care system over the years and will seek to publicise and promote existing initiatives and programmes so they can be delivered intensively in communities.The details of the programmes to be delivered under this initiative are still being developed and the shape of the initiative will be informed by the communities themselves. The community and voluntary (C&V) sector will also be a key partner in supporting this programme. I hope to begin the initiative in two locations in the autumn, which will be announced in due course.The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) recognises that the effective delivery of a community-based model of mental health is not possible without the full integration of the C&V sector. Harnessing the skills and experience that exist in this sector is of utmost importance to ensure that this is used to benefit people with mental ill health, but also to promote mental health and work on prevention and early intervention.Action 17 of the MHS, to ‘fully integrate the community and voluntary sector in mental health service delivery with a lifespan approach including the development of a protocol to make maximum use of the sector’s expertise’ aims to address closer cooperation between Statutory and C&V sectors in relation to mental health services.It is anticipated that a review of the scope, scale and capacity of the C&V sector will commence shortly. The review, which will be conducted by an independent review team, will include collaboration with a group of C&V organisations with a primary focus on mental health, and will be fundamental in helping to drive the full integration and utilisation of C&V and Statutory mental health services.Given the synergies between Live Better and the MHS my departmental officials will liaise together to ensure appropriate engagement with the community and voluntary sector takes place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the guidance provided to permanent secretaries in relation to issuing letters to heads of departments without prior Ministerial approval.
answer_text: My department has not issued any guidance to permanent secretaries in relation to issuing letters to heads of departments without Ministerial Approval.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement that has taken place with the Minister of Health in relation to the funding for Nexus after March 2024, following the launch of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, which launched on 16th September, recognises the need for services for victims to deliver a consistent trauma informed response across a diverse range of needs. It does not displace existing policy remits or funding arrangements. Nexus was a key partner in the Codesign process to develop the Framework. We met with the Minister of Health in March 2024 in relation to the end of the contract with Nexus and the wider issue of ensuring sustainability of service provision for victims and survivors of VAWG. As you will be aware, following direction from the former Health Minister, Nexus were awarded a further 12-month extension to their contract.  Our officials continue to work closely with counterparts in the Department of Health on a range of violence against women and girls related issues, including implementation of DoJ/DoH-led Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy. Representatives from both the Department of Health and Department of Justice also sit on the EVAWG Strategic Framework Oversight Board which is to be chaired by the Junior Ministers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the co-operation that will take place between the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls and the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme to ensure a focus on women who are subject to coercive control or intimidation by paramilitary organisations.
answer_text: Our officials in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Programme team have been working closely with their counterparts in the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime (EPPOC) for some time. Representatives from EPPOC were co-design partners in the development of the EVAWG Strategic Framework and similarly, EVAWG is represented on the EPPOC Programme Board. Both Programmes are closely aligned on their guiding principles of being data-driven, trauma-informed, and taking a public health approach to tackling violence, and have a shared approach to data and utilise common metrics.  Officials have collaborated on a range of issues, including a shared data platform, and the recent EPPOC ‘Ending the Harm’ campaign, which had a focus on violence against women. Engagement is also ongoing on the development of forthcoming campaigns from both Programmes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) when they received news that the British government would not fund the Casement Park redevelopment in time for 2028; and (ii) any commitments they sought from the British government in terms of the timeline, scale and standard of the redevelopment.
answer_text: Ministers were informed of the UK Government’s decision not to fund the Casement Park redevelopment in time for 2028 following receipt of a letter from the Secretary of State for NI to the Minister for Communities on the evening of Friday 13 September.The issue has been discussed with the Government on a number of occasions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether she had prior knowledge of the letter from her Permanent Secretary to the Chief Constable of the PSNI in week commencing 12 August 2024, reprimanding him for seeking additional funding from the Prime Minister; and (ii) whether she approved this letter.
answer_text: I have made it very clear that the correspondence to which you refer was issued by the Permanent Secretary in his role as Principal Accounting Officer. The responsibilities of a Departmental Accounting Officer are set out in more detail in Chapter 3 of Managing Public Money Northern Ireland. I have previously stated that I am not routinely sighted on this type of correspondence, and as such there was no requirement for me to approve this letter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for (i) his assessment of the current speed limit for heavy goods vehicles on a single lane carriageway; and (ii) whether he has any plans to bring this into line with the rest of the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware that in April 2015 England and Wales adopted new national speed limits for heavy goods vehicles over 7.5 tonnes, and that the new limits were subject to evaluation during a 3-year long pilot.  We are also aware that Scotland, similar to the North, has yet to change its HGV speed limits, but that it is currently undertaking a national speed management review, as set out in their road safety framework to 2030.While I can confirm that at present there are no plans to change our HGV speed limits, our approach to speed limits in general is one of the topics that I will consider while in office.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with (i) Executive colleagues; and (ii) Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in relation to the environmental enhancement and regeneration of Newry ship canal.
answer_text: My Department does not have responsibility for the environmental enhancement and regeneration of the Newry Ship Canal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with (i) Executive colleagues; and (ii) Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in relation to the environmental enhancement and regeneration of Newry ship canal.
answer_text: I have not discussed the environmental enhancement and regeneration of Newry Ship Canal with Executive colleagues or Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, nor have these matters been raised with me.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the investment in wastewater infrastructure upgrades by his Department and arm's-length bodies in each of the last ten years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The table below details wastewater investment (£m) for the last 9 years, by local council area.
