question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the current status of the Female Surgical Ward in Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The former female surgical ward was originally located on the left side of the third floor on Daisy Hill Hospital which is where the new Elective Overnight Stay Centre and Women’s Health Hub are now located.Elective Overnight Stay Centre Daisy Hill Hospital was appointed as a regional elective overnight stay centre by Health Minister Robin Swann in October 2022. Whilst no longer labelled as separate 'male' and 'female' surgical wards, there are surgical beds for both male and female patients in the new dedicated Elective Overnight Stay Centre.With emergency surgery concentrated at Craigavon Area Hospital, the Trust now have additional theatre time through Daisy Hill’s Elective Overnight Stay Centre to benefit more people waiting on planned procedures.  The Trust have a really enthusiastic and committed surgical team, continually striving to improve and develop services and keen to work with colleagues regionally, to tackle unacceptable waiting lists across Northern Ireland. Since April 2023, over 7,237 patients have been treated at the Elective Overnight Stay Centre across a range of specialties including general surgery, gynaecology, ENT, breast and urology. Women’s Health Hub A new women’s health hub which opened last year is also located in the former female surgical ward on the third floor of Daisy Hill Hospital beside the Elective Overnight Stay Centre, and offers rapid access for gynaecological and early pregnancy support. The team of gynaecologists, obstetricians, specialist nurses and midwives, aim to make sure that women get the most suitable care in the right place, as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what opportunities for fostering reconciliation can be harnessed from extending Ireland’s Ancient East to include County Armagh and County Down.
answer_text: The extension of the Fáilte Ireland brands into the North, is an objective within my Department's Business Plan to deliver regional economic growth. Broadening Ireland's Ancient East to Armagh and Down should encourage greater numbers of people to visit the region, either if visiting from the South or for international tourists visiting the island of Ireland, leading to greater economic sustainability in the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department has taken to ensure that wheelchair users have barrier-free access to air travel.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure, my powers relating to our three main airports are limited to the Airports (NI) Order 1994.  These are powers to control noise, control land in the interests of the safe and efficient use of airports, make byelaws, provide for airport constabularies and airport consultative committees and to give grants to assist capital expenditure.Civil Aviation policy and regulation in the UK is largely a reserved matter which rests with the Department for Transport (DfT) and the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA).  The Department for Infrastructure does not have any remit in relation to passenger accessibility at our airports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why Irish language signage has not been included throughout the new Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) whether this decision is compliant with the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: I am committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services and in our society.  I am continuing to engage with Translink in relation to Irish language signage at Grand Central Station and I’m confident that the matter can be resolved.The Identity and Language Act (NI) 2022 is fundamentally about appointing Commissioners, who, once appointed, will publish best practice and standards. Work is continuing, led by the Department for Communities, to develop an action plan that will sit alongside the new Irish Language Strategy. My Department is represented on the Cross Departmental Working Group for this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9283/22-27, whether a warning letter has been served, setting out the steps to be taken and time limit for the removal of the unauthorised advertisements, in accordance with PPS17 (Control of Outdoor Advertisements).
answer_text: Enforcement of advertising via PPS17 is a matter for the relevant District Council and it would be inappropriate for the Department to comment in this regard. I can confirm that those responsible for erecting the billboard adjacent to the Egyptian Arch have been asked to remove it by DfI officials, however no warning letter has been served.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister why the draft Programme for Government did not include actions and associated timelines in relation to the Irish language.
answer_text: The draft Programme for Government (PfG) sets out our proposed collective priorities of The Executive. The draft PfG however, does not include everything that every department is bringing forward separately, such as the provisions of the Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022, for which The Executive Office is responsible. This includes the establishment of the new bodies, namely: •	The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;•	The Irish Language Commissioner; and•	The Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.We have made our initial decisions regarding the recruitment process, and further advice is being developed to enable the competitions to be launched. We will keep Members updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current three year waiting list for paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion surgery; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to address the waiting list for the 94 children currently waiting for this surgery.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  My officials within SPPG have now carried out some initial scoping on the question that you have raised with senior officials in BHSCT and have been informed of the following.There are 93 children currently on the waiting list.  Each case is constantly assessed by the specialist team at the RBHSC and dealt with as quickly as possible based on clinical priority and chronological order.  Due to the introduction of a new Encompass ICT system at BHSCT it is not possible to it is not possible to examine the data further at this stage.  It is hoped that when the Encompass system is fully operational across all Trusts in NI that the extraction and publication of data will be more streamlined.For example, at the moment a paediatric surgeon is validating the list so waiting times on the list may change dependent on the clinical priority. This validation process will identity which children are suitable for possible transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  Clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine this.  BHSCT and Alder Hey are discussing the feasibility of this initiative.Belfast Trust are also hoping to provide a waiting list initiative in October, to carry out additional sessions in October, with patients being nursed in RBHSC over the weekend as well as this, discussions are underway to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon a gastrostomy surgery list in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this if it can arranged.There is also a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC underway and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining potential partnership working with the Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital and will be dependent on unscheduled care pressures. My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average construction cost per unit of social housing in each of the last five years, broken down by scheme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not collate scheme-specific data on construction costs only for new social housing. Scheme-specific cost information is also deemed to be commercially sensitive. Total Cost Indicator (TCI) Allowances provide average benchmark figures for the overall cost of new social housing delivery. TCI Allowances are ‘all-in’ costs for new social housing development and include land costs, construction costs and on-costs (professional fees etc). The table below details the average TCI Allowances for new social housing applicable during the period 2019/20 – 2023/24. The average TCI Allowances are based on a typical New Build General Needs 3-person/2-bedroom house.  YearAverage TCI AllowancesSpring 2023£183,617Autumn 2022£177,883Autumn 2021£158,606Spring 2021£151,472Spring 2020£148,333Spring 2019£140,883”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to ensuring that every person with cystic fibrosis should be able to access (i) a cystic fibrosis social worker; and (ii) a cystic fibrosis clinical psychologist.
answer_text: (i) The Cystic Fibrosis Service for Children is managed centrally by Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC), and by Belfast City Hospital (BCH) for Adults.  All adults who are living with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) have access to Social Work support. Referrals can be made into Social Work from other professionals, agencies or self-referrals.  (ii) The current waiting time to see the Psychologist is 12 months for non-urgent referrals.  An Annual Review process ensures all adults with CF are offered an appointment for assessment with the psychologist each year.  In children’s services two members of the Paediatric Psychology Service deliver the post to children, increasing the robustness of the service, and it is well embedded into the Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT).  Similar to adults, the Annual Review process ensures all children with CF are offered an appointment for assessment with the Psychologist each year, and currently the Psychologists work closely with the MDT to prioritise need and ensure waiting times support quick access.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when roadworks will be carried out on the road connecting Cranny Road and Cashel Road, Mullaghbawn.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Newry, Mourne and Down council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of mutualisation of the landlord arm of the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive is faced with a significant investment challenge. Transformation of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, particularly focused on securing its access to borrowing, provides the opportunity to sustain public housing without acting as a drag on already stretched public services. I am actively pressing for a solution that does not require reclassification or significant reform, that will transform it onto a sustainable footing to increase routine & planned investment, to deal with maintenance backlogs and a program of retrofit to improve energy efficiency in homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the barriers people with cystic fibrosis face in retaining employment.
answer_text: A person with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) may worry about how CF might affect their working life and if they will still be able to achieve everything they want to. Cystic Fibrosis is often an invisible condition with the symptoms often hidden unless the individual is very unwell, but it is important for employers to remember that a person with CF will still have a daily treatment regimen, some of which may be done before and after work. The health of a person with CF can also fluctuate so their treatment regimen may change, or they may need to go for appointments or hospital stays more often. CF does not affect cognitive ability.It is in everyone’s interest that employees continue to work for as long as possible. If an employer has any concerns about an individual’s performance, they should discuss it with them. The Equality Act 2010 establishes legal rights for people with a disability in a number of areas, including employment. Employers have a duty to not treat people with a disability less favourably. If problems with an employee are related to their CF, then there is a duty to make reasonable adjustments in the workplace.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average time taken to report a positive HPV smear test result to a patient’s GP.
answer_text: For a HPV positive cervical screening test in Northern Ireland, during the three-month period June – August 2024: the average time from date the smear was taken to it being authorised by the laboratory (at which point it is issued to the GP practice) was 21 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the planning application processing times in each of the last 5 years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My department publishes quarterly and annual statistical bulletins presenting a summary of planning performance across Northern Ireland.  In terms of the information you seek, council processing times for the past five years can be found at the attached link.  The information is contained within the annual statistical tables for each year with processing times for major applications in table 3.2 and for local applications within table 4.2.Planning activity statistics | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average referral time for a colposcopy following a positive HPV smear test with abnormal cell changes.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland (i) prior to; and (ii) following its publication of Probation Practice in Northern Ireland, a follow-up review of the Inspection Recommendations.
answer_text: I did not have any specific discussions with the Chief Inspector of Criminal Justice on this follow-up review of Probation Practice prior to or following its publication. Departmental Officials provided input for the report in respect of Strategic Recommendation 1. However, I note the positive comments made in the report about the progress made by the Probation Board for Northern Ireland in addressing the recommendations made in the initial report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5947/22-27, and given that 140 women in England have received payouts in August 2024, to detail any consideration his Department has given to implementing a redress scheme for those suffering complications after having vaginal mesh implants.
answer_text: The Patient Safety Commissioner for England, Dr Henrietta Hughes, made recommendations on redress for those who were harmed by Sodium Valproate and Pelvic Mesh implants in the “Hughes Report”, which was published on 7 February 2024. Whilst Dr Hughes’ remit was specifically for England, it is acknowledged that patients from across the UK have been affected by these issues.  Financial redress for Sodium Valproate and Pelvic Mesh remains a reserved matter and the three Devolved Administrations are awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC). The UK Government have not yet taken a decision on redress. The Department continues to give careful consideration to the findings and recommendations within the Hughes Report, including continued engagement at official level with counterparts in England, Scotland and Wales. I have recently written to Baroness Merron, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Patient Safety, Women’s Health and Mental Health at the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), requesting an update on the consideration of the Hughes Report recommendations and have sought assurances that consultation with the Devolved Administrations will take place in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to find and secure an alternative location to prevent the Newry Early Years Children and Family Centre from closing.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust fund the supported daycare and community pathway services for children with disabilities through a contract held by Early Years – the organisation for young children.The lease which Early Years – the organisation for young children have at their Newry Early Years Children and Family Centre premises, will come to an end on 30 September 2024. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust and Early Years Organisation have been working hard over the last number of months to secure alternative accommodation to provide these vital services but unfortunately, all options have been exhausted at this time. Regrettably, the Early Years Organisation is unable to continue to offer the supported daycare and community pathway services for relevant children with disabilities after this time. Both the Nurture Cove, pre-school and Reaching Further, after-school programmes will cease from 30th September 2024. In the meantime, all families will have a SHSCT keyworker/ social worker who will be visiting each family over the next fortnight to look at alternative provision to support families in the interim period.The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have a preliminary meeting with an alternative after-schools provider this week to see if they are able to undertake this element of the service moving forward while the Trust now seeks alternatives for the remainder of the service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the causes of the recruitment challenges faced by Community Transport organisations; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to address those challenges.
answer_text: I continue to recognise and value the important services provided by community transport for many disabled people and older people in our communities.  I understand how critical it is to help support people to access their local services and I acknowledge the key contribution of staff, drivers and volunteers towards managing and providing transport options for those who rely most on these services.  I am aware that current funding pressures and year by year budgeting makes recruitment of staff challenging within community transport organisations and across the wider voluntary and community sector.  I realise that the level of wage an organisation can offer, and in particular driver’s wages, may impact on the organisation’s ability to both recruit and retain key staff.  I am also aware of the associated costs relating to the requirement for the minibus drivers within community transport to hold a full category D1 licence.  In recognition of the increased staff costs, I have awarded an additional 4.2% funding in 2024/25 for front line community transport providers above the levels provided in 2023/24.  This increase is not as much as I would like to provide but it’s certainly a significant increase along with my commitment to maintain the funding into 2025/26, subject to allocation of funds to my Department.  I hope this will help alleviate some of the pressures faced by service providers and support the workers in receiving a proper pay rise.I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to protect and support front line transport services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time for a patient to be seen by a breast cancer specialist following an urgent referral, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's plans to allocate funding to upgrade wastewater infrastructure in Newry in order to facilitate residential and commercial development.
answer_text: My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges. In terms of funding for 2024-25, I have provided NI Water with a total budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society.To help ensure that the funding available is suitably prioritised to maximise investment, NI Water works with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency and the Utility Regulator, to develop a prioritised list of wastewater infrastructure schemes, to deliver defined quality improvements or major upgrades within the available budget. These proposed schemes are evidence-based, agreed by all key water stakeholders and prioritised with a strong emphasis on environmental compliance, headroom capacity issues and the potential to ease development constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the funding allocation; (ii) the number of service users; (iii) the total journey numbers; and (iv) the cost per journey for Rural Community Transport organisations in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department provides grant support to Rural Community Transport Partnerships to deliver the rural Dial-a-Lift scheme and some Partnerships provide the Disability Action Transport Scheme which is supported by grant, in their operational areas on behalf of Disability Action.Information is only available by each organisation’s distinct operational area and cannot be mapped against Health and Social Care Trust areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of pharmacies that will cease to offer free delivery of prescriptions as of September 2024.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) on behalf of the Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan (2022-25). Prescription delivery is not part of the Commissioning Plan and is not a service that is commissioned by SPPG from community pharmacies. SPPG, therefore does not hold information on the number of pharmacies that will cease to offer free delivery of prescriptions from September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department’s roadworks schedule for (i) Newry; and (ii) Armagh will be finalised.
answer_text: The 2024-25 work programmes are currently being finalised and will be included in my Department’s Annual Roads Reports to Councils. The work programmes for the Newry area will be included in the Newry, Mourne & Down District Council Report which is currently planned to be presented to Council on 23 September 2024.The work programmes for the Armagh area will be included in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council Report which is currently planned to be presented to Council on 28 October 2024. Following the presentation to Council, each Report will be published on the Department’s website and issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the Irish government’s announcement to establish a statutory inquiry into historic abuse at schools run by religious orders, whether her Department will launch an similar inquiry.
answer_text: Historic abuse at schools run by religious orders is part of the work being taken forward by an Interdepartmental Working Group (IDWG) that is responsible for investigating, and commissioning research to examine the extent and systemic nature of Historical Clerical Child Abuse (HCCA) in Northern Ireland. The purpose of the research work is to support the IDWG to provide informed, evidence-based recommendations to Ministers on any future action required in regard to non-recent child abuse within the faith sector. Three research projects have been commissioned:A.	A review of what records are held within the faith sector and by criminal justice agencies and social services, to understand how they responded to historical allegations of clerical abuse within the faith sector; B.	A review of Victim and Survivors experience and expectations; and  C.	A review of current safeguarding practices within the faith sector.The research is a first step in determining what further action may be required. The recommendations will enable Ministers to decide on any future action required, including any future action required for an effective investigation into HCCA and possible remedies for victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when works will commence on the Cregganbane Road, Newry.
answer_text: My officials in Southern Division will confirm the planned programme of works for the Newry, Mourne, and Down area as part of my Department's annual report to council on 23rd September 2024.The Report will be published on the Department’s internet site in an accessible format to meet the requirements of Section 75 and will also be placed in the Assembly library shortly after the presentation to Council to allow access by MLAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department could follow the actions taken by the Irish government in relation to river drainage basin protection and management; and (ii) for his assessment of the consequences of continued inaction from his Department for rivers and water courses.
answer_text: I am aware that the Water Action Plan 2024 - A River Basin Management Plan for Ireland was published on 5 September 2024.  Although still in draft, Northern Ireland’s third cycle River Basin Management Plan has been prepared and consulted on. However, I will need time to review it in the context of emerging information on water quality, including reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office, the Office for Environmental Protection and the Lough Neagh Report, before bringing it to the Executive for approval. I am aware of the urgency of this issue and will complete this work as quickly as possible. 	I do not agree that there has been continued inaction from my department. Although the River Basin Management Plan remains in draft, work has continued on the delivery of my Department’s Soil Nutrient Health Scheme and the launch of both SEUPB’s (Special EU Programmes Body) PEACEPLUS call for ‘Water quality & catchment management’ and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s annual ‘Water Quality Improvement Strand’ grant. In addition, NIEA has held a number of working group meetings for key stakeholders. I have also made it a priority to drive forward implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan which incorporates a range of actions to improve water quality across all our water bodies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13467/22-27, for an update on her engagement with insurance industry bodies in relation to insurance costs.
answer_text: I met with insurance industry bodies on 3 September 2024 to directly discuss my concerns around increased insurance premiums faced by consumers here. I challenged the industry bodies on the scale of the price increases reported, which they suggested are being driven by a number of different factors. Some of which they argue are being experienced globally due to inflationary pressures in the industry, whilst others are more localised to the North. In addition, concerns were discussed regarding the number of providers either exiting the local market or withdrawing some products here. I reinforced the need for the industry to do all it can to control price rises, and also to support communities and businesses facing financial pressures.While neither my Department nor the Assembly has any legislative authority in relation to insurance, I have a role in bringing matters of concern here locally to the British Treasury and regulatory authorities in London. Therefore, following the meeting, my officials have now re-engaged with the British Treasury and the Financial Conduct Authority to seek their assessment of the issues raised at that meeting; and to ask what actions they are taking to address those within their regulatory area of responsibility

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why retired firefighters have not received the pension uplift awarded in April 2024; and (ii) when they will receive their pension uplift and associated back payment.
answer_text: Historically the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) have processed the pension increase in July or August of each year, which included any arrears owed since the April increase was confirmed.  NIFRS has been undergoing a period of significant change with the pension’s administration and undertook a project to outsource to HSC Pensions’ Service.  The final stages of this project involved the transfer of the pension payroll to ensure that they would have a single, automated, end-to-end pension administration with improved resilience and support for their Firefighters and Pensioners. This will reduce the risk of human error from manual calculations of increases and arrears and provide a more efficient service for the member in the longer term.  The pensioner payroll migrated across in February 2024.There was a need to complete additional data cleansing and reconciliation as well as testing of the software application to ensure the pension increase was accurately implemented. Unfortunately there have been some technical issues with the software that has delayed the project and NIFRS have been working with the software supplier to resolve these issues and administer the increase. NIFRS administrator has advised them that the fixes required to the system have now been delivered to their test environment and they have commenced the rigorous testing required prior to moving and processing the increase in the live system.HSC Pension Service have advised they plan to apply the increase this month and issue and pay this along with any arrears due in payments scheduled for the 1st October 2024.  This will be conditional on the successful testing of the software and NIFRS are keeping Pensioners updated through the website.  It is NIFRS aim, once they have the process implemented, to process the pension increase much earlier than they have been doing historically but they unfortunately have to go through the process of a new system implementation to achieve this.Please be assured that NIFRS are doing all they can to implement the pension increase as quickly as possible and prevent any future delays.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13467/22-27, for an update on her engagement with (i) the Treasury; and (ii) the Financial Conduct Authority in relation to insurance costs.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 14370/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 13703/22-27 and AQW 7873/22-27, to detail what representations she has made with the new Labour government following its pledge to repeal the Legacy Act.
answer_text: I have met with the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and have made him aware of my concerns about the impact on victims and survivors, and their families, of the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023 as well as its potential impact on the justice system.  I have also raised those concerns with the Prime Minister.I note the Secretary of State’s recent statement on consulting with victims, survivors and families as part of the Government’s commitment to repeal and replace the Act and I have asked to be kept apprised of his plans for future legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any discussions he had with Executive colleagues in relation to the continued provision of the Winter Fuel Allowance; and (ii) any options that have been discussed with Executive colleagues in relation to mitigation and divergence from the UK government announcement.
answer_text: I engaged in urgent discussions with Executive colleagues following the Chancellor’s statement on 29 July 2024, sharing an Executive paper with them that detailed the estimated costs and IT implications for breaking parity on the proposed changes to the Winter Fuel Payment scheme. The Executive Committee subsequently decided to maintain parity with England, Scotland and Wales and bring forward the changes in Northern Ireland.  Given the Barnett consequential arising from the announcement on Monday 2 September 2024 by Work and Pensions Secretary, Liz Kendall MP, to extend the Household Support Fund in GB, I am seeking Executive colleagues’ agreement that any additional money allocated to the NI Block should be provided to my department. This will help ensure that vulnerable older people in Northern Ireland receive assistance with essential costs this winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when works on the Ballynaclosha Road, Newry, will commence.
answer_text: My Department has completed the design for the replacement wall at Ballynaclosha Road. Negotiations with landowners to secure the necessary land are ongoing and a resolution to some legal issues is also being sought. Subject to resolution of these matters, my Department hopes to be able to commence works this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the benefits of establishing a strategic outdoor recreation group.
answer_text: A Strategic Outdoor Recreation Group was established as part of the Outdoor Recreation Action Plan.  This Action Plan is currently being reviewed with a view to it being updated.  Any future structures will be determined as part of this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the funding allocated to building social housing in each of the last ten years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14086/22-27, for an update on his engagement with the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media in relation to the extension of Fáilte Ireland’s regional branding to bring (i) Ireland’s Ancient East; (ii) the Wild Atlantic Way; and (iii) Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands to the north.
answer_text: Following my engagement with Minister Martin from the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media, a new All Island Strategic Tourism Group has been established. This group will take forward consideration of the extension of Fáilte Ireland’s regional branding, and other key priority areas of co-ordination for the tourism sector. Its membership comprises officials from my Department, the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media and the Chief Executive Officers of Tourism Ireland, Tourism Northern Ireland and Fáilte Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the recommendations made by Parent Talk in the August 2024 briefing in relation to work being done by his Department to improve access to education.
answer_text: Recommendations from the Parent Talk briefing would be primarily for the Department of Education to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current number of commissioned beds at Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) whether there has been any discussion between his Department and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on a reduction in commissioned bed numbers at Daisy Hill Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of recent reports that MI5 has discovered new documents in relation to Operation Kenova, which were not previously disclosed; and (ii) to detail any action she will take in relation to this development.
answer_text: It is no doubt distressing for victims’ families that MI5 has discovered new documents not previously disclosed to Operation Kenova. I hope that all material has now been handed over and can assist the investigation in providing the answers families seek. I have no responsibility or governance role in relation to Operation Kenova or MI5. It will be for others to take any actions considered necessary. However, I would urge all relevant parties with information pertaining to the investigation to fully disclose the material they have.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14187/22-27, (i) how his Department assesses the safety status of illegally erected political billboards on departmental land; and (ii) who is responsible for any injuries sustained as a result of illegally erected political billboards.
answer_text: When considering the risk posed by billboards, my Department considers a range of factors related to road safety risk including, whether the billboard causes any impediment to vehicles and/or pedestrians travelling along a road, whether there is any obstruction of sightlines, and the likelihood of an errant vehicle striking the billboard. In terms of responsibility in the event of injury, it would be for the Courts to decide where any subsequent liability rests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the initiatives taken; and (ii) the resources allocated by his Department to promote access to Newry Canal for boats and sailing vessels in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Newry Canal is owned by Newry, Mourne and Down Council and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Council. Initiatives to promote access to Newry Canal for boats and sailing vessels and the allocation of resources for this work may rest with these Councils to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any actions her Department is taking to cease (i) the existence of paramilitary groups and criminal gangs; and (ii) any acts that glorify paramilitary groups and criminal gangs, both past and present
answer_text: The Department of Justice hosts the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), an evidence-based approach to violence and harm reduction, supporting communities and individuals who are vulnerable to paramilitary influence and harm across Northern Ireland. This objective is a long-term endeavour that requires action across the Executive. EPPOC measures progress across its circa100 interventions, measuring positive change across 18 areas, such as major disruptions to criminal groups, increases in factors that protect those vulnerable to criminal exploitation and reductions in risk and complex trauma. EPPOC’s work is also driving systems change in relation to trauma informed approaches and child criminal exploitation. Examples of impact include: over 1200 victims of extreme paramilitary violence supported through bespoke projects; hundreds of boys and young men at risk of paramilitary harm and/or involvement supported through social work and bespoke mentoring; a downward trend in the number of intimidations and in the types of paramilitary activity including murder, violent assaults and shootings; and 13,000 individuals involved in place-based initiatives. Further information and extensive research on the work of the Programme is available at www.endingtheharm.com and with findings detailed in the Independent Reporting Commission’s 6th report (December 2023) and the Northern Ireland Affairs Committee report: ‘The effect of paramilitary activity and organised crime on society in Northern Ireland’ (January 2024). EPPOC works in tandem with my Department’s Organised Crime Branch and with a range of operational partners through the Organised Crime Task Force.  Operational activities to disrupt criminal groups are supported by policy and legislative work carried out by my Department, and work is underway to bring forward new serious organised crime legislation to provide law enforcement agencies with the capabilities to bring those engaged in organised crime at all levels before the courts.  In addition, over the last year, I ensured that new provisions under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 were extended to Northern Ireland, to enable the investigation and recovery of proceeds of crime facilitated by cryptoassets, adding to available powers to disrupt the activities of criminal groups and prevent illegal financial gain.   	There is currently no Executive or UK Government funding in place for EPPOC beyond March 2025. A paper seeking the Executive’s agreement to continuing to support this important work has been produced and I hope it will be considered in September. 	With regard to acts glorifying paramilitary and criminal gangs, I am on the record as stating that so called ‘shows of strength’, and paramilitary trappings have no place in our society. There is nothing about this type of activity that demonstrates strength – all this is shows is desperation. Communities want to get on with their lives, not be associated with thi

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what proportion of the landmass has adequate 3G and 4G connectivity to sustain a WhatsApp call; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact this has upon economic competitiveness.
answer_text: As you are aware, Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) holding the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters ensuring that all local considerations are front and centre of any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity here.DfE understands the many challenges faced by our communities in terms of digital connectivity and indeed the issues faced by Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) when they attempt to roll out telecommunications infrastructure. My officials are involved in several ongoing initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here and help improve our economic competitiveness. The Shared Rural Network (SRN) is a project developed by the four MNOs (EE, Vodafone, VMO2 and Three) and the British Government. Locally, the SRN is expected to deliver 85% 4G coverage from all four MNOs and 98% coverage from at least one MNO.The SRN is expected to extend 4G coverage in the most cost-effective, efficient, timely and environmentally friendly way for consumers and businesses in these remote rural communities. My officials participate in the local SRN Working Group to provide a local perspective. Ofcom is expected to validate any newly declared MNO reported improvements under their SRN commitments later this year. More information about the SRN can be found at https://srn.org.uk.The Department for the Economy does not hold information on the proportion of the local landmass that has adequate 3G and 4G connectivity to sustain a WhatsApp call. Information on current coverage is published by Ofcom. Their latest report can be found at Nations reports - Ofcom and individual postcodes can be checked on their View mobile availability - Ofcom Checker. MNOs are in the process of switching off their 2G and 3G networks to release capacity for 4G and 5G networks which offer a faster and more reliable connection. MNOs are responsible for communicating these changes and any impact to their customers directly. Further information can be found at Switching off the UK’s 2G and 3G mobile networks: what you need to know - Ofcom which includes switch off dates for each of the four MNOs. Any service issues should be reported directly by customers to their provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a detailed breakdown of the engagement he has had with UEFA regarding the redevelopment of Casement Park for the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.
answer_text: I have not had any direct engagement with UEFA regarding the redevelopment of Casement Park for the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a detailed breakdown of their engagement with UEFA regarding the redevelopment of Casement Park for the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.
answer_text: We have had no engagement with UEFA officials regarding the redevelopment of Casement Park for the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will meet with me and relevant staff to discuss the drive-through phlebotomy service in Armagh.
answer_text: All information regarding the closure of the drive-through phlebotomy service in Armagh has been previously outlined and responded to within AQW 13354/22-27. The realignment of the service is regrettable; however it is a necessary step in managing the financial pressures that we find ourselves in 2024/25. I am assured that the renewed service will continue to operate at a level where all necessary testing is available and that guarantees patient safety, which of course is my primary concern. In that case I do not think it would be constructive to meet to discuss this matter as I am not in a position to revise this decision. I understand that this will come as a disappointment to you and your constituents and I thank you for the continued interest and engagement that you have in these matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures he is adopting to facilitate the removal of illegally erected political billboards on his Department's land.
answer_text: The illegal erection of any signage along the public road or on other structures such as lamp posts is an offence under The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993. Furthermore, such signage can be a distraction to motorists and as such can have significant road safety implications.  The more signs which are in place the greater risk.Hoardings are normally the subject of a planning application, and any infringements of planning laws would normally be pursued under the relevant planning legislation which in most cases would fall to the relevant District Council.Where unauthorised signage poses a clear safety risk, my Department will prioritise its removal, however, where there is no such danger, my officials will liaise closely with other key stakeholders to provide a solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the decision by the Prison Service to withdraw Irish language teaching provision in prisons.
answer_text: Provision of prison educational programmes is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Prison Service. As such they are the responsibility of the Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service.I can, however, confirm that Irish Language has not been removed from the current curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the advice his Department offers; and (ii) the support that is available to housing associations and contractors who are experiencing vandalism of housing units and machinery during the process of constructing new social and affordable housing.
answer_text: My Department offers no specific advice or support to Housing Associations and contractors in regard to vandalism of housing units and machinery during the construction phase of new social and affordable housing.    The Northern Ireland Housing Executive is responsible for the day-to-day management of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) and Housing Associations should engage with them directly regarding any site-specific issues which may impact upon scheme delivery. Contractors should engage with the Housing Association which has contracted their services in line with the terms of the contract.  My Department does not provide funding for the construction of new build affordable homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average referral time to an educational psychologist in each of the last five years, broken down by Trust area.
answer_text: Direct referrals to the Education Authority (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) are made for children at stage two of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice. The EPS calculates data from 1 September to 31 August of each year.  The table below provides average times for Stage 2 assessment from date of referral to date of first contact and are correct as of 3 July 2024.  The EPS does not operate by Trust areas so data is provided according to five EA localities.ArmaghBallymenaBelfastDundonaldOmagh2019/2087 days71 days63 days61 days81 days2020/2163 days62 days45 days42 days62 days2021/2271 days51 days46 days43 days64 days2022/2360 days44 days56 days35 days63 daysYear to date47 days40 days41 days24 days59 days

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average time taken to place a child with a statement of special educational need in a new placement where deemed necessary following completion of an annual review, in each of the last five years, broken down by Trust area.
answer_text: Neither my department nor the Education Authority (EA) hold this data.  If a child is recommended for a change of placement following Annual Review, that change and the associated consultation process is individual to that child and ensures children continue to access appropriate special education provision to meet their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of (i) the duration of the statutory assessment process; and (ii) its impact on children without a current special educational needs placement.
answer_text: The timescale for the assessment of children with special educational needs (SEN) is set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, underpinned by the Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs (SEN) 1998.  This is currently 26 weeks. The Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and associated regulations propose a reduction of the statutory assessment timescale to 22 weeks.  It is unlikely that this proposed reduction in timescales would have had an impact on the Education Authority’s (EA) ability to place children as the main issue for placements is the lack of additional classroom capacity and specialist provision pathways across the school estate in specific localities of pressure. My department continues to work in conjunction with the EA to ensure that the remaining children requiring a placement for September 2024 are placed as soon as practicably possible.  Further engagement with schools to increase capacity will continue in the new term to improve the placements process for September 2025 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why an update on the inclusion of counties in the north in (i) Ireland’s Ancient East; (ii) the Wild Atlantic Way; and (iii) Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands was not contained in his statement to the Assembly on the North/South Ministerial Council tourism meeting on 2 July 2024.
answer_text: This issue was not included on the official Agenda for the NSMC Tourism Sectoral meeting which took place on 24 June 2024. Therefore, it was not referenced in my Statement to the Assembly on 2 July 2024.I met separately with Minister Martin from the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media DTCAGSM on 24 June 2024 to discuss extension of Failte Ireland’s regional branding to the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the engagement he has had with professionals in the sectors affected by the Defective Premises Bill since the restoration of the Assembly; and (ii) the feedback from this engagement.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Assembly on 3rd February 2024 I have engaged with the following bodies which are affected by the Defective Premises Bill:Representatives of Apex Housing Association, the Chartered Institute of Housing, the Braidwater Group, and McAdam Design;Grahams Ltd.Choice Housing and H&J Martin.Ulster Garden Villages.Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations.In addition to the above, I addressed the issue of residential building safety as part of my keynote address at the Housing NI conference.In general, while the industry has welcomed the move to address the disparity highlighted by the Victoria Square situation, it has expressed concerns on a number of issues, namely the retrospective nature of the Bill, the perceived lack of consultation with industry before introducing the Bill to the Assembly, the availability of appropriate insurance products, and the application of strict liability should cases be brought for older defects that have subsequently rendered a dwelling uninhabitable.  My officials will continue to engage with all stakeholders and monitor the impact of the Defective Premises Bill as part of their work to develop residential building safety policy and legislation for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of the engagement she has had with the Chief Constable since the restoration of the Assembly in relation to (i) financial resourcing; (ii) human resourcing; and (iii) reducing processing times within the Rape Crime Unit.
answer_text: While I do not have a breakdown of my engagement with the Chief Constable, I can confirm that I have engaged regularly with him on a wide range of policing issues including discussions on financial and human resourcing within the PSNI.   I have not had any specific engagement with the Chief Constable in relation to reducing processing times within the Rape Crime Unit, as this is an operational matter for which the Chief Constable is responsible to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am, however, acutely aware of the challenges in relation to this and my Department will continue to work with the PSNI in the implementation of Gillen Review recommendations, several of which are aimed at reducing delay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures he has put in place to support students deferring their (i) GCSE; and (ii) A-level exams, who have dropped out of education due to physical or mental health difficulties.
answer_text: The Education Authority has a dedicated Education Welfare Service (EWS) which helps young people of school age and their families to get the best out of the education system. Its aim is to promote a culture of regular attendance at school.  Education Welfare Officers (EWOs) work proactively with schools to raise awareness of attendance issues using various strategies.If for health-related reasons a parent decides their young person is unable to sit their exams in a given year, they should discuss this with their child’s school. It is possible to take GCSE and A level exams in subsequent examination series, however schools are best-placed to provide specific support and guidance for pupils in terms of meeting their individual needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the current funding shortfall for the Casement Park project; and (ii) for an update on (a) the funding allocated by the Executive; and (b) the amount requested from the British government to ensure completion of Casement Park before the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.
answer_text: As Minister with overall responsibility for the Casement Park project, your question to the Minister of Finance has been passed to me to respond to. Due to commercial sensitivities, I cannot provide an update on the estimated costs to redevelop Casement Park at this time. Therefore, I cannot go into detail on any shortfall that may exist. The current allocation of Executive funding towards the redevelopment of Casement Park is £62.5 million. I am currently awaiting confirmation from the UK Government on the timing and quantum of any funding commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any engagement he has had with (i) his Executive colleagues; and (ii) his UK counterpart in relation to securing the necessary funding to ensure completion of the Casement Park project before the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.
answer_text: Following the UK Government announcement on 13 September 2024 that it would not be providing funding to seek to build Casement Park in time to host matches at Euro 2028, my department no longer has a direct role in the EURO 2028 stadium hosting strategy. However, I am engaging with all partners to see how Northern Ireland can still benefit from playing a role in the Euro 2028 tournament.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of the total number of individuals incarcerated for (i) Class A; (ii) Class B; and (iii) Class C drug offences, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Over the past 9 years (2015-2023) there were 4,095 individuals incarcerated into custody for drug-related offences.  Due to the data retention policy of the management information system PRISM, it is not possible to provide data for the year 2014.The below table breaks down the number of individuals committed into custody by committal year and by class of drug offence. Committal YearClass A drug offenceClass B drug offenceClass C drug offence201514335614020161854411802017218404180201829254827220193405734012020275438383202127551641020222765104042023348568490Source: PRISM – Management InformationTable Notes:Individuals could be committed into prison for offences in more than one drug class, therefore, the sum of each row does not equate to the number of individuals committed for drug offences for that specific year.Individuals who were committed for drug offences but the drugs class could not be determined have been excluded from this table.If an individual has been committed in more than one year then they will be counted for each year they have been committed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the progress of the new capital build for St. Joseph and St. James's Primary School, Poyntzpass; and (ii) when the project is scheduled for completion.
answer_text: Since the project was announced under the Major Capital Programme in 2013 a number of site options have been considered for this five-class base primary school. Unfortunately, it has not been possible to secure an alternative site within the school’s catchment area and as a result the Integrated Consultant Team (ICT) is currently preparing a Technical Feasibility Report (TFR) which will examine feasible options based on the school’s existing site.  It is anticipated that the TFR will be lodged with the Department for consideration in Autumn 2024. A business case will be drafted thereafter which will identify the preferred option to be progressed.        Until the preferred option is identified and approved it is not possible to provide further details of the project or provide a project programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits of making adjustments to the physical infrastructure of (i) Ardmore Police Station, Newry; and (ii) Armagh Police Station to reflect the modern society in which the stations are located.
answer_text: The Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, has operational independence in the prioritisation of policing resources, including the PSNI’s physical infrastructure. I support the Chief Constable’s operational independence and remain committed to working with him to ensure that we have a police service that is, in all aspects, properly resourced for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the (i) enhanced flood walls in Newry city centre; (ii) flood alleviation at Greenbank; and (iii) upstream floodwater storage, Newry.
answer_text: My Department are currently progressing a study to continue with identifying flood risk in Newry and assess the viability of further potential flood alleviation measures to address this. This includes consideration of additional flood walls through Newry City Centre and the potential need for upstream storage. It is expected that an overall preferred option will be identified in Summer 2024. The project team are also currently engaging with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council with a view to potentially accelerating a section of the proposed Flood Alleviation works through the Council’s Theatre and Conference Facility project. Flood alleviation measures which will reduce the risk of excessive rainfall flooding at Greenbank industrial estate were completed in January 2023 as Part of Phase 1 of the Newry Flood Alleviation Scheme. It should be noted that the benefit of these works will not be fully realised until the completion of the rest of the Newry Flood Alleviation Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to facilitating the addition of Year 11 and Year 12 to St. John the Baptist's College, Portadown.
answer_text: On 26 November 2020, the Education Authority (EA) published Development Proposal (DP) 646 proposing that ‘St John the Baptist’s College, Portadown will establish Key Stage 4 provision with an annual phased increase in the enrolment number from 300 to 500 with effect from 1 September 2021 or as soon as possible thereafter.’  The proposal was approved on 19 April 2021.The school currently has approved admissions and enrolment numbers of 100 and 490 respectively.  In 2023/2024, St. John the Baptist College had an actual enrolment of 524 pupils in Years 8-12, with 200 pupils enrolled in Years 11 and 12.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the timeline for the publication of the Newry flood review; and (ii) his assessment of the merits of incorporating (a) a warning system; and (b) communication improvements as part of that review.
answer_text: Following the significant flooding that occurred in Autumn 2023 across the South and East, an independent consultant, Jacobs, was appointed to assist and provide an independent technical insight into the causes of the flooding and to present considerations to inform any viable solutions. This final report, which has taken into account feedback from the public open days held in Portadown, Downpatrick and Newry is nearing completion and will be published late July 2024.The need to provide information around possible flood warnings is becoming more important given the impacts of climate change. Whilst my Department believes that the North would benefit from an enhanced flood forecasting system, it is at present not currently funded to provide this service. Whilst there would be finance required to establish and maintain such a system, there are other factors to be considered to ensure that any flood forecasting system here reflects our unique river catchment status whilst building on the already well-established strong network of community resilience groups. The development of a flood forecasting system was raised by many agencies during the stakeholder engagement sessions as part of the flooding review, and as such it will be included as part of the considerations of the report. My officials have already held discussions with other organisations who have flood risk management responsibilities across neighbouring administrations, and who have already developed their own forecasting systems, to ascertain if a similar approach could be developed for the North. This is at an early stage and is estimated that the costs would be approximately £2m per year with around three years to develop.  Some of the findings included in the draft report, and from the open day feedback, are around communication in respect of flood reporting, insurance, financial assistance and working with communities in areas of flood risk. My Department, along with other multi-agency partners, will seek to address those areas of work which can be acted upon reasonably quickly. In addition, my Department, as the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to significant flooding, will continue to work with partner organisations to ensure that so far as possible pre-planned arrangements are in place to help mitigate against any future flooding events.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on when a Programme for Government will be agreed and published.
answer_text: We are pleased to advise that the draft Programme for Government was published on Monday 9th September. This programme sets out the proposed priorities of the Executive for this mandate. These priorities include: Growing a Globally Competitive Economy; Reducing Health Waiting Lists; Delivering More Affordable Childcare; Ending Violence against women and girls; Better Support for Children and Young People with Special Educational Needs; Providing More Social, Affordable and Sustainable Housing; Protecting Lough Neagh and the Environment; having Safer Communities; and stabilising and transforming public services.A public consultation on the draft programme will run for 8 weeks, and 12 weeks for the accompanying impact assessments. This began on Monday 9th September. We invite everyone to have their say and get involved in this consultation and, once the consultation is complete, we will then agree a clear set of priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the engagement he has had with Translink in relation to the provision of bus and rail services for GAA fixtures.
answer_text: While the provision of services is an operational matter for Translink, I have tasked officials to contact Translink to establish what plans they have in place for that weekend given the significant demand there will be for their services. Translink have confirmed that they have put additional services on, and details are available on-line.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total number of road surface defects; and (ii) when these defects are scheduled be addressed, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Road surface defect data dating back to 2008/2009 is regularly updated and publicly available on the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects.  The data is updated each evening and can be downloaded in various formats.  The information includes: spatial location details, road section name, local district council, surface defect type (e.g. potholes, cracking, depressions, rutting), date recorded, defect status, target response time, and actual completion date.   It is important to note that there can be a time lag between when each surface defect record is repaired and when this is updated on the maintenance management system.  Repair work is also only carried out subject to a defect meeting our current intervention levels set out in the ‘Limited Service’ policy, (see Director of Engineering memorandum ‘Road maintenance standards for safety – limited service': https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/network-maintenance).You will also find my Department’s official statistics on the condition of the road network published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work his Department is carrying out in relation to improving dementia care.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system in Northern Ireland, with the region projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040. My Department recognises the importance of fostering an ethos of ongoing improvement on all issues pertaining to dementia services across Northern Ireland.  My Department contiues to monitor memory service activity data via the Service Delivery Plan, reporting process on a regular basis.  Further to this, my Department monitors Trust activity against Directed Statuory Functions, and regularly reviews Trust performance.My officials meet formally with the Dementia Service Improvement Leads in each of the 5 Health and Social Care Trusts on a regular basis, they also meet with the Senior Management teams within the Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to their performance.  These meetings give my department the opportunity to discuss, with the Trusts, any issues or barriers impacting on their performance and actions being taken to improve Trust performance. My Department has recently established a Regional Dementia Project Board in order to bring a sharpened focus on improvement in demenita care and services across the region.  This group had their inaugral meeting on 23rd May 2024 and a further meeting on 24th June 2024.  Key areas of focus for this group will be the need to improve collection of purposeful data, including sub type diagnosis rates and the need to focus on system readiness for new Disease Modifying Treatments. Task and finish groups have been proposed to address the issues and formulate an improvement methodology.  Three groups have been established: 1) Education and Training, 2) System Readiness and 3) Dementia Diagnosis and Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for a breakdown of (a) the health spend per capita; and (b) the social care spend per capita, for individuals with a diagnosis of dementia in each of the last ten years; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made of how this spend compares with (a) England; (b) Scotland; and (c) Wales.
answer_text: Our costing information does not capture health or social care spend per capita for individuals with a diagnosis of dementia.The London School of Economics and Political Science in November 2019 conducted an assessment into the total costs of dementia care across the 4 Nations of the UK. The total costs of dementia in the UK in 2019 amount to £34.7 billion at 2015 prices. England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland are £29.5 billion, £2.6 billion, £1.8 billion, and £0.8 billion, respectively. These total costs include all those associated with supporting older people living with dementia rather than the extra costs attributable specifically to dementia itself.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his engagement with the Irish government in relation to the inclusion of County Armagh and County Down in Ireland’s Ancient East.
answer_text: I met Minister Martin on 24 June and discussed regional branding, including Ireland’s Ancient East and the inclusion of County Armagh and County Down in this brand. Officials have been tasked with examining further how Failte Ireland branding can be extended to include regions in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the current attrition rates within teaching; and (ii) what steps his Department is taking to ameliorate those attrition rates.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is not the employer of teachers or principals.  The response to AQW 13812/22-27 should be read in conjunction with the response to AQW 13813/22-27.The number of full-time equivalent (FTE) teachers is 19,893 which is an increase of 33.6 from 19,860 in 2022/23.The most recent teacher headcount is 21,513, which represents an increase of 97 teachers from 2022/23.Based on these figures the attrition rate for teachers, including principals is approximately 1.22%.The Department is working with the employing authorities and education sectoral support bodies on strategic workforce planning and will be using this to identify and address any areas of concern in the recruitment and retention of teaching staff.A three year pay agreement has recently been implemented which included a significant increase to the starting salary for teachers of £30k. This will support the recruitment and retention of teachers. The Department is also involved, through the Teachers’ Negotiation Committee (TNC), in devising a programme of work relating to the workforce reviews to improve the terms and conditions and workload for both teachers and school leaders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) school principals; (ii) teachers; and (iii) education support staff that have resigned from their positions without taking up a new post in an education setting in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is not the employer of teachers, principals or education support staff.  To obtain this data my officials have asked the employing authorities and the Department’s payroll team to provide information and the responses are shown below.Principal and Teaching StaffThe table below details the number of teachers that have resigned from teaching and not returned to teaching since their resignation. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of applications made to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme, including (i) applications processed to date; (iii) applications rejected; and (iv) applications awaiting a decision.
answer_text: The Enhanced Food Support Scheme is being administered by the District Councils.  Based on information provided by councils:There were 110 applications to the Enhanced flood Support Scheme (102 from businesses in NMDDC and 8 from businesses in ABCBC). All 110 applications have been processed.15 applications have been rejected to date.57 Businesses have received Letters of offer totalling circa £3 million.18 applications have been put on hold pending the submission of additional information to council.20 applications are awaiting a decision (10 of these are from businesses that hold flood insurance and cannot be progressed until the outcome of the insurance claim is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total level of expenditure by his Department on road defect compensation claims, relative to his Department’s overall expenditure on road maintenance; and (ii) the total human resource allocation in his Department required to administer road defect compensation claims, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below details my Department’s total expenditure on roads related public liability claims in each of the last five financial years. These figures include expenditure on compensation and other related costs. Roads Claims Unit processes and administers roads related public liability claims on behalf of my Department. The table, therefore, details the salary costs in each of the last five financial years for Roads Claims Unit only.The table also details the total expenditure on structural maintenance in each of last five financial years.
