question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the representations he has made to the British government in relation to the Shared Rural Network and mobile services in (i) Newry; and (ii) South Armagh.
answer_text: My officials engage with the NI Shared Rural Network (SRN) Working Group to make representations and provide a local perspective. They receive regular updates from all areas of the programme catchment and individual Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) on implementation progress. My officials have facilitated closer working relationships with local stakeholders to aid delivery, through the SRN Working Group and wider barrier busting taskforce which will benefit the telecommunication industry beyond SRN as they continue to improve coverage here.Locally we will benefit from the mobile communications industry funded partial not-spot (PNS) element of the SRN Programme which will see 4G coverage here increase to 85% from all four MNOs and 98% coverage from at least one MNO. Progress against targets are measured by Ofcom. Mobile coverage obligations - Ofcom provides information about licence obligations and methodology and the Connected Nations Spring 2024: Interactive report - Ofcom provides latest coverage information and SRN progress. Connected Nations 2023 - Northern Ireland report - Ofcom provides specific local information.More information about the SRN can be found at SRN.Org.UK and MNOs are willing to provide more specific information on request.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the internet access service in Newry city, where people are unable to make internet calls and download speeds are slow.
answer_text: Information available to my Department indicates that 98% of premises in Newry City can access a gigabit-capable broadband service.  Project Gigabit is expected to benefit premises that are unable to access gigabit-capable broadband services and are not part of suppliers’ commercial deployment plans, subject to final approvals in advance of procurement activities. My Department has published an address checker at https://www.online.economy-ni.gov.uk/ProjectGigabit/, which allows citizens and businesses to check the status of their premises. Please note, the intervention area for Project Gigabit has not been finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how often (i) police officers, and (ii) Public Prosecution Service Serious Crime Unit staff undertake training from Nexus on response to rape and sex crimes.
answer_text: I warmly welcome the news that over 500 frontline police officers and members of the Public Prosecution Service’s Serious Crime Unit have recently received training from Nexus to better inform their response to rape and sexual crime.However, the nature and frequency of the training delivered to the PSNI and PPS are operational matters for the Chief Constable and the Director of the Public Prosecution Service respectively. You may therefore wish to direct your question to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 7873/22-27, for an update on her representations to the Secretary of State in relation to (i) the practical operation of the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information; and (ii) the interface between the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information and her Department.
answer_text: I continue to make representations to the UK Government and to seek clarity on how the costs of servicing the ICRIR are to be met. It is unclear at present what impact a change of Government might have following the General Election.My Department has had some limited engagement to date with the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information Recovery.Following a request for a meeting from the Commissioner for Investigations, I met with Peter Sheridan, who updated me on the development of the Commission and its operating model. The Commission has also requested a meeting with my officials, which is due to take place this month.I am aware the Commission has been engaging directly with the PSNI and Police Ombudsman on how those interfaces will be managed. There has also been some preliminary engagement with the Legacy Inquests Unit within the Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service and we have reached an agreement that Forensic Science NI can provide forensic services to ICRIR on a full cost-recovery basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of primary school applicants currently without a special educational needs placement for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: It is assumed the member is referring to the number of primary one children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) without a confirmed school placement for the 2024/25 academic year.The legislative duty to place children with SEN rests with the Education Authority (EA) as outlined in the Education (Northern Ireland) orders 1986 and 1996. According to information shared with my department, as of 4 July 2024, there were 1,497 children with a statement of SEN who required a primary one placement for September 2024.  1,338 (89.4%) of these children have a confirmed placement for primary one; 159 are awaiting confirmation of their primary one placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the cost of the response operation in relation to the security alert on Monaghan Street, Newry, on 20 June 2024.
answer_text: I want to take this opportunity to condemn those behind this and other similar security alerts. The cost to our community of such disruption extends beyond the direct security response to each incident, impacting on local businesses, disrupting residents and causing fear, alarm and distress in the community. With respect to the PSNI response to the security alert on Monaghan Street, Newry, on 20 June 2024 and its associated costs, these are operational matters for the Chief Constable to address. I have made clear that the Chief Constable is operationally independent from my Department and is accountable to the Policing Board. As I intend to respect this independence and the role of the Policing Board, you may wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the implications for community groups of the cessation of access to European Social Funding; and (ii) what revenue streams his Department has made available to replace access to European Social Funding.
answer_text: While my Department did provide match funding to a small number of European Social Fund (ESF) projects in 2022/23, the Department for the Economy (DfE) were the Managing Authority for ESF in Northern Ireland (NI). Therefore, my Department has not assessed the implications of the funding ending.The Department of Finance (DoF), supported by all NI Departments, lead on the loss of EU Funding and the introduction of funding streams under the UK Government’s Levelling Up Agenda, including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF).The SPF NI Investment Plan was designed and developed by Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) alongside the Northern Ireland Partnership Group, which included members of the community and voluntary sector.	My Department continues to fund and collaborate with Voluntary and Community organisations in the delivery of a wide range of bespoke employability provision and a range of more general labour market interventions to help remove barriers to employment for those furthest from the labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department plans to launch a consultation to review trust in the justice system.
answer_text: There are no plans to launch a consultation on trust in the justice system.  The Department, however, currently measures confidence in the criminal justice system through the Northern Ireland Safe Community Survey. The survey also measures confidence in police, police accountability, police engagement, perceptions of crime, and perceptions of organised crime.  It is planned to launch a public consultation on the NI Safe Community survey later this year.Results from all surveys are available on the Department’s website: Statistics and Research | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the cost to businesses on Monaghan Street, Newry, who were forced to close due to a security alert on 20 June 2024.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any changes to out-of-hours GP services in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in the last six months.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) is commissioned to provide the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service across the Southern area.  The allocated funding budget provided to the SHSCT has not changed.  The GP OOHs service is for people who need urgent medical treatment but cannot wait until their doctor's practice reopens. The local population is encouraged to utilise the service appropriately to meet any urgent health care needs which cannot wait to be seen by their registered practice when it reopens.  SPPG receive quarterly data from the SHSCT with the most recent data for quarter ending March 2024.  When comparing the data for April 2023 – September 2023 against October 2023 – March 2024 indicated the following:Contacts had increased by 16%GP hours had increased by 0.4%Nurse hours had reduced by 6.8%Pharmacist hours recommenced in October 2023 after a period with no Pharmacist.  Comparing the data for October 2023 – December 2023 against January 2024 – March 2024 indicated a 17 % increase in Pharmacist hours.  The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) are experiencing challenges in providing this service within the allocated budget.  Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) colleagues are working with colleagues in the SHSCT in relation to these challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail any alterations to parking enforcement arrangements in the last calendar year; (ii) to detail the reason for these changes; and (iii) for his assessment of the implications of these parking enforcement changes for motorists.
answer_text: Parking enforcement patrols are designed to focus enforcement in areas of most need to reduce traffic congestion, improve bus journey times and road safety and provide accessibility for all road and pavement users, including Blue Badge holders. Over the last 12 months my Department has continued to work closely with various stakeholders to meet these objectives, including: -Belfast City Council and the PSNI on a new 18-month pilot scheme to tackle parking issues impacting bin collections https://www.belfastcity.gov.uk/News/New-pilot-scheme-to-tackle-parking-issues-which-im.Newry Mourne and Down Council, following the flooding in Newry and Downpatrick in November 2023, to temporarily suspend enforcement in affected areas and extending exemptions to affected premises during their refurbishment.Translink to identify enforcement hotspots especially in Bus lanes and where more focussed enforcement using the mobile CCTV enforcement vehicle would be beneficial.  Both local universities to deal with obstructive and illegal parking such as in the Holylands and Stranmillis areas of Belfast and in the residential areas surrounding the new University of Ulster campus.The PSNI with joint enforcement operations at events e.g. the Lammas Fair to keep blue light corridors free of congestion.In April 2024, eight local councils assumed responsibility for parking enforcement in off-street car parks in their own areas. Subsequently, my Department has completed a review of the Traffic Attendant (TA) patrols in those areas. This has resulted in additional streets added to the TA patrols and/or some streets receiving more frequent visits. For example, in Newry this has resulted in more frequent patrols of the Controlled Parking Zone, loading bays and disabled bays as well as freeing-up more TA capacity to focus on illegal parking in residential areas.  My Department will be monitoring the effectiveness of these changes and will adjust as required. In November 2023, my Department introduced several new parking restrictions to address inconsiderate footway parking. Amendments to legislation now prohibit vehicles from waiting on a footway adjacent to a bus lane, bus stop, bus stand, mandatory cycle lane or mandatory school keep clears. To date, over 400 PCNs have been issued for these new contraventions and feedback from our TAs is that these new restrictions combined with enforcement is having a positive impact on parking behaviour.Finally, Enforcement patrols are specifically developed for each area and are reviewed every six months to ensure they remain proportionate and address the needs of the area. Areas that do not receive a programmed patrol are not ignored. The Department’s parking enforcement approach is flexible and, resource permitting, can be adjusted accordingly. Requests for enforcement can be made to the following email address Roads.parking@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk  at which point enforcement can be programmed in an attempt to change poor parking behaviou

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of placing people on the housing waiting list with homeless points in emergency or temporary accommodation outside their areas of choice.
answer_text: There are far too many households in Temporary Accommodation.  While the Housing Executive will endeavour to place households in temporary accommodation close to their current area, or area of choice, the sustained increase in demand for temporary accommodation means that this is not always possible.In such circumstances, any provision of temporary accommodation will be dictated by availability on a given day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has made any representations to the British government in relation to home insurance costs in Newry following the flooding in October 2023.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 13467/22-27 regarding engagement with the British Government around this issue.In addition, in the absence of the Executive, the British Government agreed with the Department of Finance in November 2023, the reallocation of Block funding to make up to £15 million of support available to respond to areas impacted by flooding in the North. Subsequently, funding was allocated to a number of departments to support businesses, households and communities in impacted areas. This included Flooded Property Relief from within my own department during the 2023/24 rating year in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area, where £805,400 in relief was awarded to 210 non-domestic properties; and 7 affected domestic properties were awarded £3,908 in relief through the Rating of Empty Homes exclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action her Department has taken to prevent and discourage abuse of election candidates, particularly via social media platforms.
answer_text: As you are aware telecommunications legislation (including the regulation of social media platforms) is a reserved matter and the UK Government Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT), is the lead department for the Online Safety Act 2023. Of course I recognise that online safety issues impact on devolved matters, including in respect of criminal justice.I had sought to influence the content of the Online Safety Act 2023, writing to UK Ministers urging more to be done to address the lack of accountability of anonymous account holders which is a key issue when seeking to prevent and detect online harms and offences.  The Act establishes a new regulatory regime to address illegal and harmful content online, imposing legal requirements on providers of internet services.  Services within the scope of the Act must follow certain rules, including protecting users from illegal content and activity online, as well as protecting children from harmful content. Recently I met with representatives of Ofcom, the online regulator, to get an update on the implementation of the Online Safety Act and their plans for it to be put into practice.  Ofcom expects the Act to deliver four outcomes; stronger safety governance in online firms; online services designed and operated with safety in mind; choice for users so they can have meaningful control over their online experiences; and transparency regarding the safety measures services use, and the action Ofcom is taking to improve them.  Ofcom will also provide guidance and set out codes of practice on how in-scope companies can comply with their duties.The Electoral Commission recognises, as I think we all do, that far too many candidates are facing harassment and abuse which can deter candidates from campaigning or standing altogether and in turn significantly impacting voter choice and access to information.  Since its report on the May 2022 Assembly election in Northern Ireland, which highlighted that 50% of candidates reported experiencing threats, abuse and or intimidation, the Electoral Commission has produced joint guidance between the Electoral Commission, the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Electoral Office for Northern Ireland and the Public Prosecution Service to help candidates understand when behaviour goes beyond political debate and may be unlawful. We also hosted a number of roundtable events with the PSNI and MLAs to address the issue from October 2022The Electoral Commission, alongside the Police Service of Northern Ireland and the Jo Cox Foundation, recently called for a campaign free from abuse in Northern Ireland ahead of the upcoming UK Parliamentary general election on July 4. The Commission continues to work closely with the Police Service of Northern Ireland to produce safe campaigning guidelines, raising awareness about the crime, and urging campaigners to educate their teams about this worrying trend.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when the road surface defects on (a) Cladymore Road; (b) Friary Road; and (c) the Clady Road and Blackquarter Road junction, Armagh, will be repaired, and (ii) when road resurfacing schemes will be developed for each of these roads.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations (subject to a consultation on the equality impacts) my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including plans for infrastructure projects across the DfI Section Offices.Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that rising insurance costs are having on the viability of small and medium-sized businesses.
answer_text: While financial services are largely a reserved matter, I am acutely aware from my regular engagement with businesses and their representative bodies, of the cost pressures facing our business community, and specifically of concerns around the rising cost of insurance. This is an issue that can be particularly burdensome for small and medium businesses, and in 2022 the Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) conducted helpful research into the matter, highlighting some of the impacts it can have on small businesses. These include forcing them to reduce their cover, placing less of a focus on business growth and in some cases, the business having to reduce their staffing levels. The report also makes a number of recommendations for the British Government, the insurance industry and the Financial Conduct Authority and I am also aware of, and support, recent efforts of a range of colleagues in the Assembly on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why the tender documents for Casement Park have not yet been issued.
answer_text: I would refer the member to AQW 13351 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions he is taking to address problems that staff in the Driver and Vehicle Agency Passenger Transport Licensing Division have encountered in coordinating the expiry of public service vehicle tests and taxi vehicle licenses to minimise the need for a taxi operator to surrender a vehicle's license for a period of time.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is aware of a small number of instances where there has been misalignment with the renewal dates for taxi vehicle licences and taxi vehicle tests.  On these occasions DVA’s Passenger Transport Licensing Division staff were able to successfully address this directly with the customers who were impacted. DVA has subsequently reviewed the timeframes for issuing taxi vehicle licence renewal reminders and are amending these to minimise the potential for expiry dates of taxi vehicle licences and taxi vehicle test certificates falling out of alignment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of placing children with special educational needs in schools without special educational needs provisions.
answer_text: Children with special educational needs (SEN) are placed, by the Education Authority (EA), in accordance with their assessed needs across the continuum of education provision in Northern Ireland’s schools, encompassing special schools, mainstream school with support as required and specialist provision classes in mainstream schools which provide smaller classes with targeted support.There are 68,000 children in Northern Ireland, almost 20% of the school population, on the SEN register.  84% of children with SEN are educated in mainstream schools, around 5% in specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPiMS) and 10.5% in special schools.The appropriate education placement is individual to each child.  For many, mainstream school is the right placement for them to thrive; for others a specialist provision or special school placement will best meet their needs.  For children with a statement of SEN, their needs are considered at an annual review which includes an assessment of whether their school placement continues to be appropriate or if a change of placement is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the financial support her Department is providing to businesses in (i) Newry; and (ii) Armagh city who are struggling with the cost of rates.
answer_text: The figures held by the Department show the rates support provided on a council area basis. For 2024/25 in the Newry, Mourne and Down council area support was provided to 5,347 business premises, delivering over £18.9 million of relief. In the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon council area, support was provided to 6,066 businesses, delivering over £23.4 million of relief. This rates support is provided to a wide range of businesses including manufacturing, hospitality, retail and services industries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to meet with the Royal College of Radiologists and Health and Social Care NI to discuss the recommendations made in their 2023 Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology Workforce Census reports.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the workforce challenges that exists across health and social care and therefore welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.  I have already agreed to meet representatives from the Royal College to discuss their recommendations in detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the towns and cities where free on-street parking is available; and (ii) the overall percentage of on-street parking where a free first-hour provision exists, broken down by area.
answer_text: My Department operates three charged Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs) in Belfast, Lisburn, and Newry. The CPZs do not have free parking. Details of the parking tariffs for each CPZ can be found at the following link https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/street-pay-and-display-tariffs-controlled-parking-zonesAll other towns and cities across the North provide free on-street parking. However, to deter all day parking and to encourage an ongoing turnover of parking spaces, some free parking locations have “limited waiting” restrictions. These locations will have signage and will limit the free parking period usually to either a one or two hour period followed by a no-return period e.g. “no return within 1-hour”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when funding will be released for the agreed implantation of dropped kerbs in the vicinity of Loanda play park, Newry.
answer_text: I can confirm funding is available and a scheme to provide dropped kerbs on Martins Lane (in the vicinity of Loanda play park, Newry) is programmed for this financial year 24/25. This work will be completed subject to the availability of contractor resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the cost of insurance in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned about the increasing cost of insurance for our citizens right across the North, adding further financial pressure to households, businesses and communities in the cost of living business crisis. Regulation of the insurance sector is a Reserved Matter, where the legislative authority rests with the British Government and regulators in London. My officials have flagged these concerns with Treasury and the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). They have also been engaging directly with industry bodies to ask them why costs have been rising so rapidly, and what the sector is doing to contain price increases. The industry has cited a number of issues, including general inflationary pressures as well as a number of more localised issues, such as the smaller number of insurance providers based here in the North compared to Britain. Regarding Newry, Mourne and Down District Council Area, I am aware from data published by Compare NI that this area shows the highest annual increase in home insurance up to Q1 2024 across the North. Whilst these are not official Government data, this is still very concerning. My officials have also sought an explanation for this from the industry bodies, but were advised this was not available at individual District Council level. I am seeking a meeting with the industry bodies to directly discuss my concerns and local issues. My officials and I will also re-engage with Treasury and the FCA to ask for their assessment of the information provided by the insurance industry bodies, and to press on them to do what they can to protect customers here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work will commence to (i) address potholes on Rathfriland Road, Newry; and (ii) resurface Rathfriland Road, Newry.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail their plans to engage with an incoming UK government in relation to securing the necessary funding to progress the Casement Park project to completion in time for the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship; and (ii) whether the redevelopment of Casement Park remains an Executive priority.
answer_text: We refer the member to the answer he received to AQW 10793/22-27 for the latest position on negotiations with key stakeholders.  The redevelopment of Casement Park remains an Executive commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when the Casement Park project will be put out to tender; and (ii) the latest date to issue the tender documents in order to facilitate a construction programme that will allow Casement Park to be built in time to host 2028 UEFA European Football Championship matches.
answer_text: While the UK Government has stated that it is fully committed to the project it has not yet confirmed the quantum or timing of funding. Until the full funding package is confirmed, I am not in a position to consider the release of the tender documents. I expect to get further information on this when a new UK Government is formed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the discussions the Executive have had; and (ii) the Executive meetings that have taken place in relation to 2028 UEFA European Football Championship matches being hosted in Casement Park.
answer_text: Casement Park remains an Executive flagship project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a breakdown of the engagement she has had with her UK counterparts in relation to securing the financial package necessary to facilitate the redevelopment of Casement Park.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer for AQW 13663-22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the engagement he had with (i) officials in his Department; (ii) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust management; (iii) frontline workers; and (iv) service users prior to the decision to close Armagh’s drive through blood testing service.
answer_text: Patients are often asked to go for a blood test with the phlebotomy service at a hospital or clinic before they attend an outpatient appointment. Phlebotomy is the procedure to take blood, usually from a vein in the arm, and is used to diagnose many medical conditions.Historically, phlebotomy clinics in the Southern HSC Trust area were offered from one of the Trust’s ‘blood rooms’ at Craigavon Area, Daisy Hill or South Tyrone hospitals before their clinical appointments. In response to the Covid-19 pandemic, to promote social distancing and reduce the risk of infection, temporary drive through phlebotomy clinics were funded in all Trusts in Northern Ireland. During this time, patients from across the Southern area, drove to the St Luke’s site in Armagh for their blood tests. Temporary funding for this service officially ceased on 31st March 2023. While other Trusts have already stopped providing drive through phlebotomy, the Southern Trust continued to run the service. This service has continued to operate at financial risk, at a cost of circa £230,000 per annum. In light of the Trust’s financial savings to be achieved in 2024/2025, cessation of this service was included in the Trust’s savings plan proposals. This proposal was approved by the Trust’s Senior Leadership Team and detailed within the Trust’s financial savings plans which were submitted to my Department for consideration.  Whilst my Department recognises that many patients, carers and staff greatly valued the convenience of the drive through option for blood tests from the dedicated and professional team at St Luke’s, given the current financial climate, my Department has accepted this proposal and the Trust is no longer in a financial position to continue to operate the drive through phlebotomy service from Friday, 28 June 2024.  From July, the Trust will return to pre-pandemic arrangements, with patients who require phlebotomy services continuing to be offered a service with the option to attend Craigavon, Daisy Hill or South Tyrone clinics.   Staff have been kept updated of the changes and will return to their substantive roles in blood clinics at Craigavon, Daisy Hill and South Tyrone hospitals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the new Translink timetable that will result in commuters from Newry, who wish to arrive in Dublin by 9:00am, boarding a train at 6:54am.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“Currently customers depart Newry at 0635 hours or 0744 hours and arrive into Dublin Connolly at 0819 and 0902 hours respectively. The new proposed timetable will see Enterprise services given priority over both NI Railways and Irish Rail local services, enhancing service punctuality and the customer experience. This means customers travelling from Newry to Dublin will depart at 0654, 0754 and 0845, arriving into Dublin Connolly at 0819, 0919 and 1019 respectively. Plans are in place to introduce an hourly frequency between Belfast and Dublin from October 2024. These service enhancements will see the number of cross-border trains double, with 15 return trips daily.  A new hourly clockface timetable is currently being finalised, which will see new departure times form Newry.   With new fleet due to arrive on the route by 2028/29 customers can continue to expect a high quality rail service along the eastern seaboard.”   For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any funding available to students seeking to complete a degree programme outside the United Kingdom and Ireland.
answer_text: Generally, my Department does not fund degree programmes outside of the island of Ireland and Britain. Higher education students’ eligibility for financial support is determined in line with the Education (Student Support) (No. 2) Regulations (NI) 2009 (as amended). A student must satisfy the relevant residency requirements and the course must be eligible to receive support. Students from here planning to study abroad as part of their higher education course may, however, be eligible for the overseas rate of maintenance loan of up to £8,078 for that period. Study USA, the Department funded flagship international mobility programme, offers undergraduates the opportunity to avail of a year-long placement at a US college.  Support is also provided for study at the University of London Institute in Paris.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will release funding for the Newry and Mourne Sub Aqua Club Search and Rescue Unit to enable it to sustain its operations.
answer_text: The Department is currently facing a significant number of competing funding challenges and it is not possible to make any additional spending commitments at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what representations his Department has made to Translink with a view to (i) harmonising the 341 bus service schedule with Daisy Hill Hospital staff start and end times; and (ii) allowing the 341 bus service to stop at Daisy Hill Hospital to allow staff to disembark.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“Translink have met with officials from Department for Infrastructure, Department for Health and the five Health Trusts to discuss how Public Transport can be developed to promote staff, patient and visitor travel to and from health sites. This includes reviewing links between Newry Rail Station and Daisyhill Hospital and the provision for staff shift patterns.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what advice his Department provides to rural residents whose areas are being littered by motorists throwing rubbish from their windows.
answer_text: While my Department does not provide specific advice to rural residents in respect of litter, residents should report any areas of concern, where road litter has become commonplace, to their district council. The Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (NI) 2011 places a duty on councils to, as far as is practicable, keep roads for which they are responsible, clean and clear of litter. Councils can issue Fixed Penalty Notices (FPN) of up to £200 for littering offences. Failure to pay a FPN will lead to a court summons and, if convicted, a fine of up to £2,500. FPNs can be issued in cases where litter is thrown from vehicles if evidence of this act is provided to councils or council officers have witnessed this act.  I intend to launch a public consultation on Northern Ireland’s first Litter Strategy during the current Assembly mandate. The consultation will consider additional steps to tackle the scourge of litter right across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the reasons why some women experiencing miscarriages do not engage with Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The loss of a baby during pregnancy and following birth can lead to profound trauma and heartbreak for both the woman and the family.  The Royal College of Obstetrics & Gynaecology define an early miscarriage as being in the first three months of pregnancy.  Symptoms of miscarriage vary for women and, unfortunately, if a miscarriage has begun there is nothing that can be done to stop it.  National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) Guideline 126 (NG126) provides guidance in relation to the diagnosis and management of miscarriage.  The advice for women in the first three months of pregnancy is that unless they are experiencing heavy vaginal bleeding, severe pain or develop a high temperature it is not necessary to attend hospital services. All women who are greater than 12 weeks pregnant and experiencing any symptoms of a miscarriage are encouraged to engage with primary or hospital services. Currently in Northern Ireland, the majority of pregnant women book their first maternity appointment after 10 weeks gestation.  Some women can experience a spontaneous early miscarriage without any complications before self-referring to maternity services and therefore may choose not to engage with primary or hospital services.  This is a choice women have and if they choose not to engage with primary or hospital services prior to their first maternity appointment, their data will not be captured and their pregnancy loss will not be recorded until a subsequent pregnancy where previous obstetric history is recorded. My Department leads on the work of the Northern Ireland Bereavement Network.  In recent months the Network launched a website which signposts citizens to bereavement support services, including those who have experienced early pregnancy or baby loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs who has responsibility for removing illegally buried waste in cases where the defendant has been found guilty but not liable to remove the waste.
answer_text: It is an offence under Article 4 of the Waste and Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997 (the 1997 Act) as amended to keep, treat, deposit or dispose of controlled waste without having been authorised to do so under a waste management license issued by the Department or in contravention of said license. 	Investigations into illegally buried waste are carried out by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) as these deposits and disposals are undertaken without appropriate licences, exemptions and permits being in place. 		As part of its response to these incidents the NIEA will seek remedial action initially in consultation with the landowner or person in control of the land and, where applicable, through service of a notice under Article 27 the 1997 Act. 		A failure to adhere to this remediation notice is a criminal offence under Article 27 (5) of this Act and may result, depending on the circumstances, in a criminal prosecution. If, at the end of criminal proceedings, the defendant(s) plead or are found guilty of a waste offence under the 1997 Act, the guilty parties are not required by the court to remediate this waste. 		To attempt to force remediation, the Department may issue another Article 27, which again if not adhered to will result in another criminal prosecution. Again this may not result in remediation of the waste. 		The Department is currently exploring additional provisions in this area under the 1997 Act under Provision 5C and Article 28, which only commenced in respect of the Department in January 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her ministerial responsibility in relation to (i) public policy on policing; and (ii) the governance arrangements for the PSNI.
answer_text: (i) As Minister of Justice my ministerial responsibility in relation to public policy on policing is set out across a wide range of primary and secondary legislation, covering many areas which provide the framework to enable the delivery of policing in Northern Ireland.(ii) As Minister of Justice I am not responsible for the day-to-day governance of the PSNI. That is the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board, as set out in Part 2 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) what advice is provided to applicants of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme in relation to providing evidence to support their application; and (ii) the rationale for the burden of proof to rest with the applicant.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020 were laid by the Secretary of State. Regulation 44 provides that “the burden of proving any issue in relation to an application or appeal is on the applicant or, as the case may be, the appellant”.Applicants are required to complete the Scheme application form and include any information they may have which is relevant to their application.  The Victims’ Payments Board (the Board) will attempt to gather additional information and evidence on behalf of applicants and has arrangements in place with a range of organisations to formally request this information, as required. TEO also provides additional resources to evidence retrieval partners to assist in the retrieval of evidence on behalf of applicants and the Board.  Where necessary, arrangements have also been made for Capita, the medical assessment provider, to request information directly from GPs or the health service.To date, funding of almost £5 million has been provided by TEO to support victims’ and survivors’ representative groups via the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS).  This has facilitated the recruitment of welfare officers and admin support staff to assist applicants with the application process, along with additional funding to provide health and wellbeing support. Representative groups continue to work closely with the Board and VSS on improving the application process for applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department’s long-term objectives for the PSNI.
answer_text: My long term objectives for the PSNI are:That policing is delivered in a way that protects and vindicates the human rights of all and preserves the fundamental responsibility of the police to serve all parts of the community.That the community has an effective, accessible and accountable policing presence to address the identified and understood needs of the community, to enhance public confidence in the police, and to deter criminality and reduce harm.That policing in its widest sense is a shared responsibility that involves effective partnership working between police services, the public, and statutory, voluntary and private partners.That policing contributes to the transformation of Northern Ireland to a more inclusive and cohesive society; and that it pro-actively responds and adapts to emerging changes in society.That the Police Service continues to be: free from external interference in operational matters;accountable within the rule of law;answerable to the community through the Policing Board for operational decisions and overall performance, and locally to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs); andaccountable to the public through the Policing Board, the Department and the Assembly for the use of public money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the task and finish group established to review and make recommendations on issues relating to finance and care homes, including (i) reviewing the care home tariff; (ii) third party top-up fees; and (iii) enhanced care rates.
answer_text: Workstream 8 of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is undertaking key actions in respect of the commissioning and contracting of care home provision across Northern Ireland. A Finance Task and Finish Group has been established within the workstream to review existing finance issues within the context of the Regional Care Home Contract Review. As part of this work consideration is being given to the regional tariff, third party top up fees and enhanced care rates. The Department of Health annually reviews the regional tariff rate for residents placed in residential and nursing care homes. The regional tariff rate is compiled on the basis of a breakdown of the costs of care and a review process takes place each year.  The Sector is thereafter advised on the uplift to the tariff rateSeparate to establishing the regional tariff rate, it is important to note that independent care providers are responsible for setting their own care home fees.Consideration is being given within the Finance Task and Finish group to a review of the current Tariff methodology (i.e. the elements that comprise the tariff). This work is at an early stage and is commercially sensitive.The review of the regional care home contract will consider the inclusion of clauses providing for enhanced care within the tariff.The recommendations from the Finance Task and Finish group review will be shared with Department of Health senior management with responsibility for this service area in order to take forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 10795/22-27, for an update on the establishment of a Tourism Partnership Board.
answer_text: I recently met with my officials to discuss membership of the Tourism Partnership Board.  Once finalised, letters of invitation will issue and the inaugural meeting of the Partnership Board will be scheduled as soon as possible thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 11015/22-27, for an update on his engagement with officials in the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to the proposed changes to Personal Independence Payments and the potential impact in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) on its Green Paper consultation exercise which is looking at whether the UK Government should make fundamental changes to how it supports disabled people and people with health conditions, and whether the system is delivering the right support to people most in need. Northern Ireland’s social security policy and legislation, including that for Personal Independence Payment (PIP), generally operates in parity with that in place under DWP in Great Britain. It is important to note that at this stage options are being consulted on and no decisions have yet been taken in relation to any future changes to PIP.  In Northern Ireland, any reform of the benefit will be subject, as necessary, to the appropriate impact assessment considerations, as well as the relevant Ministerial, Executive and Assembly approvals.Should local stakeholders wish to give their views on the high level proposals, they can do so via this link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/consultation-modernising-support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what percentage of the Civil Service office estate is currently in-scope.
answer_text: The Office Estate Review was approved by the NICS Board in June 2022 and of the 98 office buildings owned or leased by the Department of Finance (DoF) originally identified, 65 (66%) are currently in scope. The remainder of DoF’s Office Estate is primarily used as Jobs and Benefits Offices which are not included in the scope of the current review as their locations are determined by the Department for Communities. Delivery of the Review will reduce the Department’s in scope Estate by c40% by 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 4900/22-27, to detail the amount of funding allocated to support decarbonisation strategies.
answer_text: In the 2024-25 Financial Year my department has allocated £36.0m funding toward specific decarbonisation projects. These projects will contribute toward our path to net zero and climate change objectives and includes funding for energy efficiency programmes, energy management work to reduce carbon emissions across central government and for geothermal demonstrator projects whilst other projects across the Department will also contribute to decarbonisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the funding that will be allocated to community transport organisations to ensure that access for rural communities will not be impacted.
answer_text: I understand the importance to those within our rural communities who rely on community transport for accessing good and services, enabling them to be more independent and remain connected to their communities. I have made the decision to provide funding to community transport for 2024/25 at the total levels awarded for 2023/24.  This is subject to the planned Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the budget and so this allocation is indicative at this stage.  I do, however, expect to be able to confirm opening budget allocations, once I have taken final decisions following consideration of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment after an initial four-week period.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the capital spend on (i) Armagh Court; and (ii) Newry Courthouse in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The capital spend at Armagh and Newry Courthouses in each of the last five years is detailed below. These figures include investment in the building facilities as well as courtroom technology.Table 1 – Capital Spend Armagh CourthouseYearCapital Spend: BuildingCapital Spend: Technology2019/20£1,700£12,4732020/21£4,566£23,3432021/22£5,869£23,3432022/23£4,525£02023/24£15,160£0Table 2 – Capital Spend Newry CourthouseYearCapital Spend: BuildingCapital Spend: Technology2019/20£64,508£58,4672020/21£98,212£1,8242021/22£0£19,3112022/23£3,177£02023/24£45,122£0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what advice is available to patients whose appointments have been postponed due to industrial action linked to the junior doctor’s pay dispute.
answer_text: Patients across Northern Ireland were asked to attend scheduled appointments during industrial action, unless contacted by their local Health and Social Care Trust to advise otherwise. Patients whose appointments were postponed will be rescheduled in due courseAll Health and Social Care Trusts actively shared information on their social media platforms and published information about the strike impact on their websites. Some Trusts have also written to their local political representatives to update them.This webpage has links to all the Trusts’ webpages and social media channels - Health and Social Care Industrial Action Updates - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)The Department of Health issued statements to media advising of anticipated impact ahead of the two most recent periods of strike action. In addition, the HSCNI social media channels also issued a series of social media messages advising the public of alternative services for non-urgent care during the Industrial Action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has had discussions with the Minister of Finance with a view to releasing funds to resolve the junior doctor’s pay dispute.
answer_text: I met with the Finance Minister on 3rd June in advance of the June monitoring round to discuss the inadequacies of the Health Budget. We are currently facing an estimated funding gap of some £340m on the assumption that £75m is received as part of the in-year monitoring process and it is unlikely that I will be in a position to balance the budget without making some significant cuts to services. Whilst I have bid for additional funding in June monitoring, I am aware that there is unlikely to be sufficient funding available to fully bridge this gap. However, once the outcome of June monitoring is confirmed I will be in a better position to consider allocation decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on his engagement with the UK government in relation to the electronic travel authorisation (ETA); and (ii) for his assessment of the ETA's impact on the tourism sector.
answer_text: I am concerned about the impact of the introduction of the British Government’s Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme on our tourism sector. I see it as a real risk to the huge growth potential for tourism here. For that reason, I wrote to Tom Pursglove MP, the Minister for Legal Migration and the Border at the British Home Office on 26 April 2024 to seek an urgent meeting to discuss my concerns and to find solutions that would protect our tourism industry such as an exemption for tourists crossing the land border into the north. I have had no response to my letter, and we are now into the pre-election period ahead of the British General Election. I intend to write again to the relevant British Home Office Minister when one is appointed after the election next month. Tourism is performing extremely well with the number of external overnight visitors quadrupling between 1998 and 2019. Seamless all-island travel is key to this growth. In 2019, international visitors (who will require an ETA when introduced) contributed £220 million to the local economy and it is this expenditure that could be impacted by the introduction of the ETA scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for the future of cross-border health services.
answer_text: My Department is committed to exploring any cross-border solutions which improve outcomes for patients in Northern Ireland, help funding to stretch further, or create synergies that can help to address the full breadth of the problems we have in the Health Service. We work directly with the Department of Health in Dublin, as well as via the North-South Ministerial Council, the Cooperation and Working Together partnership, and through local Service Level Agreements which have been put in place between relevant providers in both jurisdictions.Cross-border cooperation is also facilitated through the PEACEPLUS Programme.  This provides funding and significant opportunities for tackling shared challenges in health and social care across both jurisdictions through partnership working.  A range of applications are currently under consideration.I will continue to engage directly with Minister Donnelly on areas for further partnership working, primarily through the North-South Ministerial Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the joint statement of 3 June 2024 by the Chairs of the Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to funding shortfalls for the Department of Health.
answer_text: The joint statement made on 3 June 2024 by the Chairs of our Health and Social Care Trusts reflects the stark position that is facing our Health Service, within the context of the 2024/25 budget agreed by the Executive.The Department of Health’s 2024/25 budget assessment sets out the impacts of this budget, impacts which will ultimately be felt by patients and service users, including the most vulnerable in our society.  I also acknowledged this in my statement to the Assembly on 3 June 2024, where measures required to live with the budget settlement would unavoidably have high and catastrophic impacts to services.I have set out the need for additional funding to avoid having to implement measures which will have such a devastating impact on our Health Service and the population it serves.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what recourse is available for businesses with grievances in relation to the administration of flood recovery funding.
answer_text: The flood recovery funding is being provided through three council run schemes. Those applicants who have been declined funding will be notified of the appeals process in their rejection letter. Any other grievance in relation to the administration of the schemes should be directed to the relevant local council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of utilising opportunities for synergies in cross-border health care delivery to ease the burden on the health service.
answer_text: My Department is committed to exploring any cross-border solutions which improve outcomes for patients in Northern Ireland, help funding to stretch further, or create synergies that can help to address the full breadth of the problems we have in the Health Service. We work directly with the Department of Health in Dublin, as well as via the North-South Ministerial Council, the Cooperation and Working Together partnership, and through local Service Level Agreements which have been put in place between relevant providers in both jurisdictions.Cross-border cooperation is also facilitated through the PEACEPLUS Programme.  This provides funding and significant opportunities for tackling shared challenges in health and social care across both jurisdictions through partnership working.  A range of applications are currently under consideration.I will continue to engage directly with Minister Donnelly on areas for further partnership working, primarily through the North-South Ministerial Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be accepting the invitation from (i) Newry, Mourne and Down District Council; and (ii) Daisy Hill SOS Committee to discuss the future of Daisy Hill Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of paramedics that have (i) joined; and (ii) left the Ambulance Service in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of staff graded as paramedics that joined and left the NI Ambulance Service (NIAS), in each of the last five financial years, is detailed in the table below.NIAS staff graded as Paramedics that Joined or Left NIAS, 2019/20 – 2023/24NIAS Paramedics *JoinersLeavers2019/2017122020/211372021/227112022/2318132023/24 017Source: Human Resources, Payroll, Travel & Subsistence (HRPTS) system* Includes staff graded as a Paramedic, RRV Paramedic or Ambulance A&E HEMS Advanced Paramedic.  Excludes existing NIAS staff who changed grade, either to a Paramedic grade (such as trainees completing the in-house paramedic programme) or from a Paramedic grade to another role (such as Ambulance Officer).In October 2023, the Department gave the go-ahead to NIAS for the recruitment of 48 additional paramedics with effect from autumn 2024. This will allow the first cohort of students graduating from the Ulster University BSc Paramedic Sciences degree programme to apply for newly created positions within NIAS. The next cohort of newly qualified paramedics are commencing with NIAS on 28th October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress made on the implementation of the NI Ambulance Service Clinical Response Model.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) Clinical Response Model has been implemented to provide a new call categorisation for emergency calls. The new measures are the Category 1, 2, 3 and 4 indicators which are standardised across the UK. Each call category has set standards and NIAS measure the response performance of these targets. This performance is measured on a continued basis and reported formally to the NIAS Trust Board and the Department of Health on a monthly basis. The Department has set improvement trajectories on these standards for NIAS for 2024/2025 and NIAS is working to improve response rates, particularly for Category 1 and Category 2 calls.  However, the service’s ability to meet the response targets are affected by current system pressures, including delayed ambulance handovers.The Department has provided additional funding to NIAS in 2024/25 for an additional 48 newly qualified paramedics to increase core staff levels and for development of an Integrated Clinical Hub.  The hub is designed to safely reduce demand for conveyance to emergency departments and use alternative appropriate patient pathways, thereby contributing to delivering improved clinical outcomes and response times.This approach will be revisited on a yearly basis within the context of the available funding envelope to ensure ongoing alignment with NIAS’s strategic goals and financial capabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to provide financial support to businesses in (i) Newry; and (ii) Armagh city struggling with the cost of rates.
answer_text: The Department for Economy does not provide any specific financial support to businesses in relation to rates support.  The provision of rates relief is the responsibility for the Finance Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on social and affordable housing development schemes in Newry and Armagh, in relation to (i) location and number of units; and (ii) programme.
answer_text: Although this issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), I sought the requested information from the NIHE Chief Executive. Due to the volume of information provided, the response has been placed in the Assembly Library (Annex A).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have introduced measures to ensure that the Executive will be working to its maximum capacity during the Westminster election campaign.
To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the average ambulance response time in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Information on the average ambulance response time in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency, is not held centrally. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's expenditure on (i) agency nurses; (ii) agency allied health professionals; and (iii) locum doctors in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ agency staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years there have been concerns of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognise that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department has therefore undertaken significant work to alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure. Work has been ongoing regionally across HSC, under the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group (ARIG) with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. The Nursing and Social Work staff groups focussed on by ARIG has shown a reduction of approximately £40m.Alongside this, work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, in addition to a dedicated workstream examining the reform of the Bank system. Further work is ongoing to reduce ‘Off-Framework’ agency for Medical and Dental, and other staff groups. The total agency expenditure by Trust for nurses, for the last 5 years (2019/20-2023/24) is set out in Table 1 overleaf.The total agency expenditure by Trust for agency allied health professionals, for the last 5 years (2019/20-2023/24) is set out in Table 2 overleaf.The total agency expenditure by Trust for agency locum doctors, for the last 5 years (2019/20-2023/24) is set out in Table 3 overleaf.Table 1 - Total Expenditure by Trust for Agency Nurses, 2019/20-2023/24 2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24 £££££BHSCT 38,494,367   45,818,273   51,478,561   70,394,649   60,037,456 NHSCT 15,375,590   18,882,571   26,401,685   36,829,663   32,642,610 SEHSCT  5,695,567     9,559,176   11,188,695   14,917,334   20,603,873 SHSCT 20,056,605   23,250,772   30,740,154   35,737,763   18,847,737 WHSCT 10,157,845   12,530,115   19,576,086   28,068,617   21,973,073 Total 89,779,974  110,040,907  139,385,181  185,948,026  154,104,749 Table 2 - Total Expenditure by Trust for Agency Allied Health Professionals, 2019/20-2023/24 2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24 £££££BHSCT  2,273,911   1,533,307   1,752,853   2,517,765   3,217,396 NHSCT  1,237,103      361,663      532,021      698,129      698,934 SEHSCT  1,331,499   1,455,964   1,161,563   1,840,954   1,835,206 SHSCT     432,452      551,208      505,574      747,769      314,771 WHSCT     838,164      320,198      598,945      463,136      540,535 Total  6,113,129   4,222,340   4,550,956   6,267,753   6,606,842 Table 3 - Total Expenditure b

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits of reintroducing the 50:50 recruitment policy for the PSNI.
answer_text: It is important that our Police Service is reflective of the communities that it serves, not just in respect of religious make-up, but also in terms of other characteristics such as gender, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, and so on.My role is to provide a legislative framework with appropriate measures in place to ensure accountability in the recruitment process in addition to the normal oversight arrangements operated by the Policing Board. There is currently provision for targeting advertising to reach under-represented groups and the recruitment process is open to all those who meet the minimum criteria regardless of their religious background.  I am not persuaded that the circumstances prevail in which the Police Service of Northern Ireland would benefit from the reintroduction of the 50:50 recruitment policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of NHS consultants who also hold employment in the private health sector.
answer_text: Information on the number of Health & Social Care consultants who also hold employment in the private health sector is not held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of keeping childhood smartphone free.
answer_text: UK Chief Medical Officers’ Commentary on Social Media and Children’s Mental Health was published on 7 February 2019. The document advises that scientific research is currently insufficiently conclusive to support UK CMO evidence-based guidelines on optimal amounts of screen use or online activities (such as social media use).Even though no causal effect is evident from existing research between screen-based activities, or the amount of time spent using screens, and any particular negative effect, it does not mean that there is no effect. It is still wise to take a precautionary approach. This needs to be balanced, however, against the potential benefits that children and young people can derive from their screen-based activities.Parents are advised to agree boundaries, outside of school and educational use, with children and young people around online behaviours and time spent using screens. Studies have reported that children and young people are worried about their parents’ screen use and want parents to engage with them, and so adults can lead by example through not using screens excessively in front of children and behaving online as they would in person.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Prime Minister’s comments that the British government will make a significant contribution to the redevelopment of Casement Park,  for an update on funding for the project.
answer_text: I have written to the Secretary of State seeking confirmation of the UK Government’s proposed contribution to the Casement Park project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage of the prison population that are diagnosed with (i) mental health issues; (ii) addiction issues; and (iii) learning difficulties.
answer_text: Individuals committed to prison are medically assessed by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, Healthcare in Prisons team.  Following this committal interview, the Northern Ireland Prison Service retains information provided on self-reported conditions.  At 1st May 2024, the prison population was 1,914 and from this number: 37% reported having a mental health issue. 51% reported having an addiction issue.10% reported having a learning difficulty.The Prison Service does not have responsibility for the diagnosis of these conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the advice he can provide residents who are concerned about illegally buried waste on agricultural land.
answer_text: In addition to the regulation of licensed waste facilities, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency investigates and prosecutes the most significant, serious and persistent waste offenders who unlawfully inflict large-scale and long-term damage on the environment. This activity also deprives the economy of considerable revenue which negatively affects the ability of legitimate and compliant waste management businesses, regulated by the Agency, to compete.  		The Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) of NIEA applies powers granted under the Waste and Contaminated Land Order (1997), as amended, and the Proceeds of Crime Act (2002) to investigate, remediate, prosecute and penalise offenders under the ‘polluter pays’ principle, for a number of waste offences including the illegal disposal of waste by burial. 		ECU often relies on information provided by members of the public to initiate enquiries and investigations surrounding potential waste offences. We would therefore advise that if any member of the public has information which would help with the detection, prevention, remediation, and prosecution of environmental waste crime, to report the matter to the NIEA’s incident hotline: Telephone 0800 807 060. 		Alternatively, you can contact the NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit by email at: environmentalcrime@daera-ni.gov.uk or Telephone 028 90569453.		You may also report environmental crime through CrimeStoppers, 0800 555 111.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the merits of keeping childhood smartphone free.
answer_text: Any concerns about the impact of smartphone use on mental and physical health during childhood are led and informed primarily by the Department of Health. My officials are working with their counterparts in the Education Authority (EA) to review current guidance on the use of mobile digital devices within education settings.   My officials and representatives from the EA also intend meeting with the Smartphone Free Childhood campaign group to hear their views and consider these as part of the review. From a curriculum perspective, the integration of digital skills into all areas of the Northern Ireland Curriculum is important both to individual learners and society at large. In a world driven by technological advancements, equipping students with digital proficiency is imperative for their future success.Using ICT is one of the three Cross-Curricular Skills at the heart of the curriculum. Developing pupils’ digital skills encourages them to handle and communicate information, solve problems, pose questions and be creative in using digital technology. This may include the use of smartphones. At all key stages, children and young people should be provided with opportunities to develop knowledge and understanding of e-safety and acceptable online behaviour. CCEA have produced useful resources for schools who should embed e-safety education into teaching and learning across all Areas of Learning within the curriculum.Guidance relating to behaviour requires that schools have a policy in place which promotes positive behaviour among pupils in order to safeguard their welfare and facilitate their educational progress at school.  Most schools will therefore set out limitations on the use of mobile phones in school through their behaviour policy.
