question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will write to (i) An Taoiseach following Ireland's decision to recognise the State of Palestine; and (ii) the Prime Minister in relation to the recognition of a Palestinian state.
answer_text: Foreign Relations including the formal recognition of states is an excepted matter for the UK Government.  There are no plans at present to write to either An Taoiseach or the Prime Minister in relation to this matter. There is no joint agreement on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have developed secondary medical conditions while (i) waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment; and (ii) on a surgical waiting list, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This information is not available centrally in the Department. HSC Trusts were asked to provide a response; however, this information was not available from any of the Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that have died while (i) on an oncology waiting list, but prior to their first consultant-led outpatient appointment; and (ii) awaiting cancer treatment, in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has identified a figure for the shortfall between the Executive’s current finances and the financial allocation required to fund the stated commitments of the Executive.
answer_text: The financial pressures identified by departments as part of Budget 20204-25 far outweighed the funding available to the Executive.Except for funding earmarked by the Treasury or the Executive for specific purposes, it is now for individual Ministers to determine how best to use the funding provided to their departments as part of Budget 2024-25This will be informed by the equality screenings and/or impact assessments, including consultations as appropriate, that departments will carry out in line with their Equality Schemes. The Executive will consider departmental impact assessments as part of the October monitoring round. This will allow the Executive to determine whether any action is required as a result. This may include addressing any identified shortfalls for Executive commitments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the development and delivery of the Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Ending Violence Against Women and Girls remains one of our key priorities, and we are committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework and Action Plan for consideration by the Executive Committee as soon as possible. We are currently giving very active consideration to final options, and it is anticipated the updated Framework and Action Plan will be brought to the Executive shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to a lock system as a solution to the ongoing challenges with the bridge portion of the Newry Southern Relief Road project.
answer_text: As part of the design process for the Newry Southern Relief Road project, a lock gate option was considered.  This option was discounted due to the severity of impact to the Scheduled Monument, construction complexity, lengthy operational times and reduced value for money when compared against other design options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development and delivery of a childcare strategy.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 sets out my plans for early learning and childcare support measures during 2024/25.  These represent an ambitious first step towards the development of a full strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer (i) AQW 10713/22-27; (ii) AQW 10794/22-27; (iii) AQW 8221/22-27; and (iv) AQW 8091/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 10713 22-27 was answered on 7th October 2024.AQW 10794 22-27 was answered on 29th May 2024. AQW 8221 22-27 was answered on 8th November 2024. AQW 8091 22-27 was answered on 27th September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the current time for processing (i) Personal Independence Payment (PIP) first time applications; (ii) PIP renewals; and (iii) Universal Credit Limited Capability for Work Questionnaires, to the point where a decision is made.
answer_text: (i) I am content that new applications to Personal Independence Payment (PIP) in Northern Ireland are being processed in a timely manner. The latest published statistics reflect that on average first applications were processed within 14 weeks during February 2024. 		(ii) PIP renewals are prioritised to ensure they are decided before the current award expires. I am content that processes are in place to ensure that there is no interruption to payment while renewal applications are progressed.		(iii) The time taken to finalise a work capability decision is dependent on claimant participation, the completion of a work capability assessment by our provider, Medical Support Services, and the finalisation of our decision on limited capability for work.  I am aware there have been issues with the through put of medical assessment outcomes from Medical Support Services to my decision makers.  To address these concerns my officials have worked with Medical Support services and put mitigations in place which have improved the time now taken to complete work capability assessments and the finalisation of the Department’s decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to carry out an updated survey of pregnancy and birth experiences, with a focus on insights from bereaved parents.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact tariff-free imports of meat products from Australia and New Zealand will have on (i) the decarbonisation of the agri-foods sector; and (ii) the agri-foods sector and family farms competitiveness indicators.
answer_text: I have concerns about the potential for increases in UK imports in sensitive agricultural commodities arising from the trade agreements with Australia and New Zealand. At present, import levels from Australia and New Zealand for meat products are modest with no significant impact on the agriculture sector to date. The trade agreements do not restrict policy efforts to decarbonise the sector.

answer_text: In conjunction with wider budget discussions, I hope to be able to make an announcement regarding the future of the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme in coming weeks. One of the themes through which grants will be available includes Environmental Improvements. This opportunity will build on the last Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme in 2022/23 when 4 rural community groups secured funds to help them establish community gardens/vegetable allotments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the benefits of holding regular meetings with representatives of the Independent Monitoring Board (IMB) for Prisons; and (ii) to what extent her Department takes into consideration the findings of the IMB for Prisons in developing strategies.
answer_text: I welcome the opportunity to meet with the IMB Executive Council at least annually and hear about their observations within the prisons, and they are able to advise me at any time of any issue that they believe needs to be brought to my attention. The Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service meets with the Chairs of the three Independent Monitoring Boards (IMBs) on a quarterly basis.  These are important meetings and provide an opportunity to receive feedback about their work and discuss NIPS priorities. There is regular dialogue between Prison Service staff and the IMB, including the attendance of Governors at IMB monthly meetings in each establishment. There is also regular engagement between officials in the Department and the IMB, including attendance at IMB Executive Council meetings, and their monthly meetings in the prisons. The feedback received from the IMBs in regular meetings with the Governors of establishments, and with the Director General, as well as the observations contained in their annual reports, are very important to the operational and strategic development of NIPS.   The observations of the IMBs and other scrutiny bodies are reflected in the themes and actions contained within Prisons 25by25, the continuous improvement programme for the Prison Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to provide a detailed breakdown of the budget allocated to her Department (a) for areas which are within the Minister’s operational oversight and responsibility; and (b) for areas which are not within the Minister’s operational oversight and responsibility; and (ii) to list the areas of responsibility which are within the Minister’s operational oversight.
answer_text: (i) I have oversight and responsibility for all areas within my Department’s total budget allocation in 2024-25 of non-ringfenced Resource DEL £1,262m including £13m for the Tackling Paramilitary Programme and Capital DEL £92m. In relation to the £13m funding for the Tackling Paramilitary Programme (TPP), this is a cross-Executive strategy, however the Executive has provided the budget to my Department, as the lead Department. The total funding for TPP is £16m, the remaining £3m will form part of June monitoring. This budget will be allocated to areas both within my Department and externally to other Departments.(ii) I have oversight and responsibility for policing and justice functions devolved to the Department of Justice as set out in the Northern Ireland Act 1998 (Devolution of Policing and Justice Functions) Order 2010.Due to the nature of the Justice system, I am not involved in operational decision making in areas such as the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, the Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland and the Judiciary and Judicial office holders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has given consideration to developing a methodology specific to Northern Ireland to record miscarriages.
answer_text: Recording of data around miscarriage is a particularly challenging issue, not least because women experiencing miscarriage can access a wide range of HSC services meaning that data recording can be made through a range of systems by a range of professionals.  In addition, some women experience miscarriage and do not engage with the HSC and so data relating to them would not be captured.It is recognised that improvement to the collection of such data across all scenarios is essential, and consideration around the methods required to secure that improvement is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to introduce a strategy to reduce pregnancy loss and infant death.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland already follows national best practice for reducing stillbirth and neonatal mortality, as set out in the latest version of NHS England’s Saving Babies’ Lives, published in June 2023: https://www.england.nhs.uk/publication/saving-babies-lives-version-three/. The Public Health Agency works closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure that appropriate plans are in place locally and regionally in line with this best practice.This work forms part of a broader programme which my Department is overseeing to improve safety and governance in relation to maternity and neonatal services in Northern Ireland. This includes a comprehensive independent review of freestanding midwifery led units, reviewing implementation of relevant NICE guidance, developing a gap analysis of implementation of all relevant reports and strategies endorsed by the Department and other relevant national reports and identifying learning opportunities. Collectively, this programme of work will ensure an ongoing focus on improvement of maternity and neonatal services for mothers, babies and their families.

answer_text: Decisions on the matters raised in the question relate to foreign policy and are therefore excepted. There has been no engagement with the UK Government, the European Union, or the US Government on them.There is no joint agreement on this issue.

answer_text: There are no plans at present to undertake a review.There is no joint agreement on this issue.

answer_text: No complaints have been received from (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; (ii) patients and (iii) service users in relation to the administration and management of electronic patient records by encompass

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a detailed breakdown of the procurement processes for external contractors and providers in each Department, including the due diligence undertaken to ensure human rights compliance.
answer_text: The procurement process and awarding of public contracts is carried out in accordance with Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (as amended) and Public Procurement Policy. Other areas of law would also be applicable including Health and Safety, Employment Law, Equality Legislation and Human Rights Law.  This regulatory and policy framework for public procurement includes a number of Procurement Policy Notes (PPNs). PPNs are Executive Policy which apply to all Departments. PPN 05/21 specifically covers Human Rights in Procurement which mandates the actions Departments must take to incorporate human rights considerations into contracts when conducting a procurement process.There are also further contractual obligations in all contracts over £30,000 to demonstrate compliance with the Human Rights Act 1998, Northern Ireland Act 1998, Modern Slavery Act 2015, and other relevant legislative standards.PPN 03/21 (Supply Chain Resilience), published in November 2021, requires Departments to map supply chains for their critical supplies contracts. This mapping exercise assists with the identification of Human Rights and Modern Slavery risks and requires departments to incorporate appropriate measures to manage these risks. PPN 01/21 Scoring Social Value requires the following requirements to be incorporated into all government contracts: compliance with relevant employment, equality and health and safety law and human rights standards; adherence to relevant collective agreements; and adoption of fair work practices for all workers engaged in the delivery of the contract. Fair work is as per the Carnegie Trust definition. This policy further mandated the payment of the Living Wage as a condition of contract for all tenders from June 2022. Any procurement processes facilitated through my Department’s two Centres of Procurement Expertise incorporate these fair work practices into the relevant contracts.Due diligence includes mapping supply chains to identify high risk areas; the assessment of tenders to evaluate the supplier’s approach to ensure human rights compliance; and managing the contract to ensure the contractor meets their obligations including the payment of the living wage.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of Executive meetings at which minutes have been taken in the last five years; and (ii) their Department’s policies and procedures for (a) capturing accurate minutes of Executive meetings; and (b) the retention of Executive meeting minutes for future reference.
answer_text: There have been 144 meetings of the Executive Committee in the last five years.  Minutes are recorded for each meeting of the Executive Committee.(a) A draft minute of each Executive Committee meeting is prepared by TEO officials, circulated to Ministers, and finalised once agreed by the Executive Committee.(b) Minutes of meetings of the Executive Committee are retained in accordance with the department's retention and disposal schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the procurement process involved prior to the appointment of Encompass for the administration and management of electronic patient records; and (ii) the value and terms of the contract awarded to Encompass.
answer_text: The encompass procurement was carried out using Competitive Dialogue, the European Union OJEU process and in accordance with the Public Contracts Regulations 2015. HSCNI care professional and non-care professional staff and a number of patients, service users and carers were engaged throughout, from pre-procurement activity such as the Discovery Days held in early 2017, development of demonstration scenarios and preparation of the Output Based Specification, through to evaluation of the bids on the basis of scenario demonstrations and the bidders’ written submissions. Epic was selected as the preferred supplier in June 2019.The value of the contract awarded to Epic, £273.426M, was publicly advertised on OJEU in 2020.  The contract term was for 10 years to 2030 with the contracting authority having the option for the term to be extended by a further 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail (a) the hospitals; and (b) the times thrombectomy treatment for a stroke is available; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to making thrombectomy treatment available (a) at all hospitals; and (b) on a 24/7 basis.
answer_text: (i) (a) Mechanical thrombectomy to treat ischaemic stroke, is a regional service commissioned in the Royal Victoria hospital, Belfast.  Patients who are potentially suitable for thrombectomy and present to one of the other 7 stroke receiving units - Altnagelvin, Antrim Area, Causeway, Craigavon, Daisy Hill, Ulster and South West Acute Hospitals, are rapidly assessed and with agreement of the thrombectomy service, blue light transferred to the Royal Victoria Hospital for the procedure.(b) The Thrombectomy service is currently commissioned in Royal Victoria Hospital, Monday – Sunday, 8am – 5pm.(ii) (a) Thrombectomy, if available 24/7,  is suitable for approximately 10% of ischaemic strokes, circa 290 procedures in Northern Ireland per annum. A thrombectomy service requires a specialist Interventional Neuroradiology workforce, with a nationally limited workforce pool, who must perform a minimum number of procedures per annum to remain competent.  In this context, it is only viable to commission a thrombectomy service in one hospital in Northern Ireland.  (b) As set out in the Stroke Action Plan, the expansion of the regional thrombectomy service to 24/7 is a key priority. It is estimated that the expansion would cost approximately £5m.  In the context of the disappointing budget outcome, and the consequent significant budgetary pressures, some difficult decisions will be required to manage within the budget allocated.   Meanwhile, the regional Stroke Programme is focusing on maximising the numbers of patients who can access the service within hours available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to make the anti-overdose drug Naloxone available.
answer_text: The Take Home Naloxone scheme, which is overseen by the Public Health Agency (PHA), has been in operation across Northern Ireland since 2012, and has resulted in Naloxone being successfully used to reverse over 1,765 overdoses. Under the Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, there is an action for the PHA to expand the capacity of Naloxone provision to people who use drugs, their peers, family members and those likely to come into contact with those at risk of overdose. Following the recent UK-wide consultation on increasing the number people and the types of professionals who can supply Naloxone to those at risk, my Department has been working with the UK Government to consider amending regulations in this area. Work is underway to bring forward these regulations to both the Westminster Parliament and the NI Assembly in the near future for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has received a bid from the Minister for Infrastructure to facilitate the incorporation of a lifting bridge as part of the Newry Southern Relief Road project.
answer_text: I can confirm that a bid for the Newry Southern Relief Road was submitted for funding within the Belfast Region City Deal. This bid was not specific regarding the details of the scheme. I understand that in April 2024 the Infrastructure Minister reviewed a previous decision made in the absence of the Executive and confirmed that the decision for a 50 metre span fixed bridge remains unchanged. The Infrastructure Minister will be best placed to provide further details regarding the Newry Southern Relief Road project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail all mechanisms available for MLAs to scrutinise PSNI operational decisions.
answer_text: MLAs can raise directly with the Chief Constable any questions or concerns on PSNI operational matters.The recommendations of the Independent Commission for Policing in Northern Ireland, known as the Patten Report, created the Policing Board. It is the Board which has responsibility for the oversight of Policing and to which the Chief Constable is accountable.The Board’s legislation states that it is constituted of MLAs and independent members which means appointed MLAs can directly scrutinise the Chief Constable on all matters, including the PSNI’s operational functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the A29 between Keady and Newtownhamilton will be included in a future road improvement scheme; and (ii) to detail the timeline for any works to be carried out.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to make a bid to the Department of Finance to fund the incorporation of a lifting bridge in the Newry Southern Relief Road project.
answer_text: The additional cost to include the provision of an opening bridge would be in the range of £25m - £30m.  There is no evidence to support that such an investment would be of benefit to the economy of Newry or elsewhere. The divergence of limited resources to the scheme would mean other schemes with proven benefit could not proceed. Therefore I am not planning to make a bid to the Department of Finance to fund an opening bridge

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the merits of facilitating the construction of a lifting bridge, as opposed to a fixed bridge, as part of the Newry Southern Relief Road project, in relation to protecting and enhancing (i) the Newry Ship Canal; (ii) the Albert Basin; and (iii) Newry’s maritime history, heritage and future potential.
answer_text: My Department has engaged extensively with the Department for Communities (DfC) Historic Environment Division (HED), who have responsibility for the designated Scheduled Monument of Newry Ship Canal which is considered as being of high historical significance. A lifting bridge would introduce new features of greater mass within the setting of the Ship Canal creating a detrimental impact on the Scheduled Monument. In contrast, a 50m fixed bridge will add features that would generally be less conspicuous in this setting.Both options would permit vessels access to the Albert Basin.  Whilst the lifting bridge would allow unrestricted access, the opening of such a bridge would be restricted to allow its operation as a strategic route.  The fixed bridge option provides a clearance of 12m height, which would provide access for the majority of vessels using the Newry Canal thereby protecting and enhancing Newry’s maritime history and heritage.It has been demonstrated through modelling that the proposed scheme will contribute to notable improvements in air quality within Newry City Centre as it will redirect traffic away from the city and along a dedicated route to Warrenpoint. This will have health benefits for residents living in Newry.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact that the absence of a Programme for Government will have upon the Executive’s ability to (i) address the challenges facing public services; (ii) deliver initiatives identified as Executive priorities; and (iii) provide effective and accountable government.
answer_text: A Programme for Government provides the Executive with an opportunity to set out its priorities and, assists in addressing the challenges facing public services. The draft Programme for Government was published for public consultation on Monday 9th September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the benefits of fostering closer co-operation and partnership between the Southern Regional College and Dundalk Institute of Technology.
answer_text: Fostering closer co-operation and partnership between Southern Regional College and Dundalk Institute of Technology has the potential to provide great benefits to our learners and to our economy. This potential has already been demonstrated through the two collaborative proposals which Southern Regional College and Dundalk Institute of Technology have recently submitted to the Special EU Programmes Body. These proposals, if successful, will result in an injection of €20 million of funding into sub regional areas that include border counties. This funding will help individuals, including those furthest from the labour market, to develop their skills in an inclusive and supportive environment. It will also help businesses to meet their labour and skills needs, adapt to technological change, improve productivity and drive innovation. I believe partnerships such as this should be encouraged as they have the potential to provide not only great benefit to our learners but also to contribute to a strong, regionally balanced economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the benefits of banning gambling advertising in sports.
answer_text: Gambling advertising is a reserved matter. No assessment has been carried out on the implications of banning gambling advertising in sports.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of increasing the legal age for smoking to 21.
answer_text: Many studies have shown that most young people start smoking in their teenage years, particularly between the ages of 15-17. There will therefore naturally be benefits of increasing the legal age for smoking to 21 as by the time an individual turns 21 the chances of them having become a regular smoker would be reduced. I have indicated my support for Northern Ireland’s inclusion in the UK-wide Tobacco and Vapes Bill. The key policy intention of this Bill is for a gradual phasing out of tobacco use by making it illegal to sell tobacco to anyone born on or after 1 January 2009 (a smoke-free generation approach).  This policy approach is more ambitious than an age 21 approach in the longer term and it relays the important message that there is no safe age to smoke. The Institute of Public Health in Ireland have recently produced a report that concludes that both these approaches to age restrictions on tobacco sales are beneficial, but that a smoke-free generation approach may see additional benefits in terms of youth initiation in the longer term. If agreed, these legislative measures will not impact on current adult smokers, rather they would only be felt when children currently 15 and under (who are not currently legally permitted to be sold tobacco) turn 18.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the systems in place to ensure the efficient recovery of data from ministerial laptops and phones in the event of accidental deletion.
answer_text: Content Manager is the official repository for NICS documents and record management and is backed up daily with regular tests performed to ensure information can be restored successfully. The department also has a digital forensics contract in place with a private sector partner which can be used to assist with the recovery of data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of people currently in prison for drug-related offences, in relation to the overall prison population; and (ii) the number of people currently in prison for offences related to (a) Class A; (b) Class B; and (c) Class C drugs.
answer_text: On 9th May 2024, there were 515 individuals in prison sentenced or remanded to custody for drug-related offences, equating to 27% of the overall prison population on that date.  192 individuals were in prison for offences related to Class A drugs, 314 related to Class B drugs, 259 related to Class C drugs and 13 for drug-related offences where the drugs class cannot be determined. (N.B.  Individuals could be in prison for offences in more than one drugs class so these figures do not sum to the total of 515 individuals).It should be noted that individuals could be in prison for multiple offences, and drug-related offences may not be the main offence for which they were still in custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total expenditure by her Department on (i) policing; (ii) investigating; (iii) prosecuting; and (iv) detaining individuals in relation to offences relating to (a) Class A; (b) Class B; and (c) Class C drugs, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The work undertaken by my Department to police, investigate, prosecute and detain individuals in relation to drugs related offences is carried out by a range of Agencies and Non-Departmental bodies that include the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS), Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS), and the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI). Work on prosecutions is also undertaken by the Public Prosecution Service (PPS).The information requested would require details of costs per case which is not recorded or collated by either NIPS or NICTS. Further detailed analysis would be needed to potentially identify the relevant information on a retrospective basis. However that could only be obtained at disproportionate cost and there is no certainty that the necessary details would be available. My Department does not hold information on expenditure relating to drugs related offences undertaken by the PSNI.  The recording of this information is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.The PPS is independent of my Department. You may, therefore, wish to write directly to the Director of Public Prosecutions to request details of expenditure that PPS may have that is relevant to your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of Amazon’s impact on (i) household refuse and recycling services; and (ii) climate change targets.
answer_text: Statistical information on the quantities of local authority collected (LAC) municipal waste managed here are reported through the Northern Ireland LAC Municipal Waste Management Statistics, produced by my Department.  While the reports provide detailed information on separate waste streams, data is not collected on packaging or waste specific to individual companies or brands. It is therefore not possible for me to assess Amazon’s direct impact on household waste and recycling services here.	The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) received Royal Assent on 6 June 2022. The Act sets a statutory 2050 net zero greenhouse gas emissions reduction target along with ‘must achieve’ targets of at least 48% by 2030, and a 2040 target to be set through secondary legislation.  Progress to meet the targets set out in the Act will be measured by the Northern Ireland Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Inventory report. These reports contain data detailing greenhouse gas emissions in Northern Ireland from 1990 to the latest reporting year. The latest report was published in June 2023 and provides emissions information up to the year 2021. While reports provide data on emissions by sector, they do not provide information on emissions by organisation. It is therefore not possible for me to assess Amazon’s direct impact on climate change targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the ability of mental health outpatient services to meet (i) existing demand; and (ii) anticipated future demand.

To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of (i) the current level of rates liability; and (ii) whether the existing rates structure requires reform to ensure harmonisation between local businesses and large internet commerce businesses.
answer_text: Levels of rates liability, and the valuations underpinning them, vary from property to property, and sector by sector. They are also affected greatly at individual bill level by any support measures provided through rating legislation, and by the district rate poundage applied to the rateable property. The Executive provides almost £250m of rate support per annum to help moderate rates bills and implemented only an inflationary uplift on the regional rate for 2024/25. Business rates already apply to both retail space and logistics/warehouse space, with liability for both sectors assessed on the common basis of the property rental value at a fixed valuation date. An Online Sales Tax (OST), to assess the issue of taxing internet commerce, was consulted upon at Westminster during the absence of an Executive. That ended in May 2022, after which the British government outlined that it would not be proceeding with such a tax. Concerns were raised about complexity and the impact on retailers, businesses and consumers. The consultation noted that many high street retailers (large and small) have an online presence as well as “bricks and mortar” occupancy. In other words, the two spheres are not mutually exclusive, and an OST could create scope for double taxation. The complexity and impact that led to the decision at a UK level would be exacerbated at a local level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s policy in relation to the inclusion of (i) fadas; and (ii) other diacritical marks, on the name badges of employees of (a) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (b) his Department.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a full list of correspondence between his Department and UK government departments in relation to advocating for financial support and direct assistance to offset the burden on the agriculture industry in relation to decarbonisation, since the passage of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I have made verbal representations to UK Government Ministers on a number of occasions as Minister on funding position of my Department, mostly recently on 23 April 2024.The future of earmarked Agricultural Support Funding is a matter I discussed with the Finance Minister on Tuesday 14 May with a view to working together with to build a detailed case to UK Government for future earmarked funding for agriculture, environment, rural and fisheries in conjunction recognising the importance of ensuring that the next funding package meets the needs of our environment, rural communities, farming and fishing businesses, along with needs in relation to our climate and ensuring a genuinely just transition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they hold data on the level of political advertising spend on (i) social media; (ii) traditional media; and (iii) illegally erected posters, 6x4 advertising and billboards for each registered political party.
answer_text: The Executive Office does not hold any data in relation to political advertising spend.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any consideration he has given to the introduction of toll roads to fund infrastructure projects; and (ii) any discussions he has had with his counterpart in the Republic of Ireland in relation to the merits of the introduction of toll roads.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made. While it is right that we consider all of the policy levers available to further transport policy objectives such as transport decarbonisation or modal shift, I am not currently considering the introduction of toll charges on any roads in the North of Ireland and have not had any discussions with my counterpart in the South of Ireland on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact his Department’s recommendation for a fixed bridge on the Southern Relief Road will have on the viability of a lifting bridge as part of the Narrow Water Bridge project.
answer_text: The Department has considered the Narrow Water Bridge project in the assessment of the Newry Southern Relief Road and while the schemes are inter-dependent regarding projected traffic figures and economic cases, the viability of a lifting bridge at Narrow Water is not affected by the design of a 50m non-opening fixed bridge on the Newry Southern Relief Road project. This fixed bridge will have a clearance of 12m for vessels whereas the Narrow Water Bridge, depending on tidal conditions, will only provide clearance between 3m and 6m before it requires opening,The final scheme proposals will be presented with the publication of the Environmental Impact Assessment Report and draft Statutory Orders this Summer, which will offer an opportunity for comment on the proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) individuals currently on a housing waiting list; (ii) families with children under-16 currently on a housing waiting list; (iii) children who are currently homeless; and (iv) new homes that need to be built in order to meet current housing demand.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) the total number of applicants on the housing waiting list as at end December 2023 is 46,461. (ii) families with children under-16 currently on a housing waiting list is 14,966.(iii) The number of children within homeless families is estimated at 18,384 at end December 2023. This is based on the families with children household classification groupings (12-20) however, this is an approximation given that some of the classifications stipulate a minimum or above, e.g. 4 or more children or 2+ children, and that household group is recorded at point of application and may not reflect current composition. Household classification 21 (large adult) has not been included as either 0 children or 1 child form part of these households, so cannot be quantified from the data.  (iv) The projected social housing need at a Northern Ireland level for the period 2023-28 has been assessed as 24,922 dwellings”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.Social housing new build is not the only way to meet housing demand: stock turnover (re-let of existing social housing accommodation) and (for transfer applicants) mutual exchange of properties are the primary means in which waiting list demand for social housing is addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a timeline for the inclusion of the North in (i) Ireland’s Ancient East; and (ii) the Wild Atlantic Way.
answer_text: Discussions are ongoing and it is not yet possible to provide a definitive timeline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration was given to pollinators before mowing the grass in the Stormont Estate.
answer_text: In recognising the part the Stormont Estate can play in protecting the natural habitat, the area for formal tended lawns have been considerably reduced in recent years with large areas now left untended as hay meadows and other areas sown out in wildflower to encourage and restore pollinator populations. The Department seeks to maintain a balance on the Estate between formal lawns and hay and wildflower meadows.		The grass cutting in the formal lawns on the Stormont Estate was delayed this year due to prolonged wet weather. During this delay, the department received queries from a number of MLAs asking for the mowing to commence as soon as possible. As the weather and ground conditions have improved, grass cutting of the formal lawns has resumed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the difference in investment in road resurfacing and surface defect works between local council areas; and (ii) what work his Department will be undertaking to address this imbalance.
answer_text: In distributing the budget available for road maintenance, allocations relating to the areas covered by my Department’s four Roads Divisions are made on the basis of need using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity (i.e. resurfacing, surface dressing drainage etc.).  These take into consideration criteria such as road length, structural condition, traffic and population (for footway resurfacing).For routine maintenance activities (i.e. patching, grass cutting etc) factors including defect information, areas of grass, numbers of gullies etc are taken into consideration in distributing the resources available. In addition to this, the capacity of my Department’s internal contractor, and any associated requirement to use external contractors, is factored in to ensure that the current ‘Limited Maintenance Service’ is delivered. Divisions will also broadly use these indicators when apportioning across council areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution of funds.However, the exact combination of indicators and metrics used within each Roads Division will vary and be tailored to the individual needs and circumstances in that Division at that time. Yearly allocations vary to reflect funding pressures within Section Office areas and are also influenced by specific allocations for high priority works such as Trunk Road Resurfacing schemes and structural stability, including coastal defence repairs. As such, it would not be appropriate for a local Section Office or Council area to receive a fixed percentage of the available allocation each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any financial assistance available to Ashgrove Rovers Ladies social soccer team to assist with costs in relation to a cross-community charity soccer match.
answer_text: There are currently no funding opportunities within my Department nor Sport NI, my Department’s arm’s length body, to which Ashgrove Rovers Ladies social soccer team could apply to assist with costs of a proposed cross-community football match. In terms of any future funding opportunities, Sport NI will regularly publish these on its website Sport NI – Funding (www.sportni.net/funding/). I would encourage any interested clubs seeking to apply for funding to register with Sport NI via their website.In addition, I am aware that Newry, Mourne and Down District Council operates a number of grant and financial assistance programmes to contribute towards community projects and I would suggest that Ashgrove Rovers Ladies contact their local Council for further details or access online at www.newrymournedown.org/residents-grants-and-funding

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive will be reinstated.
answer_text: In my reply, dated 19 February 2024, to your Assembly Question (AQW 6481/22-27 refers) regarding the reinstatement of the Cross Border Healthcare Directive, I outlined the background and closure of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS). Following the success of the scheme I introduced in June 2021, I had hoped to introduce a new reimbursement scheme this year. I included a bid for funding for a new replacement, which could have treated over 4,000 patients during this year’s budget setting process. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10315/22-27, for his assessment of the benefits to the health and wellbeing of Troubles survivors by including those suffering from post-traumatic stress disorder and other psychological trauma in the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme (TPDPS) is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board which is independent of the Department of Health and is an arm’s length body of The Executive Office (TEO).  The TPDPS is defined in the Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020 and its purpose is to acknowledge the harm suffered by those injured in the Troubles in Northern Ireland and to promote reconciliation between people in connection with Northern Ireland’s troubled past.  That includes victims with a permanent physical disablement and/or a permanent psychological disablement, including post-traumatic stress disorder or other psychological trauma.  Under the provisions of the TPDPS, an individual entitled to a payment must have a permanent disablement of at least 14% as a result of a Troubles-related incident. Successful applicants to the scheme will benefit both financially through the provision of a payment to help address the financial harms suffered as well as through a process of recognition and acknowledgment of any physical and psychological harms caused.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 10315/22-27, for her assessment of the benefits of including Troubles victims suffering from post-traumatic stress disorder and other psychological trauma, in the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme (TPDPS) is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board which is independent of the Department of Justice and is an arm’s length body of The Executive Office (TEO).  The TPDPS is defined in the Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020 and its purpose is to acknowledge the harm suffered by those injured in the Troubles in Northern Ireland and to promote reconciliation between people in connection with Northern Ireland’s troubled past.  That includes victims with a permanent physical disablement and/or a permanent psychological disablement, including post-traumatic stress disorder or other psychological trauma.  Under the provisions of the TPDPS, an individual entitled to a payment must have a permanent disablement of at least 14% as a result of a Troubles-related incident.  Successful applicants to the scheme will benefit both financially through the provision of a payment to help address the financial harms suffered as well as through a process of recognition and acknowledgment of any physical and psychological harms caused.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total patient catchment; and (ii) the total patient throughput for each hospital in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Information on the total patient catchment area of each hospital is not held within my Department. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were, therefore, asked for input. All five indicated that while each HSCT has a defined population, each hospital does not. Belfast also noted that some of its community services go beyond the Belfast HSCT area.The patient throughput for each hospital, which had any inpatients in the previous ten financial years, is outlined in Tables 1 and 2 overleaf, by five-year ranges. Patient throughput is a measure of the average number of inpatients treated in each available bed each year in wards which are open overnight. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of incident reports filed in relation to falling curtain rails, where injury was caused to (i) a patient; (ii) a staff member, broken down by hospital in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of incident reports filed is not held centrally. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) was, therefore, asked to provide a response.In total, six incident reports have been filed in the five years from 2019 to 2023, in relation to falling curtain rails where injury was caused, in the Craigavon Area, Daisy Hill, and South Tyrone hospitals in the Southern HSCT. As figures between 1 and 4 are not provided under the rules of statistical disclosure, the figure of six incident reports has not been broken down.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to meet with the PSNI to discuss road safety on the A1.
answer_text: At a recent meeting with the PSNI I had a constructive discussion on how to work collectively to improve road safety across the North.   I remain committed to the A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme which has been informed by an extensive review of the road safety issues on this road.  As part of the budget process, I will be considering my priorities and determining which schemes I can progress with the funding that has been allocated to my department. In the interim, my officials continually monitor the safety on the A1 to ensure the current measures are appropriate.  This includes regular review of PSNI collision statistics and their causation factors along this route.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If attitudes towards speeding and careless driving do not change, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to rise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to bring forward a multi-year budget prior to the end of this mandate.
answer_text: I have made clear to Executive colleagues, my desire to set a multi-year budget to allow departments to plan more effectively. However, this is dependent on the period covered by the next Spending Review, which is decided upon by the British Government.As 2024-25 is the last year of the current Spending Review period, the Executive could only develop a one-year budget. However, I have pressed both the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and the shadow Secretary of State on the need for a multi-year settlement going forward.
