question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the benefits of implementing the recommendations contained in the Review of the structural maintenance funding requirements for the Department for Infrastructure; (ii) what steps his Department has taken to implement those recommendations; and (iii) for his assessment of the impact the budget allocation will have on his Department’s ability to implement those recommendations.
answer_text: (i) The Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads) (known as the Barton Report) was completed in November 2018. The Review made five recommendations, the main one relating to the level and timing of funding made available for structural maintenance of the road network. Greater certainty of budgets, that meet the level of funding recommended in the report, would allow my Department to bring forward much more effective and properly resourced maintenance programmes. The report suggests it should be entirely possible to achieve significant efficiencies if adequate long-term budgets were in place. These savings might allow the structural maintenance budget to be reduced longer term or, better still, allow my Department to make a much needed impact on reducing the maintenance backlog, which would, in turn, yield even greater efficiencies.(ii) My Department continues to use the evidence to argue for increased funding in line with the recommended level of £143m (at 2018 prices). My Department has continued to submit bids in line with the recommendation and has included a pragmatic ramping up approach which reflects the capacity of internal and external resources. My officials continue to work closely with the Department of Finance to secure long-term funding options and, in particular, to highlight the need for multi-year budgets.  In addition, in association with my Department’s Pavement Management System (PMS) provider my officials have developed a suite of specialised reports.  These assist local engineers to have a better understanding of their network’s condition and therefore better use of targeted funding backed up by industry standard road condition data. For the 2024/25 year, my Department is also implementing a move to increase the frequency of condition surveys on the unclassified network from 10% annually to 25% annually, which will provide a more accurate picture of the current state of the unclassified road network.   (iii) My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made. It is abundantly clear that we need to increase the level of investment in structural maintenance of our transport infrastructure. However, ongoing budgetary constraints mean that my Department cannot do all that we would like. The ability to implement the recommendations of the report and increase the level of structural maintenance and the capacity to deliver, it is directly linked to the level of resources made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the implications of the Budget 2024-25 on his Department’s ability to support farmers and the agri-foods industry with decarbonisation initiatives.
answer_text: The 2024/25 budget position is extremely challenging, and I will be exploring all options to support the agri-food industry to improve environmental sustainability, including the reduction in carbon emissions. 		No department has received the level of funding it has bid for.  With increased demands on services and rising costs, I recognise our allocation won’t provide sufficient funding to do everything we want so I will need to prioritise the funding envelope we have been given.  	Funding for the delivery of some initiatives in 2024/25 to support farm businesses to reduce their carbon emissions, such as the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme, will mainly come from the HMT earmarked budget for Agriculture, Agri-Environment and Wider Rural economy. This earmarked budget was set by HMT in the Spending Review in October 2021 and cannot be used elsewhere in DAERA or the NI Block.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of (i) the benefits of enhancing support for sporting initiatives such as GAA for All; and (ii) the impact that such support would have on the emotional, cognitive and physical development of children and young people with additional needs.
answer_text: Sporting initiatives are not part of the Northern Ireland curriculum.  Physical Education (PE) is, however, a stand-alone area of learning within our curriculum and is a statutory entitlement for every pupil from foundation stage to key stage 4.  The aim of the PE curriculum is to develop physically literate individuals who have the knowledge, skills and confidence to enjoy a lifetime of healthy physical activity.Schools are the most important, but not the only, context for promoting healthy lifestyles in young people.  Pupils should have opportunities to take part in daily physical activities, either indoors or outdoors, such as physical play and regular planned sessions of PE.Although there is no statutory time requirement for PE in schools (and in fact primary legislation does not allow for this in respect of any subject), my department recommends that schools should provide pupils with at least two hours of good quality curricular PE each week.  Physical movement and exercise for some children may be a challenge, which is why programmes such as Special Olympics’ ‘Young Athletes’ are so important in promoting inclusion and exercise for all, irrespective of ability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the benefits of enhancing support for sporting initiatives such as GAA for All; and (ii) the impact that such support would have on the emotional, cognitive and physical development of children and young people with additional needs.
answer_text: Increasing participation opportunities in all sports is a priority for both my Department and our arms length body Sport NI.‘Active Living’ the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland recognises the positive impact that participation in sport and physical activity can have on both physical and mental health for children and young people.One of the goals of the strategy is to ensure that sport and physical activity is inclusive, shared, fun and offers equality of opportunity to participate for all, and that children and young people are given the best start in life through a wider choice of sport and physical activity and more suitable offerings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact the budget allocation will have on his Department’s ability to facilitate the creation of additional university places.
answer_text: I am committed to working with the higher education sector to grow student numbers in a sustainable manner, including moving towards 10,000 places at Ulster University’s Magee campus in line with the NDNA commitment.I am working with my officials to prioritise the allocation of budget within my Department and to drive forward engagement with the universities and the Magee Taskforce on options for bringing more students to our institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the rationale for earmarking £45 million for welfare reform mitigations; and (ii) the discussions she has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to how his Department plans to spend this allocation.
answer_text: Welfare reform mitigations represent a previous Executive commitment.Where funding is provided to honour a previous Executive commitment, it is normal for this to be earmarked to ensure it cannot be used for any other purpose.In making allocations to departments for Budget 2024-25, Executive earmarked funding was provided at the level identified by relevant departments in their budget submissions.As part of the Budget information gathering exercise the Department for Communities submitted details of their requirements to provide the Welfare Mitigations mandated by the Executive. Further to departmental written proposals, I conducted bilateral discussions with Ministers in preparation for the Budget. While I therefore met with the Communities Minister as part of the Budget process, the use of this allocation was not specifically covered as it must be used to deliver the mitigations previously agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of (a) the British government’s announcement in relation to changes to the awarding of Personal Independence Payment (PIP); and (b) the impact this announcement will have on PIP recipients; and (ii) what steps his Department will be taking to mitigate the impact on PIP claimants.
answer_text: My officials and I are currently engaging with officials in the Department for Work and Pensions to understand the proposed changes to Personal Independence Payment and will assess the impact in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the implications of the Budget 2024-25 on (i) the upgrade of electrical infrastructure at Daisy Hill Hospital; (ii) the installation of an MRI twin diagnostic suite at Daisy Hill Hospital; and (iii) the delivery of Newry’s primary care hub.
answer_text: In 2024-25, the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has been allocated £2m of capital funding to progress the low voltage electrical infrastructure works at Daisy Hill Hospital.The Trust submitted a proposal for an MRI twin diagnostic suite at Daisy Hill Hospital. The Trust will be invited to submit a business case for this proposal once budget affordability is confirmed. In 2024/25, £3.8m has been set aside in 2024-25 for the Newry Community Treatment and Care Centre, pending business case approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to her Department’s £25 million budget allocation for childcare, to detail what this allocation will translate to in terms of the level of free childcare provision per household.
answer_text: The Executive recognises the difficulties faced by the childcare sector and families.Budget 2024-25 demonstrates our commitment to supporting the development of a Childcare Strategy. The £25 million set aside in the Budget will be used to fund new and additional actions to be undertaken by departments within their remits on childcare.  The Education Minister has established a cross-departmental task and finish group to bring forward recommendations for the Executive’s consideration which will inform how this centrally held funding will be allocated.  Measures are under development at present and are expected to be fully costed and to detail any impacts on childcare provision when submitted to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the £45 million allocated to his Department for welfare reform mitigations in the Budget 2024-25; and (ii) what welfare reform mitigations his Department will implement through utilisation of that funding allocation.
answer_text: The funding allocated in relation to welfare mitigations for the 2024-25 financial year will allow my Department to continue to deliver the existing mitigation schemes, including providing support in full for those people impacted by the Benefit Cap and Social Sector Size Criteria.This allocation will provide funding for the Universal Credit Contingency Fund, the Social Supermarket Scheme and support for the Independent Advice Sector.  Associated administration and IT costs for the existing mitigation schemes will also be met through this funding allocation.Finally, there is funding made available to progress with my Department’s statutory obligation to assess and report on the operation of the welfare mitigation schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the financial support available to businesses struggling to pay their rates’ bill.
answer_text: Support is provided to 75% of non-domestic ratepayers, who get help with their rates bills through annual policy interventions, costing in the region of almost £250m per annum. The support provided includes the Small Business Rate Relief (SBRR) Scheme, recently extended by my Department to provide £21.5m of assistance to almost 30,000 small businesses receiving a reduction of between 20% to 50% on their 2024/25 rate bill.The Executive is also providing support in 2024/25 through Industrial Derating for over 4,400 local manufacturing properties at a cost of £71.5m, a measure which isn’t provided in England, Scotland or Wales. Other support provision includes Freight Transport Relief, Sport and Recreation Relief, Community Amateur Sports Club Relief, Non-domestic Vacant Rating relief, Residential Care relief, Halls of Residence exemption, and Hardship Relief for those in exceptional circumstances. The range of support provided is detailed in full, along with the eligibility criteria, on the nibusinessinfo.com website. If any business is struggling to pay their rates bill they should contact Land & Property Services at the earliest opportunity to arrange a payment plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what funding is available for organisations, such as the Camlough Brass and Reed Band, to pay for essential resources such as sheet music, new uniforms and musical instruments.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland delivers the Musical Instruments Programme on behalf of my Department. This is a competitive capital fund intended to improve the quality of music-making in the community by helping eligible bands, groups and artists to purchase new instruments and/or replace worn out instruments.  The number of awards is limited by the amount of funding available, which is not yet known for 24-25.The Arts Council run a number of programmes through the course of the year which might be helpful. Details of funding opportunities are published on the Arts Council website, social media channels and e-newsletter as they arise.Funding for uniforms and sheet music fall outside of my Department and the Arts Council’s remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any roads in Newtownhamilton, and the surrounding areas, are included in upcoming road resurfacing projects.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the publication of the Budget 2024-25, for his assessment of the impact it will have on (i) cancer diagnosis waiting times; and (ii) cancer treatment waiting times.
answer_text: The budget that has been set by the Executive for my Department is not sufficient to maintain services at current levels. This means it is not possible to spend as envisaged to reduce waiting lists, as we have to prioritise funding to simply maintain existing services.As part of the Government settlement at the return of the Assembly, £34m was earmarked for waiting lists. This does not even cover half of what is required to maintain current levels of red-flag (cancer) and time critical additionality. My officials are currently working with HSC Trusts to examine if it is possible to displace other hospital services to the full amount of the remaining 2024/25 investment needed (£41-46m).If we are unable to fully do so, it would lead to longer waiting times or reducing the amount of care available to red-flag and time critical patients. The resulting impact of the budget on red-flag and time critical services could be a significant deterioration in performance against cancer targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration his Department has given to the introduction of a Social Housing Decarbonisation Fund.
answer_text: My Department has developed a Housing Supply Strategy which acknowledges the importance of ensuring we have good quality homes.  One of the proposed long-term commitments of the Strategy is to ensure there is an emphasis on improving the energy efficiency of homes and ensuring a just transition to decarbonised solutions.  		My Department has also supported the Housing Executive, in securing and utilising €22.951 million from the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) for their Energy Efficiency in Social Housing Programme with match funding from the Housing Executive.  The programme improved the thermal efficiency of 1,406 non-traditional construction homes within the Housing Executive stock.		A further £57.5 million has been made available for the Housing Executive’s thermal improvement and low carbon programmes in the financial year 2024/25.		My officials are also working on developing the NIHE revitalisation programme and a potential future retrofit funding model for registered Housing Associations. 		As I have stated in answering AQO 330/22-27 and AQW 9927/22-27, given this level of investment in social home decarbonisation alongside the severe financial and budgetary constraints that all Departments are currently facing, I have no plans to introduce a separate Social Housing Decarbonisation Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reconsider his decision to decline the invitation to meet with councillors from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in relation to service provision at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: I received a letter from Ms Marie Ward, Chief Executive of Newry Mourne and Down District Council on 16th April 2024. This letter requested that I reconsider attending the meeting with senior health officials to discuss Emergency General Surgery services at Daisy Hill Hospital, and I have responded directly to Ms Ward on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the action his Department will be taking to ensure the new Casement Park stadium will (i) be inclusive and welcoming to people from all communities and sporting backgrounds; and (ii) fulfill its potential as a beacon for reconciliation and a united people.
answer_text: The Department entered into a funding agreement with the UCGAA in December 2013. This legally binding funding agreement commits compliance with all obligations in relation to anti-discrimination and equality legislation. Additionally, the project must be operated in compliance with sections 75 and 76 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what support his Department can make available to farmers whose land remains flooded and whose crops cannot be planted due to unseasonably high levels of rainfall and flooding.
answer_text: I am aware that this year’s very wet and late Spring is presenting challenges for farmers operating in almost every sector within the industry. I am conscious of the wider impact if wet weather continues in the time ahead and I have made representations to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention if the situation lasts for much longer.Meanwhile, my officials in the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) continue to monitor the situation very closely, maintaining contact with stakeholders and farmers across the range of sectors within the industry.CAFRE has released a number of advisory articles on the management of farming operations following this period of prolonged inclement weather and tailored advice is also being offered at group meetings and through one-to-one discussions with individual farmers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress she has made in securing funding to facilitate the construction of Casement Park.
answer_text: As Minister with overall responsibility for the Casement Park project, your question to the Minister of Finance has been passed to me to respond to. The current allocation of public funding towards the redevelopment of Casement Park is £62.5 million. The UCGAA contribution remains at £15 million, while the Irish government announced in January 2024 its intention to contribute €50 million (circa £43 million). I am currently awaiting confirmation from the UK Government on their funding commitment towards the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) when his Department’s tourism strategy was scheduled to be published; and (ii) when it will be published.
answer_text: It is my intention to bring forward a Tourism Action Plan in response to my Department’s recent consultation. At the Tourism NI conference on 23 April, I announced that to help take this work forward I will establish a Tourism Partnership Board made up of representatives from tourism and hospitality, Executive Departments, and local government. It will help develop and oversee an Action Plan that will be co-designed and co-delivered with the industry as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail his Department’s annual expenditure for lawn and grass cutting in each of the last five years; and (ii) to provide his assessment of the benefits, including carbon offsetting, of allowing grassed areas to become wildflower meadows.
answer_text: Expenditure specifically incurred on lawn and grass cutting is not recorded within our finance systems, however I have provided the figures for grass cutting, weed control and tree maintenance for the past five complete financial years in table below.Grass Cutting, Weeds and Tree Maintenance2018-192019-202020-212021-222022-23£m5.75.96.06.66.7The Department’s approach to verge management was recently changed to increase the focus on environmental benefit, without compromising on roads safety or our duties to deal with noxious weeds on DfI maintained land. This change occurred because I recognise the environmental benefits of road verges and our approach seeks to encourage the establishment of wildflowers in these areas. Wildflowers, and in particular native species, promote biodiversity, provide habitats for pollinators, and can enhance the aesthetic value of an area. Additionally, wildflower meadows serve as carbon sinks, by helping to sequester carbon from the atmosphere and thereby contribute to our efforts to combat climate change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the timeline for completion of the tendering process for the Casement Park project; and (ii) when he expects the construction of Casement Park to be completed.
answer_text: Following the announcement by the UK Government on 13 September 2024 that it will not be providing funding to seek to build Casement Park in time to host Euro 2028, I will be engaging with all partners on the next steps for the project in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will implement the recommendations contained in the strategy document published by the Royal College of General Practitioners, entitled A Workforce Fit for the Future.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners  ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy and was pleased to speak at its recent launch.  I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice that are set out in the strategy and the impact of the increasing demand and workload pressures on GPs that it highlights.  My Department is working with GPs to help General Practice to deliver, to grow and to be in the vanguard of how we transform health and social care for our population and for our workforce. My Department has taken forward a range of actions that will help address a number of the workforce and workload challenges and recommendations set out in the RCGP strategy.  These include:  	Focussing on building the GP workforce, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.Taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists; all of which are making a difference to the capacity of primary care services and how those services are delivered; and are contributing to improved patient outcomes.Taking speedy action to progress the 2023/24 Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Board recommendation for GPs.  I recently approved the DDRB recommendation of a 6% uplift for independent General Medical Practitioners.  In addition, I also approved an uplift of 6% for staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift (3% recurrent) for other expenses.  Providing significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services.  This has included £3.4m funding to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services to support the capacity of GP practices to meet the anticipated increase in demand over the winter period.  My Department also made £4.3m funding available to assist GP practices to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes this winter.  In addition to this, £2.7m was reallocated from other areas of the GP budget to boost capacity in the service and help manage demand.  This included non-recurrent funding support for additional costs of indemnity.  Continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland.  This includes taking forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland. Establishing the GP Access Working Group to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for both patients and practices.		The development of guidance that will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care.  	E

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits of (i) transforming the Education Welfare Service in the context of the Education Welfare Service Transformation Project Report; and (ii) releasing funding for the Pay and Grading Review with a view to ensuring all children have access to a full-time education.
answer_text: The transformation of the Education Welfare Service (EWS) is essential to enable the smooth running of the service and to support schools a timelier and more effectively manner. The transformation project is currently paused due to ongoing industrial action. The proposed approach to transformation focusses on ensuring a continuous improvement of the service through the following key areas:  The development of a more preventative and proactive model of delivery.Collaborative working with pupils, parents, and wider partners to provide accessible and visible services.Developing consistent policy, standards, and guidance.Ensuring appropriate IT and office infrastructure. Developing an Outcomes Based Accountability (OBA) Framework.Career Progression and Professional Development for the workforce.I remain committed to addressing the pay and grading issues and will be working with the Finance Minister to find a resolution for education support workers.  Recognising the importance of these workers, some of whom are amongst the lowest paid in the sector, the Executive has agreed to seek approval from the Treasury to reprofile some of the funding provided in the financial package for this purpose.  This is aimed at ensuring that support staff are paid at an appropriate level for the vital work they do in support of our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress on the development of Newry’s Primary Care Hub.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any actions they have taken in relation to the establishment of a Citizen’s Assembly on a New Ireland; and (ii) any discussions they have had with the Taoiseach’s office in relation to the establishment of a Citizen’s Assembly on a New Ireland.
answer_text: We have not taken any actions in relation to the establishment of a Citizen’s Assembly on a New Ireland and we have had no discussions with the Taoiseach’s office in relation to this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for a breakdown of (i) air pollution levels; (ii) overall air quality; and (iii) the fluctuation in those indicators, for every town and city in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Information on air pollution levels, overall air quality and fluctuation in air quality indicators is available on the DAERA website, www.airqualityni.co.uk. This data is available on a real time basis, and the website contains all historical data for every monitoring station in Northern Ireland.  Annual reports on Air Quality are also available on this website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s policies, including relevant protocols and estimated processing times, in relation to the investigation of age discrimination complaints in a Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care Trusts have a range of Policies and Procedures in place to manage staff complaints and concerns. Trusts referenced the HSC Regional Conflict, Bullying and Harassment (CBH) Policy and Procedure as an appropriate policy with regards to complaints on age discrimination.  The CBH Policy outlines that Formal Procedure – Investigations should be completed within a period of 8 – 12 weeks, however processing times are often impacted by a variety of reasons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s protocol for having new medications licensed in Northern Ireland that are not yet licensed elsewhere in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the licensing of new medications in Northern Ireland (NI). Currently medicines are licensed via two authorisation routes when placed on the NI market. As detailed below, these authorisation routes permit marketing authorisation holders (MAHs) to licence new medications for the NI market and not have an equivalent licence for them elsewhere in the United Kingdom (UK). Firstly, NI continues to follow the EU acquis for medicines while Great Britain (GB) does not. As a consequence, all novel and innovative medicines that fall under the scope of the EU’s Centralised Authorisation Procedure (EU CAP) are licensed for the NI market when granted an authorisation by the European Commission (EC). These EC authorisations are not licensed for the GB market. MAHs must apply for a separate authorisation from the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) to have these products licensed for the GB market. Secondly, all other medicines that fall outside the scope of the EU’s CAP can be authorised by the UK Competent Authority, MHRA. MAHs can apply to MHRA for an authorisation to license their product for either the GB, NI or the whole UK market. As medicine licences are currently authorised via these two authorisation routes, via the EU CAP or via the MHRA, this permits MAHs to apply for new medicine licences that are only authorised for the NI market and not elsewhere in the UK. My Department is not responsible for applications or approvals of new medicine licences for the NI market.Under Windsor Framework arrangements, from 1 January 2025, the EU CAP will no longer apply in NI, which means all medicine licences for the NI market will be authorised by MHRA. MAHs will no longer be able to apply for a GB only licence for their products, but the option to apply for an NI only or UK wide licence will remain. Therefore, a new medicine licence could be authorised by MHRA that is still only available for the NI market and not elsewhere in the UK from 1 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the street and road surfaces that have received approval and funding from his Department for resurfacing in the Newry and Armagh constituency for the 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) when those resurfacing works are expected to be completed.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the merits of open competition for public procurement contracts.
answer_text: The over-riding procurement policy requirement is that all public procurement must be based on value for money, and this should be achieved through open competition, unless there are compelling reasons to do otherwise.Open competitions for public procurement contracts promote transparency, value for money, efficiency and effectiveness in public expenditure and contributes to the competitiveness of suppliers and contractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the efforts he is making to ensure that planning offices are fully resourced.
answer_text: Resourcing of council planning offices is a matter for each individual council. This resource is supported by the Transferred Functions Grant, administered by the Department for Communities. However, I recognise the importance of securing the long-term financial sustainability of the whole planning system and my officials are working in collaboration with local government through the Planning Improvement Programme to consider this matter going forward.In addition, it is my intention to bring forward an inflationary uplift to planning fees this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the benefits of incorporating best practice processes from the planning system in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way and where appropriate best practice from other planning systems will be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the merits of introducing a structural warranty system for construction.
answer_text: I understand that this question relates to the construction of infrastructure assets. My Department has robust processes that apply during the design, construction and maintenance of structures to ensure they are fit for purpose.For example, when a new bridge or structure is to be constructed near the road network, Technical Approval of the design must be in place before construction is started. Contractors working for my Department operate a quality management system which manages the risks during the construction phase. Throughout the working life of a Department bridge, routine inspections are carried out in compliance with the requirements of the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges to ensure the bridge remains in serviceable condition. Additionally, NI Water requires appointed Contractors/Consultants to carry Professional Indemnity insurance to the value of the contract, to cover any emerging defects after the contractual defects period is over. This is currently required to be in place for 6 years after the contract but is being extended to 12 years as their frameworks are renewed.I am assured that construction risks are appropriately managed by my Department and that no additional structural warranty system is needed for infrastructure assets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the criteria his Department will be assessing hospitals on when considering where to locate regional centres of excellence; (ii) the consultation models to be used during that assessment process; and (iii) the stakeholders that will be consulted as part of that process.
answer_text: The Elective Care Framework published in June 2021 sets out my approach to transforming the delivery of elective care.  Centres of Excellences, shaped as Elective Care Centres, are at the heart of this policy.These Centres will improve patient outcomes by increasing the productivity, efficiency and reliability of the service.  By providing services for the region, they will also drive equitable access to care.We already have a number of Centres of Excellence in place – Day Procedure Centres are operational at Lagan Valley Hospital and Omagh Hospital supporting a range of surgical specialties.  We have three Elective Overnight Stay Centres at the Mater Hospital, South West Acute Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital for intermediate complexity surgery that may require an overnight stay in hospital.  In addition, there are cataracts centres at Downe, South Tyrone and Mid Ulster Hospitals, an orthopaedic hub at Musgrave Park Hospital which includes the Duke of Connaught Unit (a dedicated orthopaedic Day Procedure Centre) and Rapid Diagnostic Centres have been established at Whiteabbey and South Tyrone Hospitals.Decisions on future Centres of Excellence will be shaped by a variety of factors including, for example, the outcome of service reviews and the draft Acute Hospital Reconfiguration Blueprint.   A wide range of stakeholders (clinicians, other professionals, managers and services users and others) are engaged as part of these review processes.  Decisions will also be taken in the context of the available budget, infrastructure and workforce required to ensure sustainable delivery.  My Department will ensure that it complies with its statutory obligations on consultation and public engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a timeline for the publication of a review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grants provision.
answer_text: The review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants is examining a range of options for increasing the numbers able to access free school meals and uniform grants.  The options being considered include raising the income thresholds that apply to some of the means tested criteria, the introduction of universal free school meals provision to pupils in some school years and those that are a combination of the two. My officials are currently updating the modelling of the options to take into account the managed migration of those in receipt of some benefits to Universal Credit that is being led by the Department for Communities. I plan to launch a public consultation on the options for changing the eligibility criteria in the autumn of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work to repair and resurface (i) Crossbane Road; and (ii) Rathcumber Road, Derrynoose, will commence.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.The Crossbane Road and Rathcumber Road will both be considered for inclusion in a future programme of work when they will be prioritised against the many other roads in need of investment.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many concerns for safety calls have been received by the PSNI in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The recording and collation of concerns for safety calls is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of registered intermediaries which can be engaged by her Department or its bodies as appropriate
answer_text: There is currently a panel of 34 Registered Intermediaries. The Department draws from this panel to engage Registered Intermediaries to assist victims, witnesses and suspects / defendants with communication difficulties during the police interview and at trial.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has liaised with the PSNI in relation to ensuring accessibility and inclusivity in relation to (i) their social media channels through alternative text; and (ii) speed detection vans blocking footpaths and thereby impeding accessibility.
answer_text: In response to part (i) of your question, I can advise that this is a matter entirely for the PSNI.  You may wish to direct your question to the PSNI.  In response to part (ii) of your question, I can confirm that Safety Camera vehicles are operated by the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership.  You may wish to direct your question to the Partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total value of grant funding provided by (i) his Department; and (ii) the National Lottery to (a) the IFA; (b) the GAA; (c) Ulster Rugby in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information required to answer this question, I have arranged for their information to be provided in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the grants available for the Newry Maritime Association to celebrate their bicentennial by illuminating the lighthouse at Greencastle, County Down.
answer_text: My Department supports grants to community-based festivals under the Community Festivals Fund (CFF).  Local councils administer the grants and provide match-funding.  The Association may wish to check with their local council for eligibility and application process for CFF.   The Association may also wish to contact the Fundraising Advice Officer within NICVA who can provide advice on securing funding alongside providing details of available grant funding through the NICVA grant tracker. Contact details can be accessed at this address: https://www.nicva.org/services/fundraising.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of (i) the total number of (a) PSNI officers; and (b) civilian staff in the Rape Crime Unit; (ii) the number of senior detectives in the unit; and (iii) the average length of service for (a) PSNI officers; and (b) civilian staff in the unit.
answer_text: The allocation of policing resources is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the impact of Troubles-related violence and trauma on his Department; and (ii) the resource implications for the health service in relation to this.
answer_text: (i) the impact of Troubles-related violence and trauma on his Department;Research indicates that 40% of the adult population have experienced a significant Troubles related trauma, such as the death of a loved one or injury to themselves.A study suggests that 118,000 adults in Northern Ireland will meet the criteria for PTSD at some point in their lifetime (8.8% of the population). Troubles related events are a factor in 27% of cases.We know however, that most individuals who have been affected by troubles related to trauma do not come forward for treatment. In one study 85% have not done so. (ii) the resource implications for the health service in relation to this. The Regional Trauma Network (RTN) has been established to address the long-term mental health consequences of the Troubles. The RTN incorporates a wide range of support and services for victims and survivors both in the statutory sector, and in the community & voluntary (C&V) sector.  The RTN enables a co-ordinated approach to supporting individuals with the right care, at the right time, in the right place – and this can involve Health and Social Care Trusts and C&V organisations working together to meet assessed needs.  The RTN has been fully operational since the beginning of the 2023/24 financial year. In respect of that year (i) Circa 9,000 individuals have availed of services within the RTN through the Victims and Survivors Service and its 47 community and voluntary partner organisations, and (ii) Circa 600 individuals have availed of services through the Health and Social Care Trust RTN Teams.   In addition, many thousands of victims and survivors are receiving care and treatment from generic mental health services across all five Trusts.The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme was established by section 10 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019 and The Executive Office (TEO) is responsible for taking forward the implementation of the Victims’ Payment Regulations 2020.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) have established a regional administrative and clinical team to retrieve available medical records for applicants to the Scheme and to complete a report on permanent disablements attributable to a Troubles Related Incident which meets the relevant criteria of the Scheme.  The team has received requests to seek medical records for 970 individuals since it became operational on 1st July 2023. Funding for the HSC RTN central team and the 5 trust trauma teams is secured on a recurrent basis. The TPDP Scheme has been fully resourced by The Executive Office based on projected demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the current resource provision in the PSNI in relation to the investigation of rape and sexual violence cases.
answer_text: The allocation of policing resources is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he will be taking to address the rise in waiting times for children which have increased by 172 per cent since 2016, according to the 2024 report by the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health, Worried and Waiting: A review of paediatric waiting times in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It should be noted that despite the increasing waiting lists, Trusts are working very hard to see and treat large numbers of children and I would like to commend staff for their efforts. Despite the high number of children attending appointments, the number being referred for secondary care services is increasing exponentially, and we therefore need to understand the cause of that rise in referrals.Before the publication of the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health (RCPCH) Worried and Waiting Report, I had a very productive meeting with representatives of the RCPCH to discuss the report and its recommendations in detail and listen to suggestions of how to improve services.In addition to considering the issues raised by the RCPCH, the Child Health Partnership Programme Board is working hard to rebuild and reconfigure services following the pandemic – we will work closely with the RCPCH on this.I have already said that tackling waiting lists is a priority, but the scale of the waiting list crisis demonstrates the need for multi-year funding.  However, I am also keen to ensure that steps are taken within current resources to manage demand to ensure that children and young people are seen in a timely manner in the right settings appropriate to their individual needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of flooding preparedness, response, and recovery in Newry, Mourne and Down and Armagh.
answer_text: In accordance with Civil Contingencies Framework arrangements, a joint organisation learning review into the Autum 2023 flooding is well progressed. This Review is co-ordinated by The Executive Office with Local Government, the Department for Communities and my Department leading on those aspects for which they are responsible.An independent consultant (Jacobs) has been appointed to assist and provide an independent technical insight into the causes of the flooding and to present considerations to inform any further flood risk management interventions. Already a multi-agency debrief has been held and engagement with elected representatives and stakeholder groups has taken place in Newry, Downpatrick and Portadown at the end of March 2024. The next steps are the drafting of the review report and the development of emerging recommendations.It has always been considered necessary for the public and business affected by the flooding to engage with the review process. To enable this an open day will take place in both the NMD and ABC Council areas during the early summer. At this stage, not only will those affected be able to contribute, but it is also intended to have representatives from the insurance industry and the independent consultant who is working on the review in attendance. The findings and recommendations of this independent review will be made publicly available with the intention that a formal report of the ‘Autumn 2023 Floods’ will be compiled by the end of the Summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the strategy framework his Department is working under in relation to addressing any structural inadequacies in our health service.
answer_text: There are two key strategic documents: “Systems Not Structures – Changing Health and Social Care” (‘the Bengoa Report’) and Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together, which sets out my Department’s response to the recommendations included in the Bengoa Report.   To further address recommendations within the Bengoa Report, my Department has been progressing a series of service reviews as recommended in the Bengoa report and Transformation programmes/ projects, including developing a framework for the future structure of our Acute Hospital network.    Three strategic governance groups have been established to provide oversight of this work – the Performance and Transformation Executive Board, the Improvement and Transformation Advisory Board and an Expert Clinician Panel.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the completion date for A1 road safety improvements.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times increase reliability and improve road safety.I remain fully committed to delivering this important road safety project.  The statutory Orders for the scheme were made in summer 2021 and work on the project is continuing through the development of contract documents and Business Case. In recognition of current and future potential funding challenges, my Department is exploring options to deliver the project in smaller phased packages.  As any delivery of this project, either in full or in phased packages, will be dependent on the availability of funding, it is not possible to detail a completion date for the project.Details of the current status and timescales for of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a1-junctions-phase-2-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he has had with counterparts in other jurisdictions in relation to addressing any structural inadequacies in our health service.
answer_text: My officials and I continue to engage with colleagues across the UK and Ireland, not necessarily to address any structural inadequacies, but instead in assessing best practices and benefits with better outcomes as part of undertaking service reviews and developing strategies.   A prime example of the benefits of cross border partnership working is the development of the North West Cancer Centre.  The Centre continues to deliver high quality and effective cancer care for patients for both Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.  The All-Island Congenital Heart Disease Network is another example of successful working across jurisdictions to bring benefit to patients across the island.  My Department has also engaged with Alderhey Children’s Hospital in Liverpool to develop a service level agreement for the provision of a hospital consented paediatric pathology service. In the longer term, I remain keen to explore the potential for an all-island approach for the delivery of these services. The first step in this would be to carry out an all-island feasibility assessment, which would require the commitment of Governments in both jurisdictions to proceed.My Department has also commissioned the ‘Getting It Right First Time’ (GIRFT) team to undertake independent, external rapid reviews of various services.  To date, reviews have been completed in Orthopaedic, Urology and Gynaecology services and Urgent and Emergency Medicine.  GIRFT is a clinically-led, data driven programme of healthcare quality improvement, developed by the NHS in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his Department’s policy; and (ii) the position of law, in relation to the construction and erection of party political advertising and paraphernalia on Department for Communities property.
answer_text: My Department does not have a policy relating to the construction and erection of party political advertising and paraphernalia on Department for Communities property.Departmental Solicitors Office has advised that the Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (NI) 2015 (S.R. 2015 No. 66) (“the 2015 Regulations”) apply to party political advertising.  The 2015 Regulations are made under powers contained in section 130 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (“the Planning Act”), that relate to the control of advertisements.Councils are responsible for the day-to-day operation of the advertisement planning control system.  Responsibility for this aspect of planning law lies with the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to address issues with parking apps, where individuals are paying for parking but still receiving Parking Charge Notices.
answer_text: My Department’s cashless app, JustPark, is available to use in the Department’s three Park & Ride car parks in Belfast and the on-street Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs) in Belfast, Lisburn and Newry.  There have been no recent issues of the nature you describe with the JustPark app. My Department provided an off-street enforcement service for eight local councils which ended on the 31st March 2024.  These councils have procured their own enforcement service provider, which includes a new cashless parking app called ‘RingGo’. Issues relating to this off-street cashless parking app are a matter for the councils. Contact details for the local councils can be found at the following link https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/local-councils-in-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department’s policy; and (ii) the position of law, in relation to the construction and erection of party political advertising and paraphernalia on Department for Infrastructure property.
answer_text: Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised signs or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.Election posters are subject to control under planning legislation i.e. the Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015. Under these regulations election posters may be displayed, without submitting an application, provided they do not pose a risk to road users, including pedestrians using pavements, and that they are removed within 14 days after the close of the poll.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment on whether the failure to produce a budget prior to the commencement of the 2024-25 financial year is consistent with her obligations in law
answer_text: I would refer you to AQW 9834/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of patients from the Newry and Mourne area who have undergone emergency general surgery at Craigavon Area Hospital in the last 12 months; and (ii) how many of these patients have experienced complications or secondary medical conditions.
answer_text: In the last twelve months, from 1 April 2023 to 31 March 2024, there were 976 people discharged following an emergency admission to general surgery in Craigavon Area Hospital who reside in the Newry, Mourne and Down Local Government District Area. This figure denotes individuals, and so these individuals may have had emergency general surgery within Craigavon Area Hospital on more than one occasion within the year. Please note, only provisional data is available for 2023-24 at this time.Information on complications or conditions caused by surgery cannot be identified from the Hospital Inpatient System.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that have received hospital treatment in corridors and other non-ward settings, broken down by hospital, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients receiving hospital treatment in corridors and other non-ward settings is not held centrally. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) was therefore asked to provide a response.Both South Eastern and Western HSCTs responded that they do not hold this information for the last five years, nor would they acknowledge if the patient was nursed with a chair, corridor, escalated bed, or non-ward setting.Belfast and Northern HSCTs indicated that they are unable to provide this information as the data is not captured on the Patient Administration System. Southern HSCT also confirmed that they cannot accurately provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of his Department’s (i) budget; and (ii) expenditure, in each of the last ten years, including how much expenditure was allocated to each (a) Health and Social Care Trust; (b) hospital; (c) facility; and (d) specialism.
answer_text: ANSWERBreakdown of Department’s budget is shown in Annex A.  As Departmental restructuring took place in 2016/17, there are two data sets provided in Annex A. Table 1: 2013/14 to 2015/16 - DHSSPS pre-restructuring. Table 2: 2016/17 to 2022/23 - DoH post-restructuring. Breakdown of Department's expenditure is shown in Annex B.  This is the same information that was provided in the AQW 8438/22-27. As Departmental restructuring took place in 2016/17, there are two data sets provided in Annex B. Table 3: 2013/14 to 2015/16 - DHSSPS pre-restructuring. Table 4: 2016/17 to 2022/23 - DoH post-restructuring. As some functions will have moved both into and out of the Department at this time the figures will not be directly comparable. In addition, the Review of Financial Processes project and the closure of the Health and Social Care Board were both implemented from 1 April 2022, which has resulted in some figures being aligned to different categories from this point onwards. Total budget allocated to each Health and Social Care Trust is included in Annex C.  These are the budgets allocated from DoH to Trusts extracted from the Financial Allocation System. It will not agree to expenditure as reported by Trusts, as Trust expenditure will be impacted by other income streams outside DoH budgets.Total Expenditure by Hospital Site is included in Annex D. These figures are fully absorbed costs, which means that they include capital charges and overheads.  To provide costs by Site, or Service, an element of apportionment is used to fully absorb these costs.From 2019/20 to 2020/21 per Ministerial agreement, the annual costing returns were temporarily suspended during the Covid-19 pandemic and to allow a regional costing system to be implemented.  It was agreed that during these years the 2019/20 costs would be uplifted by an indicative 2.5% for 2020/21 and 3.1% for 2021/22.2022/23 cost data is not yet available. These costs are the first costs calculated through the new regional patient level cost system (PLICS), they are undergoing extensive quality assurance and Trusts are not in a position to publish at this time.(c) Total expenditure by Facility Costs is not collected by Facility, other than Hospital site shown in Annex D.  Community and Personal Social Services are reported by Programme of Care and Service Line, which is included in the Specialisms reported at (d).(d) Total Expenditure by Specialism is included in Annex E.Annex ATable 1: Department of Health Budget Breakdown 2013/14 – 2015/16 for DHSSPS pre-restructuring £kBudget by category2013/142014/152015/16Hospital and Community Health Services3,069,5603,083,8492,977,009Social Care Services878,180922,520963,407Family Health Services – General Medical Services800,626828,719277,631Family Health Services – Pharmaceutical Services37,53439,783479,769Family Health Services – Dental Services11,3668,781104,703Family Health Services – Ophthalmic Services6,7936,47322,385Health Support Services2,1852,173119,076Publ

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the decision to record all calls to Lifeline may have on callers.
answer_text: Lifeline is commissioned by the Public Health Agency (PHA) and delivered by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT). A call recording pilot was introduced by Lifeline on 1st February 2024, the pilot is scheduled to last for 12 weeks.  Lifeline, BHSCT and PHA carefully considered the introduction of this pilot including the potential impact on callers along with the potential benefits. Lifeline undertook stakeholder engagement ahead of the introduction of the pilot which informed its development. The introduction of call recording aims to improve quality assurance, quality improvement and governance within the Lifeline service. All callers to Lifeline will hear a recorded message that informs the caller that all calls are recorded for training and monitoring purposes and if they wish to have an unrecorded callback to speak to the counsellor. If the caller advises the counsellor when they connect that they do not wish their call to be recorded, the counsellor will make arrangements to call them back from an unrecorded line. The callback from Lifeline will be from a withheld number, so the caller ID will not display. It is important to remember all callers have the right to remain anonymous, regardless of whether the call is recorded or not. A person can remain anonymous by not providing any identifiable details. Lifeline counsellors explain confidentiality within the service at the outset of a call.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether patients receiving treatment in corridors have the same levels of clinical and patient safety as those receiving treatment in wards.
answer_text: My Department recognises that patients are experiencing significant delays for admission to hospital beds across all of our acute hospital sites leading to crowding in Emergency Departments (ED) and delayed ambulance off loads with a subsequent delay for NIAS in responding to emergency 999 calls. Although the issues are system-wide, pressures do culminate in ED at the front door of the hospital. We are doing everything we can to reduce this pressure. We are continuously working to improve our range of community services, unscheduled care and same day emergency care services to avoid hospital admission and improve patient flow where possible.Unfortunately, the demand for hospital services has resulted in some of our patients being cared for in additional care spaces such as beds, chairs and trollies in non-clinical areas, including corridors within ward and department areas. Whilst this is not the standard we want in providing services, our priority remains to ensure the best possible care and treatment to patients. Our staff are working in difficult circumstances to protect the safety and dignity of patientsAll additional care spaces being used have been risk assessed by Trust staff with the aim to use these areas for the shortest period of time as possible. Senior staff within the Trusts monitor the use and de-escalation of any additional beds and care spaces on a daily basis.As patient safety remains my Department and Trusts’ priority, careful consideration is made in identification of patients who are deemed appropriate for allocation to an additional bed/care space, utilising an agreed set of exclusion criteria.  A patient who is placed in an additional care space receives both medical and nursing care dictated by their clinical need to the same safety standard of all patients placed within a designated ward bed space. Trusts will increase the number of staff on duty for these respective areas so that these patients have access to appropriate care, although mitigations against deficits in the privacy and dignity of patients are much more challenging and, regrettably, at times insufficient to address privacy and dignity concerns.
