question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail grants available for farmers seeking to decarbonise; and (ii) for his assessment of how these supports compare to those available in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: DAERA is developing a new programme of Farm Support and Development, designed in consultation with the Northern Ireland agricultural industry and other key stakeholders. The schemes and measures to be introduced will provide levers to contribute to statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, with a firm focus on just transition. As part of this programme, a new Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme aimed at reducing livestock greenhouse gas emissions and improving efficiency in the beef sector was introduced on 1 January 2024.  This is a new payment support scheme for beef farmers in Northern Ireland and the online opt-in opened on 15 April 2024.A Capital Investment Measure is being developed under the new programme which will provide support to incentivise investment in carbon reduction technology and equipment to support low carbon farming, subject to budget availability.Further incentivisation measures are under development.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what support he will be making available to farmers whose livestock have been unable to graze on their land due to persistent heavy rainfall in March and April 2024.
answer_text: I am aware that this year’s very wet and late Spring is presenting challenges for farmers operating in almost every sector within the industry. In recognition of this, I have written to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention and I intend to raise this for discussion at the next meeting of the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. 	Meanwhile, farmers should avail of the latest technical advice and support through the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE). Farmers seeking advice should contact their local CAFRE Advisor.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures his Department is adopting to encourage and support hauliers to decarbonise.
answer_text: The pathway for the decarbonisation of road freight will require the introduction of a range of alternative fuels including Hydrogen Fuel Cell technology, Compressed and Liquefied Natural Gas, and Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil for heavier vehicles.My Department officials continue to engage with industry stakeholders and officials from both the British Government’s Office of Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV), and the South’s Zero Emission Vehicles Ireland (ZEVI) to consider how new technologies and alternative fuel solutions can be adopted to meet the challenge of decarbonising the Freight Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many police officers, on average, are assigned to the investigation of (i) murder cases; and (ii) rape and sexual violence cases.
answer_text: The allocation of policing resources is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impacts of the changes to Category A in drug tariffs on pharmacists.
answer_text: In 2011, in response to the outcome of a Judicial Review, my predecessor agreed that NI would establish its own Drug Tariff and that it would use the English Drug Tariff as a reference for pricing. This policy position has remained unchanged since that time.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) in England consulted on a number of community pharmacy drug reimbursement reform proposals back in 2019. As the NIDT is currently reflective of the English Drug Tariff, any changes made to the English Tariff may be reflected in the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff, and therefore a subsequent targeted consultation was undertaken in March 2022 in relation to the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff.One of these proposed reforms was around the determination of reimbursement for generic medicines in Part 1, Category A of the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff.My pharmacy staff regularly meet with community pharmacy representatives (CPNI) to discuss Northern Ireland Drug Tariff issues and have been keeping CPNI appraised of potential changes to the Category A pricing mechanism.From April 2024 onwards, via 18 month transitional arrangements, DHSC is imposing changes to the pricing methodology for Category A Medicines in Part VIIIA of the English Drug Tariff (EDT). Part 1 of the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff is entirely reflective of Part VIIIA EDT, and therefore in Northern Ireland, the same transitional arrangements will be implemented from April 2024.Detailed modelling is currently being undertaken as part of an impact assessment and this will be shared with CPNI following routine quality assurance processes. The actual impact of these changes will continue to be monitored during the transitional implementation period and furthermore an offer has been made that consideration will be given to mitigate net material impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the benefits of establishing a financial support scheme to assist with care home costs for individuals living with dementia.
answer_text: I appreciate that choosing a care home can be one of the most difficult and emotional decisions a person may have to make, and this can be even more challenging if a person has dementia. An individual can choose to participate in their Health and Social Care Trust’s financial assessment which will look at income and capital to calculate how much they need to pay if applicable. I understand that a Task and Finish group has been established by the Department of Health to review and make recommendations on issues relating to finance and Care Homes including reviewing the Care Home Tariff, Third Party Top Up fees; and enhanced care rates, all of which will have implications on care home costs for people living with dementia and their relatives.It would then be for the Health Minister to bring forward any financial support scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of introducing a financial support scheme to assist with care home costs for individuals living with dementia.
answer_text: The Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF) has been established to provide a formal mechanism for the Department and representatives of the Social Care Sector (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors) to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future. 		The SCCF includes workstreams that will focus on improvements for the delivery of care within Nursing and Residential care homes. This includes  a Workstream on Commissioning and Contracting  which has been set up to review and update the current regional residential and nursing home specification and contract and establish processes for its ongoing review going forward. 		As part of this Workstream a Task and Finish (T&F) Group has been established to review and make recommendations on issues relating to finance and Care Homes. This will look at the Care Home Tariff, Third Party Top Up fees and enhanced care rates all of which will have implications on care home costs for people living with dementia and their relatives.The work of this T&F Group is ongoing but its recommendations will inform the key actions in respect of the commissioning and contracting of residential and nursing home placements and the commissioning and contracting of home care service provision across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of a national fitness test day with standardised assessments, akin to those proposed by Professor Niall Moyna of Dublin City University, so that his Department can gain an understanding of the health and fitness of the population.
answer_text: The threshold fitness test recommended by Professor Moyna, Professor of Clinical Exercise Physiology in the School of Health and Human Performance at Dublin City University, look to be a useful measure of overall fitness for many, especially those already physically active. The four UK Chief Medical Officers publish a series of six sets of evidence-based physical activity guidelines covering the whole of the life course from early years to older adults, for those living with disabilities and for pregnancy and after childbirth. These can be found online at https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/physical-activity-guidelines. These guidelines are aimed at health professionals, policy makers and others working to promote physical activity, sport, exercise and active travel. The guidelines are designed to aid health professionals and others to provide individuals and communities with information on the type and amount of physical activity that they should undertake to improve their health.An assessment of the levels of physical activity and the attainment and knowledge of these physical activity guidelines and the health of the population more broadly form part of the annual Health Survey Northern Ireland. An assessment of the levels of physical activity will be included in the 2023/24 Health Survey Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the hospitals that (a) currently have; and (b) previously had High Dependency Units (HDUs) in the last ten years; and (ii) the duration those HDUs were operational.
answer_text: Details of High Dependency Units (HDUs) are not held centrally within the Department, and therefore each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were asked for input.Belfast HSCTBelfast HSCT indicated that the Royal Victoria Hospital, Belfast City Hospital, Mater Infirmorum Hospital, and Cardiac Surgical Intensive Care Unit (ICU) all had critical care beds from 2014/15 to 2019/20. Critical care beds include intensive (level 3) and high dependency (level 2). The beds within each unit are interchangeable depending on the level of care that each patient requires. During the pandemic, critical care capacity changed in line with regional response. For 2022/23 and 2023/24, the Royal Victoria Hospital, Belfast City Hospital and Cardiac Surgical ICU had critical care beds, while the Mater Infirmorum did not. Table 1 below shows the breakdown of these beds between the hospitals.Table 1: Critical care beds in Belfast HSCT for the last ten financial yearsYearRoyal VictoriaBelfast CityMater InfirmorumCardiac Surgery ICUTotal Beds2014/15259618582015/16259618582016/17279618602017/18279618602018/19279618602019/20279618602020/21COVID-19 pandemic: number of beds changed in line with regional response.2021/222022/233012018602023/24301201860Source: Belfast HSCTIn their Paediatric Service, the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children has had 12 funded level 3 beds in the Paediatric Intensive Care Unit since 2012.Northern HSCTNorthern HSCT indicated that from January 2014 until January 2022, Antrim Hospital had six level 3 ICU beds and two level 2 HDU beds. From February 2022 until December 2023, they had six level 3 ICU beds, and three level 3 HDU beds.In Causeway Hospital, there have been two level 3 ICU beds and two level 2 HDU beds from January 2014 to December 2023. Over the COVID-19 pandemic, the ICU and HDU beds were escalated across both units as required.South Eastern HSCTSouth Eastern HSCT responded that within their trust for the last ten years, only the Ulster Hospital has had an HDU, and this has been operational for this whole period.Southern HSCTSouthern HSCT replied that there has been an HDU in both Craigavon Area and Daisy Hill Hospitals for the whole time period requested, 2014 to 2023.Western HSCTWestern HSCT indicated that Altnagelvin Intensive Care has ten beds, which are funded as seven level 3 Intensive Care Support beds, and three level 2 High Dependency Support beds. All beds within the ICU are adaptable to the needs of patients, who may be admitted as a level 2 patient but progress into level 3 intervention, or vice versa. These have always been available over the last ten years. Although Altnagelvin has no standalone HDU, there are the level 2 beds available.South West Acute Hospital has six beds funded in Intensive Care, which are used as required by patients. This includes both level 2 and level 3 beds, but does not include a standalone HDU. These have been available for the whole ten year period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department is adopting to ensure the responsible and equitable integration of Artificial Intelligence in healthcare systems to maximise patient outcomes.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Generative AI technologies offers opportunities to improve both productivity of the existing HSC workforce and the quality of care services delivered, with benefits including the freeing up of capacity to provide services to those that need it most, in settings and ways that best meets their needs.The potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy, equitable and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service user’s needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The Department of Health and its partners in the HSC are establishing structures to develop these enablers and explore the capabilities of providers and products, with a particular focus in the first instance on medical image analysis for diagnostic purposes, an area with growing evidence to support clinical effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department last carried out a speed limit safety assessment on the A1.
answer_text: The national speed limit for dual carriageway applies along the A1 except on separate stretches at Banbridge (6.5 Km) and Dromore (5 Km) where the speed limit is 60mph.South of Loughbrickland, the A1 has been subject to extensive dualling upgrades.  A high quality dual carriageway from Cloghogue to the border was completed in August 2007, whilst dualling from Loughbrickland to Beech Hill was completed in November 2006.  The last major upgrade which provided a fully grade-separated high quality dual carriageway from Beech Hill to Cloghogue was completed in July 2010.  The design speed and speed limits for these sections was taken into consideration during the design and development of these schemes.The proposed A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme from Hillsborough to Loughbrickland has been informed by extensive review of roads safety issues along this section.  The proposed scheme has been developed to address the most prevalent issues, particularly focusing on the closure of all the gaps in the central median, providing 4 grade separated junctions, closing a number of minor roads which connect with the A1 and altering all remaining roads and accesses so they operate on a 'Left in - Left out' basis only.  It is planned that a review of the speed limit, including the sections around Banbridge and Dromore, will be undertaken on completion of this scheme.  This review will take into consideration the safety improvements and the importance of this key transport corridor between north and south.In the interim, my officials continually monitor the safety on the A1 to ensure the current measures are appropriate.  This includes regular review of PSNI collision statistics and their causation factors along this route.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will provide a timeline for the implementation of the recommendations in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The draft All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) report was released for public consultation for the purposes of Strategic Environmental Assessment from 25 July to 29 September 2023. Work is ongoing to analyse the responses received and finalise the Review. Once finalised, one of the key initial steps will be prioritisation of the Review recommendations and development of an implementation plan. My officials are already engaging with their southern colleagues on these matters.In the meantime, the draft AISRR report released to public consultation includes a Roadmap which presents a timeline for the possible future development and delivery of key interventions, broadly broken down into short term (from today to c. 2030); medium term (2030 – 2040) and long term (2040 – 2050) which gives an indication of when interventions may be delivered in full.In addition to developing an implementation plan, more detailed work will be needed to test the feasibility and affordability of the recommendations and guide future investment decisions. The implementation of each key intervention will be subject to feasibility studies and business case approval, and the necessary funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the requirement for pharmacists to prescribe branded generic medications at a financial loss.
answer_text: It is good practice to prescribe medicines generically. This enables any suitable product to be dispensed, thereby saving delay to the patient and in most cases expense to the Health Service. Brand prescribing of medicines is recommended where there is a clinical need to ensure a patient always receives the same brand; or where there are a number of brands of a particular drug available with a range of prices and the HSC has agreed a particular brand (cost-effective choice) is prescribed on the basis of it being cost-efficient and contributing to the delivery of efficiencies within the pharmaceutical budget. The list of HSC cost effective choices are kept under review by SPPG. Over the last number of years the majority of branded generics have been retired following feedback from community pharmacy contractors and issues with the supply chain.  Contractors are not reimbursed on an individual dispensed medicine basis. Under current funding arrangements for community pharmacies, there is an agreed level of retained purchased profit (also known as “margin”) within the system. This figure is currently a minimum of £26.5 million. In order to determine that the remuneration to pharmacy contractors is both fair and reasonable, (and delivering the agreed level of retained purchase profit), the Ongoing Margin Survey is carried out each year. The aim of the margin survey is to estimate the level of “margin” community pharmacies are able to retain from purchasing medicines. The Ongoing Margin Survey is managed by a multidisciplinary group which has representations from Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the Department of Health and both the operational and analytical teams from the Business Services Organisation (BSO). An Independent Assessor verifies calculations and attends all meeting of the multi-disciplinary group.As previously stated, the current agreed level of retained purchase profit (“margin”) is a minimum of £26.5 million per annum. Results from the last three years which have been completed (19/20, 20/21 and 21/22) indicates levels of margin in excess of this figure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what actions his Department has taken to publicise and raise awareness of the Mobile Action Plan among local councils.
answer_text: The Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI), published on the 23 June 2022, was developed in collaboration by a cross sector working group. Although my department led on coordination, each MAP NI member has responsibility for the delivery of actions pertaining to their own organisation, including publicity and raising awareness, this includes local council representatives.Members of the MAP NI working group signed up to a co-ordinated launch of MAP NI in which all members agreed that they were responsible for raising awareness of the MAP NI within their respective Departments / Organisations. This included the consideration of press articles and links on their own websites highlighting the MAP NI publication. Subsequently the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT) was established on 13 December 2022 as the delivery vehicle for the actions identified within MAP NI. Local council representation transferred from the Northern Ireland Local Government Association (NILGA), a working group member during MAP NI development, to the Society of Local Authority Chief Executives and senior managers (SOLACE) at the establishment of the NIBBT as MAP NI transitioned into the implementation phase. Local councils are now represented by the Full Fibre Northern Ireland (FFNI) consortium who coordinate council input, deliverables and awareness.  To aid with the transition from NILGA to SOLACE, on 23 May 2023, at FFNI’s request, DfE Telecoms Officials presented to the SOLACE Executive Board to help them raise awareness of MAP NI and potential resource implications for local councils. This included an indication that additional council officials would be required to participate in the NIBBT Subgroups and indeed chair the Planning Subgroup.It is the responsibility of council officials to publicise and raise awareness of the MAP NI and the progress of NIBBT should they deem it necessary within local councils. It is my understanding that each council has now nominated a Digital Champion, who may be well placed to assist in this information delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total number of student teachers who (i) graduated from third level institutions; (ii) secured permanent employment at schools in Northern Ireland, within 12 months of graduation; (iii) entered permanent employment at schools outside of Northern Ireland, within 12 months of graduation; and (iv) are not in employment within 12 months of graduation, broken down in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: (i) The total number of Bachelor of Education (BEd) and Post-graduate Certificate in Education (PGCE) graduates from the four Higher Education Institutions in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five academic years, are as follows:2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/23Primary242244244240240Post Primary283284280282275Total525528524522515The total number of graduates who registered with the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) in each of the last five accounting years, (which includes those who have undertaken their Initial Teacher Education (ITE) qualification outside of Northern Ireland), are as follows:Graduates by YearTotal Number of Graduates2023/248222022/238422021/228832020/219072019/201006Note: The data above relates to all entrants by date of award of their ITE qualification.(ii) The accuracy of the data held by the GTCNI is underpinned by an annual school list of teachers process, which allows the GTCNI to obtain up-to-date teacher employment data from schools across Northern Ireland. However, this latest round of data collection was impacted by industrial action within the teaching workforce. Consequently, this question cannot be answered due to concerns over the accuracy and completeness of the data held by the GTCNI.(iii) and (iv) Neither the Department nor the GTCNI hold the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what research has been undertaken to understand the role of (i) mobile connectivity; and (ii) fixed connectivity on the provision of public services, including (a) digital exclusion; (b) social care provision; (c) transport provision; and (d) economic management.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) holding the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile and fixed connectivity. My Department maintains regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters, ensuring that local considerations are taken into account in relation to improvements in connectivity here. Officials from my Department engage in multiple initiatives to facilitate improvements to mobile connectivity. These initiatives include the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland, which identifies issues which inhibit the rollout of mobile networks here. Associated actions are currently being delivered by the DfE chaired Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce which has a cross sectoral/departmental membership.Significant research has been carried out from various organisations and institutions on the impact improved fixed broadband plays in benefitting citizens and businesses. These findings emphasise the importance of recent investments in broadband infrastructure locally. I have included a selection of studies and research papers into broadband and its benefits and impacts.In November 2013, the British Government carried out a ‘Broadband Impact Study’ to understand the impacts of publicly funded interventions and help maximise value for money on future investments. This study included economic, social and environmental impacts of fixed broadband. The study can be found at: UK Broadband Impact Study.In 2019, Openreach commissioned Cebr (an independent economics and business research consultancy) to assess the impacts of a nationwide full fibre rollout. The report indicates that full fibre broadband can make it easier for residents in remote and rural communities to access public services. The report also details the social and economic benefits of full fibre broadband, as well as the impact to transport in both rural and urban areas. The findings can be found at: Openreach full fibre impact.More recently, in June 2023, the House of Lords’ Communications and Digital Committee researched the impact of ‘Digital Exclusion’, which includes references to public services, social care and economic impacts. The report can be found at: Digital exclusion.Analysys Mason drafted a report in March 2024 on, ‘Why the UK needs to use advanced connectivity’. This report describes how advanced networks like 5G and Fibre to the Premises result in significant economic, social and sustainability benefits. The report is available at: Analysys-Mason why use advanced connectivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what provisions exist in the Housing Executive to support those who have recently left prison with no fixed address to ensure they have equal access to housing.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:The Housing Executive has worked alongside Housing Rights and the Department of Justice (including the Northern Ireland Prison Service and Probation Board for Northern Ireland) in reviewing a protocol for the management of the accommodation and related support needs of people in custody in Northern Ireland (Prisons Protocol) with the revised protocol effective from December 2023. The Protocol provides a framework for co-operation which enables the accommodation and associated support needs of those entering (including those on remand) and leaving custody to be met.The Prisons Protocol is guided by the following aims:To provide a formal framework for inter-agency co-operation for agencies involved in the delivery of accommodation and support services to people entering or leaving custodyTo prevent homelessness by meeting the accommodation and associated support needs of those entering and leaving custodyTo reduce the likelihood of re-offending by helping to establish stability in lifestyles of those resettlingThe delivery of the Prisons Protocol is supported by an Operation Group and Strategic Advisory Group with representation at operational and strategic level respectively from each of the partner agencies. Each group meets quarterly and seeks to ensure that the protocol is being delivered in line with the aims noted above along with the identification and resolution of any issues in the implementation of the protocol. Additionally, as part of the Inter-departmental Homelessness Action Plan the Housing Executive has been working with the Department of Justice to implement a test and learn pilot for Housing Advisors from the Housing Executive to work within the custodial setting to address accommodation issues for those exiting custody. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) what progress has been made in meeting the actions of the Mobile Action Plan; (ii) whether the target dates announced at the launch are being met; and (iii) how often the taskforce has met since its launch.
answer_text: The Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI), published 23 June 2022, contains a total of eight actions and was developed by the cross sectoral MAP NI Working Group. The Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT) was established at its first meeting on 13 December 2022 delivering MAP NI action seven.Due to the fractured nature of Telecoms policy here, several organisations, departments in central government and also local government have various policy responsibilities therefore the NIBBT is a cross sector group including the mobile industry and Ofcom. The Taskforce has been structured into an Oversight Group, meeting quarterly and chaired by the Department for the Economy (DfE), and three subgroups dealing with specific themes and MAP NI actions, namely:Communication Subgroup – chaired by DfE – MAP NI actions one, three and four;Planning Subgroup – chaired by Belfast City Council’s (BCC) Head of Planning – MAP NI actions two, five and eight; andPublicly Owned Assets Subgroup – chaired by the Department of Finance (DoF) – MAP NI action six.Since its establishment on 13 December 2022, the NIBBT Oversight Group has met a total of five times. MAP NI Implementation Progress Reports are supplied by the chairs of its three subgroups. The Communication Subgroup, chaired by my department, has met a total of seven times since its inception in June 2023. During this period, MAP NI actions one, three and four have been and continue to be progressed. The Northern Ireland Local Government Association (NILGA) is leading on the development of a NI Councillors’ Guide to Digital Connectivity which we expect to be published in the coming months. The subgroup recently conducted a survey of council elected members and officials to test current levels of understanding, assess their information needs and gather a baseline measurement to aid evaluation. The subgroup is in the process of identifying suitable products and delivery methods to meet these needs. In conjunction with the Matrix Panel, the subgroup is currently conducting a survey of local businesses to similarly identify their information requirements before planning suitable activities.  The Planning Subgroup, chaired by BCC’s Head of Planning, first met on 7 December 2023. The subgroup is currently progressing MAP NI actions two, five and eight. Actions have been assigned to members to investigate and report back to the group with a paper on Best Practice Guidance, a paper on the current legislation in the north and Britain to inform a discussion on which local  legislative changes might be the most useful and members have been asked to identify the top five planning barriers from the industry and government perspective. An update was also produced on the current position of Local Development Plans (LDPs) across the eleven local councils.The Publicly Owned Assets Subgroup, chaired by the DoF, will first meet on 16 April 2024 to take forward MAP NI action six. The chair has been working close

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the (a) incentives; and (b) financial support available to assist firms with green and decarbonisation incentives; and (ii) how this is comparable to the support available to firms in neighbouring jurisdictions.
answer_text: My department is working in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a new scaled phase of its Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Business. The Programme will include a new Energy Efficiency Capital Grant Scheme (EECG) which will be open to the wider business base and is due to launch in the coming weeks. The EECG will include grant support for energy efficiency measures along with low carbon and renewable technologies, all aimed at reducing emissions and helping businesses take control of their energy use by lowering their energy demand. The wider aspects of the ERE Programme include support in the form of Technical Consultancy, Resource Matching Through Industrial Symbiosis and a Resource Efficiency Capital Grant which are already in operation.  Similar capital grant schemes have been provided in neighbouring jurisdictions and I would refer the member to the following for further information;The Sustainable Energy Authority of Ireland deliver a variety of business grant and support schemes, such as the Non-Domestic Microgen Scheme, EXEED Grant Scheme and the Support Scheme for Renewable Heat. Further details can be found at www.seai.ie.  Enterprise Ireland deliver funding and grant support to businesses such as their Enterprise Emissions Reduction Investment Fund for Capital Investment for decarbonisation processes and energy monitoring and tracking systems. Further details can be found at www.enterprise-ireland.com. Scottish Enterprise deliver funding and grants support for businesses which includes investment funding, European funding and funding for start-up and micro-enterprise. Further details can be found at scottish-enterprise.com. 		The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero also provide a variety of business energy schemes, loans and grants. Further details can be accessed at www.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the benefits of providing enhanced funding and access to sports for young people, including those with additional needs
answer_text: The Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland - Active Living recognises the positive impact that participation in sport and physical activity has for everyone, including young people and those with additional needs. A key theme of the strategy is promoting participation, inclusion and community engagement in sport and physical activity.In addition, Sport NI’s core focus continues to be on growing and sustaining participation in sport and physical activity for all in our communities, through delivery of outcome 1 of their Corporate Plan, “People adopting and sustaining participation in sport and physical activity”.Funding opportunities will focus on these areas.  By way of example, my Department supports the Active Living No Limits Forum which aims to increase suitable participation opportunities for people with disabilities. Through this Forum, funding was secured for a number of projects to help remove barriers to participation for those with a disability, including investment of £900k for the Disability Sports Hub Project across all 11 district councils.My Department also provides funding to Special Olympics Ulster which provides sport and physical activity opportunities for people with an Intellectual Disability in Northern Ireland. They deliver a number of programmes to children and young people promoting the benefits of sport and physical activity, including their Young Athlete Programme.Details of specific funding to the sector, including for young people, can be found on the Government Funding Portal which can be accessed through the link - https://govfundingpublic.nics.gov.uk/Home.aspx

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to returning the number of X5 buses in Newry to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: The potential to return the number of X5 buses in Newry to pre-pandemic levels is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7957/22-27, whether he will meet with the Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library in relation to their core funding for 2024-25 and beyond.
answer_text: The 2024/25 budget allocation for my Department is not yet known; it will be set by the Executive.  All funding requirements will be considered and prioritised when a budget allocation has been awarded.  My officials continue to be in regular contact with Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library staff; given this contact, I do not plan to meet with the Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the benefits to passengers of increasing the number of X5 buses in Newry.
answer_text: The provision of an attractive public transport bus service between Newry and Dublin Airport and Dublin City is a key priority for my Department, and its timetable, utilising the X1 bus service, is designed to meet the overall demand on the route, taking account of commerciality and public service obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, whether he will supply all the information requested in (i) AQW 7527/22-27; (ii) AQW 6072/22-27; and (iii) AQW 6478/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 7527/22-27 was answered on 21 May 2024 AQW 6072/22-27 was answered on 8 July 2024 and AQW 6478/22-27 was answered on 3 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the benefits of establishing a single department with responsibility for all stages of education with a view to ensuring pay parity for teaching staff.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the report of the Independent Review of Education. Partnership working and collaboration between the two departments is critical to support all our learners, and the Education Minister and I will be engaging on areas of mutual responsibility and interest. I am also committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers and have asked officials to consider options going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a table outlining the performance of each stroke unit against the metrics and benchmarks used by his Department to measure and monitor their clinical performance, in each of the last 15 years.
answer_text: The 5 HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland (NI) participate in the Stroke Sentinel National Audit Programme (SSNAP) which measures the quality and organisation of stroke care across England, Wales and Northern Ireland.  SSNAP, which commenced in 2013 with all HSC Trusts signed up by 2016, provides key performance data to benchmark, standardise and manage stroke acute and community performance.  SSNAP performance data is used by the NI Stroke Network to: standardise practice in the delivery and reporting of stroke care identify and take forward opportunities for service improvementsupport the commissioning of stroke services in NI. SSNAP reports can be publicly accessed via their website SSNAP - Home (strokeaudit.org).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 5515/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a detailed breakdown of the (i) number; (ii) location; and (iii) party political affiliation, of political advertising billboards which are illegally and permanently erected on land owned by the Department for Infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department is aware of a political advertising billboard situated on Departmental land at Camlough Roundabout, Newry and another at Doran’s Hill, Newry.  These billboards display advertising related to Sinn Féin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of delaying line closures in order to aid GAA fans' travel by train to Croke Park on Easter Sunday.
answer_text: This Easter, Translink is carrying out preplanned and essential engineering works on the railway line between Belfast and Lisburn and Belfast and Portadown to ensure the timely delivery of the new Belfast Grand Central Station. As the largest integrated transport hub on the island of Ireland, this important project will improve connectivity for everyone in the North, cross border and beyond.Full details of the extra bus and train services available to fans travelling to Croke Park are on Translinks web site https://www.translink.co.uk/events

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he has had with (i) industry representatives; (ii) his UK counterparts; and (iii) the Committee for the Economy with respect to addressing rising insurance costs.
answer_text: The regulation of Financial Services including insurance services is a reserved matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what additional train services will be operating on Easter weekend to facilitate GAA fans travelling to Croke Park.
answer_text: This Easter, Translink is carrying out preplanned and essential engineering works on the railway line between Belfast and Lisburn and Belfast and Portadown to ensure the timely delivery of the new Belfast Grand Central Station. As the largest integrated transport hub on the island of Ireland, this important project will improve connectivity for everyone in the North, cross border and beyond.Full details of the extra bus and train services available are on Translinks web site https://www.translink.co.uk/events

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of legislating to introduce a farmer’s bill of rights.
answer_text: I have agreed to meet with the Farmers for Action Steering Committee to discuss the proposed Farm Welfare Bill.		Whilst I am committed to supporting the agriculture industry, any specific considerations will need to be balanced against associated challenges for export competitiveness, supply chains and impacts on local consumers. 		I am also conscious that the regulation of anti-competition practice and agreements are reserved matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what oversight mechanisms his Department will introduce in relation to the delivery of Phase 2 of the flood recovery funding.
answer_text: The administration and delivery of the Flood Support Schemes is the responsibility of the local council, including internal audit review arrangements of the schemes. By way of oversight, my department will conduct appropriate administrative and on-the-spot checks of the expenditure declared by the council, prior to funding being awarded. Furthermore, as with all projects involving public funding of this nature, my department will undertake a Post Project Evaluation of the schemes which will assess whether the schemes’ objectives were met and whether value for money was achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what advice he would provide to residents with safety concerns in relation to the structural integrity of walls and boundary treatments adjacent to their properties.
answer_text: These matters are largely private matters between residents and 3rd Parties, including builders, developers, insurers and lenders.  It would be a matter for the resident to take whatever action they deemed appropriate in conjunction with the appropriate party to allay any concerns they may have about such an issue.  It would not be appropriate for me to provide advice on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the benefits of updating the criteria for the awarding of intimidation points through the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Housing Selection Scheme, including intimidation points, has been in place relatively unchanged for decades.  Long-awaited updates are underway via the implementation stage of the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. This includes exploration of options for intimidation points. I will consider next steps in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on progress of the Stroke Action Plan since it was published in June 2022; and (ii) to detail which actions have been delayed.
answer_text: Progress has been made against a range of the priorities set out in the published Stroke Action Plan. This includes the development of a regional model for TIA (mini-stroke); the completion of a Thrombolysis audit; the introduction of Artificial Intelligence software in five hospitals to assist in the interpretation of scans; and the development of a specification for rehabilitation and long-term support.    Work is ongoing on other key priorities. This includes Thrombectomy, where a costed model identifying requirements to support expansion has been developed. In addition, agreement on a revised process to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care has been secured. Unfortunately, due to capacity and funding constraints it has not been possible to implement each of the priorities within the timescale set out in the Action Plan. Whilst work is underway,  it should be noted that a lack of additional funding is likely to continue to act as a constraint on implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the waiting time for access to specialist clinical stroke psychologists, in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how this compares to waiting times in (a) March 2023; and (b) March 2022.
answer_text: Please refer to AQW 8865/22-27 response indicating that the full implementation of clinical psychology pathways has been delayed by workforce and financial challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in the absence of a 24/7 service, how he will ensure that everyone who is eligible for thrombectomy treatment for a stroke can access the procedure.
answer_text: The expansion of the regional thrombectomy service to 24/7 remains a key priority in Northern Ireland (NI).  To move this forward, the Regional Stroke Network has established a Thrombectomy Expansion Task and Finish Group which has developed a high-level costed model to deliver 24/7 thrombectomy.  The Network has also established a Clinical Reference Group to improve equity of access across the region.   Stroke patients are assessed for thrombectomy eligibility  both in hours and out of hours.  Patients presenting out of hours can be assessed using advanced imaging to determine if they are eligible to proceed to thrombectomy the following morning when the service becomes available.  This is less than ideal but provision of a full 24/7 service will require significant investment which may be challenging in the current financial climate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether restoration of the Armagh to Portadown rail line is being treated as an Executive priority with respect to the implementation of the recommendations of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review was released to public consultation from 25 July to 29 September 2023. Consideration of responses to the public consultation is ongoing and my officials are currently working to finalise the Review. As the Review is yet to be finalised and approved none of the Review recommendations, including restoration of the Armagh to Portadown rail line, are being treated as an Executive priority. Once published, further work will be needed to prioritise the Review recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions, including those in relation to the Armagh to Portadown line.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3112/22-27, for an update on when the reconstruction of the retaining wall and the road at Ballynaclosha Road will be completed.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising the design for the replacement wall at Ballynaclosha Road. Negotiations with landowners to secure the necessary land are ongoing and a resolution to some legal issues is also being sought.Subject to resolution of these matters and budget availability, my Department hopes to be able to commence works early in the new financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the challenges his Department faces in relation to reconstructing the Armagh to Portadown rail line.
answer_text: Translink is undertaking a feasibility study on reinstating the Portadown to Armagh railway line.  The reconstruction challenges will be considered as part of the feasibility study, which is expected to be completed by spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what advice his Department provides on the issue of wild goats entering private gardens and destroying the land and plants.
answer_text: Local ‘wild’ goats were originally domestic animals that escaped and have established self-sustaining herds that behave as wild animals. As such, these feral goats are outside anyone’s ownership, although some landowners may have free-ranging herds. The responsibility for controlling them ultimately rests with the landowner/ occupier.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the benefits of the restoration of the Armagh to Portadown rail line for (i) the Armagh area; and (ii) the North.
answer_text: Translink is undertaking a feasibility study on reinstating the Portadown to Armagh railway line.  The potential benefits of the restoration will be considered as part of the feasibility study, which is expected to be completed by spring 2025.My Department is also working towards finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review which considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. I am keen that we are ambitious around our rail network, and that we maximise the potential of rail to support social and economic development and the environmental sustainability of our transport system. The review is an ambitious piece of work that has the potential to transform our rail network. It offers an opportunity to deliver real and lasting change for people, communities and our island economy through faster speeds; greatly improved frequency; new routes, particularly across the west and north; and widened accessibility and connectivity across the island.The benefits of enhanced rail connections are many. Investment in the railway drives jobs and growth, stimulates development and regeneration, and unlocks housing supply by creating better transport links. Those links can bring communities previously cut off from public transport within easy reach of towns and cities, boosting access to services, jobs and education, and helping to address regional imbalance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of sports capital funding provided by his Department in each of the last ten years, broken down by sporting organisation.
answer_text: I can confirm that since my Department was established in May 2016, a total of just over £28.3million of sports capital funding has been disbursed. Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.  However, a breakdown of investment in each year is detailed below: YearTotal Capital Investment2016/17£	8,641,379.452017/18£	3,220,747.542018/19£	3,830,516.892019/20£	3,170,246.522020/21£	2,233,651.952021/22£	2,359,277.362022/23£	3,806,269.492023/24 (not final confirmed figure)£	1,084,668.96Total£	28,346,758.16The information also includes funding which was disbursed via Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department. This element of the question is considered an operational matter for Sport NI and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me of the information which has been placed in the Assembly Library. I would advise that for any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly. Making direct contact with the arm’s length body will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the extent to which rising business rates are a contributing factor in the closure of small and family-run businesses.
answer_text: In the context of assisting small and family run businesses here, my Department has extended the legislation to provide support to around 29,000 small businesses through the Small Business Rate Relief scheme for the 2024/25 year. As regards rising business rates, there are three elements that make up rates liability in our business rates system. The first element, the regional rate set by the Executive, even taking into account the inflationary increase passed earlier this month, has still fallen by almost 15% since 2019 as a result of the outcomes from the 2019 Business Rate Review. The second element, over which the Executive also has control, is the valuation of a property.  Again, after the 2019 Business Rate Review, my Department followed through on its commitment for more frequent revaluations to keep valuations aligned with the market. My Department has now conducted more frequent revaluations (occurring in 2015, 2020, 2023) than England, Scotland or Wales, and is preparing for a fourth revaluation in 2026. The third element of a rate bill is the district council rate poundage. My Department has no control over decisions of elected Councils in this regard, but one of my first actions as Minister was to write to the Communities Minister, who is the policy competent Minister for Local Government Finance matters, advising him that my Department has been discussing research proposals with the Ulster University to look at the economic impact of the increasing poundage differentials across the 11 Councils here. Ultimately, any legal or policy intervention in this area would need to be led by the policy competent Department, i.e. the Minister for Communities, and taken forward by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits of introducing a module on culture, incorporating education on organisations like the Armagh Rhymers and other custodians of our culture and heritage.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland curriculum already provides significant scope for the teaching of the culture and heritage in schools.Young people are able to explore cultural identity, heritage, language and diversity through curricular areas including: language and literacy; history; music and the Arts more broadly; Personal Development and Mutual Understanding in primary; and local and global citizenship within Learning for Life and Work at post-primary. To enhance curriculum delivery, schools can also choose to engage with external organisations if they so wish.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department is taking to provide aesthetic improvements for Newtownhamilton, Co. Armagh, to aid regeneration.
answer_text: Funding of £222,294 has been provided to take forward a range of regeneration measures to improve the aesthetics of Newtownhamilton, such as improvements to shop frontages, replacement and installation of new footpaths, bins & fencing.  This funding is part of the Covid Recovery Small Settlements Programme and is a collaborative approach to regeneration between my Department, DAERA and DfI to deliver a programme of projects to support smaller settlements as they recover from the impact of the pandemic and to address regeneration needs. The measures undertaken were identified by a locally established Steering Group made up of local councillors and representatives from businesses and the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps his Department is taking to facilitate economic regeneration for Newtownhamilton, Co. Armagh.
answer_text: Addressing regional economic imbalances is one of my core priorities.  While development of policies to support local economic development are primarily matters for the relevant district council to take forward, my Department and its agencies will work with local partners to ensure that economic development meets local needs and priorities.  Local retail is also an important part of the fabric of local economic activity and I intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the High Street Task Force recommendations to take this agenda forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7316/22-27, to detail the (i) number; and (ii) location of schools who have been forced to refuse places to eligible students due to a lack of capacity, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the merits of leveraging the geographic location of Daisy Hill Hospital so that it can become a cross-border hub hospital with a particular specialism; and (ii) what plans his Department has to leverage the geographic location of Daisy Hill Hospital so that it can become a cross-border hub hospital with a particular specialism.
answer_text: I refer to my response to AQW 7314/22-27 which refers to some existing services which are already provided by Daisy Hill Hospital on a cross-border basis. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust is also contributing to the regional hospital configuration blueprint project led by the Department of Health and has started the journey of developing a new long term corporate vision and strategy for the organisation. The development of Daisy Hill as a key part of the regional hospital network will continue throughout this process. My Department and the Southern Trust are always willing to explore further cross border collaboration in specialties where there is a demonstrable population health need and a sustainable long-term solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to what extent the personal injury discount rate, set by her Department, impacts on the cost of insurance policies; and (ii) for her assessment of any unintended consequences as a result of the personal injury discount rate.
answer_text: Under the Damages Act 1996, as amended by the Damages (Return on Investment Act) (Northern Ireland) 2022, the personal injury discount rate for Northern Ireland is not set by my Department, but by the Government Actuary using a prescribed methodology. In anticipation of the review of the rate by the Government Actuary due to begin on 1 July 2024, my Department is presently considering whether any modifications are required to that methodology, and, if necessary, will bring forward draft regulations for approval by the Assembly.Any change to the discount rate – whether an increase or a decrease – affects the amount of compensation payable to claimants in personal injury cases, and therefore affects the liabilities of insurers. While this means that the discount rate may have an impact on the cost of insurance policies, the extent to which this is the case is not known by my Department, nor is it a matter for my Department. The purpose of the discount rate is to give effect to the legal principle of 100% compensation, i.e. that a claimant is entitled to be fully compensated for their losses, but no more and no less. Any other consequences of the rate are similarly the outworking of the legal principle of 100% compensation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the benefits of introducing graduated licencing for new drivers.
answer_text: The safety of all road users is a key priority for my Department, and there is strong international evidence to support the introduction of a Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) scheme.  GDL will establish a revised training and testing regime for car drivers and motorcyclists.  It will also introduce some post-test restrictions for drivers/riders.  The aim, and the key anticipated benefit, is to reduce the over-representation of new, mainly young, drivers/riders in fatal and serious road collisions.The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides the statutory framework for the introduction of GDL. Following extensive consultation, a suite of subordinate legislation is being developed to take forward the GDL proposals, which will introduce:•	A Mandatory Minimum Learning Period of 6 months;•	Removing the 45mph speed restriction on learner and newly qualified drivers and enabling learning and driving on the motorway;•	A Programme of Training and learner Logbook;•	Passenger restrictions for newly qualified drivers;•	A new ‘R’ plate (post-test) and an increase in the restricted period.It is recognised that the cornerstone of any improvement in driving standards is the learning process and the Mandatory Minimum Learning Period, the Programme of Training and the use of a Logbook are aimed at changing the current mind-set when considering or learning how to drive. For this approach to be effective, it must be supported by a form of assessment that covers the full range of knowledge, understanding, skills and behaviours required to drive safely.I have asked my officials for an update on the GDL programme, and the potential timeline for full implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what additional resources his Department is deploying to repair road defects.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial position for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired. As a result, there has been a continued deterioration in our infrastructure with large numbers of potholes and other defects developing across the road network, especially after periods of particularly cold or wet weather.Therefore, in early February 2024, I announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding was used between then and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. In late February, I made an additional £13.2m funding available for essential maintenance of the road network. I also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represented a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.The level of repairs that can be carried out in any financial year depends on the funding available to my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support currently exists to assist parents with increases in the cost of their childcare.
answer_text: The Department for Communities can provide support for childcare costs for eligible claimants. This includes a childcare element in the calculation of the benefit award that can provide up to 85% reimbursement of an eligible amount the customer claims. The current maximum limits are: £950.92 for one child, and £1,630.15 for two or more children.   Additional help with upfront childcare may be provided through the Department for Communities’ Advisor Discretion Fund which provides a non-repayable grant to help eligible people move into employment by meeting the cost of upfront childcare up to a maximum amount of £1500.  Support is also available from the Department for the Economy for students who have childcare costs.Working parents, earning at least the National Minimum Wage for 16 hours per week, can receive support for their childcare costs through the UK-wide Tax-Free Childcare (TFC) scheme.  The scheme is also open to self-employed parents.  Under TFC, the Government contributes 20% towards childcare costs of eligible parents, up to £2,000 per child per year and up to £4,000 for a child with a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of repairing every road surface defect.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired. This means defects greater than 50mm depth are being repaired across all roads, including low trafficked rural roads. On high traffic urban and rural roads, defects greater than 20mm are also being repaired.In 2023-24, it was estimated that c.£55-60m would be required to deliver normal Essential Maintenance Service levels, and £35m would be required to deliver the reduced 'Limited Service' that has been in place since 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether plying for hire enforcement action should be taken against Class A, B and C licensed taxis who are waiting to pick up a customer from a pre-booked booking (i) inside the Belfast taxi zone; (ii) on the public street outside commercial or residential premises; and (iii) on a dedicated taxi stand.
answer_text: Enforcement action should not be taken against Class A, B and C licensed taxis used to lawfully wait inside the Belfast taxi zone and on the public street outside commercial premises to collect a pre-booked fare.  Only Class B taxis may enter onto a dedicated taxi stand situated inside the Belfast taxi zone.  If a Class A taxi is suspected of plying for hire within the Belfast zone where the public are likely to avail of its services, enforcement officers may direct the vehicle away from the immediate vicinity, unless they can provide evidence of a pre-booked fare. Further information on taxis and their services is available at: Taxis and their services | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what grants are available to improve the aesthetic and street appeal of our town and city centres.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of direct and indirect support to enhance and improve the physical, economic, and social appeal of our town and city centres.  In addition to the extra support for town and city centres that was provided in response to Covid-19, the Department has been and continues to deliver programmes which are designed to support the long-term viability and vitality of town and city centres.  These include Public Realm and Environmental Improvements, Revitalisation Schemes, Comprehensive Development Schemes, Urban Development Grants, Village Catalyst Grants and support for the development of Business Improvement Districts.  This represents an ongoing and significant investment in our town centres. The Department will continue to deliver these programmes subject to budget availability.  The Department also helps facilitate the appeal of towns centres through legislation, such as that for Pavement Cafes and the management of event spaces.Recently the Department has supported a new strategic approach to the regeneration of town and city centres through the work of The Ministerial Advisory Group for Architecture and the Built Environment (MAG) who have created a new model for our city, town and village centres in the future through the co-production of the Living High Street Craftkit.  This includes a series of tools that can facilitate engagement with communities (businesses, residents, services etc.) to co-produce, place specific Action and Delivery Plans with visions and sustainable solutions for our “Living High Streets”.My Department also supports the Architectural Heritage Fund to deliver its ‘Harnessing Heritage’ grant programme in Northern Ireland. This provides support to communities and social enterprises to develop viable options and plans for heritage at risk, many of which are located in town and city centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for a breakdown of the levels of financial support from government currently available to first time home buyers; and (ii) how this is comparable to the support available to first time home buyers in neighbouring jurisdictions.
answer_text: My Department currently provides significant investment to Co-Ownership, our delivery partner for shared ownership housing, to assist lower income households, particularly first time buyers, including those with low or no deposits, into homeownership.An allocation of £158 million in Financial Transactions Capital funding is approved to support over 4,400 households into home ownership over the period 2020/21 and 2024/25.In relation to the availability of, and level of funding for, interventions for first time buyers in neighbouring jurisdictions, this sort of information would not be routinely held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the implications that the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023 will have for those seeking justice through the European Court of Human Rights.
answer_text: Referral of matters to the European Court of Human Rights would be subject to its admissibility provisions, normally after domestic remedies have been exhausted.The Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023 includes provision that prohibits new civil claims related to the Troubles being brought forward from 17 May 2023.  Any claims that were filed with the court before this date will be allowed to continue to their conclusion.  The Act captures claims brought in tort, delict or under fatal accident legislation. The prohibition does not apply to an action where the court of first instance has given a final judgement on the matter in dispute before the section comes into force.  This means that subsequent appeals in these circumstances would not be caught by the Act.Provisions of the Act are currently the subject of on-going proceedings, which are now before the Court of Appeal in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what additional resources are being committed to the Legacy Investigation Branch to enable them to initiate prosecutions before 1 May 2024.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Police Service of Northern Ireland.  The funding for the Legacy Investigation Branch is a matter for the Chief Constable who has responsibility for the PSNI's budget distribution

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Operation Kenova Interim Report.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Operation Kenova interim report.  It includes important findings on the activities of the agent known as Stakeknife, particularly for victims’ families, and I sincerely hope that families find some value in the information it provides. The report makes a number of recommendations for the UK Government and others to consider. No doubt those directly linked will wish to take time to fully understand the contents and any associated implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the benefits of recording and preserving the unique dialect and aphorisms from South Armagh.
answer_text: I have made no such assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact her Department’s payments delay budgetary management policy has upon (i) access to justice; and (ii) the delivery of justice.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 5466/22-27. The department has been operating within an inadequate budget allocation. While legal aid has the third highest budget allocation within the department, as the department has multiple funding obligations across the justice system, the department has insufficient funding to meet the value of legal aid bills submitted for payment in 2023/24. As the department cannot spend in excess of its allocated budget, and in recognition of the demand for payments, the department secured £20m of additional funding for legal aid in 2022/23, and a further £14m of additional funding in 2023/24. The total budget available for legal aid in 2023/24 is £109.4m, which represents the highest level of expenditure in the history of the legal aid scheme. There is no evidence to suggest that anyone who is financially eligible with a meritorious case has been denied access to legal aid. Indeed, the period 1 March 2023 to 29 February 2024 saw a moderate increase of 0.6% in the applications for legal aid granted compared to the same period last year. There is no evidence which suggests that the supplier base has changed significantly in the last year. The number of firms of solicitors registered to undertake legal aid work in the period March 23 to February 2024 was 425, a 2.3% reduction on the same period last year. The number of barristers registered to undertake legal aid work in the same period was 535, a 0.9% decrease on the previous year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the implications on small family farms of a reduction in the compensation rate for cattle removed under the Bovine TB programme.
answer_text: Last September, the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland directed my Department to consult on a proposal to reduce the compensation rate for cattle removed under the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) programme. The consultation sought views on reducing the amount paid for a bovine animal slaughtered for bTB control, from the current 100% of market value paid per animal, to 90% of market value paid in year 1 followed by a further reduction to 75% of market value paid in year 2.  Based upon an overall compensation cost of £38 million in the 2022/23 financial year, this would, if implemented, result in a total cost to farm businesses of £3.8 million in the first year of implementation rising to £9.5 million in the second year.  As the percentage paid per animal slaughtered would apply equally across all farm businesses, there has not been any separate consideration of the impact on small family farms.  The consultation closed on 8 March 2024 and my officials have now begun the process of analysing the responses. When this work is complete, they will provide a detailed summary for my consideration. I am acutely aware of the devastating impact that a bTB breakdown can have, and the financial challenges this causes for farmers and farming families.  I therefore appreciate that any proposal which suggests reducing bTB compensation is difficult for all sections of the farming community.  I await 2024/25 budget settlement which will play a role in terms of future decisions across a range of areas in my Department, including compensation rate for cattle removed under the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
