question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the challenges faced by the PSNI with respect to (i) recruitment; (ii) budgetary constraints; and (iii) the ability to fulfil key functions.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland is under the direction and control of the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Recruitment, budget management and fulfilment of key functions are all operational matters for the Chief Constable, and I am committed to respecting his operational independence and the role of the Policing Board.  You may therefore wish to direct the specifics of your question to the PSNI.More widely, it is important to note that whilst the Budget for 2024-25 has not yet been set, it is anticipated that budgets will continue to be constrained.  Whilst I am supportive of the PSNI position, the wider financial environment will undoubtedly influence any final budget for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a detailed breakdown of the (i) number; and (ii) location of illegally erected political advertising billboards on land owned by the Department for Infrastructure.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not collect or collate this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the economic uplift that the expeditious delivery of the Casement Park project would bring to (i) West Belfast; and (ii) the North.
answer_text: As with any stadium development, a redevelopment of Casement Park will have direct and indirect benefits which could positively impact on the local community. The benefits will be further quantified and tested through the business case process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the current waiting time for medical assessments with respect to (a) Personal Independence Payments; (b) Universal Credit; and (c) Employment and Support Allowance applications; and (ii) what action his Department will be taking to reduce these wait times.
answer_text: My Department measures performance in the provision of Health Assessments for Personal Independence Payment, Universal Credit and Employment and Support Allowance, through average clearance times in claimant referrals.  Clearance times are measured from the date a provider receives a claimant referral, to the date the Health Assessment Report is submitted to my Department for decision making.  The average clearance time for a PIP assessment is 27.8 Working Days, against a target of up to 35 days.  The average clearance time for an ESA and UC assessment is 41.9 Working Days, against a target of up to 12 days.  My Department works closely with providers to improve service quality and efficiency.  Current underperformance in ESA and UC clearance times is driven by provider capacity issues, and a highly competitive marketplace for Health Professionals.  My Department anticipates that current initiatives, aligned with those employed by DWP, will significantly reduce ESA and UC clearance times over the next 8 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of (i) the financial implications of the British government’s non-commitment with respect to funding for the Casement Park project; and (ii) whether her Department is able to independently meet the financial commitment required to facilitate the construction of Casement Park.
answer_text: Discussions are ongoing with all key partners, including the UK government, in respect of the overall funding package towards the redevelopment of Casement Park.  A decision on funding will be for the Executive, and not the decision of any one department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what schemes are in place to allow patients to access reimbursement for private health care; and (ii) for his assessment of the benefits of ensuring that any future schemes can be accessed retrospectively.
answer_text: At present there are no patient driven funding schemes offered by Health and Social Care (HSC) for private treatment received following the closure of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS). Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment into reducing waiting lists.When in operation, the RoIRS was subject to strict criteria which are set out and available on the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) website: (https://online.hscni.net/travelfortreatment/roi-reimbursement/). The guidance clearly states that the SPPG will reimburse treatment costs under the RoIRS if the patient had sought and received prior approval from the SPPG for the treatment under the scheme. It advises that retrospective applications will not be considered. The SPPG required that patients sought prior approval for two main reasons: to ensure that such patients met the same thresholds and qualifying criteria as those seeking treatment in the HSC system; and, to protect the patient from the financial consequences of purchasing treatment for which they may not have received reimbursement. In Northern Ireland, all patients on a hospital waiting list are managed chronologically based on their clinical need, with urgent patients seen and treated first. Management of waiting lists is one of the mechanisms that upholds the principle of equity of access to HSC services. However, where a patient chooses to pay for private treatment, they do so in the understanding that this takes them outside the HSC system’s arrangements and its principles. The current policy is that patients cannot be reimbursed for expenses incurred in private treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients (i) on a surgical waiting list, broken down by specialism; (ii) waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment; and (iii) waiting more than twelve months for a red flag cancer referral, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how much his additional £1 million student hardship funding would equate to per student, in the event of a 100 per cent uptake.
answer_text: Student support funds are a discretionary fund, administered by the higher education institutions in line with terms and conditions set by the Department. The support funds are targeted towards students with the greatest financial need and are not an entitlement for all students.  It is therefore not possible to confirm the number of students who would be eligible if there was 100 per cent uptake.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to reaffirm his commitment to ensure Casement Park will be ready for the 2028 UEFA European Football Championship.
answer_text: The Executive’s commitment to providing £62.5m for the redevelopment of Casement Park remains. There was no Executive Commitment to ensure Casement Park was ready for the 2028 UEFA Football Championship, however I am engaging with partners to ensure there is a lasting legacy from the Competition for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the coroner's call for a public inquiry into the death of Sean Brown in 1997.
answer_text: The Coroner, Mr Justice Kinney, has decided that the inquest cannot proceed due to the Public Interest Immunity (PII) issues involved. He has stated his intention to write to the Northern Ireland Secretary of State Chris Heaton-Harris, asking the Government to establish a public inquiry into Sean Brown’s death. Mr Justice Kinney has stated that he expects a reply to his request within four weeks.I deeply regret the further pain and anguish caused to Sean’s family, in waiting for justice for such a long time, and having embarked on the inquest process, only to discover that it cannot be completed. While it is for the UK Government to decide how to respond to the call for a public inquiry, I fully support the Brown family in their desire to find out the truth about what happened to Sean.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will continue to fund Nexus NI.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the lighting on the A1 between the border and the B113 exit conforms with the standards set out in the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges; (ii) when a safety audit on (a) lighting; (b) junction design; and (c) the visibility of road markings was last undertaken for the A1; and (iii) when the next safety audit on the A1 will be undertaken.
answer_text: My Department’s policy in relation to the provision of street lighting is set out in the following policy:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/street-lighting-rsppg-e072-2019.pdf It states that an economic appraisal must be carried out to decide if lighting is required. In the case of the A1, the policy determined that lighting was not required on the mainline but was installed at the junctions of B113 at Ellis Holding Road and at Cloghogue.Road Safety Audits are carried out on road improvement schemes. The most recent road safety audits on the A1 were carried out in relation to schemes to provide a central reserve barrier at various sections between Royal Hillsborough and Banbridge and for the A1 Junctions Phase 2 scheme. Lighting, road markings and junctions are designed and maintained in accordance with my Department’s Standards applicable at that time. Road Safety Audits will be carried out as and when future road improvement schemes are designed and built.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6628/22-27, whether he will meet with the Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library to discuss the social cost of a funding withdrawal.
answer_text: My Department has recently provided additional financial support to the Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library in the form of a further discretionary grant for 2023/24.  As funding has been fully restored for 2023/24, I do not consider a meeting necessary at this point.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he will be taking to enable access to the drug Kuvan, which is available elsewhere for the treatment of phenylketonuria.
answer_text: Kuvan (also known as sapropterin) has been routinely available in Northern Ireland (NI) for treating hyperphenylalanaemia in people aged up to 21 years with phenylketonuria, and pregnant women, since October 2021 in line with the recommendations made in NICE Technical Appraisal (TA) 729.Subsequent to publication of NICE TA 729, the availability of sapropterin has been extended to eligible patients of all ages in England. This extension of use was based on a lower price being agreed by NHS England out with the NICE arrangements for access to a generic version of the medicine which is now available.In December 2023 my Department’s Permanent Secretary made a bespoke public interest decision under the Executive Formation Act to approve a regional position for the commissioning of generic sapropterin for patients of all ages. This decision was made on the basis that the acquisition cost of generic sapropterin available to the HSC is equivalent to the NHS England price and so treatment at this price is therefore deemed to be cost-effective by NICE.My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) issued a Service Notification to HSC Trusts on 2 February 2024 setting out the expectations for implementation of this decision, in line with Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether they will intervene to guarantee future funding for Nexus NI; and (ii) whether they will raise this issue with their Executive colleagues.
answer_text: We welcome the announcement by the Minister of Health on 4 March 2024 that a 12-month extension will be granted to Nexus to continue providing specialist counselling for victims of sexual abuse.It is vital that victims and survivors continue to receive specialist quality services. We support the Minister of Health’s ongoing commitment to providing this service and look forward to being kept informed about the future provision for those impacted by sexual abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the barriers which preclude women and girls from reporting violence by men; and (ii) the extent to which these barriers can be removed through (a) an Executive strategy; and (b) reforms of the policing and criminal justice system.
answer_text: There are a range of factors that may preclude women and girls from reporting violence by men, including the fear of the potential consequences of reporting; feelings of shame or embarrassment; concern that they will not be believed and in some circumstances, victims may not recognise what they have experienced is abuse.In cases involving domestic abuse, other specific factors can include the fear of an escalation of abuse; financial dependency upon their abuser; concerns about children, including that children will be removed/taken away; emotional attachment or loyalty towards their abuser; and concerns about the wider family’s response or family pressure.Lead responsibility for the development of an Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) strategy falls to the Executive Office (TEO) but will require a partnership approach across all Departments.  Given the strong interdependencies with the work of my Department, my officials have been working closely with TEO to inform the development of an EVAWG strategy and to ensure that it aligns with my Department’s ongoing work to remove the barriers to reporting and to tackle different forms of gender based violence, including domestic and sexual violence.This includes leading collaborative work with the Department of Health to develop a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategy for Northern Ireland which I intend to publish jointly with the Minister of Health later this year. This strategy, which has clear links to EVAWG, will take forward a range of work to protect and support victims and to address abusive behaviours that will benefit all victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including women and girls who are disproportionately affected by this type of crime.There are a number of support services already in place to help victims of domestic and sexual abuse to overcome barriers in reporting.  My Department provides funding for support services being delivered both by Victims Support NI and NSPCC; as well as advocacy services which are delivered by ASSIST NI. In addition, in April 2021 I launched a Sexual Offence Legal Advisors (SOLAs) scheme which provides free legal advice to victims of sexual abuse; I recently extended funding for this service until March 2025.  I also acknowledge that appearing in court can be a source of trauma for victims of domestic and sexual abuse therefore my Department has established Remote Evidence Centres that allow victims to give their evidence by video link from a facility away from the courthouse where there is no chance they will encounter the defendant or their supporters. A financial eligibility waiver was also introduced in 2022 to provide victims of domestic abuse with legal aid to respond to Article 8 Children Order proceedings, such as residence or contact proceedings, where they might ordinarily be ineligible. A victim availing of the waiver would still be liable to make some contribution to the costs.  The waiver will help ensure the interests of t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department was represented at the Women’s Aid Armagh Down conference on 28 February 2024.
answer_text: Due to competing priorities and pressures, no representative from the Department of Justice was available to attend the Women’s Aid Armagh Down conference on 28 February 2024. However there is ongoing frequent engagement, both through formal structures and informally, between my officials and stakeholders, including Women’s Aid and the Women's Aid Federation Northern Ireland (WAFNI) to inform the strategic response to tackling domestic and sexual abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits for Newry City of making adjustments to the perimeter wall of Newry Courthouse.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to make adjustments to the perimeter wall at Newry Courthouse, a key component of the physical infrastructure of that building. Physical security infrastructure at all Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) venues is deployed on the basis of regular security assessments, undertaken by NICTS, with the support of other agencies and expertise where necessary.Where a security assessment recommends adjustment, upgrade or removal of any item of physical infrastructure, the associated monetary and non-monetary benefits are assessed at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the High Court's ruling that the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023 is not compatible with European human rights legislation; and (ii) what impact this ruling has on (a) Access to Justice; and (b) Justice Delivery, within her Department.
answer_text: I have previously expressed my concerns about whether the new arrangements for dealing with the past were compliant with the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR), including to the Committee of Ministers and the Northern Ireland Affairs Committee. 		I am aware that the judgment handed down on 28 February included declarations that a number of the Act’s provisions, including those relating to immunity from prosecution, are incompatible with the ECHR and should be disapplied.  Mr Justice Colton also concluded that the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information (ICRIR) is capable of carrying out an article 2/3 compliant investigation, and the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland has said publicly he remains committed to implementing the new body. I also note that the UK Government has lodged an appeal against the judgment.		In terms of the impact of this ruling on my Department, I am concerned that in addition to the continuing legal uncertainty, there remains a lack of clarity around how the new Commission will operate in practice, how it will interface with justice organisations, what the costs associated with this work will be, and how those costs will be funded. I have, therefore, written to the Secretary of State seeking urgent clarification on those matters. 		While the Act will end investigations by the PSNI or the Police Ombudsman, as well as inquests not yet at final determination stage, in respect of Troubles-related incidents, and will prevent new civil claims being brought forward, there will be a continuing resource burden on justice organisations in relation to those legacy cases that are not caught by the Act’s provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what engagement she has had and plans to have with the (i) Minister of Education; and (ii) Minister of Health, in relation to addressing the challenges of improving the (a) education; (b) attitude; and (c) response to incidents of men’s violence against women and girls.
answer_text: As you are aware, lead responsibility for the development of an Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategy falls to the Executive Office (TEO).  However, I recognise that many of the interventions delivered by the Department of Justice contribute directly to tackling gender-based violence, including violence against women and girls, and as such will support the delivery of outcomes under the EVAWG strategy. Given the interdependencies and linkages, particularly in relation to tackling domestic and sexual abuse, my Department continues to work with the Executive Office to support the development and delivery of the EVAWG strategy and to ensure that there is appropriate alignment with the cross-Departmental strategic response to domestic and sexual abuse.Although no arrangements have been finalised yet, I hope to meet with Executive colleagues, including the Health and Education Ministers in the coming months to discuss a range of shared issues including the response to violence against women and girls. I also intend to publish a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) strategy jointly with the Minister of Health, later this year.  This DSA Strategy will take forward a range of work to protect and support victims and to address abusive behaviours which will benefit all victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including women and girls who are disproportionately affected by these types of abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many patients visited Emergency Departments (EDs) for a miscarriage, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in the last five 5 years; (ii) how he plans to improve the level of care afforded to those who visit an ED when experiencing a miscarriage; and (iii) whether he will facilitate a private area, away from the communal waiting room, in each ED for those suffering a miscarriage.
answer_text: Emergency Department information systems do not code patients attending the service in the same way that inpatient and daycase systems do. As patients presenting at ED will not have a firm diagnosis at the time they are presenting. Diagnosis and treatment will be part of the patient’s wider hospital journey. Therefore the accuracy of the information presented is not as robust as other systems.The below table provides a breakdown of  the number of patients that visited Emergency Departments (EDs) for a miscarriage, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in the last five 5 years. Each Trust has provided caveats to the information and these are noted below the table.TrustBelfast Trust*South Eastern TrustNorthern TrustSouthern TrustWestern Trust201917910015019527520201226616810616420218692169108189202269881569619320231926614966165Total648412792571986**South Eastern Trust 2023 to end July only in line with fully completed clinical coding, please note South Eastern Trust activity is admissions via ED and excludes those patients not admitted.  Belfast Trust Notes:The data provided only includes patients with a diagnosis recorded of;Threatened abortion (miscarriage)Incomplete abortion (miscarriage)Complete abortion (miscarriage)Missed abortion (miscarriage)Early / threatened / premature / pre-term labourIncomplete abortion (miscarriage) with infection South Eastern Notes:Miscarriage is not routinely diagnosed within the Emergency Department (ED) in SET.  Patients who present with a positive pregnancy test and either vaginal bleeding +/-with or without  pain are referred to the Obstetric and Gynaecology team for assessment and diagnosis.  Pain and/or bleeding in Early Pregnancy is a common symptom therefore it is difficult to quantify whether a miscarriage is the reason for these concerns.  The table above shows the number of miscarriages diagnosed and clinically coded where a patient has been admitted as an inpatient via the Emergency Department .Southern Trust Notes:Miscarriage is identified by UDDA Diag Description (1-5) = Missed Abortion or Complete Abortion (as provided by ED Staff on 04/03/2024)As at 04/03/2024 for the period of 01/01/2019 to 31/12/2023 - UDDA Coding is 93.6% completed. This means there may be patients who attended ED with a miscarriage not coded and therefore not included in this data.Western Trust Notes:The data provided is based on Diagnosis recorded on Symphony of "Complete abortion (miscarriage)", "Incomplete abortion (miscarriage)", "Miscarriage", "Miscarriage with complication" or "Missed abortion (miscarriage)".(ii) How the Minister plans to improve the level of care afforded to those who visit an ED when experiencing a miscarriage; Early Pregnancy Clinics are available in most Maternity/Gynaecology units across Northern Ireland.  These clinics will have telephone triage and self-referral triage in place that successfully help to divert those experiencing pregnancy or loss away from ED units, however it is not possible

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) his Department’s responsibility with respect to dredging and the environmental maintenance of Newry canal; and (ii) the action his Department has taken to ameliorate a recurrence of the flooding event after Newry canal breached its banks in December 2023.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspection and maintenance on the Newry Canal where it is designated. We do not have responsibility for maintenance of the section of the Newry Canal that flows south through the city from Win Business Park towards Victoria Lock. Newry, Mourne and Down District Council manage the canal over this extent.My Officials have advised that dredging can remove sediment and debris temporarily, but these materials often reaccumulate over time and experience has shown that this is not effective to mitigate flooding on the scale experienced in Newry, Autumn 2023.My Department is scheduled to input into a multi-agency review which will consider the preparation, response and recovery aspects of the November flooding that affected Newry. There will be an opportunity for elected representatives to provide feedback on this flooding event in person during a stakeholder engagement event planned for Newry on the 27th March 2024. Invitations to the event will have more detail and are due to issue soon from the consultant carrying out the review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have lobbied the UK government to introduce a scheme to facilitate the reception of humanitarian refugees displaced by the war in Gaza.
answer_text: Immigration policy is not devolved and therefore the remit to establish schemes for people seeking refuge here remains with the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the new reimbursement and invoicing arrangements under the Coroners Act (Northern Ireland) 1959 for funeral directors (i) in relation to the collection, transportation and storage of remains until the Coroners' decision is made; and (ii) when the PSNI refers a sudden death and her Department's contracted funeral director is unable to carry out such duties.
answer_text: The most recent Coroners Removals contract was awarded through a procurement exercise overseen by Construction & Procurement Delivery (CPD) in the Department of Finance, on 27 September 2022. Aside from the financial reimbursement, which is commercially sensitive, there are no new arrangements within the contract. Contractor invoicing is processed via Account NI. The Coroner is responsible for having arrangements in place to remove the remains of the deceased to the Northern Ireland Regional Forensic Mortuary, for the purpose of their investigation into the cause of death. The contract to carry out this work corresponds with the policing districts within Northern Ireland. A contracted funeral director is available on demand, twenty-four hours per day, every day of the calendar year.  There has not been an occasion where a contracted funeral director has been unable to carry out their requirement under the contract, however, should such an occasion arise, the contract provides contingency for the Coroners Service to approach a contractor in an adjacent policing district.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact that an increase in the regional rate would have upon (i) households; (ii) businesses; and (iii) local councils.
answer_text: Recognising the pressures on households and businesses the Executive has agreed to adjust the regional rate for 2024/25 in line with inflation. If the Assembly approves this it would mean a domestic property with an average capital value of £123,000, would pay 46p a week more on the regional rate element of their bill. For businesses with a rateable value of £50,000, it will mean an additional £10.73 per week on that element.The regional rate funds Executive spending and has no impact on local councils. Local councils set their own rates to fund their individual expenditure requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether a cardiovascular health check should be done at age 30.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, GPs undertake necessary checks with anybody presenting with specific cardiac issues. The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) do not currently recommend screening for cardiovascular disease at age 30. The Department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of the introduction of a cardiovascular strategy.
answer_text: Due to the current resource constraints faced by my Department, there are no plans to take forward a cardiovascular strategy for Northern Ireland. However, the Northern Ireland Cardiac Network, whose role is to identify and address key priorities for the prevention, diagnosis, treatment and long-term management of cardiac disease, has developed a three-year work plan, which is currently being progressed.My Department will continue to work closely with the Cardiac Network and other regional networks such as stroke and diabetes to improve outcomes for those suffering from cardiovascular disease.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the long-term economic impact of school leavers moving to a university in Britain; (ii) whether that economic impact is mitigated where school leavers move to a university in the Republic of Ireland; and (iii) what action his Department will take to make it more accessible for students to move to a university in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Around 75% of those going into higher education choose to study here.  This is higher than in Wales or any region of England. Of the cohort who study elsewhere, around 50% return within 15 months of graduation.  Overall, the Skills Barometer suggests that skills at level 6 and above are only marginally undersupplied here, though there are variances between subjects.  Notwithstanding this position, I want to ensure that those who want to study in the north or on the island of Ireland have the opportunity to do so.  To support this, I am committed to removing barriers to people here who want to study in the South and vice versa.  To that end, I will build on the positive engagement I have already had with my counterpart in the south, Minister Harris, and have tasked my officials with working with their counterparts in the Department for Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science to look at addressing any barriers that exist for students who wish to study on the island of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of identifying from birth, through a national screening programme, inherited lipids that signal a risk of cardiovascular disease.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) reviewed screening for Familial hypercholesterolaemia (FH) in children in 2020 and found that there is not enough evidence to recommend population screening.  Familial hypercholesterolaemia (child) - UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC). The Department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures he is adopting to ensure carers are financially supported to enable them to look after loved ones.
answer_text: I recognise the important role unpaid carers play within the wider community as well as the vital support they provide to those who they are caring for.  Carer’s Allowance is the main benefit in the social security system targeted towards unpaid carers – its primary purpose is to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment in order to provide regular and substantial care for a severely disabled person.  It was never intended to be a wage for caring or to replace lost or forgone earnings in their entirety.  As part of the annual benefits up-rating exercise the weekly rate of Carer’s Allowance will increase by 6.7% to £81.90 from April 2024.  For claimants who are able to combine their caring responsibilities with some paid employment the earnings limit in Carer’s Allowance will also increase to £151.00 per week.Depending on individual circumstances carers on a low income may be able to access additional financial support through income-related benefits, such as Universal Credit, and their associated carer premiums/elements.  The main rates in these benefits will also increase by 6.7% from April 2024.In addition, the Department’s Make the Call service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is accessing all the benefits, supports and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.I have also noted the recommendations relating to additional support for carers that were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022. The recommendations made in that report and any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of (a) the water and sewerage capacity in Newry; and (b) the impact that the current capacity has upon development in Newry; and (ii) what steps his Department will take to expand capacity.
answer_text: The issues you raised are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6072/22-27, and in relation to providing detail on the allocation of social housing, those in housing stress and those who are homeless, when this became an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and not the Minister responsible.
answer_text: The allocation of social housing; and numbers of those in housing stress and who are homeless are operational matters for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE).Following the Speaker’s comments in Assembly plenary on 12 March 2024, I will seek operational information from the NIHE Chief Executive and provide it in response to Assembly Questions.Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the practical implications on a Taxi Operators business model to ensure they are compliant with recent court rulings concerning the Private Hire Vehicles (London) Act 1998; and (ii) whether his Department plans to amend the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008 and the Taxi Operator Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 to reflect this change
answer_text: My officials are currently assessing what legislative changes may be needed to reflect and align with the recent court rulings and I will make a decision on this issue in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have sent a letter of congratulations to the newly-installed Uachatarán Cumann Lúthchleas Gael.
answer_text: We offered our congratulations in person to new GAA President, Jarlath Burns, on his new role.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department holds information on housing need.
answer_text: The NIHE in its role as the strategic Regional Housing Authority for Northern Ireland, is responsible for the identification and analysis of housing need. The NIHE has published Strategic Housing Market Analyses (SHMAs) that set out projections of future housing need and demand at Northern Ireland and Local Government District (LGD) levels. The reports are available for public access at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/housing-market-analysis Where the Department places reliance upon information on housing need, this information originates from the Housing Executive, which holds the statutory duty to regularly examine housing conditions and need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current number of oncology and haematology beds in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC); (ii) whether the current number of oncology and haematology beds in the RBHSC meets current service needs; and (iii) whether he plans to allocate funds to the expansion of oncology and haematology department in the RBHSC in line with the growth in demand.
answer_text: There are 10 haematology and oncology beds in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC)This capacity meets the current service demand.  There has been a sustained approach to investing in staffing infrastructure in paediatric oncology and haematology services since 2012/13.  In total £1.8m has been made available for additional consultant, nursing, pharmacy, psychology, physiotherapy, dietetics, occupational therapy and play specialists.  This supported the expansion of the service from 8 to 10 beds in 2017. A very small number of cancers in children are too complex to be fully managed by the team in Belfast.  Specific pathways for care for these rarer tumours have therefore been established in partnership with highly specialist providers in England.  The NI Cancer Registry information shows that the incidence of paediatric cancer has remained stable over the last 20 years (2000-2020) and colleagues there have confirmed that there has been no statistically significant growth in incidence over this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many new PSNI recruits have graduated in passing out ceremonies in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The recruitment and training of PSNI officers is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost to his Department for compensation paid to motorists as a result of road defect claims, broken down by Assembly constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to claims can be found online at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019 Table 7 in the above link provides details of the vehicle damage claims expenditure resulting from road defects and other road issues during the last five full financial years.  However, please note that my Department maintains its records by Roads Section Office, which broadly align with Council areas, and not by Assembly constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits of embedding driving instruction and road safety awareness into the curriculum from age 15.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland curriculum is designed to have limited prescription which gives schools flexibility in what they teach and the approaches that best suit the needs of their pupils. The curriculum is able to adapt to meet the changing trends and developments within society. It also accommodates the evolving nature of education, enabling the incorporation of contemporary topics and skills, and encouraging a dynamic and responsive learning environment. Road safety education provision within schools has an important role in developing children and young people’s attitudes and behaviours to become safer road users, as children and as adults in later life. The curriculum provides a range of opportunities for schools to highlight road safety. At post primary level road safety is referred to in the Personal Development element of Learning for Life and Work. The flexibility in our curriculum enables schools to engage with external providers who can offer driving lessons should the school decide it is a priority.  However, it is important that young people are taught to drive by appropriately qualified and registered driving instructors which is a matter for the Department of Infrastructure.The Department of Infrastructure provide educational road safety resources which primary and post primary schools can avail of.    The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides a range of GCSE and Vocational Qualifications that support Learning for Life and work including, Motor Vehicle and Road User Studies GCSE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of applications; (ii) the number of places offered; and (iii) the number of rejections to each (a) primary; and (b) post-primary school in each of the last 10 years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the benefits of initiating dialogue with the Irish Department of Health with a view to establishing Daisy Hill Hospital as an acute cross-border hospital; (ii) what engagement he has had with the Irish Department of Health to date on this issue; and (iii) what engagement he plans to have in the future with the Irish Department of Health on this issue.
answer_text: Daisy Hill Hospital is already an acute hospital providing a wide range of inpatient and outpatient services, including on a cross-border basis where appropriate, for example, an area-wide renal dialysis service which treats patients from some border areas of the Republic of Ireland, 24/7 emergency department and ENT services. Whilst there has been positive progress in stabilising medical staffing and acute inpatient medicine at Daisy Hill, rising demand for services, workforce and finance remain as very serious challenges both across the Southern Trust and the wider Health and Social Care system regionally. The Trust is contributing to the regional hospital configuration blueprint project led by the Department of Health and has also started the journey of developing a new long term corporate vision and strategy for the organisation. The development of Daisy Hill as a key part of the regional hospital network will continue throughout this process.My Department and the Southern Trust are always willing to explore further cross border collaboration where there is a demonstrable population health need and a sustainable long-term solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of Special Education Needs (SEN) provision; (ii) what consideration he has given to a comprehensive reform of SEN systems and processes; and (iii) for his assessment of the performance of the Education Authority with respect to its statutory obligations in relation to SEN.
answer_text: Since 2017 there have been a range of reports that have resulted in over 200 recommendations.  These reports reference the challenges faced by children, young people and their families in trying to navigate an often complex system in order to access support.  It is clear that, even with the significant investment of circa £500m, the system is not meeting the support needs of children and young people.  There is a clear requirement for significant transformation of our system that will ensure high quality, child centred and sustainable Special Educational Needs (SEN) support.  The first recommendation from the Ipsos Review of SEN requires that the department lead and implement transformational change on SEN/Disability (SEN/D) and the wider system.  The End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN and associated workstreams have been established to ensure that the systemic reform needed to provide quality outcomes for our children and young people with SEN is prioritised and implemented. Whilst it is clear that across the system there are significant operational challenges facing the Education Authority (EA), I am committed to ensuring that EVERY CHILD is happy, learning and succeeding and that children with SEN get the right support, delivered by the right people, at the right time and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what action he proposes to take to avert the planned industrial action by Translink workers on 27 February, 28 February and 29 February 2024; and (ii) for his assessment of the likely impact the industrial action will have.
answer_text: Following the Executive’s agreement to allocate the Department additional resource budget of £87.8m, an additional budget allocation was provided to Translink to enable it to negotiate a 2023-24 pay award with trade unions. I welcome the positive development on these negotiations and the unions agreement to suspend the forthcoming strikes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the implications of the funding announcement made by the Irish government with respect to the redevelopment of Casement Park.
answer_text: I note the Irish Government’s recent announcement of its intention to contribute over £40 million to the Casement Park project. This is just one part of the overall funding package. Additional funding requirements will have to be agreed with partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many inpatients in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust are unable to be discharged due to a lack of access to domiciliary care and care in the community.
answer_text: Information on the number of inpatients in the Southern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust who are unable to be discharged due to a lack of access to domiciliary care and care in the community is not held centrally. This information has been provided by the Southern HSC Trust for the position on 21st February 2024 and is detailed in the Table 1 overleaf.Table 1: Number of inpatients in the Southern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust who are unable to be discharged due to a lack of access to domiciliary care and care in the community, for the position on 21st February 2024 Number of patients unable to be discharged due to lack of access to domiciliary careNumber of patients unable to be discharged due to lack of access tocare in the community3146 Source: Health and Social Care TrustsSouthern HSC Trust advise that at times, discharges and delays, while included in the information provided in Table 1, do not necessarily indicate a service shortfall. Rather, they may arise from patients or families being reluctant to consider interim solutions while awaiting their preferred choice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to the Irish government’s announcement of €12.5 million for a new hourly-frequency rail service between Belfast and Dublin, (i) for his assessment of the benefits of that service including a stop in Newry; and (ii) whether he will be lobbying the Irish government to that effect.
answer_text: (i)An hourly service will double the current frequency and significantly enhance sustainable transport connectivity between the two largest cities on the island of Ireland.  This includes a stop at Newry. With more frequency and capacity it will make it even easier for people to better connect and make greener transport choices which will bring economic and social benefits for all.My officials will continue to liaise with the Department of Transport on the development of this important economic transport corridor between Belfast and Dublin.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why scheduled school enhancement programme works at St. Mary’s Primary School, Barr, were cancelled at Stage 4; (ii) the total expenditure on the project between application in January 2017 and cancellation in March 2023; and (iii) whether he will be making funding available for the project to be brought to completion.
answer_text: The School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at St. Mary’s Primary School, Barr has not been cancelled but paused at pre-tender stage due to the extremely challenging capital budget position. The total capital expenditure for this project to date is £347,988.95.At present, budget constraints and pressures in regard to SEN placements mean capital funding is not available for the project to proceed to construction. I will continue to make the case for increased investment in the education estate, to allow much-needed projects, including the project at St. Mary’s Primary School, Barr, to be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the impact of the €50 million funding allocation made by the Irish Government with respect to Casement Park; and (ii) whether the funding announcement by the Irish Government will enable Casement Park to progress on schedule for the Ulster GAA Football Final to be played there in 2027, followed by the UEFA European Football Championship in 2028.
answer_text: The Irish Government’s recent announcement of its intention to contribute over £40 million to the Casement Park project is just one part of the overall funding package. Additional funding requirements will have to be agreed with partners.It is understood that the 2027 Ulster GAA football final is due to be played in May 2027. The project programme that is being developed does not facilitate the playing of a major final in this timeframe, regardless of the recent funding announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the role of community pharmacies in reducing the burden upon GP practices, Out of Hours services and Emergency Departments; and (ii) the benefits of enabling pharmacies and pharmacists to become independent prescribers.
answer_text: Community pharmacies are contributing to reducing the burden on GP practices (GP), Out of Hours (OOH) and Emergency Departments (ED)  in:The provision of the Pharmacy First Service to provide a triage in dealing with everyday health conditions, Emergency hormonal contraception, sore throats and uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infections (UTI) in women aged 16-64 years.The provision of care services such as: smoking cessation, Flu and Covid vaccinations (including care homes), care home support services, living well campaigns and a needle exchange scheme. Burdens are also reduced on GPs, OOH and through the provision of the community pharmacy emergency supply service. Community pharmacy also contributes to the load on other sectors by the provision of local pharmacy rotas, the palliative care network, and the provision of support to patients who have difficulty in adhering to complex medicines regimes.  I am committed to seeing our valuable health service staff resource develop their skills. For a number of years, my Department has advocated for the development of prescribing qualifications by various professions. I very much look forward to seeing more pharmacists becoming qualified independent prescribers as this will enable further redesign and modernisation of pathways for patients to receive the right care at the right time in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail overall access to (i) 4G; and (ii) 5G services in (a) Newry; (b) Belfast; (c) Lisburn; (d) Armagh; and (e) Derry; and (ii) what steps his Department can take to enhance 4G and 5G connectivity in areas that are lagging behind.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) holding the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters ensuring that all local considerations are front and centre of any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity here.The Department for the Economy does not hold information on the number, location or operational coverage of any radio or telecommunication infrastructure. This information is held by the owners of this infrastructure, the Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), Vodafone, VMO2, Three and BT(EE). Information on current coverage is published by Ofcom. Their latest report can be found at Nations reports - Ofcom and individual postcodes can be checked on their View mobile availability - Ofcom Checker. DfE understands the many challenges faced by our rural communities in terms of digital connectivity and indeed the issues faced by MNOs when they attempt to roll out telecommunications infrastructure. My officials are involved in several ongoing initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here. The Shared Rural Network (SRN), is a project developed by the four Mobile Network Operations (MNOs) (EE, Vodafone, VMO2 and Three) and the British Government. Locally, SRN is expected to deliver 85% 4G coverage from all four MNOs and 98% coverage from at least one MNO.The SRN is expected to extend 4G coverage in the most cost-effective, efficient, timely and environmentally friendly way for consumers and businesses in these remote rural communities. My officials participate in the local SRN Working Group to provide a local perspective.More information about the Shared Rural Network can be found at https://srn.org.uk.To help address wider challenges faced by the MNOs with regard to telecommunications infrastructure roll out, my officials led on the development of the published cross-sectoral Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI) and established the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT), which aims to identify the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provide solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity here. You can view the MAP NI implementation updates at https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/articles/map-ni-implementation-progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many people in (i) the criminal justice system; (ii) prison; and (iii) juvenile detention centres have special educational needs, including (a) speech and language; and (b) communication special educational needs.
answer_text: The responsibility for assessing health and/or educational needs does not fall to my Department.  A significant number of those in contact with the criminal justice system have a range of pre-existing health and educational needs, many of which will be unmet at the point of entry into the system.  Where those working within the youth or adult justice system are aware of a particular need, they will advocate for this to be addressed by the appropriate statutory authority.  Within the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS), the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust employs three speech and language therapists to work across the three adult prison estates.  Their role involves screening for speech, language and communication difficulties and providing ongoing treatment and support to those who require it.  In addition, in order to consider the needs of any prisoners with a Special Educational Need and/or Disability, who are undertaking a course of study, a SEN coordinator ensures that SEN needs are met in accordance with current legislation.  They will also make referrals, where necessary, for speech and language therapy.In order to respond to the needs of children and young people in custody, a shared therapeutic and mental health service operates across both the Juvenile Justice Centre and Lakewood Secure Care Centre.  This shared service, named Acorn, consists of a multi-disciplinary team that covers a number of professional disciplines, including psychology, psychiatry, occupational therapy and mental health nursing.  Plans are under development to expand the team to include speech and language therapists, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5948/22-27, (i) whether compensation will be made available to those who received inaccurate negative cervical smear test results which then resulted in pre-cancerous changes not being detected; and (ii) what measures he intends to implement to avoid further inaccurate cervical screening results.
answer_text: Cervical screening reduces the risk of developing cervical cancer by identifying women at higher risk of developing cervical cancer (pre- cancer) and offering investigation and treatment where appropriate. However, like other screening tests, cervical screening cannot identify every single case of pre-cancer. It is not a diagnostic test. Any decision regarding compensation would be a matter between the individual involved and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT).The Royal College of Pathologists report, published in October 2023, contained eight recommendations and the SHSCT fully accepted these and committed to work with the PHA and my Department to implement them in full.  As you are aware, since 11 December 2023, primary HPV testing has now been in introduced into the Northern Ireland cervical screening pathway, which is a much more effective test at detecting who is at higher risk of developing cervical cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the primary impediments to economic development in (i) Newry; and (ii) Armagh city.
answer_text: I recognise that different areas face different economic impediments.  This  is why regional balance is one of the key priorities in my economic vision. My officials are currently working with councils to identify their main economic priorities and interventions to address these will be included in the Sub-Regional Economic Plan I will publish in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits to (i) students; and (ii) teachers of implementing the pay award level currently being lobbied for by NASUWT.
answer_text: Any agreed pay award between TNC Management Side and NITC will have a positive effect on our students and teachers as it will lead to the cessation of all industrial action by teachers including strike action and action short of strike. Teaching remains an attractive career choice for students in Northern Ireland, the number of qualified teachers greatly exceeds available jobs, an above inflation pay rise would be likely to inspire more students to consider teaching as a profession.I acknowledge the challenges affecting all employees within the education sector in Northern Ireland due to increased cost of living. Negotiations are ongoing between TNC Management Side and teachers’ unions to address pay concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the benefits of establishing a government-backed insurance programme for businesses operating (i) on a floodplain; (ii) in an area with high insurance premiums; and (iii) in areas where insurance is unattainable.
answer_text: Financial services including insurance are reserved matters so both the benefits and the costs of progressing an insurance scheme for businesses would be a matter for the British government.Currently, a government-backed insurance scheme for householders at risk of flooding (The Flood Reinsurance Scheme (Flood Re)) is in operation until 2039.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the benefits of establishing a permanent emergency relief fund for businesses impacted by flooding, fire and natural disasters.
answer_text: It is normal business practice to take out insurance to protect against risks, however I appreciate that in some limited cases cover is prohibitively expensive or not available at all. In all circumstances, it is important that we work across government to manage the risk and minimise the impact of natural events such as storms and prolonged heavy rainfall.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail Invest NI’s (i) statutory responsibility; (ii) record of intervention with respect to (a) flooding; (b) fire; and (c) natural disaster recovery for businesses, under its purview, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: There is no statutory mechanism available to Invest NI to provide financial support to businesses with respect to flooding, fire, or natural disaster recovery. Invest NI has offered indirect support to businesses in these unfortunate circumstances, for example property searches or layout planning for temporary accommodation. It is not possible to provide detail of this indirect support over the past 10 years. As per AQW 6758 22-27, there are currently no permanent support schemes in place to assist businesses with recovery from fires, flooding or natural disaster recovery, however, local Councils launched and administered The Enhanced Flood Support Scheme and the Flooding Hardship Scheme to provide support to businesses impacted by the flooding in October and November 2023. Both schemes are now closed, local Councils continue to assess applications and aim to begin making payments in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what permanent support schemes are available to assist businesses with recovery from (i) fires; (ii) flooding; and (iii) other natural disasters.
answer_text: The Minister is acutely aware of the devastating impact natural disasters can have on business owners. There are currently no permanent support schemes in place to assist businesses with recovery from (i) Fires; (ii) flooding; and (iii) other natural disasters. It is normal business practice to take out insurance to protect against risks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a breakdown of the level of financial support provided by (i) his Department; and (ii) statutory agencies in his Department's remit to the businesses at Loughbrook Industrial Estate, Bessbrook, who were impacted by the fire on 7 December 2023.
answer_text: It is normal commercial practice for the risk of fire to be covered by insurance, not by government. However Invest NI is engaged with its client companies based at Loughbrook Industrial Estate and is providing advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to (i) improve; (ii) increase resources; (iii) streamline; and (iv) reform the system for processing planning applications in this mandate.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way.  I intend to take some time to review the progress that has been made to date and will continue to work closely with key stakeholders to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system.  Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the social contribution made by the Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library, Armagh; (ii) the anticipated social cost of a withdrawal or reduction in its service provision; and (iii) the benefits of reinstating core funding for the library.
answer_text: I am advised that the Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library has for several years submitted a business case to the Department in order to avail of a discretionary grant.  The business case articulates the library’s aims and purpose, what the discretionary grant is used for, and the consequences of the grant not being provided.  The Cardinal O’Fiaich Memorial Library is therefore best placed to answer your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5947/22-27, what advice his Department would give to individuals with concerns following their own negative experiences and injuries resulting from pelvic mesh implants.
answer_text: Anyone who has concerns relating to their pelvic mesh implants should contact either their GP or the consultant who performed their surgery to ensure they receive all appropriate care.In relation to the report by the Patient Safety Commissioner in England, my Department will take time to consider the findings and recommendations. Further communications will issue at an appropriate stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the effectiveness of the management of Newry Canal, with respect to flood risk minimisation, in the period immediately preceding the floods in November 2023.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspection and maintenance on the Newry Canal. The water levels in the Newry Canal are managed by Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.  During the whole period, both prior to and during the flooding which occurred in November 2023, my Department worked in close liaison with the Council who managed the water levels in the Newry Canal. My Department is scheduled to input into a multi-agency review which will consider the preparation, response and recovery aspects of the November flooding that affected Newry. There will be an opportunity for elected representatives to provide feedback on this flooding event in person during a stakeholder engagement event planned for Newry on the 27th March 2024. Invitations to the event will have more detail and are due to issue soon from the consultant carrying out the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what provision exists for services in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust for adults with ADHD; and (ii) how he plans to address the gap in services relative to other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: There is no adult Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) diagnostic service in the Southern Trust as this has never been commissioned and staff have not received training/experiential learning. Intervention service is limited to service users with a confirmed diagnosis (received in CAMHS services or previously via adult mental health services) requiring ongoing medication management. If a service user was previously diagnosed and was discharged following disengagement, but seeks to restart medications, the decision to accept the referral would be made on a case by case basis. Private assessments are not accepted by services.ADHD provision for adults has grown organically across Health and Social Care Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. While this has meant a level of provision is available in several Trusts, it is recognised that this has also led to a gap between what can be provided by Trusts in terms of demand and the level of prevalence of ADHD, leading to individuals waiting for assessment and support.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), alongside Departmental policy colleagues, are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult Services under review, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reinstate the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) why the Sports Council for Northern Ireland Lottery Funding accounts from 2016 had not been signed off prior to 2023; and (ii) to detail the total sum of Sports Council for Northern Ireland Lottery funding spent in each year from 2016 to 2023.
answer_text: The question relating to the ‘Sports Council for Northern Ireland Lottery Funding accounts’ is an operational matter for Sport NI. You should, therefore, seek the information requested directly from Sport NI and direct your question to Richard Archibald, Interim Chief Executive Officer at Assemblybusiness@sportni.net.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the benefits of bringing Lough Neagh into public ownership.
answer_text: The scope of my officials’ current work on Lough Neagh is focused on the blue green algae and water quality issues.  It does not address the Lough’s ownership. I recently had a constructive meeting with the Earl of Shaftsbury where we discussed a range of issues including ownership.		The Lough Neagh Partnership has been awarded funding to research future management of the Lough, including public ownership.  I look forward to seeing their study which will provide stakeholders with a solid basis on which to make future decisions.I have been clear that there are no quick fixes to this issue and that I cannot fix this alone. We need collective action across government, private and public sector and in the community.  I intend therefore to continue my engagement with the Earl of Shaftsbury, partners and stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what mechanisms her Department will  introduce to address the (i) appeals; (ii) withdrawal of funding; and (iii) requests for the return of funding, in relation to the flood relief being administered by local councils.
answer_text: On the 21st February I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding. As with the previous Flood Scheme these new schemes will be administered by the local councils, and as such responsibility for the processes involving appeals, withdrawal of funding and requests for the return of funding will sit with them. The councils plan to open the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme for applications early next week with the two hardship schemes opening in the following couple of weeks. Further details on scheme administration will be provided then.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average number of daily passengers travelling by train from (i) Newry to Belfast; (ii) Portadown to Belfast; (iii) Newry to Dublin; (iv) Belfast to Dublin; and (v) Portadown to Dublin, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: The management of passenger number using Translink services is an operational matter for Translink. You should therefore direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what measures and strategies are being adopted to preserve and conserve our wildlife.
answer_text: My Department is developing a Nature Recovery Strategy aimed at protecting and restoring our natural environment, which will include actions relating to vulnerable wildlife and their habitats.The strategy is currently under development, with a view to launching a consultation later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will write to congratulate the team responsible for the use of specialist radiotherapy treatment for the first time on the island of Ireland.
answer_text: Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) is not currently routinely commissioned in Northern Ireland for the treatment of renal cancers.  SABR is a promising non-invasive treatment option for primary renal cell cancer.  Dr Aidan Cole, Consultant Clinical Oncologist and Senior Clinical Lecturer at Queens University Belfast led on a pilot phase of treatment for 5 patients in Belfast Trust.  Dr Cole is part of the UK SABR Consortium which sets guidelines for SABR treatments in the UK.  He has been part of a Sub-Committee tasked with commissioning SABR for primary renal cancer.  NICE are currently reviewing evidence base regarding SABR for renal cell cancer indication. A submission to NHS England is under review. This treatment may be considered suitable for renal cancer patients not deemed suitable for surgery, or other conventional techniques, who would have no alternative treatment options and with expectation that their tumour would grow and cause local symptoms and potentially spread to other organs. Pilots have similarly taken place in other UK centres and the evidence base for this treatment is growing.  We await clarity on the benefits and cost effectiveness from the pilot and revised NICE guidance. Where regional commissioning/investment is required, the timescale for implementation will be dependent on the complexity and scale of the issue, available resource and other existing commissioning priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail fish stocks in (i) Newry Canal; (ii) the Clanrye River; and (iii) Acton Lake, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Newry Canal, the Clanrye River and Acton Lake fall under the jurisdiction of the Loughs Agency. The Loughs Agency does not have information for the Newry Canal and Acton Lake for the specified period.The Clanrye is predominantly a trout/sea trout river with low numbers of salmon present also. Electrofishing surveys provide the juvenile stock information during the summer surveys. The Clanrye catchment semi-quantitative (5 minute timed) juvenile electrofishing survey results for trout for 2019-2023 are: CatchmentNo. of sites fishedAverage no. 0+ trout5 year 0+ rolling averageAverage no. 1+ trout5 year 1+ rolling average2019Clanrye131.82.73.23.82020Clanrye  2.8 3.22021Clanrye202.62.84.83.62022Clanrye151.42.83.33.32023Clanrye120.71.64.54.0Juvenile salmon catches from the Clanrye River for 2019 – 2023 show less than one for average no. 0+ salmon across the sites fished, 5 year 0+ rolling average, average no. 1+ salmon, and 5 year 1+ rolling average across the period.The redd numbers would also indicate adult fish spawning in the river, although there is no differentiation between trout or salmon. Redd numbers for the Clanrye River for 2020 – 2024 are:YearTotal Number of Redds Counted on the Clanrye River2020/2152021/22152022/23720203/2423
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will write to the two centenarians, from Newry and Bessbrook, who recently celebrated their 100th birthday.
answer_text: We are very pleased to send, through you, our congratulations and best wishes to them on this remarkable achievement.  If they wish to confirm they are content for us to do so and to provide their personal details to our offices, we would also be delighted to write personally to them to offer our congratulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what revenue raising measures she will be adopting in this mandate.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Executive has given a commitment to the British Government to develop a Sustainability Plan with a pre-requisite that the right level of funding is provided.  However, it is essential that the Executive is afforded adequate time to give proper consideration to this important matter. This cannot be considered in isolation, without also fully examining the other essential aspects of such a plan, including a productivity plan, long-term budget planning, and a capital plan.  But most importantly the British Government needs to put in place a fair, and appropriately baselined, needs-based Fiscal Floor which recognises our underfunding for the start of the next Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities the total number of people (i) waiting for the allocation of social housing; (ii) in identified housing stress; and (iii) homeless, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The total number of people waiting for the allocation of social housing, those in housing stress and those who are homeless broken down by constituency are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact of illegally erected party political billboard advertising upon (i) road safety; (ii) land title; (iii) visual amenity; (iv) community relations; and (v) government integrity.
answer_text: With the exception of an advertisement relating specifically to a pending parliamentary election, or to a pending election to the Assembly or a district council, Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to erect unauthorised signs or advertisements, including party political advertising, on any tree, structure or other works in or on a road.One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised advertisements pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard.  Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with PSNI and other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.
