question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide the total capital expenditure on infrastructure projects relative to (i) the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) Britain, broken down per capita, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: I have provided details of my Department’s total capital expenditure on a per capita basis, since its inception in 2016, in the table below.Year2016-17£2017-18£2018-19£2019-20£2020-21£2021-22£2022-23£Final Outturn per capita*221.5225.6275265.3308.6395.5416.8*Source: 2022 Mid-Year Population Estimates for Northern Ireland 31 August 2023My Department does not hold the relevant comparable data for the other jurisdictions, nor details on the total capital expenditure for infrastructure projects in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average waiting times for (i) assessment; (ii) statementing; and (iii) intervention and support for children and young people with special educational needs, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs (1998) (the Code) sets out the  overall statutory timeframe to complete a  statutory assessment of special educational needs (SEN) as 26 weeks.Once a referral has been submitted to the Education Authority (EA), it has six weeks to process and decide if it will progress the case for a full assessment of a child’s SEN.  There are no average figures available from the EA for this period.The tables below, provided by the EA, show average waiting times for each phase as of Thursday 15 February 2024.  If a decision is taken to progress with a full assessment, advice will be sought from medical and other professionals; this phase of the assessment process has a 10-week timeframe.AVERAGE NUMBER OF WEEKS CONSTITUENCY 15.2Belfast East 16.3Belfast North 15.0Belfast South 15.7Belfast West 11.7East Antrim12.1East Londonderry 15.3Fermanagh and South Tyrone 18.8Foyle 14.1Lagan Valley 12.0Mid Ulster 18.1Newry and Armagh8.9North Antrim16.2North Down 12.6South Antrim16.3South Down 15.7Strangford 16.3Upper Bann16.1West Tyrone Average: 15.1Following assessment, if it is decided to progress to a statement of SEN, a proposed statement is prepared and issued to parents/carers to ensure that it presents a positive and accurate appraisal of their child’s SEN and that the provision proposed represents an appropriate response to those needs.  This phase of the process has a 2-week timeframe. AVERAGE NUMBER OF WEEKS CONSTITUENCY 4.0Belfast East 4.6Belfast North 4.6Belfast South 5.6Belfast West 0.0East Antrim0.0East Londonderry 0.0Fermanagh and South Tyrone 0.0Foyle 0.5Lagan Valley 0.1Mid Ulster 0.2Newry and Armagh0.0North Antrim0.3North Down 0.2South Antrim0.1South Down 0.1Strangford 0.2Upper Bann0.0West Tyrone Average: 1.3Following parental consideration of the proposed statement and initial discussion in relation to the parental representations in relation to school preference, the EA must consult the Board of Governors (BoG) of any grant-aided school which it proposes to name in a statement.  The timeframe for completion of this phase is 8 weeks. AVERAGE NUMBER OF WEEKS CONSTITUENCY 9.7Belfast East 11.0Belfast North 10.6Belfast South 11.1Belfast West 5.0East Antrim5.0East Londonderry 5.5Fermanagh and South Tyrone 4.3Foyle 7.1Lagan Valley 5.5Mid Ulster 5.9Newry and Armagh5.3North Antrim8.0North Down 5.8South Antrim6.9South Down 8.6Strangford 6.7Upper Bann5.2West Tyrone Average: 7.1 Intervention and support for children and young people with a statement of SEN will be outlined in their final statement and will be unique to the child therefore average timescales do not apply.It is clear that there are delays within the process when seeking additional advice from medical and other professionals (i).  The Code sets out circumstances where there may be some exceptions to the time limits within which a Health and Social Care Trust or other professional submits information. While the

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Southern Relief Road; and (ii) whether a bridge solution will be incorporated to facilitate the entry of tall ships into the Albert Basin, Newry.
answer_text: Newry Southern Relief Road is nearing the completion of the specimen design, the preparation of the draft Statutory Orders, and an Environmental Impact Assessment Report (EIAR), in advance of undertaking a statutory public consultation.  The draft Orders and EIAR are expected to be published in Spring 2024, based on a decision taken under the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 that a 50m span fixed bridge over the Newry Ship Canal was the preferred option. The decision was taken after detailed consideration of a range of factors, including engineering, economic, heritage impact and environmental assessments including the impact on the Scheduled Monument of Newry Canal. I am going to review the decision taken under the NI Executive Formation Act to have a 50m span fixed bridge over the Newry Ship Canal . I am very conscious of the need to advance the building of the Newry Southern Relief Road given the huge benefits it will bring to the residents and businesses of Newry both economically and environmentally. With this in mind I will confirm the outcome of my review within a number of weeks.  Additional scheme information is available via the linkNewry Southern Relief Road - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a similar scheme to compensate women injured by pelvic mesh implants will be rolled out in Northern Ireland, as has been recommended in England.
answer_text: My Department will take time to fully consider the recent report from the Patient Safety Commissioner for England, including the findings and recommendations in relation to financial and non-financial redress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action he can take to address the cost of childcare for parents.
answer_text: I would like to thank the member for their question. I can assure him that I am absolutely committed to supporting people and recognise the importance in unlocking opportunities for parents to create the right incentives for work. Whilst childcare policy responsibility rests with the Department of Education, the benefit system plays an important role in supporting parents to access childcare. Universal Credit  provides financial support, as part of the Universal Credit award, to help households meet the cost of approved childcare for children whom a claimant is responsible for.  Eligible households will be reimbursed for up to 85% of the cost of childcare, subject to a monthly maximum upper limit. In last year’s Spring Budget these amounts were increased by nearly 50% and are currently up to £950.92 for one child and £1,630.15 for two or more children.Universal Credit work coaches may also provide a non-repayable grant from the Advisor Discretion Fund to help eligible people meet the initial upfront cost of childcare up to a maximum amount of £1500.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Armagh East and West link road.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.The publication of the prioritised list of major road schemes, that will continue to be progressed, explains that development work on the Armagh East Link the Armagh North and West Link is currently paused.  Details of the current status of the Armagh East and West Link Road projects will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A28 Armagh East - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk) and  & A3 Armagh North and West Link - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of cervical screening in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The ongoing cervical cytology review has two components: a review of previous cervical screening slides (samples) and a ‘call forward’ for a new cervical smear test where a previous slide is no longer held or is no longer suitable for review.   The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) had prioritised those slides considered to be at the highest risk, to be reviewed first. As of 13 February 2024, 1,966 slide reviews have been completed.  The Trust has invited 1,463 women to attend for a new cervical smear test and 562 women have attended to date.My department continues to work closely with PHA and SHSCT to ensure that the review is progressed in a safe and timely manner, with the intention of it being completed within the next few months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether maternity services are secure at Daisy Hill Hospital for the long-term future; and (ii) for his assessment of the consequences of removing maternity services from Daisy Hill Hospital as outlined in a report undertaken by Getting It Right First Time .
answer_text: (i) Southern Health and Social Care Trust  is committed to supporting long-term maternity services at Daisy Hill Hospital. Daisy Hill has become the Trust-wide base for elective caesarean sections for women across the entire Southern area, with 664 planned caesarean sections completed in the 2023-2024 year.The Trust notes that the continued provision of services is dependent on appropriate workforce across both the medical and midwifery professions and the training of new staff to replace retirements and resignations into the future.  (ii) The Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) report did not recommend removal of maternity services from Daisy Hill Hospital but that the “Southern Trust should consider the strategic position of maternity inpatients at Daisy Hill Hospital and consider converting or developing the gynaecology site as an elective hub.”(doh-ni-gynaecology-report-jan-24.pdf (health-ni.gov.uk)  Page 24Southern Health and Social Care Trust have committed to developing Daisy Hill as the strategic base for planned elective caesarean sections for the Southern Trust.They also remain committed to ensuring the provision of safe, high quality maternity services for women across the Southern Trust area, continually reviewing and taking account of national and regional policy guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients that have accessed maternity services at Daisy Hill Hospital from outside the North; and (ii) how much income this activity has generated for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not readily available and could only be compiled at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the current arrangements at Maphoner GP Surgery, Mullaghbawn, will be extended beyond 1 April 2024.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH) can advise that Maphoner Surgery General Medical Services (GMS) contract is held by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) until 31 March 2024.  The SPPG are currently in discussions with SHSCT in relation to holding the contact for a further period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the health specialities offered across the health service, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) NI-wide performance ranking, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Inpatient activity data is published annually in August. This includes information on inpatient specialties within each hospital. The most recent release issued in August 2023 for 2022/23 dates this information back to 2013/14 and can be found at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hs-inpatient-hts-tables-22-23.csv Outpatient activity data is published annually in August.  These publications provided data broken down by hospital up until 2019/20.  From 2020/21 onwards this data is only available at HSC Trust level.  The most recent release issued in August 2023 for 2022/23 can be found at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hospital-statistics-outpatient-activity-statistics-202223Previous publications can be found at this link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hospital-statistics-outpatient-activity-statistics-200910-202122Appendix 1 within these PDF publications shows a breakdown of specialties by hospital and from 2020/21 by HSC Trust.There is no mechanism within the Department for performance ranking at a specialty level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the consequences of allowing planned cuts to services at the NI Children’s Hospice to proceed; and (ii) whether he plans to reverse these planned cuts.
answer_text: I recognise and applaud the vital work of the NICH and all hospices in Northern Ireland.As requested by me, DoH officials have engaged in detailed discussions last week with NI Hospice.It is clear to me that the financial pressures facing NICH go much deeper than any reduction of Departmental support.  This reduction involved the ending last summer of an additional but temporary annual payment of £170k. This decision was notified to the NICH in June 2023 and took effect from September 2023 which resulted in £85K reduction in this financial year.  I took the decision on Friday 09/02/2022 to reinstate that funding in full for this year.This payment is in addition to the core annual funding of £1.6m which my Department is providing directly to NICH this year. This core funding included a £420,000 component which had not been recurrent, meaning it would have to be subject to confirmation each year. I can confirm that, going forward, this will be made recurrent, giving the Hospice greater certainty.I am keenly aware that the financial challenges facing NICH go much deeper than this or next year. Like all health care providers, it has encountered significantly rising running costs, reflecting inflationary pressures.Regrettably, the NICH has advised my Department that the restoration of the full £170k for this year will not lead to the reversal of the service reduction made public this week.The NICH and NI Hospice parent body will need to develop longer-term solutions, to which I am sure Government will want to be supportive.Given the current absence of a budget for next year, and the well-documented financial uncertainties and challenges for Government, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.I am also very conscious that other hospice organisations, and many other health and care providers, are also grappling with growing budget pressures and will be seeking further statutory finance.I am grateful for the support and personal interest of the First Minister and deputy First Minister regarding NICH’s position. I am also aware of the fact that other NI Departments have provided financial support to NICH in the past.I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of (a) the current number of nurses being trained annually; and (b) attrition rates within nursing; and (ii) what steps his Department will be taking to retain and increase the number of nurses.
answer_text: (a) I remain committed to investing in the workforce of the future. My Department has committed sustained investment in pre-registration nursing and midwifery training over recent years, reaching an all-time annual intake high of 1335 places, across local universities, in 2022/23 (inclusive of the 300 New Decade New Approach uplift). Whilst commissioning was limited to 1025 pre-registration training places in academic year 2023/2024, this was in part due to budgetary constraints facing the Department.  (b) Current workforce data provided from the NMC register for Northern Ireland indicates that the retention rate is steady. The number of leavers since April 2023 equates to approximately 1.5 percent of the register, which is the same as five years ago.I acknowledge the staffing pressures faced across the HSC system. I will shortly be considering proposals from my officials for education and training commissioning for 2024/25 across the range of pre-registration healthcare programmes in the context of the funding made available to my Department from the Northern Ireland Executive.In addition, the Nursing and Midwifery Retention Report was published in March 2022, outlining a series of recommendations to support nurses and midwives to remain in their posts, and my officials will continue to work with employers to ensure that those recommendations are implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the impact the absence of a violence against women strategy is having upon the prevalence of violence against women and girls; and (ii) whether she will introduce a violence against women strategy in this mandate.
answer_text: Violence against women and girls is unacceptable and must not be tolerated in Northern Ireland.  I agree that a Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy is essential and that is why I brought a proposal to the Executive in the last mandate that the Executive Office (TEO) take this issue forward on a cross-Departmental basis. I, therefore, welcome the ongoing work TEO is leading to develop one; I understand that work is well advanced.  I am clear that cross-Departmental co-operation and support will be required to deliver this strategy effectively.  My Department has a strong interest in this work and my officials will continue to work with TEO to support the development and delivery of this strategy and to ensure that there is appropriate alignment with the cross-Departmental strategic response to domestic and sexual abuse, which is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health, with inputs from the Department of Education, the Department for Communities and TEO.  Work is well advanced to develop a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategy which I hope to publish jointly with my ministerial colleague, the Minister for Health, later this year.  This DSA Strategy will take forward a range of work to protect and support victims and to address abusive behaviours which will benefit all victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including women and girls who are disproportionately affected by these types of abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he is taking (i) to improve the financial sustainability of Councils; and (ii) to decrease the rates burden on households and businesses.
answer_text: Councils are separate legal entities and are responsible for their own budgets and district rates. If any further funding becomes available for the current financial year I will consider the funding for councils in the context of my Department’s overall budget priorities.My Department’s budget for 2024/25 has not been agreed yet by the Executive and I am therefore unable to confirm any funding at this stage for councils for the next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current state of care in the community; and (ii) what steps he will take to (a) expand access to domiciliary care and care in the community; and (b) improve pay and conditions for carers.
answer_text: Domiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures, however, my Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service. It is clear that the current system is not sustainable, too many people are waiting for all, or part of a care package. There are a range of factors which can impact upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff. This is a significant challenge for the independent sector to increase capacity in the context of a competitive wider recruitment market. Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.(a) As you may be aware, following the publication of an Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation, in May 2023, my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF) to commence the delivery of reforms, with a number of workstreams established across a range of key issues. SCCF workstreams are developing a plan to grow capacity in domiciliary care across the region, a new model for domiciliary care and take forward key aspects of social care workforce development. There has been an additional investment of £5M allocated to the HSC Trusts for 2023/2024 (recurrently) to specifically target independent sector provision of care packages for the over 65 population. In addition, Trusts have been allocated recurrent funding of £697,000 to establish an early review team. The Early Review Team is being piloted currently across all five HSC Trusts and is designed to bring some of the previously allocated domiciliary care hours back into the system by ensuring reassessment of need within two to eight weeks of hospital discharge.My Department is working with Digital Health and Care NI (DHCNI)  regarding introduction of a digital system to manage statutory domiciliary care services and improve efficiencies of allocation of domiciliary care hours.We are also working with the NI Innovation Lab (DoF) to assist in the consideration and design of a future delivery model for domiciliary care focusing on cost effectiveness, sustainability and user-centred design, with the aim of moving from time and task based approach, to an outcomes focused delivery model.(b) I recognise that improving pay and conditions for domiciliary care workers in the statutory and independent sectors has to be a priority. Establishing a fair, standardised system for pay, terms and conditions is complex in a mixed economy of care system. However. it is also clear that improved pay, and terms and conditions for our healthcare workers are key components to future reform of the system. An immediate priority on my return to this Department is to secure pay settlements for healthcare staff in the statutory sector and work is actively continuing in that area.Whilst my Department has no say in the rates that independent

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many parking fines have been issued by his Department over the past 10 years, broken down by town and city.
answer_text: Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued for On-street, Off-street and Bus Lane contraventions by year and by town/city for the last 5-years can be found at the following link https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-dataAs per my Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule, older PCN data is not retained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the progress of the Casement Park project; and, (ii) whether the project programme will facilitate the Ulster GAA Senior Football Final being played there in 2027.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park is a flagship project for the Executive. UEFA’s requirement is for construction to be completed by June 2027. Officials in my Department and wider tournament stakeholders are working to an integrated project programme that can meet this requirement.  This includes procurement of a new contractor for the main construction works using a UK Government Framework.I understand that the Ulster GAA football final you refer to is due to be played in May 2027. The project programme that is being developed does not facilitate the playing of a major final in this timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the commuter rail service schedule from Newry to (a) Belfast, and (b) Dublin; (ii) what assessment has been made of the harmonisation of the frequency and schedule of trains departing from (a) Portadown to Belfast; and (b) Newry to Belfast for morning commuters; and (iii) whether he has plans to enhance rail scheduling from Newry to Dublin in order to improve the morning commute.
answer_text: NI Railways and Irish Rail currently provide 8 return services per day (Monday to Saturday) from Newry to Belfast and 9 return services per day (Monday to Saturday) from Newry to Dublin. There are also two morning commuter services to Belfast, departing Newry at 06:35 hours and 07:30 hours.  Both trains arrive in Belfast at 07:33 and 08:33 respectively.  Regretfully passenger numbers on these two services are very low.  To harmonise the entire Portadown corridor with services from Newry would require additional fleet provision, crews, and would incur significant additional costs. NI Railways and Irish Rail are currently reviewing the schedule between Belfast and Dublin with a view to increasing services in due course, subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the rollout of flood compensation in Newry, Mourne and Down; and, (ii) when additional funding will be made available for impacted business owners.
answer_text: Some immediate support was provided before Christmas, but that was not sufficient to deal with the full impact of the flooding for the businesses that had been affected.Upon taking up post, I pressed my officials to look at how we could respond quickly to meeting the gaps in the support provided to businesses impacted by the flooding.  A package of additional support was announced on 21st February.  This included an enhanced flood scheme which provides support of up to £100k to flooded businesses and two hardship schemes.  The schemes are currently open to applications on NMDDC website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the decision to withdraw emergency general surgery from Daisy Hill Hospital without a Minister in post (ii) whether he plans to reverse the decision.
answer_text: I have assessed the decision taken by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust to permanently consolidate emergency general surgery services at Craigavon Area Hospital.In the absence of a functioning Executive, a senior officer of a NI Department can take decisions, ordinarily taken by a Minister, in accordance with the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation Etc) Act 2022 and having regard to the associated Guidance published by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland on 16 December 2022.I am assured of the Permanent Secretary and Department’s review of the Trust’s decision is in line with the 2023 Guidance Change or Withdrawal of Services - Guidance on Roles and Responsibilities and that the decision taken was compliant with the Northern Ireland Act and cognisant of the associated Guidance. I have no plans to reverse the decision taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of what action can be taken with respect to primary care services that would (i) alleviate pressures on hospital emergency departments and (ii) ensure the safety of both patients and staff.
answer_text: Effective working between primary and secondary care is vitally important to ensure pressures across both services can be managed. In October 2020, my Department published the No More Silos Action Plan which set out 10 key actions to ensure that urgent and emergency care services across primary and secondary care can be maintained and improved in an environment that is safe for patients and for staff. The work that commenced under No More Silos is now being taken forward as part of the implementation of the Urgent and Emergency Care Review. This has seen the introduction of a range of urgent care services across all Health and Social Care Trusts over the last three years.  This collaborative work has resulted in all 5 Health and Social Care Trust areas now having a local Phone First Service that schedules patients, where appropriate, to same day appointments across a range of urgent care services.  This includes Urgent Care Centres and Rapid Access Clinics, as an alternative to attending our overcrowded Emergency Departments.Whilst GPs currently provide a level of urgent services within their practices, what is clear, is that demand is outstripping capacity across our Health & Social Care system.  The newly commissioned urgent care services provide a safe and timely access point for both patients and our GPs via referral, and utilisation of these local Phone First numbers.This work will continue to ensure greater access into urgent care as a safe alternative to attending Emergency Departments.  It will include planning for the implementation of a regional HSC 111 service, and the further integration of legacy Primary Care Out of Hours services into a reformed Integrated Urgent Care service for Health and Social Care.I have also commissioned NHS England to deliver a ‘Getting It Right First Time’ (GIRFT) review of emergency medicine for Northern Ireland to provide us with new metrics and benchmarks to inform how best to manage the demand for emergency care.  The GIRFT team has produced a report with several regional recommendations, which I hope to publish shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures he is taking to address disparities in the numbers of GPs relative to other physicians.
answer_text: Throughout my tenure as Minister for Health, I have recognised that even before having to cope with the strain of a sustained pandemic situation, GP-led services could not continue to meet rising demand and expectations without urgent action to address the pressures facing the service.  My Department is implementing a range of measures to support primary care workforce provision.  It is continuing to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places with a Working Group established to explore issues relating to the GP workforce to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst its work is still ongoing, the Working Group’s recommendation for an immediate increase of 10 additional GP training places as an interim measure for the 2022/23 academic year was accepted, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the work of the Working Group and subject to the availability of funding.On 26 September, I announced a package of £5.5million funding to support General Practice in Northern Ireland.  This includes targeted help to parts of the service that are most under pressure by investing £1m in a new Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to help attract GPs in hard-to-recruit areas.  It also boosts the support team for GP practices who are experiencing difficulty by an additional £680k.  In addition, it also includes investment of £3m to support practices across Northern Ireland through the winter period, giving them the capacity to provide additional in-hours sessions to help meet heightened demand and up to £800k additional investment in Out of Hours services.  In October 2022, I also announced further steps to support GP recruitment and retention by streamlining processes.  Under the new process being brought forward, GPs who qualified in the Republic of Ireland in the last two years will be exempt from sitting the knowledge test given the similarities between GP training in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.   This is a pragmatic step which removes an unnecessary barrier to qualified GPs practising in Northern Ireland.  GPs who have trained in Australia, Canada, New Zealand and South Africa are now able to use a streamlined process, the Certificate of Eligibility for GP Registration (CEGPR SP) if certain conditions around their training and professional experience have been met.  This will make it easier for GPs who have practised in these countries to work in Northern Ireland, potentially making it easier for doctors who left Northern Ireland to pursue their professional development abroad to now return home.My Department has also established a GP Access Working Group which includes representatives from General Practice, service users and officials among its membership.  The Working Group’s remit is to exp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average GP to patient ratio (i) in Northern Ireland; and (ii) in each GP Federation area.
answer_text: A General Medical Statistics annual statistical report is published by Business Services Organisation (BSO); the most recent report was published on 28th June 2022 and can be accessed at: General Medical Services Statistics (hscni.net) .  This report sets out a range of statistics in respect of the provision of General Medical Services in Northern Ireland.(i) the BSO report for 21/22 advises that the Northern Ireland average is 70.2 GPs per 100,000 registered patients (or 1 GP for every 1,425 registered patients).   It should be noted that the report highlights that this analysis does not take account of varying levels of full and part-time working by GPs across areas.(ii) the BSO report captures the number of GP practices per 100,000 population.  The report indicates that, at a GP Federation level, North Belfast has the highest number of GP Practices per 100,000 population (20.3), closely followed by Antrim Ballymena (19.5) and East Belfast (19.3).  A breakdown across each individual GP Federation is available in table 1.5c of the Annexes to the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his assessment of the consequences on the broader health service of the rising number of vacancies in GP practices.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services to their patients.  As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for identifying and recruiting the number of GPs and other staff required to deliver those services. I wish to assure you that my Department continues to work closely with GP representatives and other stakeholders to support the recruitment and retention of GPs.  In that regard, I would refer you to my response to AQW 5249/22-27 which sets out the actions that I and my Department have taken to support GP numbers and also the important work my officials will continue to take forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to address (i) burnout; and (ii) mental health challenges, being experienced by rural GPs as a consequence of the overloading of work, due to high patient to GP ratios.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to address GP vacancies at Mullaghbawn Surgery.
answer_text: Mullaghbawn Surgery is a 2 GP Partnership with 5369 patients as at July 2022.  One of the GP Partners is retiring from the practice on 28th October 2022.  GP Practices are independent contractors and the responsibility for recruitment of all staff including GP Partners and Salaried Doctors is that of the practice.  However, SPPG have been in close contact with the practice over the last 6 – 9 months, and providing support to ensure that practice remains stable and is in a position to recruit a new GP Partner. In September, I announced a £5.5m package of General Medical Services (GMS) part of which is to help recruit GPs in traditionally difficult to recruit areas like Mullaghbawn and provide additional funding to the GP Crisis and Improvement team that provides support to practices experiencing difficulty.I am also taking action to streamline the processes for GPs qualified in the Republic of Ireland, Australia, Canada, New Zealand and South Africa to take up roles in Northern Ireland.  Under current rules, GPs who have trained outside the UK, or who have not practised in the UK in the past two years, are required to undertake an initial knowledge assessment exam as part of the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme before they can practise in Northern Ireland.Under the new process GPs who qualified in the Republic of Ireland in the last two years will be exempt from sitting the exam.  Which would also be of benefit to the GP Practice in Mullaghbawn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the health implications of underage drinking; and (ii) what action his Department has taken in relation to educating young people about the dangers of underage drinking.
answer_text: (i)	Consumption of alcohol among our young people is an ongoing issue of concern, which has potential to impact on a young person’s immediate health and wellbeing, safety, academic achievement, and longer term health and wellbeing as an adult.The most recent Young Persons Behaviour and Attitudes Survey (YPBAS) in 2019 shows that since 2000, there has been a decline in both the proportion of young people ever having drank alcohol and the proportion of those who drink, and that report having been drunk. Updated YPBAS figures for 2022 will be available later this year.(ii)	While the YPBAS figures are encouraging, we must not take them for granted, and that is why the Public Health Agency continues to fund a range of services which directly/indirectly inform and educate young people about the risks and dangers involved with alcohol and/or other drug use. PHA has also produced the You, Your Child & Alcohol booklet to offer parents advice and guidance on how to discuss alcohol with their child, and encourage them to think about how their own relationship with alcohol can influence their children. My Department will continue to monitor any emerging trends in this area. In line with Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, all of the relevant evidence, advice and guidance in respect of alcohol consumption will be updated as required by the PHA to provide adults, parents and young people with the information they need to make informed choices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of the benefits of expanding sex education in schools; and (ii) what plans her Department has to expand the provision of sex education in schools.
answer_text: These are historical questions from 2022 and the answer below is therefore based on the current position in respect of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE).Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age.  The Department requires all grant-aided schools to have an RSE policy and taught programme that has been developed based on the ethos of their school and in consultation with staff, governors, parents and pupils.The legal minimum content to be taught by schools is set out in the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 as high-level Areas of Learning.In June 2023, the Secretary of State of Northern Ireland, made and laid Regulations under his powers in Section 9 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019.  The Regulations make changes to the Northern Ireland Curriculum to add teaching about sexual and reproductive health and rights covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion as part of Learning for Life and Work at key stages 3 and 4.The Regulations also require the Department to issue guidance by 1 January 2024 to ensure that:pupils receive education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion; andthe education received by pupils is age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate.My Department therefore issued guidance to schools on 1 January 2024 on the changes to the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 (“the 2007 Order”) made by the Secretary of State. The guidance outlines the circumstances in which a parent can request to have a pupil opted out from the new elements of Relationships and Sexuality Education, ‘age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion”.In line with the 2007 Order, the guidance does not prescribe the detailed content of what that schools are to teach.  The Board of Governors and the Principal have statutory responsibility to deliver the minimum curriculum content but they also have autonomy over when, how and who is involved in curriculum delivery, aligned with the ethos of the school. This includes the change to the curriculum made by the Secretary of State with regards to RSE.The guidance also sets out the circumstances in which, at the request of a parent, a pupil may be excused from the new elements of the Learning for Life and Work curriculum introduced by the Secretary of State.In April 2023, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) published a thematic evaluation of the preventative curriculum (of which RSE is a part).  The Department has established a task and finish group that is considering currently how best to respond to the ETI findings.  Further guidance on RSE in schools will be considered by my Department to ensure that the wide-ranging needs of our yo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the increase in underage vaping, that has begun to include the use of vapes laced with the drug spice; and (ii) what action has been taken to educate young people about the dangers of vaping.
answer_text: Recent reports from other parts of the UK indicate a rise in youth vaping. The Department will continue to monitor this position and will consider appropriate mechanisms to reduce youth uptake as part of our strategic work on tobacco control. The Public Health Agency (PHA) continues to work with community and voluntary organisations, local Councils, the PSNI, the education sector, and Health & Social Care partners to raise awareness of issues around young people using the synthetic drug ‘spice’. My Department introduced regulations on 01 February prohibiting the sale, and proxy purchase, of nicotine inhaling products to under 18s. These regulations are enforced by district council enforcement officers and apply to all types of nicotine containing e-cigarettes, including disposable devices. PHA commissions a smoking prevention uptake programme for children and young people called 'Smoke Busters' which includes information on the harms associated with vaping. The programme is facilitated by Cancer Focus NI and is delivered in primary schools across NI, targeting 6,000 Year 6 and 7 pupils each year.  In addition, the PHA are exploring (in liaison with CCEA) opportunities for a post primary education programme regarding vaping, as well as the development of age-appropriate resources warning of the harms associated with vaping, that can be accessed by schools, community youth groups and parents/carers.

This information is available on NI Direct’s Cost of Living webpage: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/cost-living. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will write to the Irish Cricket Team to congratulate them on their success in the T20 World Cup.
answer_text: This Question was initially asked to my predecessor, however, I was delighted when Ireland finished runner-up in its group stage of the T20 World Cup in 2022.  Due to the passage of time, rather than writing to congratulate the team on their success in 2022, I will write to wish them well for the T20 World Cup due to take place in the West Indies and the United States this June and for their historic first Test Match to be hosted in Northern Ireland, against Zimbabwe at Stormont this coming July.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will meet with me and constituency colleagues in relation to the future of Kingsmill Primary School.
answer_text: Development Proposal (DP) 688 - Kingsmills Primary School will discontinue with effect from 31 August 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter – was published by the Education Authority (initially) on 28 September 2022, and republished on 9 November 2022.On 24 April 2023 a decision was taken to approve the proposal and the school closed with effect from 31 August 2023.The evidence informing the decision, and the decision, are accessible on the Department’s website at the following link: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/education/DP%20688%20-%20Kingsmills%20Primary%20School%20-%20Submission.pdfAs this matter has now concluded, a meeting is not required.  However, I am of course content to engage with the member on area planning or educational matters generally.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total level of funding available for the aesthetic enhancement of town and city centre areas; and (ii) how much her Department has spent on aesthetic enhancement of town and city centres, broken down by local government area, in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what the decisions are that require Executive approval.
answer_text: The decisions that I am required to bring to the Executive for approval are those that in line with section 2.4 of the Ministerial Code of Conduct:(i)  cut across the responsibilities of two or more Ministers;(ii) require agreement on prioritisation;(iii) require the adoption of a common position;(iv) have implications for the Programme for Government;(v)  are significant or controversial and is clearly outside the scope of the agreed programme referred to in paragraph 20 of Strand One of the Agreement;(vi)  are significant or controversial and which has been determined by the First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly to be a matter that should be considered by the Executive Committee; or(vii)  relate to a proposal to make a determination, designation or scheme for the provision of financial assistance under the Financial Assistance Act (Northern Ireland) 2009 shall be brought to the attention of the Executive Committee by the responsible Minister to be considered by the Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Emergency General Surgery will be reinstated at Daisy Hill Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health whether the decision to remove Emergency General Surgery from Daisy Hill Hospital has been rural proofed.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the Southern Health and Social Care Trust last advertised General Surgery vacancies at Daisy Hill Hospital; (ii) how many applications were recieved for these vacancies; and (iii) how many appointments were made from those applications.
To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the efforts to return Emergency General Surgery to Daisy Hill Hospital.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the benefits of engagement with social enterprises that promote utilisation of surplus renewable energy.
answer_text: My department is supporting the growth and development of social enterprises through its social enterprise work programme. I am also pursuing decarbonisation as one of my economic priorities.I am developing a social enterprise action plan that will enable specific actions to be taken that will support Social Enterprises that are working on decarbonisation to take advantage of future trading and investment opportunities that will occur as I develop the green economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits of (i) a review of the Northern Ireland legal aid system, including the role of the Taxing Master; and (ii) the introduction of a zero tolerance approach for both fraud and attempted fraud.
answer_text: (i) Departmental officials are developing a methodology for a review of criminal and civil legal aid to deliver against the Department’s statutory obligations to review legal aid remuneration that will look at issues including transparent and accountable systems for providing fair remuneration to providers of legal services, and ensuring value for public money.  Detailed plans will be brought to the Assembly in due course.The Taxing Master is a member of the Judiciary and has both a payment assessment role and an appellant role in respect to some Legal Aid cases.  It is not the intention of the Department to review the appellant role of the Taxing Master.The Department has commenced work to review the payment assessment role of the Taxing Master. A public consultation “Legal Aid Taxation Reform and Statutory Charge Registration” was published on 16 March 2022 and included proposed legislative amendments to the Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 as amended. There were two responses to the consultation, one from a member of the public and one from the Law Society. The proposed amendments are the first in a series of primary and secondary legislative changes needed to move the payment assessment function into the Department.  In support of this, the Department plan for the proposed amendment to the Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 to be laid before the Assembly in the second year of the current mandate.  This is subject to the formation of an Executive and the wishes of the new Justice Minister. While there is no functioning Assembly, there is no legislative vehicle available to bring about the necessary changes required to amend the role of the Taxing Master.(ii) The Legal Services Agency already has a zero tolerance to fraud and attempted fraud. The Agency investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud and attempted fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, or attempted fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner is prosecuted. This approach is set out in the Agency’s Internal Fraud Policy which is publicly available. The Agency also promotes an anti-fraud culture, with a focus to prevent, detect and investigate fraud which includes working with other Agencies and law enforcement and regulatory bodies. This work is led by a dedicated Counter Fraud Unit with accredited fraud investigators. The Agency has delivered fraud awareness training to its staff, has a e-learning package for new staff and has prescribed annual mandatory fraud awareness training for all staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will publish a list of legal practitioners, both individuals and firms, who have been sanctioned or prosecuted for legal aid fraud in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner is prosecuted. In the last 5 years no practitioners have been sanctioned for legal aid fraud. One individual was prosecuted and acquitted of legal aid fraud. As that individual was acquitted no name has been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the benefits of engagement with social enterprises that promote utilisation of surplus renewable energy.
answer_text: I believe that engaging with social enterprises is essential on all matters relating to Green Growth and not just surplus renewable energy. In developing the Green Growth Strategy, my Department has engaged and continues to engage with many key stakeholders from across a range of public, private and the voluntary & community sectors, including social enterprises. To achieve Net Zero, we are all going to have to work better together and social enterprises have an important role to play in that, particularly on aspects such as the green and circular economy. On the 3 October 2022 I attended the launch of the NIE Networks Investment Plan consultation. Investment in the electricity network is key to enable the full utilisation of current and future renewable energy generation. I would encourage all renewable energy producers including social enterprises to make their views known to NIE Networks before their consultation process closes on 7th November 2022. Further details can be found on the NIE Networks website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: The Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner or applicant is prosecuted. In the last 5 years:(i) 	three cases of suspected fraud involving legal practitioners and twenty-one relating to applicants have been identified by the Agency. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what initiatives she has launched to promote more long-term involvement and participation in sport for girls and women.
answer_text: In March 2022, my Department launched ‘Active Living’ the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland. One of the key priorities of the strategy is increasing lifelong participation in sport and physical activity, with a focus on under-represented groups, including women and girls.Under the strategic Theme of “Promoting Participation, Inclusion and Community Engagement”, a key goal is to ensure that sport and physical activity is inclusive, safe, diverse, shared, fun and offers equality of opportunity to participate for all. This includes a continued focus on achieving greater participation amongst females in their chosen sport and/or physical activity.  As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Increasing and sustaining women and girls participation in Sport is fundamental to Sport NI’s work. Sport NI’s £8m Sports Systems Investment Governing Bodies Programmes funded by the National Lottery are supporting sporting governing bodies to increase diversity and inclusion in sport, many of whom are focussing on growing female participation. In FY 2023-24 three District Councils were awarded Sport NI funding totalling £75,000 for programmes aimed at promoting participation in sport for women and girls. Sport NI collaborative research published in FY 2023/24 in both the Children's Sport Participation and Physical Activity Study and Kids / Young Persons Life & Times Studies was monitoring the participation gap between boys and girls. Also, in partnership with the Home Country Sports Councils, Sport NI launched Moving to Inclusion (https://movingtoinclusion.co.uk/) an Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) hub.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly. Making contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what initiatives have been rolled out to support schools to provide extra foundation stage learning for children that were in lockdown throughout their foundation years; and (ii) how is the Department assessing the ability of these children to catch up, particularly children with special educational needs.
answer_text: (i) In an effort to address the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic, the Engage Programme was launched in September 2020 using Covid-19 funding provided by DoF.   The programme continued in 2021/22 and 2022/23 and over £57m in funding was allocated to educational settings across the three years of the programme.The Engage programme aimed to limit any long-term adverse impact of the COVID-19 pandemic by supporting pupils’ learning and engagement through provision of high quality one-to-one, small group or team teaching support in every school/setting in Northern Ireland.  Due to budget pressures, the Engage programme came to an end on 31 March 2023.(ii) The Programme provided flexibility and autonomy to allow settings to assess the diverse needs of their individual pupils in light of the pandemic and provide bespoke interventions to best meet those needs in their unique setting environment.  This included the needs of pupils with Special Educational Needs (SEN).  One of the key strengths of the programme was that it was shaped by those delivering it on the ground, which allowed for the delivery of a range of approaches, for example, to increase the confidence and motivation of pupils. Separate to the Engage Programme, settings can also capture, evaluate and report on how they are meeting the needs of their most disadvantaged pupils through the Targeting Social Need (TSN) Planner, which is available to Nursery, Primary and Post Primary schools.  This online app helps settings to assess their pupils’ needs each year, evaluate the impact of their interventions, and plan effectively for the following year. This enables settings to address the needs of those pupils who need the most support, including pupils with Special Educational Needs.  Information provided by schools in the TSN planner 2022/23 are available online at: TSN Planner Report 2022 23.PDF (education-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the economic benefits of the Cross-Border Projects awarded funding under Shared Island Local Authority Development Funding Scheme announced by the Taoiseach on 29 September 2022.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures she has implemented to ensure school staffing levels are retained, particularly in relation to special education teaching staff, as schools manage challenging budgets and increasing energy costs.
answer_text: The Department’s 2023-24 opening Resource budget was £66.4m (2.5%) less than the budget set by the Secretary of State in 2022-23. Despite this budgetary outcome, in 2023-24 the Department protected and minimised the impact of budgetary pressures on its core services.The Department prioritised maintaining the current year’s Age Weighted Pupils Unit (AWPU) cash value at 2022-23 levels.  In addition, the Education Authority’s (EA) block grant for special educational needs has been retained at 2022-23 levels.  For details of school staffing levels, including but not limited to special education teaching staff, please refer to Teacher workforce statistics in grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland, 2022/23, available on the Department’s website.  The data shows that there has been no impact on teacher numbers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what initiatives she plans to introduce to promote more long-term involvement and participation in sport for girls and women.
answer_text: One of the key priorities of ‘Active Living’ the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland is increasing lifelong participation in sport and physical activity with a focus on under-represented groups, including women and girls.One of the goals under the strategic Theme of “Promoting Participation, Inclusion and Community Engagement” is to ensure that sport and physical activity is inclusive, safe, diverse, shared, fun and offers equality of opportunity to participate for all. This includes a focus on achieving greater participation amongst females in their chosen sport and/or physical activity.As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Increasing and sustaining women and girls participation in Sport is fundamental to Sport NI’s work. Sport NI’s will be planning the next phase of its Sports Systems Investment Governing Bodies Programmes funded by the National Lottery which supports sporting governing bodies to increase diversity and inclusion in sport, with a strong focus on growing female participation. Sport NI has developed a Women in Sport Action Plan and will be implementing the actions in 2024-25. In FY2024-25, Sport NI will also be planning further investments both in District Councils, and for sporting and community organisations via a match-funding programme. It is intended that both these programmes will contain elements that encourage the participation of women and girls in sport.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly. Making contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the last time Bessbrook, The Model Village, was promoted as a tourist destination.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 4011/22-27, to detail (i) how many people are currently on a housing waiting list; and (ii) how long it will it take to clear that waiting list, based on current housing output.
answer_text: From 2022, social housing waiting list and allocations data held by the Housing Executive form part of the Department for Communities Housing Bulletin, which is published quarterly as a National Statistic at the following web address https://osr.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/national-statistics/.  In line with National Statistics protocols, users should refer to the most recently published data: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-housing-bulletin-april-june-2022.The rate of progress in reducing numbers of households in housing stress will be determined by long term policy and investment.I have initiated a comprehensive programme of interventions which will deliver real change for those in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what funding is available for the exploration of sustainable hydro-electric power generating initiatives.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive, budgets have not been agreed for the 2022-23 financial year or beyond. The lack of certainty around capital funding makes it difficult for the Department to plan strategically for capital schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Armagh East and West Link Roads; and (ii) what action he has taken to progress these projects.
answer_text: The future progression of both Armagh schemes is being considered in the context of the forthcoming Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan, which will outline investment priorities across our strategic transport networks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to what extent development projects are being prevented from progressing as a result of inadequate sewerage infrastructure; and (ii) what he is doing to address this problem.
answer_text: i)   The progression of development projects in relation to sewerage infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.(ii)     I and the Communities Minister, Deirdre Hargey MLA, held a joint housing and wastewater infrastructure workshop with key stakeholders on 6 September 2022, to seek solutions to wastewater issues constraining housing supply. A follow-up workshop is planned in November 2022, to identify potential actions to help facilitate housing growth.  The Communities Minister and I also met with the Chartered Institute of Housing to discuss the impact that wastewater issues are having on the delivery of housing growth and to highlight potential solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Blue Green Infrastructure projects in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives. There are a number of ongoing projects and schemes that have been completed and are currently planned for Newry. Phase 1 of the Newry Loops scheme has now been completed. The scheme runs from Damolly Roundabout to McCanns Corner Roundabout.My Department is currently taking forward the design for the second phase of Newry Loops from McCanns Corner Roundabout to Derrybeg Lane in Newry, including the provision of a Toucan Crossing giving access to the train station.  Delivery of this Phase 2 work within the 2022/23 financial year will be dependent on contractor availability.There are no schemes in the Armagh area on a current programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of red-flagged cancer patients currently awaiting treatment; and (ii) how many of those patients have been waiting for more than 12 months, broken down by Trust.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail her assessment of the benefits of creating a cost of living taskforce.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to list the dental practices in Newry and Armagh that currently have spaces available for NHS patients.
answer_text: The BSO have records of which General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) have a contract with the Department of Health to provide NHS treatment, and in which practices they work. The BSO does not have information as to the capacity of each GDP’s register.Patients can find a list of all General Dental Practices with at least one practitioner contracted to provide NHS treatment at http://online.hscni.net/other-hsc-organisations/hsc-service-finder. It can be used to search for dental practices in the patient’s area, sorted by those closest to their address and then extending further afield. A contact number for each dental practice is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail her assessment of the advantages of waiving the recovery of benefit overpayments, in the context of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: A waiver of a benefit overpayment can be granted in exceptional circumstances. If approved the individual is not required to repay the outstanding balance. Information on the Department’s waiver process and other support available to individuals experiencing financial hardship is available on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/overpayments-benefits-and-financial-support	The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people may be experiencing because of the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3655/22-27, for his assessment of the benefits of his Department holding information on the number patients having developed secondary health complications as a result of awaiting treatment for their initial presentation.
answer_text: Whilst individual patient records would contain information on secondary health complications, analysis of this would take a major data linkages exercise and resource investment. The wider issue is around waiting times in Northern Ireland, which were on the brink of crisis before Covid-19 and the pandemic has simply made our unacceptable waiting times even worse. The Elective Care Framework, which I published in June 2021, is a five-year plan with firm, timebound proposals to tackle the backlog of patients waiting for assessment and treatment across Northern Ireland. It is not a quick fix, but a number of proposals are already underway, enabling many patients to access treatment across a range of specialties.However, without an agreed budget in place there remains uncertainty around the implementation of this Framework. The ability to plan strategically for the future continues to be hugely limited by the lack of a financial settlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 3812/22-27, to detail whether replacement funding from the Shared Prosperity Fund will match the funding provided by European Social Fund.
answer_text: I draw the Member’s attention to the recent letter signed by the 5 main party leaders to Simon Clarke, Secretary of State for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities, urging “the UK Government to honour its manifesto commitment to replace the European Social Fund in its entirety and to provide certainty and security for the many people who depend on these programmes by fully replacing the European Social Fund."The Finance Minister is leading on negotiation of replacement funding with the UK Government Department responsible for the Shared Prosperity Fund. However, I continue to strongly advocate for funding to be directed to key priority areas which includes activity currently funded through the European Social Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3358/22-27, when will the Southern Trust provide the information to the Department.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3357/22-27, to detail where the £5.5m package of support will be allocated, broken down by Trust area.
answer_text: I can confirm that all GP practices across Northern Ireland are eligible to apply for funding from the £950k which I have made available in respect of the new Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme.  GP Practices would have to satisfy a range of criteria in order to be eligible for a payment under this scheme, details of which will be provided to all GP practices in the coming weeks.My Department has also boosted funding for the GP Crisis Response and Improvement Team by an additional £680k full year effect.  This funding will enable the team to recruit additional GPs, thereby enabling a greater amount of GP practices to receive additional clinical input in light of the difficult circumstances they may find themselves in.A further £3m has been made available and will be used for the Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service (NILES), Managing Winter Pressures.  Under the Winter Pressures initiative, this funding will be distributed to all practices on a capitation basis where they have contracted to provide the service.    The package of support measures also includes £850k additional funding for GP Out of Hours (OOHs) winter pressures.  All five GP OOHs Providers have been invited to submit Winter Pressures Proposals to detail how they could utilise funding to increase clinical capacity to cover winter demand.  Subject to approval by my Department, the additional winter pressures funding will be allocated to the five Providers.
