question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the benefits of access to EMDR therapy as a treatment mechanism for trauma; and (ii) the extent to which EMDR therapy is available in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Finance what steps he is taking to address the £661 million overspend in the Executive’s finances.
answer_text: During the course of the year my department has issued a range of advice in relation to managing departmental budgets and overspend risks.  A full list of these are provided in my response to AQW 4638/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of expanding provision for Adult Day Care Services in the Southern Trust.
answer_text: The provision of day services and recreational activities is primarily delivered through buildings-based day care. Adult day care provides supported people with opportunities to participate in a wide range of stimulating and enjoyable activities in a safe, social setting. This may also support unpaid carers to have a break from their caring responsibilities and support the health and wellbeing of both the unpaid carer and person using the service. The approach and form of support will vary according to the needs of the supported person and the carer.In addition, Trusts commission community-based day opportunities which will engage adults with a learning disability in areas such as paid supported employment; accredited further and higher education; volunteering; social enterprise activity and opportunities to meet and make friends and use local leisure and recreational facilities. In January 2022, I approved the Pathway to remobilise adult day services, which has been monitored by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) since May 2022. Data on the current levels of provision, disaggregated by Trust can be found at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  During the reporting period, the number of people receiving a partial or no service has decreased. SPPG continue to engage in performance meetings with all Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to full day care, respite/short break service remobilisation and to agree mitigating actions to support continued progress towards full remobilisation to pre-pandemic capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the (i) Southern Relief Road; and (ii) Narrow Water Bridge Project.
answer_text: Work is progressing on the development of the Newry Southern Relief Road.  The project has been included within the Belfast Region City Deal and officials are working closely with City Deal officials to advance the project.  Work is continuing to develop the design of the road and prepare the draft Statutory Orders, along with an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Report in advance of public consultation.  The current proposal is based on a fixed bridge that will facilitate access for vessels under 12 metres; however, I am also aware of the support locally for a lifting bridge over the Newry Ship Canal.  My officials are considering a range of factors relating to a possible opening bridge and are working to resolve this issue and progress the EIA Report.My officials are working closely with the Irish government, Louth County Council and other stakeholders to deliver the Narrow Water Bridge project as quickly as possible. The required Bridge Orders were made by my Department on 24 February 2022 and became operational on 26 March 2022.  Louth County Council has recently commenced the initial stage of a procurement process to appoint a contractor to construct the bridge. Subject to the necessary Irish Government approvals, it is currently envisaged that the tender documents will issue later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much funding Newry Gateway Club has received from her Department and its arms length bodies, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not have a funding relationship with Newry Gateway Club.  No funding has been provided in any of the last five years by my Department or it’s arm’s length bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the criteria by which his Department determines eligibility for the installation of traffic calming and speed control measures at locations on the roads network.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to road safety engineering, including the provision of traffic calming measures and speed control measures, is outlined in a number of documents that are publically available to view at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/road-safety-engineering.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of visitors to each tourist attraction, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NISRA’s Visitor Attraction Survey provides the official source of information on the number of visits to visitor attractions each year. The survey reports on the number of visits rather than visitors as a person may visit an attraction more than once.The number of visits to visitor attractions are available on the NISRA website at - https://www.nisra.gov.uk/publications/2021-northern-ireland-visitor-attraction-survey-publications. Tables 12-20 show the number of visits by visitor attraction category for 2013-2021. Within each table columns H-L show the number of visits to the attractions for the last five years for which the information is available (i.e. 2017-2021).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how her Department works collaboratively with other Departments, agencies and stakeholders to ensure that emerging needs are met within the Community and Voluntary Sector in relation to (i) disability services; (ii) youth services; and (iii) adult day care.
answer_text: Departmental collaboration in action includes extensive co-design of the Disability and Work Strategy, the establishment of Labour Market Partnerships within each Local Government District, and delivery of programmes such as Workable (NI), Access to Work (NI), Condition Management Programme, and JobStart. These structures provide important engagement and collaboration channels, and are supported through wider, distinct and regular engagements with the voluntary and community sector related to the range of funded labour market provision for those with barriers to work.In respect of the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF), my Department and Executive colleagues, led by the Department of Finance, continue to engage regularly with the UK Department for Levelling Up Housing & Communities, HMT and Devolved Governments regarding all funding streams under the Levelling Up agenda and continue to press Whitehall on plans for Post 2025 funding.My Department funds the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to commission the Supporting People Programme. The Programme grant funds the provision of housing related support services for vulnerable people, including people living with a disability and young people, to enable them to live as independently as possible in the community. Adult day care services are not funded through the Supporting People ProgrammeMy Department is represented at the Supporting People Strategic Advisory Board (SAB), which is a partnership of local housing, Health & Social Care, Probation, Department for Communities and Service Provider representation. The SAB meets quarterly and its purpose is to advise the Housing Executive Board of strategic funding priorities of the SP Programme and it also provides assurance that processes and practices are fair, transparent and equitable. In addition, my Department also facilitates a quarterly Joint Policy Forum, to support new and existing co-operation and engagement between my Department and Department of Health, Department of Justice and Housing Executive regarding the Supporting People Programme. The Joint Policy Forum aims to progress cross-cutting issues in terms of wider strategic policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the law in relation to hunting live animals with dogs on agricultural or common land, where permission has not been offered by the landowner.
answer_text: There are no specific regulations relating to the act of hunting live animals with dogs, unless the animals have Protected Species status.It is my understanding that unauthorised access on land would be classified as trespass and that this is a civil matter for the landowner to consider.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the benefits of a trifold tourism marketing strategy for the Newry Mourne and Down District, which promotes each of the District’s three Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty, in order to attract foreign and domestic tourists to the District.
answer_text: Tourism Northern Ireland and Tourism Ireland have undertaken significant research on what attracts both international and domestic visitors.  The beauty of our landscape is a key attraction for visitors, and the outdoors and the opportunity to connect with nature increasingly hold great appeal for our target markets. Tourism Northern Ireland’s remit is to promote all regions of Northern Ireland as a holiday and short break destination to the Northern Ireland (NI) and Republic of Ireland (ROI) markets. Much research has already been completed in the NI domestic market review and the recent ROI Market Review, on what is required to attract visitors from these markets, and the marketing strategy that should be adopted to maximise visitor revenue. This information may be found on www.tourismni.com.Tourism Northern Ireland’s marketing strategy recognises that a segmented approach in terms of motivators to travel is required to stimulate demand.Currently, Tourism Northern Ireland promotes products and experiences to these defined segments on a regional basis, either by county or regional area depending on the type of media used. Regional press advertorials for instance, have proven to be extremely effective at promoting a particular area, with a focus on the experiences, accommodation providers and attractions unique to that region.Tourism NI and Tourism Ireland already work closely with colleagues from Newry Mourne and Down District Council and would be delighted to meet with representatives from the area to discuss marketing opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what financial provision his Department has made for adult autism services, detailed by (i) assessment; (ii) diagnosis; (iii) post-intervention; and (iv) employment based support, broken down by Trust area.

To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for his assessment of media reports that the Irish Minister for Finance is to consider planning public finances in the context of Irish unity; and (ii) whether he has taken action to implement similar plans.
answer_text: I have written to the Irish Finance Minister welcoming his confirmation that the Irish Government will begin the process of planning financially for Irish unity. I have asked Minister Donohoe to contact my Department should he require fiscal data that could inform the broader discussion.Any decision to implement local public finance plans in the context of Irish unity would be significant and cross-cutting and would require Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the continued absence of an Executive will have on (i) patient care; (ii) waiting lists; and (iii) progress on the delivery of elective surgery hubs.
answer_text: Waiting times in Northern Ireland were on the brink of crisis before Covid-19 and the pandemic has simply made our unacceptable waiting times even worse. Despite the best efforts of our exhausted workforce, waiting lists in Northern Ireland continue to grow.In the absence of an Executive and an agreed budget, I continue to do the best I can to deliver high quality Health and Social Care services for the public with the resources I have available.The Day Procedure Centre (DPC) at Lagan Valley Hospital (LVH) supports a range of specialties from across the region to undertake the most urgent clinical cases. Approximately 4,600 patients have been treated at the Regional Day Procedure Centre at LVH, and approximately 9,000 patients have received endoscopy and Cysto/TP biopsy procedures at the centre. This is additional regional capacity to help reduce long waits.Omagh Hospital has also been set up as a Regional Day Procedure Centre. This will see seven regional theatre lists per week across urology and general surgery – two specialities with some of the longest waits across the HSC. So far 53 patients have been seen across 22 sessions. Going forward, this means an extra 1,750 patients across these specialities will be treated per year. In addition, 10 regional endoscopy sessions at Omagh Hospital will see an extra 3,000 patients per year.In line with the Review of General Surgery, and subject to Trust processes, the Mater Hospital becoming an elective overnight stay centre.  This will be a centre for intermediate complexity procedures requiring 0-3 days stay covering a range of specialties. The implementation of the Mater as an elective overnight stay centre is taking place on a phased basis, initially with day procedures in the first instance and then a move to procedures requiring an overnight stay. The first day procedure patients have been seen, and overnight stay patients are expected in November.In June 2021 I published the Elective Care Framework which sets out a five-year plan, with a dual focus on clearing the existing backlog of patients waiting for assessment and treatment across Northern Ireland and closing the gap between capacity and demand.As well as those mentioned above, there are a number of initiatives in the Framework that are already well underway:The introduction of mega clinics to maximise patient throughput In addition, the Duke of Connaught Orthopaedic Day Procedure Unit at Musgrave Park has been operational since April 2022. The next phase involves the development of Elective Overnight Stay Centres for patients requiring intermediate complexity surgery and a stay of 0-3 days.  The Mater was announced as the first centre in June 2022.This will be taken forward on a phased approach with a range of specialities, for example, general surgery, urology, gynaecology and ENT.In addition, allowing a private Independent Sector provider to use vacant theatres in the South West Acute Hospital in Enniskillen has already seen hundreds of p

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement his Department has had with the Department of Work and Pensions in relation to future funding for projects that have relied on the European Social Fund for their funding.
answer_text: The Department of Work and Pensions has no remit in terms of future funding provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether homes heated by wood pellet burners will receive financial support, in line with the £400 Energy Bills Support Scheme.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the benefits of stocking the Clanrye River with fish, in order to help rejuvenate the ecology of the river and its environment.
answer_text: The Clanrye River falls within the Carlingford catchment area and is under the remit of the Loughs Agency. 		Fish stocks are often affected by issues such as poor habitat, which stocking will not address. There are also issues associated with the use of hatchery-reared fish to consider, which could negatively affect wild Atlantic salmon stocks. I am advised that Loughs Agency invests significant resources in evidence-based habitat improvement and restoration to enhance freshwater habitats and improve survival of wild stocks. This approach is ecosystem-based, encompasses essential processes, functions and interactions of the Clanrye river catchment and its flora and fauna, the geology and relatively low gradient are natural limiting factors to the theoretical maximum number of fish that can be sustained by the river.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what protections will his Department put in place to ensure the safeguarding of the environment and endangered species, after the proposed removal of the EU standards and regulations that remained following the UK's withdrawal from the European Union.
answer_text: Following EU Exit, environmental protections and standards have been preserved in domestic legislation. The UK Government has recently introduced the Retained EU Law (REUL) (Revocation and Reform) Bill which proposes that retained EU Law is repealed, amended or extended from 31 December 2023. My officials are considering the potential impact of the REUL Bill along with colleagues across the NICS and in other UK jurisdictions.  It is my intention going forward that Northern Ireland continues to have a clear statutory basis for protecting the environment and human health as has been the case throughout the changes resulting from the UK’s withdrawal from the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the benefits of the devolution of tax powers.
answer_text: The Fiscal Commission’s landmark report clearly identifies the potential benefits of greater local control over taxation. Increased fiscal powers would allow locally elected and accountable ministers to set taxes more in line with local needs and circumstances to deliver on our social and economic priorities. It would give us more options to grow the economy, raise revenue for public services, and encourage the transition to zero carbon.The Scottish and Welsh Governments both have more control over their tax affairs and it is now time for a mature discussion on the potential benefits of greater control over taxation here, which is why I launched a consultation on the devolution of fiscal powers on the 4th October (www.finance-ni.gov.uk/consultations/devolution-fiscal-powers). It will however be for an incoming Finance Minister to bring recommendations on the Commission’s report to a restored Executive, including clearly setting out the opportunities and benefits of tax devolution, as well as how the risks identified by the Commission can be managed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many interactive or electronic whiteboards in school classrooms are malfunctioning or broken and are awaiting replacement, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on the repair status of electronic whiteboards in school classrooms. The Education Authority have advised that they do not provide electronic whiteboards to schools and have therefore no information on the numbers in schools or the number of malfunctioning devices.  The Education Authority have a contract for schools to procure electronic whiteboards should schools wish to order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how many prosecutions there have been of those involved in illegal animal baiting in NI, in each of the last 10 years; and (ii) what measures her Department has taken to discourage this illegal activity.
answer_text: There is no specific offence for animal baiting.  Such offences may be prosecuted under the Protection of Badgers Act 1992 or under the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985.  Information has been provided in relation to the number of prosecutions and convictions at courts, for cases involving offences under this legislation that relate to killing or injuring wild animals, or interfering with their shelter, that were dealt with in the period 2012 to 2021, the most recent ten years for which information is available. Defendants prosecuted or convicted at courts for offences related to killing or injuring wild animals, 2012 – 2021YearProsecutionsConvictions2012*02013002014*02015**20160020173*2018002019*02020*02021*0Total11*Note:1. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI or other bodies. 2. Figures relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 3. Figures relate to defendants who were prosecuted or convicted at court, or received an out of court diversion for at least one offence under the legislation specified..4. Offences included are relevant offences under Article 10 of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 and under the Protection of Badgers Act 1992. * means a figure less than three has been treated under rules of disclosure.As well as the convictions highlighted in the table, seven individuals were dealt with by way of out of court diversionary disposal in the time period reported.  It is possible offences involving animal baiting were prosecuted under other animal welfare legislation during the period.  However, Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain detail on the circumstances in which an offence occurred.  Therefore, it is not possible to identify whether prosecutions or convictions at courts for offences under other animal welfare legislation related specifically to animal baiting.Policy responsibility for the welfare of animals sits with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA).  Policy responsibility for any measures to discourage animal baiting, including the creation of any new criminal offences and penalties under the Welfare of Animals (Northern Ireland) Act 2011 for the purposes of improving animal welfare would be a policy matter for that Department

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how long it will take to clear the housing waiting list, based on the current output.
answer_text: The rate of progress in reducing numbers of households in housing stress will be determined by long term policy and investment.I have initiated a comprehensive programme of interventions which will deliver real change for those in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits of embedding the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child into NI law.
answer_text: The UK ratified the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC) in December 1991.  Each devolved administration, including Northern Ireland, takes forward the UNCRC as appropriate to its own requirements.  Further detailed analysis would be required, however, to assess the implications of embedding the UNCRC into law in Northern Ireland.  Any change to the law would also likely require Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the announcements made by An Taoiseach on the cross border infrastructure projects to be funded by the Shared Island Local Authority Development Funding Scheme.
answer_text: I welcome the announcement of funding allocations from the Shared Island Fund to a range of projects in the areas of tourism, biodiversity, heritage, and climate action. I welcome the fact that the lead Local Authorities, from across the island, have worked closely together to identify cross-border projects that can be delivered in partnership and will ultimately create a more connected, sustainable and prosperous island.I am keen to see progress and to explore further, options for joint north/south co-operation

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children are still awaiting a decision on applications for school uniform grants, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The number of parents/guardians awaiting the outcome of their Free School Meal and Uniform Grant applications as at 11 October was 5,900. A breakdown of these applications by constituency is not able to be provided. The EA continues to work to a target of applications being processed within four weeks.  Following the opening of the EA’s Free School Meal application portal in early June, the EA undertook extensive communications via social media, the EA website, letters to schools and individual emails to parents/carers in receipt of FSM in 2021/22 academic year, encouraging them to apply before 31 July to ensure support would be in place for the start of the school year. Despite this publicity campaign, a significant number of applications were received after this date. Unfortunately this has resulted in some of these applications not being able to be verified ahead of the start of the school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children are still awaiting a decision on applications for free school meals, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The number of parents/guardians awaiting the outcome of their Free School Meal and Uniform Grant applications as at 11 October was 5,900. A breakdown of these applications by constituency is not able to be provided. The EA continues to work to a target of applications being processed within four weeks.  Following the opening of the EA’s Free School Meal application portal in early June, the EA undertook extensive communications via social media, the EA website, letters to schools and individual emails to parents/carers in receipt of FSM in 2021/22 academic year, encouraging them to apply before 31 July to ensure support would be in place for the start of the school year. Despite this publicity campaign, a significant number of applications were received after this date. Unfortunately this has resulted in some of these applications not being able to be verified ahead of the start of the school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of daytime opportunity places available for adults with learning disabilities in each Trust; (ii) the number of adults with learning disabilities who have daytime opportunity places in each Trust; and (iii) the number of adults with a learning disability on a waiting list to access daytime opportunity services in each Trust.
answer_text: (i)Day opportunities continue to be provided to adults with learning disabilities in each Trust. It should be noted that day opportunities information is not readily available and is often dependent on manual counting by Trusts. The information provided is based on the best available information currently. The Trusts have reported the following number of day opportunities are currently available in each Trust.NHSCT: 941BHSCT: 725SHSCT: 450 (statutory day opportunities only included in this figure)SEHSCT: 829WHSCT: 626(ii)The Trusts have reported the following number of adults with learning disabilities who have daytime opportunity places in each Trust. It should be noted this information is based on available data, and in relation to Trusts is largely based on information as at 31 March 2022.NHSCT: 627BHSCT: 501SHSCT: 327SEHSCT: 603WHSCT: 552(iii)The Trusts have reported that there are no adults with a learning disability on a waiting list to access daytime opportunity services within the NHSCT, BHSCT, SHSCT or SEHSCT. The WHSCT reported there are 9 individuals on a waiting list to access day opportunity services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29600/17-22, when the electricity upgrade to Daisy Hill Hospital is scheduled for completion to allow the installation of an MRI suite at the hospital.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 29600/17-22, the Trust is currently working with the Department of Health to address a number of queries on the recently submitted Outline Business Case for the Electrical Infrastructure upgrade up DHH site. The Trust intends to resubmit the OBC to DOH by 7th October 2022. Pending formal approval of the business case, the programme of works is expected to commence within 22/23 year and conclude 2028/2029. The DHH site will accommodate an MRI scanner when the programme of works concludes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the benefits to communities, schools and businesses in the local area, of making adjustments to the perimeter wall of Newry Courthouse to reflect the modern society in which the courthouse is located.
answer_text: Physical security infrastructure at all Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) venues is deployed on the basis of regular security assessments, undertaken by NICTS, with the support of other agencies and expertise where necessary.Where a security assessment recommends adjustment, upgrade or removal of any item of physical infrastructure, the associated monetary and non-monetary benefits are assessed at that time.There are currently no plans to make adjustments to the perimeter wall at Newry Courthouse, a key component of the physical infrastructure of that building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions he has had with the Minister for the Economy on funding projects which are currently funded by the European Social Fund, beyond March 2023.
answer_text: Since the formation of the Executive in January 2020, I have discussed the loss of EU Funding and provided updates to the Executive when it was sitting, these meetings included the Minister for Economy. In the ongoing absence of an Executive, I have continued to push the British Government for full replacement spending power and an appropriate role for local Departments in the funding which underpins the British Government Levelling Up Agenda, particularly the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF).I have kept all my Ministerial colleagues updated on these issues and I have recently written to provide them with an update on the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). Unfortunately the absence of the Executive has limited my ability to take action in relation to this unacceptable and underfunded approach by the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment he has made of the benefits of a safety campaign to raise awareness among other road users and pedestrians of potential risks from quiet electric and hybrid vehicles.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to promote road safety and this is a responsibility that I take very seriously. My Department does this through a range of road safety educational activities, engineering and other initiatives. My Department has a rolling programme of educational activities, including the road safety public information campaigns.  Regrettably over 95% of road traffic collisions where someone dies or is seriously injured are due to human error. Road safety campaigns are focused on where they will be most effective, where the need is greatest and where most lives can be saved by targeting the most at risk on our roads and challenging those attitudes and behaviours that lead to road deaths and serious injuries.The PSNI data also persistently shows that the main causation factors for road deaths and serious injuries here are careless/inattention, drink/drug driving, driving at excessive speed   and failure to wear a seatbelt in the event of a collision. The data also shows those groups who are over-represented in road casualties and who are more vulnerable road users, such as young males, older road users and children.As such, the Department’s public information campaign portfolio comprises many campaigns which address the main road user attitudes and behaviours which lead to death and serious injuries here, as well as reflecting those most at risk on our roads.Although there is not a specific campaign addressing the risks of quiet electric and hybrid vehicles, the messages contained in the current road safety campaigns convey general road safety benefits to road users. The road safety social media channels on Facebook ShareTheRoadToZero and Twitter roadtozero also allows for flexible messaging and would be useful in this case to provide wider advice for paying extra attention.My officials will continue to monitor road safety problems and emerging trends and will continue to challenge them through the DfI road safety campaign programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he has had with the Communities Minister in relation to the ending of the European Social Fund in 2023 and the impact that the loss of this funding stream will have on young people with disabilities across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) will replace funds previously delivered by the EU from April 2023. The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on work to secure replacement funding on behalf of the NI Executive, liaising with the Department for Levelling Up, Housing & Communities (DLUHC) on how the SPF will be delivered in Northern Ireland. In discussions with DoF and DLUHC, DfE officials, working collaboratively with Department for Communities (DfC) colleagues have been advancing the position that SPF implementation in Northern Ireland must include provision for inclusion and employment programmes for disabled people, essential to addressing the employment gap and economic inactivity rate in Northern Ireland.I am committed to continue working collaboratively with the sector and colleagues in other departments to ensure that provision currently offered, and under development, meets the needs of those who want to develop their skills and employability potential.In addition, DfC with its policy responsibility for employability, is developing a new Disability Employment Strategy which will align to and deliver upon an employment chapter within the new overarching Executive Disability Strategy - a commitment under New Decade, New Approach. Colleagues in DfC have advised that, as a core theme, the Disability Employment Strategy will seek to set a long-term commitment to reducing the disability employment gap, and key in achieving this ambition will be accessible skills and employability provision for those with disabilities and health conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he has had with the Health Minister in relation to the ending of the European Social Fund in 2023 and the impact that the loss of this funding stream will have on young people with disabilities across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) will replace funds previously delivered by the EU from April 2023. The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on work to secure replacement funding on behalf of the NI Executive, liaising with the Department for Levelling Up, Housing & Communities (DLUHC) on how the SPF will be delivered in Northern Ireland. In discussions with DoF and DLUHC, DfE officials, working collaboratively with colleagues across Executive departments, including Health, have been advancing the position that SPF implementation in Northern Ireland must include provision for inclusion and employment programmes for disabled people, essential to addressing the employment gap and economic inactivity rate in Northern Ireland.I am committed to continue working collaboratively with the sector and colleagues in other departments to ensure that provision currently offered, and under development, meets the needs of those who want to develop their skills and employability potential.In addition, DfC with its policy responsibility for employability, is developing a new Disability Employment Strategy which will align to and deliver upon an employment chapter within the new overarching Executive Disability Strategy - a commitment under New Decade, New Approach. Colleagues in DfC have advised that, as a core theme, the Disability Employment Strategy will seek to set a long-term commitment to reducing the disability employment gap, and key in achieving this ambition will be accessible skills and employability provision for those with disabilities and health conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) how many projects his Department are funding through the European Social Fund; (ii) how much money his Department provides each year to projects funded through the European Social Fund; (iii) what plans he has to ensure projects are funded when their European Social Fund funding ends in March 2023; and (iv) whether he has asked officials to prepare plans to help fund the projects going forward.
answer_text: Sixty-six Projects are currently being funded through Priorities 1, 2 & 3 of the European Social Fund (ESF) Programme 2014-20.The amount paid to projects over the period of the Programme (i.e. from 1 April 2014) varies each year.  On average, under Priorities 1&2, the Department has provided c£6m per annum as a fixed proportion of Programme costs. However, in 2020-21 the Department was able to use over £21m of one off available national funds to replace EU monies in that year. This enabled us to extend financial support into this current financial year, when the EU funding would otherwise have been exhausted. The Apprenticeship Programme, Priority 3 is also part-financed under the ESF Programme and on average, the Department has contributed c£9m per annum to this Programme. The Department of Finance (DoF) leads the cross-departmental Future Policy & Finance Workstream that works with the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) on funding streams associated with the Levelling Up agenda, including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). The Shared Prosperity Fund SPF will be the primary source of funding for activities currently funded by ESF once funding ends in March 2023. My Department will provide whatever assistance it can to our Voluntary & Community sector partners in presenting their business cases and advocating for continued funding & support.My officials continue to work with DoF & DLUHC to try to ensure that replacement EU funding streams are directed to priority areas, in particular those aspects that fall within our policy remit, primarily young people not in education, employment and training (NEETs). We continue to press for clarity regarding delivery in NI and DLUHC’s proposed role for the NI Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of young people with disabilities who benefit from the European Social Fund services that are supported by his Department and the Trusts; and (ii) whether he has raised the possible loss of services with his Executive colleagues.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of the withdrawal of European Social Fund support on mental health charities.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact that the proposed PSNI recruitment freeze will have on the ability of the PSNI to address domestic violence.
answer_text: The prioritisation of Policing resources is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  My Department and I continue to work very closely with PSNI colleagues in addressing domestic abuse.  Other work being taken forward by my Department in this area, in conjunction with police, includes the domestic abuse offence, a stalking offence, a range of sexual offences under the 2022 Justice Act and implementation of recommendations from the Gillen review.  By working together with PSNI we can protect and support all those across our society affected by a wide range of abusive behaviours.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many patients have developed secondary health complications as a result of awaiting treatment for their initial presentation; and (ii) how many of those patients are children, broken down by Trust area for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The requested information is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the impact of the withdrawal of European Social Fund support on the mental health charities that also recieve funding from her Department.
answer_text: My Department does not provide direct funding to mental health charities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of Departments and Trusts collaborating effectively to deliver a coordinated response to the mental health crisis.
answer_text: My Department is committed to continued collaboration and communication with Trusts across the region to ensure the work of Mental Health services is joined up and in line with the high level ambition to ensure good mental health across Northern Ireland.  Implementation of the 10 year Mental Health Strategy is ongoing. Collaboration between the Department and HSC Trusts, alongside other key stakeholders, is crucial for successful implementation. There are several actions in the Strategy which aim to deliver a co-ordinated response to the mental health crisis.  For example, Action 31 outlines the need for a Regional Mental Health Service, operating across the five Trusts, with regional professional leadership that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development. Once implemented, it will ensure that patients are offered and have access to the same services and treatments, no matter where they live. The Mental Health Workforce Review, Action 32 of the Strategy, is also a key enabler to identify the future workforce to deliver a fit for purpose clinical model providing equitable access across Northern Ireland.  Insight from the Trusts has been vital to ensure there is a consistent delivery model across all Trusts which aligns to the vision of the Regional Mental Health Service.The Strategy also highlights that, to ensure we have the right services to meet the needs of the population, we must have data to measure outcomes.  Action 34 therefore seeks to develop a regional Outcomes Framework in collaboration with service users and professionals, to underpin and drive service delivery. This work has been informed by a wide range of stakeholders, including Trusts.Looking to the future, the Strategy states that the Trusts and the Department will seek to create a more innovative and research focussed culture.  This will allow us to shape research to include our specific needs, such as the legacy of the Troubles, the use of technology, particularly given the experience during the Covid-19 pandemic, and develop a person-centric tailored approach to treatments and services ensures higher quality outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the harm caused by the sewage recently deposited at Killough Beach in County Down; and (ii) what measures he will take to prevent occurrences of sewage deposits across the North.
answer_text: (i)  Potential pollution incidents should be reported to the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), on their 24 hour Incident Hotline on 0800 807 060 or e-mail emergency-pollution@daera-ni.gov.uk. Once alerted to a potential pollution incident, NIEA will commence an investigation to determine the reason for the incident and damage caused.  You should therefore, direct your question to NIEA regarding this past incident at   emergency-pollution@daera-ni.gov.uk.(ii)  The management of wastewater infrastructure across the North is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of the discontinuation in funding for the role of social prescribers working in rural community and voluntary sectors.
answer_text: The Social Prescribing Rural Enhanced project commenced in October 2019. Through my Departments Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Programme a contract is in place to continue to fund the project until 30 June 2023. This commitment will bring my Department’s total contribution to delivering Social Prescribing in rural areas to £888k.A Regional Social Prescribing Development Board, which includes DAERA representatives, has been established to take a co-design and co-production approach to working with a wide range of stakeholders, including across government departments to provide regional leadership and co-ordination of effort in relation to Social Prescribing. This work will continue in the months ahead and I welcome the fact that further discussions are planned with the intended outcome of identifying a sustainable solution for Social Prescribing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many domiciliary care packages are currently active within the Southern Trust; (ii) how many new care packages have been recommended within the last six months; and (iii) how many of those recommended have been (a) provided; and (b) rejected, broken down by provider.
answer_text: Unfortunately, Southern Trust did not respond within the allocated time and have not responded to date with this information.I can confirm that in a comparison of domiciliary care hours delivered in Southern Trust between November 2021 and May 2022, there has been an increase of 9,267.25 domiciliary care hours delivered.There remain ongoing challenges with providing domiciliary care packages due to difficulties with recruitment and retention of domiciliary care staff with particular challenges in rural areas due to high fuel costs. Providers operate rolling recruitment exercises in an attempt to fill workforce vacancies. The Health Trusts have processes in place to risk assess and prioritise those awaiting a package of care.  The local HSC Trusts are promoting Direct Payments as an alternative to traditional packages of care and we would encourage as many people as possible in the community to avail of this option. I will continue to support the domiciliary care sector in their attempts to meet the current demand for packages of care in the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of a discontinuation in funding for the role of social prescribers working in the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: A number of social prescribing services are funded from within the HSC and whilst most do not currently have recurrent funding, none of these schemes are at immediate threat of discontinuation.Any discontinuation of funding secured through wider partnerships has the potential to impact on the services provided in relation to social prescribing. My Department will continue to work with stakeholders, including other Departments and local councils, through the Regional Social Prescribing Development Board to support the development of social prescribing services in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total excess deaths in each of the last five years, compared to (i) the Republic of Ireland; (ii) England; (iii) Scotland; and (iv) Wales.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. The information requested on annual excess deaths is provided in table 1 which covers the last 5 full calendar years. Deaths registrations data for England and Wales, Scotland and the Republic of Ireland are produced by the Office for National Statistics (ONS), the National Records of Scotland (NRS), and the Central Statistics Office (CSO) respectively. Excess deaths are defined as the number of deaths in the reference year, above or below the average of the previous five years i.e. what can be regarded as ‘normal’ deaths. In 2021, due to, among other things, the varying impact of Covid-19 across jurisdictions in 2020, NISRA used a different 5-year average (2016-2020) to the ONS and NRS (2015-2019); this should be borne in mind when making country comparisons for 2021.  Data on excess deaths in the Republic of Ireland are not available in the format requested. However, the Central Statistics Office have signposted 2 relevant sources. One is an experimental statistic on excess deaths using death notifications as opposed to registrations (https://www.cso.ie/en/releasesandpublications/fb/b-mpds/measuringmortalityusingpublicdatasources2019-2021/); and the other is data on excess mortality as presented by Eurostat for EU countries (https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/statistics-explained/index.php?title=Excess_mortality_-_statistics).   Table 1:  Excess Deaths, 2017-2021YearExcess Deaths NIExcess Deaths England and WalesExcess Deaths  Scotland201796020,8032,246201859022,3552,27720192354,6471,12220201,87575,8456,33320211,40654,2575,827As outlined above, different definitions for excess deaths across jurisdictions may be used due to variations in the population, local circumstances and other factors EuroMOMO (European monitoring of excess mortality for public health action) is a tool designed specifically for making comparisons across countries in Europe and over time. The link below shows a series of interactive graphs and maps comparing excess mortality across countries using z-scores. The z-score is the number of standard deviations a given data point lies above or below the mean and the graphs show the variation in deaths in each country from 2016 to present.https://www.euromomo.eu/graphs-and-maps#z-scores-by-country

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to list the GP practices currently recruiting for General Practitioner posts, broken down by Trust area; and (ii) for his assessment of the benefits of offering enhanced pay awards for GPs to work in rural practices.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services to their patients.  As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for identifying and recruiting the number of GPs and other staff required to deliver those services.  The information requested at point (i) above is therefore not available.Turning to point (ii), I am very aware of the pressures facing primary care services across Northern Ireland.  I have therefore recently announced a £5.5m package of support measures.  The package provides targeted help to parts of the service that are most under pressure by investing £1m in a new Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to help attract GPs in hard-to-recruit areas and boosting the support team for GP practices who are experiencing difficulty by an additional £680k.  The package also includes investment of £3m to support practices across Northern Ireland through the winter period, giving them the capacity to provide additional in-hours sessions to help meet heightened demand through the winter; up to £800k additional investment in Out of Hours services; and a commitment to progress work to address the issue of GP Indemnity.  My Department has also been working to address the longer term, root cause issues.  This includes increasing the number of GP training places in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 and expanding the MDT model with an additional £1.5m investment this year. Overall, my assessment is that it is essential that we continue to utilise a range of measures – short, medium and long term – to ensure that our primary care services, including those provided in rural areas, are sustainable, now and into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the laws governing the construction of billboards and the erection of illegal signage, including party political advertising.
answer_text: Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements, including party political advertising, to DfI property, such as lamp posts. This does not apply to an advertisement relating specifically to a pending parliamentary election or to a pending election to the Assembly or a district council.One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise with other key stakeholders to seek to provide a solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the volume of refuse generated by Amazon packaging; and (ii) the cost to local councils for managing the disposal of this refuse, broken down by council area.
answer_text: Amazon are a registered producer under the current producer responsibility scheme regulated by NIEA (https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/producer-responsibilities-regarding-packaging-waste) and as such will pay some costs towards management of packaging waste. However, this data cannot currently be broken down by brand in each council area.As part of the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for packaging measures I am bringing forward companies, such as Amazon, will pay full net costs of managing household packaging waste which they place on the market. Improved data on packaging and packaging waste will be introduced which will require sellers to report the packaging placed on the market in each UK nation enabling data to be collected and reported for Northern Ireland. Work is also progressing on the new Digital Waste Tracking system which will provide a more detailed insight into the waste chain than is currently available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the extent to which Blockchain technology is utilized within the health service network to (i) protect the integrity of patient data; and (ii) to identify trends and patterns in diagnostics and treatment.
answer_text: Blockchain technology is not currently used in Northern Ireland Health and Social Care organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of the installation of an MRI suite at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: The benefits of installing an MRI suite at Daisy Hill Hospital would include facilitating MRI scanning of inpatients and Emergency Department patients onsite so that they would not require transfer to Craigavon Area Hospital for their scans; and provision of additional capacity for outpatient scanning, thereby reducing the overall waiting list for MRI.My response to AQW 29600/17-22 refers to the electrical upgrade required at Daisy Hill Hospital before installation of an MRI can be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact that digital maps are having on the protection of local road and place names.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any assessment of the impact that digital maps are having on the protection of local road and place names. The protection of road and place names is a matter for local councils, communities and the digital map providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the reconstruction of the retaining wall and the road at the Ballynaclosha Road will be completed.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to finalise the design of the replacement structure and obtain all of the necessary technical approvals. It is anticipated that construction work will commence in late Autumn 2022 with completion envisaged during Spring 2023.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the excess death figures published by NISRA; and (ii) what actions his Department is taking to address those figures.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) introduced the Good Smartphone Activated Medics (GoodSAM) system across Northern Ireland in 2019. The initial phase involved the activation of NIAS staff who volunteered outside their contractual hours.The scheme was subsequently extended to include volunteer staff from across Health and Social Care and partner agencies.In 2023, the scheme was further expanded to allow members of the public, who have been trained in CPR and registered as GoodSAM volunteers to be alerted to cardiac arrest calls in their vicinity.The GoodSAM application is a publicly available mobile phone app that enables emergency services to alert appropriately trained members of the public to medical emergencies nearby, potentially allowing CPR to commence ahead of the arrival of the first ambulance resource.NIAS also utilises a system known as ‘The Circuit’ to advise 999 callers and bystanders of the location of publicly accessible defibrillators in their area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many young adults with complex needs are resident in care homes, broken down by constituency for each of the last five years

To ask the Minister of Health how many young adults transitioning from children’s services have been placed in elderly care homes for respite care in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This information is not routinely collected by my Department; therefore, the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts have provided the following responses.In the previous five years, no young adults, who have transitioned from Children’s Disability Services, have been placed in elderly care homes for short break provision. However, the Southern HSC Trust has confirmed that some commissioned short break providers have beds registered for a range of needs, and in the previous five years, ten young adults who transitioned from children’s to adult services have received a short break in a care home that is also registered for care of the elderly. Due to the small number, this cannot be broken down by Constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being taken to ensure that there are sufficient numbers of qualified taxi drivers.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North.  This role requires ensuring that those drivers that hold a taxi licence are suitably qualified, and this has been done since 2014 by means of a test.  Since the introduction of the taxi driver theory test, the Department has made a number of changes to the minimum required standard and the associated learning material.  Officials are currently reviewing the available training material, with a view to helping candidates better prepare for the taxi driver theory test.  The training manual, “All you need to know to be a Taxi Driver”, which is available on NIDirect, is under review to create a more streamlined, user-friendly version. As a result a revision aid has been developed, based on the information contained in the training manual.  This revision aid will soon be available through NIDirect and will help to support candidates in acquiring the knowledge needed for the taxi theory test.  Regarding the number of licenced drivers choosing to drive taxis, the industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation and there are many factors that contribute to the current shortage of taxi drivers.  As we recover from the Covid pandemic potential drivers will compare the industry to other driving jobs, including those that do not require them to be responsible for managing running costs, or that involve unsociable hours. All of these factors influence the attractiveness of the industry, both to existing licenced taxi drivers that are choosing not to drive for the industry at present, as well as potential new recruits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road resurfacing works scheduled to take place in the Newry and Armagh constituency in the 2022-23 financial year.
answer_text: The Department does not hold or collate information on a constituency basis but rather it is held on a Council or DfI Section Office area basis.  The Newry and Armagh electoral area falls partly within the Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Council and Newry, Mourne and Down Council areas.Resurfacing programmes for both of these Council areas are now, as is the case for other areas, being finalised following confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the respective Department’s Council Reports, which will in due course be presented to the relevant Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.I am pleased to be able to confirm that after some delays due to legal challenges, an asphalt resurfacing contract covering the Newry area was awarded in June 2022 and delivery of much needed resurfacing works has commenced on site. In addition, my officials are hopeful that a resurfacing contract covering the Armagh area will be awarded shortly. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the scheduled works to upgrade and enhance road safety on the Crieve Road in Newry will commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when access to GP's, including face-to-face consultations, will return to pre-March 2020 levels.
answer_text: The issue of access to GP’s and General Practice services has had significant profile throughout the COVID-19 pandemic.Primary Care’s response to the COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the implementation of new and innovative ways of working to enable General Practice, along with all other areas within the Health and Social Care system, to react quickly and adapt flexibly to the demands and challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic.  Despite the demands and capacity limits that the pandemic has created, GP’s have maintained all of the core elements of General Practice services.GP Practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate.  The General Practice telephone first consultation allows patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems. The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  The GP decides, based on their assessment, if a face to face appointment is appropriate, if the patient can be managed over the telephone or if they should be signposted or directed to other appropriate services.   This approach helps the GP to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of people who seek clinical support and advice from our Primary Care Practitioners.It is important to emphasise that the Department of Health is aware of the need to support GP’s in their continuing efforts to meet the growing demands for Primary Care services.  The Department has made significant investment over the past two years to enable GP’s to improve and manage their systems efficiently.My Department will continue to provide investment and support to GP Practices in order to help navigate these difficult times and ensure that patients can access their GP Practice when they need to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of general surgeons that have been recruited in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of (i) the Narrow Water Bridge Project; and (ii) the Southern Relief Road in Newry.
answer_text: My officials are working closely with the Irish government, Louth County Council and other stakeholders to deliver the Narrow Water Bridge project as quickly as possible. The required Bridge Orders were made by my Department on 24 February 2022 and became operational on 26 March 2022. Louth County Council, as the lead project partner, has recently published a Prior Information Notice to provide advanced notice of the tender process to appoint a contractor. Subject to completion of ongoing development work and necessary approvals, it is currently envisaged that the tender process will commence later this year.Work is also continuing on the development of the Newry Southern Relief Road.  The project has been included within the Belfast Region City Deal that was signed in December 2021 and officials are working closely with City Deal officials to advance the project.  Work is progressing to develop the design of the road and prepare the draft Statutory Orders, along with an Environmental Impact Assessment Report in advance of public consultation.  The current proposal is based on a fixed bridge that will facilitate access for vessels under 12 metres; however, I am also aware of the support locally for a lifting bridge over the Newry Ship Canal and officials are considering a range of factors relating to a possible opening bridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what engagement she has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to an above-inflation pay increase for teachers and non-teaching staff.
answer_text: I previously met with the Minister of Finance in January 2022 and secured his agreement to fund a two-year pay deal for teachers from 1 September 2021 which was made to, and subsequently rejected by, the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council in February 2022.  I have not had any further engagement to date with the Minister of Finance in relation to an above-inflation pay increase for teachers.The majority of non-teaching staff employed in the education sector are contractually linked to the national NJC award.  Department of Education officials are not involved in NJC pay negotiations and have no influence over the amount or timing of pay awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of visits he has made to the Newry and Armagh constituency in the last 12 months; (ii) the names of the firms he visited in the constituency; and (iii) the dates on which those visits were made.
answer_text: All external meetings I have attended up to the 31 March 2022 are published publicly online at www.opendatani.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the current status of the South Armagh Policing Review.
answer_text: As the Member is aware, the delivery of local policing services, including the South Armagh Policing Review, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Policing Board.You will, therefore, wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the waiting times for life saving cardiac surgery in each Health and Social Care Trust for each of the last five years; and (ii) how many operations have been carried out in each Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure who holds responsibility for the removal of flags from street lights.
answer_text: Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution. The display of a flag of a proscribed terrorist organisation is a criminal offence that may be capable of investigation by the PSNI, and I would encourage anyone with information in regards the display of such items to contact the PSNI.I note the commitments to respect for different cultures and identities contained in the New Decade New Approach. Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of staff morale within the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the largest employing authority in Northern Ireland with over 60,000 posts across a range of professions and disciplines - ranging from teachers, teaching assistants, cleaning, estates and transport staff, bus drivers, school patrol officers, social workers, youth workers, and educational psychologists - alongside staff working in its regional offices in greater Belfast, Ballymena, Omagh and Armagh.The recent Landscape Review of the EA, by Baker Tilly Mooney Moore highlighted that the EA has undertaken a significant amount of work to transition from its legacy organisations, in addition to providing support for its staff through initiatives such as the Health and Wellbeing Strategy.The Report also acknowledges that more must be done across a broad range of areas including better workforce planning and data to inform the staffing / roles / levels required to deliver services and improved communications. Delivering improvements in people resourcing and capability will in turn lead to improved education services for children and young people.  As I outlined in my Statement of 22 June 2022, my Department is fully committed to working with the EA to progress the issues identified, and to shape the continuing improvement of the organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment on what safeguards should be introduced to ensure the safety and privacy of users of community social media applications such as Nextdoor.
answer_text: I would encourage anyone using the internet to make sure their device, information and privacy are as secure as possible; there is a range of helpful advice and precautions to keep people safe online available at the ‘Staying Safe Online’ section of NI Direct website: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/staying-safe-online; and on the PSNI website at: https://www.psni.police.uk/advice_information/internet-safety/While, as Justice Minister, I do not carry out assessments of the specific safeguards that should be introduced to ensure the safety and privacy of users of community or other social media applications, I fully support the need for safeguards for online use and want to ensure that the internet can be used positively and safely by anyone.  It is clear that a regulatory and legal framework is required to help protect the privacy and safety and users.  In that respect, I broadly support the UK Government’s Online Safety Bill, currently making its way through Parliament.  Telecommunications legislation (including the regulation of social media platforms) is a reserved matter, and the Bill will introduce a new regime to regulate online content on platforms across the UK.  The Bill imposes duties of care on providers of internet services to address illegal and harmful content online to keep their users safe.  An independent regulator, Ofcom, will oversee the regulatory framework which when enacted, will end the reliance on self-regulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 323/22-27, (i) what impact the current Consultant Psychiatry vacancy at Daisy Hill Hospital will have on the mental health outcomes for patients in the Daisy Hill service area; and (ii) what mitigations are in place to safeguard the mental health outcomes of patients in the Daisy Hill service area until the vacancy is filled.
answer_text: The Southern Trust has put appropriate cover arrangements in place to support individuals known to the mental health team in Newry while the recruitment process for consultant psychiatrists progresses.  Referral triage responsibilities are currently being provided by the three consultant psychiatrists already in post, and medical advice for individuals known to the Newry Mental Health Team is currently being provided by the consultant psychiatrist within the Home Treatment Crisis Response Team.  The Trust considers that these arrangements are appropriate in the short term to safeguard the mental health outcomes of patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many complaints have been received by her Department from Personal Independence Payment claimants on the Capita assessment process in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on complaints broken down by constituency.The total number of complaints received in the Department in relation to the services provided by Capita in each of the last five years, are provided in the table below. From March 2017 until the end of February 2022 (the date of the last published statistics), over 221,000 cases have been referred to Capita for assessment.Year20172018201920202021 ComplaintsReceived861661615653

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when AQW 261/22-27 will be answered.
answer_text: AQW 261/22-27 was answered on 19th July 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1023/22-27, whether his Department will meet the required capital and development costs to secure a twin diagnostic suite at Daisy Hill Hospital, including the provision of an MRI and second permanent CT scanner.
answer_text: In advance of an agreed budget my Department continues to evaluate what can be funded within the draft 2022-25 budget settlement and will keep this position under review.  I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care including the provision of a twin diagnostic suite at Daisy Hill Hospital, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. However, my ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Once this project becomes affordable within my Department’s capital budget, the Southern Trust will be invited to submit a business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current provision of daycare for young adults, aged 18 to 30 years, in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) what plans there are for expanded provision of daycare for young adults.
answer_text: (i)In February 2022, I wrote to the Health & Social Care Trusts in regards to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care.Information regarding overall adult day care provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. Information regarding current day care provision for adults with a learning disability was recently published on the DOH website. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThe latest position reported by Trusts regarding day care provision for adults with a physical and sensory disability, (including young adults aged 18-30), indicates day care provision is increasing; with attendance levels at least 70% in comparison to pre-pandemic levels.  There is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus. A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. (ii)While it is clear that the Pathway has been an enabler to remobilise services, there remains further adjustments to public health guidance  before services can be fully restored to pre-pandemic levels.  The PHA have issued revised guidance to Trusts on 28 June 2022 to clarify the position on physical distancing and management of close contacts. It is anticipated that this will better enable Trusts to balance Covid-19 risks with wider risks to the wellbeing of service users and carers, therefore enabling the restoration of adult day care and transport. In terms of Short Breaks, my officials are considering a change in policy for admissions to care homes for my consideration in the coming weeks. Aligned to this process, the Department has requested performance and capacity data across all Trusts, which will be published on the Department’s website.  I have clearly outlined that this data will be collected and published on a regular basis to monitor remobilisation against rebuild timeframes. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing a periodic communication to all MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the traffic calming and road safety measures for the Crieve Road in Newry.
answer_text: I am advised the Department has previously assessed Crieve Road for the possible provision of safety improvements, including traffic calming and a footway link, however it is considered that a scheme at this location is a lower priority than other schemes across the Council area competing for inclusion within our work programmes.  The scheme will remain under consideration for potential inclusion in a future works programme.  Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.
