question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement she has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to PSNI funding shortfalls following reports this has impacted on the recruitment and training of police officers.
answer_text: Since taking up the role of Minister of Justice in January 2020, I have engaged with the Finance Minister on many occasions to ensure that the PSNI is properly resourced and I will continue to do so.The proposed draft Budget for 2022-25 is insufficient to increase police officer numbers to 7,100, and further progress to increase police officer numbers toward the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) target of 7500 will not be possible unless further funding is made available. I have raised concerns regarding the Police Budget with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues on multiple occasions since the publication of the proposed draft Budget 2022-25.While I do not have regular meetings exclusively with the Finance Minister, I did have bi-lateral meetings with him in November 2020 and November 2021 to discuss the draft 2021-22 budget and the proposed draft 2022-25 budget respectively, as part of a round of ministerial engagement. I have also written to him and my officials have actively engaged with DoF officials, including intensive discussions over recent weeks regarding this years contingency planning envelope.There have been a number of changes to the funding position for 2022-23 from the announcement of the Draft Budget, and there is now a significant amount of additional resource funding available for allocation in 2022-23. In the absence of an Executive, this funding cannot be allocated. The reality is there is no Executive or Assembly in place to approve a Budget.   It is imperative that we come back together as an Executive and work collectively to ensure that all available funds are effectively utilised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether she had advance knowledge that the Northern Ireland Office was not inviting the controlled or maintained sectors to its event on A More Confident and Inclusive Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether she has been in contact with the Secretary of State in relation to this matter.
answer_text: Neither I nor my officials in the Department of Education had any role in setting the agenda or identifying the speakers, facilitators or attendees for the Northern Ireland Office (NIO) Wilton Park conference “A More Confident and Inclusive Northern Ireland”.  This was presented as a provisional programme during the latter stages of preparing for the conference and I was not advised about who was or was not invited to attend.As Minister of Education for all children in all schools across all sectors in Northern Ireland, I considered that the provisional programme represented a missed opportunity to reflect the contribution of all our schools and the diversity of our education system here.  On this basis I wrote to the NIO expressing my view and asking them to review the programme before it was finalised. Unfortunately this did not happen.I have also written to the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland setting out my view about this missed opportunity.  I have invited him to join me in a programme of school visits here to demonstrate the role of schools in the controlled and maintained sectors and the diversity they represent, and to engage with some Shared Education partnerships.  I am clear that all our schools make a contribution to peace and reconciliation and to building a shared and cohesive community, and I consider that it would be positive and enjoyable for the Secretary of State to see this first-hand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to media reports that more than 70 sexual offence cases have taken more than five years to come to court, including cases involving minors, (i) for her assessment of the reason for this timescale; and (ii) to outline the measures her Department is taking to reduce this timescale.
answer_text: There are a range of reasons why cases can take longer than anticipated to complete. Whilst I cannot comment on specific cases, I recognise the negative impact that delay in the criminal justice system can have on victims, witnesses and on defendants and addressing avoidable delay remains a priority for my Department.  However, reducing the time it takes to complete criminal cases is a challenging and complex issue and reforms take time to embed and for their impact to be seen.In addition, like many other areas of public service, COVID has had a significant impact on efforts to speed up the justice system and has resulted in significant backlogs, which will take time to clear. There is no doubt the budget situation also presents challenges to recovery efforts, which will in turn continue to have a negative impact on delay.  However, we recognise that the system not only needs to recover but it is widely agreed that delay in general is an issue which needs to be addressed.  The Department has been progressing a programme of work with regard to Speeding Up Justice along with colleagues from across the system. This includes work to take forward the implementation of the provisions of the Criminal Justice (Committal Reform) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, which will help tackle delay in the most serious cases, which are heard in the Crown Court and will improve the experience of victims and witnesses on their journey through the criminal justice system in such cases.The Department is also working with justice partners to develop a Serious Sexual Offences Process, building on the work of the Indictable Cases Process, which seeks to encourage earlier engagement between the key parties, and focus on the key issues.Other initiatives in this broad programme of work included;performance reporting which has helped focus on specific issues, Crown Court Cases Performance Groups, which were established in 2019 to identify issues and lead performance improvements at a local level, the introduction of Case Progression Officers to all Crown Courts to provide additional administrative support to the judiciary in helping to aid case progression; and Speeding up the Justice System was the focus of an inquiry by the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) in May 2021, which looked at a report from the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) into avoidable delay in the criminal justice system in 2018.  Work has commenced on the development of a strategy in line with the report, which will seek to address the recommendations and push forward work to help reduce avoidable delay across the whole system and for all cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what is average length of time for a criminal justice case to come before the courts where a minor has been allegedly groomed, sexually assaulted or raped.
answer_text: Provisional figures for 2021/22 indicate that the median time taken from when an offence is reported until the case is dealt with at court was 840 days, where the main offence at disposal was a sexual offence involving a child. The median time taken for a case to come before the court (the median number of days from the date an offence was reported to the first court date after PPS decision to prosecute) was 483 days in 2021/22.Note:1. Figures relate to the number of calendar days between the dates listed.  Court recess dates are not taken into account.2. Figures relate to cases disposed at all courts during the time period specified. The period reported is a financial year, running from 1st April to the following 31st March.3. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, Harbour and Airport police in Northern Ireland that resulted in a court disposal.  Appeals are not included.4. Figures relate to case where the principal offence was a sexual offence that specified that the victim was a child in the offence description.5. Figures for 2021/22 are provisional.	In September 2019, in order to reduce the adverse impact of delay on child victims of sexual offences, a judge-led voluntary protocol was introduced to expedite serious sexual offence cases involving children under 13 in the Belfast Crown Court.  This has made a positive difference to the children and their families, and has recently been extended to September 2022.  		My Department, in co-operation with justice partners and the Lady Chief Justice’s Office, are building on the lessons being learned from the judge-led voluntary protocol and are developing a permanent protocol to ensure that these benefits can be extended to all child witnesses of serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland.  Implementation of the permanent protocol and wider roll out will however be subject to additional funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities in light of the comments by the Housing Executive's Chief Executive on the scale of homelessness in Northern Ireland, what measures her Department is taking to ensure that people are safe and have a roof over their head.
answer_text: The Housing Executive recently published the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, which outlines its overall approach to addressing homelessness. The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 is supported by annual action plans with the Year 1 Action Plan detailing 44 actions to help those who are homeless or threatened with homelessness.  The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 is intrinsically linked to the draft Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which was subject to public consultation in early 2022.  A final version of the Action Plan is due for publication in the coming months and will outline the Housing Executive’s efforts to support those households who avail of temporary accommodation.  The Housing Executive’s Housing Solutions and Support Teams will also provide support and assistance for any households who present as homeless to the Housing Executive and will seek to provide person centred services which are tailored to meet the needs of each household and support them to achieve sustainable housing solutions. This will include the identification of any support needs and subsequent engagement with other localised services in order to provide as far as possible wrap around support. This support can include floating support to prevent homelessness or temporary accommodation in cases where emergency accommodation is required.The Housing Executive has responded to the increase in demand for accommodation by extending capacity within the categories of Single Let accommodation and the use of non-standard accommodation for emergency short term placements.  In addition to funding services which support the delivery of statutory duties, the Housing Executive also provides funding to a number of Voluntary Sector Organisations which cover a range of support services including floating support, advice, and multidisciplinary services to assist those in danger of homelessness or in Temporary Accommodation. The prevention of homelessness remains a priority for the Housing Executive, as outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, and upon confirmation of any additional funding the Housing Executive will seek to deliver strategic priorities such as the Homelessness Prevention Fund in 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of applications to his Department's Farm Business Improvement Scheme Capital Scheme Tier 2 Tranche 2 scheme; (ii) the average time to process applications to the scheme; (iii) the total budget his Department allocated to the scheme; and (iv) whether the scheme was under or oversubscribed against the initial budget allocation.
answer_text: 199 applications were submitted for the Farm Business Improvement Scheme (FBIS) Capital Tier 2 Tranche 2 before the scheme closed on 20 May 2022.The time required to process applications varies depending on the quality of the information received, however, experience from the previous tranche of the scheme indicates that it takes between 4 to 9 months before applications reach the Letter of Offer stage.In terms of budget, my Department allocated up to £16 million for successful applicants. Early analysis of applications indicates the value of support requested is higher than the initial budget allocation, however, this will be reduced to within the scope of the agreed budget once the required eligibility checks and scoring of applications have been completed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of why Northern Ireland recorded the second strongest economic performance of any UK region in the 3rd quarter of 2021.
answer_text: The Office of National Statistics' GDP data for UK regions and countries  reports quarterly economic activity within the UK. In Quarter 3 (July to Sept) 2021, Northern Ireland, Scotland and England showed positive quarterly growth in gross domestic product (GDP) of 1.4%, 0.9% and 0.6%, respectively, while Wales showed negative growth of 0.3%. In assessing the driving factors behind this, the Northern Ireland Composite Economic Index (NICEI), while not produced on a fully equivalent basis to the ONS GDP data, indicates that Northern Ireland economic growth in Q3 2021 was largely driven by strong contributions from the services sector, which reached a series high in Q3 2021. My Department was at the forefront of the Executive’s response to the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on our local economy with over £500 million worth of support to our local businesses.  It is my belief that there is strong correlation between the welcome, strong performance of our economy in Q3 2021 and the efforts made in assisting local businesses during the pandemic by both the Executive and UK Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many children have not received a place at Rathore School in Newry after post-primary admissions for the upcoming academic year; and (ii) in which schools are they being accommodated.
answer_text: Due to the small number of pupils involved and in line with data protection legislation, the Education Authority is unable to provide a response to this question in its current form.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) which Department has responsibility for Translink; and (ii) who is the Board of Translink accountable to.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company (NITHC) is a public corporation and its responsibilities include holding and managing property assets and the oversight, in terms of support, control and supervision, of its private limited subsidiary companies, the principal ones being Ulsterbus, Metro (previously Citybus) and Northern Ireland Railways, trading jointly as Translink.  The Minister for Infrastructure is accountable to the Assembly for the activities and performance of NITHC.The Chairman of NITHC is responsible to the Minister for Infrastructure. The Board has corporate responsibility for ensuring that NITHC fulfils the aims and objectives set by the Department and approved by the Minister.Matters such as network planning and timetabling of buses and trains are operational matters for Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the information his Department holds in relation to the uplift in the productivity and profits of manufacturers in Northern Ireland, following the introduction of the Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not hold such data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) total funding which was initially allocated for the Newry Health Hub; (ii) initial timeline for delivery of the project; (iii) total funding that is currently earmarked for the development of the site; and (iv) current timeline for delivery of the project.

To ask the Minister of Justice, further to media reports of a social media video including allegedly sectarian comments from a pastor, what engagement she has had with the Chief Constable on whether the threshold for (i) criminal conduct; and (ii) prosecution has been reached.
answer_text: I am aware of the incident reported on 7 June 2022 of an online video showing allegedly sectarian comments from a Pastor.I have not discussed this matter with the Chief Constable. The threshold for criminal conduct and prosecution is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  PSNI have advised that the incident is being treated as a hate crime and that investigation is ongoing. 	Sectarianism must be confronted and addressed in all areas of society, which is why I am of the view that creating a sectarian aggravator is necessary to address sectarian offending in the Hate Crime Bill, which I intend to introduce in this Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to media reports of a social media video including allegedly sectarian comments from a pastor, (i) what engagement she has had with the PSNI; and (ii) what was the outcome of that engagement.
answer_text: I am aware of the incident reported on 7 June 2022 of an online video showing allegedly sectarian comments from a Pastor.PSNI have advised that the incident is being treated as a hate crime and that investigation is ongoing. 	This is an operational matter for PSNI and I respect their independence.Sectarianism must be confronted and addressed in all areas of society, which is why I am of the view that creating a sectarian aggravator is necessary to address sectarian offending in the Hate Crime Bill, which I intend to introduce in this Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement she has had with the Chief Constable on whether the conduct of those in the video footage of people singing about the death of Michaela McAreavey reaches the threshold for (i) criminal conduct; and (ii) prosecution.
answer_text: I utterly condemn the conduct of those involved in such deeply offensive and disturbing actions. My thoughts are with the Harte and McAreavey families, I can only imagine the hurt and anguish they suffered through such despicable behaviour.   On becoming aware of the online video footage I contacted the Chief Constable to seek reassurance that the PSNI was reviewing the footage to assess if criminal offences had been committed.Misogyny and sectarianism have no place in our society and must be challenged and responded to appropriately. As I am sure you will appreciate, investigation into criminal conduct and matters relating to the threshold for criminal conduct and prosecution are for the PSNI and the Public Prosecution Service to determine.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding her Department has provided to (i) sporting; (ii) cultural; and (iii) fraternal organisations in each of the last five years, broken down by recipient organisation.
answer_text: Funding awards made by my Department and its arm’s length bodies including those which have primary responsibility for the distribution of funding to (i) sporting and (ii) cultural organisations are recorded on the https://govfundingpublic.nics.gov.uk/Home.aspx which is publically available.   In relation to part (iii) information is not recorded under the heading ‘fraternal organisations’.You will be aware of the extensive financial support provided by my Department to mitigate the impact of Covid-19.  The details of successful grant awardees for relevant funding programmes delivered during 2020/21 and 2021/22 financial years can be found at  Annex A, which will be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many (i) pre-school; (ii) primary; and (iii) post-primary pupils are currently without a confirmed school place for the incoming academic year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the following information in respect of the number of pupils yet to be offered a place at each phase.The Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) has a two-stage admissions process which is based on parental preference.  Stages 1 and 2 of the process ended on 28 April 2022 and 16 June 2022 respectively. At 22 June 2022, the EA has advised that 14 children of target age or who have deferred entry to Year 1 and applied for a second year of funded pre-school education, whose parents have stayed with the process to the end, have not yet been offered a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.The breakdown by constituency is as follows:East Belfast - 1East Antrim - 1East Londonderry - 1Fermanagh and South Tyrone - 1Foyle - 2Mid Ulster - 3Newry and Armagh - 2Upper Bann - 3In respect of primary admissions, there are three children still to secure a school place, as follows:East Belfast – 2Fermanagh and South Tyrone - 1The number of children who applied for post-primary school through the Year 8 admissions process and who are still to be placed, broken down by parliamentary constituency, is published by the EA at the following link: https://www.eani.org.uk/publications/breakdown-of-statistics-re-p7-pupils-who-have-yet-to-be-placed-0  In respect of 14+ transfer in the Dickson Plan area, two children are still to secure a place and both reside in Upper Bann.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the total estimated cost for the installation of an MRI scanner and the associated upgrade in electrical infrastructure required to facilitate its installation.
answer_text: In June 2021 the Department wrote to all Health Organisations as part of the Budget 2022-25 process seeking to update the costs in the draft 10 year capital plan, given the increase in the price of materials, and asked that they identify any new projects that have emerged since their original priorities were submitted.    The Southern Health and Social Care Trust submitted a new proposal for a twin diagnostic suite at Daisy Hill Hospital, including the provision of a MRI and second permanent CT scanner. The estimated cost of this development is £9.1m.The Trust has advised the cost associated with the purchase of a new MRI scanner at Daisy Hill Hospital is £1.1m whilst the associated capital cost to upgrade the electrical infrastructure to support this service on the hospital site is £10m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether it is within her remit to instruct or direct the PSNI to adopt changes in its operational procedures.
answer_text: The terms of Section 33 of The Police (NI) Act 2000 state the PSNI is under the direction and control of the Chief Constable.  PSNI operational procedures are, therefore, a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board it would be inappropriate for me to seek to influence PSNI operational processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what engagement she has had with the PSNI in relation to the video footage of people singing about the death of Michaela McAreavey, recently circulated on social media; and (ii) the outcome of that engagement.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to Assembly Written Question 1115/22-27, setting out my reaction to the deplorable actions of those involved in the video footage circulating on social media and my engagement with the Chief Constable.  The PSNI must be allowed to follow due process as part of its ongoing investigations into this matter. Misogynistic and sectarian behaviour is utterly offensive, has no place in an inclusive society and must be challenged appropriately.  I continue to keep the Harte and McAreavey families in my thoughts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recruitment of emergency general surgeons at Daisy Hill Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Education how many schools (i) have an automated external defibrillator; (ii) do not have an automated external defibrillators ; and (iii) how many automated external defibrillators in schools were funded by the Department, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: According to Education Authority (EA) records 233 schools have an automated external defibrillator (AED). The EA had a tender contract in place for 4 years that enabled schools to purchase an AED at a very competitive cost. The figures held by the EA relate to the number of AEDs purchased by schools through this contract. Schools may also have purchased AEDs from outside the EA contract and had AEDs donated by parents, PTAs, community groups etc. The EA is unable to monitor the numbers of AEDs purchased by schools outside the contract and therefore is unable to provide a figure in this regard. The EA does not hold a constituency break down of the schools with an AED. The Department does not hold a record of the number of schools who do not have an automated external defibrillator. No automated external defibrillators in schools were funded by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Cost of Living payments announced by the British Government will be delivered in the same timeframe as England, Scotland, and Wales.
answer_text: The Cost of Living payments announced by the British Government, that are to be paid to pensioners and those in receipt of Universal Credit, Tax Credits, Pension Credit, legacy benefits and disability benefits, are planned to be delivered here in the same timeframe as England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what plans she has to work with the Minister of Health to ensure that there is an automated external defibrillator in every school.
answer_text: I recognise the importance and value for schools to not only have access to an Automated External Defibrillator (AED) on or near their premises, but also to have the appropriate training in place on how to use an AED. While there is no legislative requirement for schools to have an AED, my Department has worked with the Department of Health in the past to develop guidance for schools in installing and maintaining AEDs. My Department recently implemented a change to the curriculum which from September 2022 will see cardiopulmonary resuscitation training and awareness of the use of defibrillators taught to all pupils at Key Stage 3.I have written to the Minister of Health to ask for his views on whether a collaborative programme between our Departments could be considered that will encourage schools to consider installing an AED, and to offer my Department’s support in promoting this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the procurement for (i) resurfacing; (ii) surface dressing; and (iii) minor works schemes, detailed by division.
answer_text: ResurfacingThe current asphalt resurfacing procurement strategy consists of four phases, each of which comprises six new term contracts.   The assessment of tenders for the first phase, which includes contracts for Magherafelt; Omagh; Dungannon; Strabane; Down; and Newry & Mourne, is at an advanced stage and it is anticipated contracts will be awarded later this month.The second phase, which includes contracts for Cookstown; Derry; Ballymena; Armagh; Belfast North; and Larne & Carrickfergus, is currently out to tender and it is anticipated contracts will be awarded in late summer 2022. It is envisaged that tenders will issue for the third and fourth phases during late summer 2022 and autumn 2022, respectively. Surface DressingAll parts of the North are currently covered by a surface dressing contract with a total of seven contracts in place. Four contracts were awarded in May 2022 with the remaining three, which were awarded in 2019, still live. Minor Improvement Works All parts of the North are currently covered by minor works contracts, which are due to expire in 2023. Preparations are underway for two new competitions for replacement contracts. It is anticipated the eight replacement contracts will be awarded in early 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many second homes exist within the constituency of Newry and Armagh, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: My Department does not collect or hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what is the recommended timeframe target by which a patient who has suffered a Non-ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction should receive an angiogram; (ii) what percentage of those patients receive an angiogram in that timeframe; and (iii) what is the average waiting time for those patients to receive an angiogram.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance (NG185) is to consider coronary angiogram within 72 hours of first admission for people with non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) or immediately if their clinical condition is unstable.  The Myocardial Ischaemia National Audit Project Report (2021) states that, from the period of April 2019 to March 2020, the proportion of people receiving angiograms within 72 hours of admission with NSTEMI in Northern Ireland is 51%.  Average waiting times were not captured by the Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many teachers resident in Northern Ireland, who qualified over the last five years, are in (i) full-time teaching posts; (ii) long-term substitute teaching posts; and (iii) actively seeking a teaching post.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on engagement he has had with counterparts in the Irish Government in relation to Daisy Hill Hospital becoming a cross border health hub.
answer_text: My Department has already agreed cross-border engagement and co-operation on health service delivery with the Republic of Ireland in a number of areas. To develop any cross-border health service proposals would require political agreement in both jurisdictions and detailed joint policy work involving officials from both sides of the border. This is highly complex and would require significant time and resource. With the ongoing significant service pressures across Northern Ireland and in the local Newry area, it is my view that Daisy Hill would be best utilised in serving its local population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how many people own more than one property within the constituency of Newry and Armagh; and (ii) how many of those properties are vacant.
answer_text: My Department does not collect or hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice in relation to online abuse via social media platforms and orchestrated trolling by bot accounts, to detail (i) her Department’s level of awareness on the extent of the issue; and (ii) what specific actions her Department has taken to (a) protect people; and (b) hold those responsible to account.
answer_text: Criminality is all its forms is unacceptable and criminal activity facilitated by the use of technology remains a key aspect of crime in Northern Ireland.  The Organised Crime Task Force Annual Report and Threat Assessment for 2020/21 sets out the most common forms of cyber-enabled and cyber-dependent crimes as well as the operational response.  While trolling by Bot accounts is not specially referenced in this report, these fake accounts, generated in order to deliver a message or publish information automatically can be used to spread spam or amplify phishing attacks. I have provided a link to the most recent report for your information - https://www.octf.gov.uk/publications/octf-annual-report-and-threat-assessment-2020-21. Internet law and regulation is a reserved policy area, and the UK Government Department for Digital, Culture, Media & Sport (DCMS) is the lead department for the Online Safety Bill.  However, the issue of online safety is of wider interest and has the potential to impact on devolved matters, including in respect of criminal justice.  While I am supportive of the Bill in principle and endorse the need for new laws to require all services in scope to proactively remove and prevent users from being exposed to harmful and illegal content, I have also sought to influence the content of the Bill in terms of those areas where I consider it should be strengthened. It is vital that all possible safeguards are put in place to protect the vulnerable and that robust powers are available to hold those responsible to account.  I remain fully committed to working collaboratively with the UK Government as this legislation progresses.  My officials continue to engage with their colleagues in other Northern Ireland departments and their DCMS counterparts on an ongoing basis.  In addition to the Online Safety Bill the UK Government has announced a review of the Computer Misuse Act, including whether law enforcement agencies have the appropriate powers to investigate and prosecute associated offences.  There will be updates to Parliament on the review in due course.  My officials along with PSNI representatives are involved in this work and will keep me informed of progress.Any form of abuse is unacceptable. Due to the worrying levels of online and physical abuse and harassment experienced by some election candidates and canvassers during the recent NI Assembly election campaign, you may be aware that I have recently written to party leaders and Assembly colleagues on this issue.  I have invited views regarding these experiences which I will ensure are shared with Home Office officials who are progressing the Online Safety legislation. I also want to ensure that the experiences of those who had to endure these incidents are captured with a view to looking at what practical measures can be taken to address what happened and prevent further incidents in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the annual health service spend on the independent sector for each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what adjustments have been made to his Department’s approach regarding the supply and cost of fertilizer, fuel, grain and food as a consequence of the conflict in Ukraine.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the pressures that Northern Ireland producers are facing in relation to unprecedented rises in input costs of energy, feed and fertiliser which simply cannot be absorbed by farm businesses. It must be balanced quickly by price adjustments throughout the supply chain if the economic viability of primary production and domestic supplies of agricultural goods are to be sustained. Unfortunately, end markets for agricultural produce and food are currently responding too slowly to offset these rising costs, causing a major squeeze on cash flow and loss of profitability. This is undermining confidence throughout the industry and will inevitably result in a contraction in production due to mounting losses if the situation is not corrected very soon. This correction must come in the form of increased farm gate prices for all sectors. I have written to retailers to voice my concern regarding farm gate prices for primary food producers.I continue to engage with the UK Government and raised the current issues that our industry is facing at the recent Inter-Ministerial Group meeting. I have also engaged with a wide range of agri-food stakeholders and industry representatives to see what mitigation measures are possible.  My officials have significant ongoing engagement and intelligence gathering from industry stakeholders, DEFRA, other devolved administrations and the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (ROI) on a number of cross cutting issues relating to reducing the impacts of volatility in price and supply of key industry inputs.  Since the situation that our agri-food industry is facing is a UK-wide problem I have written to the Secretary of State, George Eustice MP, to urgently consider a range of measures, including financial assistance for UK farmers to support them through the crisis.  I have also written to the Finance Minister, Conor Murphy seeking funding to help address the challenges currently facing the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland. Officials at The College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) lead an industry taskforce (banks, producer and supply chain organisations, farming unions and Rural Support) to enhance the communication of advisory messages to the agriculture industry around the cost and availability of inputs and provide the sector with practical help and support. CAFRE has a dedicated webpage providing advice on managing the rising costs of production and has begun delivering a series of on-farm events, initially to beef and sheep producers and then in early June to dairy farmers. These events focus on good grassland management, fodder calculations, use of clover and calculation and management of cash flows. Rural Support, who provide impartial and confidential support and guidance to our farm families on a wide range of issues from business to personal wellbeing are also attending the CAFRE events. Farmers and growers are experiencing difficulties with cash flows and there is a need
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what initiatives her Department has brought forward to encourage all teenagers to play and have fun.
answer_text: The Executive Children and Young People’s Strategy (CYPS) 2020 – 2030 includes eight outcomes designed to support and improve the well-being of all children and young people, one of the eight outcomes is “the enjoyment of Play and Leisure”.  Given the cross-cutting nature of this commitment, it is supported by initiatives in a number of departments.  Relevant Department of Education activity is outlined below.     Youth work funded by my Department provides opportunities to build self-esteem, work as part of a team, make friends, socialise and take part in enjoyable and structured activities in a safe environment.  Young people of all abilities and diverse interests participate regularly in fun activities on a voluntary basis.The Outdoor Learning Service supported by my Department provides opportunities to engage in a range of fun outdoor activities that encourage physical exercise and provide opportunity to learn new skills. I provided £4.3m for a Summer Scheme in 2021. The main purpose was to help pupils in Primary, Post Primary, Special Schools and EOTAS settings make a successful return to school in August / September 2021.  There was a focus on emotional health and wellbeing, as well as activities which were interactive and fun, with plenty of scope for outside play.  In the summer of 2021/22 my department also invested £5m in a summer youth activity programme. The programme sought to provide a range of fun activities aimed at supporting the well-being of children and young people aged 4 – 25 years. A play messaging campaign funded by my department ran during 2021/22 finishing on the 31 March 2022.  It conveyed the benefits of play for all children and young people. In an effort to embed this message within strategic policy development, I have allocated funding to support the work of PlayBoard NI.  This will enable them to advise and support all government departments which have a role in improving play and leisure outcomes for children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the impacts on the health service, including its ability to tackle waiting lists, caused by budgetary uncertainty related to there being no Executive in place and the absence of a three year budget.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed budget for 2022-23 the Finance Minister wrote to departments in March setting out a way forward which involved DoF issuing departments with contingency planning envelopes for the 2022-23 financial year.  These envelopes provided departments with an assessment of the minimum funding they could reasonably expect for 2022-23 and allowed departments to plan for expenditure until such times as a Budget could be agreed. I have agreed an approach for my Department to proceed with opening allocations which will continue to fund activity in the first quarter of 2022/23 at previous levels while controlling spending in line with the advice from the Finance Minister. However I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid the ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement. My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.My Department needs a multi- year budget agreed to cover the next three years in order to provide a level of certainty on our future funding position and to enable us to start developing longer term plans to deliver services within the funding envelope provided and to sustain services going forward. This lack of certainty is very unhelpful to our Health and Social Care system and will likely impact on our ability to address key issues such as tackling Northern Ireland’s waiting lists and funding the cancer and mental health strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the current British Government legacy proposals.
answer_text: I was made aware of the Government’s current proposals on the morning of the publication of the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Bill on 17 May. I consider the proposals, which mark a significant departure from the Stormont House Agreement, to be fundamentally flawed and an irresponsible interference with the justice system. They do not meet the needs of victims and their families and risk undermining confidence in the rule of law, as well as falling short of the requirements of Article 2. There are many points on which we need further clarity but I do not see how the proposals can achieve the stated outcomes, nor how they will be workable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any new criteria required to be met, as a result of Brexit, to enable a cross-border haulage company to acquire a Goods Vehicle Operator Licence.
answer_text: The process for application and the types of licence to enable a cross-border haulage company to acquire a Goods Vehicle Operator Licence remain the same after Brexit. Two main changes have been brought in as a result of Brexit, and these are with regard to Light Goods Vehicles, and Transport Managers;Light Goods VehiclesPrior to Brexit, it was the recognised by the EU that large Light Goods Vehicles (LGV) between 2.5 tonnes and 3.5 tonnes were increasingly being used to bypass regulations. New legislation was introduced in Europe to address this and, as part of the TCA, it was agreed that UK operators using LGVs internationally between the UK and the EU would need to be licenced in the same manner as Operators using HGVs. The Goods Vehicles (Licensing of Operators) (Amendment) Regulations 2022 were introduced by Westminster on 20 May 2022 to bring this requirement into law. While some form of legislation would have been brought in if Brexit had occurred or not, it does have a greater effect in the North due to the nature of cross border trade. LGVs operating domestically are not covered by these regulations.Transport Manager Every Goods Vehicle Operator is required to have a designated and suitably qualified Transport Manager (TM) who is legally responsible for ensuring that vehicles and drivers meet the regulations for the legal operation of a goods vehicle operator. A TM has always had to be resident in the territory that the operator works in, which before Brexit was taken to be any member state in the EU. Prior to Brexit a Transport Manager could be resident in the North and be the TM for a business in the South of Ireland and vice versa. UK EU exit legislation allowed EU resident TMs to continue working as TMs in the UK in the hope of reciprocity. However, during Brexit negotiations, the residency requirement was maintained by the EU. Therefore, since Brexit, TMs resident in the North can no longer work as a TM for a company in the South of Ireland, or elsewhere in Europe, conversely, up until the introduction of the Goods Vehicles (Licensing of Operators) (Amendment) Regulations 2022 TMs resident outside of the North could still work here.  Whilst the process for becoming a Goods Vehicle Operator is largely the same for LGV and HGV operators, the new regulations amended the Qualifications of Operators Regulations 2012 by requiring a Transport Manager be a resident of the ‘Party’ in which their operator is established.    This means that a transport manager working for an operator in the UK (HGV or LGV), whether directly employed by the operator or contracted in, must be resident in the UK.    For the small number of transport managers working for operators in the North who had addresses outside the UK at the operative date, transitional arrangements permit them to continue in their existing posts indefinitely.    However, should they wish to move to a new operator in the UK, or add another UK operator to the operators they are currently co

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action her Department is taking to address the increase in domestic abuse cases reported in Newry, Mourne and Down in 2021/22.
answer_text: As Justice Minister, a key priority for me has been tackling public safety, protecting people from violent and abusive behaviours and securing justice for those affected.  The domestic abuse offence that was introduced in February this year represents a step change in this area, ensuring better protections and safeguards are in place and more effectively bringing abusers to justice.While any increase in domestic abuse is a concern it can reflect an increased willingness to report.  I recognise that domestic abuse is an under-reported crime, and much effort goes into ensuring that victims have the confidence to come forward and report.  The increase in the Newry, Mourne and Down area was the lowest increase across Northern Ireland.  An increase in recorded incidents and crimes may also be due in part to an increased awareness of the support services available.  My Department ran a ‘#stillabuse’ campaign in February to raise awareness of the domestic abuse offence coming into operation.  The campaign also aimed to increase confidence in reporting incidents to relevant authorities and agencies. As you may be aware, my Department jointly funds the Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs).  Newry, Mourne and Down PCSP are involved in a number of initiatives to tackle domestic abuse, including working with Women’s Aid Armagh Down to support the Domestic Abuse Safety and Support Service Worker based in Daisy Hill Hospital, Newry.  This is an independent specialist role, offering support to abused women.  It aims to increase immediate and long-term safety, with referrals mostly coming from Emergency Department and maternity services.  The wider Domestic Advice and Safety Support (DASS) pilot also provides early intervention across the emergency department and maternity services in Craigavon Area Hospital.  A number of domestic abuse initiatives are also being undertaken in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area involving a range of other agencies, including PSNI, PBNI, Women’s Aid and Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.  For example, a Domestic Violence Senior Social Work Practitioner has been appointed within Children and Young People’s Services (CYPS). Plans are in place to recruit Domestic Abuse Support Workers within CYPS Social Work Gateway services in that area, to provide early support to children and families.An Operation Encompass pilot was rolled out in September 2021, now covering 135 schools across Police District D, which includes the Newry, Mourne and Down area, and which fall within the boundaries of the South Eastern and Southern Trust areas.  I understand that a plan is in place to introduce this across all schools within those Trust areas by September 2022. Operation Encompass aims for all children experiencing domestic abuse to receive timely support in their school via informing the school of incidents without delay.In addition, Early Intervention Services in partnership with Family Support Hubs, Women’s Ai

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when public transport services, including cross-border services, will be restored to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: The management of public transport services, including cross border services, is an operational matter for Translink. You should therefore direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30597/17-22, to detail the annual variation in Emergency Department activity at Daisy Hill Hospital in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on activity at emergency departments (ED), including the number of attendances at Daisy Hill, is published annually by my Department and available to view on page 56 of the Hospital Statistics: Emergency Care publication at the link below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hospital-statistics-emergency-care-activity-202021

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a Consultant Psychiatrist will be appointed to Daisy Hill Hospital's Mental Health department.
answer_text: There is one permanent Consultant Psychiatry post currently vacant in Daisy Hill Hospital.  The recruitment process to fill this vacancy is ongoing and the Southern Trust is aiming to fill the post at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30039/17-22, to detail any business case (i) considered; (ii) prepared; or (iii) submitted to the Minister of Finance, for the expansion of the power infrastructure at Daisy Hill Hospital to facilitate the installation of an MRI machine.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust plans to procure a permanent second CT Scanner and an MRI Scanner on the Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH) site, however, this is not possible until an electrical upgrade is complete. An Outline Business Case was received for the DHH Electrical Upgrade on 6 May 2021.  Given the constrained capital position, the Trust were asked to review the preferred option in the business case to assess whether and how the project could be phased, and this is to be presented within a revised business case.As part of the 10-year capital priorities, the Southern Trust submitted a proposal to upgrade the electrical infrastructure at Daisy Hill Hospital starting in 2024/25.Delivery of this scheme will be dependent on a revised business case receiving appropriate approval and available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what measures her Department (i) has in place; and (ii) will put in place to ensure that the police have information about individuals who have learning difficulties, severe autism or mental health issues to help inform their approach.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is operationally independent from my Department and the Chief Constable has operational responsibility for the discharge of PSNI functions.  The Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).  Therefore, the primary responsibility in relation to operational policing approach lies with the PSNI.  I am committed to respecting the Chief Constable’s operational independence and the role of the NIPB.The Member may wish to note that, in 2019, my Department published the “Improving Health within Criminal Justice Strategy and Action Plan”, which is led jointly with the Department of Health.  The Action Plan contains a number of actions aimed at improving the health outcomes for all those who are in contact with the criminal justice system, including actions relating to the sharing of information, where it is in the best interests of the individual.  Efforts to progress this work were somewhat impeded by the need to redirect staffing resources to manage the COVID-19 pandemic and there will be a renewed focus by both Health and Justice over the next number of years in order to drive this work forward.I also understand that the PSNI has a number of measures in place, including an Information Technology system, to recognise an individual’s needs and vulnerabilities that may require additional or specific approaches and safeguards to support them when they come into contact with the police or the wider criminal justice system.Should you require additional information about the specific measures that the PSNI has in place, I would suggest that you write directly to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what processes are in place to ensure individuals with Down’s syndrome, and people representing them,  are treated with respect and dignity throughout the benefits assessment process.
answer_text: My Department aims to provide a fair and equal service.The Department makes every effort to ensure that an individual with Down Syndrome, and those representing and or supporting them, are treated with dignity and respect at all stages of the assessment process.  Staff undertake regular training in relation to dealing with vulnerable customers and to identify formal and informal representatives of vulnerable customers and ensure that they are acting with the consent of the customer.In addition, I can confirm that senior officials from Disability and Carers Service have engaged with the Ups and Downs Campaign Group to hear the concerns of parents of children with Down’s syndrome and to agree anapproach to avoid unnecessary renewal applications where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what timely measures her Department is taking to monitor Capita’s assessment processes, to ensure people are sensitively and fairly served and enables them to access the benefits they are entitled to.
answer_text: Departmental officials monitor and manage Capita’s assessment process across a range of key performance and quality indicators on an ongoing basis.A monthly independent Customer Satisfaction Survey is also undertaken to ensure that Capita and the Department continue to develop and meet customer expectations in our administration of PIP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding streams that are accessible to social enterprises and youth organisations that focus on building an inclusive and cohesive community in Armagh through various projects that aim to break down barriers and encourage self-development among young people.
answer_text: Social Enterprises and youth organisations in Armagh may be able to benefit from Neighbourhood Renewal funding for projects that seek to improve life chances and opportunities for those living in the most deprived urban areas.  Other available funding streams include those from Arms Length Bodies for example the Small Grants Programme which is delivered by The Arts Council and SportNI’s ‘Project Re-boot Activate’ Programme.Details of grant recipients and available funding programmes are accessible on the Government Funding Database, via the link below:https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk/

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the immediate measures her Department is considering to mitigate the impact of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents measures being introduced.However, I am committed to continuing to support individuals, families and communities who are experiencing real hardship. To giving them the helping hand they need to heat their homes, to put a meal on the table.I am determined that my department will continue to deliver for all those who are in need.I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all of the options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to her announcement in December 2021 to deliver 100,000 new homes, (i) how many will be built in Newry and Armagh; and (ii) when will they be built.
answer_text: Due to no functioning Executive or agreed budget, options relating to the Housing Supply Strategy are currently being considered.
